review_content: I had so much fun with this movie. It’s a callback to the goofy, 1960s-era Superman comics and that simple, kid-friendly comic book logic. It isn’t concerned with the technical details of things like pocket universes or black holes and honestly, I’m on board with that. The plot is goofy and often pretty dumb, but the film never takes itself too seriously. The moments that really work are when David Corenswet shows Superman’s earnest, boy-scout side - the version of the character who genuinely believes he’s the world’s protector. He channels Christopher Reeve’s Superman perfectly while still keeping things lighthearted. I really enjoyed the score and I'm looking forward to the sequel. This is a universe when Aquaman could work!

review_content: I don't have high expectations from a Superman movie - merely be entertained. This movie suffers the same problems which makes me avoid amovies today.   Superman plots were almost always silly, its the nature of the beast. It doesn't need to be but I don't mind. What I do mind is using humor for it's own sake. It kills the pace and some scenes you just want to end. And can we just make one of these movies without social commentary on the side ? Did I mention any of the characters ? No ? It's because they're that bland. [spoiler] And if the final scenes with this Supergirl are an indication I sure stay away from that one. [/spoiler] You recognize Gunn's style but the formula doesn't work here for me.  Positives ? Getting right to the story instead of starting all over with the origin was a good move. Everyone pretty much knows it anyway. I'm glad they stayed around the two hour mark because even that felt longer. And Krypto was a great addition. Althought I almost fear they might be tempted to do a spinoff if we say that to often.

review_content: I really didn't like this, despite the flashy action scenes and clean visuals. This is Gunn making yet another Guardians of the Galaxy, but with some DC characters and maybe there's a weird version of Superman in here too. As others pointed out, there's no sense of wonder here or any kind of depth.. There's literally some scenes where the conflict between Lex and Superman gets verbalised literally, as if the audience is really stupid and can't understand nuance or symbolism. There's something about him feeling inferior or whatever and Superman being 'human' despite him being alien. The rest of the movie is just trying to be funny and slightly edgy in classic Gunn fashion. His tone works for Suicide Squad and the Guardians but has no place in a Superman movie.  Everything here is also drenched in this weird contemporary nihilistic tone where the only thing that matters is either political or else it's kinda just funny because whatever I guess? You'll see it perfectly in Supergirl's appearance towards the end and the trailer for her new movie. Nothing really means anything and everyone is supposed to just be having a great time, laughing. It's entertainment isn't it? 'Be entertained', the movie tells you.  I still prefer Man of Steel. I've always loved that dark version of Superman. It's not perfect and has issues but there's sense of profound weight and meaning behind that version of the character, especially through its exploration of more religious symbolism.

cast_names: Brad Pitt, Damson Idris, Javier Bardem, Kerry Condon, Tobias Menzies, Kim Bodnia, Sarah Niles, Will Merrick, Joseph Balderrama, Abdul Salis
review_content: F1 is a classic sports movie, where the idea is to join in the tension of the competition and the fight for victory, seasoned with the idea of a man who should already be thinking about retirement, stubbornly fighting to prove that he can still stay in the race and teach the younger ones a lesson.    This makes F1 a somewhat predictable film, but it doesn't matter because, as the end approaches, you find yourself rooting for all the characters, anxious to see whether Sonny Hayes will achieve victory or not, and combined with the visual spectacle of the races, it makes the film extremely exciting.    Perhaps the only negative point is that it's very noticeable that the director, Joseph Kosinski, comes from directing Top Gun: Maverick, as F1 virtually repeats the same idea, but swapping planes for race cars. And while that doesn't make it a bad movie, it does take away any element of surprise, which isn't helped by the fact that the film follows the playbook of struggle and overcoming adversity found in any sports movie, but in a sport of millionaires.    But the film is great in every sense of the word, and I'm sorry I didn't see it in the theater.

cast_names: Brad Pitt, Damson Idris, Javier Bardem, Kerry Condon, Tobias Menzies, Kim Bodnia, Sarah Niles, Will Merrick, Joseph Balderrama, Abdul Salis
review_content: F1 is a high-octane, exciting and enjoyable ride that captures the sheer velocity of the sport. While the story occasionally falls into the trap of being a bit stereotypical—hitting some of the familiar "veteran vs. rookie" beats we’ve seen in sports dramas before—it never gets in the way of the pure entertainment on screen. The script is surprisingly sharp, backed by some snappy dialogue that keeps the non-racing scenes moving. Brad Pitt is on top form here, bringing a weathered charisma to the lead role that anchors the film’s emotional stakes. However, the real star might be the technical execution; the film features some of the best cinematography in years, utilizing incredible POV shots and visceral camera angles that make you feel like you’re strapped into the cockpit. It’s a technical marvel that balances its formulaic elements with pure, cinematic adrenaline.

cast_names: Brad Pitt, Damson Idris, Javier Bardem, Kerry Condon, Tobias Menzies, Kim Bodnia, Sarah Niles, Will Merrick, Joseph Balderrama, Abdul Salis
review_content: Overall    F1: The Movie is a motorsport drama about a fictional former F1 star driver who comes out of retirement after three decades away. Director Joseph Kosinski is most recognized for Top Gun: Maverick, but the quality wasn’t the same here. The amateurish story, character development, and over-dramatization impaired what could have been an excellent movie. Four Oscar nominations are on the line for later this year, which surprised me. Best Sound, Best Visual Effects, and Film Editing are well-deserved, but I don’t agree with Best Picture.     Would I recommend this?    No, you are welcome to skip this unless you’re an F1 fan. It’s heavily cliched from the first scene to the last, making it cringeworthy and difficult to endure. This was second-tier to the brilliant Ford v. Ferrari (2019) and Rush (2013), which featured intricate stories and poignant characters. I can’t believe Apple Film Studios earned more than $650 million from this. My rating is 7/10.    Plot (spoiler-free)    Sonny Hayes (Brad Pitt) was a gifted star Formula One racer in the 90s until a major accident sidelined him. He returned thirty years later after his former teammate, Ruben Cervantes (Javier Bardem), asked him if he wanted a shot at the F1 world title. Bardem’s team was struggling and needed a veteran driver to team with the egoistic rookie, Joshua Pearce (Damson Idris). The two have vastly distinct personalities and must work together against stiff competition. Time and money are on the line.    Interesting Tidbits    Most of the track incidents mirrored actual ones from various decades and drivers. Ten real Formula One teams joined the filming after negotiations with the Federation Internationale de l’Automobile (FIA). Lewis Hamilton, one of the greatest Formula One racers, helped produce this movie. Apple designed the lightweight car-mounted cameras used and only 10cmx10cm. They tried to maintain the racing realism. Production occurred in 2023 and 2024 during Grand Prix weekends, including the British Grand Prix where they set up their own garage between the Mercedes and Ferrari garages. They emulated these real garages and engineers to capture the live atmosphere. Idris and Pitt, the focal actors, did their own driving throughout in Formula 2 cars made just for this movie (and Formula 3 cars to prepare before filming). Pitt garnered over 6,000 miles of driving in his car during the production. The corporate sponsorships and deals with brands generated $40 million. They filmed over 800 hours of total footage, and editing took almost two years. Hollywood studios were involved in a bidding war for this script that had Brad Pitt confirmed until Apple Studio won the bid. This film topped the box office on release weekend and since has generated over $650 million total globally. The Formula One champion, Max Verstappen, skipped the private screening and world premiere of F1 because he preferred authenticity over dramatization.     Technicals    F1: The Movie has much going for it, but significant shortcomings. The best aspects were the performances, direction, editing, score, cinematography, camerawork, and pacing. Many shots were impressive, especially at the speeds they were driving. It reminded me of Top Gun: Maverick and the excellent shots in that. The authentic sounds, savant Hans Zimmer’s score, and high-speed racing kept you on the edge of your seat. The atmospheric races fill you with tension and blood-pumping thrills, even though they are unrealistic. Pitt’s remarkable charisma and Idris’ flamboyant personality demand that you keep watching. Visual effects, tight editing, and sensational cars dominate the scenes. If we stopped here, this would yield a legendary film. Sadly, I can’t stop here in the name of impartiality. The rest of this review will tear this film apart.    The clichés are predictable and overly dramatized until they become ridiculous. Although I don’t expect realism, I still expect a modicum of plausibility. This mocks the time, training, dedication, and hard work athletes put into their respective sports to the point of humiliation. Verstappen made the correct call not showing up for the world premiere. Next, let’s focus on the abysmal script. The superficial narrative is shallow and without substance. There is no character development outside of the hero, Brad Pitt, because, of course, only the hero matters (sarcasm). The dialogue is unnatural and uninspiring. I experienced little empathy for the characters. They throw in a romantic arc that doesn’t fit. The buildup lacks sufficient depth. The film leaves you feeling nothing by the end. Finally, what were the childish tactics they used? I don’t watch motorsports, but I’ve watched racing shows like Top Gear, documentaries on motorsports, and racing clips for over twenty years. Hamilton was involved, so I’m baffled to see this.    *Obtained trivia facts from IMDb’s trivia page and plot/basic history/name information from Wiki

review_content: A grounded, character-driven MCU entry that thrives on damaged personalities, strong team chemistry and a surprisingly thoughtful third act; while the Sentry is underdeveloped and some characters are mishandled, Thunderbolts* succeeds by focusing on flawed people doing the right thing almost by accident.

cast_names: Florence Pugh, Sebastian Stan, Julia Louis-Dreyfus, Lewis Pullman, David Harbour, Wyatt Russell, Hannah John-Kamen, Olga Kurylenko, Geraldine Viswanathan, Wendell Pierce
review_content: Well this one really caught me by surprise. I didn't expect much from the movie, but at the time it had quite a pull, so after postponing for a while I finally caught up.    A team formed by Yelena, Bucky, Red Guardian, Ghost, Taskmaster and John Walker. [SPOILER] Valentina recruits them for government missions, but they end up being part of a trap that forces them to confront their past and work together to survive. [/SPOILER] The chemistry between them works because they're all broken characters trying to find their place.    What hit me the most was how the movie treats depression and anxiety. It's not just a background detail, it's something that consumes these characters. Seeing them deal with that, with the weight of everything they've done and what's been done to them, connected with me in a way I didn't expect from a Marvel movie. There are moments where you see how anxiety paralyzes them, how depression takes away their will to keep going. It doesn't romanticize it or solve it with a motivational speech.    Florence Pugh continues to be the best thing to happen to the MCU in years. Her Yelena has that perfect balance between dark humor and vulnerability that makes you unable to look away. Sebastian Stan as Bucky finally has room to breathe and explore the character beyond being Steve's traumatized best friend. David Harbour brings the heart the movie needed.    [SPOILER] Valentina's betrayal and the twist about the true mission worked because the movie planted the doubts well from the beginning. The scene where they realize they've been manipulated has weight because we had already connected with them. [/SPOILER]    There are moments where you can tell Marvel still doesn't dare to fully let go of formulas, and the third act becomes a bit more generic than I would have liked. But compared to what Marvel has been putting out lately, it feels somewhat fresh. Although the "villain", I don't know, didn't quite convince me...    I liked seeing an MCU movie that doesn't try to save the world or connect with twenty other movies. Just a group of losers trying not to die and finding something like a family along the way.

cast_names: Florence Pugh, Sebastian Stan, Julia Louis-Dreyfus, Lewis Pullman, David Harbour, Wyatt Russell, Hannah John-Kamen, Olga Kurylenko, Geraldine Viswanathan, Wendell Pierce
review_content: The Avengers assemble (or do they) in _Thunderbolts*_. When CIA director Valentina Allegra de Fontaine faces a congressional investigation for her black ops projects, she attempts to kill off her black ops agents Yelena Belova, John Walker, and Ava Starr, but instead of killing each other they band together. Julia Louis-Dreyfus, Florence Pugh, Sebastian Stan, Wyatt Russell, and Hannah John-Kamen all return to reprise their characters and give strong performances. Additionally, the fight scenes are exciting and the special effects are impressive. However, the plot is predictable, and a fair amount of knowledge about the previous MCU films is needed to understand who the characters are. Yet despite its problems, _Thunderbolts*_ is entertaining and one of the better entries of Phase Five of the MCU.

review_content: The premise is incredibly good. The ending scene is satisfying but still feels like a miss. In between, it's mixed.    Construction is original and top level. I love when a story is structured correctly. When a movie is just one continuous single straight storyline, it gets boring real fast.    Were it just for the original base idea and the narrative structure, this would be easily an 8 or 9. But it felt really long. Some parts are really slow, maybe some characters were unnecessary. Still some very good scenes here and there (like when she demonstrates she can hurt the parents).    The whole ending, when we starts to learn the truth is just a bit disappointing, and the final struggle scenes felt unnecessary.    I don't get the "Weapons" things though, and maybe the assault rifle vision is just a misdirection, but it feels so forced and out of place. It almost breaks the fourth wall to tell this is to misdirect the audience and not to serve the story.

review_content: It's bad. Not only is it bad. It looks bad. The fact that this is oscar nominated for Special Effect is only evidence of a DIRTH of production in the industry.    The T-Rex or maybe T-Rex+ looks pretty good when we first saw it and then never again.    This movie takes a while to get going but it's purpose becomes clear. Jurassic Park was a great thriller. Great characters mostly doing interesting things with feelings and fear and really really threatening dinosaurs. Lost World was a sequel with characters we loved. III was unnecessary mcguffin hunt with flying dinosaurs. Jurassic World restarted the formula with a theme park a characters that kinda sucked. No offense to Bryce Dallas Howard she's a delicious human being but her character sucked. Which is a shame because I WANTED to like her. Chris Pratt was almost the opposite. Dumb people doing dumb things and then I think Jake Johnson died at the end. It took the dinosaur threat that's been neutered the whole movie and made it dangerous or it tried to. Fallen Kingdom was just a completely flawed concept. Dominion I've blacked out. I know how FK ended with the release but I barely remember Dominion. This continues. Could have easily just skipped Dominion.     In a world where Dinosaurs are everywhere... well not EVERYwhere just in "oxygen-rich" equatorial territories. I'm doing my best not to point out how stupid this is even as an attempt to make up for the stupid situation they had to write from. Anyway the primary plot is about a bounty hunter-type who has to take a crew to collect blood samples from the biggest dinos in the regions. Their blood will help cure heart disease. Our bad guy is a pharmaman who wants those sample so his company can make millions. Our angel is the innocent scientist who hatches a plot to steal the samples and make them open source. Along for the ride is a family boating in the ocean who got attacked and maydays for help. Daddy, Teen Daughter, Lazy Boyfriend, and Quiet Child. Our merc lady has a crew of people who... spoiler-alert will mostly die in _mostly_ reasonable ways.    The big draw for this dinosaur movie is that we don't have any dinosaurs. Instead they're all mutated former dinosaurs. Get ready to see cool sea dinos that are now amphibious. Get ready for regular dinosaurs with fear fins on them. You like a T-Rex? What about a T-Rex with a battering ram for a forehead and extra arms that have opposable thumbs. Get ready to be more confused that the T-Rex doesn't talk when it grabs someone and brings them to it's mouth and just when you think it's gonna say something... it doesn't.    From here we have all the fun stuff you expect, a pet dino, wow look at that big dino, new T-Rex is doing cool stuff, an interior chase, 10k volt fences, and an ending fight with a Rex.    More important for the oscar watch is that it looks bad, the editing is poor, the writing is weak, the acting is just passable. Calling this a good Jurassic World movie is daing it with faint praise. The Jurassic World franchise is awful. It's so bad it makes me miss Jurassic Park III and that movie is not good. This being the best of the JWorld franchise would mean nothing if I could even settle on that. The best smelling dog poop still makes you gag.

review_content: All right, so on the one hand, we had literal terrorists and on the other, we had KKK-wannabes. The KKK-wannabes are at least framed as bad guys, but was I somehow supposed to be rooting for the terrorists? Because I was definitely not. Other than the fact that none of these characters were established as even semi-likeable or remotely redeemable people, there was also zero backstory to any of them or their so-called cause. They were literally just a bunch of woke punks, thinking they’re above the law, simply because they’re just oh so important and morally superior. Right. Okay.   Secondly, this entire movie is SCREAMING left-wing conspiracy theory. So much so that it gets to a point where I can’t take the events even remotely serious anymore. For example, you’re simply just not going to convince me the entire military would set up an elaborate kidnapping scheme just to arrest an innocent child. Like, what was the reason? Are they saying the entire military is just so racist, they’re desperate to kill a random mixed-race child? Right. Okay.  Lastly, this entire movie just feels empty. It was clearly only made to convey a certain ideology, not tell an actual story. If I had to guess, though, what message it was trying to tell us other than “left ideology is good, right ideology is bad”, I would say that there are hints of; both ideologies are simply an excuse for bad people to enact violence for their own personal pleasure, and that one I can at least get behind. Still, it was a weak story, told with little depth.  Honestly, I was just bored.

review_content: Impressively boring! Departing from the source material is fine but Del Toro is so determined to contort this into his own preferred shape – a gothic romance slash weird parenting allegory – that you wonder why he bothered to stay close to it at all. It’s all a bit trite: half the people who encounter the monster immediately try to kill him and the other half barely blink. And then there’s the unearned and ludicrous ending which tries to have its cake and eat it regarding Victor’s monstrousness and contains dialogue that no human being could have uttered before about 1992. I also don’t agree with those who say “at least it looks good”. It looks expensive. The costumes look good. That is the most you can say. The actual filmmaking looks like overlit steadicam dogshit – and they somehow completely bricked the monster, which is the one visual thing you had to get right. That’s the whole job! Elordi seems cosmically doomed to be the best thing in bad films forever. Only reason the rating isn’t lower

cast_names: Oscar Isaac, Jacob Elordi, Christoph Waltz, Mia Goth, Felix Kammerer, Charles Dance, David Bradley, Lars Mikkelsen, Christian Convery, Nikolaj Lie Kaas
review_content: Overall    Frankenstein is a horror drama based on a classic 1918 book of the same name by Mary Shelley. I’m not a fan of remakes because they usually exist for monetary reasons. Filmmakers have too many ideas at their disposal to keep rehashing the same ones endlessly. However, Director Guillermo del Toro did an excellent job recreating this story in his own style. He took significant creative liberties with the source material, resulting in something unique and, dare I say, modern. While imperfect, there’s much to appreciate here for cinema fans and readers alike. Del Toro’s experimental, gothic style isn’t something I always prefer, but it was ideal for Frankenstein. This received 9 Oscar nominations, including Best Picture, Best Cinematography, Best Sound, Best Original Score, Best Actor in a Supporting Role (Jacob Elordi), and Best Adapted Screenplay.    Would I recommend this?    Yes, I recommend this! Del Toro executed it masterfully in a way that appears fresh. It’s difficult not to admire this thought-provoking, philosophical story where the created becomes the master and the hunter becomes the hunted. People have written and filmed countless variations over two centuries, yet the original still holds sway. Many 19th-century ideas have aged poorly, but this is not one of them. Frankenstein’s monster speaks more about humanity and life than anything else, which resonates with audiences. One who is broken must live on, for life doesn’t pause for pain. Overall, I suggest you go watch this! My rating is 8/10.    Plot (spoiler-free)    Dr. Victor Frankenstein (Oscar Isaac), a brilliant medical scientist, proposes to cure death through science. His colleagues refuse to allow it, claiming it sacrilege to play God. An arms merchant, Henrich Harlander (Christoph Waltz), seeks Victor to research the science behind reanimation. In return, Harlander offers unlimited funds and resources, including dead bodies from the ongoing Crimean War. Victor creates his monster (Jacob Elordi), and both must live with the consequences. The two must also come to terms with Elizabeth Harlander (Mia Goth), whom Victor loved, and William Frankenstein (Felix Kammerer), Frankenstein’s brother and Elizabeth’s fiancé.    Interesting Tidbits    By Del Toro’s orders not to use CGI or simulation, the team crafted every set. Elordi, who plays the creature, studied Japanese butoh dance and Mongolian throat singing to capture the creature’s mannerisms and styles. It often took the crew ten hours to apply his full-body makeup. He sometimes stayed up through the night for the makeup before early morning shooting. Andrew Garfield was the original cast for the monster before he dropped for Elordi. Del Toro considered turning Frankenstein into a trilogy, but I’m glad he didn’t. Given how a single movie dragged several times in two and a half hours, a trilogy wouldn’t be a smart idea.     Technicals    Frankenstein shone on the big stage behind a massive $120 million budget. The best facets were the 19th-century atmosphere on grand sets, cinematography, visuals, acting, script, music, directing, makeup, and costuming. Within the script, Del Toro included symbolism and witty dialogue. Performances are solid overall among the central cast, which includes Isaac, Elordi, Goth, and Waltz. The side characters were serviceable. Elordi deserves the Supporting Actor Oscar for his excellent portrayal of the monster, who displayed solid character depth. That which people don’t comprehend seems monstrous, which requires strong emotional acting. The positives complement the atmosphere and vibe required for such a cinematic experience. What makes the script succeed is that it maintains the philosophical perspective essential to Frankenstein. Overall, it is poetic justice filled with heart, redemption, and hope.    Poor pacing is where the issues begin. The editors should trim (or remove) several scenes that meandered more than necessary. I’m uncertain of which scenes, but the current framework led to cohesion problems and stretched playtime. Both diluted what could have been. Second, Victor Frankenstein evoked little emotion in me. Shelley’s novel draws sympathy for Frankenstein and his creation—rightfully more for the latter. Here, I experienced similar empathy towards the monster, but Frankenstein himself appeared lacking. Finally, not every character had proper closure. I experienced empathy for Elizabeth, but lacked closure. Henrich Harlander felt present then absent for long stretches. Even William Frankenstein appeared out of sync. Aside from Victor Frankenstein and the monster, I felt wanting.    *Obtained trivia facts from IMDb’s trivia page and plot/basic history/name information from Wiki

cast_names: Oscar Isaac, Jacob Elordi, Christoph Waltz, Mia Goth, Felix Kammerer, Charles Dance, David Bradley, Lars Mikkelsen, Christian Convery, Nikolaj Lie Kaas
review_content: Some stories are like lightning on the horizon. We know they’ve lit up the sky countless times before, but there’s always the chance that a new flash might reveal details we hadn’t seen yet. Guillermo del Toro’s cinematic take on “Frankenstein” leans exactly into that tension between familiarity and discovery. Instead of treating the material like an untouchable monument or a purely reverent adaptation, the director approaches it as something alive, something that can be emotionally rearranged without losing its tragic backbone. The result is a film that doesn’t try to compete with the cultural memory of the story, but rather reactivates it, digging into its moral and spiritual implications with a level of gravity that’s rare in contemporary horror. What comes out of that approach is less a monster spectacle and more an existential drama about creation, responsibility, and abandonment.    The narrative structure already signals that ambition by starting at a later point in the conflict, when creator and creature are locked in what feels like a mythic pursuit, before circling back to reconstruct the path that led them there. That movement immediately creates a sense of inevitability, as if both of their fates were doomed the moment artificial life was conceived. Oscar Isaac plays Victor Frankenstein as a man whose scientific conviction overrides any ethical hesitation, but without reducing him to the usual caricature of the mad scientist. There’s something deeply human in his obsession, a genuine belief that pushing beyond natural limits represents a moral step forward for humanity. That ambiguity carries the character throughout the film. Victor never sees himself as a villain. In his mind, he’s simply the one brave enough to attempt what others are too afraid to even imagine.    The emotional center of the story, though, belongs to the creature played by Jacob Elordi. Del Toro builds the character not just as a striking physical presence, but as a consciousness slowly coming into being, a being learning to perceive the world while simultaneously realizing it doesn’t belong in it. The creature’s intellectual journey, especially when it begins to acquire language and the ability to reflect, turns the horror into something deeply melancholic. Elordi conveys that evolution with a delicate balance of fragility and intensity. There are quiet moments that reveal more about the character than any speech could. A simple gesture of care toward a small animal, for instance, shows a kind of humanity so tangible that it makes the world’s reaction to the creature even more painful to watch. The monster here isn’t frightening because of how it looks, but because of the tragedy of its awareness.    That approach gains even more strength through the characters orbiting the story. Christoph Waltz brings an intriguing presence as a mentor figure whose early sympathy hides more complicated motivations, almost functioning as a morally ambiguous conscience within the narrative. Mia Goth, meanwhile, injects a restless energy into the family dynamic surrounding Victor, hinting that fascination with the macabre and the unknown can spread like a contagion. These characters expand the moral scope of the story, suggesting that Victor’s experiment isn’t just a single act of scientific arrogance, but an event whose consequences ripple outward through the people around him.    Visually, “Frankenstein” reaffirms Guillermo del Toro’s aesthetic signature in almost hypnotic fashion. Anyone familiar with his work will immediately recognize his ability to turn the grotesque into something visually poetic. Here, that sensibility shows up in obsessively detailed production design where every element seems tied to the theme of artificial creation. The creature’s body, assembled from different parts struggling to coexist, echoes through the film’s environments, costumes, and visual compositions, all of which evoke fragmentation and recomposition. A palette dominated by deep reds and heavy shadows creates an atmosphere that constantly shifts between dream and nightmare. The cinematography leans into stark contrasts, turning laboratories, frozen landscapes, and Victorian interiors into spaces loaded with visual symbolism.    Alexandre Desplat’s score strengthens that sensory dimension with a constant dramatic presence, guiding the narrative while still allowing moments of silence where the film can breathe emotionally. Those pauses are crucial, because that’s where the movie really finds its identity. Del Toro seems far less interested in jump scares or traditional horror spectacle than in exploring the creature’s fundamental loneliness. The film ultimately becomes a meditation on belonging, or more precisely, on the impossibility of it. As the character begins to understand its own condition, the story takes on a philosophical tone, questioning not just the limits of science, but also the moral responsibilities that come with the act of creation.    That reflective layer makes “Frankenstein” something more complex than a simple story about an experiment gone wrong. The film becomes an investigation into what it means to create life while refusing to accept the consequences of that act. The central tragedy isn’t just that Victor challenges nature, but that he fails to recognize the humanity of what he has created. In that sense, the creature becomes an uncomfortable mirror of the society that rejects it. Its monstrosity doesn’t come from how its body was assembled, but from the way it’s treated by those who should, in theory, recognize it as part of the same world.    In the end, “Frankenstein” feels like a deeply personal project within Guillermo del Toro’s filmography. The director has always been drawn to marginalized figures and creatures seen as aberrations, and here that obsession reaches one of its most mature expressions. The film balances visual spectacle, emotional tragedy, and philosophical reflection with clear authorial confidence. More than simply retelling a familiar story, it tries to rediscover its moral pulse, reminding us that some narratives survive the passage of time precisely because they continue to reflect our most intimate fears. In this case, the fear of creating something that might one day force us to face the possibility that we ourselves are the real monsters.

cast_names: Oscar Isaac, Jacob Elordi, Christoph Waltz, Mia Goth, Felix Kammerer, Charles Dance, David Bradley, Lars Mikkelsen, Christian Convery, Nikolaj Lie Kaas
review_content: Guillermo del Toro’s Frankenstein is a visually stunning and thought-provoking masterpiece that feels less like a remake and more like a deeply personal re-imagining. Del Toro treats the source material with incredible care, crafting a version of the tale that is uniquely his own while leaning into the gothic beauty he’s known for. The emotional core of the film is Jacob Elordi’s excellent performance as the Creature. He brings an overwhelming sense of sympathy, pain, and sadness to the role, making the monster feel profoundly human. While the film is a little too long and feels overdrawn in certain stretches, the sheer artistry on display keeps it grounded. It’s a haunting, beautiful achievement that may test your patience with its pacing, but rewards it with a truly great cinematic experience.

cast_names: Oscar Isaac, Jacob Elordi, Christoph Waltz, Mia Goth, Felix Kammerer, Charles Dance, David Bradley, Lars Mikkelsen, Christian Convery, Nikolaj Lie Kaas
review_content: Please take Del Toro’s name off the bill so people can finally stop trying to justify this train wreck of a film. “Grotesque” in the worst possible sense of the word, it stands as a striking example of bad taste in both its presentation and its performances, with the exception of Jacob Elordi (whom I’m seeing here for the first time and does a solid job).    The characters are paper-thin, wandering aimlessly until the next plot development drops out of nowhere, only to serve yet another half-assed take on the tired "man is the real monster” trope. Del Toro’s films have never been praised for the writing, but they usually made up for it with a memorable visual identity. Here, everything just feels cheap and poorly executed, dragging the whole production down even further.    The opening sequence (textbook example of a streaming-era hook and completely unrelated to the rest) is so dreadful that what follows almost feels like an improvement by comparison. Still, aside from a couple of decent scenes, the film remains a mess. I wish I were part of the collective hallucination that somehow turned this into an awards contender.

movie_title: Wake Up Dead Man: A Knives Out Mystery
cast_names: Daniel Craig, Josh O'Connor, Glenn Close, Josh Brolin, Mila Kunis, Jeremy Renner, Kerry Washington, Andrew Scott, Cailee Spaeny, Daryl McCormack
review_content: Another wacky whodunnit / howdunnit from Johnson and Craig that drops, stylishly, into the crossroads between an Agatha Christie novel and the _Clue_ movie. This time around, the setting is a lovely chapel in upstate New York, a magical blend of old world architecture and lush foliage that makes for some magnificent, painterly screen compositions. Therein, a tight cluster of unfriendly parishioners huddle around a charismatic, outspoken clergyman and collectively ward off any newcomers who might stop in for a service. Until the main man winds up dead, that is, mysteriously stabbed in the back in the middle of a service. That lures the famed detective, Benoit Blanc, and the requisite swirling tempest of intrigue that always seems to follow in his wake.  As in the two preceding _Knives Out_ murder mysteries, Blanc sucks up all the air in a room when he’s around, so it’s good that he holds out until the second act to make his grand appearance. Daniel Craig seems to lean a little further into the comical eccentricity of this character each time he takes up the mantle, and while _Wake Up Dead Man_ finds his ridiculous accent somewhat diminished, Blanc’s mustier appearance and renewed personality quirks ensure he’s never at risk of having a scene stolen. Not that he wants for competition. Josh Brolin is feisty and magnetic as the defiant deceased pastor at the center of all the fuss, while his overly-protective flock is studded with equally colorful, if reductive, role-players. They all mix and stir like ingredients in a busy stewpot, each frantic and suspicious in their own way, for their own reasons.  Unfortunately, the game seems a little less exciting this time around, with neither the unreliable narrators of _Knives Out_ or the clever misdirections of _Glass Onion_. This is more straightforward than the preceding films, a bit less playful and inventive if no less colorful. It’s still a good time, and I was surprised by its ability to treat organized religion with dignity (fully expected a few cynical japes there), but most of the lingering flavor lies in Craig’s portrayal of the headline hero and not the mystery itself.

review_content: This was a creature comfort murder mystery that you can just chill back into your seat and enjoy, however I think it is a downgrade from the original Knives Out engagement with the audience. The plot is interesting, the pacing is okay, but there is less investigative development than within the common crime & detective genre. I believe a greater emphasis on the logical reasoning from the detective would have made this movie better, the final hypotheses seem to fall out of the sky and caught me off guard. It was not an "aha" moment, it was more of a "okay then". It was still a good few hours as it is with all the detective movies though.

review_content: The John Wick spin-off _Ballerina_ is a messy but exciting action film. The story follows an orphan who’s taken in by a ballerina troupe and trained to be an assassin, but goes rogue to get revenge against the man who killed her father. Ana de Armas, Anjelica Huston, Gabriel Byrne, and Keanu Reeves lead the cast and give solid performances. However, it doesn’t really feel like it’s part of the world of John Wick, and John Wick himself feels shoehorned in (which doesn’t make a lot of sense since it’s set during _John Wick 3_ when he was in exile). But the fight scenes are extraordinarily kinetic and action-packed. _Ballerina_ is entertaining and full of thrills, but doesn’t really fit into the John Wick franchise.

cast_names: Arden Cho, May Hong, Ji-young Yoo, Ahn Hyo-seop, Yunjin Kim, Ken Jeong, Lee Byung-hun, Daniel Dae Kim, Joel Kim Booster, Liza Koshy
review_content: “KPop Demon Hunters” is the kind of surprise that reminds you commercial animation can still find creative paths inside formulas that seem worn out. The premise is instantly appealing, blending the world of K-pop with supernatural fantasy and following a trio of idols who split their lives between packed stadiums and demon-slaying battles. The film knows it’s working with a high-impact concept and leans into it without hesitation, building a narrative that confidently shifts between musical spectacle and supernatural adventure. There’s a clear structural familiarity, but also a genuine effort to carve out its own identity, especially by weaving in elements of Korean mythology and using pop culture as a thematic lens.    Visually, the film is a treat. The animation goes for a vibrant style that pulls from both comic book aesthetics and contemporary anime influences, creating a sense of motion that perfectly matches the energy of K-pop. At times, it’s genuinely impressive in its inventiveness, especially in action sequences that almost turn into stylized music videos. The inevitable comparisons to recent genre-defining animated films make sense, not because “KPop Demon Hunters” is trying to reinvent the wheel, but because it understands how visual language can drive storytelling. When the film is in motion, it really pulses.    The soundtrack is another essential pillar of the film’s success. The songs are clearly designed to work both inside and outside the movie, and that shows in how seamlessly each musical number is woven into the story. There’s a sharp understanding of pop logic here, not just in the immediate catchiness of the songs, but in how they reflect the characters’ internal struggles. That connection between identity and performance is where the script finds its most interesting layer, especially in the protagonist’s arc, which revolves around a secret that threatens her public image. The film does a solid job linking that conflict to the idea of manufactured celebrity and the pressure of living up to impossible expectations.    The issue is that, narratively, the film doesn’t always trust its own language. Whenever it slows down, there’s a tendency to over-explain emotions and themes that were already clear through visuals and music. That creates a sense of repetition in certain stretches, as if the script is afraid of losing the audience and keeps reinforcing its intentions. Because the structure is relatively tight, that extra weight stands out even more, breaking the flow that works so well in the more dynamic moments. You can easily imagine a more elegant version of this film that would let the subtext breathe, allowing the aesthetics to carry more of the emotional load.    On top of that, the central message becomes more ambiguous the more you think about it. At first glance, the film feels like a story about self-acceptance and embracing who you are, even when that puts you on the margins. But the way the resolution plays out suggests something more contradictory, almost as if acceptance is framed as an individual exception rather than a real shift in perspective. That ambivalence doesn’t ruin the experience, but it does leave a slightly uneasy aftertaste, like the emotional message isn’t fully aligned with the story’s own conclusions.    Still, “KPop Demon Hunters” works because it understands the power of pop entertainment when it’s executed with personality. It stands out thanks to its energy, visual appeal, and ability to connect with different audiences without losing momentum. Even with thematic stumbles and some narrative redundancy, the film finds strength in the blend of spectacle and emotion, delivering an experience that’s hard to ignore. It may not be a genre-defining landmark, but it’s the kind of success that proves mainstream animation still has room to take risks, as long as it has something genuine to offer beyond the formula.

cast_names: Kaitlyn Santa Juana, Teo Briones, Rya Kihlstedt, Richard Harmon, Owen Patrick Joyner, Anna Lore, Alex Zahara, April Telek, Andrew Tinpo Lee, Tony Todd
review_content: What a mixed bag of a film. The opening scene has a fantastic setup but descends into a muddy CGI-fest; something that blights a lot of the subsequent death scenes. I was initially disappointed that it didn't stick with the 50's setting but the film does use it to smartly expand the mythology to incorporate inherited fate and survival as a family curse felt decades on.  It just doesn't really do an awful lot with the concept once established and instead allows it to introduce a ton of plotholes and illogicallities. These aren't helped by the inconsistent pacing giving you time to think about them.  Performances are serviceable rather than great. The obvious exception is Tony Todd's final appearance as Bludworth. Filmed while dying of stomach cancer, his quiet, improvised goodbye; looking into the camera and urging us to cherish each day, is a heartfelt moment in a film that otherwise feels it could have been scripted by committee. The way his character backstory is woven into the film was quite neat also.  The kills range from okay to good, with the exception of the final scene, which is pretty throwaway (a characteristic of many FD films) However, the setups just don't feel as coherent or carry the tension of the series best. The aforementioned CGI, while not completely awful, is overused throughout and acts to blunt the impact of the kills.  Despite the failings of the film, it's pretty inoffensive, fairly short and I had an okay time with it. Can't agree with those complaining about the increased humour; I've seen 5 of these now and a tonal shift isnt the worst thing in the world.

cast_names: Kaitlyn Santa Juana, Teo Briones, Rya Kihlstedt, Richard Harmon, Owen Patrick Joyner, Anna Lore, Alex Zahara, April Telek, Andrew Tinpo Lee, Tony Todd

review_content: Gerard Butler's mother Margaret fell ill during production in Belfast, so he would go back to Scotland most weekends to be with her. She passed away in February 2025 at age 81. Butler, who grew up without a father, shared how touched he was that director Dean DeBlois paid tribute to her at the end of the live-action remake of the animated franchise: "I was so excited for her to see it, but I had a feeling she wouldn't. So Dean very kindly dedicated the movie to her." DeBlois shared that he brought the idea to Universal Pictures so he could surprise Butler with the dedication. "I knew that the loss of his mother was a deeply felt wound and that he was having a tough time recovering from it," DeBlois said. "So to honor her with a dedication in the credits just seemed like the right thing to do in that moment." Butler, who had already voiced Stoick in the franchise's animated films, shared how his mother knew, "Since I was a kid, I wanted to be an actor. If Stoick had a mum, that would have been my mum. She was an amazing woman, but she was strong, she was fiery, and she was graceful, and she was beautiful. When I saw the movie, I noticed that I had put more of my mother than of myself into that role."

review_content: As another reviewer said, Astrid didn't seem to change her facial expression more than 3 times the entire movie. Her botox lips destroyed my suspension of disbelief every single time she showed up, she really ruined it for me. The CGI is good, I enjoyed the larger scale dragons, and the casting I thought was fine otherwise, especially Hiccup and Tuffnut. The storyboarding is practically the same as the original, but the tone of the movie converted the lighthearted animation into a high-stakes action film with a forced chemistry between Hiccup and Astrid. Apparently they're making a live action for the second movie too. Really milking this franchise.

cast_names: Mason Thames, Nico Parker, Gerard Butler, Nick Frost, Gabriel Howell, Julian Dennison, Bronwyn James, Harry Trevaldwyn, Murray McArthur, Peter Serafinowicz
review_content: TLDR: don't waste your time just watch the original, its better and if you have a kid you want to laugh a lot the original is 100% better.  had to delete my previous comment after rewatching the original. my first comment was how the script had changed, it wasn't as funny, the characters didn't look right to me because im a stickler to original characters looking the same such as Astrid and the twins aren't blond, snotlout looks like a tiny kid and ruffnut just looks... in general completely wrong compared to the original.   but AFTER the rematch I realized they took a LOT of the meat out of the original probably to cut costs for production, clearly this new version is supposed to cater to adults that likely haven't seen the original or for their dragon loving kids. but 90% of the humor was removed, the script feels rather dry, and somehow of the best scenes were removed altogether.   I would never watch this again unless paid to do so, but the price would have to be pretty good since I had already felt it was a waste of my time and wound up doing other things while it played and that was only roughly 30 minutes in. after I finished watching this dumpster fire of my ruined childhood I wound up binge watching the original movies and had a blast! I knew they were funny and amazing but forgot how good they were!   so in the end this movie gutted the original, wasn't captivating in the least, made me feel like my childhood was getting ruined with each minute I watched this and I think its a dumpster fire.

review_content: For anyone wondering:   If you never watched the original movie, and you are thinking about watching this, don't, just watch the original movie.   If you have watched the original movie and liked it, and now you are thinking about watching this, don't, just go rewatch the original movie.   Now, if you did not like the original movie, just rewatch the original and see if now you like it... only after that if you still don't like it... Then watch this?    Beware: the story, the scenes and script of the movie, almost line by line, are the same (with the script being a little bit arguable worse?).   Maybe the worse performances here and the "live action" and darker lighting scenes aspect are going to make you enjoy it? I really doubt that.   It's just an unnecessary live action remake of a still great animation that holds up 15 years later, in my opinion. review_content: This may not be the worst movie I’ve seen this year but it’s certainly the stupidest. Rami Malek I’m certain isn’t cut out for being a lead. He was unconvincing as a Bond baddie and even less so as a CIA agent, even a nerdy one! The plot of this is too ridiculous for words and the way in which this incompetent idiot just zips around from country to country without any back-up, resources or even contacts is just laughable, particularly when paired with the ease with which he locates his, supposedly top tier terrorists from basically public information. Despite continuously losing everything he always seems to be able to acquire everything he needs. The movie just chooses to ignore all the stupid crap it can’t explain and explains the rest with a smattering of IT BS. The ending is also super lame.  This is more Jason Been than Bourne. 5/10 and that’s being really generous.

review_content: The visual effects in this movie are shockingly bad and completely at odds with the aesthetic and spirit of Minecraft. For a film carrying a reported $150 million budget, the production value is bafflingly poor. Nothing looks cohesive or intentional, and the digital environments feel cheap rather than stylized. Instead of embracing the blocky simplicity that defines the game, the film opts for visuals that feel generic, overprocessed, and strangely unfinished.    The cast does little to help. Nearly everyone seems uncomfortable, but the worst offender is Jason Momoa, whose performance is aggressively miscalculated and distracting. Jack Black, on the other hand, leans fully into his usual manic, goofy persona. While it’s clearly a recycled act, it oddly fits the chaos of the movie better than anything else on screen. He feels like he understands the assignment, even if the assignment itself is deeply flawed.    Tonally, this movie plays like a Gen Alpha and younger Gen Z fever dream. The humor feels dated almost immediately, and the constant winking at the audience becomes exhausting rather than charming.    There is very little sense of narrative, worldbuilding, or emotional grounding. It feels less like a film and more like a loud, overstimulating content feed stretched to feature length. Instead of celebrating creativity, which is the heart of Minecraft, the movie reduces the property to noise and references.    A deeply misguided adaptation that misunderstands what made the game special in the first place.    2/10.

cast_names: Jason Momoa, Jack Black, Sebastian Eugene Hansen, Emma Myers, Danielle Brooks, Jennifer Coolidge, Rachel House, Allan Henry, Bram Scott-Breheny, Moana Williams
review_content: I know this isn’t exactly a Christmas movie but I saw it pop up on my TV earlier and I simply just couldn’t resist not stepping into this super cool world with the legend himself known as Steve. I honestly love this movie and it no way deserves the hate from some reviewers, look I get it’s not a total masterpiece but it’s super duper fun and incredibly silly and I just love it for that.   This should literally just be called Jack Black the movie as you literally can’t get any more bloody Jack Black than this, which of course isn’t a bad thing as I freaking love Jack Black with his one of a kind voice and humour. He is obviously the standout in this film as he is super awesome and so funny here with his wacky, iconic lines. I also like Jason “the garbage man” Momoa as he was also brilliant in this and cool as hell.  My favourite part in this other than Jack Black is the villagers as they’re just super hilarious and I love the weird sounds they make, I still can’t get over how one actually managed to date Stifler’s  mom though but I mean come on like who can’t resist that charming voice of his, I also love how fast she moved on from Finch.  I know the movie’s not perfect but I still love it a a lot as it’s so funny, so cool and totally freaking awesome. I also kinda love the vibe of it too and I could instantly tell it was directed by the same director as Napoleon Dynamite, Jared Hess as it has that kinda feel to it. I am once again scoring A Minecraft Movie a totally sweet 10 out of 10.  10/10

cast_names: Jason Momoa, Jack Black, Sebastian Eugene Hansen, Emma Myers, Danielle Brooks, Jennifer Coolidge, Rachel House, Allan Henry, Bram Scott-Breheny, Moana Williams
review_content: I’m amazed by how impressed I remain with this movie. Maybe it’s not so great in an objective, snooty cinematic sense, but as a proudly weird, hyperactive, self-indulgent, legitimately funny bit of bald entertainment that makes no qualms about what it is or why? Man, they really nailed it. This isn’t a soulless corporate cash-in, it’s a heartfelt homage with a wild’n’crazy spirit and a loud’n’proud voice.    I certainly expected the worst when I heard Microsoft was producing this. I grimaced when I learned Jack Black had been tagged for the leading role. I furrowed my brow when it was revealed that Jared Hess would be helming the director’s chair. I like the game, I like Tenacious D and I like _Napoleon Dynamite_. But pouring those diverse ingredients together in the same vat? No way that should’ve worked so well. And now we await the sequel with similar trepidation.

cast_names: Jason Momoa, Jack Black, Sebastian Eugene Hansen, Emma Myers, Danielle Brooks, Jennifer Coolidge, Rachel House, Allan Henry, Bram Scott-Breheny, Moana Williams
review_content: Video-game movies always walk a weird tightrope: either they try to please everyone and satisfy no one, or they become so specific that only the hardcore fans understand what’s going on. Minecraft lands right in the middle. It’s peculiar, a bit chaotic, clearly aimed at kids… and yet it has moments that work much better than I expected.    The first thing that stands out is the tone. It’s loud, colorful and never tries to take itself seriously. For children, that’s already enough: big creatures, silly gags, impossible chases, and that “improvised adventure” feeling that actually resembles how people play the game. For adults, well… it depends on your tolerance that day. Sometimes it’s tiring, sometimes it slips down easily, but it isn’t the pain some other adaptations have been.    Emma Myers is easily the highlight. She’s got a natural glow, a charisma that lifts even the most basic scenes, and she’s becoming one of those actors who improve anything they’re in. Jack Black does his usual over-the-top chaos (fun but familiar), and Momoa brings more comedy than expected, even if the script doesn’t always help him.    The biggest issue is the lack of imagination. Minecraft —the game— lives off total creativity, of building whatever you want from nothing. The movie, instead, plays it safe: a predictable adventure, familiar jokes, and set pieces we’ve all seen before. It works, but it doesn’t leave a mark. There are moments where you feel all the energy is going into noise, not spark.    Even so, it delivers on its basic mission: entertain without overthinking, give kids a fun time, and offer a tiny bit of nostalgia to anyone who spent hours mining and building. It won’t be remembered forever, but it doesn’t need to be. And, somehow, within its imperfections, it ends up being kind of likable.

cast_names: Ben Affleck, Jon Bernthal, Cynthia Addai-Robinson, J.K. Simmons, Allison Robertson, Alison Wright, Daniella Pineda, Robert Morgan, Grant Harvey, Andrew Howard
review_content: Outside of Affleck and Bernthal I couldn’t care less about anyone in this movie. It tried to be a little bit “deeper” and more emotional than the first but it came off as trying way too hard. Why should I care about agent Medina? Why should I care about the kid who we never really meet. Why should I care about the mom for that matter? The “boss” character was not scary or intimidating or interesting. His head henchman was the real threat here.   There are some good bits to be found. Bernthal outraged about not getting his dog fast enough. The square dancing scene. And maybe the final broo-ha-ha action at the end stood out specifically. I have an autistic wife and can relate to a lot of what Affleck portrayed on screen but sometimes it veered off into that stereotype zone a bit too much. Pretty good overall though.   And finally, nothing was ever explained and everything just happened to work out for everyone involved? A thin, confusing story and forgettable characters held this one back unfortunately.

cast_names: Liam Neeson, Pamela Anderson, Paul Walter Hauser, Danny Huston, CCH Pounder, Kevin Durand, Liza Koshy, Eddie Yu, Michael Beasley, Moses Jones
review_content: There are few things more miserable than a zany screwball comedy that doesn’t work. Sorry to say it, but I found the new _Naked Gun_ thin, try-hard and utterly excruciating. It waves its wackiness under our noses, somehow carries things further over the top than the original films, and winks, gratingly, after every weak punchline. Felt like I was being constantly elbowed in the ribs here. I got the joke, thanks, I just didn’t think it was very funny. Repeat that about two hundred times. Upon reflection, there were precisely two ideas that hit me right and drew a laugh, and those were quick one-offs that disappeared faster than a flash in the pan. Otherwise? Pandering, repetitive, lowbrow nonsense. The first _Naked Gun_ was lowbrow, too, and equally hit-or-miss, but it never felt like a suck-up and its well of ideas was damn near bottomless. And it had a far more versatile, expressive star in the driver’s seat.    At first glance, casting Liam Neeson in that primary role seems like a daring choice; a good sign that the new producers aren’t afraid to take a few risks and differentiate their take from the franchise’s earlier installments. Neeson isn’t known for his comedy chops, but that idea was mined to tremendous effect in a short segment on the Ricky Gervais series, _Life’s Too Short_, back in 2011. Then, as now, Neeson was only regarded as a stern, gravel-voiced action star and Gervais leaned into it, using the star’s severe demeanor and one-minded focus on dark subjects to deliver a fabulously unsettling series of improv takes. We try the same thing here, but his edges are softer, the setups are more transparently scripted and he’s more brazenly self-aware. He also appears positively geriatric. Leslie Nielsen never looked so pained in his action scenes, but then, Leslie was also a decade younger, despite what outward appearances might lead us to believe.    Maybe this caught me on a bad night. Maybe I’ll find a few more reasons to chuckle if I lighten up and settle in for a re-watch alongside a more receptive audience. Maybe I’ll owe Neeson (and supporting star Pamela Anderson, who bored me to tears) a big apology. Or maybe this just sucks.

review_content: While it doesn't quite reach the groundbreaking heights of the original, Zootopia 2 is a fantastic extension of the world that avoids a "sequel slump." The film shines brightest as an emotional exploration of Judy and Nick's partnership, moving past the "honeymoon phase" to look at how their differing worldviews actually clash now that they're full-time partners. The animation remains top-tier—it looks amazing, especially with the introduction of the semi-aquatic Marsh Market and the textures on the new reptile characters like Gary. Though the mystery is a little predictable and follows a familiar "conspiracy" beat, the puns are as sharp as ever and the new cast members (like Ke Huy Quan and Fortune Feimster) bring great energy. It’s a heartfelt, vibrant follow-up that offers something for the whole family, even if it plays it a bit safer than the first.

cast_names: Ginnifer Goodwin, Jason Bateman, Ke Huy Quan, Fortune Feimster, Andy Samberg, David Strathairn, Idris Elba, Shakira, Patrick Warburton, Quinta Brunson
review_content: Zootopia 2 — A Confident, Joyous, and Razor‑Sharp 10/10 Triumph    Zootopia 2 doesn’t just return to a beloved world—it expands it with the confidence of a franchise that knows exactly what made the original resonate. What emerges is a sequel that’s funnier, sharper, more emotionally grounded, and visually richer, all while deepening the themes that made the first film iconic.    :fox: A Story That Balances Heart, Humor, and Stakes  The film’s greatest strength is its narrative clarity. Judy and Nick—now fully realized partners—face a conflict that challenges not just their dynamic, but the very idea of what Zootopia stands for. The sequel smartly avoids repeating the first film’s mystery structure; instead, it builds a story about trust, ambition, and the cost of progress in a city that’s always one step away from chaos.    Every emotional beat lands. Judy’s drive, Nick’s charm, and the evolving trust between them give the film a warm, beating heart. The humor is sharper, the pacing tighter, and the character moments more intentional. It’s a sequel that respects its audience.    🎨 A Visual Feast With Real Cinematic Weight  The animation is nothing short of stunning. Zootopia’s districts—Sahara Square, Tundratown, Rainforest District—are more alive than ever, each rendered with meticulous detail and dynamic lighting that gives the world a tactile sense of scale. Crowd scenes feel massive, action sequences are fluid and inventive, and the film’s color palette is bold without ever losing nuance.    This is Disney animation at its most confident.    :loud_sound: A Soundscape That Elevates Every Scene  The score blends energetic modern motifs with emotional orchestral swells, giving the film a sonic identity that feels both familiar and evolved. Ambient sound design—city chatter, environmental textures, species‑specific movement—adds layers of immersion that reward repeat viewings.      🏆 Why It’s a 10/10  Because it does everything a sequel should do—and more.    It deepens the world without overcomplicating it    It evolves the characters without betraying them    It sharpens the themes without preaching    It delivers spectacle without sacrificing intimacy    It respects the original while confidently forging its own identity    Zootopia 2 is a rare sequel that feels both necessary and inspired. It’s heartfelt, hilarious, visually dazzling, and thematically resonant—a film that reminds you why animation can be one of the most powerful storytelling mediums.

review_content: Bugonia is the kind of movie that knows it’s weird, leans all the way in, and then asks you to keep a straight face while it does it. Emma Stone and Jesse Plemons are excellent together and play off each other beautifully, with Stone once again going gloriously all-in on being odd, intense, and completely committed. It’s a strange, over-the-top sci-fi suspense comedy, shot very well, with the director doing a smart job of painting in the backstory and explaining just enough to keep the whole thing afloat, while also sneaking in a little ecological warning for humanity. My main issue is that beneath all the stylish weirdness, the plot is actually pretty predictable and the pacing can feel slow, like the movie is very pleased with itself for taking the scenic route. And while there is a twist at the end, it’s less a jaw-dropping reveal than a “yes, that’s more or less what I figured” moment, so it doesn’t land with quite the punch it seems to think it does. Still, even if it’s more good than great and nowhere near awards-level greatness, it’s an entertainingly bizarre ride with two terrific performances at the center.

cast_names: Emma Stone, Jesse Plemons, Aidan Delbis, Stavros Halkias, Alicia Silverstone, J. Carmen Galindez Barrera, Marc T. Lewis, Vanessa Eng, Cedric Dumornay, Momma Cherri
review_content: At first glance, Bugonia looks like one of those low-budget independent films that come and go without making much of a splash. But nothing could be further from the truth. Beneath that modest exterior lies a surprisingly powerful film, with a very well-crafted narrative and several twists you don’t see coming, which constantly keep you on your toes as a viewer.    That said, one gets the feeling that part of the film’s impact is down to the fact that it stars Emma Stone. Not because she’s bad—she does a solid job—but because her presence now seems almost a constant in Yorgos Lanthimos’s films, to the point where it’s hard to imagine the film would have had the same media buzz without her at the top of the bill.    The one who truly steals the show is Jesse Plemons. He’s one of those actors who often go unnoticed, but here he delivers an absolutely devastating performance. Every scene in which he appears carries weight, makes you uncomfortable and leaves a lasting impression, building a character who is equally deeply unsettling and fascinating.    Special mention must be made of the use of sound, particularly in relation to his character. Very cleverly, the film uses sound design to reflect his disconnection from reality, creating an almost sensory experience that puts you inside his head and makes you feel his isolation in a very effective way.    Bugonia is one of those films that deceives: it looks small, but it plays in the big leagues. Daring, unsettling and with performances that elevate the material far beyond what one would expect when first watching it.

Of course, there's plenty of details along the way that were unpredictable, and there's a steady supply of darkly hilarious interactions, but ultimately this felt like one where it was less than the sum of its parts.  Incredibly well made and enjoyable, but not something I feel the need to re-watch anytime soon.

cast_names: Emma Stone, Jesse Plemons, Aidan Delbis, Stavros Halkias, Alicia Silverstone, J. Carmen Galindez Barrera, Marc T. Lewis, Vanessa Eng, Cedric Dumornay, Momma Cherri
review_content: The Epistemology of the True Believer  Consider what it means to be right for the wrong reasons. Bugonia constructs its entire dramatic architecture around this question — two men, Teddy and Don, who have evacuated every social contract, every institutional trust, every shared agreement about reality, and filled the resulting void with something that looks, from the outside, indistinguishable from madness. They kidnap Michelle Fuller. They believe she is an alien. The film asks us to sit inside that belief with them, to feel its interior logic, its strange coherence. For a while, it succeeds. Then it makes the one choice that dismantles everything it had built.  The conspiracy is real. The aliens exist. Teddy was right all along. Watch how this single revelation retroactively drains every preceding scene of its most dangerous implication. What had functioned as a portrait of belief as shelter — of paranoia as the only architecture available to men whose relationship with meaning has fully collapsed — becomes instead a story of prophecy. Teddy is not a man who chose a comforting delusion over an unbearable reality. He is simply a man who saw clearly when others could not. And in making this choice, the film inadvertently argues the most reckless idea imaginable: that the break from consensus reality might just be the first step toward truth.  Ask yourself what the film loses in that moment. We had been watching something genuinely unsettling — not the threat of alien infiltration, but the threat of two human beings so fully untethered from shared epistemology that they could hold a woman captive and experience it as righteousness. There is a version of this story where the uncertainty is never resolved, where we are made to inhabit both Michelle’s terror and Teddy’s conviction simultaneously, where the film refuses to arbitrate between them. That version understands what makes true believers so terrifying: not that they might be wrong, but that the structure of their belief is identical to the structure of being right. Bugonia glimpses this possibility and then retreats from it entirely.  Plemons locates something real inside Teddy nonetheless — a specific, load-bearing dignity, the kind that forms when a person has fully committed to their own mythology. Every gesture carries the weight of someone who has already rehearsed his own martyrdom. Stone, given far less to work with, is asked to function primarily as a narrative unknown — opaque by design — and she holds that ambiguity with precision for as long as the film allows her to. But notice how her character’s trajectory is the film’s most significant structural failure: Michelle begins as a cipher and ends as a confirmation. She exists not to be understood but to be revealed. The film is not interested in what it would mean to be her.  Will Tracy understood, in The Menu, how to use satire as a scalpel — how to implicate the audience in the very system being dissected, how to make the joke land and wound simultaneously. Here, the satirical targets are less stable. Corporate alienation, epistemic collapse, the aesthetics of conspiracy — these are gestured at rather than inhabited. The film builds its set with genuine craft and then, in its final act, burns it down not to illuminate anything in the ashes but simply because the spectacle of burning is itself the point.  There is a particular kind of disappointment in a film that asks the right questions and then answers them. The mystery of Michelle — is she human, is she other, does the distinction even hold — was never meant to be solved. It was meant to be experienced. The moment it resolves into confirmation, the film collapses the very space it had opened. What might have been an excavation of how belief constructs identity, of how the mind builds cosmologies to survive the absence of meaning, becomes instead just another story where the madman turns out to be the prophet. Some films leave you in the uncertainty. This one escorted you to the exit.

cast_names: Glen Powell, William H. Macy, Lee Pace, Michael Cera, Emilia Jones, Daniel Ezra, Jayme Lawson, Sean Hayes, Katy O'Brian, Colman Domingo
review_content: The Running Man starts as a very straightforward action film, but what really hooks you is how easy it is to believe in the world it presents. This isn’t a distant or sophisticated dystopia: it’s a recognizable, almost immediate future where big corporations rule, entertainment devours everything, and violence is sold like just another sporting event. And it does so at such a relentless pace that, as happened to me, you forget you were even tired.    Edgar Wright plays a double game here. On one hand, he delivers pure spectacle: chases, constant adrenaline, sharp editing, and a ticking-clock feeling that never lets up. On the other, he drops a fairly clear critique of media circus culture, public opinion manipulation, and how the system turns suffering into content. It doesn’t always dig as deep as it could, but it never hides what it’s pointing at.    The film works best when it leans into its most physical and urgent side. There’s a constant, almost exhausting energy that keeps you locked in, even when some ideas don’t fully land. It’s true that at times it feels more interested in keep running forward than in looking back and fully closing what it sets up, but the ride is so entertaining that it’s hard to hold that against it.    Glen Powell carries the whole thing with solid charisma. He doesn’t need to be a classic hero or a flawless symbol; he just needs to feel believable inside this brutal machine. The cast and tone support him well, and Wright proves he can handle blockbuster scale without completely losing his personality, even if it’s more restrained here than in some of his previous work.    It may not be as devastating as its premise promises, nor as incisive as the source material suggests, but as an experience it works extremely well. It’s one of those movies you enjoy while it’s happening, that keeps you glued to the screen and leaves you, when it’s over, with the uncomfortable feeling that not everything you’ve seen is that far from becoming reality.

review_content: The film adaptation of The Housemaid falls short in several key aspects, especially when compared to the original book. While the story itself is compelling, the movie struggles to capture the same depth and tension that make the novel so engaging.  In the book, the narrative flows much more naturally, allowing the suspense to build gradually and keeping the reader fully immersed. The characters feel more developed, and their motivations are clearer, which makes the twists more impactful.  In contrast, the film feels rushed at times, skipping over important details and emotional nuances. As a result, the story loses some of its intensity and coherence. Overall, while the movie is watchable, it doesn’t do justice to the richness and pacing of the book, which remains the superior version of the story.

review_content: Perfectly watchable, but I was hoping for a lot more out of a Ben Affleck/Matt Damon led film.  The set-up felt a little cliché/clunky, but once we get into the titular rip I could see the potential.  But the potential is mostly unrealized, as things pretty quickly devolve into a lot of uninspired action sequences in the back half.  Ben Affleck one handing an automatic rifle out the window of an SUV just doesn't do it for me.  Still, the story did just enough to keep me invested and I don't regret watching.

cast_names: Matt Damon, Ben Affleck, Teyana Taylor, Steven Yeun, Catalina Sandino Moreno, Sasha Calle, Kyle Chandler, Scott Adkins, Daisuke Tsuji, Nestor Carbonell
review_content: This movie positively surprised me. It managed to keep me confused and suspicious from beginning to end — and honestly, I still don’t fully trust anyone after watching it.    The story starts abruptly and throws you straight into a confusing situation that slowly unfolds as the film progresses. It’s the kind of movie that demands your full attention to detail; if you miss a scene, you’ll feel even more lost.    I watched it with a friend, and we spent the entire time coming up with theories, which made the experience even more enjoyable. By the end, we were satisfied with how everything was explained and how all the pieces came together. I strongly recommend it.

review_content: "Highly trained" officers just walking out into a gunfight in the middle of the street and not even bothering with cover, it's always so annoying to see.    The trope "we don't know who to trust" is always so annoying too. Like, just call it in to absolutely everyone then?    I wish modern film making didn't always do all these extreme closeup shots, there's no field of detail so everything feels like a set. It's a constant closeup to the  extent where they start feeling like legless puppets.    The whole thing had kind of an amateur film maker vibe to it honestly, but the story was decent.    Anyway, Desiree, the girl in the house... She gets 20% because I guess she was the one who tipped of the cops or whatever, but won't the cartel know? Won't they just hunt her down and kill her, to make an example? I mean they'll easily know she got 4 million...

review_content: **Things I liked about the movie**    **++** The CGI is great for Stitch.   **++** I like the Tutu character they added to the story; she was funny.  **++** Billy Magnussen was a good Pleakley    **Things I didn't like about the movie**    **--** Why make all the damn changes? Bubbles as the social worker was awesome in the original and think it would have been funny to have a giant as the social worker from the get-go in live action.   **--** It's too long... The ending is dragged out needlessly  **--** Jumba is about 100 times better in the animation than in live action. Zach Galifianakis didn't seem to want to be there.   **--** The jokes they did keep in the movie just don't hit the same when it's live action. I don't know why, but Lilo wasn't as likeable for me in the live action compared to the original.    I have watched both the live-action and the animated version with younger family members and the animation gets way, way more laughs. I really don't think there's anything in the remake that is done better than the original. So yeah, just watch the animation. How this made a bazillion dollars I don't get lol
