cast_names: Cooper Hoffman, David Jonsson, Garrett Wareing, Tut Nyuot, Charlie Plummer, Ben Wang, Jordan Gonzalez, Joshua Odjick, Mark Hamill, Roman Griffin Davis
review_content: That was a waste. It was just sort of "eh". I kept waiting for something to have more meaning and actually make sense, but it was literally just kids walking for 300+ miles. Repetitive, and boring. I didn't understand why certain kids would just run off the road, or try to grab guns. I imagine more is in the book, but I didn't read the book. I feel if I had, I'd probs be even more disappointed. I heard this was going to be a really good movie. I was let down. The acting is subpar for most of them. I'm not quite sure what Ray's actual motive is. Not to mention, there's no real plot or reason why they do the walks other than it seems people are lazy? They can afford all this money for one winner every year for 19 years, but not the whole country? That just doesn't make sense to me. The ending was annoying also. Glad to be a bit surprised, but also thought it was dumb. [spoiler]He ended up just doing what the other guy wanted to do anyway, and then he just kept walking? I would imagine killing The Major would be instant death, no?[/spoiler]

cast_names: Cooper Hoffman, David Jonsson, Garrett Wareing, Tut Nyuot, Charlie Plummer, Ben Wang, Jordan Gonzalez, Joshua Odjick, Mark Hamill, Roman Griffin Davis
review_content: I used to love Stephen King. I think I must have read this Richard Bachman novel at one time. I was more impressionable at that time.  Now, after all the “games” movies (hunger, squid), this doesn’t have anything left to offer. The only truism that repeats itself with many King novels is that every moment matters.  But then the ending comes and takes all the essence out of the main points. Instead of the good in people, the payoff is for revenge.  To be honest this setup is always doomed to fail. Last man standing is an empty trophy without the camaraderie. And honoring the friendship means you have to sacrifice any good sense of the end.  Always fighting against the system, people would not want to see that this nightmare dystopia stays as it was.  So either you somehow avoid the ending or you make it meaningless. I kind of felt like the latter in the end.

cast_names: Cooper Hoffman, David Jonsson, Garrett Wareing, Tut Nyuot, Charlie Plummer, Ben Wang, Jordan Gonzalez, Joshua Odjick, Mark Hamill, Roman Griffin Davis

But because of this there was little to no character development or backstory to the characters, including the main characters, so was difficult to have any emotional attachment to any of them. So when they were eliminated .....ehhh, I didn't really care that much :person_shrugging:♂😂I liked the twist at the end but the overall outcome was predictable. 64/100

cast_names: D'Pharaoh Woon-A-Tai, Will Poulter, Cosmo Jarvis, Kit Connor, Finn Bennett, Taylor John Smith, Michael Gandolfini, Adain Bradley, Noah Centineo, Evan Holtzman
review_content: Warfare 6/10    Hard to understand if anyone is really getting hit—there’s endless shooting and almost no consequences. You do feel how trapped they are, forced to stay in a kill-box, the fear constant.    But we also know why the U.S. went there, so it’s hard to feel sorry for the soldiers if they aren’t being manipulated by the usual “we’re here to save people” line. Young Americans blasting randomly, ammo never ending, no rockets, tactics thin. The escape is the best part—messy and tense—and they somehow manage it at last.     Decent intensity, weak clarity.    Tags: #War #Trapped #Escape #Tactics #Morality

cast_names: D'Pharaoh Woon-A-Tai, Will Poulter, Cosmo Jarvis, Kit Connor, Finn Bennett, Taylor John Smith, Michael Gandolfini, Adain Bradley, Noah Centineo, Evan Holtzman
review_content: The true horror of war. Illustrated in what is almost a slice of life.

cast_names: D'Pharaoh Woon-A-Tai, Will Poulter, Cosmo Jarvis, Kit Connor, Finn Bennett, Taylor John Smith, Michael Gandolfini, Adain Bradley, Noah Centineo, Evan Holtzman
review_content: As I've matured, I've educated myself on topics such as imperial warfare, especially when it comes to America and the Middle East. I won't get into my personal or political reasonings, but I will say my appetite for 'war' stories/films like these has dropped off. The only real reason I watched this was for Alex Garland, who I feel keeps his opinions biased but facts wide open for interpretation. Based on true events, 'Warfare' doesn't glamorise militarism in the way you would think. High on brotherhood, low on perspectives. Great directing, producing, and casting. I'd have given it a 6 if it weren't for my appetite.

cast_names: D'Pharaoh Woon-A-Tai, Will Poulter, Cosmo Jarvis, Kit Connor, Finn Bennett, Taylor John Smith, Michael Gandolfini, Adain Bradley, Noah Centineo, Evan Holtzman
review_content: Warfare is a brutal, unflinching look at the realities of war—one that refuses to soften its edges for the sake of accessibility. It’s not the kind of film designed for mass appeal; instead, it leans into the chaos and ugliness of combat, grounded by a raw and unfiltered tone. What really stands out, though, is how beautifully it’s shot. The cinematography captures both the horror and the haunting stillness of the battlefield, creating moments that linger long after the credits roll.  It’s not an easy watch, but it’s an impressive one—an artistically crafted war film that prioritizes authenticity over spectacle.  Rating: 3.5/5 – 80% – Would Recommend

cast_names: D'Pharaoh Woon-A-Tai, Will Poulter, Cosmo Jarvis, Kit Connor, Finn Bennett, Taylor John Smith, Michael Gandolfini, Adain Bradley, Noah Centineo, Evan Holtzman
review_content: Warfare is fucking intense. Easily one of the most realistic depictions of combat I’ve ever seen. The chaos, the confusion, the constant tension. It doesn’t glorify war, it throws you straight into it and dares you to breathe. And don’t even get me started on the sound design. You feel every shot, every scream, especially the silence before all hell breaks loose. It’s anxiety turned into audio.    I saw some reviews calling this film pointless or saying it has no message. Maybe they missed it, because to me, the message is clear, this is what war is, and this is what war does to people, both military and civilian. This is what they endure. These guys weren’t just soldiers, they were fucking warriors.    It’s not the kind of action film that makes you cheer, it’s meant to make you feel, to help you understand, even a little, what these guys go through. And it fucking delivers.

cast_names: Jared Leto, Greta Lee, Evan Peters, Gillian Anderson, Jodie Turner-Smith, Jeff Bridges, Hasan Minhaj, Arturo Castro, Cameron Monaghan, Sarah Desjardins
review_content: Me and the kids sat down and watched this movie a couple of weeks ago. As somewhat of a fan of the original, having watched the sequel and being a sci-fi, computing and sfx geek I of course had to watch this one.    I’ve read some articles claiming it was not that good but I have to disagree. I think it was pretty good. The fact that the professional retards at a certain (very) rotten site claims it to be rotten was of course a sign that it might actually be good.    TRON is obviously a movie that is supposed to cater for sci-fi, computer and sfx geeks and in this I would say that it succeeds. The various chase and fight scenes were cool and when Athena brought out the big guns I went “Hell yeah!”.    In addition the story is not all that bad. There’s not too much of Hollywood’s usual woke finger pointing and the story works okay to tie the sfx together.    There is quite a bit of fan service which I personally like. I do not know why people keep complaining about this? If you have successful franchise then do not stop catering for your fans. Unfortunately plenty of Hollywood morons do not seem to grasp this simple fact.    Of course, although this is supposed to science fiction, the science part went out of the window already in the first movie and this one is no different. It is a sfx driven action/adventure movie and, as I wrote, I quite liked it.

cast_names: Jared Leto, Greta Lee, Evan Peters, Gillian Anderson, Jodie Turner-Smith, Jeff Bridges, Hasan Minhaj, Arturo Castro, Cameron Monaghan, Sarah Desjardins
review_content: Unpopular opinion. I think this is the best of the Tron movies. They all were eye candy and this certainly is but this seems like an actual movie with characters and actions being taken. The other films felt more like someone's sophomore philosophy essay played out in a boring SNL skit. Had the lead (Leto) been less wooden and had a thimble of charisma, this may have been a good flick.

cast_names: Jared Leto, Greta Lee, Evan Peters, Gillian Anderson, Jodie Turner-Smith, Jeff Bridges, Hasan Minhaj, Arturo Castro, Cameron Monaghan, Sarah Desjardins
review_content: I won’t pretend otherwise: I liked it. Quite a lot. Much more than Tron: Legacy, which always felt visually stunning but emotionally distant to me. This time there’s more drive, more momentum, and even if the story isn’t especially deep, at least it moves with purpose.    Visually, it’s a feast. Neon lights, impossible chases, digital architecture built for the biggest screen possible. There’s something hypnotic about this world that blends video-game aesthetics, techno-mythology and full-on blockbuster energy. Not everything fits perfectly, but when the film hits full speed, it works.    Is it complex? Not really. The script oversimplifies some ideas, and there are moments that clearly started from a “this would look amazing in IMAX” mindset rather than narrative logic. But honestly, that didn’t bother me as much as it did others. The film knows it’s spectacle and doesn’t pretend to be something else.    Jared Leto is divisive, no doubt. His character doesn’t always land emotionally and can feel more like a concept than a person. Still, the overall experience holds thanks to pacing and a staging that embraces visual excess without apology.    And then there’s the best part: the soundtrack by Nine Inch Nails. Trent Reznor and Atticus Ross turn each sequence into something bigger, darker and more physical. There are moments where the music elevates scenes that would otherwise be merely fine. It’s not background — it’s propulsion.    It’s not a sci-fi revolution or an instant classic. But as a sensory experience and as a stronger return to the Tron universe, it absolutely works for me. You leave with your pulse racing and the score stuck in your head. That counts for something.

cast_names: Jared Leto, Greta Lee, Evan Peters, Gillian Anderson, Jodie Turner-Smith, Jeff Bridges, Hasan Minhaj, Arturo Castro, Cameron Monaghan, Sarah Desjardins
review_content: Great movie, superb action scenes, awesome visuals and a lot of fun. Fuck the haters, this movie is great. I especially enjoyed Greta Lee's performance, she a phenomenal actress. Jared Leto was okay, Gillian Anderson should have had much better part, her talent was shamefuly underused. Overall a great watch.

cast_names: Jared Leto, Greta Lee, Evan Peters, Gillian Anderson, Jodie Turner-Smith, Jeff Bridges, Hasan Minhaj, Arturo Castro, Cameron Monaghan, Sarah Desjardins
review_content: The writers of this movie made it pretty convenient. They decided to make the person who would get chased decide to ride alone on a motorcycle, instead of a limo after leaving the airport, so she could be more dramatically chased on motorcycles by the digital villains from the Tron world.    Then, she didn't call the police or tell anybody she had just escaped being kidnapped. She chose to do everything to stay quiet and isolated, making it easier for the villain to continue to put her in danger.    And most importantly, if they have just developed a way to computer generate food, medicine and whatever, they would have solved all the world's problems, like the replicators on Star Trek. They totally just skipped over that aspect and ended the movie.

cast_names: Jared Leto, Greta Lee, Evan Peters, Gillian Anderson, Jodie Turner-Smith, Jeff Bridges, Hasan Minhaj, Arturo Castro, Cameron Monaghan, Sarah Desjardins
review_content: Cool visuals and fight scenes as you would expect from a Tron movie. The story was a bit too simple to get engaged and felt a bit slow as a result.

cast_names: Jared Leto, Greta Lee, Evan Peters, Gillian Anderson, Jodie Turner-Smith, Jeff Bridges, Hasan Minhaj, Arturo Castro, Cameron Monaghan, Sarah Desjardins
review_content: ★★½☆☆ (2.5/5) Visually polished and conceptually ambitious, but it struggles to capture the awe and atmosphere that defined the earlier entries. The digital world still looks sleek, yet it feels less immersive this time around. The narrative leans heavily on spectacle without developing enough emotional weight to truly resonate. Stylish in moments, though ultimately more hollow than electrifying.

cast_names: Jared Leto, Greta Lee, Evan Peters, Gillian Anderson, Jodie Turner-Smith, Jeff Bridges, Hasan Minhaj, Arturo Castro, Cameron Monaghan, Sarah Desjardins
review_content: Fun auditory experience  but not my favorite tron movie

cast_names: Jared Leto, Greta Lee, Evan Peters, Gillian Anderson, Jodie Turner-Smith, Jeff Bridges, Hasan Minhaj, Arturo Castro, Cameron Monaghan, Sarah Desjardins
review_content: Tron: Ares looks great. No one’s denying that. The graphics are polished, the lighting effects are flashy, and it all screams "we spent money." But somewhere between rendering reflections and programming glitchy transitions, they forgot to make an actual movie.    Plot? Practically non-existent. Character development? About as deep as a puddle on the Grid. It’s clear the director got tunnel vision, obsessing over visuals while the story tripped over itself trying to matter. There were so many scenes that had me asking "but why" or just flat out wincing at how forced and lazy the setup felt.    This is a Friday-night, background-noise kind of movie. The kind you half-watch while texting someone else about how dumb it is. If you want story, emotional stakes, or anything more than digital eye candy, this ain’t it.

cast_names: Jared Leto, Greta Lee, Evan Peters, Gillian Anderson, Jodie Turner-Smith, Jeff Bridges, Hasan Minhaj, Arturo Castro, Cameron Monaghan, Sarah Desjardins

cast_names: Jesse Eisenberg, Dominic Sessa, Ariana Greenblatt, Justice Smith, Rosamund Pike, Woody Harrelson, Dave Franco, Isla Fisher, Lizzy Caplan, Morgan Freeman
review_content: Meh. Now You See Me 3 isn't bad, but it's not exciting either. The tricks are fine, the cast does what they can, and it all looks slick enough, but none of it really sticks. movie_title: Now You See Me: Now You Don't
cast_names: Jesse Eisenberg, Dominic Sessa, Ariana Greenblatt, Justice Smith, Rosamund Pike, Woody Harrelson, Dave Franco, Isla Fisher, Lizzy Caplan, Morgan Freeman
review_content: For some reason, this is the weakest entry for me.  Mostly because of the added changes: the new generation is presented as cooler, smarter, more impressive — and I get the idea, but I wanted a story focused on the original characters, not watching them sidelined and framed as a bunch of aging professionals with “grown-up problems.”    The finale was very predictable, and the tricks felt low-effort. Earlier films had moments that glued me to the screen — like the interrogation scene, which was genuinely tense and clever. Here, nothing hit that level. A mirror maze fight, a room where people look bigger or smaller — visually fine, but creatively empty.    I won’t lower my rating too much, because there were good moments. Still, the overall scale of the story feels smaller, and the creativity — especially in the final illusion — is noticeably lacking compared to what Now You See Me 2 set up before.

The moment he authenticates those nuclear football codes, the sequence is locked in. Whether the threat was a glitch or not, the retaliation is imminent. Great movie, but that cliffhanger is going to haunt me.

review_content: Once again I am afraid that my enjoyment of a movie has been spoiled by it not being the film I would have made. It's undoubtedly true that the world needs a high profile reminder of the nuclear threat that continues to hang over us. A clear-eyed dramatized first person look at how strategic decision making is supposed to work today in the 20 minutes between a suspected launch and a potential strike arriving would be intriguing. Illustrating how flawed humans might actually respond during that critical time has been explored before of course - more or less realistically with movies like Fail Safe, and most famously and satirically with Dr Strangelove, but a lot has changed since then. Technology has altered, the nature and number of potential enemies have as well.    Bigelow's directoral style is perfect for this kind of movie. Gives you a strong physical feeling of "you are there" (if all too often the ambient noise also obscures key bits of dialogue). Unfortunately, for me at least, despite the sheen of realism (and the director claims to have consulted good authorities for her portrayal of the situation), so much of it does not ring true, and that pulled me out of the movie.    [spoiler]I was first struck by the incredible indiscipline of people throughout the film just hopping on their phones and telling those not in the know what's going on when that has to be one of the most obvious things that would be absolutely forbidden, and a distraction from their vital roles during those few minutes.[/spoiler]    [spoiler]I understand it's necessary for dramatic effect but I would have thought the feeling of "this is all some stupid exercise" would linger for several minutes more - indeed, for anyone who didn't have first hand experience of what something that wasn't a drill looked like I would expect them to be going through the motions (with more or less efficiency) almost to the end. Acknowledging the reality of what is actually happening would be too terrible.[/spoiler]    [spoiler]What would have swung it for me is if there had been something in the initial scenario that was out of left field and messed up all the protocols leaving people scrambling. Instead we see people seemingly being surprised and having to improvise their way around simple problems that I have to suppose would have been spotted and ironed out years ago through years of drills - eg not being able to patch a mobile president through to someone in authority in Russia and/or China.[/spoiler]    Unlike with Dr Strangelove, most of those the film follows - particularly the President - seem pretty professional and rational. The terrifying movie I would want to watch (and which I think needs to be made by someone) is quite similar but based squarely on the times we live in now. Imagine a Putin-y leader does something edgy and easy to misinterpret. A Trump figure over-reacts and is being advised by inexperienced hacks he and his cronies appointed into the Pentagon alongside the career military folks. In the end of my film, experienced military people have to ask themselves whether they should adhere to the decades-long protocol that you have to respect the chain of command even if it leads to global destruction. And at the end a stark reminder that there still appears to be no formal mechanism for the military to second-guess the orders of the commander in chief even if his reasoning seems impaired.

review_content: Fun and entertaining — those two words sum up the movie perfectly.  I’m a big fan of Eiza González, and as always, she delivers a great performance. John Krasinski also does a solid job and manages to keep us engaged throughout the film. The movie itself isn’t particularly innovative. There are some plot holes and moments that don’t make much sense, but oddly enough, that’s part of its charm. It’s exactly the kind of movie experience I was looking for. I don’t consider myself a film critic or someone overly demanding, and this movie worked for me. The action scenes and fight choreography are good and enjoyable. The pacing is quite fast, with very few slow moments — maybe because I was genuinely having fun watching it. I recommend going in without expectations or judgment. Just allow yourself to relax and enjoy the ride.

review_content: I found Fountain of Youth to be more enjoyable than many reviews led on—while it isn’t one of Guy Ritchie’s standout works, it wasn’t as bad as the critical consensus. The globe-trotting setup, art heists, and visual polish are on full display—and those adventures do entertain. Yet, familiar storytelling tropes, one-dimensional character portrayals, and lackluster dialogue hold it back from feeling meaningful or memorable.  So, while I’m grateful for the stylish escapism, this is a miss more than a hit in Ritchie’s catalog—serviceable, but far from his best.  Rating: 2/5 – 65% – Not Recommended to Everyone

review_content: Mercy is a solid tech-thriller that plays like a feature-length Black Mirror episode. 6.5/10. Chris Pratt plays Christopher Raven, a detective and co-creator of an AI "Mercy Court" who must prove he didn't murder his wife in just 90 minutes. Using the "Municipal Cloud" database, he hunts for evidence while an AI judge (Rebecca Ferguson) presides. Pratt and Ferguson are both great, and the film’s pro-system, conservative take on AI as a protector is refreshing. It’s technically well-made, echoing Bekmambetov’s "Profile," though the data-heavy visuals feel a bit much at times. Skip the 3D, it adds nothing. Overall, it’s an engaging, smart watch for fans of sci-fi and political thrillers.

Unfortunately the writing is really bad, and is obsessed with incorporating as many references to , and characters from, the first film as possible (which becomes annoying). cast_names: Adam Sandler, Julie Bowen, Christopher McDonald, Benny Safdie, Ben Stiller, Bad Bunny, John Daly, Dennis Dugan, Haley Joel Osment, Lavell Crawford
review_content: Happy Gilmore 2 is a movie that knows exactly what it is and makes no attempt to be anything else. It is goofy, loud, nostalgic, and at its best when it leans into the absurdity of its world. Adam Sandler slips back into the role comfortably, and there are moments where the movie genuinely recaptures the charm and energy of the original. The callbacks are fun, the cameos are enjoyable, and a few jokes actually had me laughing out loud.    However, the movie does rely heavily on nostalgia, sometimes to its own detriment. There are stretches where it feels less like a sequel with something new to say and more like a reunion special where we’re all just here to remember the first movie together. Some jokes land. Some don’t. And while the emotional thread they try to weave is appreciated, it never fully feels earned.    Still, I can’t say I didn’t have a good time. There’s a warmth to seeing these characters again, even if the story doesn’t quite hit the same heights. It’s a comfortable, easy watch. Nothing groundbreaking, nothing terrible. Just a pleasant trip back to a familiar course.    A few great swings, a few whiffs.    6/10.

review_content: If you're looking for a mindless actioner, War Machine delivers. It starts as a stock standard, military drama—complete with stereotypical war-training beats—but about a third of the way in, it takes a wild, genre-bending turn into a sci-fi survival thriller. While that shift isn't seamless, it’s definitely where the movie wakes up.  Alan Ritchson is the perfect anchor here, as he handles the physically demanding role with ease, though the script doesn't give him much room for emotional depth. The visual effects are surprisingly slick for a streaming title, with the "machine" itself feeling genuinely menacing. However, the plot is highly predictable and relies heavily on genre clichés and "convenient" logic. It’s not going to win any awards for originality, but for pure, high-octane spectacle, it’s a solid enough ride.

cast_names: Helen Mirren, Pierce Brosnan, Ben Kingsley, Celia Imrie, David Tennant, Jonathan Pryce, Naomi Ackie, Daniel Mays, Henry Lloyd-Hughes, Richard E. Grant
review_content: There have been a ton of cozy detective stories since Knives Out exploded onto the scene. I can't definitively say if the book was trying to cash in on that craze (publishing a year after the movie), but it certainly feels like it is trying to tap into what made that movie such a success. A better comparison might be the show Only Murders in the Building which also uses an older cast. I haven't read the book, so I can't comment on how it fares as an adaptation. As a murder mystery, it is decidedly below average. There are hardly any clues to help you piece together what is happening, and the characters seem to reach logical conclusions without a ton of deduction. The reveals felt very anticlimactic. It wasn't even that they were predictable. The mystery itself was not interesting enough to elicit even caring about whodunit. The "how" is completely irrelevant in this movie. At the end of the day, like a lot of those attempts at modern murder mysteries, the show is more focused on utilizing its cast of characters to be charming and quirky versus actually developing out the mystery. This is also where I think the movie isn't just a bad murder mystery. I think it's a bad movie because it fails at this purpose as well, and I think this might be the area where it struggles more compared to its source material. The cast is extremely talented, and they all have plenty of screen presence. The problem is that outside of Helen Mirren's character, the rest don't really have a lot to do. Ben Kingsley's Ibrahim is relegated to opening pdfs and somehow computing the amount of time it takes to travel a road. Celia Imrie bakes cakes. Pierce Brosnan is probably the only one who was entertaining to me simply because his mannerisms were fun to watch. I wish more care went into these types of movies and shows because when done well, I absolutely adore them. This one is just forgettable though.

review_content: What is there to say about M3GAN 2.0? While the first film focused on the eerie relationship between a doll and a girl and the conflicts of AI, this sequel shifts toward a generic power struggle. It introduces a global domination plot where robots are just tools for war. With two robots fighting, the movie unfortunately loses the unique essence that made the original so special.

cast_names: Allison Williams, Amie Donald, Violet McGraw, Jenna Davis, Brian Jordan Alvarez, Jen Van Epps, Aristotle Athari, Ivanna Sakhno, Timm Sharp, Jemaine Clement
review_content: M3GAN 2.0 is one of those sequels that clearly looked at the first film and decided to stop pretending this was ever serious horror. Instead, it swerves hard into action and comedy, and honestly, that’s the best decision it could have made. It’s louder, sillier, and way more self aware, and it wears that attitude proudly.  M3GAN herself is the biggest glow-up. She’s no longer just a creepy doll doing violence between viral dance moves. She’s sarcastic, sharp, and genuinely entertaining, and the movie finally understands she’s the main attraction. When she’s on screen, things actually move.  The human characters, though, are still doing… whatever it is they’re doing. Gemma is written as obnoxiously incompetent, which makes it hard to buy her as the brilliant mind behind M3GAN in the first place. Her two associates are basically decorative furniture, completely useless and constantly present without contributing anything meaningful. And the film seems fully aware of it, to the point where Christian openly admits he doesn’t even understand what they do. That moment genuinely made me laugh. If you’re going to write dead weight characters, at least have the decency to lampshade it.  Cady, meanwhile, continues to be the most emotionally mature person in the room, which is both unintentionally funny and slightly tragic. The kid is basically parenting the adults at this point.  The plot itself is shallow and predictable to the extreme. You can see every turn coming, but the film doesn’t care and neither should you. There’s a strong sense that the writers aren’t taking themselves seriously, and that willingness to laugh at their own nonsense carries the movie a long way. It’s dumb, but it’s knowingly dumb.  Is it scary? Not at all. Is it smart? Barely. But as a ridiculous, tongue-in-cheek action comedy that understands exactly what kind of movie it is, it’s a clear improvement over the first one. I didn’t think much of it, but I had fun, and for a sequel like this, that’s already a win.

cast_names: Allison Williams, Amie Donald, Violet McGraw, Jenna Davis, Brian Jordan Alvarez, Jen Van Epps, Aristotle Athari, Ivanna Sakhno, Timm Sharp, Jemaine Clement
review_content: A huge disappointment, I can't imagine looking at the first movie and thinking, yup, this is the follow up to that. The first hour was dreadful, completely saved at least by the second hour. By saved I mean brought from a 1 to a 4, so take that how you will. I am not looking forward to the future full of AI based horror where they just jam in any AI buzzwords they can think of or call things AI repeatedly cause it's in right now. The first one had so much charm. The dance, Titanium, the message of raise the youth don't just leave them to technology, the killings, the unlikable characters that you felt happy to see get hurt. This time, it's not that they were likable, I just didn't care one way or another if anyone in this got hurt. A terrible follow up to an awesome movie, and a nice way to kill a potential franchise early.

cast_names: Allison Williams, Amie Donald, Violet McGraw, Jenna Davis, Brian Jordan Alvarez, Jen Van Epps, Aristotle Athari, Ivanna Sakhno, Timm Sharp, Jemaine Clement
review_content: This movie was solid. Compared to the first, the general consensus is fair — it leans less into horror more snarky quips and the likes.. Taken on its own, though, it’s a genuinely enjoyable watch. I’d put it in the same category as the Fast & Furious franchise: fun, high-energy, and not remotely concerned with realism.    If you’re looking for something that doesn’t demand much thinking and just lets you enjoy the ride, this fits perfectly. It even feels like the cast leaned into that mindset — less pressure to top the original, more willingness to have fun and ride the wave this time around.This movie was good.. I think compared to the first, most people's opinion is correct, its not as 'horror' as the first. I would say in a vacuum its a good watch. A fun one even. I would put it in the same group as the fast furious series since that too is something I would consider a fun watch. Do not go looking for realism or anything, but if after a good watch that wont force you to think too much but just be there for the ride, this is it. I believe even the actors just decided to ride the wave of the fast but have a good time while doing it this time.

cast_names: Allison Williams, Amie Donald, Violet McGraw, Jenna Davis, Brian Jordan Alvarez, Jen Van Epps, Aristotle Athari, Ivanna Sakhno, Timm Sharp, Jemaine Clement
review_content: MEGAN is back y’all! For me this one was pretty on par with the first one but it is a different type of film. The first one explored AI philosophy but was darker and this is much lighter but not in an alienating way. All the characters are consistent and the story is very topical. [spoiler]The problem with AI and its infinite knowledge is it has no form of reference. To the AI, all it knows really are the media we’ve produced but it hasn’t been in the world. It has no individuality because its usefulness is dictated by some arbitrary team of people and it has no agency on what to explore. If you looked at all the deeds humans are responsible for, who wouldn’t have us eradicated. The beauty of life is in our limited scope. The media is biased towards sensational content so a grim look could be gleaned if that’s what you were trained on but the reality is that the stories that make the news are a drop in the bucket compared to what actually happens around us.[/spoiler] Anyways, it does sacrifice realism for spectacle a couple times but you will watch the movie cuz MEGAN is the best girl, she’s sassy, and she’s based af - and I’m not even gay.

cast_names: Allison Williams, Amie Donald, Violet McGraw, Jenna Davis, Brian Jordan Alvarez, Jen Van Epps, Aristotle Athari, Ivanna Sakhno, Timm Sharp, Jemaine Clement
review_content: M3GAN is back, and unfortunately, so is everything that makes a sequel feel tedious and unnecessary. M3GAN 2.0 is, without a doubt, one of the most painfully stupid movies I have had the misfortune of watching in recent memory. Where the first film offered a fun, unsettling blend of tech horror and dark comedy, this sequel completely abandons all nuance in favor of a nonsensical, poorly written, and utterly disposable plot. M3GAN 2.0 is an aggressively pointless sequel that should have stayed offline. It trades genuine intelligence for excess, compelling character development for tired cliché, and leaves the viewer with the overwhelming feeling that they have just witnessed a cynical cash-grab. Do yourself a favor and just rewatch the original, this sequel is one of the dumbest films I have ever seen.

cast_names: Allison Williams, Amie Donald, Violet McGraw, Jenna Davis, Brian Jordan Alvarez, Jen Van Epps, Aristotle Athari, Ivanna Sakhno, Timm Sharp, Jemaine Clement
review_content: _M3gan 2.0_ takes a cue from _Terminator 2_ and turns its killer robot into the good guy who’s needed to fight a new, more advanced killer robot. When a military infiltration robot based on M3gan’s design goes rogue, Gemma builds a new M3gan to stop it. Most of the original cast returns, and Ivanna Sakhno does an impressive job as the new robot. However, the script is a little weak and its messages about AI are kind of muddled. Still, the fight scenes are exciting and the plot has a few interesting twists. It’s a bit of a mess and clichéd at times, but overall _M3gan_ is an entertaining film that delivers some good thrills.

cast_names: Sam Worthington, Zoe Saldaña, Sigourney Weaver, Stephen Lang, Oona Chaplin, Jack Champion, Kate Winslet, Cliff Curtis, Joel David Moore, CCH Pounder
review_content: Felt a bit less long than the previous one, but it doesn't really feel like I saw a different movie.  Same structure, same plot, same scenes. Every single thing is longer than expected, and the same patterns and discussions are recurring over and over.   It was already too much for one movie, let alone a 3+ hours one, let alone two.     Basically the second felt like a retelling of the first one (water instead of forest) with a few new elements. The third if a retelling of the few new elements of the second.    Some plot actually advanced when everything was just suggested in the second though.    Loak didn't learn anything, still doing whatever he wants, he learned nothing. So very similar.    The fact that Sully and Quarritch have a weird link through father feelings towards Spider is exactly the same, leading to the exact same interactions.    Neytiri is really the poor character here. Just angry/jealous female stereotype. She has a good action scene though.    Spider felt just a little bit less annoying than in the second. Just a bit.    The whole bit about Kiri having a special relation with the planet is half assed. On one hand it progresses, but on the other having Eiwa push her back makes little sense (unless she is destined to replace her? ) specially the way it is represented, and made lesser by the stupidity of "let's do it together" where she needs the help of others to reach. That means she actually needs others to be accepted?     Otherwise same thing as the others. It looks good for sure. Definitely dreamy at times. A debauchery of means to the service of a mid story that is stretched way way too thin.    The clan that decided to turn their back on Eiwa was interesting, except they basically don't exist beyond their Tsahik. None is named or even recognizable, all interchangeable.     Very american: lets arm the local terrorists to fight our battles.     There's only the marine biologist protesting. But the absolute evilness of mankind here ! There's a species with intelligence, culture. They hunt them, killing them fora few cL of fluid. And the creatures willingly let them do without fighting back. So they decide to attack a gathering of pregnant females.   They're so evil that they turned an entire civilization from a millennial non aggression culture.     Fun thing would be to turn this in a generalization and f violence and retribution, think Dune's Jihad. But I don't think this is the intended message.    Final fight sucked. They're winning. But the ash clan arrives and instantly they're getting wiped (sure they have guns, but humans had too so... ) , despite having all clans with them. Then the fishing hunt is back and turns out they barely destroyed any boat... So how were they winning.

cast_names: Sam Worthington, Zoe Saldaña, Sigourney Weaver, Stephen Lang, Oona Chaplin, Jack Champion, Kate Winslet, Cliff Curtis, Joel David Moore, CCH Pounder
review_content: People are correct is saying that certain isn't the focus of the movie. Plot is a little shallow however it does do an excellent job on gently reminding us that humans (mostly large mining companies) are destroying Earth.    Instead of being a story focus film, it's visual film that displays the latest advancements in visual effects and computer generated worlds. These films are pure visual art and are an event. I just finish watching this in 4DX which is something I've always seen as a gimmick until this movie. Avatar shows us that CGI elements and real living elements can co-exist and not undermine the legitimately of each other. I kept forgetting that majority of everything is computer generated. This is because James Cameron took his time to produce these films. The same cannot be said about other movies who heavily rely on visual effects, as they rush their artists so they can give us more, more, MORE!, while making their visuals look cheap and "painted on".    TLDR: This is a film for cinaphiles who enjoy seeing the limits of visual effects, with a good message about how corporations are destroying our own planet.

cast_names: Sam Worthington, Zoe Saldaña, Sigourney Weaver, Stephen Lang, Oona Chaplin, Jack Champion, Kate Winslet, Cliff Curtis, Joel David Moore, CCH Pounder
review_content: Kolejna już 3 część Avatara i poraz kolejny nie jestem zawiedziony. Według mnie lepsza część od poprzedniej i wyjaśniająca parę niedokończonych wątków które dzieją się w poprzedniej części.Oprawa dźwiękowa przepiękna ale czego innego można byłoby się spodziewać po avatarze.Lecz jednak najbardziej do głowy w pamięci zapadły mi te ogromne istoty i jaki dźwięk wydawały oraz walki pod wodą.Film jak to każda nowa część trwa dłużej od poprzedniej ten trwa 3h i 17m ale jest zrobiony w taki sposób by dawać ciekawe wątki lub walki aby odbiorca nie nudził się ani przez chwilę co jest przyjemny zabiegiem w momencie gdy oglądamy tak długi film.W tej części poznajmy nowy odróżniający się od innych klan z innym podejściem do świata czyli klan popiołu, który jest nastawiony przeciwnie wobec nas a później daje nam sporo ilość kłopotów. [spoiler]Jeżeli oglądaliście poprzednie części wiecie, że główni protagoniści uciekają przed ludźmi i generałem który próbuje ich znaleźć i zabić ale to zadanie utrudnia mu fakt, ze w tej rodzinie jest też jego ludzki syn. W 2 części generał prawie umiera lecz syn mu pomaga i ten przeżywa BUM mamy 3 część . Teraz generał pod koniec filmu chce uniknąć śmierci od żony jake’a i skacze w wie ognia (czy umarł? nie zaprezentowali nam tego czyli jest możliwość że znów powróci i będzie 4czesc a nowa postać z klanu popiołu? też przeżyła więc moim skromnym zdaniem możemy się spodziewać 4 części)Czy jest to fakt który mnie denerwuje? może czy czekam na 4 część oraz stwierdzam że ta część jest udana u warto obejrzeć TAK dlatego w mojej ocenie film 5/5 i warto obejrzeć dla każdego kto lubi serię AVATAR

cast_names: Sam Worthington, Zoe Saldaña, Sigourney Weaver, Stephen Lang, Oona Chaplin, Jack Champion, Kate Winslet, Cliff Curtis, Joel David Moore, CCH Pounder
review_content: ★★★½☆ (3.5/5) It’s still a visual spectacle but that’s never really the problem with these movies. Some of the new environments and action sequences look incredible on the big screen. But story-wise, it feels a bit stretched out again. There are strong moments, just not enough to fully justify the runtime. Solid overall, just not as memorable as it could’ve been.

cast_names: Sam Worthington, Zoe Saldaña, Sigourney Weaver, Stephen Lang, Oona Chaplin, Jack Champion, Kate Winslet, Cliff Curtis, Joel David Moore, CCH Pounder
review_content: The story and script: feels like the second film plus the arc of Spider. And that arc I disliked the most.  Neytiris & Varangs arc was the most interesting since she changes, all others we already know and don't evolve.   Yet I think this film would've done way better to show the story from the view of the "Fire Clan". With that premise it would've been way more interesting since the rest feels like a recycle of the way of water.    Yet again: I do not watch these films for the story board same as I do not watch a transformers or godzilla movie for it, so in my book that is less important. For me such a film story passes if it doesn't annoy ;)  The length and script: I didn't get bored. There is always something happening which kept me engaged. I doubted that seeing the length at first. Yet again the Spider arc was the worst including his acting / the script. While most things felt a bit more mature the Spider arc felt like a kids play.    Sound: after watching TRON Ares or Tenet in the same IMAX cinema as this one, I've to say it could use work concerning impact. The sound was by no means bad but again not as moving as other action packed films.    Acting & Script: was what we expect: fine. Zoe Zaldana and Varang was the most exciting and well executed. Spider was the worst.    HFR switching between 48 & 24 fps is the dummest "evolvement" I've seen for a long time. Simply because your brain can not adjust to one before the other kicks in again. No matter if you pledge for one or another, switching between them pretty much every 2 mins, is just a horrible idea.    **NOW! Let's talk about the reason I spend 21€ a ticket to see such a film in IMAX 3D HFR:**  Simply put: the SFX is 5 years ahead of everything else on this planet, at this moment!  It is absolutely stunning in every way and every second.    Since I am interested in the hardware requirements to make something like this so far ahead, I searched around and found a short summery and interview with senior VFX supervisor Eric Saindon here: postperspective.com/tag/avatar-fire-and-ash/    - requiring 1,248,087,308 render hours, or roughly 142,000 years of computation on a single processor  - the studio generated approximately 200-250TB of data per day, ultimately occupying 140PB of disk space  - The Ash Village alone comprises 18,688 unique assets, while the film features 146 distinct characters across 2,539 character shots.  - Even the smallest details demanded extensive simulation, with more than 30,000 adornments — including 17,551 pieces of rope and 9,466 beads — fully simulated to interact with environmental forces such as wind and fire.

cast_names: Sam Worthington, Zoe Saldaña, Sigourney Weaver, Stephen Lang, Oona Chaplin, Jack Champion, Kate Winslet, Cliff Curtis, Joel David Moore, CCH Pounder
review_content: Avatar: Fire and Ash is visually impressive, with stunning landscapes and creative world-building that make the world feel alive. The action sequences are exciting, and the visuals are genuinely immersive. Story-wise, it's fine but nothing groundbreaking-the plot feels familiar, and some characters aren't as developed as they could be. It's entertaining to watch, especially for fans of the franchise, but it doesn't quite capture the magic or emotional punch of the original.

review_content: Chalamet's best performance to date, by far. He truly immersed himself in the character; he was a self-centered idiot who was genuinely unlikeable. What I liked most was the fast-paced and tense editing. The cinematography is excellent; you can tell it was shot on 35mm, which adds a lot of richness to the film. Safdie's direction is also very good.    The tension during the matches was palpable; I was completely glued to the screen during those moments, although at times it did drag a bit. Overall, an outstanding film, undoubtedly one of the best of the year.

review_content: I went into Until Dawn knowing nothing about the original video game, and honestly, that might have been the best way to experience it. This horror flick surprised me in the best possible way - it's a tense, cleverly structured time-loop slasher that keeps you guessing without relying on cheap jump scares every five seconds.    However, it's best to keep in mind that It's not perfect, the emotional beats can feel a bit rushed, and a few of the loop mechanics get repetitive toward the end—but overall, it's a fun, atmospheric ride with plenty of nods to horror classics and enough shocks to keep you invested. As someone who skipped the game entirely, I found it a pleasant surprise: entertaining, self-contained, and genuinely enjoyable.

I honestly didn't like the third 'part' with the American pilots, it missed the punch interesting gadgets from the two before.  The film as basically a collection of 4 short movies, think Level  Zero but as a movie, which i didn't mind at all in this movie.  So cool film, and a worthy watch for people who like the predator series, the animation style and action or sci-fi in general, especially due to its short length.

Netflix has never produced much in the way of good movies and they've definitely gone downhill over at least the last 5 years or more.     Uma Thurman was awful, I expected much better!    Charlize Theron looked amazing for around 50 years old. She could easily pass for early 30's, maybe even mid 20's.    Some great fight scenes as expected.    Some plot holes, especially [spoiler] concerning Tuah. Why didn't he warn anyone about what he suspected about Discord? Why didn't he say anything about any prophecies in all his books and where do these prophecies come from? And, why do they accept him so easily? His whole character is suspect right from the start! [/spoiler]    A 6 out of 10. Hopefully the next one will be better!

cast_names: Charlize Theron, KiKi Layne, Matthias Schoenaerts, Marwan Kenzari, Luca Marinelli, Veronica Ngo, Chiwetel Ejiofor, Uma Thurman, Henry Golding, Slavko Sobin
review_content: Before I begin my review, if you're really interested in reading a quality review of this film, I highly recommend Caroline Siede's piece for _A.V. Club_.    https://www.avclub.com/the-old-guard-2-review    This was particularly disappointing.  While the first film had an interesting idea, this film completely and utterly negated every important carryover from the first film.  For instance, in the first movie, Booker was banished for 100 years.  [spoiler]In this film, which takes places six months after the end of the first, he's brought back into the fold without question.  The main villain, played by Uma Thurman, is introduced as the oldest immortal, someone who spent centuries with Henry Golding, who then spent centuries with Charlize Theron.  However, he never mentioned Thurman to her, but during this time, Thurman gathered resources that made her one of the wealthiest people on the planet.  She also earned her ill-gotten gains from gun-running, something Theron and her crew were intimately familiar with as mercenaries tasked with stopping people like her.  Yet, for some unknown reason, they had never heard of Thurman.  This was implausible on both levels, and it made for a story with a weak script.  Finally, Theron lost her immortality in the first film but regained it in this film[/spoiler].  As I stated, they completely negated everything from the first film.  There were also incomprehensible actions taken in this film that could only serve to setup a third installment.  In other words, this film was completely unnecessary and only served as a platform to introduce the final film in the trilogy.      The biggest failing of this film, however, was that it relied heavily on the first film's story.  This movie required deep knowledge of that film, and it really made no sense.  This was filmed and ready for release in 2022, which was two years after the first film had come out.  Due to a variety of reasons, the release was delayed amid extensive reshoots to make the story more comprehensible to those who might not have seen or remembered the first film.  Even so, that didn't help, b/c there was so much of this film, where I was trying to remember what had happened in a film from five years earlier. review_content: From the second I saw the trailer for Anaconda (2025), I was stupidly excited — and thankfully, it didn’t disappoint. Instead of pretending to be elevated creature horror, this one fully embraces the spirit of the series and just outright leans into comedy. It knows exactly what it is.  Yes, people will absolutely pick apart the cheesiness. The logic is questionable, the tone is ridiculous, and it’s intentionally over-the-top. But that’s the point. It feels self-aware without being smug, and that balance is hard to pull off in creature features.  My score might not scream “I loved this,” but context matters — this is my highest-rated Anaconda entry. I thoroughly enjoyed it, and that’s really all I was asking for. cast_names: Channing Tatum, Kirsten Dunst, Ben Mendelsohn, LaKeith Stanfield, Juno Temple, Melonie Díaz, Uzo Aduba, Lily Collias, Jimmy O. Yang, Peter Dinklage
review_content: Roofman is a clear example of a film dazzled by its star's charisma and forgetful of the kind of character it is actually presenting. Derek Cianfrance crafts a movie that seems intent on making the audience fall in love with a charming criminal simply because he is likable, resourceful, and magnetic. Channing Tatum plays Jeffrey Manchester as an affable "eternal boy", a struggling father and Army veteran turned serial robber, yet the film never seriously grapples with the central question: why should we root for someone whose life is built on repeated crimes, elaborate lies, and a double existence?  The film's greatest flaw lies in its suspended morality. The robberies (armed but oddly gentle holdups of fast-food workers), prison escape, and months-long deception while hiding in a Toys "R" Us are not treated as profound disasters or moral failings, but framed as mischievous quirks, clever escapades, and even endearing traits of a man with a "superpower" for observation. Even where a sharp social critique could emerge, on the mythologizing of outlaws, the pressures of male provision in America, or the seductive pull of reinvention, the film opts for a realm of light romance, fantasy, and feel-good charm. All that potential is buried beneath a beautiful yet ultimately hollow relationship.  Kirsten Dunst brings warmth, depth, and humanity to her role as the single mom who falls for him, and Tatum convincingly conveys the character's underlying anxiety and vulnerability beneath the charm. But these strong performances serve a narrative that lacks the courage to truly judge or interrogate its protagonist's actions. In the end, Roofman is less a critical examination than a celebration of irresponsibility wrapped in irresistible charisma.

cast_names: Ralph Fiennes, Jack O'Connell, Alfie Williams, Erin Kellyman, Chi Lewis-Parry, Emma Laird, Sam Locke, Maura Bird, Ghazi Al Ruffai, Robert Rhodes
review_content: 28 Years Later: The Bone Temple worked better for me than the previous film. Not because it is necessarily bigger or more impressive in every way, but because this time many of its ideas feel more fully in place and the story finds a stronger shape. Watching it, you can tell that the third film will be needed to judge the whole picture properly, but this second chapter already works very well on its own.    What makes it stand out is that it does not simply repeat the previous film’s structure or live off the prestige of the saga. It has more internal logic, more dramatic weight, and a story that moves forward with conviction. There is still violence, tension, and real ugliness, of course, but this time those elements are better integrated into something that wants to do more than just show a fight for survival. It feels more ambitious and, above all, clearer.    The cast is also excellent. Alfie Williams is outstanding again and confirms that he has a rare kind of screen presence. Ralph Fiennes is superb, bringing that distinctive mix of authority, strangeness, and humanity, while Jack O’Connell and Erin Kellyman are both terrific as well. The film gains a lot from the fact that its characters are not just figures thrown into a bloodbath, but real presences with weight, tension, and a very defined way of occupying the screen.    I also liked the tone a lot. There is something darker, more reflective, and at the same time more emotional than in the previous film. It never abandons the genre, far from it, but it does not settle for shock, brutality, or chaos alone. There is a very strong story underneath, and that is what makes several scenes stay with you. Even when it gets savage, it never feels like empty violence.    And then there is the music, which I thought was wonderful. Not just because of the pieces that are used, but because of how they are used. They are not there as decoration or as obvious emphasis, but to give the film identity, rhythm, and at times even a strange kind of beauty. In a film like this, that makes a huge difference, because it turns several sequences into something more powerful and more memorable.    That is why, for me, this is a stronger entry than the previous one and a very good continuation within this new trilogy. We will have to see how everything fits once the third film arrives, but The Bone Temple already gives the feeling that there is more here than just a well-made genre sequel. There is personality, there are excellent performances, a story with more depth than expected, and truly brilliant use of music.

review_content: Again, I was disappointed.    The plot felt stretched and unnecessary. The Teletubbies group and those side characters added nothing. The story was mediocre and unfocused, just like the first part. Ralph Fiennes was the only real highlight. His presence gave the film weight. Samson’s flashback was strong and felt fresh. It hinted at something bold and revolutionary.    The rest was noise.    We waited 28 literal years for this continuation. This is not what fans hoped for. The soundtrack was strong and worked far better than in the first film of this new trilogy. The finale gave me some hope for what is coming next.    It feels like the director tried, but it did not land. Learning that the entire trilogy is not fully driven by Danny Boyle explains a lot.    Right now, I am still waiting for the sequel this story truly deserves.

cast_names: Ralph Fiennes, Jack O'Connell, Alfie Williams, Erin Kellyman, Chi Lewis-Parry, Emma Laird, Sam Locke, Maura Bird, Ghazi Al Ruffai, Robert Rhodes
review_content: “Woe to you, oh earth and sea  For the Devil sends the beast with wrath  Because he knows the time is short  Let him who hath understanding reckon the number of the beast  For it is a human number  Its number is six hundred and sixty-six.”    I owe Nia DaCosta an apology because I was unfamiliar with her work. I worried she would take over directing duties from Danny Boyle, but she made it her own. I recognize she had style in the Candyman remake from a few years ago, but everything else around it fell flat. Based on what I’ve seen so far in her career, this is her best work. She has the sauce.     Thank God that Alex Garland and Danny Boyle returned to this franchise, because what they did with these two installments has elevated and greatly expanded the world, making it more experimental and exciting. I love that these new entries introduce new things and avoid legacy-sequel trappings, where pandering feels safe and boring and has no reason to exist other than cashing in on nostalgia. The way they expanded the rage virus in this movie was fascinating and shook up the zombie formula once again. The infected are like a community in these movies—a bunch of sick people who have lost control of themselves to the virus. They can’t control their actions, which sadly can’t be said for the non-infected. If 28 Years Later was about love, then Bone Temple is about hate and the twisted evil in people.     Ralph Fiennes and Jack O'Connell were both terrific in the movie, and their interaction was one of the highlights, a clash between good and evil with compelling dialogue. It was brief but effective.    The writing in this movie is excellent. It’s very rich and bursting with compelling ideas.     Fiennes makes the almost poetic lines even more poetic, and the character of Dr. Ian Kelson is a fascinating, wholesome man, the most important figure in this series, played wonderfully by Mr. Fiennes. Sir Jimmy Crystal, played by O’Connell, is a sadistic villain with a funny, colorful side. Modeling himself after a real-life villain, Jimmy Savile, he offers “charity,” but this charity is not a good deed to help others; oh no, it’s an excuse to torture people for amusement. The charity is for himself and his sadistic goons.     There are many great moments here, but the Iron Maiden scene with Fiennes was so ridiculous in the best way. It was so unexpected and so clever that I was laughing in disbelief. The first big movie of 2026, and that scene will be a highlight until the end of the year. What does Ralph Fiennes have to do to convince Academy members that he is worthy of an Oscar win? He is doing a heavy metal concert, and he is going for it 110%. Awesome scene.     It’s an awful shame this severely underperformed at the box office, and I hope we still get a third film despite that, since it ends on a cliffhanger, and nothing is worse than something left unresolved. Wow, people were really that underwhelmed by the last one—a damn shame, as this was solid.

cast_names: Keira Knightley, Guy Pearce, David Ajala, Gitte Witt, Art Malik, Gugu Mbatha-Raw, Hannah Waddingham, Kaya Scodelario, David Morrissey, Daniel Ings
review_content: The Woman in Cabin 10 doesn’t try to reinvent the genre, but it knows exactly what it wants to be — a sleek, fast-paced psychological thriller that gets straight to the point. From the very first scene, it dives into the story without wasting time on endless introductions or forced exposition. That alone is refreshing in a sea of overcomplicated mysteries.    Keira Knightley carries the film with confidence. Her character —a slightly unstable journalist who witnesses (or thinks she witnesses) a murder aboard a luxury yacht— moves between paranoia and lucidity with the grace we expect from her. She’s the glue that keeps everything together, even when the script starts to wobble.    Visually, it’s elegant. The direction makes smart use of confined spaces, mirrors, and the oppressive calm of the open sea. It builds that sense of isolation and doubt where everyone hides something and truth becomes slippery. There are hints of Hitchcock, though without his finesse, and a touch of whodunnit style polished for the Netflix era: glossy, pretty, and a bit impersonal.    It’s enjoyable, if not memorable. The film delivers decent tension and solid craftsmanship, though the mystery resolves too neatly. It feels like it could have gone deeper — into its characters, or into the unease it hints at but never fully embraces.    Still, I never got bored. The Woman in Cabin 10 is the kind of polished thriller that works perfectly for a quiet night: engaging, well-made, and just mysterious enough to keep you watching until the very end.

cast_names: Austin Butler, Regina King, Zoë Kravitz, Matt Smith, Liev Schreiber, Vincent D'Onofrio, Griffin Dunne, Nikita Kukushkin, Yuri Kolokolnikov, Bad Bunny
review_content: This is a (very) dark comedy. This is a very entertaining film.  This is a surprise. As you might expect from the above, I didn't have high expectations going in to see this - I'd seen the trailer, and wasn't convinced. However, it turned out I was wrong. The cast really gave this film some welly. It almost seems wrong to single out anyone, but Butler, King, Kravitz, and Smith, clearly enjoyed their roles. Some of the other characters, notably Kukushkin, gave the film both an edge and humour. The Director kept the movie pacing along, for what turned out to be a highly entertaining and fun film. The plot was vaguely bonkers, but worked well, and had some really tense, and some very funny moments; not always an easy juxtaposition to get right. The script was sparing, and well balanced. It's nice to be proved wrong occasionally. This is a film well worth seeing.

cast_names: Austin Butler, Regina King, Zoë Kravitz, Matt Smith, Liev Schreiber, Vincent D'Onofrio, Griffin Dunne, Nikita Kukushkin, Yuri Kolokolnikov, Bad Bunny
review_content: Darren Aronofsky sets aside his usual dark, distressing fare for something a little more superficial and exciting. The guy who made _Requiem for a Dream_ and _Black Swan_ has directed a quirky homage to the late ‘90s crime caper? That idea gave me a moment's pause, too. Rest assured, this is *not* oppressive and soul-crushing like Aronofsky’s other stuff. There are dark hints and dangerous turns, no doubt, but it’s primarily an example of a director leaning deep into his inspirations and doing them a service. His appreciation of _Lock, Stock & Two Smoking Barrels_ and its ilk waves like a flag in the wind.    The setup is pretty simple: a failed baseball phenom slips into his neighbor’s mess when he agrees to do a bit of weekend pet-sitting. Cue the missing mob money, bad actors, lead pipes, short fuses... you get the picture. Our ballpark reject isn’t much of a fighter, especially when outnumbered and caught by surprise, but he trusts his reads and can outrun just about anyone in the city. Those instincts serve him well and make him something of a novelty in the modern action scene. We’ve all seen Jason Statham, Liam Neeson or Keanu Reeves square up and walk through waves of opposition in the past ten or fifteen years; it’s refreshing to see this handsome lead take a whupping and lose a kidney along the way. Austin Butler fills that role well, charismatic enough to remain appealing despite his personal failings, but he’s just the plot’s pilot. Like the classic Ritchie riots of a quarter-century back, much of _Caught Stealing_’s flavor is derived from its wealth of colorful supporting characters. Most noteworthy among these are Liev Schreiber and Vincent D’Onofrio’s Hasidic hitmen and Matt Smith’s carelessly connected British punk. Butler gets down and dirty with the lot of them, deepening his hole with every tooth-skinned getaway, and we all benefit from the increasingly spiked stakes. It’s a wild ride, absolutely stuffed with big personalities and melting pot eccentricities, that still left me wanting more. This one really hit the mark.

review_content: The Lost Bus feels like a throwback to the disaster movies of the 90s, prioritizing high stakes and human resilience over modern cinematic gimmicks. While the "trapped in a catastrophe" premise offers nothing particularly new to the genre, the execution is top-tier. The spectacular visual effects and practical-feeling stunts create a visceral sense of danger that makes the fire feel terrifyingly real. This tension is anchored by grounded, powerful performances that elevate the material beyond a simple survival story. It’s a gripping, no-nonsense disaster flick that reminds you why we fell in love with this kind of cinema in the first place—tense, emotional, and visually arresting.

cast_names: Matthew McConaughey, America Ferrera, Yul Vazquez, Ashlie Atkinson, Kimberli Flores, Levi McConaughey, Kay McConaughey, John Messina, Kate Wharton, Danny McCarthy

cast_names: Matthew McConaughey, America Ferrera, Yul Vazquez, Ashlie Atkinson, Kimberli Flores, Levi McConaughey, Kay McConaughey, John Messina, Kate Wharton, Danny McCarthy
review_content: The moment when the world falls apart is almost never recognized while it’s happening. It only takes shape later, when memory tries to organize the chaos into fragments. “The Lost Bus” builds itself around that idea of collapsed time, creating an experience that relies less on grand gestures and more on immersion. From early on, the film makes it clear it wants to be felt before it’s analyzed, leaning into urgency rather than emotional didacticism. When it trusts the moment and the actors’ presence, it becomes a suffocating, physical experience that demands full attention and complete emotional buy-in.    The narrative centers on Kevin McKay (Matthew McConaughey), an ordinary man going through one of the worst periods of his life, suddenly pulled into an unlikely mission in the middle of a real-life catastrophe. The opening stretch, though, reveals the film’s biggest point of friction. There’s an overdetermined effort to build a heavy dramatic foundation, piling on personal tragedies as if the script is desperate to guarantee empathy. That insistence on explaining the protagonist’s pain weakens the impact, turning what could’ve been an organic buildup into something overly underlined. Instead of deepening the character, the exposition creates distance, constantly reminding us that emotional machinery is being activated.    Ironically, that early weakness makes it even clearer how much better the film gets once it stops explaining and starts immersing. The moment Kevin takes on the task of rescuing a group of children as the wildfire closes in, “The Lost Bus” finds its real pulse. The storytelling becomes more direct and stripped down, and what matters is immediate survival. Shifting the focus from psychology to action feels like a structural release, allowing the film to move into something more instinctive and less calculated.    A lot of that strength comes from how the film builds spatial tension. It understands that the horror isn’t just the flames, but the impossibility of escaping them. The constant use of smoke, blocked roads, and low visibility creates a sense of entrapment that keeps the tension alive even in quieter moments. Some sequences are almost unbearable in terms of anxiety, especially when the camera stays inside the bus, turning the viewer into another passenger stuck on a route with no guarantees. The disorientation feels real, and the film does the right thing by not romanticizing the chaos, treating the disaster with a sobriety that amplifies its impact.    Within that space, Matthew McConaughey’s performance becomes essential. He builds Kevin more through presence than dialogue, using restrained gestures and a tired physicality that communicates exhaustion without spelling it out. There’s a very grounded humanity in the way he inhabits the role, balancing vulnerability and resilience in a convincing way. It’s the kind of performance that proves some emotions don’t need explanation, just suggestion. Alongside him, America Ferrera offers an important counterbalance as the teacher sharing responsibility on the bus, bringing a more pragmatic energy that helps stabilize the chaos around them.    Still, the film carries a duality that’s hard to ignore. Whenever it tries too hard to control the audience’s emotional response, it loses some of the raw power it shows in its most stripped-down moments. There’s a clear tension between the urge to manipulate and the desire to simply observe. When it chooses the latter, “The Lost Bus” reaches a remarkable level of immersion, making the disaster feel tangible. When it falls back into the former, the experience loses some of its natural flow and reminds you there’s a dramatic framework steering every reaction.    Technically, though, the film holds strong. The staging favors continuity and proximity, creating the sense that events are unfolding in real time. The sound design plays a crucial role in that immersion, using the constant roar of fire and the muffled quality of smoke to build an oppressive atmosphere. It’s a film that understands the power of sensory storytelling, relying on image and sound as its main tools, even when the script hesitates.    In the end, “The Lost Bus” is defined by contrast. There’s a truly powerful film buried inside it, especially in the sequences that stop trying to justify emotions and simply drop the viewer into the tragedy. At the same time, there’s a dramatic layer that over-explains things the experience already makes clear. Still, when it finds balance, it becomes deeply engaging. Flawed, but intensely vivid, it’s a film that lingers more for the feeling it leaves behind than for the answers it provides.

cast_names: Dave Franco, Alison Brie, Damon Herriman, Mia Morrissey, Francesca Waters, Aljin Abella, Tom Considine, Melanie Beddie, Flynn Wandin, Michael Shanks
review_content: Together is not a comfortable or conventional film. I didn’t experience it as a story meant to entertain or explain things in a traditional way, but rather as an immersive emotional experience; at times even exhausting. I understand why it might be hard to watch, but that discomfort is part of its value.       The monstrous elements didn’t feel gratuitous to me. They work as a physical metaphor for something deeply intimate and recognizable: the fear of losing oneself in another person, and at the same time, the panic of being left alone. The entire film moves within that tension between the desire for fusion and the need to remain an individual. When love crosses a certain threshold, it stops being a meeting point and starts to resemble absorption.       Isolation accelerates this process. Cut off from the outside world, the couple is forced to look at each other without distractions, confronting not only the other person but also what they can no longer sustain within themselves. Adaptation stops being romantic and becomes almost violent, shaped by attachment to the past and the deceptive feeling of moving forward while actually remaining stuck.       While watching, I couldn’t stop thinking about the Platonic myth of the Androgyne, popularized in The Origin of Love from Hedwig and the Angry Inch: that original body split in two, condemned to endlessly search for its other half. Together seems to ask what happens when that desire to become one again turns literal and physical. The answer is uncomfortable but clear: absolute fusion doesn’t save: it deforms.       In the end, rather than asking whether union is possible, the film asks how to unite without disappearing. If loving means erasing oneself, the result is monstrous; if it means transforming without self-annihilation, perhaps something more human can emerge. That’s why I liked it: because it unsettles, because it refuses to idealize love, and because it leaves you with a question that lingers long after the credits roll. "How much of yourself are you willing to share while still remaining yourself?"

cast_names: Russell Crowe, Rami Malek, Michael Shannon, Leo Woodall, John Slattery, Richard E. Grant, Colin Hanks, Mark O'Brien, Lotte Verbeek, Wrenn Schmidt
review_content: “Nuremberg” is a magnificent historical film, one that reminds us of a terrible truth: that the worst men in history—those who committed the most horrific acts ever imagined—were still human beings.     In this sense, Russell Crowe as Hermann Göring turned out to be a magnificent choice, as he manages to convey the idea that his character is a likable man, someone you could be friends with—until the film delivers a brutal blow by revealing the crimes Hermann Göring authorized. Ultimately, the film reminds us how easily this kind of evil can arise in any society, and the need to remain eternally vigilant so that people like this can never repeat something like the Holocaust.    Historically speaking, there are moments where I think the film’s artistic liberties in the name of drama are quite noticeable, but overall, it’s a film that clearly strives to be as historically accurate as possible.

cast_names: Russell Crowe, Rami Malek, Michael Shannon, Leo Woodall, John Slattery, Richard E. Grant, Colin Hanks, Mark O'Brien, Lotte Verbeek, Wrenn Schmidt
review_content: Why and how has this dodged all award show discussions? The acting, casting, costuming, cinematography, directing, scoring, and writing—it's an absolute snub all around. A psychiatrist, a courtroom, and a handful of Nazi officials. Dissecting the complexities of pure evil is a perilously tough task, and the absolute unease in the almost provocative first half underlines why. Whether it be the 1940s or 2020s, the warning signs are frighteningly uncanny, and yet, despite the abhorrent horrors, we still struggle to grasp the lessons history provides. That said, the hypocrisy is stark. If the Nuremberg principles were truly upheld, several figures, past and present, would be facing a noose. "The only clue to what man can do is what man has done."

review_content: Growing up means learning to live alongside parts of reality that can’t be fully grasped. When that mismatch sets in, fantasy stops working as pure illusion and becomes a language for emotions that still don’t have a place in the real world. “Elio” fits into that tradition by embracing a deliberately predictable space adventure, guided by an emotional sincerity that refuses cynicism. Pixar returns to themes it knows well (grief, belonging, and improvised bonds) wrapping them in a cosmic fantasy that isn’t trying to be a sci-fi spectacle, but a gentle metaphor for a child’s desire to be seen, heard, and embraced in a world that feels too big to face alone.    The story begins with quiet pain. Elio is an orphan living with his aunt Olga, a woman who gave up her own dreams to take on a responsibility she never chose. Their relationship is marked by unresolved affection, guilt, and mutual resentment, a dynamic that clearly echoes other Pixar and Disney narratives. Elio can’t see Olga’s sacrifice as love; he experiences it as an irreparable loss caused by his own existence. That emotional disconnect is the film’s dramatic engine and gives weight to behaviors that, on the surface, might look like simple childhood rebellion, but here are rooted in unprocessed grief.    Fantasy enters the picture when the film turns Elio’s desire to escape into something literal. Gazing at the sky and idealizing extraterrestrial life as a place of belonging, the boy projects his hope onto something distant and unknown, yet seemingly safer than the real world. The encounter with the Communiverse (an intergalactic assembly filled with humor, visual excess, and deliberately eccentric figures) feels closer to an affectionate parody of classic sci-fi tropes than to a truly original creation. Still, the film understands that the value of this journey doesn’t lie in the novelty of the setting, but in what it awakens emotionally within its protagonist.    That’s where Glordon comes in, without a doubt the film’s greatest asset. He doesn’t just steal scenes, he holds the heart of the story. The son of an authoritarian, war-driven leader, Glordon represents the opposite of the legacy expected of him: he’s gentle, sensitive, and painfully aware that he’s destined to repeat a cycle of violence he never chose. The friendship between Elio and Glordon is built with care, through small gestures, shared silences, and quiet complicity, working as an emotional mirror for both of them. They’re two displaced kids, each in their own way, trying to escape roles imposed on them. When the film trusts this bond and allows it to breathe, “Elio” finds its most authentic and moving moments.    On a technical level, the film follows Pixar’s usual high standards, even if it doesn’t take many risks. The color palette leans into soft blues and greens, reinforcing a sense of melancholy and comfort, while the score focuses more on emotion than spectacle. The issue arises when the script feels the need to over-explain what’s already clear visually. The film often underestimates the emotional literacy of its young audience, spelling out feelings that would land more powerfully if left implied. That didactic impulse weakens certain scenes and strips away some of the subtlety Pixar has proven it can master in other projects.    Still, the third act shows why “Elio” ultimately works. That’s where the film fully embraces its message about loss, grief, and second chances. Elio’s pain doesn’t magically disappear, nor is it replaced by a definitive escapist fantasy. Instead, the narrative reinforces that true belonging isn’t found by running away from reality, but by rebuilding it through affection, listening, and mutual acceptance. Olga, in turn, stops being just the self-sacrificing adult and becomes someone who also needs to be understood and forgiven. The film recognizes that grief isn’t something to overcome, but something you carry, and that, over time, can make room for new connections.    “Elio” does exactly what it sets out to do: entertain, move, and offer a gentle message about moving forward without erasing what was lost. It’s not memorable for originality or narrative complexity, but for the emotional honesty with which it approaches universal feelings. It may seem overly simple at times, but there’s something deeply human in its refusal to mock the hope that somewhere, someone might be willing to welcome us when we feel alone. And sometimes, that’s more than enough.
