cast_names: Yonas Kibreab, Remy Edgerly, Zoe Saldaña, Brad Garrett, Brandon Moon, Jameela Jamil, Young Dylan, Jake T. Getman, Matthias Schweighöfer, Ana de la Reguera
review_content: There’s something immediately pleasant about Elio. From the very first minutes, it feels colorful, lively, and welcoming, with Pixar’s craftsmanship evident in every frame. Everything works… and yet, there’s also a lingering sense that we’ve been here before. Not in this exact setting, but certainly in this structure, this emotional journey, and this careful way of moving forward without straying too far off course.    The story touches on meaningful themes — loneliness, feeling out of place, the need to be seen — and does so with sensitivity. The issue isn’t what it wants to say, but how it chooses to say it. Pixar seems to have internalized its own formula so thoroughly that it now executes it almost on autopilot. Emotional beats arrive right on cue, lessons are clearly signposted, and the adventure never truly risks spinning out of control. Everything is solid, even charming, but genuine surprise is rare.    Visually, however, Elio is operating at a very high level. The universe design, colors, textures, and sheer imagination behind every creature and environment remind us why Pixar remains a benchmark in animation. Some sequences instantly captivate and deliver that sense of wonder the studio knows so well how to create, even when the story itself doesn’t fully take off.    Where the film falls slightly short is in its emotional weight. It aims to move the audience, and at times it succeeds, but without the depth or boldness of Pixar’s strongest works. There’s a feeling that it avoids pushing too far, as if reluctant to challenge or unsettle viewers. The result is a warm, empathetic experience that nevertheless feels a bit lightweight by the studio’s own standards.    In the end, Elio is an enjoyable, well-crafted film that’s easy to like. It’s far from a misstep, but it doesn’t redefine anything either. It works as family entertainment, delivers what it promises, and leaves a pleasant aftertaste. At the same time, it reinforces the sense that Pixar is currently in a phase where familiar structures feel safer than creative leaps into the unknown — and that may be its greatest limitation.

review_content: Rental Family is a beautifully observed, soulful dramedy. Brendan Fraser brings a tenderhearted, "puppy-dog" weariness to his performance and is the film's emotional anchor, radiating a wounded gentleness that makes the character deeply relatable. The film excels at exploring the bittersweet intersection of performance and real connection. While the premise could have been played for cynical laughs, director Hikari treats it with profound empathy, showing how these artificial arrangements can lead to genuine healing.  It isn't without its flaws; the second act feels a bit episodic and overstuffed as it jumps between different rental clients, and the tone occasionally veers into mawkish, saccharine territory. Some might find the "feel-good" ending a bit too neatly wrapped up given the ethically "iffy" nature of the deceptions involved. However, the stunning cinematography and authentic performances (Akira Emoto also brings a touch of class and gravitas) make it a moving, thoughtful meditation on the roles we all play to stave off loneliness.

review_content: Rental Family works almost entirely because Brendan Fraser absolutely nails this role. The story is emotional without feeling manipulative, and it’s crafted with the kind of restraint that makes the bigger moments land harder. It’s polished, sincere, and clearly built for awards season conversations. You can feel the prestige DNA in every scene.  It’s not necessarily one I see myself revisiting often — it’s heavy in the right ways — but I can absolutely imagine many people falling in love with this one.  Rating: 4/5 – 85% – Would Recommend

review_content: Elm Street energy, but not quite dream legend status    The Black Phone 2 swings bigger than the first film, leaning hard into dream logic and synth-heavy dread. The score buzzes and scrapes in all the right ways, giving the whole thing that A Nightmare on Elm Street echo less grounded abduction horror, more surreal nightmare playground.    There are a lot of cool ideas in here. The mythology expands, the tone gets stranger, and the movie clearly wants to elevate The Grabber into something more iconic, more myth, more presence, more than just a basement-bound villain. And honestly? That ambition is appreciated.    But bigger doesn’t always mean better. Some of the nightmare modes feel borrowed rather than owned, and while the ideas are strong, the execution doesn’t always hit as hard as it should. It’s enjoyable, stylish, and confidently made just not quite the leap forward it’s aiming for.    Solid sequel with fun dream horror energy and a stronger myth building push, even if it never fully becomes the next Elm Street. Cool ideas, good atmosphere, but not legendary.

cast_names: Mason Thames, Madeleine McGraw, Ethan Hawke, Miguel Mora, Demián Bichir, Jeremy Davies, Arianna Rivas, Maev Beaty, Graham Abbey, James Ransone
review_content: Black Phone 2 — 4K UHD Review (9/10)    Black Phone 2 arrives on 4K with the confidence of a sequel that knows exactly what worked the first time: atmosphere, dread, and a grounded emotional core. What surprised me is how much more visually assertive this entry is. The film leans harder into color, shadow, and texture, and the 4K disc takes full advantage of that—especially if you care about HDR formats.    Film Quality (The Movie Itself)    This sequel sharpens the original’s strengths rather than inflating them. The tension is tighter, the pacing more deliberate, and the performances—especially from the younger cast—carry a rawness that makes the supernatural elements feel even more unsettling.    The direction embraces a grimy, analog aesthetic: dim hallways, sodium-lit streets, and claustrophobic interiors. It’s a horror film that doesn’t rely on jump scares but on the slow tightening of a psychological vise. That restraint pays off.    Verdict on the film: 9/10.    It’s confident, unnerving, and emotionally grounded—exactly what a horror sequel should be.    4K Image Quality    The native 4K presentation is a major upgrade over streaming. Fine textures—fabric, skin, dust, chipped paint—are rendered with a clarity that enhances the film’s gritty realism. Grain is intact and cinematic, never smeary or noisy.    Where the disc really shines is in its handling of darkness. This movie lives in the shadows, and the 4K transfer preserves detail without washing out the black floor or crushing subtle gradients.    HDR10 vs. Dolby Vision    HDR10  HDR10 gives the film a slightly harsher, more contrast-heavy look.    Highlights pop aggressively—flashlights, street lamps, reflections on metal.    Blacks stay deep, but depending on your display calibration, some shadow detail can get a bit tight.    Colors lean more neutral and grounded, which fits the film’s gritty tone.    If your display is well-calibrated (especially tone mapping and black level), HDR10 can look excellent—clean, sharp, and punchy.    Dolby Vision  Dolby Vision is the more refined presentation.    Shadow detail is noticeably smoother, revealing subtle textures in dark rooms and nighttime scenes.    Highlights are controlled rather than explosive, giving the image a more natural dynamic range.    Colors gain a touch more depth—skin tones, muted reds, and the film’s signature desaturated palette feel more dimensional.    Dolby Vision’s dynamic metadata helps this movie tremendously because so much of the tension happens in low light.    Which Is Better?  For Black Phone 2, Dolby Vision is the superior experience.  The film’s visual language depends on shadow nuance and controlled contrast, and DV preserves that without sacrificing intensity. HDR10 is still strong, but Dolby Vision feels more cinematic and more faithful to the film’s mood.    Audio  The sound mix is immersive and unsettling.    Low-end rumbles subtly during supernatural moments.    Whispered voices and environmental cues are placed with precision.    Dialogue remains clear even in dense scenes.    It’s a mix that supports the tension without overwhelming it.    Final Score: 9/10  Black Phone 2 on 4K is a standout horror release—moody, atmospheric, and technically impressive. The Dolby Vision presentation elevates the experience with richer shadow detail and more controlled highlights, making it the recommended format for the most immersive viewing.

review_content: Black Phone 2 takes a noticeably different approach from the original, which makes it an interesting—if divisive—sequel. I loved the first film, and this one didn’t hit me the same way, largely because it shifts tone and focus rather than trying to recreate that lightning-in-a-bottle tension. That said, I can absolutely see why some viewers might prefer this direction, as it expands the concept and leans into new ideas instead of retreading familiar ground.  It doesn’t fully land for me, but I respect the swing. It’s thoughtful, different, and engaging enough to justify its existence, even if it doesn’t reach the heights of its predecessor.  Rating: 3/5 – 75% – Worth Watching

review_content: Going into it, I knew this movie was a high-concept rom-com that involved a love triangle, but I didn't know what the high-concept premise actually was.  That's my favorite way to experience movies and this is another example that validated that preference.  People always say things like, "Oh, let me tell you what it's about - the premise comes out in the first 10-15 minutes anyway!", but let me tell you - those first 15 minutes are a lot more fun when you're experiencing the set-up along with the characters, rather than already knowing exactly where it's headed.  In any case, this one has a very fun premise, lots of great humor, and even the big romantic beats landed (difficult to pull off with a love triangle).  It also helps that I'm a Miles Teller fan and he was excellent here.

review_content: Eternity is a rock-solid, genuinely unique rom-com that caught me off guard in a good way. It’s not laugh-out-loud hilarious, but it doesn’t really need to be. The charm carries it. The leads have strong chemistry, the performances feel natural, and there’s a mass-appeal quality baked into the whole thing.  The story is unique enough to stand out, even if the actual beats are somewhat predictable once you settle into it. But that predictability doesn’t hurt it — it just makes it comforting. It’s polished, easy to digest, and hits the emotional notes it’s aiming for.  This is the kind of romantic movie that feels built to be revisited, and I’ll probably circle back to it sooner rather than later.  Rating: 4/5 – 85% – Would Recommend

cast_names: Tom Hiddleston, Benjamin Pajak, Nick Offerman, Chiwetel Ejiofor, Karen Gillan, Mia Sara, Mark Hamill, Annalise Basso, Jacob Tremblay, Cody Flanagan
review_content: The movie starts with the ending — thanking Chuck — while the world is slowly coming to an end. This was portrayed beautifully throughout the first chapter. For some reason, Chuck’s billboard is shown all over the town, and we see people walking through empty streets, with one kid skateboarding alone. It delivers a beautiful message: if you love something, just do it. This moment brings a tear to my eye, as it portrays how growing up slowly takes away our joy and how we keep repeating the same routines over and over again. The first sequence ends with a couple staring at the sky as stars begin to fall.  The second sequence begins, and we witness Chuck’s life in reverse. Now we see three different perspectives and slowly discover who Charles really is. We begin to understand why everyone is thanking him in the first place. On a beautiful day, a girl sits in the busy streets playing drums. A businessman named Chuck walks toward her as she changes the rhythm. Chuck then places his bag down and starts dancing after remembering something from his past — though this is not revealed at that point and only becomes clear later in the film.  He dances soulfully, bringing smiles to everyone’s faces and gathering a crowd. A girl in the audience feels the rhythm of the drums and starts dancing as well. At one moment, Chuck invites her to dance with him, and together they perform beautifully. All three of them create a magical performance. Afterward, the drummer asks Chuck why he started dancing in the first place, but at that moment, he himself does not know. As the sun sets, we see Chuck return to the same spot, staring at the ground where the drummer had performed earlier, and the story moves into the third chapter.  In the third chapter, Chuck is shown as an eighth grader who has lost both of his parents and is living with his grandparents. There is silence at the dinner table every day because the family has suffered a tragic accident. Along with his parents, Chuck also lost his unborn six-month-old sister. After six months, we witness his grandmother dancing in the kitchen to rock and roll music while cooking — something she loves deeply.  At this point, we understand why Chuck began dancing earlier in the film after hearing the drums. He learned dance moves from his grandmother and watched many classic movies with her, which made him a wonderful dancer. Later, he joins a dance club at school, where he learns and performs with others, eventually dancing at the school ball.  The final act reveals why Chuck does not remember everything: he has been diagnosed with a brain tumor and has only a short time left to live. This explains his fading memories. Slowly and finally, we witness him dying in a hospital bed, surrounded by his loved ones.

review_content: An initially intriguing mystery and a cast in strong form ultimately couldn't prevent me from being rather disappointed by “The Life of Chuck.” The tone is far too sentimental and pseudo-profound. Added to its shortcomings are an overly imposing narrator and a rather lame resolution to the mystery. On a technical level, however, there is little to complain about except for the manipulative score, and I would also give the film credit for having few dull moments. But ultimately, I expect much more from Mike Flanagan than a somewhat competently directed film.

cast_names: Tom Hiddleston, Benjamin Pajak, Nick Offerman, Chiwetel Ejiofor, Karen Gillan, Mia Sara, Mark Hamill, Annalise Basso, Jacob Tremblay, Cody Flanagan
review_content: "It's the waiting, that's the hard part"    I rewatched this after rewatching The Long Walk, two King adaptations that were among my favorite releases of the year. While these stories are going for something different, it's an interesting double feature that takes a look at life and death from the point of view of an author when he is very early in life to then checking in with him when he is much older.     The obvious difference is the sheer violence and brutality but the similarity lands with the idea to always find the beauty in life to keep that light alive. This is the deeper exploration of the Life Of Chuck even when the pain can seem unbearable. It is all those moments and more that make up you and your life, the moments that live in you forever. That's the beautiful part.

cast_names: Tom Hiddleston, Benjamin Pajak, Nick Offerman, Chiwetel Ejiofor, Karen Gillan, Mia Sara, Mark Hamill, Annalise Basso, Jacob Tremblay, Cody Flanagan
review_content: The Life of Chuck sneaks up on you. It opens like a riddle, fragments scattered out of order, quietly daring you to trust it. The film drip-feeds clues at just the right pace, never rushing, never spoon-feeding, letting the meaning take shape in your own head.    What really sells it is the tone. This is a modern fable, but one with restraint. It is wholesome without being soft, emotional without leaning into sentimentality. There is a deep kindness running through it, yet it never tips into preachy or saccharine territory.    The performances are uniformly strong, but the young Chuck is something special. His dance scenes are genuinely beautiful. Awkward, joyful, and deeply human. They capture a kind of innocence and freedom that feels rare on screen, and they linger long after the credits roll.    By the end, the film clicks into place not as a puzzle to be solved, but as a reflection to sit with. It is about life, memory, and the quiet significance of ordinary moments. Gentle, strange, and deeply affirming.    If you like. . .Big Fish or The Curious Case of Benjamin Button

cast_names: Joel Edgerton, Felicity Jones, Nathaniel Arcand, Clifton Collins Jr., John Diehl, Paul Schneider, Kerry Condon, William H. Macy, Will Patton, Alfred Hsing
review_content: Directed by Clint Bentley, “Train Dreams” is a film that clearly comes from a very specific aesthetic ambition. The goal is to build a contemplative, meditative narrative that follows the entire life of an ordinary man during the expansion of the American railroads, using the train as a constant metaphor for both progress and destruction. It’s a beautiful idea on paper, and in terms of intention, the film knows exactly what it wants to be: a reflection on memory, loss, and belonging, anchored in a lyrical portrait of time passing. Bentley leans into a sensory approach, with images that evoke memories more than objective events, trying to turn Robert Grainier’s life into a kind of elegy for lives that slip through the cracks of official history.    Technically, the film is very well executed. Adolpho Veloso’s cinematography has a tactile quality, with carefully composed shots that make the most of natural light and wide landscapes, creating images that feel like they were pulled from a weathered old photo album. Bryce Dessner’s score reinforces that melancholic atmosphere, helping sustain the realistic fable tone Bentley is going for. There’s a clear influence from Chloé Zhao’s cinema, especially in how nature and memory intertwine, and in many moments “Train Dreams” really does achieve undeniable visual beauty. It’s a film that knows how to compose striking frames and understands the power of visual suggestion.    Performance-wise, Joel Edgerton carries the film with a restrained, deeply physical performance, building Robert more through silence than dialogue. He conveys the feeling of a man crushed by time and by the weight of unmade choices, which gives the character a strong presence even when the script doesn’t explore him as deeply as it could. Will Patton’s voiceover works as an emotional extension of the protagonist and, at times, genuinely adds texture to the narrative. Still, it also reveals a certain dependence on verbalizing feelings that could’ve emerged more organically through staging. Smaller appearances from actors like Felicity Jones and William H. Macy are effective, but they feel more like symbolic fragments than fully developed characters.    The problem is that, despite all this formal competence, “Train Dreams” suffers from a narrative stagnation that’s hard to ignore. The attempt to create a dreamlike flow ends up sacrificing rhythm, and the film often feels stuck repeating emotional states without a clear dramatic progression. The episodic structure, which should suggest the span of an entire life, sometimes comes across as a series of vignettes that don’t build enough cumulative weight. There’s a noticeable sense of bloat, a constant feeling that time drags more than it transforms, and that makes the experience less engaging than the material suggests it could be.    Overall, “Train Dreams” is a beautiful and technically admirable film that ultimately stumbles over its own ambition. There’s sensitivity in the direction, strong aesthetic care, and a genuine poetic intent, but it lacks the dramatic pulse needed to hold everything together. The film feels more interested in contemplating the idea of a life than actually letting that life breathe in front of us. The result is a work that impresses formally but stays emotionally distant, like a memory you feel should move you more than it actually does.

cast_names: Joel Edgerton, Felicity Jones, Nathaniel Arcand, Clifton Collins Jr., John Diehl, Paul Schneider, Kerry Condon, William H. Macy, Will Patton, Alfred Hsing
review_content: Overall    Train Dreams is a fascinating historical drama adaptation of a 2011 fictional novella of the same name by Denis Johnson. Most of the events unfold in the early twentieth century until 1968 and appear genuine. I struggle to explain Train Dreams because it’s unlike most movies. Rather than following traditional Hollywood plot structures, it delves into a reflective exploration of the human condition. Life’s essence lies not in events themselves, but in how we process them post-event and what we take from each encounter. The style here isn’t revolutionary (Italian neo-realism came first), but it’s refreshing to witness done well. In director Clint Bentley, one can perceive echoes of past giants Roberto Rossellini, Ingmar Bergman, Jean-Luc Godard, and Satyajit Ray. This covers themes including loneliness, acceptance, growth, loss, pain, and time. This nostalgic movie touches on every emotion, and prioritizes sentiment over plot or high-energy action. Oscar nominations include Best Cinematography, Best Original Song, Best Picture, and Best Adapted Screenplay. I see it in the running for each except Best Picture.    Would I recommend this?    Yes, I recommend this, though it may not be for everyone. I understand many will find this dull or depressing, especially the slow progression. Much like poetry, the messages and themes subtly weave into the story and require thoughtful consideration. Despite its abstract nature, I sense it resonates with everyone at some stage. The passage of life and time, the people we encounter, and our formed memories carry something poignant in their grasp. My rating is 8/10.    Plot (spoiler-free)    Robert Grainier (Joel Edgerton), a logger and railroad worker, lives on the frontier for 80 years, from 1888 to 1968. His melancholic world began alone in orphanhood and ended alone in seclusion. Between the two points, he experiences growth, change, loss, and time. People enter and exit his life, including Gladys Olding (Felicity Jones), but he carries himself as a simple man with dignity. The world transformed around him from a natural frontier to modern technology, and we witness that shift from his perspective.    Technicals    Train Dreams doesn’t come across as a typical period drama. The style, presentation, and narration are reminiscent of a documentary more than the usual movie. The best aspects are the authentic atmosphere, acting, direction, the cinematography, visuals, script, and the soundtrack. Raw and introspective are the best ways to define the experience. The performance by Edgerton, Felicity Jones, and the rest of the cast is excellent. Cinematography and photography focus on nature, society, people, and thoughtful intricacies like meditation. Music, dialogue-less visual scenes, and landscapes do a wonderful job of helping audiences absorb what they depict. Ultimately, the movie concerns everything that makes us human but doesn’t leave us with answers. This encourages you to connect and empathize with Grainier thematically without telling you how to live. It’s impressive that the entire production process wrapped up in just 29 days. As for issues, the pacing felt super slow and occasionally lost my attention. It’s not always easy to stay present throughout. Second, I think it could use more events, but I’m uncertain what else they could add. This isn’t the typical Hollywood work, so that makes it challenging.    *Obtained trivia facts from IMDb’s trivia page and plot/basic history/name information from Wiki

review_content: Deep Cover is one of those movies that you don't expect much... and in the end you end up having a great time. It has that classic ‘buddie movie’ feel to it, but with a slightly different twist that makes it stand out among so many rehashes.    The plot is a constant mess, but in a good way. Every time you think it can't get any messier, wham, another twist and you're left scratching your head. But the good thing is that, instead of being overwhelming, it hooks you. It's one of those films where you can't stop watching the protagonists get into one garden after another.    Orlando Bloom is surprisingly good, very into his role, with a commitment that we haven't seen in a long time. And watch out for Bryce Dallas Howard, who appears so physically changed that it's hard to recognise her at first. Even so, she delivers, although her character is less playful than one would expect.    A film that goes down well on its own: fun, with pace, without taking itself too seriously and that knows how to play with what it has. If you're looking for a good time with no other pretensions, Deep Cover is a more than decent option.

You definitely need to be in the right mood for the movie, but as long as you accept it for what it is and no more. It would be a self parody. If his style was not already self parody. Strangely, I liked it precisely because it didn’t take itself seriously.

cast_names: Benicio del Toro, Mia Threapleton, Michael Cera, Riz Ahmed, Tom Hanks, Bryan Cranston, Mathieu Amalric, Richard Ayoade, Benedict Cumberbatch, Jeffrey Wright
review_content: Definitely a Wes Anderson movie, no doubt about it.   Has all the distinct characterizations of his previous movies.  Wonderful dead pan acting, dry humor, silliness, amazing sets and an otherworldly distinct design, great cast.   Unlike his other movies though, it's a bit incoherent.    I'm left wondering what the plot was.    It seemed to move in too many disparate directions.  On the surface it was about a rich tycoons strange business dealings, on the other hand, maybe it was about family?    Perhaps regret at living only for the deal?    I'm not sure.   Then there were the weird religious themes.     Whatever it was, I was left scratching my head, mildly amused but unfortunately disappointed.

The acting and casting seem monotone and stiff, which is strange as there's more than a fair amount of talent involved. The continuity feels rushed and the editing choppy. review_content: [Disney+] Two good actors star in a rather watered-down remake of what was once a film that conveyed the cruelty to which marital conflicts can descend. It aims for a more realistic portrayal of disputes, but remains in a middle ground where there are no real battles, only skirmishes of sarcastic remarks that seem too harmless to be genuinely interesting. What results is a chronicle of a rather dull marriage that touches on responsibilities and unbalanced personal growth within the couple, but lacks the biting satire and bite of the original film.

review_content: "I Don't Know What You Did Last Summer." Completely unmemorable, unoriginal, and uninspiring. movie_title: I Know What You Did Last Summer
cast_names: Chase Sui Wonders, Madelyn Cline, Sarah Pidgeon, Jennifer Love Hewitt, Freddie Prinze Jr., Jonah Hauer-King, Tyriq Withers, Sarah Michelle Gellar, Billy Campbell, Brandy Norwood
review_content: The legacy sequel that nobody wanted, _I Know What You Did Last Summer_ is the worst entry in the series. A year after five friends cause a car to crash and fall off a cliff, they’re stalked by a masked killer in a fisherman’s slicker. The writing is incredibly bad; the characters aren’t relatable, the crash scenario is rather lame, and the “who’s the killer” mystery just isn’t that interesting. But worse than any of that is what is done to the original characters. _I Know What You Did Last Summer_ is a pale imitation of the original that gets everything wrong and doesn’t deliver any thrills or scares.

review_content: Family Man 2 is a pretty meh film overall. The story lacks urgency, the characters feel flat, and the whole thing plays more like a forgettable TV movie than a memorable feature. Aside from a few postcard-perfect shots of Paris, there’s not much to hold on to. The real surprise comes from Kit Harington, who completely outshines the rest of the cast. While everyone else seems to coast through their roles, he brings an unexpected intensity — a raw, almost visceral anger that finally gives the film some weight. Ironically, his performance also highlights the movie’s main flaw: one genuinely committed actor stuck in a production that never rises to his level. In the end, Family Man 2 is mostly worth watching for Harington… and for Paris. The rest fades quickly from memory.

review_content: I went in expecting a gripping sci-fi adventure, but Project Hail Mary was a massive disappointment that failed to live up to the hype. 2/10. The premise had potential: Earth is facing a new ice age because a micro-organism called "Astrophage" is draining the sun’s energy. Ryland Grace (Ryan Gosling), a former scientist turned teacher, is sent on a suicide mission to the star Tau Ceti to find a solution.  While the visuals were decent and the alien character "Rocky" provided a few funny moments, the film is an overlong slog at 2 hours and 36 minutes. The character of Ryland Grace is incredibly irritating and "cringe", he is portrayed as a self-centered coward who has to be drugged and dragged onto the mission, making him impossible to root for. The central friendship between Grace and Rocky, which should have been the heart of the movie, felt forced and completely failed to move me emotionally. Compared to director Lord and Miller’s previous work or Drew Goddard’s "The Martian," this felt immature and poorly written. Unless you want to be bored by a "cringe" protagonist, I’d recommend skipping this one.

review_content: This was not a good continuation.  Even if the plot and budget seem to be right, the authors have unfortunately failed completely here.  How, of course, after the collapse of the bunker, the family literally travels through half of Europe in a nutshell where they meet friendly people without exception, expect unlimited free hospitality and have everything they need, just like a magic hand.  The son's diabetes seems to be cured in the bunker within five years, as well as the father's burnouts and mom's makeup and hair are just as perfect as the always clean clothes. The environmental conditions are at least as flexible as the writers in building an arc of tension.  Even in this anarchist post-apocalyptic world, every woman is more respectfully carried on hands than in the real world today, and not even anyone would come up with the idea of touching her, let alone criticizing her. Also, the family has of course climbed to the top within five years in the bunker hierarchy and the man today is elite fighter in single use regardless of his deadly diagnosis.  Forgotten are the hunter-metre-high tsunami waves that theoretically would have to have flooded entire continents, the 500 degrees hot storms that are allowed to have a lasting impact on the environment and, of course, the electromagnetic storms and continuous impacts that only appear when one of the 2,000 attempts to take place in the first place is a part of the basic story still considered.  3/10  In summary, there simply cannot be so many coincidences and the story is completely maneuvered away.

review_content: My expectations going in to this were incredibly low - I'm not a Kevin James fan, I didn't enjoy Reacher, I haven't liked the vast majority of made-for-streaming films that I've seen, and the general sentiment with reviews was negative.    Imagine my surprise when I found myself enjoying it. Obviously this isn't 'cinema', I doubt I'll be rewatching it, and the production values felt more like a YouTube video than Hollywood, but there are far worse ways to spend an hour and a half. Comedy can be incredibly hit or miss in films - Christmas Vacation makes me smile and laugh so much that it hurts whereas The Naked Gun doesn't work for me at all, but other people could say the inverse. For me, the comedy in Playdate worked, being just the right side of the incredibly thin line where the somewhat slapstick bizarre absurdity either sticks the landing or falls flat. Not everything landed for me, but the majority of it did, and the comedic timing of the actors and the editing were spot on. Most of the needle drops worked for me too, instead of rolling my eyes like I did with the needle drops in Atomic Blonde.    The film doesn't overstay its welcome or pretend to be something it isn't, it knows what it is and it excels in that regard. You won't see it nominated for an Academy Award, but, and I can't believe I'm saying this, I look forward to a sequel.

cast_names: Kevin James, Alan Ritchson, Sarah Chalke, Isla Fisher, Alan Tudyk, Stephen Root, Benjamin Pajak, Banks Pierce, Jason William Day, Hiro Kanagawa
review_content: _Playdate_ is an entertaining, light-hearted comedy that knows its lane and stays in it. It’s easy to watch, moves at a brisk pace, and delivers consistent laughs without ever aiming much higher than that. Alan Ritchson and Kevin James are the clear standouts, playing off each other with great chemistry. Ritchson, in particular, continues to prove he has strong comedic instincts, while James feels comfortably in his element.    The film sets up several fun supporting players but doesn’t fully capitalize on them. Isla Fisher and the so-called “Mom Mafia” feel like they were primed for a lot more screen time and sharper comedic beats, yet they’re mostly sidelined. The same goes for Alan Tudyk, whose presence promises more chaos than the script ultimately allows. Both feel like missed opportunities in a movie that could have benefited from a deeper bench.    There are plenty of good laugh moments sprinkled throughout, and the humor generally lands thanks to solid timing and likable performances. That said, the movie rarely surprises. It follows a familiar comedic structure and resolves itself exactly the way you expect, without much bite or emotional weight.    Reminded me a lot of a worse version of Kevin James' own _True Memoirs from an International Assassin_ and Mark Wahlberg's _Family Plan_.      Playdate works as disposable fun. It’s the kind of movie you enjoy in the moment and then quickly forget. Pleasant, competent, and occasionally very funny, but not especially memorable.    6/10.

In countries like Turkey, we often see the same pattern — mafia, police, and government all connected.  It was very sad that the professor behaved so bravely, yet he was killed.  He tried to send a message to the embassy, but they didn’t let him.    So Mike didn’t come just for his brother — he had another reason.    The last parts were nonsense, though.  They didn’t kill the woman.  And suddenly that woman came back, they shot at each other, and no one died — that felt unrealistic.    Also, why didn’t they damage the crane after moving to the other side?  There were lots of soldiers, but only a few got killed — that part didn’t make sense either.    By the way, Kathmandu is the capital and largest city of Nepal. Now adays there are protests.

review_content: The Bad Guys 2 is a rare animated sequel that amplifies everything that made the original sing: razor-sharp visual wit, kinetic action, and warm-hearted character work. Under Pierre Perifel’s assured direction and a screenplay that balances clever heist mechanics with genuine emotional stakes, the now-reformed gang is sent on a globe‑trotting caper that never loses sight of its characters. The film looks and feels like a modern cartoon heist movie — smart, fast, and unashamedly fun.    Voice performances are a highlight. Sam Rockwell leads with charismatic restraint, Marc Maron and Awkwafina supply perfectly timed comic counterpoint, and the ensemble — from Craig Robinson to Zazie Beetz and Maria Bakalova — finds fresh, distinct flavors for each member of the team and the new antagonists. The script gives actors room to play, and the result is a cast that’s as entertaining in quieter, character-driven beats as it is in big set-piece comedy.    Visually, the movie is exceptional. The animation embraces a stylized aesthetic — bold silhouettes, expressive character animation, and meticulously designed environments — that serves both comedy and action. Action sequences are staged with cinematic clarity and imaginative choreography; the film alternates slick, suspenseful robbery moments with broad physical comedy that lands for both kids and adults. The emotional moments are earned, and the pacing keeps the family audience fully engaged without ever feeling rushed.    Technical presentation: If you have the option, watching The Bad Guys 2 in 4K Dolby Vision is a transformative experience. The HDR implementation elevates the film’s already vivid palette: colors gain depth and punch, specular highlights on metallic set pieces and glossy surfaces sing without blooming, and shadow detail is preserved so the stylized chiaroscuro reads with cinematic nuance. Dolby Vision’s scene‑by‑scene tone mapping maintains consistent contrast and preserves the filmmakers’ intended look — bright, saturated action beats feel explosive while low‑light moments retain texture and atmosphere. In close-ups, fur, fabric and background texture snap with extra clarity in 4K, and the resolution keeps fast camera moves crisp without introducing distracting artifacts. In short, the 4K Dolby Vision transfer doesn’t just look sharper — it deepens the movie’s visual storytelling, making set pieces and small gestures equally compelling.    Why it earns a 10/10: The Bad Guys 2 is a full package — smart, hilarious, heartfelt, and technically polished. It respects the intelligence of its audience, gives its ensemble room to shine, and couples audacious visual inventiveness with sincere emotional cores. For families, animation fans, and anyone who loves a tightly executed heist comedy, this is essential viewing. For the best possible presentation, seek out the 4K Dolby Vision edition and, if your system supports it, pair it with a robust surround setup to get maximum immersion.

review_content: Shelter starts off like classic Statham Comfort Food: scowling, punching, brooding, repeat. For the first few minutes, I thought, “Yep, here we go, another Jason Statham movie where Jason Statham does Jason Statham things.” And then, annoyingly for my own cynicism, I ended up liking it a lot.    The plot is straightforward and not exactly allergic to predictability, but Statham knows his lane better than almost anyone. The smart move here was not trying to turn him into something he’s not, but letting him do what he does best: intensity, menace, and just enough buried feeling to make you think, “Wait, is this man… acting?” Turns out, yes.    Bodhi Rae Breathnach is fantastic and absolutely someone to watch. She brings real heart to the movie and more than holds her own opposite Statham, which is no small feat when your scene partner looks like he could headbutt a tank. Bill Nighy, meanwhile, is Bill Nighy, meaning he casually strolls in and improves everything by existing.    The action scenes are sharp and well-shot, the dialogue is better than this genre usually gets, and the movie has more emotional depth than I expected. Also, yes, they kill the dog, which feels like the filmmakers studied at the John Wick School of Audience Trauma, except here the movie spends enough time with the dog that it actually hurts.    As a general action movie, I’d give Shelter a 7/10. As a Statham movie? Easy 9/10. And since Statham produced it, I’m fully on board for more Michael Mason movies. Give the man a franchise. Bourne has amnesia, Reacher has a toothbrush, and Michael Mason has that thousand-yard stare that says everyone in a 20-mile radius is about to regret their life choices.

cast_names: Rachel McAdams, Dylan O'Brien, Edyll Ismail, Dennis Haysbert, Xavier Samuel, Chris Pang, Thaneth Warakulnukroh, Emma Raimi, Kristy Best, Francesca Waters
review_content: There’s a version of Send Help that absolutely works. You can feel it trying to break through. A nasty, stripped-back survival story with Lord of the Flies chaos and Misery style psychological tension. That’s a strong hook. But the film never commits, and what we get instead feels messy and oddly directionless.  For a director like Sam Raimi, it’s surprising how muted this feels. You’ll get the occasional burst of goo, some frantic energy, a moment that reminds you who’s behind the camera… then it fades straight back into something far more generic. It genuinely feels like someone else’s film with Raimi popping in every twenty minutes to “Raimi it up.”  The leads are fine. Not bad, not standout. They’re stuck dragging a script that doesn’t give them anything meaningful to build on. Motivations feel thin, and scenes play out like loosely connected ideas rather than a story with momentum. It’s frustrating, because the premise is doing a lot of heavy lifting that the writing just doesn’t support.  There are flashes of what this could’ve been. The pig hunt sequence is easily the standout. It’s scrappy, tense, and finally feels alive. For a moment, you get a glimpse of the film you signed up for.  Send Help is a missed opportunity. There’s a much better, nastier film hiding in here, but it never quite claws its way out.  If you liked…Lord of the Flies, Misery, Sweetheart

movie_title: Chainsaw Man - The Movie: Reze Arc
cast_names: Kikunosuke Toya, Reina Ueda, Tomori Kusunoki, Shogo Sakata, Maaya Uchida, Natsuki Hanae, Karin Takahashi, Fairouz Ai, Yuuya Uchida, Shiori Izawa
review_content: "I've technically never hung out with a girl before". Bro is lying or just doesn't count Power lol.   I saw some of the manga panels about the movie date earlier today (or I guess yesterday since it's 2 AM rn lol), and didn't realise it happened this early.  I'd heard a bit about the Reze Arc (and how she dies at the end), but not many details - and I can't believe my prediction about her being a devil was spot-on lol. I feel like it's just one of those things you intuitively know if you've experienced enough media.  I'm honestly a bit disappointed she gets killed at the end. Would've been interesting to have her return in season two (if that gets made), but whatever. This always happens ffs.  Honestly, I just kinda feel bad for Denji by the end. Can't believe Reze was going to the cafe, and then Makima had to go and fuck it up ffs. I bet she never tells him or anything, either.  Whatever. I knew it'd happen from the start, so I can't really say much about it - but I can still be disappointed lol.  Solid movie overall. The animation was fucking insane, and I'm really curious as to how the hell they storyboarded half of it (especially the final fight).  Guess I gotta read the manga now...

movie_title: Chainsaw Man - The Movie: Reze Arc
cast_names: Kikunosuke Toya, Reina Ueda, Tomori Kusunoki, Shogo Sakata, Maaya Uchida, Natsuki Hanae, Karin Takahashi, Fairouz Ai, Yuuya Uchida, Shiori Izawa
review_content: It's been a long time since a story transmitted so much loneliness and at the same time so much hope, only to snatch it all away. I was fascinated by the relationship between Denji and Reze because it feels real within all the madness of demons and chainsaws, they were simply two kids who never went to school, who never had a normal life and who find comfort in each other. It hurt me so much to see how [SPOILER] Reze, who at first seems to only be manipulating Denji for his heart, ends up falling into her own trap and decides she wants to escape with him. [/SPOILER]    Visually, I thought it was insane. MAPPA, as always killing the game. There are scenes that are art, especially the ones playing with light and rain. And the tension that something bad was going to happen... [SPOILER] The scene where she's going to the cafe to meet him... determined to leave everything behind and seek that happiness, and suddenly Makima appears. [/SPOILER] It's tragic, because in this world, seeking freedom seems to be a death sentence.    Perfect pacing, I didn't feel like a single minute was wasted. It went from super intimate and beautiful moments, like the pool one, to frenetic action. It's not just an action movie, it's a drama about freedom and the price of trying to be human when the world forces you to be a monster.

review_content: War of the Worlds gives the alien invasion classic a screenlife makeover, swapping battlefields for surveillance feeds. The result is messy, noisy, and exhausting, with Ice Cube miscast as the lead, Eva Longoria and Clark Gregg wasted, and themes of technology and privacy drowned out by chaotic editing. Decent effects can’t save the film from tonal missteps, an incoherent story, and the most ridiculous product placement imaginable.

review_content: To be honest I loved every second of this movie. I enjoyed the flashbacks and development of characters with seemingly no backstory. The animation quality is above anything else I've ever seen and its topped my favorite animation ever, which was the last episode of last season.     The story isn't great sure, but I think there are parts that makeup for that. And overall I thought this was a truly top tier movie.

movie_title: Demon Slayer: Kimetsu no Yaiba Infinity Castle
cast_names: Natsuki Hanae, Takahiro Sakurai, Akira Ishida, Hiro Shimono, Yoshimasa Hosoya, Saori Hayami, Mamoru Miyano, Reina Ueda, Yuichi Nakamura, Lynn
review_content: A flashback audiobook: this movie is the epitome of every trait i dislike about anime.    The constant need to explain everything on screen and even recount what we literally just witnessed.  Every movement needs a name and god forbid you move without announcing what you're about to do (and subsequently explain what you just did).    The main issue is the incessant disruption of any rhythm or momentum the movie could ever hope to gain by frequently inserting flashbacks mid-action.    These quirks ruined the movie, which really is sad considering how good it actually was at times.    The Akaza backstory carries the movie, everything else is so laborious.  (The flashback containing his backstory got so good i almost expected it to be interrupted by a flashback)

cast_names: Natsuki Hanae, Takahiro Sakurai, Akira Ishida, Hiro Shimono, Yoshimasa Hosoya, Saori Hayami, Mamoru Miyano, Reina Ueda, Yuichi Nakamura, Lynn
review_content: It was a mixed bag. It didn't feel like a movie but more like a (very) extended episode. I didn't mind the flashbacks much as they're to be expected in most anime and they give depth to characters we know nothing about -but some could be cut shorter. They were a welcome change of scenery, especially as the repetitive views of the infinity castle made it look like one long battle. Animation quality was amazing with fast paced battles and acrobatic camera angles. In the end it did feel like it needed to progress the story more and spend less time on past events.

movie_title: Demon Slayer: Kimetsu no Yaiba Infinity Castle

review_content: I don’t know, I was really hyped for this movie, and in the end I felt a bit disappointed. At the same time, though, I still enjoyed watching it, which makes my feelings kind of mixed. What bothered me the most was that almost an hour of the movie relied heavily on flashbacks. I get that this is very Demon Slayer–style, but it still affected the pacing for me. I really hope the next movie will be better and deliver a stronger, more engaging experience overall. Despite that, I still recommend watching it, and it’s definitely not a bad movie.

movie_title: Demon Slayer: Kimetsu no Yaiba Infinity Castle
cast_names: Natsuki Hanae, Takahiro Sakurai, Akira Ishida, Hiro Shimono, Yoshimasa Hosoya, Saori Hayami, Mamoru Miyano, Reina Ueda, Yuichi Nakamura, Lynn
review_content: 1 word to describe this duology: AMBITIOUS!  The idea  of the story could be written and spread over 15 to 20 episodes. Instead, they compacted it into 2 films. This part 1 is already 155 minutes long. And the animation is spectacular and historically legendary.  What surprised me the most was the screen writers and director atayed focus on keeping the key ingredients of Kimetsu no Yaiba, which is The Heart of a Warrior.   Tanjiro Kamado was written to illustrate the Japanese Heart of a Warrior. The burning will to move forward without losing his core values. It was masterfully and brilliantly filmed and visualized at the ending the battle between Tanjiro + Gitu VS Akaza. Everyone carries their own samurai sword in what ever form, including fists. Such a rare quality to habe in today's cinematic film.  IMHO, Disney's MCU and WB's DCU are trash.  Infinity Castle Part 1 reminded me of 1988's Akira. Regardless how old the film is, Akira is still the standard masterpiece of Japanese animation cinematic film. Infinity Castle will be there as the top 5 iconic animation masterpiece.  I found out about 2 decades ago, the first sign of a film is a masterpiece is you can watch it 10x or 100x, you'll still find yourself amazed by the film.   Kimetsu no Yaiba Infinity Castle Part 1 is such masterpiece that you can watch 100x, I will still be amazed by this film.

cast_names: Vince Vaughn, Lorraine Bracco, Talia Shire, Susan Sarandon, Linda Cardellini, Brenda Vaccaro, Drea De Matteo, Joe Manganiello, Campbell Scott, Richie Moriarty
review_content: This was a great way to bring in 2026 for me. Great cast drives story of a grieving son that starts a restaurant to honor his recently passed mother by staffing it with real grandmas to make their best recipes. Heartwarming and sweet (& savory!) and gives you the feels. A great film to watch when you feel cynical about the world and remind you that good can happen in the world.

cast_names: Vince Vaughn, Lorraine Bracco, Talia Shire, Susan Sarandon, Linda Cardellini, Brenda Vaccaro, Drea De Matteo, Joe Manganiello, Campbell Scott, Richie Moriarty
review_content: Some films don’t try to reinvent anything; they just want to offer a pleasant time. Nonnas falls squarely into that category, with a warm, down-to-earth proposal meant to be enjoyed with family. Its greatest strength lies in the concept: bringing together several Italian grandmothers around the kitchen to build a story of community, nostalgia, and affection.    The plot unfolds without major surprises, but with a charm that’s hard to resist. It doesn’t aim to be a thriller or a complex drama; instead, it leans on the everyday, on small gestures, and on light humor that adds to the emotion rather than detracting from it. In that sense, it knows exactly what it wants to deliver—and it succeeds.    The cast is another highlight. The actors bring charisma and skill, throwing themselves into a story that’s simple but effective. Together they make the characters endearing, even when the film edges toward the sentimental or predictable.    Visually, it stands out for its food and setting photography—almost like an ad, but effective at conveying a homely atmosphere. You can practically smell the recipes being prepared on screen, a detail that enriches the experience and reinforces the cozy tone.    In the end, Nonnas doesn’t aspire to be more than what it is: a kind, sentimental, and pleasant film. It may feel too light for those looking for depth, but for anyone in search of a heartwarming time, it has the right ingredients to leave a good taste. And even more so, knowing that the restaurant actually exists.

cast_names: Vince Vaughn, Lorraine Bracco, Talia Shire, Susan Sarandon, Linda Cardellini, Brenda Vaccaro, Drea De Matteo, Joe Manganiello, Campbell Scott, Richie Moriarty
review_content: While the plot feels like a Hallmark premise and the story follows a predictable path, the performances and execution elevate it into a wholesome good time.  And it certainly doesn't hurt to have Vince Vaughn as your lead.  And while family-type connection is the main theme, the element that actually hit harder for me was the idea of risk taking - taking the leap to build the type of things that make life worth living.

cast_names: Vince Vaughn, Lorraine Bracco, Talia Shire, Susan Sarandon, Linda Cardellini, Brenda Vaccaro, Drea De Matteo, Joe Manganiello, Campbell Scott, Richie Moriarty

cast_names: Vince Vaughn, Lorraine Bracco, Talia Shire, Susan Sarandon, Linda Cardellini, Brenda Vaccaro, Drea De Matteo, Joe Manganiello, Campbell Scott, Richie Moriarty
review_content: Nonnas is one of those movies that you know more or less where it's going to go from the beginning, but it still brings a smile (and a tear or two). The story is based on real events, which always gives it a plus, and pulls a lot of family fiber: mothers, grandmothers, grandchildren ... and, of course, food as the center of everything.    The film does a good job of showing regional differences (especially within the Italian-American community), and has some funny moments born out of that clash of cultures. It's a mix of comedy and drama with that sentimental touch that, while predictable, works quite well.    Susan Sarandon is simply formidable. At 78 years old, she still steals scenes with tremendous naturalness, and is, without a doubt, the best thing about the film. On the other hand, Vince Vaughn... well, nothing new: he's still flat, unable to transmit much, and a bit out of place compared to the rest of the cast.    An important detail: if you can, watch it in the original version. The accents and the way the grandmothers speak are part of the charm and much is lost in translation.    Is it a revolutionary film? No. Is it predictable? Quite predictable. But if you like family stories that celebrate food, love and the bonds created around a good table, Nonnas will touch your heart.

review_content: With over 200 movies based on or inspired by Bram Stokers novel ‘Dracula‘, director, producer and screenwriter Luc Besson did a very personal, light and you might say fresh or modern take on the source material. With a budget of appr. $ 52 Mio the movie earned worldwide $ 41 Mio.  On the positive side of this movie is the good camera work, the set design and costumes and the formidable score by composer Danny Elfman.  What’s really bad about this movie is the script, is the lack of intensity, is the feeling that I‘m watching a satire or comedy like ‘The Naked Gun‘ than a tense drama about loss, grieve, the eternal love and the conflict between the light and the dark. No, there are so many distracting scenes and dialogues in this movie that I almost think that Luc Besson did everything by purpose, transferring the story from 1480 A.D. to Paris, France 1880, making it a light entertainment for viewers, even geographically making France and Romania border neighbors.   I laughed a lot in this movie. If this was intended, well done Luc.  Unfortunately it’s a quite forgettable movie, not in the same league as other great movies from Luc Besson like ‘Nikita‘, ‘Léon‘, ‘The Fifth Element‘.  If you are into vampire themed movies, you might want to watch this one, too. Others, who love Francis Ford Coppolas ‘Bram Stokers Dracula‘ (1992) or John Badhams ‘Dracula’ (1979) can skip this one. A waste of time.

cast_names: Caleb Landry Jones, Zoë Bleu Sidel, Christoph Waltz, Matilda De Angelis, Ewens Abid, Guillaume de Tonquédec, David Shields, Bertrand-Xavier Corbi, Raphael Luce, Anne Kessler
review_content: yeah i am getting bored of all the ramakes of the 1992 version.  Hollywood need to make something different.  And adding Nosferatu vibes still doesn't it make it any better.

cast_names: Caleb Landry Jones, Zoë Bleu Sidel, Christoph Waltz, Matilda De Angelis, Ewens Abid, Guillaume de Tonquédec, David Shields, Bertrand-Xavier Corbi, Raphael Luce, Anne Kessler
review_content: It is probably the worst and most idiotic film ever made about Dracula. To begin with, Luc Besson must think that people haven’t seen Coppola’s version, because he copies it relentlessly, even the music. He over-explains everything as if the audience were stupid, and the film is full of mistakes and absurdities, like the perfume scene… It’s cringe-inducing to watch that part, or the ridiculous nonsense with the gargoyles.  Caleb Landry’s performance is just an over-the-top exaggeration of Oldman’s already heightened portrayal — which was wonderful, but here it’s simply embarrassing. By the way, who decided that Caleb is attractive? He looks as if Mick Jagger and Willem Dafoe had a child together and that child became a junkie. It’s a shame to see Christoph Waltz in such trash.

review_content: What a peculiar movie. So many things done great and as just as much done really poorly. On the good side there's the concept, the leading actor's performance, the garments, some of the locations (the castle exteriors), some visual effects... But then... what's with the casting of the leading actress and, specially, María? They are beautiful, of course, but looked like they belonged to the 21st century and no were else (lip injections,...) And over that, makeups and hairstyles (María has a smokey eyeshadow and perfectly molded ginger curls when she's clearly a brunette) that you definitely couldn't see at that time; the tone of the movie shift shifts from drama to comedy in a really random way; there are obvious inconsistencies (he bites half of the Versailles court??); some poor creative choices (scenes that drag or simply don't work, like the scene with the dances of the women that he attracts with his perfume) and weird script decisions (wouldn't have had more sense for the gargoyles to turn into lifeless stone than to children?). It's just a pity, it had potential to be much better. As a said, there are whole scenes that work wonderfully, but then...

review_content: Relay ended up being a great movie club pick and a reminder of why this genre is such a comfort zone for me. While the central twist is a bit predictable and there are some plot holes if you start pulling at the threads, the movie still delivers a tight, engaging ride. The pacing keeps things moving, the tension works, and it’s easy to stay invested even when you can see where some pieces are heading.  This is the kind of thriller that doesn’t need to reinvent the wheel to be effective. It hits familiar beats well and settles nicely into that “reliable, satisfying watch” category that I’ll always have a soft spot for. Not flawless, but absolutely enjoyable.  Rating: 3.5/5 – 80% – Would Recommend

review_content: Worst movie I have ever seen. They telegraphed the predictable double crossing from the beginning. It makes no sense that the team chasing Ash would be so emotional over failures, as they knew they had a patsy involved. Also the patsy wouldn’t be emotional while alone in her apartment. The plot element regarding the seeds business going public as a time deadlines… would have found the team (and subsequently the pharmaceutical company hiring them) liable for billions in defamation. And all of the action shots at the end where Ash (who somehow knows martial arts and can subdue men bigger than him, and also how to expertly fire weapons) is at times limping, at times running, then limping again. The entire action sequences at the end was of a lower quality than your average ChicagoPD episode. Also the weird political commentary that we should feel sad that the main character became a drunk because he was bullied after 9/11, despite him being neither a victim or perpetrator of that attack, is incredibly disrespectful to the actual victims. Worst movie I have ever seen.

review_content: Eddington struck me as an engaging, unsettling, and very uneven film. It holds your attention because Ari Aster knows how to load the atmosphere, tighten scenes, and create the feeling that everything is about to blow up. He sets it during the COVID-19 era, but not just to use the pandemic as background decoration. What really interests him is the fear, paranoia, conspiracies, and moral tension that ended up poisoning everything.    What works best is the way it portrays a world in which nobody seems to be listening to anybody else. Everything is filtered through phones, rhetoric, suspicion, and the need to be right. The film captures that feeling of collective madness quite well, that warped reality of a society trapped between public performance, private delusion, and constant conflict. That is where it has real bite and real venom.    Joaquin Phoenix once again proves how good he is at playing awkward, twisted, even ridiculous characters without ever becoming uninteresting. Pedro Pascal also brings strong presence, and together they carry a film that sometimes seems determined to sabotage itself. Aster still has a sharp eye for the sickly, the absurd detail, and the kind of tension that never fully settles down.    The problem is that it tries to do too many things at once. Political satire, black comedy, thriller, social portrait, violence, absurdism... and it does not always fit those elements together with the same precision. At times the mix works really well, but at others the film drifts, overindulges itself, and starts to feel excessive. You can tell it has ideas, but not all of them land equally well.    It also does not help that several characters feel more like symbols of a sick society than people with real inner life. That strengthens the larger reading, yes, but it also takes away some emotion. And in such a long film, so full of noise and agitation, you miss a little more humanity beneath all the bitterness and collective hysteria.    Even so, I liked it. I do not think it is a fully achieved work or Ari Aster’s best film, but it does have personality, tension, and a sharp enough view of a period that still feels raw. It is uneven, sometimes too pleased with itself, but also uncomfortable and forceful enough to be worth watching.

cast_names: Joaquin Phoenix, Deirdre O'Connell, Emma Stone, Micheal Ward, Pedro Pascal, Cameron Mann, Matt Gomez Hidaka, Luke Grimes, Amélie Hoeferle, Clifton Collins Jr.
review_content: Angry, aggrieved, impotent white guy goes on murderous rampage. I'm so f'ing sick of that depraved American cliche being offered up as "entertainment".   And how in the world do you take 2 hours of slow burn, interpersonal drama and stick that ending on it?   Writers: OK, guys. We've touched on all the major social issues.  Created a real ominous atmosphere with just dialog. Kudos to Jimmy who named our angry and aggrieved character "Cross" - that's ::chef's kiss::. How we going to wrap this thing up?   Studio Exec: How about a massive gun battle?  Writers: Huh? How? The movie is kind of down to just 1 guy at this point.   Studio Exec: Doesn't matter. Fly in some mercenaries.   Writers: You want us to have a massive gun battle with some nameless, faceless mercenaries that haven't been featured in this movie at all to this point?   Studio Exec: Perfect! And don't forget an explosion. Cars blowing up and body parts. All that shit.   Writers: Um....  Studio Exec: And a chase! Then the gun battle.   Writers: You want our elderly, asthmatic, full blown covid character to be chased through the desert and then he fights off a bunch of mercenaries?  Studio Exec: Brilliant. And can we give our hero a big f'ing gun? Like, some real Rambo kind of shit? A "say hello to my little friend" moment as he sprays bullets indiscriminately throughout the town?   Writers: Hero? (did he just call the sheriff a hero?)  Studio Exec: And some sex. That sells.   Writers: Our only female character at this point is the elderly mother-in-law.   Studio Exec: She'll do. Oh, don't forget to get Joaquin's cock on camera. It's in his contract.   Writers:....  Studio Exec: Great meeting, everybody! I smell box office gold.

cast_names: Joaquin Phoenix, Deirdre O'Connell, Emma Stone, Micheal Ward, Pedro Pascal, Cameron Mann, Matt Gomez Hidaka, Luke Grimes, Amélie Hoeferle, Clifton Collins Jr.
review_content: I went in with rock-bottom expectations, lowered even further by the half-assed COVID satire shoved in the viewer’s face in the opening scenes. For the entire first half, it’s a struggle to keep focused on what’s going on. The pacing is excruciatingly slow, with a grotesque humor that rarely gets biting. Then the film finally starts to pick up and pay off what it had set up in what previously seemed like pointless scenes. Similarly to “Beau is Afraid”, it suffers from an excess of confidence and a complete lack of restraint, but that’s probably part of the charm of these movies. It’s easy to see why it won’t work for many people, but at least it stands out for its peculiar way to portray a reality that still struggles to find its way onto the screen.

review_content: I didn't make it through the whole movie. I kept trying to figure out WHAT this movie was supposed to be. At first, I thought it was a gangster movie, but eventually I decided it was supposed to be a comedic parody. I started to laugh at scenes that were supposed to be serious because I thought they were purposefully bad. After reading reviews, apparently the movie is actually supposed to be a serious remake... Also, I was trying to figure out if they tried to make Denzel look really old. His face, his walk, etc... then I figured out he's actually 70. Props to him, but the character he's playing suffers because it seems he should be in his 40s or even 50s.  Spoilers that aren't really "spoiling" anything: [spoiler] the scene where the cops found some sort of "proof" and it was just a green headband??? When the son is returned and asks if the ransom is still going to be paid and Denzel responds "There's a whole room full of people who are trying to prevent that from happening". When the kidnapping happened and Denzel was on the phone and immediately a team of cops show up at his house and his wife is telling them to move flowers to specific parts of the room so they have an area to set up in. Or whenever Denzel get's in a conflict with another character, they almost immediately back down and support him. I thought all of these were supposed to jokes. [/spoiler]

cast_names: Denzel Washington, Jeffrey Wright, Ilfenesh Hadera, Elijah Wright, Aubrey Joseph, A$AP Rocky, John Douglas Thompson, LaChanze, Dean Winters, Wendell Pierce
review_content: Spike Lee has always been a director of boundless energy, someone capable of turning any story into an explosion of style, rhythm, and message. But in From Heaven to Hell, that spark seems to dim. There are flashes of his unmistakable touch —the vibrant camera work, the love letter to New York, the pulsing soundtrack— yet the film as a whole feels uneven, as if the story stumbles forward without ever truly pulling you in.    It starts strong, with a gripping premise and that urban-thriller vibe Lee handles better than anyone. However, as it goes on, the script scatters across too many ideas and twists that never quite connect. It’s visually striking, yes, but emotionally distant. You end up watching rather than feeling, and for a filmmaker who usually throws you right into the heart of his films, that’s surprising.    Denzel Washington, of course, elevates everything he touches. His presence remains magnetic, his performance as controlled and powerful as ever, yet even he can’t fully rescue the story once it loses its rhythm. He shines —as always— but in a film that doesn’t seem to know how to make the most of him.    There are interesting reflections on morality, power, and ambition, but the movie takes too long to reach them. What could have been a tense, vibrant thriller turns into something flat, saved only by flashes of inspiration and a final act that, though better, arrives too late.    From Heaven to Hell isn’t a failure, far from it, but it falls short of what one expects from the partnership between Spike Lee and Denzel Washington. It’s watchable, stylish, competently made —but missing the electricity that makes a good film unforgettable. In the end, it’s fine… just not soulful.

review_content: An interesting concept, even if i already saw several similar one.    Sadly it focuses less on investigating the wall itself than on the characters sad little dramas that feel way too present for little interest. Not only the wall, by the way, several events are just basically ignored.    The owner just [spoiler]videotaped everything in his secret room[/spoiler] ? Barely mentioned. His [spoiler]hands are cut off, so probably by the wall[/spoiler], no one cares, it's not even mentioned, even though it means he might have at least partially [spoiler]opened it[/spoiler]. Not clear how he died either.  The girl [spoiler]gets killed[/spoiler] ? Yeah ok, let's move on.    When they finally focus on the issue, it's just a sped up magic computer resolution. It was already stupid when in movies people typed two minutes on a keyboard and came up with a nice solution with UI and everything, now it's a full mobile app.  Also the [spoiler]number of flashes[/spoiler] was different the first time they tried it, not just the order. Maybe it's different per door ? I'm not sure they try it on the same door as Anton on the second try, but in the end, it's a different one anyway. Or maybe the [spoiler]number of flashes changes depending on the door, but the order to push the buttons stays the same [/spoiler] ? That would be even more stupid. Though a [spoiler]24 combination code[/spoiler] is already pretty stupid.    Clearly they should have worked a bit more on the actual interesting part of the scenario.    Weird, and convenient, for the movie, that the wall cuts the water and not the electricity. Yeah it would have been annoying if they spent the whole movie in the dark, but what was the point of focusing on the water being cut ?    The ending ([spoiler]just a defense mechanism that should not have been turned on[/spoiler]), is a nice idea, but then what ? No one [spoiler]from outside knows out to turn it down[/spoiler] ? Or is even trying apparently ?

cast_names: Matthias Schweighöfer, Ruby O. Fee, Frederick Lau, Salber Lee Williams, Murathan Muslu, Sira-Anna Faal, Axel Werner, Alexander Beyer, Josef Berousek, Daniele Rizzo
review_content: Our opening is filled with personal tragedy for our main characters, and they desperately need something new for their relationship to thrive. As bewildering as this is, it's actually kind of exciting in a bizarre way.  New characters enter the picture, and they're in the same situation, and they've been injured in their attempt at freedom.  Again, we've new characters, and they seem just as lost, and the terror of what's happening is strong to set in for them all.  With our now newest character, we see that all is not what it seems, and the deception and mayhem is upon them all now.   This is sci-fi at its most imaginative, and it's something entirely more than meet the eye, with technology playing the biggest role.

cast_names: Matthias Schweighöfer, Ruby O. Fee, Frederick Lau, Salber Lee Williams, Murathan Muslu, Sira-Anna Faal, Axel Werner, Alexander Beyer, Josef Berousek, Daniele Rizzo

Acting got a little better towards the middle, but that might just be the sunk cost fallacy talking. Writing though was bad throughout.

review_content: There’s something uniquely promising, and deeply unsettling, about the idea of confinement that the subgenre of enclosed-space thrillers has been tapping into for decades. It’s a setup that’s given us everything from thought-provoking movies like “Exam” to more stylized carnage like “Escape Room.” That’s exactly why it’s so frustrating to watch a film like “Brick” waste such an intriguing premise with an execution that desperately wants to feel clever but stumbles on bland archetypes, visually lifeless direction, and a kind of drama that feels artificial pretty much the whole time. This new German thriller from Netflix does make an effort to explore modern-day paranoia, fear of tech, and the breakdown of collective Unity, but it lacks what’s most essential for this kind of story: dramatic intensity, a real sense of urgency, and characters we actually care about.    The story kicks off with a solid hook: after a fight, a couple on the verge of breaking up suddenly finds that their apartment building has been sealed shut by an impenetrable black magnetic wall that blocks every possible exit. From there, “Brick” sets up a survival plot where a bunch of neighbors, total strangers until that point, have to work together to find a way out, navigating the building and unraveling the mystery behind this weird material surrounding them. Visually, the film has its moments: the look of the walls and the spaces slowly being taken over by the characters creates an immersive element, and some of the camerawork does a decent job of conveying the oppression and confusion. The problem is that this visual style rarely translates into any real tension. The atmosphere feels cold, not because that’s the thematic point, but because there’s just no emotional pulse driving it.    The biggest issue with “Brick” is that it leans on weak, predictable characters – most of them feel like cardboard cutouts with generic traits that fall apart under even the slightest emotional weight. The central couple, played by Matthias Schweighöfer and Ruby O. Fee, kind of has the makings of a compelling dynamic, dealing with grief and emotional exhaustion, but the way their conflict unfolds is flat and often way too heavy on exposition. As for the rest of the supporting cast, it feels like they were picked out of a cliché catalog: the paranoid junkie, the sweet old man, the aggressive, ripped conspiracy theorist. Everything’s so functional and by-the-numbers that even when the script tries to shake things up (like playing with the antagonist’s motives or blurring lines between reality and paranoia), it just feels like recycled ideas from thrillers that were way bolder.    Even when the suspense ramps up toward what should be a tense climax, the movie holds itself back too much. With a story like this, you expect the claustrophobia to actually make you feel something – sweaty palms, heart racing, maybe even a bit of panic. But “Brick” seems almost afraid to go there. When violence happens, it’s clean, sterile, like the film doesn’t want to commit to either horror or thriller territory. Add in a forgettable score, limp editing, and a third act that tries to tie up earlier mysteries in a neat but uninspired way, and what you get is a movie that had glimmers of a great idea but never delivers anything with real impact.    In the end, “Brick” wants badly to be a modern dystopian parable, a symbolic take on individualism and paranoia, while also appealing to survival-thriller fans with puzzles and twists. But what we’re left with is a film that never fully commits to any of those ideas. Like the fly it keeps showing trapped in a glass (its most obvious metaphor), “Brick” just spins around in circles, making some noise but never truly disturbing or leaving any real impression.

review_content: 'The Pickup' ended up being better than I thought it was going to be early on. Still not a good movie, mind you. Obviously the writing et al. lets this one down just as much, but it was more visually that made me care less about it - it's an ugly film, especially 'inside' that damn truck.    The world of film didn't feel lived in either, namely on that long highway; I know they mention it'll be a "ghost town" but that's a cop-out. The pairing, meanwhile, of Eddie Murphy and Pete Davidson is narrowly passable, albeit arguably. Davidson is the better, Murphy underwhelms.    Keke Palmer is the star of this particular show, her performance gave the flick the required energy anyway. Eva Longoria is alright, at least for the forgettable role that she is given. The characters of Jack Kesy and Ismael Cruz Córdova are exactly that too, despite having potential to be decent.    It's the ending that stopped me from disliking this 2025 release. It holds enough action that I was satisifed with what I was watching. There was some hope for this, I could see glimpses of goodness throughout (e.g. that Clark gag is good), but all in all it's real meh.

review_content: Yeah… these movies do absolutely nothing for me. I thought maybe the second film could at least avoid all the flaws the first movie had, but it didn’t. I had the exact same issues here that I had with the first film. It was super messy and I just didn’t enjoy it at all.  Look, I’m not going to say too much about this movie, as otherwise I’m gonna say the exact same things that I said about the last one. I will say though that at least this was a little more creepier, as those Marionette dolls are pure nightmare fuel. I wish we saw a lot more of them though because they were saving the movie for me and were easily the best part of this.  Okay I know I’ve already said how the movie didn’t avoid the flaws from the last one, but I guess I’m happy they didn’t overdo the whole dream sequences again. They were easily the weakest part of the first movie, so i’m glad it didn’t happen again because I would’ve turned this off straight away.   So first we had Stu and now we’ve got freaking Billy Loomis. Okay I’ll admit that was quite cool how they brought two of the most iconic Slashers ever (well to me anyway) into the same exact film again, and of course they were business partners. It sucked how Skeet Ulrich was only in like one scene though.  I do think this movie was ever so slightly better than the last film (and that’s a stretch by the way) but it’s still not a great movie overall. I really don’t get why people actually like these movies as 80 odd percent on Rotten Tomatoes is actually CRAZY.   I’m gonna give it another 5 out of 10 as it simply just wasn’t for me. It’s funny how it’s called Five Nights At Freddy’s and all I’ve given them is fives, I promise I didn’t do that intentionally.  5/10
