review_content: For me, Five Nights at Freddy's 2 was another win for the franchise. To be honest I was grinning the whole time because it builds so perfectly on the first movie while cranking up the scares and lore. The new animatronics look incredible with those upgraded practical effects, and the 1987 pizzeria setting feels even creepier and more claustrophobic than the original location. Josh Hutcherson returns as Mike, bringing the same heart and desperation, and the expanded cast adds great layers.  The jump scares hit harder, the atmosphere is thick with dread, and all the fan-service Easter eggs and timeline nods had me geeking out - total nostalgia overload from playing the games back in the day. It's darker, bloodier, and more ambitious without losing what made the first one special.  I really hope there's a Part 3 down the line since there's so much story left to tell, and this sequel has me hooked for whatever nightmare comes next.

cast_names: Josh Hutcherson, Piper Rubio, Elizabeth Lail, Matthew Lillard, Freddy Carter, Wayne Knight, Mckenna Grace, David Andrew Calvillo, Teo Briones, Audrey Lynn-Marie
review_content: Below average. The biggest problem with Five Nights 2 is the plot, which feels rushed, confused, and poorly thought out. The story jumps between ideas without explaining any of them, as if it assumes the audience already knows what is happening or simply will not care.   Characters make baffling decisions that exist only to move the movie toward the next scare, not because they make sense as people. Motivations change scene to scene, backstory is teased but never clearly delivered, and important rules of the world are introduced and then ignored. Instead of building mystery, the film dumps vague lore and expects it to feel deep. The timeline is muddy, the stakes are unclear, and by the time the movie reaches its climax, it feels unearned.   Scenes meant to be emotional fall flat because the script never gives the audience a reason to connect to anyone. The ending is especially frustrating, wrapping nothing up while still trying to set up future sequels. It feels less like a complete story and more like a messy collection of ideas stretched into a movie.

review_content: Tyler Perry is one of those directors who tends to split audiences, but with “Straw,” he delivers a film that, while still slipping into some of the over-the-top habits we’ve seen in his other work, carries a raw emotional and social punch that’s hard to ignore. The story follows Janiyah (played with fierce intensity by Taraji P. Henson), a single mom whose day goes from bad to nightmarish, spiraling into a tragic misunderstanding that paints her, in the eyes of the media and the police, as an armed criminal about to commit a violent act. From the start, the script moves at a frantic, suffocating pace, throwing Janiyah into a storm of disasters that could’ve come off as far-fetched, if they weren’t so deeply rooted in the social commentary the film wants to make: the quiet collapse of an ordinary woman up against a system that’s never been on her side.    Visually, “Straw” is one of Perry’s most polished films to date. Justyn Moro’s cinematography ditches the artificial gloss that drags down a lot of Perry’s earlier work, opting instead for a bleak, oppressive color palette that heightens the claustrophobia and sense of hopelessness. Nick Coker’s editing also keeps things tight and tense, even when the script stumbles into some unnecessary twists. It’s clear that Perry still struggles with reigning in his narrative impulses, there are some jarring plot twists, flat secondary characters, and dialogue that hammers its message a little too hard. But these flaws are softened by the sheer force of Henson’s performance. She carries the movie with a raw, gut-punch intensity, flipping seamlessly between despair, rage, and vulnerability with devastating authenticity.    There’s something powerful about how “Straw” manages to turn melodrama into a form of protest. Janiyah’s journey may be over-the-top at times, but it hits hard because it reflects a reality that countless poor Black women in the U.S. face every day. The film isn’t going for realism in the strictest sense, it’s more interested in painting a heightened, even theatrical portrait of what it feels like to be judged, dismissed, and discarded by a society that demands endless resilience from people already carrying way too much. And in that way, the movie’s strongest moments aren’t necessarily the final twists or the high-stakes standoff, but the quieter ones where Janiyah’s rage and exhaustion are allowed to take center stage.    “Straw” may not be a perfectly balanced drama, but it’s a film that’s got something to say, and it says it with the urgency and messiness of a scream. Its power lies in the discomfort it creates and the questions it raises about how and why a woman gets framed as a threat when all she’s looking for is a shred of dignity. It’s flawed, no doubt, but also necessary in its fury and painfully effective in the way it holds up a mirror to an America that still demands heroic survival from its most marginalized citizens.

cast_names: Taraji P. Henson, Sherri Shepherd, Teyana Taylor, Sinbad, Rockmond Dunbar, Ashley Versher, Mike Merrill, Glynn Turman, Shalèt Monique, Diva Tyler
review_content: Harta is one of those films that grabs you by the gut and doesn’t let go, even when you’re fully aware it’s pulling every string to manipulate you. It’s not subtle, and it doesn’t try to be—and maybe that’s why it works in certain moments. The story of a woman pushed to the edge by a system that seems to take pleasure in crushing her is as painful as it is necessary.    Taraji P. Henson carries the film entirely on her shoulders. Her performance is full of quiet fury, vulnerability, and dignity. You can’t help but empathize with her, even when the script forces her into unlikely or overly dramatic situations. She makes you care, even when the film itself sometimes forgets how to do it.    Tyler Perry, once again, tries to say many things. Some land, others miss. At times, the message gets lost in melodrama or clumsy dialogue that insists on explaining everything rather than letting the emotion speak. Still, some scenes work—especially towards the end—because they hit deep.    Visually, it’s safe, but emotionally it feels honest. In a year full of cold, calculated releases, this bluntness feels refreshing. Even when it goes too far, there’s something genuine in the pain it channels—something that speaks to a quiet anger many people carry.    Harta isn’t perfect. Far from it. But when a film makes you cry out of sheer frustration, even through its flaws, it means something. That kind of impact doesn’t fade easily.

cast_names: Jeanne Goursaud, Dougray Scott, Lera Abova, Emanuel Fellmer, Annabelle Mandeng, Kayode Akinyemi, Nina Liu, Susanne Michel, Kris Saddler, Anon Mall
review_content: Without reinventing the genre, Extraterritorial delivers exactly what it sets out to: an exciting, well-crafted action film that holds your attention from start to finish. It's especially refreshing to see a strong, determined, and charismatic woman leading the story—not as a gimmick or statement, but simply because she’s the best person for the job. And she proves it in every scene.    The plot strikes a solid balance between tension, mystery, and pacing. While the villain doesn’t break any molds—yes, he’s the classic by-the-book baddie—the overall narrative doesn’t suffer. Everything flows naturally, and it’s clear the main goal here is to entertain. And in that, it succeeds.    It may not have any unforgettable scenes or jaw-dropping twists, but as a solid piece of escapist cinema, Extraterritorial holds up well. The action sequences are handled competently, there are a couple of genuinely effective moments, and the film stays on track even when the script wobbles a bit.    All in all, it’s a movie that delivers a good time. Nothing more, nothing less—but done with energy and craft. And sometimes, that’s exactly what you need.

review_content: Emily Bader brought a sort of magic to the screen with this one. But, Poppy was annoying right from the start. I cannot stand people who drag others around like rags while figuring out what they want. Thank goodness Sarah dodged a massive bullet (even though it took several years of being emotionally cheated upon). Poppy is the sort of friend who NO girlfriend would want a 1000 feet within their guy. She is with a new guy every summer (or maybe even more often) and still keeps staring at Alex's lips. As for their so-called chemistry, I never quite felt it fervently. It always felt like some adultery popping up on Poppy's uncertain wonderland of fantasies where people are all toys for her whimsies. I don't personally understand this whole point of going on duo trips while you have got partners. There were no lines to begin with—to not know where they got blurred—and they have wronged every single one of their partners. As charming as Tom Blyth was in the start, I lost all respect as he slowly turned less and less loyal to Sarah, all while in denial. The proposal was unjustifiably the worst sin. Horrible character arcs—oh, well it feels realistic? Well, then people absolutely suck and I have lost all faith in romance.  Nevertheless, the movie set was as charismatic and enchanting as it could get. The destinations were alluring and the cinematography just flowed beautifully. The tracks were in good favour. Even though Poppy and Alex's destin(y)ation sucked, their entire journey also just sucked. Two people who should have never run into each other—mesmerizing visual creation, repulsive un-love story.

review_content: This was a huge surprise for me and basically came out of nowhere. I popped on Netflix in hopes of finding something to watch and decided to go with this. I’m extremely happy I decided to give this one a chance. I’m pretty sure my beautiful girlfriend watched this just a few weeks ago. The funny thing is certain aspects of this movie reminded me of us.  I’ve always been a complete sucker for a good romantic comedy and this delivered. I really liked how they developed the story and the way it was told. The chemistry between Emily Bader and Tom Blyth was excellent. I really enjoyed the dialogue between the two of them. I laughed a decent amount and really enjoyed my time with this one. It’s a lot of fun to watch the dynamic and friendship build between the two leads. It’s definitely slow at moments but was building a great relationship. It honestly felt pretty realistic and refreshing to watch. I really enjoyed this one a ton. I love when you find a hidden gem every once in a while.

review_content: I came into this movie with low expectations, and while it's not some ground breaking achievement or even something I'd ever watch again, I had fun.     Pros  - The special effects were well done  - Some creative and gory kills!   - Didn't drag on too long     Cons   - Because Ben was a guy in a suit and animatronics, you don't see fast or erratic movement like you'd see from a real chimp.  Most of the movie he sits in one spot or moves very slow (for a chimp)   - The premise is kind of silly, but that's sort of the point    There are complaints from some folks about the characters making bad decisions.  That's accurate, but also, thats like Horror 101.

review_content: People making poor low IQ level decisions in horror movies I can forgive, it's a trope of genre. But chimp infected with rabies, a virus that essentially destroys any intelligence there is in the brain, behaving like only it's aggression was dialed up to 12 while still retaining most of its mental capacity and motor skills intact, that is where I draw the line... they could've slapped sci-fi label and used some alien or undiscovered virus to explain chimps behavior, but they didn't do that...

cast_names: Johnny Sequoyah, Jessica Alexander, Troy Kotsur, Victoria Wyant, Gia Hunter, Benjamin Cheng, Charlie Mann, Tienne Simon, Miguel Hernando Torres Umba, Amina Abdi
review_content: As a survival thriller, the movie works really well. The tension, brutality, and constant sense of danger keep the adrenaline high throughout. It’s intense and entertaining if you’re looking for a fast, suspenseful ride.  However, the portrayal of rabies-infected chimpanzees feels far from realistic. Chimps are incredibly intelligent animals, but the level of planning and behavior shown in the film pushes well beyond what feels believable.  So if you watch it purely as a survival thriller — for the tension, action, and suspense — it’s a solid experience. Just don’t go in expecting scientific accuracy or realism.

review_content: I really wasn't expecting this to be as good as it was. I understand why people are calling it silly goofy, but honestly, it surpasses most low-budget horror films I've seen. The idea feels refreshing, although giving away a pivotal plot point in the opening sequence feels nonsensical. Otherwise, I'd go as far as to call it brilliant, which is rare for films that play on clichés. The acting/casting was great, and the story/pacing was just as captivating as the insanely uncanny practicals/visuals/directing. As far as entertainment goes, seeing Ben go Koba-level sadistic for 90 mins was bananas.

cast_names: Lee Byung-hun, Son Ye-jin, Park Hee-soon, Lee Sung-min, Yeom Hye-ran, Cha Seung-won, Oh Dal-su, Lee Suk-hyeong, Kim Hyeong-mook, Woo Jeong-won
review_content: Park Chan-wook is truly a master at making films that defy genre classification. And I really don't know what he's done to the Academy that his works are regularly ignored at the Oscars.    “No Other Choice” is a socio-critical dark comedy in which an unemployed skilled worker feels he has no other choice but to go down a dark path in order to get back on track. And many other characters also have this feeling of “having no other choice,” which is, of course, nonsense at its core. Because there are so many moments when other options are obvious, but they simply don't want to be seen. Ultimately, the spiral of escalation continues, leading to some truly hilarious moments.    Furthermore, as you would expect, the film looks fantastic and features strong performances. And then there's the very effective use of music, which is partly responsible for the best scene in the entire film. Last but not least, the story also gives the viewer a lot to think about, especially when it comes to the impact of automation and AI on our working lives. In this regard, it's definitely worth watching the film more than once. From me, “No Other Choice” definitely gets a strong recommendation.

cast_names: Lee Byung-hun, Son Ye-jin, Park Hee-soon, Lee Sung-min, Yeom Hye-ran, Cha Seung-won, Oh Dal-su, Lee Suk-hyeong, Kim Hyeong-mook, Woo Jeong-won
review_content: From the outset, the film poses an uncomfortable question: how far are we willing to go for our family? And from there, it delves into increasingly murky territory, combining film noir with a very dark humor that makes you laugh one moment and leaves you feeling quite uneasy the next.    Technically, it's a joy to watch. The editing, photography, and camera work are meticulously crafted, with constant attention to detail evident in every shot. Everything is designed to create atmosphere, to make you uncomfortable without needing to spell it out, and in that sense, the film plays in a very high league.    It's impossible not to be reminded of Parasite on more than one occasion, both in terms of tone and the way certain social and family dynamics are portrayed. The comparison is inevitable, although No Other Choice has its own personality and opts for a somewhat slower pace, perhaps even too slow in some sections, which may test the patience of more than a few viewers.    Where there is no doubt is in the acting. The protagonist, Lee Byung-hun, is absolutely impressive, carrying much of the emotional weight of the story. At his side, Son Ye-jin shines with a restrained and very powerful performance, forming a leading couple that elevates the film several notches.    Overall, No Other Choice is a dark, elegant, and very well-made thriller that simmers slowly and leaves a lasting impression, although it is not exactly a comfortable experience or suitable for all audiences.

cast_names: Lee Byung-hun, Son Ye-jin, Park Hee-soon, Lee Sung-min, Yeom Hye-ran, Cha Seung-won, Oh Dal-su, Lee Suk-hyeong, Kim Hyeong-mook, Woo Jeong-won

cast_names: Lee Byung-hun, Son Ye-jin, Park Hee-soon, Lee Sung-min, Yeom Hye-ran, Cha Seung-won, Oh Dal-su, Lee Suk-hyeong, Kim Hyeong-mook, Woo Jeong-won
review_content: [IFFR '26] Park Chan-wook's virtuosity makes the film far darker than the novel or Costa-Gavras's adaptation, yet simultaneously so utterly hilarious that even Lee Byung-hun's memorable performance feels like an extension of the slapstick comedy characters. The dehumanization wrought by capitalism has become more tangible, and thus the film takes on a far more perverse tone. Psychology speaks of "workplace death" when referring to the process of adapting to being laid off, which also requires a period of mourning. Taking these ideas to their ultimate conclusion, this entertaining comedy thriller offers profound commentary on the fate of humankind in a dehumanized society.

cast_names: Shane Jensen, Larry Fessenden, Stuart Rudin, Anya Krawcheck
review_content: Good Boy had me hooked from the premise alone. A creepy, dog-centric horror-thriller with a lonely guy and his increasingly unsettling canine companion. The setup promises solid psychological unease and some clever twists on pet-owner dynamics, and early on it delivers genuine tension and a few effective chills.    Unfortunately, the story never quite lives up to its potential. The plot feels underdeveloped, with character motivations that stay surface-level and a third act that rushes through ideas without earning them. The CGI in the final sequences also disappoints—it's noticeably low-budget and pulls you out of the immersion at the worst possible moment.    That said, the real MVP is Indy the dog, whose performance (and those soulful, eerie stares) carries huge chunks of the film. The practical effects with the pup are strong, and the animal acting is genuinely impressive—Indy sells the unease better than almost anything else on screen.    It's a mixed bag with real promise that doesn't fully deliver, but if you're a dog lover who can forgive some rough edges, it's watchable.

cast_names: Shane Jensen, Larry Fessenden, Stuart Rudin, Anya Krawcheck
review_content: The initial idea behind Good Boy is, on paper, quite interesting. It has that slightly different premise that makes you think something disturbing, or at least original, might come of it. The problem is that the film stays there: at the idea stage. It doesn't know how to develop it or take it anywhere really powerful.    As a horror film, it doesn't work at all. It's boring, it's not scary, and it doesn't generate any tension. To top it all off, the sound editing is quite unpleasant, more annoying than disturbing, which ends up taking you out of the film instead of drawing you in.    Visually and narratively, it's very obvious that this is a very low-budget production, and not exactly one that knows how to make the most of its limitations. Everything feels a bit poor and flat, lacking spark and ambition.    That said, there is one element that clearly stands out: the dog. Indy is a real delight. His expressions are so expressive that they border on human, and you end up genuinely suffering for what happens to him on screen. In fact, as a curiosity, the filming lasted no less than 400 days spread over three years, precisely because of the difficulty of working with a dog actor, and you can see the love and patience behind his performance.

review_content: This is one of those films that you are not sure if this is something supernatural or we follow this dogs perception of his masters decent into "madness". The fun of the film, though slowly paced - but I think that helps make the tension build - is trying to figure out which way they are taking you - is it supernatural entities coming. after them or are we just seeing on film what Indy is "feeling" or "perceiving".    An enjoyable watch for me.  Definitely better than average and I applaud the attempt that the film makers made here to do something a little bit different.

It becomes clear that the Company takes the child’s memories and sends An-na into space so she can create the perfect mother. Sadly, An-na dies, and in her final wish she decides to experiment with her own memories to build the ideal synthetic mom.  She does this almost like playing a video game, trying to become a better person in each simulation. After nearly 15,000 experiments, she finally succeeds—especially when she shows true empathy, such as with the girl trapped in the elevator and the pregnant woman.  At that point, the Company completes the test and sends the spaceships back to Earth. [/spoiler]   And the actors were fantastic too.

review_content: If you go into The Great Flood looking for a straightforward, high-stakes disaster flick, prepare for a disappointment. What should have been a tense survival story instead collapses into a convoluted and over-complex mess. The film tries way too hard to be "clever" with its narrative structure, but it ultimately fails to stick the landing, leaving the viewer more confused than engaged. Instead of leaning into the spectacle and scale of the disaster, it gets bogged down in its own high-concept ambitions. It’s a frustrating watch that overthinks itself right out of being enjoyable—a classic case of a movie losing its way by trying to be something it’s not.

review_content: I love the plot. I love that this isn’t a typical end-of-the-world kind of movie.  Here’s how I interpreted it: Ja-In was a real AI baby, and Anna was a real human at the beginning. The first flood we see actually happened in real time. That event forced Anna to leave Ja-In behind because she had to complete her mission.  All the scenes of Anna and Ja-In that come after are not happening in real time — they exist within memory data that has developed its own intelligence. This shows that the AI’s emotions are already functioning on a deeper level.  Which also means that, in real time, both Anna and Ja-In are already dead. Anna died during the mission, while Ja-In died after being left behind in the apartment — specifically in the closet where Anna told him to wait for her.  That’s why, at the end, Ja-In says he waited a very long time for his mom to come back. That memory reflects his final moments. Anna never returned in real time, but she managed to return within the memory data.  At some point, Anna’s consciousness reached what I assume was a passable threshold — enough to trigger the system to reproduce clones of both of them.  Personally, this movie gave me strong Interstellar vibes — emotional, quiet, and heavy in a way that lingers. I really enjoyed it.

cast_names: Renate Reinsve, Stellan Skarsgård, Inga Ibsdotter Lilleaas, Elle Fanning, Anders Danielsen Lie, Jesper Christensen, Lena Endre, Cory Michael Smith, Catherine Cohen, Andreas Stoltenberg Granerud
review_content: One of my first impressions of Sentimental Value touches on something I've noticed across films and TV shows from countries in the North (the US, Canada, and Europe) where there tends to be a strong devotion to the father figure, while the mother is often portrayed as bad, incapable, or mentally ill. In contrast, films and shows from the South (Latin America, Asia, and I guess parts of Africa) tend to place that devotion on the mother, while the father is typically absent, a poor parent, and someone who never takes accountability for his actions. I've read on X and heard from friends that they didn't connect with this movie because Stellan Skarsgård's character is a role we're already used to seeing in Latin American stories, so it feels nothing new to us, but I do believe it's a challenging role for Northern audiences, a behavior that defies their social norms. That's the lens through which I'm basing this review.  I really like how this movie starts like a documentary, or someone giving you a lecture about what they've discovered, in a way that helps you understand the meaning of home. It reminded me of how Interstellar begins. Joachim Trier seems to consistently look at trauma, mental illness, or psychological patterns and build a film around them, and in this case, I believe the focus is the trauma that parents inflict on their children, and how a lack of communication, and any intention to address one's feelings, results in traumatized adults who don't know how to handle certain emotions, especially when those emotions are triggered by their parents or situations involving them.  [spoiler]I never heard Stellan's character say sorry for his years of absence. He simply reappears after his ex-wife dies, and[/spoiler] the story unfolds from there, a father who shows no remorse for what he did, yet seems conflicted about how to communicate with his older daughter. With her, at least, he senses something is wrong. But years of silence are probably the main reason they can't connect. Knowing he doesn't know how to act around her, [spoiler]he never attends any of her theater performances[/spoiler], and that only deepens the trauma Renate's character already carries toward him. She is angry, furious, and hurt, because he acts as though nothing happened. That's a behavior we often see in older parents, you can never change the way they act or think, it’s frustrating, but it's the reality, and it's remarkable how truthfully this film portrays it and how well it's written.  The most interesting thread is how the phrase _"like father, like daughter"_ plays out here. [spoiler]Both the father and the older daughter have gone through deeply painful experiences [/spoiler]that prevent them from fully connecting with each other. What makes this so compelling is the idea that if you never open up to your parents or your children about what's weighing on you, you'll never know that someone so close to you may have gone through something remarkably similar. And if you had given yourself that chance to talk, to share, the story might have been entirely different. I think this is one of the film's core messages, that never addressing our feelings distances us from others (it doesn’t makes us stronger as some may think), and possibly from healing itself. Because who doesn't feel relief when they can finally get something off their chest?  One thing that did bother me or upset me is the ending, [spoiler]where the father's own trauma becomes something of a justification, or even a form of redemption, for everything he put his older daughter through after the divorce,[/spoiler] but I suppose that at a certain point, when our parents are old, we have to give way or make peace with the fact that they will never truly acknowledge their mistakes. And in "understanding" a possible reason for why they acted the way they did, we find something like acceptance that may heal our own pain and demons, because we are reflections of our parents, and knowing they acted wrong, we choose not to become that kind of person.  **This is honestly one of the best films from 2025, alongside No Other Choice.**

cast_names: Renate Reinsve, Stellan Skarsgård, Inga Ibsdotter Lilleaas, Elle Fanning, Anders Danielsen Lie, Jesper Christensen, Lena Endre, Cory Michael Smith, Catherine Cohen, Andreas Stoltenberg Granerud
review_content: Sentimental Value is about the distance people keep from themselves and each other. Stellan Skarsgård is vulnerable, flawed, and completely real. The whole cast matches him. There’s a sorrow running through this film that feels inherited, something the characters carry without knowing how to put down. The cinematography mirrors that, shifting between intimate and cold, detached and warm. The mix of Norwegian and English adds to the authenticity and plays with it in ways that feel natural and deliberate. It portrays complex, uncomfortable, often unbearable emotions with a honesty that’s hard to look away from. I connected deeply with that feeling of running from something in your own head. The most surprising moments come from the most unexpected connections.  It’s heavy but balanced with dark humour that earns its place and eased my emotional outpouring. The pacing runs a touch long, but I walked away feeling resonant and moved.

review_content: "Code 3" is exactly the kind of film you rarely find: **educational, funny, and frightening all at once**. The mix of realistic operations, dry humor, and the absurd yet relatable dangers of everyday life makes the movie a genuine experience. You laugh at the quirky moments but are also confronted with harsh reality—all without artificial exaggeration. The chemistry between the characters is spot-on, and the action scenes are so gripping that they truly pull you in.

cast_names: Rainn Wilson, Lil Rel Howery, Aimee Carrero, Yvette Nicole Brown, Rob Riggle, Page Kennedy, Xolo Maridueña, Eric Jungmann, Ayesha Harris, Luka Jones
review_content: _Code 3_ aims to blend dark comedy with a grounded exploration of paramedic work, but ends up failing at both. Instead of balance, the film leans into shock value and heavy-handed messaging, leaving little room for nuance or genuine insight.    The comedic side is especially unpleasant. Much of the humor revolves around vomit, blood, grotesque injuries, and death — exaggerated to the point of being almost unwatchable. I understand the intention to highlight the brutal, chaotic aspects of emergency response, but when you show only the extremes, without contrast or depth, it becomes meaningless. By that logic, should films about caregivers in nursing homes be nothing but urine and feces?    The film also attempts to critique low wages, burnout, and lack of respect for paramedics. But instead of integrating these themes naturally, _Code 3_ delivers them with the subtlety of a brick: blunt dialogue, on-the-nose exposition, even big numbers flashed on screen. It’s more of a lecture than a narrative.    To make matters worse, the doctor in the hospital is portrayed with cartoonish hostility — unreasonably rude, dismissive, and exaggerated to the point of parody. This completely undermines the grounded tone the film is trying to build. When one part of the world is hyper-realistic and another feels like a bad sketch comedy character, the internal consistency collapses.    The acting is adequate. Rainn Wilson stands out, though largely because the role feels written for his established persona rather than giving him space to show something new.    _Code 3_ had the potential to be sharp, grounded, and darkly funny. Instead, it’s overbearing, messy, and oddly shallow — more focused on gross-out gags and soapbox speeches than delivering a meaningful or compelling story.

review_content: I'm not sure how to rate this film.  It wasn't an easy watch.  This took me weeks to finish and I wanted to turn it off.  Not because it's bad, but because Tim is a good actor.  This typed, outside of Tim's performance, I don't understand all of the story... there must be a long story prior to the film beginning that the audience can only guess at.    While taken to absurd lengths, part of what makes this film uncomfortable is how relatable many of the scenes are - sometimes from multiple perspectives.    This is an interesting film, although the awkwardness is so strong it almost hurts.  I wasn't able to predict the story.  I kept thinking it would go in one direction, but then it went in another.  I did hope more parallels would be drawn between Craig (Tim) and Austin (Paul).  A few times, it seemed as if that was an angle that was being explored (that Austin was just a few steps removed from Craig, which I think is a societal truth of sorts).    Anyhoo.  I finished it.  It was painful.  I now feel for Craig's family.  After everything that happened, it seemed like he was given a second chance... and then.  Did the end actually happen?  What actually happened in the film?  At the beginning, Craig seemed mostly normal... and then just went off the rails over the course of the film.  I can interpret this in a variety of ways, but...    I almost want to watch this a second time.  I won't.  It didn't feel great.  All this typed, I'm looking forward to The Chair Company (Tim Robinson's next project, which airs in a few days).

review_content: With Tim Robinson and Paul Rudd sharing the spotlight, Friendship was always going to have a certain level of charm baked in—and it delivers. Personally, I really enjoyed the movie; Robinson’s absurd, offbeat comedic style meshes surprisingly well with Rudd’s more affable, grounded persona. The result is a film that feels both quirky and likable, with plenty of funny moments that play to each actor’s strengths.    That said, Robinson’s style of humor is divisive, and this movie doesn’t really do much to win over those who aren’t already on board. If his brand of comedy isn’t for you, Friendship probably won’t change your mind. But for fans, or those open to his style, it’s a very entertaining and enjoyable ride.    Rating: 4/5 – 85% – Would Recommend

review_content: "Another Simple Favor" is a blend of dark comedy and mystery. It follows Stephanie, a video-blogging mom who starts investigating the mysterious disappearance of her friend Emily. As the story unfolds, secrets come to light and the characters’ relationships grow more tangled and complex.  The plot is engaging and full of unexpected twists, with a sharp, satirical tone that makes even the darker moments enjoyable. The performances are excellent—Blake Lively delivers a cold and captivating portrayal of Emily, while Anna Kendrick shines as the quirky and determined Stephanie.  The direction is well-executed, and the fitting soundtrack helps amplify the film’s mix of mystery and humor.  My rating: 7/10  A smart, entertaining film perfect for fans of stories that combine mystery with dark comedy. Definitely worth a watch if you enjoy films with secrets and surprises.

cast_names: Susan Lorincz
review_content: “The Perfect Neighbor” is a documentary that understands that sometimes the most devastating way to tell a story is simply not to interfere with it. Geeta Gandbhir builds the film out of existing footage, mostly police body cams, and that choice goes far beyond a stylistic gimmick. It becomes the very core of the experience. By removing formal interviews, explanatory narration, or any kind of dramatized reconstruction, the film puts the viewer in an uncomfortable position. There’s no mediation and no safe distance. We’re just there, watching interactions that at first feel routine but slowly reveal a disturbing pattern of hostility, exhaustion, and neglect.    The film’s biggest strength lies in how it handles time. Instead of jumping straight to the tragedy in a sensationalist way, Gandbhir focuses on accumulation. Every police visit, every seemingly mundane conversation, every recorded call adds another layer of inevitability. You start to realize, even before the outcome, that something is deeply wrong and that no one is managing to stop the cycle. That gradual build is what makes the impact so powerful. There’s no sudden shock, just a slow emotional compression, like the film is tightening its grip until everything finally breaks.    Another key element is how the documentary establishes the community itself. Before the violence happens, the film spends time observing everyday life in the neighborhood. Kids playing, adults chatting, neighbors who know and support each other. This isn’t just background texture but a crucial dramatic element. By humanizing the space and the people in it, the film makes the tragedy feel tangible and real. When the rupture comes, it doesn’t land as an isolated event but as the destruction of a fragile balance that had been slowly eroded by obsessive and disproportionate behavior.    The constant use of body cam footage intensifies that sense of urgency. There’s something especially unsettling about watching situations this serious through recordings that are supposed to function as bureaucratic documentation. The camera doesn’t beautify anything. It doesn’t pick angles or craft visual language. It just records. And that lack of traditional aesthetics makes everything heavier. Conversations, silences, tones of voice, tired looks. Nothing is filtered. You’re not watching a narrative engineered for emotion but a reality that imposes itself with brutal force.    By the time the film reaches the central moment of the tragedy, the impact doesn’t come from surprise but from confirmation. Gandbhir doesn’t need dramatic tricks because the weight has already been built. What makes it so infuriating is the clarity that none of it came out of nowhere. The film constructs, with almost clinical precision, the sense that there were enough warning signs, enough calls for help, enough visible exhaustion. That awareness makes the documentary even more painful because it shifts the discussion away from chance and toward collective responsibility.    The aftermath is given real space, and the film is smart not to turn the ending into a false sense of closure. Instead of chasing catharsis, it lingers in the reverberations. The grief, the silence, the presence of children, the emptiness that settles in. That emotional extension prevents the viewer from treating the case like just another story consumed and forgotten. The documentary offers no relief and doesn’t attempt to tidy the world back up after the tragedy. It leaves the wounds open.    It’s also striking how the film handles the central figure at the heart of the conflict. Without condemning narration or fiery speeches, Gandbhir lets the images speak for themselves. That choice makes everything even more unsettling because it removes guided interpretation. The viewer isn’t handed conclusions. You witness behaviors, hear the words, observe reactions. It’s a kind of storytelling that fully trusts the audience’s intelligence and also the disturbing power of reality when it’s presented without decoration.    In the end, “The Perfect Neighbor” is a difficult documentary not just because of what it shows, but because of how it shows it. Its strength lies in refusing to soften, over-explain, or turn pain into spectacle. By shaping real footage into a clear and relentless narrative progression, the film creates an experience that angers, saddens, and lingers. It isn’t interested in comfort or elegant moral closure, but in bearing witness. The film stays with you precisely because of its brutal honesty. It’s cinema that unsettles because it doesn’t try to be bigger than the facts. It simply lays them bare, and that alone is enough to devastate.

cast_names: Susan Lorincz
review_content: “The Perfect Neighbor” is a hard-hitting documentary that is difficult to watch. The film makes do without talking head sequences, relying instead almost entirely on police footage from body cameras and, of course, on editing and score, which are so incredibly important for a work like this. The result is a well-structured and captivating documentary that draws attention to many current problems in the US, especially regarding racism and lax gun laws. What can certainly be criticized, however, is the fundamentally “voyeuristic” feeling that always accompanies such true-crime documentaries. "The Perfect Neighbor" is definitely not immune to this, either. But at least the film seems significantly less manipulative than many of its genre counterparts. And overall, it is definitely worth watching, although it should only be recommended with a clear trigger warning.

review_content: Let me start by saying the woman (murderer) did get what she deserved in the end (although one could most certainly make the argument under State law she did not) for 2 main reasons 1. she clearly did have premeditation after searching "stand your ground" earlier that day, although I don't think it's proof of premeditation just because someone researches their rights, I have no issue with someone knowing th4eir rights... as a matter of fact people should educate themselves, as the police imply I did find the timing of it to be far too suspect to overlook and 2. how she claimed she could not find her phone while the neighbor was pounding on her door but magically was able to find it to call 911 (for the second time that night) immediately after shooting the victim while 'hyperventilating' was again far too much of a coincidence to just gloss over and ignore.    Warning - this will be long. If you don't like reading this comment isn't for you.    Trigger warning - if you are a 2nd Amendment freak, woke and/or racist you'll be triggered by what I am about to say, especially if you are American.    There are several issues not only with the people in this doc but the doc itself which you find at the end clearly had an agenda to make white people out to be racist and murderers and gloss over many other important facts in this case (despite it being in the bio it's about "fear" "prejudice" and "stand your ground laws"?) such as -    1. it's far too easy in the US for just anyone to get their hands on a gun. If you can honestly say you think either the white woman (killer) or the black mother (victim) would have been dead at the end of the night had there been no gun then you're just an idiot. Sure, she could have grabbed a knife, opened the deadbolt and then the door and stabbed the mother to dead. Sure, the mother, in the midst of her anger could have possibly broken the door down/in and attacked the woman in her home... but seriously? what are the odds either of those two possibilities really happens. I'm sure there are cases, anecdotal, that would suggest it can happen and even has happened but for every one of those instances there will be hundreds, if not thoousands, of cases where a gun was used. It just makes it easier to confront/defend ones self when a  gun is used then it is your fists or even a knife.    2. The authorities exacerbated the situation with their own biases and racism, repeatedly. The first black female cop in the doc was having nothing to do with the elderly woman (doctor?) who was the complainant especially after the one black woman said she made racist remarks about (I am paraphrasing) "you black people".. Instantly I knew this was going to be a problem. The black female cop was clearly upset as she barely let her partner talk, repeatedly interrupting him, as he made his own bias statements about (paraphrasing) "kids being kids" and giving his own example of when he was a kid.   Calling the elderly white woman "psycho" prior to responding to one of her calls says all you need to know about not only their niases but their state of mind while performing their jobs and just how much help they were going to end up being to deescalate the situation.  I will say on at least one, possibly two, occasions the male white cop did say something about just keeping the kids away but sadly the die had already been cast.  I knew as soon as the cops let the one black woman get away with throwing a sign at the elderly white woman that the neighborhood was going to feel empowered to do what they wanted to, to get away with whatever they wanted to and to feel liek they were on the side of right (no one was in this doc) and the situation would only escalate. Despite the fact the black woman herself said she threw the sign and I think even said she didn't mean to hit her (I can't recall for sure but she said something that made it sound like she knew what she did was wrong to me) but other witnesses claimed she threw the sign (over her shoulder) while walking away. So what are you saying as a cop when you don't charge this woman? it's ok to thrown things and assualt her? what are you telling the children who are all aware of what happened? Certainly not to leave the woman alone and to respect one anothers rights and privacy.    3. The "adults" and parents in this situation were poor examples for these children. Racist, using the term "Karen" which the kids then repeatedly use. Not one of them had the sense to watch their kids, constantly claiming they were watching but every call to the police none were seemingly around until a child went to get them?? Sadly, it is quite typical today that a persons children becomes someone elses problem because they just can't be bothered to watch over them themselves and it just so happened this white woman was having nothing of it. Yes, you can most certainly make the argument she took it too far, she was aggressive herslf in her complaints. The "get off my lawn" stereotypical old person comes to mind but there in lies another problem with the "adults" in thsii situation, they knew she had issues not only with the children getting too close to her home and leaving things lying around but mentally as well, referring to her as "psycho", "schizophrenic" and "bipolar" and a "Karen" among other things. As a parent/adult one would think it is your responsibility knowing these things to keep your children away from the woman but instead they not only did the aforementioned and didn't seem to really watch their children properly but took it a step further and not only had a mob mentality themselves but weaponized their own children against her. One question I found myself asking over and over again was is there really nowhere else these kids could have played? a park maybe? a school yard? could not one of these adults actually have been outside watching?? not one tell the kids to pick up after themselves even if it was simply to avoid losing, having stolen or damaging their belongings and not just because this woman had an issue with it?    4. Racism and disrespect from all sides. Not only the elderly white womans repeated use of the N word but also calling the children things like "retards" and "Jackasses", again, this should have been a warning to the adults to keep thier children away IMO, a grown adult is talking to children like that and your first thought is just let my kids go play near her I can't be bothered watching over them?? but also the racism of the parents/adults, children and even cops using the terms like "Karen", "Psycho", "schizophrenic", "Bitch" and "bipolar" (I probably missed a few too!) which seems to be most prevalent in the United States, the "US versus THEM" state of mind which seemingly stems from politics (Dems vs Cons, Right vs. Left, Woke vs anti-woke etc) as both "sides" politicize racial tensions.  The doc seemingly goes out of it's way to demonize the white lady for using racist terms but glosses over the other people involved using racist terms aswell.They even go so far as to give us statistics at the end that more black people are killed under stand your ground laws then black and that the percentages even went up after the law was implemented but if their goal was to demonstrate how stand your ground laws were/are a bad thing they fell horribly short of that goal instead it came across as though it was just further racism (this time on the producers part) to imply that white people are just using it as an excuse to murder black people while they completely gloss over the fact that there would also have to be a greater number of blacks attacking whites to even give those white people the opportunity to use stand your ground in the first place which takes us right back to the black mother (victim in this case) who went over, aggressively, and pounded on the whote womans door leading to her getting shit. You can try all you want to sugar coat her actions and make it out like she was doing what a mother should but that would just be more part of the problem than it is factual or honest. Her shild was not hurt, she should have prevented any situation like that from ever arising by keeping her children away and if she felt something needed to be said she should have called the police herself (but she probably knew nothing would be done since the police already established that throwing things at one another is ok there) but instead she used it as an exuse to confront her.    5. Wokeism. Don't get me wrong here, no one should support things like racism, sexism, ageism, bigotry etc but wokeism went from being a good idea, seemingly to treat everyone else with respect and as equals, to some demented, twisted form of racism, sexism, ageism and bigotry of it's own.   You can't tell me this woman would have ever been charged, let alone convicted!, under Florida State stand your ground law had it not been for racism. The fact that a white person defended themselves (again, under State law whether you agree with it or not) against a black person would not be stood for! The pressure on law makers and the system to charge and convict her because of the protests "NoJustice, No Peace", whose justice? Clearly it means black justice and that people are supposed to ignore all the mistakes that were made by the parents, adults, authorities, and even children (who IMO were weaponized and had poor examples of how to be respectful to others). Ignore the fact that any of these people could have spoken up and suggested their kids should play elsewhere, should be more respectful. Ignore the fact that these people (no, not all black) weaponzied these children to harass  an elderly woman with obvious mental health issues and seemingly let them get away with calling her names as well, what parent/adult does that? doesn't tell their kids to stop? clearly in this case it's ones that the children are the bad behaviour from in the first place. Throwing racial terms like "Karen" at her, and I really don't care if she used racial language first like the N word 2 wrongs don't make a right and this situation is proof of that, and even teaching their own children that it was not only acceptable but to weaponize it against her as well. Far too many people in this case, even the police, seemed to just be fine with it. Racism bad but it's also good when used against a white person? smh.    So in the end I'm left with one question still.... was it worth it? You have an elderly white woman in prison for 25 years for manslaughter (I'm shocked they still allow it to be called "man"slaughter since it was a woman to be honest, maybe they just haven't gotten to that part yet?), you have a black mother of (4? at least 3 anyways but I believe she had 4) children who lost her life, 3/4 children without their mom who will undoubtedly grow up to be racist themselves and just confound the problem and a community left without one of their own who thought their mob mentality, and parenting skills (I use the term lightly) was somehow acceptable/excusable, that two wrongs make a right. It's sad that it was the son who lost his mom who asked the only intelligent question out of them all (I am paraphrasing again), and I include the people making the doc as well in this, "maybe my mom would be alive if I just didn't play over there and left my stuff". No young man, a child should not be the one that has to think of these things and take on the responsibility that the adults should have had form the beginning but it was quite telling to me that his guilt showed he knew there was something wrong with what they were doing, what they were allowed to do, and what the consequences of those actions turned out to be.    "The perfect neighbor" falls short of telling the truth and truly delving into the bigger issue(s), many of which I covered in this (rant?), and had they not gone into it with their own racist agenda to make white people out to be bad they could have called it "The perfect neighborhood" and asked the more hard hitting questions, are parents properly watching their kids? are parents raising their children right? would it have made more sense to keep the children away? are gun laws too lax in the US? is there enough being soent on mental health in the US? has wokeism gone too far? should stand your ground laws be revoked so people don't feel so empowered to kill others so freely? heck, even ask things like is enough money being spent on parks and after school programs for children?  This doc, and it's producers, could have taken this opportunity to expose the "Perfect Storm" of racism, wokeism, mental health issues, poor parenting, mob mentality, poor policing, disrespect and even the 2nd Amendment and the results when they all clash and could have showed the devastating results with 1 person dead, another in prison, a community divided and 2 families devastated.  It's sad that it was 2 young boys, albeit not so surprising in this case as they are still young and innocent and not fully indoctrinated yet by their government and adults around them, who said the most intelligent things in the whole movie, the victims son "maybe my mom would still be alive if I hadn't gone over there" and the killers nephew "guns are dangerous", and not a single adult who had anything intelligent, respectful or responsible to say including the Reverend Al Sharpton who could have simply said some nice things about the mother, the children and their community but wanted to make it out like attacking another person, someone with mental health issues no less!, because you're upset the way they treated your child is acceptable despite knowing what the consequnces were, you were literally at her funeral ffs!!! A woman lost her life because of what you are about to excuse, defend and endorse! He could have even made the bold move to use that platform to speak out against the stand your ground law, racism from both sides or even gun laws in general (or lack there of), and asked people to come together, but no he used it to compound the problem and leave people thinking the actions of one side were acceptable and those of the other were the only ones that were wrong facepalm all for yet another moment in the spotlight. Which is going to accomplish what exactly? leave more people, more parents, thinking it's ok to attack others (again, especially rhose with racist tendencies and mental health issues!) and find themselves losing their lives as a result as well???   The judge claiming she wasn't fearful but rather shot the black woman in anger? Tell me he wasn't influenced by the protests without telling me he was influenced by the protests. Granted, I didn't watch this trial, don't even know if I could have, like I have others (OJ, Anthony, Arias and Zimmeman) every minute from beginning to end so I can't go by what was said during the trial, only this doc, but how in the world could the judge know how she felt that night? that she didn't have any fear? and that she did it out of anger instead? sure it's possible, I've already demonstrated many reasons above why she might have been angry enough to murder the black woman (and why the black woman should never have even gone to her door), but it was even the victims own son who stated that his mom was "angry" and "pounding on the door" which would be enough to frighten most people (I even considered what if she was scared out of guilt knowing she did in fact throw something at the boy? it would still be "fear"  and that she might have to face the consequences for such actions) but it would still be fear and not anger, the fact was the only person that could be proven to be "angry" was the mother (victim) by her sons (and others'?) statement(s) and yet the judge couldn't convict her without ingoring her fear, which I am sure he was made well aware of how worse the protests would have gotten had he found her not guilty, but was unbelievably obvious he only ignored it to get around the stand your ground laws and of course no ones going to stand up for the crazy white woman and risk being called a racist themselves and potentially be "cancelled" (again, more evidence of wokeism gone too far and becoming that which is was purportedly against in the first place).   Like I said at the start, I think the woman (killer) got what she deserved in  the end for taking someone else's life but the sad thing is she was led to believe she had the law on her side and as a clearly mentally ill person would have been mroe susceptible than most others, none of the adults, authorities, law makers etc were held accountable for their actions/inactions just the woman lowly white woman who legally bought a gun, was on her own property, repeatedly made cries for help, faced mob mentality and thought she was doing what she had the right to do under the law and defend her own home from an enraged black mother who felt she was doing the right thing, and had the law on her side as well, by defending her young child(ren).  As hard as I try I simply can't see any 'right' in this case nor the usefulness of such a doc other than to use the lose of this black mother to push their agenda and fan the flames of racism and disrespect in an already racially heated country like the United States.  If you learn ANYTHING from this doc I hope it's not to act like any of the adults that are in it.  Respect one another.  Ask yourself if the words or actions you are using would be used against you by the other person how would you feel? and if the answer is you would feel disrespected, that they were being racist or that it would anger you than perhaps reconsider your words/actions and just walk away. Turn the other cheek.

movie_title: Good Luck, Have Fun, Don't Die
cast_names: Sam Rockwell, Juno Temple, Haley Lu Richardson, Michael Peña, Zazie Beetz, Asim Chaudhry, Tom Taylor, Georgia Goodman, Artie Wilkinson-Hunt, Dominique Maher
review_content: Good Luck, Have Fun, Don't Die starts out extremely strong. It utilizes a Terminator-esque framing story, showcasing a time traveler who comes from the future to stop a pending apocalypse, asking help from a group of eclectic and random diners. The standout of this first half are flashback stories into the backstories of these diners, each flashback playing out like a Twin Peaks/Black Mirror episode that illustrates the way current trends in relation to technology and social media are leading to this horrific future. Each of these sections utilize absurdity to great effect. I think Juno Temple's Susan has the best overall "episode" within this framework, and Haley Lu Richardson's Ingrid has the weakest. Regardless, I think all of these flashbacks do a good job of communicating the central themes of the movie. The problem is that once the movie gets through these flashbacks, it starts focusing on its framing story, which starts to feel less like a thematically driven satire and more just zany sci-fi. There are plenty of fun and weird moments in that second half, and the movie has some very impressive cinematography considering it probably did not have a huge budget. Still, I couldn't help but feeling like it left its strong first half underbaked. The movie starts by showcasing how society has become numb to a variety of elements and how it impacts humanity, but by the end, it is more than happy to settle with having a disembodied big bad be the real villain all along. I think this shortchanges the message just so that there can be some bombastic sequences at the end. I also think that having this singularity-style AI ends up making it feel more akin to Terminator than a prescient movie set in the modern times with our current generative AI, a real missed opportunity. Overall, I think the second half might look really good and have some cool sci-fi moments, but it really dampens the strength of the first half and makes the movie end up feeling mediocre.

review_content: The Secret Agent sneaks up on you. It is more interested in mood than momentum and often drifts into side stories. Sometimes this works brilliantly. Sometimes it slows the film to a crawl. Meanwhile, you unknowingly grow more attached to characters and sink deeper into the story. When the plot finally hits its stride, you are far more engaged than you realized. This is not an intense thriller, but it is visually striking and beautifully acted. You may not always know where it is going, but the journey is fascinating.

review_content: I really really want to like this movie more. The concept around it is amazing. The world it builds feels real and lived in. It was fascinating to peer into Brazilian culture.  However, The Secret Agent is just utterly way too long. The entire second part of the film dragged pretty heavily.  [spoiler] The finals scenes started off really well. Lots of intensity and action. However, the death of Armando/Marcelo felt pretty devoid of emotion, especially so close after the previous action. In some ways, it almost feels unresolved.   In addition, the finals scenes were confusing and felt like it lacked the lushness of the rest of the film.  [/spoiler]  Overall, I think it’s a beautiful world with a story that could have been better if its time was managed more wisely.

review_content: There are parts of a movie I like within this movie, but as a whole, I felt like the pacing and the story in general were glacially slow. The beginning 15 minutes and the 10 minute section of the assassination were really good, but outside of that, things were just very slow. I can appreciate the vibe they were going for in the movie and making it authentic to Brazil, but it just didn't make for that enjoyable of a watch. I also think the ending and Armando's off screen death, while thematically makes sense, is once again not very enjoyable. Couple that with the I feel like weird inclusion of the present-day storyline, and I just feel like things didn't come together.   The beginning of the movie had me pretty excited, but overall, even though the world building and some of the character building were interesting, they weren't enjoyable to watch, and because of that, I just felt like it was kind of a useless use of my time. Light 5/10

review_content: Story/Plot: 4/10  Acting/Pe mances: 5/10  Direction/Cinematography: 5/10  Sound/Music: 4/10  Overall Enjoyment/Impact: 4/10  The Astronaut promises a cosmic journey but mostly delivers a snooze worthy orbit around cliches. The plot drifts like a spacecraft with a busted thruster-slow, aimless, and occasionally confusing-leaving you wondering if you accidentally pressed fast-forward. The actors do their best, but it's like asking a cat to perform Shakespeare: admirable effort, zero emotional impact. Visually, there are glimpses of starry wonder, but the cinematography quickly collapses into generic space movie mode. And the soundtrack? Let's just say it doesn't exactly make you feel like you're floating among the stars-it feels more like floating through a bargain-bin elevator music playlist. The Astronaut is watchable if you have nothing better, but don't expect liftoff... you'll mostly just drift in orbit until the credits roll.

review_content: "She has to go," pleads the distraught husband/father.  But the police stand by. Do nothing.  The police have just told him that his wife had terrorised their daughter for a year with vulgar, abusive phone messages.  But she's not in cuffs. They even let her hug the victim.  It could not not get any creepier. Why the kid gloves?  Fascinating story. But I could have done with a WHOLE lot more contrition. The mother is given far too much time by the doc to justify her behaviour and even erase it. "It's not who I am today" just does not wash.

The central premise driving the plot is quite plausible considering the mental instability of much of the population.  The central character you identify with, and the movie emotionally engages you.  The acting is all solid.      The movie isn't too long.  The cast is less than a dozen people.  It keeps the scale very small and fully utilizes the characters and elements that it has.  Very watchable and well executed.  It's not great, but it is a very good movie.

review_content: I'm convinced the only love people have for this movie is the hype around The Rock's makeup. I've been an MMA fan since Gerard Gordeau made some teeth fly. That movie was dreadful. Emily was the best, Rock - yeah, he was good too. I get it's an MMA movie, but why on earth would they let Bader be in a supporting role? At least Bas was just Bas, no acting needed. Bader was terrible. Slow moving, god-awful fight scenes -- which I'll say should have been expected, it's about Mark and the life, not the action -- but still, do better choreo.     I'm a movie watcher, as in, my phone is in another room. I had to go get it because I wanted to see how it unfolded, but what an interesting, poorly paced, lack of any feeling despite how hard they tried mess.

Interesting setting in the early days of MMA fighting, but they have made it into a boring movie. Flat colors symbolize the flat story and performances. A24 could try to make The Empire Strikes Back and end up making The Last Jedi. Defeat snatched from the jaws of Victory...which oddly mirrors the subject matter in some ways.

review_content: Starts out really strong, and then, about midway through, goes for a big tonal shift and pretty much changes genres. The first half is excellent, as a slapstick action rollercoaster with very dark subtext and excellent racing sequences. The latter half is a dark emotional gut-punch indie drama, in large part light on jokes and action. Like a very good roast beef that someone just had to sprinke some caviar and truffle oil on top, for seemingly no reason. Both sides work well, but don't mesh well, leading to a bit of a mixed bag.     That being said, the performances are excellent (Glusman at his career best), the plot has a few twists and turns and the driving sequences are excellent (and, most importantly, mostly real). Recommended!

review_content: My Oxford Year tries to sell itself as an emotional romantic drama set in a postcard-perfect setting, but neither its scenery nor its attractive cast can hide the obvious: this is a flat, prefabricated, soulless film. The story leans on every young adult romance cliché—improbable love, culture clashes, personal discovery—but does so with such lack of energy that it becomes frustrating.    The chemistry between the leads is almost nonexistent, turning every supposedly emotional moment into a weightless formality. Even the supporting cast, with seasoned actors who could add gravitas, seem trapped in a script that gives them nothing interesting to say or do.    Visually, it delivers, with Oxford and other British locations looking like a tourist brochure, but the pretty packaging can’t make up for the narrative emptiness. The film tries hard to feel moving, but everything comes off as artificial and forced, as if it were trying to force tears without earning them.    Ironically, one of its most memorable moments has nothing to do with the plot, but with the appearance of Coldplay’s Yellow, which at least stirs something amid the boredom. Beyond that, what remains is a forgettable exercise that doesn’t even qualify as a “guilty pleasure”—it’s simply dull.

review_content: Clown in a Cornfield is a solid, no-frills slasher that delivers exactly what genre fans expect. It doesn’t reinvent the wheel or introduce a new horror icon, but it executes the formula well enough to keep things entertaining from start to finish. The kills are satisfying, the atmosphere feels appropriately tense and grimy, and it carries that throwback energy that makes classic slashers fun.  It’s not particularly memorable, but it’s competent and enjoyable—a popcorn horror movie that hits the right beats without overstaying its welcome. I’m genuinely looking forward to seeing where the sequels take it.  Rating: 2.5/5 – 70% – Worth Watching

review_content: It is not just philosophical about how fragile and unpredictable life is, it also subverts your expectations about narrative in life and on the screen.  There’s various clues to some narrative that are never realised, yet anything that happens is unexpected. It plays out in cycles and things repeat but it’s not going anywhere.  The biggest idea here - if you have been damaged and hurt, it’s not a protection or guarantee; you can be destroyed at any moment just the same.

review_content: Make it up as we go trip to nowhere with good sound and visuals. It's a case of style over substance, this movie likes to focus on the mundane like people dancing and staring at nothing instead of giving us a story and building up it's characters. Characters are distant and we don't learn much about them so it's hard to care, I cared about the dog more than anyone else. Why was *that* moment (and anything involving [spoiler]explosions[/spoiler]) so unintentionally funny and flat at the same time? Bad execution all the way. I watched this with headphones since I had heard it's biggest asset was sound and I'll just say there's nothing groundbreaking. The repetitive music is so annoying, I wouldn't last more than 20 minutes at a rave. There's a great moment when everybody's dancing in the third act that was kinda emotional though but then something happened and it was back in 'I feel bad for laughing at that' land.

cast_names: Sergi López
review_content: “Sirāt” feels deliberately designed to shatter any expectation of narrative or emotional comfort. Oliver Laxe starts from an almost banal premise: a father and son crossing the Moroccan desert in search of a missing daughter, only to slowly dismantle the idea of the journey as something transformative or redemptive. What begins as a road movie built around unlikely companionship, driven by pulsing electronic music and a fleeting sense of communal belonging, gradually turns into an oppressive, brutal, and deeply distressing experience. Laxe has no interest in offering clear arcs, resolutions, or catharsis; the film exists as a continuous emotional state that steadily decays as the characters’ choices grow more desperate.    Narratively, “Sirāt” works in an anti-classical mode. There’s very little exposition, no real effort to psychologically flesh out the characters or contextualize their pasts. Luis (Sergi López) is defined solely by his silent obsession with finding his daughter; Esteban (Bruno Núñez Arjona) by his fragile, observant presence; the ravers who travel with them by gestures, bodies, and small everyday interactions. This minimalism can feel frustrating at first, but it’s essential to the effect the film creates. Laxe opts for a surface-level narrative in the literal sense: everything unfolds in the present, in the now, without explanatory layers that might offer the viewer a sense of safety. So when violence erupts abruptly in the second half, it doesn’t come with warnings or traditional dramatic buildup, it simply happens, the way it does in life.    That tonal shift is the film’s most divisive element, and also its most powerful. The first half of “Sirāt” almost numbs the viewer, repeating moments of travel, improvised parties, casual conversations, and hypnotic electronic music. There’s a sense of time being suspended, as if these characters exist outside the real world, living in a limbo of pleasure and searching. It’s precisely this repetition that creates a false sense of security. When death arrives, it shocks not only because of its graphic impact, but because it instantly shatters that illusory bubble. From that point on, the film becomes a nightmare of constant anxiety, where every wrong decision (and there are many) pushes the group further into an increasingly suffocating dead end.    Technically, “Sirāt” is striking, especially in its sound and music design. The EDM soundtrack isn’t just cultural background, but a central narrative element. During the raves, diegetic sound creates a collective, almost spiritual trance that brings bodies together and dissolves individual identities. Later on, the desert’s silence, broken by sudden sonic blasts or mechanical noises, becomes deafening. Laxe understands sound as a tool of emotional manipulation: sometimes it envelops us, sometimes it abandons us completely. Watching the film without a strong sound setup means missing a crucial part of its impact.    Visually, the mise-en-scène reinforces this progression from ecstasy to collapse. The desert, initially filmed as a space of freedom and movement, gradually turns into a hostile territory, indifferent to human pain. The camera often keeps its distance, refusing explanatory close-ups or moments of emotional intimacy. This choice heightens the sense of helplessness: we’re not invited to “feel along” in a conventional way, but to witness something unfolding beyond our control. When violence occurs, it isn’t stylized or heroic: it’s blunt, sudden, and deeply disturbing, both in what it shows and what it leaves implied.    The performances are another major strength, especially considering that much of the cast isn’t made up of professional actors. Sergi López carries the film with a restrained, opaque performance that captures the thin line between determination and self-destruction. His Luis isn’t a tragic hero, but a man incapable of turning back, even when every sign suggests that moving forward will only deepen the pain. The surrounding cast brings a raw physicality and presence that give the group real authenticity, making their losses even harder to watch precisely because they feel so real, so possible.    Still, it’s impossible to ignore that “Sirāt” ends by leaving a deliberate sense of incompleteness. There’s no classic beginning-middle-end arc, no clear lesson, no emotional closure. The film ends almost as if it simply refuses to provide answers. For some viewers, that may feel like a narrative weakness; for others, it’s thematic consistency. Laxe seems to suggest that certain experiences, especially those marked by loss, guilt, and irreversible choices, don’t organize themselves into comforting narratives. They simply remain, like open wounds.    Above all, “Sirāt” is a heavy film, capable of triggering deep discomfort and leaving a lingering sense of unease. Its second half is nearly unbearable not only because of explicit violence, but because of the moral anguish of watching characters persist in disastrous choices, justified by pain and desperation. The film brutally exposes how thin the line can be between moving forward and self-destruction, and how obsession, when stripped of any sense of limits, can become just as lethal as external violence itself. This isn’t a film made for frequent rewatches, nor for anyone seeking comfort. But as a singular, intense, and unsettling experience, “Sirāt” stands as cinema that doesn’t ask permission to wound, it only warns, far too late, that the chosen path offers no way back.

review_content: As I said in my review of the first Troll... I grew up with the stories, the drawings and the paintings, in the very valley where the most famous illustrator of the Norwegian trolls and other folk tales lived for much of his life...    Norwegian folklore, and trolls especially, is very close to my heart. Hence... I was really looking forward to this follow-up to a film that I ended up liking despite its faults.    "Troll 2" is a bit of a step back from the first one. It's more or less the same story as the first one, but this time with two trolls and a new city. The acting is... I'm sorry to say... mostly horrendously bad, and actually hampers a lot of my enjoyment of this film.    To be honest... I was a bit disappointed by this. This doesn't mean I didn't enjoy parts of "Troll 2", but I really wish they had gone a bit of a different route. There's so much lore around trolls in Norway that it wouldn't really be that hard...    Anyway... if you want to see some of the illustrations and paintings I were talking about above. Look up Theodor Kittelsen on Wikipedia as a start.

review_content: Really enjoyed this despite some grating scenes - mainly the barking dog - that kept putting me on edge. Jennifer Lawrence is excellent, with good support from Robert Pattinson and Sissy Spacek.  At times I did find it hard to work out what was in her mind and what was real - in particular the LaKeith Stanfield character. I couldn't figure out if the whole thing was imagined from a fleeting glimpse of the man or if a full blown affair happened and it was only the scene where she approached him and his family that was in her mind. Same goes for when she helps Harry tie his shoelaces.  The ending doesn't give much away either, leaving it all open to interpretation what actually happened. If I'm honest I like it that way.  Some nice music choices too.

cast_names: Jennifer Lawrence, Robert Pattinson, Sissy Spacek, LaKeith Stanfield, Nick Nolte, Gabrielle Rose, Clare Coulter, Sarah Lind, Luke Camilleri, Victor Zinck, Jr.
review_content: Lynne Ramsay explains nothing, and that’s exactly what makes it perfect: you enter as a voyeur into a house that’s slowly collapsing. At first I thought it was supernatural horror (the atmosphere certainly suggests it), but the real horror is the raw reality of motherhood and postpartum depression. No easy metaphors, no cliché tears—just rage, frustration, desire, and creative paralysis shown with brutal honesty. The characters aren’t detestable; instead, they make you ask, “What would I do?” It left me shaken and thinking long after.

review_content: I went into Honey Don’t! expecting a messy, low-rated curiosity and walked out wondering why everyone seemed to have such a bee in their bonnet about it. Is it a little silly? Absolutely. Is it stylish, playful, and weirdly charming? Also yes. Honestly that’s kind of the point.    Honey herself is a great lead: confident, mischievous, and fully aware that she’s the most interesting person in every room she walks into. The movie leans hard into that energy, especially when it comes to her relationships. The lesbian romance(s) at the centre of the story are flirty, chaotic, affectionate, and very human. They aren’t written like a lecture or a manifesto; they’re written like people actually falling for each other, getting tangled up, and enjoying it.    Maybe the criticism is due to the film’s kinkier or more sexually charged scenes, but they’re handled with a wink and a grin. Nothing feels grim, exploitative, or heavy. It’s more like the movie is nudging you in the ribs and saying, “Relax, this is supposed to be fun.” The intimacy between Honey and her partners is playful and affectionate, not graphic, and it fits the movie’s cheeky tone perfectly.    What I especially appreciated is that the film doesn’t turn any of this into a soapbox. There’s no grand, preachy speech about identity or politics. It just lets these women exist, flirt, mess up, make out, and care about each other, the same way straight characters have been allowed to do on screen forever. That easygoing normalcy is refreshing.    So yeah, Honey Don’t! might not be everyone’s cup of tea, but for me it was a breezy, funny, slightly naughty good time. If a movie about stylish lesbians being dramatic, romantic, and a little bit kinky makes some people uncomfortable, that sounds more like their problem than the film’s. I’m happy to sip my honey and enjoy it anyway.
