cast_names: Margaret Qualley, Aubrey Plaza, Chris Evans, Lera Abova, Jacnier, Gabby Beans, Talia Ryder, Charlie Day, Kristen Connolly, Josh Pafchek
review_content: > Sorry, I think I left some lipstick down there.  >   > It's okay, that's the only place I ever wear it.    I wasn't much of a fan of _Drive-Away Dolls (2024)_, but the second installment of the so-called "Lesbian B-movie trilogy" caught my attention right away -- starting from its title.    Margaret Qualley returns in lead role and she's amazing as always -- and simply breathtaking. I liked her character, Honey O'Donahue. She feels genuinely different from her role in the previous film. Chris Evans is another standout for me.    The film has a fun, stylish vibe -- even the opening credits are pretty nice -- but its biggest issue is the narrative. It feels completely directionless. We spend almost the entire movie following the plot involving the church, only for it to be tossed aside out of nowhere. It's totally misleading and gives way to a completely out-of-the-blue twist involving Aubrey Plaza. While watching, it took me a few minutes to even believe that the movie was actually going that route and that wasn't just a fantasy or something like that. The idea _could_ work, but it didn't land for me at all.    _Honey Don't (2025)_ starts with a good premise and interesting, solid characters. It just feels like it needed a few more rounds of revision on the script -- enough to take a promising concept and shape it into something great.

review_content: I Swear shines a spotlight on Tourette Syndrome, something I feel that I personally had not been exposed to or taught about at all. In that way, this movie felt unique compared to others in the genre that also focus on a character dealing with a disability. I actually felt like I was learning about it, not just from the perspective of a character who is struggling with it but also from the perspective of understanding those who have it. I truly believe that I would have been like one of the many characters in this movie who was offended by Davidson had I not watched it. In that way, the movie actually feels like the truest form of advocacy, and I really appreciate the fact that Kirk Jones decided to focus on this topic. Robert Aramayo does a fantastic job, not because of his physicality in capturing the tics but rather because of how he is able to really showcase the interiority of John Davidson, his emotional state, that is not always in line with the words and expressions that are being showcased. His body movements, voice, and eyes are sometimes all working in completely different directions, and his performance is really impressive when you think about that. With that said, I don't think this movie is perfect. I always look at movies like this and ask if they manage to do something more than just selecting an important topic. With this movie, I sometimes was left wondering if the actual filmmaking itself elevated the movie beyond its subject matter or central performance. The camerawork and directing is just in the background, often not doing much. I think that can be fine, but it also makes the movie feel less memorable. I do think the movie does a good job in building the emotional relationships between John and Dottie and Tommy, showcasing the impact of kindness. That aspect led to some heartfelt scenes that I do think made an impression. I just wish we got more into why John becomes the man he is at the end of the movie in terms of him discovering his true purpose. I feel the movie rushed that revelation a bit. Overall, I think this movie is an important watch, grounded by a strong performance. I just think it doesn't necessarily go beyond that though.

review_content: “What is that?. Crack cocaine you cunt”  This movie was absolutely incredible and I think everyone should watch this at least once. It just changes your perspective on things and is such an uplifting and inspiring journey, with so many funny and emotional moments.  The story was amazing, showing John’s struggle  with Tourette’s syndrome, and the things he has to cope with in everyday life. It was terrible how people treated him, and he got into many fights because he accidentally said the wrong stuff to the wrong people. It was hard to watch the part where he got jumped by those horrible, bastard scumbags.   Robert Aramayo’s acting was phenomenal and I think he deserved every award going. I cant get over the fact that he’s not even Scottish either, I mean that’s crazy. I’m sure he’ll be in more stuff to come after this movie and so he should.  There were of course many hysterical moments as some of the stuff John said was so funny, i’m not making fun of him though but I mean come on, that car scene must’ve caught a few laughs, it was so hilarious. “my dad’s a fucking jellyfish”. I also found it funny when said he made the tea with spunk. That was brilliant. “I used spunk for milk”.   The ending was pretty sweet, with John being a big voice and massive inspiration for those with Tourette’s syndrome and even for those without it, making us a lot more aware of his condition. I think a story like this deserves even more than a 10 rating. What an incredible story.  10/10

cast_names: Robert Aramayo, Maxine Peake, Peter Mullan, Shirley Henderson, Scott Ellis Watson, David Carlyle, Michael Dylan, Christina Ashford, Sanjeev Kohli, Steven Cree
review_content: Crack cocaine, you cunt! Half-price heroin!  Especially after hearing about what happened at the BAFTA awards this film feels even more important to understand now, seriously people like Jamie foxx who clearly doesn’t know how Tourette’s works this won awards for showing how great they show the real struggles John lives with so I don’t get why people have made a huge deal about what happened it’s ridiculous to me.  Anyway I’m focusing on this incredible movie going into it I didn’t know where they was going to go with the actual story all I knew it was about Tourette’s and what we got was so much more from moment one I was booked from the backstory seeing him as a kid slowly getting the symptoms alongside people’s reactions to it proving there wasn’t much information on the disease, I also didn’t know you can get Tourette’s like that I thought you was just born with it so this does a excellent job at explaining everything.  I have to say I found this to be the funniest thing I’ve watched in the cinema in so long come on guys how could you not laugh at the things he said and does it’s freaking hilarious and btw the disease itself must be awful to live with so of course I don’t think that’s funny one bit but the stuff he says it’s very hard to not laugh at I honestly tried not too.: “I use spunk for milk!”  There was literally too many moments that made me cry laugh to mention him just kissing lampposts had me in stitches, it’s no just about the comedy and educational stuff for me this was a really wholesome nice movie seeing him as a kid then grown up struggling with simple things like going shopping and worrying about not being able to get a job and finding someone who would be able to cope with him.  Robert Aramayo 100% deserved that BAFTA his performance was absolutely outstanding you would believe he really does have Tourette’s himself with how good of a job he does and with a Scottish accent aswell even more impressive, Johns relationship with Dottie and Tommy I loved them so much it was lovely to see him being accepted for who is after watching how his family reacted to him.  The ending you couldn’t of asked for more it was next to perfect, John getting the caretaker job permanently and also deciding to help young kids with the disease so they can find ways to manage there symptoms and teach schools, police and society in general what Tourette’s is and spread awareness, and omg the library scene was emotional John being able to find some peace and comfort was a nice moment.  Lastly with this being a true story obviously this is a very important film and story it’s also funny asf but a fantastic story loved it will be up there with my favourites at the end of the year for sure. 10/10  “The problem is not Tourette's, the problem is that people don't know enough about Tourette's.”

cast_names: Robert Aramayo, Maxine Peake, Peter Mullan, Shirley Henderson, Scott Ellis Watson, David Carlyle, Michael Dylan, Christina Ashford, Sanjeev Kohli, Steven Cree
review_content: - Great acting  - Relatable  - No hyperbole drama  All below is just a TED talk about how I relate to this movie:  Only 30mins in and I'm honestly crying how close this hits home.  Growing up with ADD being close to 2m tall at 14 years old while wearing glasses I've always been drawing unwanted attention for shit from either adults or children my own age... Which resulted in either no understanding from adults or being bullied all the way through adult life.  It was a different time and age, it was a taboo topic and not much was known about multiple mental health issues. "stop it" & "just try harder" was basically as far as assistance and help went...  I feel like this movie will relate to a lot of people be it for mental problems or just being felt not understood.

review_content: [IFFR '26] Director Kirk Jones uses comedy as a vehicle for normalization, and that is a special achievement, even though his own script treads more or less conventional narrative ground. But it is the humanity of the characters that transcends the story, especially a moving performance by Robert Aramayo, which doesn't settle for a superficial representation of the spasms or vocal tics caused by Tourette syndrome, but rather unfolds a profound portrayal of initial helplessness and eventual acceptance.

review_content: Damned Trinity is a western that blends revenge, buried secrets, and violence with a classic feel. Set in 1870s Montana, it follows a young man who returns to fulfill a promise to his father and finds himself caught between a stern sheriff and an unpredictable outsider.  Pierce Brosnan and Samuel L. Jackson bring charisma and presence even in simple scenes, and the dusty Western setting works well for the story's tone. The film doesn't reinvent the genre: at times the plot seems predictable and relies on clichés, but it still entertains and maintains interest thanks to its shootouts, twists, and narrative energy.  My rating: 8/10.

review_content: Scream 7 is frankly disappointing. The gap between this and the original Scream isn’t just about time — it’s about creativity. What once was a smart, self-aware horror film has turned into a string of absurd situations and poor decisions.  It stopped being scary a long time ago and now feels more like a collection of nonsense. The characters show almost no basic survival instinct, reacting in ways that feel completely unrealistic, which kills any sense of tension.  In the end, it’s predictable, unconvincing, and, worst of all, boring. Instead of fear, it delivers frustration. A clear sign the franchise is running out of ideas.

The good acting performances save the movie from complete mediocrity, but as said, it is mostly for fans that are willing to ignore the many imperfections.

cast_names: Neve Campbell, Courteney Cox, Isabel May, Jasmin Savoy Brown, Mason Gooding, Roger L. Jackson, Anna Camp, Joel McHale, Celeste O'Connor, Sam Rechner
review_content: Scream 7 tries to reset the board by dragging Sidney Prescott back into the spotlight, this time with Ghostface targeting her daughter in a quieter suburban setting. On paper, that’s a strong hook. Back to roots, legacy stakes, full-circle horror. In execution, it feels like déjà vu dressed up as evolution.  The problem is the series has always thrived on being smarter than the genre. Here, that edge is blunted. The meta commentary is still there, but it’s no longer cutting, just self-aware noise. Instead of dissecting horror tropes, it leans on them, almost proudly.  There are flashes of life. A couple of kills are genuinely inventive and brutal, reminding you why this franchise once set the standard. The opening especially hints at something sharper, but it never builds on it. It’s like watching a band play their greatest hits slightly out of tune.  And yeah, the big issue… the returns. Bringing back dead characters used to feel cheeky or psychological. Here it tips into outright irritation. The film leans heavily on resurrected faces and legacy cameos, to the point it starts to feel like fan service stitched together rather than a story. Instead of raising stakes, it lowers them. Death stops meaning anything.  Even the killers, while fitting the usual twisted logic, don’t carry the same punch. The motivations are there, but the impact just doesn’t land like earlier reveals.  You can feel what they were aiming for. A “return to Sidney,” a nostalgic reset, a bridge between eras. But instead of reigniting the spark, it exposes how thin the formula has become after seven entries.  Not a disaster, but a clear sign the formula’s running on fumes. Entertaining in bursts, but the soul of Scream is fading.  If you liked…Scream, Halloween Ends

cast_names: Neve Campbell, Courteney Cox, Isabel May, Jasmin Savoy Brown, Mason Gooding, Roger L. Jackson, Anna Camp, Joel McHale, Celeste O'Connor, Sam Rechner
review_content: _Scream 7_ is a fun, bloody ride that scratches the slasher itch, but it feels a bit like the gang’s been chasing the same Mystery Inc. van for too long.    Picture this: Sidney Prescott (Neve Campbell, still the unflappable queen) is living her quiet life in some sleepy town, running a coffee shop like she’s finally retired from the Ghostface grind. She’s got a teenage daughter named Tatum (yep, named after the original bestie), a goofy cop husband (Joel McHale bringing the dad-joke energy), and she’s trying to pretend the past is just a bad dream. But nope—Zoinks! A new Ghostface shows up, mask on, knife out, and starts slicing through Tatum’s high school crew and anyone who gets too close to the Prescott family legacy.    The kills are creative enough. There’s a solid opening at the old Macher house turned tourist trap that had me chuckling at how meta it gets and the tension builds nicely when “Stu” pops up on video calls, taunting Sidney like he’s back from the grave. It’s pure Scooby-Doo vibes: the gang (Sidney, Gale, a few survivors) splits up to investigate, following clues to an old mental facility, uncovering red herrings, and piecing together who’s really behind the mask. The AI deepfake twist for Stu is clever in a modern horror way, like if Fred had rigged a projector to make it look like the Black Knight was real, but it lands with a satisfying “ruh-roh” reveal.    What works: Neve Campbell owns every scene she’s in, the mother-daughter dynamic adds fresh stakes, and the final showdown delivers some brutal, satisfying stabbings and headshots. The humor hits the right self aware notes without going full parody.    Overall, it’s not the scariest entry, but it’s entertaining enough to keep the franchise alive, like finding one last Scooby Snack in the van after thinking the adventure was over. Worth a theater watch if you’re a fan, or a solid streaming pick for a spooky night. Jinkies, they pulled it off again… mostly.

review_content: Song Sung Blue (2025) is a solid rendition of a story that’s been told before. The structure and emotional beats feel familiar, but the film handles them well enough to remain engaging throughout.  Where it really finds its audience is with fans of musicals, romantic dramas, or anything connected to Neil Diamond’s music. The performances and musical elements carry much of the charm, and if you fall into that demographic, it’s easy to see this being a very enjoyable watch.  It doesn’t necessarily stand out as something I’d recommend to everyone, but the right audience will absolutely connect with it.  Rating: 3/5 – 75% – Worth Watching

cast_names: Hugh Jackman, Kate Hudson, Michael Imperioli, Fisher Stevens, Jim Belushi, Ella Anderson, King Princess, Mustafa Shakir, Hudson Hilbert Hensley, Shyaporn Theerakulstit
review_content: Some music stories aren’t born on bright stages where legends are crowned, but in small bars, county fairs, and makeshift stages where the applause comes more from persistence than from fame. “Song Sung Blue,” directed by Craig Brewer, looks exactly at that kind of journey, the kind built more on stubborn passion than on the machinery of the music industry. The film starts from a simple, almost naive idea: two people who find in music a way to survive their own frustrations. From there, it tries to turn that partnership into a romantic melodrama about love, late ambition, and emotional resilience. The problem is that somewhere between good intentions and predictable storytelling choices, the film swings between moments that feel genuinely moving and others that fall into the most familiar clichés of musical biopics.    The story follows Mike (Hugh Jackman), a Vietnam veteran trying to rebuild his life while earning a bit of money doing musical impersonations at modest events. Tired of performing other people’s songs and personas, he starts craving something more personal, even if he doesn’t quite know how to reach it. Meeting Claire (Kate Hudson) becomes the catalyst for that shift. The chemistry between the two characters is immediate and convincing enough to carry the first half of the film, especially in the early scenes where they begin sharing the stage. As the duo “Lightning” and “Thunder,” they turn what starts as a tribute act into something that feels closer to a shared life project. Brewer does a good job capturing the youthful excitement of that beginning, that spark of discovery when two people realize they’ve found a common purpose.    Still, the film takes its time finding real dramatic momentum. The first stretch drifts between performances, explanatory dialogue, and small tensions that feel more like setup than real conflict. There’s a constant sense that the story is just warming up its engines without fully deciding where it wants to go. That hesitant pacing shifts abruptly after the accident that completely changes Claire’s life. From that point on, “Song Sung Blue” leaves much of its initial lightness behind and takes on a darker tone, focusing on the physical and emotional aftermath of the tragedy. Interestingly, that’s when the film finally gains real dramatic weight. The pain, the frustration, and the duo’s attempts to rebuild their lives feel more convincing than the musical rise the story initially seemed eager to celebrate.    The performances help carry that tonal shift. Hugh Jackman plays Mike as a stubborn showman, someone whose genuine charisma is mixed with a certain arrogance that sometimes makes him hard to root for. Early on, his big energy almost feels excessive, as if the character is always performing a little too loudly for the world around him. As the film goes on, though, Jackman finds a more interesting balance, letting Mike’s vulnerabilities show through the confident front. Kate Hudson, on the other hand, seems to land the right tone from the very beginning. Her Claire radiates enthusiasm and sensitivity, turning the character into the real emotional core of the story. Even when the narrative gets heavier, Hudson maintains a warmth that keeps the film from sinking completely into pessimism.    Despite those strengths, the screenplay often undercuts the dramatic potential of the material. The dialogue repeatedly falls back on lines that feel overly literal or predictable, as if every emotional moment needs to be spelled out for the audience instead of simply lived by the characters. That approach sometimes makes the film feel like an accidental caricature of the very genre it’s trying to honor. More than once, you can predict exactly what a character is about to say before they even open their mouth, which weakens the impact of the story’s more dramatic beats.    That reliance on convention also shows up in the film’s overall structure. “Song Sung Blue” follows a chain of events that feels like a checklist of biographical music drama tropes. A modest rise, a personal crisis, a comeback attempt, another emotional setback. The issue isn’t the structure itself, but how quickly and compactly many of those events are presented. Brewer squeezes years of developments into a narrative flow that sometimes feels rushed, turning moments that could’ve carried real dramatic weight into simple plot markers.    Visually, the film opts for a fairly restrained approach. Brewer doesn’t try to turn the musical performances into big cinematic spectacles. Instead, he films them with a sense of intimacy that reflects the modest nature of Lightning and Thunder’s career. That choice works best in scenes where music becomes a space of emotional connection between the characters. At the same time, the direction rarely finds a visual language that elevates the material beyond its conventional storytelling.    In the end, “Song Sung Blue” feels torn between two impulses. On one side, there’s a sincere human story about two people who find in music a way to stay together while facing life’s hardships. On the other, there’s a screenplay determined to shape that story into a predictable dramatic formula, filled with artificial dialogue and compressed turning points. When the film simply observes the small gestures of partnership between Mike and Claire, it reveals something genuinely touching. When it tries to dramatize every step of their journey as if it were building a sweeping emotional epic, it loses some of that authenticity.    Maybe the most curious thing about “Song Sung Blue” is that it works best when it stops trying to be grand. In the moments when the characters simply sing together, plan another show, or try to start over after a setback, the story finds a kind of truth that doesn’t need big speeches to exist. By the end, the film becomes an uneven but occasionally sensitive portrait of two people who turned a shared love of music into a way of facing life. It’s not a perfectly tuned symphony, but there are moments when its slightly off-key notes reveal something unexpectedly human.

review_content: “Mountainhead” is one of those films you could tear apart in seconds if you felt like being picky. The story is fairly simple, almost a long joke stretched to feature length, and it doesn’t pretend to be deeper than it is. But the script moves so fast, hits so sharply, and knows exactly what reaction it wants from you that, even if you’ve seen a thousand satires about rich guys playing with the world like it’s a toy, you end up going along with it.    What works best is the tone. That mix of discomfort and short, nervous laughter. A sense of humor that punches rather than smiles, mostly aimed at these tech “visionaries” who behave like overgrown children. And yes, it’s impossible not to think of Succession —not only because of the spiteful energy, but the way it portrays people who are brilliant on paper and dangerously clueless in practice.    The plot itself isn’t groundbreaking. It works, it keeps you watching, but it never fully breaks out of its own bubble. Sometimes you can see Armstrong building the narrative skeleton mostly to serve the clever scenes, not the other way around. Still, those scenes land more often than not: snappy dialogue, perfectly timed humiliations, and a rhythm that blends lightness with real bite.    What the film does nail is that uncomfortable clarity about how close this may be to reality. Even when it feels obvious, there’s something true in how it shows these men improvising their way through crises with the confidence of people who’ve never had to face consequences. It’s funny, until you realize it’s also terrifying.    Not a masterpiece, but a solid, well-paced, deliciously nasty ride with a cast clearly having fun. For what it wants to be, it delivers.

cast_names: Steve Carell, Jason Schwartzman, Cory Michael Smith, Ramy Youssef, Daniel Oreskes, Hadley Robinson, David Thompson, Ali Kinkade, Ava Kostia, Amie MacKenzie
review_content: Had very much the flavor of a four handed play that happens to be set in a fancy house (though truthfully it doesn't even seem *that* billionaire-ish. In its favor it was a good introduction to some of the weird thinking exhibited by our tech bro class and some of the performances - particularly that of Cory Michael as definitely-not-Elon-Musk - were occasionally pitch perfect. There was the occasional fun set-piece (a scene outdoors on the mountaintop for example). But (among other lapses) it's hard to believe that this group of tech titans would really be able to escape their retinues for any extended period, and most of the writing and plotting was pretty ham-fisted. I was definitely interested in the premise - I hope someone else with more heft has a go at a glimpse (satirical or realistic) inside the heads of these people...

review_content: Acabei de ver o filme Mountainhead, a primeira hora foi bastante agradavel, estavam a falar de temáticas relevantes e atuais, como o uso de LLMs com filtros e sem filtros, o seu uso malicioso para gerar fake news - mundo começa literalmente a desabar - a intenção da criação do famoso AGI, conversas sobre as pessoas virarem “super pessoas” com toda a nova tecnologia adquirida, entre outras tematicas. mas do nada na segunda parte do filme 3 dos 4 amigos unem-se para matar o 4 elemento que possui uma empresa de Inteligencia Artificial com filtros ditos seguros e fidedignos, que complementaria a IA de um deles que é o homem mais rico do mundo, mas como se recusou vender a sua empresa entao decidem mata-lo. no final fazem uma extorsão (prendem-no numa sauna e ameaçam-no queimar vivo ate ele ceder basicamente) e no final nem houve uma grande revelação, um grande plot twist, nada. o dono da casa que era o menos rico deles, “só” tinha 500M, era o unico que nao tinha atingido a casa do biliao, acaba por vender a sua empresa por 2B, mas e depois? isso acrescentou o que na narrativa? o homem mais rico do mundo e o outro que tinha a IA com filtros seguros acabam a dar um abraço e a terem uma conversa ambigua, aberta e sem uma grande conclusão ou desfecho. a personagem do Steve Carrell (um dos motivos pelo qual vi o filme) tinha cancro e simplesmente acabou com ele a seguir sozinho num carro enquanto olhava para os outros 2 abraços. Primeira parte do filme 8/10, segunda parte um 4.

cast_names: Joe Keery, Georgina Campbell, Liam Neeson, Lesley Manville, Sosie Bacon, Aaron Heffernan, Andrew Brooke, Rob Collins, Darrell D'Silva, Clare Holman
review_content: Surprisingly fun, quick flick. I'm not a fan of zombie films. That said, I still anxiously enjoyed both the horror and comedy aspects of this. The casting was brilliant, as was the production, both cgi and practical. It's mostly small-scale, but that's why it works so well. As for the story, it's over-the-top and nonsensical, but the pacing held my attention, and in the end, it all felt worthwhile. All in all, it doesn't take itself too seriously but doesn't throw it all away, either. Although, the end-credit scene wasn't necessary at all.

cast_names: Joe Keery, Georgina Campbell, Liam Neeson, Lesley Manville, Sosie Bacon, Aaron Heffernan, Andrew Brooke, Rob Collins, Darrell D'Silva, Clare Holman
review_content: “What the hells wrong with him”  “He’s got COVID”  Cold Storage was loaded with a bunch of ridiculous one-liners, fun moments and a shit ton of gore, which is everything you need for a classic horror-comedy. I'm surprised by how much I actually enjoyed this movie because I knew absolutely nothing about it going in, apart from Steve being in it, which is totally not the reason why I checked this movie out I mean come on…. WRONG of course he bloody was dingus.  The movie didn’t have many showings so I’m glad I managed to catch one of the last, and it's one that I'm super hyped | got watch on the big screen. I'm a huge sucker for anything totally bonkers or just pure chaos and this definitely delivered of those two things. It was pretty wild. I dunno why I never watched it when it first came out because it's honestly right up my street.  It’s got kind of a wacky feel to it which I loved, with a ton of comedy that made me burst with laughter. I seriously didn’t expect this to be so funny but those silly one liners completely broke me. “I think I’m fucked up now”   Joe Kerry was a huge stand out here, or should I say his hair, because I need to know how this guy has the most perfect hair in existence. I’m not too sure about his blonde look though… anyway he was great and I liked his character a lot. I thought him and Georgina Campbell were a fun little duo as well.  Of course we can’t talk about this movie without mentioning Qui Gon Jinn himself, Liam Neeson and he was fantastic as always, He was also quite the badass as well…. up until his back went. Damm backs right ?  I have no complaints about this film honestly. I enjoyed it so much and it was definitely an unexpected treat for sure as I had no idea it would be this good. I can’t wait to own this on blu ray now and place it in my horror collection. What a freaking fun movie man.   “Open your mouth I want to throw up into it”

cast_names: Joe Keery, Georgina Campbell, Liam Neeson, Lesley Manville, Sosie Bacon, Aaron Heffernan, Andrew Brooke, Rob Collins, Darrell D'Silva, Clare Holman
review_content: “What the hell ‘s wrong with him?” “He’s got Covid”  Call me easy to impress maybe but I can’t deny how much fun I had watching cold storage it’s like 28 years later and zombieland had a baby, I’m surprised most people aren’t liking it I honestly don’t have many complaints with it tbh.  The only things I would change is that I wish sosie Bacon had a bigger role than she did I really like her as an actress and she should be getting bigger roles after her performance in smile.  My other negative is actually more of a positive the writing at times ain’t the best but for this type of film it goes well with the campy tone they went with it really feels like a horror comedy.  And the comedy was 100% the best thing about it some of the dialogue and one liners were hysterical, what the people looked like when the fungus virus effects them was so freaking weird and if went well with the humour perfectly, I wouldn’t say this was horror exactly it only falls into that category because of the grossness and gory effects used. “Open your mouth I want throw up into it”  One of my main motivations to watch this of course was Steve “ the hair Harrington “ or should I now call him “teacup” wtf have he done to his hair it doesn’t suit him please change back! I’m happy he’s already doing great after stranger thing not only acting but in his music career as Djo love “the end of the beginning” anyway I thought this was a really good choice for his first film after stranger things it actually feels similar to it in some ways with the virus.  Naomi was also really good and I think her and Joe keery had pretty decent chemistry together hopefully I’ll see her in more things, I don’t think we’ll be seeing her boyfriend Mike any time soon though lol.  Liam Neeson is always a delight anytime he’s on screen a little different type of role I’m used to seeing from him it’s still comedy but kind of Sci-fi aswell haven’t seen him do anything like this before, I liked his conversations with “Abigail” who was trying to help him get what he needed to get rid of the fungus for good but the government didn’t believe it was that serious.  Enjoyed this much more than I expected didn’t go in with too many expectations and knew little about the plot very happy with cold storage my type of film really comedy mixed with horror themes and they did a great job overall 9/10.  And Btw……..  “ I think I’m fucked up now”

review_content: The Toxic Avenger was a shock standout—easily one of the most purely enjoyable new movies I’ve watched in a while. It’s unapologetically campy, knowingly cheesy, and delightfully over-the-top with its gore, leaning fully into its own absurdity. What really worked is how it channels the chaotic, irreverent energy of something like Kick-Ass or The Boys, but filters it through a grimier, weirder, more punk-rock lens.  It’s not aiming for prestige; it’s aiming for a very specific vibe, and it absolutely nails it. The tone is consistent, the humor hits, and the splatter-filled set pieces feel like a proper evolution of the cult classic while still carving out its own identity. For fans of wild, off-the-rails genre films, this is a blast.  Rating: 4/5 – 85% – Would Recommend

movie_title: If I Had Legs I'd Kick You

review_content: Holes, holes, and more holes. I can't recall the last time a film made me purposefully feel so much nauseating frustration. The camera work, sound work, dialogue, and acting all intertwine so well in achieving exactly how motherhood can feel, and I feel most people, men specifically, will still struggle to grasp an understanding of why. More so with the film's sharp symbolism, satire, and surrealism. Being able to recognise and relate, regardless of the experience required, shows the exceptionality within the writing and directing enough. I wish I had someone to talk to about this. Honestly, I might bump up the rating on a rewatch.

movie_title: If I Had Legs I'd Kick You
cast_names: Rose Byrne, Conan O'Brien, A$AP Rocky, Danielle Macdonald, Delaney Quinn, Mary Bronstein, Ivy Wolk, Mark Stolzenberg, Manu Narayan, Christian Slater
review_content: This A24 film is better than the 6.6 it gets on review sites.  It's one of those movies you'll have to watch twice in order to get all the symbolism and parallels between what's being said on the front and in the back, between scenes and between characters.  [spoiler]   Rose Byrne plays someone who has the weight of the world on her shoulders, seeking help everywhere but not getting it while trying to manouevre the world where everything is a problem, everything is made into a problem and everything become a problem... and a bigger problem and bigger. All while being the problem for other people who lead their own lives with their own problems - however big or futile they might seem.  [/spoiler]  [spoiler]  There's some great moments here: from the hole in the ceiling to the reference of abortion/birth and the "umbilical cord" scene. The neighbour yelling "fuck them crutches" (he doesn't need help, doesn't need something to lean on VS our main character and others needing it all the time). The intentional "invisibility" of the sick child that turns into something potentially very sinister towards the end of the film recalling back to the news about murder moms/babysitters... etcetera right up to the end where she is unable to go into the crashing waves that keep sending her back to her problems. There is no way out... she has no legs to kick. She is helpless... There is no peace, except for... her.  [/spoiler]  [spoiler]  Furthermore and in my personal opinion it's about the futility of therapy and so-called help. People who say they'll help but don't, helpful videos and other such things that don't solve the actual problem but merely solve what's around the problem... if at all.  [/spoiler]  [spoiler]  I'm glad they added the last few minutes on the beach. A nihilist would have ended it with black. Unless... it already did go to black according to another review/theory I saw.  [/spoiler]

movie_title: If I Had Legs I'd Kick You
cast_names: Rose Byrne, Conan O'Brien, A$AP Rocky, Danielle Macdonald, Delaney Quinn, Mary Bronstein, Ivy Wolk, Mark Stolzenberg, Manu Narayan, Christian Slater
review_content: Some structures don’t collapse all at once. First there’s a small crack, then another, until the whole ceiling starts pressing down on whoever’s standing underneath it. “If I Had Legs I’d Kick You” builds its entire experience around that feeling of gradual collapse, turning the routine of an exhausted woman into a tightening spiral of anxiety, frustration, and guilt. Mary Bronstein’s film isn’t looking for comfort or quick redemption. Instead, it watches the process of emotional wear and tear with uncomfortable honesty, creating a portrait that refuses to romanticize burnout but also refuses to simplify it. At the center of this slow implosion is Linda, played by Rose Byrne in a fearless performance, a mother and therapist trying to keep up the appearance of control while everything around her starts to give way.    The narrative follows Linda during a particularly suffocating stretch of her life. Her daughter is dealing with a mysterious illness, the apartment where they live suffers bizarre structural damage that leaves a gaping hole in the ceiling, and her support system slowly seems to dissolve in front of her. Bronstein treats this chain of events as an accumulation of small everyday catastrophes that eventually add up to something overwhelming. The question that gradually emerges is simple and devastating: who takes care of the caregiver? Linda spends her days absorbing other people’s pain, both at home and in her therapy office, but when she needs support herself, all she finds is silence or pressure. Her distant husband, medical bureaucracy, the relentless demands of motherhood, and even the patients who rely on her emotional stability all contribute to a cycle of expectations that no one could realistically meet.    Rose Byrne carries that weight with an extraordinary performance that refuses to soften the character’s most uncomfortable edges. Her Linda isn’t an idealized image of maternal resilience. She’s someone on the verge of burnout, constantly swinging between self-control and destructive impulses. Byrne works with incredibly precise physical and emotional nuance, letting exhaustion seep into every gesture, every pause, every failed attempt to keep it together. The result is a deeply human presence that still manages to generate empathy even when Linda seems completely lost.    Around her, the film builds relationships that only reinforce the feeling of isolation. Conan O’Brien appears in a surprising turn as Linda’s therapist, bringing a strangely unsettling energy. Instead of offering support, his character ends up feeling like another wall Linda keeps running into without getting any real response. Christian Slater, playing her distant husband, represents another layer of that dynamic, someone involved in the situation only from afar, delivering criticism and demands that ignore the emotional complexity Linda is dealing with. Together, these characters help paint a picture where Linda is surrounded by voices, but rarely by real listening.    One of the film’s most interesting elements is how Bronstein uses small parallel situations to mirror Linda’s psychological state. A therapy session with an anxious new mother ends in an abrupt and almost absurd way when the patient suddenly runs away and leaves her baby behind. At first, the moment feels like just another strange incident in Linda’s work routine. But as the story unfolds, it turns into an unsettling reflection. The immediate reaction is shock and judgment, but over time that image starts echoing in unexpected ways, suggesting that the emotional collapse tied to motherhood can take many forms, all of them difficult to admit.    The film also shows notable care in its formal choices. The pulsating score creates a constant sense of unease, almost as if the viewer’s own physiological rhythm is being destabilized. Anxiety isn’t just suggested here. It becomes part of the sensory experience of the film. Visually, the hole in the apartment ceiling becomes a recurring and disturbing symbol. More than just a structural issue, it turns into a metaphor for Linda’s internal state, a dark opening threatening to swallow everything around it. The image reappears in dreams and quiet moments, reinforcing the idea that the external collapse mirrors an equally deep internal one.    Another significant detail is Bronstein’s decision to keep Linda’s daughter mostly out of the visual frame for much of the film. By withholding the child’s face, the narrative shifts its focus. The issue isn’t the child herself, but the emotional impact the situation has on the mother. By avoiding turning the daughter into an object of pity or guilt, the film keeps our attention on Linda’s psychological experience and on the crushing pressure building around her.    There’s something deeply uncomfortable about the way “If I Had Legs I’d Kick You” confronts the viewer. The film offers no easy solutions and makes no attempt to lighten the weight of the issues it raises. If anything, it insists on exposing the cracks that are usually hidden beneath the social expectation of maternal strength. At a cultural moment when conversations about emotional exhaustion and mental overload are becoming more common, Bronstein’s film almost works like a magnifying mirror for those tensions.    By the time it’s over, “If I Had Legs I’d Kick You” is hard to shake off. Not necessarily because of what happens on the surface, but because of what it makes you feel as you watch this woman trying to hold everything together while the world seems to crumble around her. The question that lingers after the credits is simple and uncomfortable: who actually holds up the people who spend their lives holding everyone else up? Bronstein doesn’t give a clear answer, but she offers an honest enough portrait for that question to keep echoing long after the final scene.

movie_title: If I Had Legs I'd Kick You
cast_names: Rose Byrne, Conan O'Brien, A$AP Rocky, Danielle Macdonald, Delaney Quinn, Mary Bronstein, Ivy Wolk, Mark Stolzenberg, Manu Narayan, Christian Slater
review_content: > I just want someone to tell me what to do. No one will tell me.    It's been a while since I was left with this strong feeling of anguish and misery. Mary Bronstein signs the script and the directing, and she fully succeeds in what she sets out to do. The stress and anxiety here reach levels of what the Safdie Brothers, Darren Aronofsky, and Gaspar Noé are famously known for. Unlike _Uncut Gems (2019)_ though -- which made me actively despise its protagonist --, this film never pushed me towards hating Linda, only towards a sense of exhaustion and despair. Linda isn't sanctified or demonized, she's just human.    There's really no way to "enjoy" watching _If I Had Legs I'd Kick You (2025)_, but paradoxically, that doesn't mean it should be avoided. You just need to be prepared for what's coming at you: something that feels like a fever dream, or a bad acid trip accompanied by a relentless panic attack. By the way, what an incredible title for a work of art.    I was very surprised -- and positively impressed -- by Conan O'Brien's appearance. His portrayal of a therapist who hears but doesn't truly listen, combined with his apathy towards what Linda is desperately trying to communicate, is both terrifying and agonizing. It's emotional neglect disguised as professionalism.    And then there's Rose Byrne, delivering an absolutely astonishing performance. She carries the entire film on her shoulders. Watching Linda progressively get closer and closer to a complete mental breakdown is both moving and maddening. She's trapped in a chaotic, endless downward spiral of shit and apathetic, rude people, with no one offering even the smallest amount of help. It's really despairing to witness her struggles while being completely powerless to intervene and assist.    What makes her performance feel so raw and special is that she plays someone who is actively trying _not_ to break down. She never pushes for big emotional outbursts. Instead, it's almost like the emotions spill out against her will, the same way they do in real life. Linda is pushed far beyond her physical and mental limits, into complete depletion. It's devastating.    Although apparently kind of controversial, I actually liked the decision of having close-ups on Rose Byrne pretty much the entire film. It intensifies the range of emotions she conveys and amplifies the constant sense of discomfort that permeates every scene. More importantly, it visually reinforces Linda's isolation. We're invited to focus solely on her, to really _see_ her -- something no one else in her life seems willing to do.    Keeping her daughter mostly off-screen prevents us from developing an emotional bond with the child, forcing our attention instead onto the crushing toll motherhood takes on Linda. Her husband, heard almost exclusively through phone calls, becomes another source of tension rather than support. Instead of helping, he adds pressure through his detached, dismissive presence and lack of real concern for Linda herself.    There are moments where the film flirts with the horror genre, enhancing the paranoia and dread, and making the narrative feel increasingly unpredictable. At the same time, the story can be read in two very different ways: either as something entirely literal and grounded, or through a more allegorical, almost David Lynch-esque lens.    I've seen an interpretation that I found very interesting. From what I recall, in this reading, Linda suffers from severe psychosis. This would be supported by the hole in the ceiling manifesting her hallucinations, while everyone and everything (even the hamster) around her appears hostile and uncaring. She's probably not even a real therapist. Her patients act as a form of mirrors of herself: the young woman as her teenage self, the young man as her desire to feel wanted, the woman with the baby as another version of her own motherhood. She worries obsessively about the baby, only to suddenly abandon it. The parallel between that woman's husband on the phone and Linda's own husband reinforces this. Both women eventually run toward the sea too.    In this interpretation, Linda is traumatized by an abortion -- a literal and emotional "hole". That void echoes throughout the film: the hole in the ceiling, the hole in her daughter's stomach, the hole inside herself. It's why she sees herself as an incompetent, failed mother. From another angle, the hole in the ceiling may just as easily represent Linda's existential terror and emotional emptiness. Whether or not these readings are correct don't really matter -- I'm not entirely sure what I believe in either --, but I'm certain the film invites these kind of reflections.    _If I Had Legs I'd Kick You (2025)_ offers no romanticization of motherhood. There is no redemption arc and no journey of self-discovery. It's a demanding, tormenting experience that drains your energy completely. At the same time, it serves as a sobering reminder of how many of us are lucky enough to rely on support systems -- people who listen, who help, who allow us to take a break when life becomes overwhelming and whatever fucked-up situation gets thrown our way. It's also a reminder to check in on those we love, to _truly_ pay attention.    I can totally understand Linda running into the waves. In her place, I'd have done the same -- and probably much earlier.

review_content: Before watching this film i revisited Downton Abbey from the start. I watched the series in order all together now, the films...and in the end the finale. IMHO it is a film portraying the change of times integrating the changing of the class system and the economic crash in America. However....why did Robert have to leave? I understand the torch is passed to Mary and the final scene where she sees past memories with Mathew and the great Maggie Smith  are very emotional....but ...its all very tightly and put under the table neat. As if...to say farewell to a world we loved and lived with for many years...decades even. I m quite emotional right now after habing watched it all...I wish.....it wasnt so...I wish Robert and Cora lived on in the house Robert gave his life too and loved...i wish Mary was not alone in that big house.  .but I respect the creators vision. Goodbye Downton Abbey. You have been a part of my life for many years watching it with my mum before she passed. In a way all things seem to end and so is the way of life. Please excuse my sentimentality. I was hoping for something.....less sad more .......like Downton in its glory. Yet its a proper send off. Well done to all. You will be missed.

cast_names: Nicolas Cage, Julian McMahon, Justin Rosniak, Alexander Bertrand, Rahel Romahn, Nicholas Cassim, Finn Little, Charlotte Maggi, Nina Young, Miranda Tapsell
review_content: A man, originally from Luna Bay, Australia, engulfed in a unyielding shadow of his childhood past connected to this place, before moving to California with his mother, returns with his son with one goal: to share a loving father-son moment of surfing in the same waters as he once did, with a twist.    He also wants to surprise him with a view of a house, the same one his father owned: a house he's been devoting his life and career to amass the necessary funds to purchase it, presumably at the cost of his marriage, wrapped in the reveal that it's for him and both of them. There's one obstacle: the Bay Boys, a gang of tribalistic surfers. Led by yuppie pseudo-guru Scally (Julian McMahon), they thwart him and his son from surfing there, subsequently turning their hostility up a notch by continuing to hound and torment him.    Descent into madness; Nicolas Cage's forté, his triumphant bread-and-butter. This is him at his best, either when his characters start conventional and tame, with sound configuration of the mind, psychologically and emotionally, only to descend into madness and crazed lunacy, gradually unraveling and sinking into deeper levels of unhinged craziness, whether that continues to be a dominant and prominent aspect from start to finish or tapers off, or when they're already like that from the get-go.    His performance was unsurprisingly effective, at least to those subscribed to the trailblazing apotheosis of Nicolas Cage: Hollywood's cornerstone of an immortalized actor and a Renaissance man. Some people may see him only as a mediocre actor. I disagree. In this particular film, Cage wasn't the sole highlight performance. Julian McMahon (Scally) (Rest in Peace, by the way; he died from lung metastasis at age 56 on the 2nd of July earlier this year) stood out, too, as well as Nicholas Cassim's performance (the homeless guy, credited as "The Bum"), particularly at the end.    The story offers a surface-level take on tribalistic, toxic masculinity, devoid of further exploration. Moreover, it didn't go anywhere. There was barely any payoff/climax, leaving you hanging, unless the semblance of a payoff and climax through Cage's character ultimately achieving his goal/mission of surfing with his son and Scally getting his comeuppance serves as a sufficient alternative.    Nevertheless, there was enjoyment here for me. I just think this film could've been even better; it was on the cusp. Something was missing, and I don't know what it is.    I'm reminded of Riley Stearns' _The Art of Self-Defense_ in 2019 (an even better film, with more of a lasting and palpable air of uneasiness and slight eeriness. A karate Sensei antagonist in that, a yuppie guru in this. Personifications of toxic masculinity: two exploitative men using the abstract concept of masculinity to twist the minds of other men and taking advantage of them under the guise of the absurd ideology of a "real man," what constitutes a "real man," and the process of becoming one; utter nonsense.    It highlights how easy it is for the wrong people, who present themselves as a role model in sheep's clothing, to infiltrate and worm their way into certain people, influencing them in all of the toxic and dangerous ways, particularly men, and especially young(er) men: a very real, tragic thing that's spreading like a plague more forcefully and energetically than ever.    The missing thing could be that it wasn't entirely cut from the same cloth as _The Art of Self-Defense_; it didn't tap more into (and fully embrace) the initial, unsettling undertone and uneasiness.    The beautifully vibrant and serene visuals were excellent. The overall cinematography, from particular camera angles to some of the camerawork, was well done, especially the warped editing of the shot as Cage's character is getting increasingly disoriented due to the overwhelming heat and lack of food and drink at 0:53:30, the overhead, twirling shot when he woke up at 1:18:05, and the editing of the spinning shot of him in the center, with everyone around him edited into the shot and spinning, as they're all high at 1:20:13; that overall scene, in particular, was filmed and edited splendidly.    François Tétaz's occasionally eerie score, embodying a similar-sounding composition as scores in films from the 1930s/1940s, specifically horror films (and I haven't even watched any of them yet; cultural osmosis is a powerful thing), was just as excellent. The titular cue, "The Surfer," stood out the most; that electric guitar sounded heavenly.

review_content: Surprised by all the “incoherent story” comments. This plays on both a literal and satirical level without requiring much imagination to bridge gaps. A kid raised to idolize violence, pain, and sacrifice as the pinnacle of manhood eventually reaches the logical conclusion of those teachings. The survivors of that cycle reinforce the system not out of malice, but as a way to give meaning to their own suffering. It’s what worked for them. It’s how they were raised.    The Biblical imagery (which the poster isn’t exactly subtle about) frames Cam as a potential cycle-breaker, someone who might finally deliver us from CTEvil. There are obvious jabs at the NFL throughout, but football itself isn’t really the boogeyman here. It feels more like a stand-in for any zero-sum hierarchy where careers are nasty, brutish, and short.    My one real qualm, beyond the clumsy “I AM FOOTBALL” line, is the ending’s reliance on spectacle. The resolution feels intentionally over the top, as if the filmmakers couldn’t imagine dismantling a system like this without escalating to operatic violence. But maybe that’s the point; maybe tearing down long-standing social structures was never going to be clean or polite.     Ultimately it's an entertaining film with great performances that left me thinking, and that's more than enough to be worth a stream this weekend.

review_content: I went into HIM expecting the worst after hearing how brutal the reactions were—but to my surprise, this turned out to be a genuinely engaging psychological thriller. It has that “A24-lite” sensibility: a grounded concept with an off-kilter tone, unsettling atmosphere, and a narrative that leans more on mood than spectacle. It’s not really a home run for any specific audience—horror fans may want more scares, football fans may want more football, and concept-film fans may want something deeper—but it threads the needle well enough to create a compelling experience. It sits in a weird middle lane, but that’s part of its charm, and it kept me hooked the whole way through.  Rating: 3/5 – 75% – Worth Watching

cast_names: Marlon Wayans, Tyriq Withers, Julia Fox, Tim Heidecker, Jim Jefferies, Maurice Greene, Indira G. Wilson, Heather Lynn Harris, Don Benjamin, Guapdad 4000
review_content: I didn't really know what to expect going into Him; I was intrigued by the trailers and Jordan Peele's producer credit, but the lacklustre critical and audience responses to its release definitely tempered my expectations.  That said, I was surprised by how much I enjoyed it overall.  It's certainly lacking in some areas: the editing can make it hard to follow certain scenes, the CGI x-ray sections have promise but end up just looking kinda cheesy, and some ideas the film seems to be developing early on end up dropped without a good reason.  I don't often say this, but I think this movie would have benefited from being a bit longer, as it feels like it's trying to fit too much into a brief runtime. However, I liked the film's exploration of themes of nepotism, celebrity worship and the cult of personality, and the all-consuming drive to be "The One".  In terms of films I've seen, it felt like it mixed elements of Whiplash, Fight Club, and The Neon Demon into an intriguing new combination that had me guessing at what was real vs. in Cameron's head until the end of the movie.  Plus it's a really visually appealing film; the editing may not always hold up, but the sets, colours, and cinematography always kept me invested in whatever was happening on-screen.  Maybe there are other films that tackle its subject matter in more interesting or profound ways, and that's why some people didn't think it hung together as well?  I don't know, but with the context I had at the time of initial viewing, I think Him is a compelling movie that I don't at all regret checking out.

review_content: It's good for describing everything that lead to the final dive, but there's almost nothing about that fateful dive if not the last 3-4 minutes of the documentary. I was expecting at least 10-20% of the film about the last dive, but I was wrong. All in all, I still recommend it. It's a good insight into an issue that happens in many big companies and corporations: visionary, delusional, and arrogant CEOs that eventually destroy their company with their own hands by silencing inside critics and constructive feedbacks. Unfortunately, this time it lead to the deaths of five human beings. You can't be arrogant with science, and this is evidence of the consequences of doing just so.

review_content: Some films, even when technically solid, just don’t leave a mark. iHostage is one of them. Based on a real case, it has moments of tension and does what it’s supposed to, but it fails to deliver on an emotional level. The direction is competent, the pacing holds up, and the atmosphere builds suspense fairly well. The issue is: there’s no emotional weight. The characters aren't well developed, you don’t connect with them, and as a result, you don’t really care what happens.    Even though it tries to keep us on edge, you never truly worry about anyone. And if you don’t care what happens to the characters, it’s hard to feel invested in the story. The harsh reality behind the real events could’ve been used for something far more powerful, but here it just becomes a sequence of tense scenes that don’t quite land.    It’s not a bad movie—watchable, and some might enjoy it more than I did. But that buzz about it being “unmissable” is clearly overhyped. iHostage doesn’t offend, but it certainly doesn’t move you either.

review_content: As a longtime Silent Hill fan who's replayed the games countless times (Silent Hill 2 is still my gold standard for psychological horror), I was pumped when I heard Christophe Gans was returning after the solid first movie back in 2006. That original film nailed the foggy atmosphere and creepy vibes, so my expectations were sky-high for Return to Silent Hill.    Unfortunately, it fell flat for me in a lot of ways. The special effects and CGI look pretty dated and cheap. (Especially the monsters.) Pyramid Head and the other creatures felt more like rubbery suits with bad digital overlays than the genuinely unsettling designs from the games. They just didn't hit that same level of dread or body horror that made the franchise so iconic.    That said, the cast had some good moments - Jeremy Irvine does a decent job carrying the emotional weight as James, and there are flashes where the performances feel authentic and raw. The atmosphere tries to recapture that oppressive Silent Hill feel, but overall, it didn't live up to what I hoped for after all these years.

review_content: She Rides Shotgun takes its time to settle in, but when it hits, it really hits. It feels like a modern western mixed with a road movie and a deeply human drama. There are no flashy twists or forced spectacle here. This is a film about people, old wounds, and the fragile, powerful bond that forms when a father has to protect his daughter even though he doesn’t fully know how.    Taron Egerton is excellent. At times he hints at a future action hero, but what truly works are the quieter moments, when fear, clumsiness, and guilt show through. His character is far from perfect, and that’s exactly why it works. He learns on the run, makes mistakes, flees… and still tries to do the right thing.    The real revelation, though, is Ana Sophia Heger. Her performance is remarkable. She never overplays a scene or reaches for easy emotion. A look, a pause, a silence — that’s where she shines. It’s an incredibly difficult role for someone so young, and she carries it with astonishing confidence.    The direction is measured and confident. Violence appears only when necessary and never overstays its welcome. The emotional weight comes from restraint, not manipulation. Yes, it can make you cry — it certainly did for me — but those tears come from something real.    It may follow a familiar structure, but the honesty of its emotions makes that feel irrelevant. A tough, tender, deeply human film that lingers well after it ends.

cast_names: Dave Bautista, Jack Champion, Sophia Lillis, Whitney Peak, Bobby Cannavale, Kate del Castillo, Tony Dalton, Inde Navarrette, Zaire Adams, Blu del Barrio
review_content: There’s no trick here: the film knows exactly what it is and doesn’t try to hide it. Straightforward action, gunfire, chases, a clearly juvenile tone, and a story that moves forward without stopping too much to think about whether everything makes sense or not. And, oddly enough, that honesty works in its favor. It doesn’t aspire to be smarter than it is, nor to disguise its seams.    Everything revolves around Dave Bautista, who once again delivers sheer physical presence. It’s not the most complex role of his career, and it doesn’t need to be. His charisma and the way he fills the screen carry much of the runtime, even when the script starts repeating ideas or leaning heavily on well-worn clichés of action cinema involving cartels, special forces, and young people in over their heads.    The pacing is one of its strongest points. It doesn’t linger, it doesn’t turn solemn, and it doesn’t get lost in unnecessary subplots. It goes straight to the point most of the time, which makes many of its implausibilities easy to accept. When there’s no time to think, there’s also no time to question too much of what you’re watching.    The problem arises when it tries to mix tones. At times it feels like it wants to be a carefree teenage adventure, and at others it turns serious, violent, even dark. That combination doesn’t always fit, leaving the sense that the film never fully decides what kind of story it wants to tell. It doesn’t break it, but it does make it somewhat uneven.    Narratively, it’s very basic. There are no real surprises or twists that change the game. Everything works through the accumulation of action scenes and the energy of its lead. If you’re looking for depth, nuance, or something that lingers afterward, you won’t find it here. This is fast consumption, almost late-night video-store fare.    Overall, it’s an entertaining, occasionally effective, and clearly forgettable film. It works as uncomplicated action filler, goes down easily, and little more. It doesn’t deceive, it doesn’t promise anything it won’t deliver, and it leans on its star to get by. Sometimes, that’s enough.

review_content: Regretting You translates Colleen Hoover’s novel into a clean, accessible romantic drama that leans heavily on its cast to carry the emotional weight. The story follows Morgan Grant (Allison Williams) and her daughter Clara (Mckenna Grace) as they navigate the fallout of a devastating accident and the secrets it uncovers, forcing both to confront betrayal, grief, and the messy process of rebuilding trust.     🎭 Performances  The film’s strongest asset is its acting. Williams brings a quiet, wounded maturity to Morgan, while Grace continues her streak of emotionally articulate teen roles. Their dynamic feels lived‑in, and the tension between them—rooted in miscommunication and buried pain—gives the film its most compelling moments. Dave Franco and Mason Thames round out the cast with solid supporting turns that never overshadow the central mother‑daughter arc.     🎬 Direction & Tone  Director Josh Boone opts for a restrained, character‑first approach. The pacing is deliberate, sometimes to a fault, but it allows the emotional beats to breathe. The film avoids melodrama more often than expected, though certain revelations still land with the predictability of a YA‑to‑screen adaptation.     🎥 Visual & Technical Notes  Tim Orr’s cinematography gives the film a warm, autumnal palette that suits its themes of loss and renewal. The 116‑minute runtime feels slightly stretched, but the editing keeps the narrative coherent and accessible. Nathaniel Walcott’s score is understated, supporting the drama without overwhelming it.     ❤ Emotional Impact  The emotional core works. Even when the script leans into familiar tropes, the sincerity of the performances keeps the story grounded. The film’s exploration of grief, forgiveness, and the complicated bond between mothers and daughters resonates, especially for viewers who appreciate character‑driven drama.    ⚖ Verdict  Regretting You doesn’t reinvent the genre, but it delivers a heartfelt, well‑acted adaptation that honors its source material. It’s polished, earnest, and emotionally engaging—just not particularly surprising.

review_content: The good sides: the visual style of the movie is very interesting — quirky and unusual. It feels like a dark fable of sorts, with a haunting yet whimsical atmosphere. It’s another story about a girl forming a friendship with a man, and it’s engaging to watch how their relationship develops. Mads Mikkelsen is excellent in this role, it suits him perfectly.  The bad sides: the story leaves you with many questions about the characters, their backgrounds, and what’s going on in general — such as why someone is after the girl or who is the main character — but unfortunately, none of these questions are answered. This makes the story feel unfinished, almost like the first episode of a TV show, except it isn’t one.

cast_names: Jeremy Allen White, Jeremy Strong, Paul Walter Hauser, Stephen Graham, Odessa Young, Gaby Hoffmann, Marc Maron, David Krumholtz, Harrison Gilbertson, Grace Gummer
review_content: Like the album, Nebraska, the movie is a somber and intense emotional journey. It’s slow, lacks any huge climax or payoff, but you understand exactly why it needs to be that way.    Though I would say that in many ways this is a movie about depression more than it’s about Springsteen. The film depicts it extraordinarily well to the point where at times it was uncomfortably accurate. The distancing from others, the isolation, lying in bed all day, and the bouts of frustration and rage that often boils over.    However, I wanted this movie to be better. As a Springsteen fan, I wasn't completely sold that JAW was the right choice to play him.

cast_names: Jeremy Allen White, Jeremy Strong, Paul Walter Hauser, Stephen Graham, Odessa Young, Gaby Hoffmann, Marc Maron, David Krumholtz, Harrison Gilbertson, Grace Gummer
review_content: The best thing a music biopic can do is send you home wanting to play an album. Springsteen: Deliver Me From Nowhere does exactly that. Instead of stadium anthems and triumphant montages, it places you in a small, almost claustrophobic room where a thin, restless artist records Nebraska as if whispering a confession.    Scott Cooper keeps the film deliberately restrained. He avoids spectacle in favour of a dry, introspective tone that can feel distant at times. For viewers expecting a conventional rise-and-fall biography, this may seem underwhelming. But that narrow focus is precisely what sets it apart from assembly-line rock biopics. This isn’t about glory; it’s about isolation and the need to strip everything back.    Jeremy Allen White delivers a remarkably nuanced performance. Rather than impersonating Springsteen, he captures an emotional essence —the hesitation, the inwardness, the weight of expectation. His vocal work adds authenticity, and there are moments when you forget you’re watching an actor playing an icon. Instead, you see a man struggling to reconcile success with inner emptiness.    The film isn’t flawless. Some flashbacks to childhood and the strained father relationship feel overly familiar, and at times there’s a faint whiff of reverence that softens the edges of what could have been a harsher portrait. Structurally, it remains more conventional than its tone suggests.    Where it truly comes alive is in the creative process. The lo-fi recording sessions, the refusal to polish imperfections, the anxiety about alienating audiences —those scenes carry a quiet power. You feel the solitude that shaped Nebraska, and the artistic risk behind choosing vulnerability over spectacle.    It’s not monumental cinema. It’s a character study —sometimes uneven, but sincere. And if it leaves you listening to “Atlantic City” with fresh ears, then it has done something meaningful.

cast_names: George Clooney, Adam Sandler, Laura Dern, Billy Crudup, Riley Keough, Grace Edwards, Stacy Keach, Jim Broadbent, Patrick Wilson, Eve Hewson
review_content: There’s something deeply sad about Jay Kelly, but not a forced or solemn sadness — it’s the kind that sneaks in quietly. From the very beginning, it’s clear this isn’t about glamour or Hollywood mythology, but about what’s left when the spotlight goes out and you’re no longer sure who you are without it. The film moves at an unhurried pace, sometimes almost shy, and trusts looks and silences more than emphasis.    George Clooney feels surprisingly exposed here. Not because he does something radically different from before, but because for once he seems willing to accept weariness, melancholy, and a certain sense of emptiness without constantly trying to be likable. It’s a restrained, finely tuned performance built on pauses and small gestures, and without making any noise, it ends up being one of his most honest roles.    Adam Sandler is the real surprise. Far from any histrionics, he plays a fragile, tired character who’s far more complex than he first appears. He doesn’t steal scenes or push emotions — he’s simply there, supporting the tone. Even supporting actors like Stacey Keach, so far removed from his tougher or more iconic roles, bring an unexpected humanity that adds a great deal.    The film isn’t perfect. At times it lingers too long in its own world and risks becoming self-absorbed. Some scenes could have been tighter, and a few narrative detours don’t fully land. Still, when it works — and it often does — it achieves something difficult: talking about the cost of fame without cynicism, cruelty, or preaching.    Jay Kelly is a crepuscular tragicomedy, emotional and occasionally painful, that works best when it allows itself to be small. It doesn’t shout or chase easy applause. It’s one of those films that quietly stays with you afterward, like an uncomfortable but honest memory. And these days, that already counts for a lot.

cast_names: George Clooney, Adam Sandler, Laura Dern, Billy Crudup, Riley Keough, Grace Edwards, Stacy Keach, Jim Broadbent, Patrick Wilson, Eve Hewson
review_content: Some have called this a case of George Clooney playing George Clooney. It's hard for audiences to know for sure but certainly some of the elements reflect milestones and events in Clooney's career even if the family elements are mostly fictional. It's enjoyable, has some nicely added characters with at least a little bit of backstory, and i felt Adam Sandler showed again that he fits very well into straight, non comical performances. I particularly liked how the writers/director/actors threaded the needle of showing on one hand theirs was a financially dependent manager-talent relationship, while still showing they developed a genuine, valued human connection. A difficult balancing act, but it was a believable portrayal of how friendships can form in many ways and yet still be significant. An enjoyable film. I liked it a lot.

review_content: I think that this movie is terribly misunderstood.  The film explores the price of fame, and those who gladly choose to pursue it at the cost of... well, the cost will vary from one person to the next.  I think that is what makes Sandler's character so interesting.  He does have an understanding of what he is missing out on in life, but he keeps going back to the well.   He was in love with his client, and the client was in love with fame.  In that sense, it kind of reminded me of "Love, Actually", where the aging rock star announced that the love of his life was his manager. review_content: Olivia Holt and Connor Swindells steal Christmas in the _Jingle Bell Heist_. A cybercriminal recruits a department store sales clerk to help him pull a jewelry heist, but when that falls through they set their sights higher and go after the store owner’s personal vault. Both Holt and Swindells give good performances, and the writers do a nice job at making them sympathetic characters. And the production values are quite impressive; especially the soundtrack, which brings a lot of energy to the film. However, the actual heist ends up begin fairly mundane and not as interesting as the prep work that they do to setup the heist.  Yet despite a weak spots, _Jingle Bell Heist_ is a fun and entertaining holiday film.

cast_names: Vahid Mobasseri, Mariam Afshari, Ebrahim Azizi, Hadis Pakbaten, Majid Panahi, Mohamad Ali Elyasmehr, George Hashemzadeh, Delmaz Najafi, Omid Reza, Ali Rastegari
review_content: A story centered on a very familiar yet endlessly compelling dilemma: is revenge worth it? Should we lower ourselves to the level of those harmed us, or should we rise above it? And ultimately, what is justice?    Once we reach the main plot point, the film is suddenly injected with a surge of energy. Each new character introduced brings a distinct and welcome dynamic to the group. There's also a good amount of dark, chaotic, and situational comedy throughout, which I really enjoyed and I wasn't expecting. I got the impression that every character already knew, from the very first moment, that the man inside the box was indeed their torturer. Their hesitation feels less like doubt and more like fear -- fear of fully committing to what they're about to do -- making it easier to subconsciously question whether he truly is "their man". I'm not familiar with any of the actors, but they do a tremendous job. I also love how the title ties into the narrative.    You can clearly see _It Was Just an Accident (2025)_ is a work of someone who masters the art of cinema. The control Pahani has over his film is impressive. The sharp dialogue and long takes open space for intriguing discussions and clashing worldviews. However, towards the end of the second act and the beginning of the third, these conversations and situations start to feel repetitive, causing the film to lose a significant amount of momentum. Even when the long-awaited confrontation finally arrives, it didn't quite connect with me emotionally. That said, the very final shot is minimalist and absolutely gut-punching -- a haunting closing note that will stay with you forever.    Through this story, Jafar Panahi presents a striking snapshot of the Iranian society, filtered through his personal experiences and perspective on the current regime. It's pretty much guerrilla filmmaking from someone who refuses to be silenced or to give up. It's brave, thoughtful, and unsettling. I'm very much looking forward to exploring more of his work.
