cast_names: Vahid Mobasseri, Mariam Afshari, Ebrahim Azizi, Hadis Pakbaten, Majid Panahi, Mohamad Ali Elyasmehr, George Hashemzadeh, Delmaz Najafi, Omid Reza, Ali Rastegari
review_content: Given the recent wave of protests, a film about the effects of Iran's brutal regime on its own people is particularly timely. The fact that this wave has subsided is largely due to the increased use of violence against protesters, as well as the fact that opposition members are unable to organize due to the state's excessive surveillance. This general distrust is very evident in "It Was Just an Accident."     The film follows a group of former political prisoners who come across their alleged tormentor. The plot, which primarily concerns how the group should respond to this revelation, is consistently suspenseful and conveys a great deal of tension. Visually, I found the film less convincing, though it is not bad. And, in any case, the story is the most important aspect of this work, and director Jafar Panahi deserves all the credit in this regard.    As long as the mullah regime remains in power, more stories like "It Was Just an Accident" or "The Seed of the Sacred Fig" will no doubt emerge. And they deserve to be heard.

cast_names: Vahid Mobasseri, Mariam Afshari, Ebrahim Azizi, Hadis Pakbaten, Majid Panahi, Mohamad Ali Elyasmehr, George Hashemzadeh, Delmaz Najafi, Omid Reza, Ali Rastegari
review_content: Despite what the title might suggest, “It Was Just an Accident” is a film of absolute control, the kind where every frame, every pause, and every physical movement feels meticulously designed to keep the viewer in a constant state of moral tension. Jafar Panahi builds the narrative like a silent emotional trap: it all starts in a banal, domestic way, with a family driving through the night, until a small event (running over a dog) shifts the course of the story. This seemingly trivial act works as a warning of what’s to come, because Panahi makes it clear early on that under oppressive regimes, nothing is ever truly small or isolated. Chance, here, is just the surface of something far deeper and far more corrosive.    The script unfolds like a moral thriller that rejects any conventional mystery structure. The uncertainty surrounding the identity of the captured man isn’t there to create suspense in the traditional sense, but to expose the internal fractures of characters shaped by extreme experiences of institutional violence. Panahi shifts the focus from the question “who is he?” to “what should we do with him?,” turning the film into a disturbing study of justice, revenge, and memory. The narrative refuses to offer easy answers, choosing instead to follow the psychological erosion of people who, even when physically free, remain trapped by traumas that shape every decision they make.    The film’s power lies in how it translates the experience of repression into concrete actions and physical spaces. The car, the van, the desert, the workshop, all of them work as symbolic extensions of invisible prisons. Panahi handles space with remarkable intelligence, moving between suffocating confinement and the false sense of freedom suggested by the vastness of the desert. Even when the characters are in open environments, the mise-en-scène makes it clear there is no real escape, only different degrees of emotional and moral imprisonment.    Visually, “It Was Just an Accident” embraces restraint, which only amplifies its impact. The cinematography avoids obvious stylization, opting instead for a raw, realistic approach, punctuated by precise choices of light and color that evoke specific mental states. The recurring use of red tones in key moments isn’t arbitrary: they act as memory triggers, linking trauma, violence, and guilt in images that linger long after the scene is over. Panahi’s camera observes more than it judges, creating a distance that forces the viewer into an active position when facing the dilemma at the film’s core.    The performances are one of the film’s strongest pillars. The cast works with impressive restraint, avoiding melodramatic outbursts in favor of minimal gestures, loaded silences, and looks that say more than any line of dialogue. Each character carries a different way of dealing with the past, and the script is careful to place them in conflict not only with one another, but with themselves. The result is a set of deeply human performances, where pain isn’t displayed as spectacle, but as something internalized, corrosive, and persistent.    One of the most surprising aspects of “It Was Just an Accident” is the humor that emerges in the middle of the tragedy. Panahi introduces almost absurd situations, small everyday frictions, and ironic bits of dialogue that momentarily relieve the tension without ever diluting what’s at stake. This humor doesn’t function as escapism, but as a narrative strategy, reminding us that even in extreme contexts, life insists on surfacing in all its strangeness and contradiction.    In the end, the film stands as a powerful reflection on the ethical limits of survival under regimes of systematic violence. Panahi offers neither catharsis nor clear redemption; instead, he confronts us with the idea that justice, when contaminated by trauma, can easily turn into yet another form of oppression. “It Was Just an Accident” is a quietly devastating film, operating with surgical precision and leaving deep marks precisely because it refuses to simplify human suffering. It’s political cinema in the most radical sense of the term: not one that points the way forward, but one that forces the audience to face uncomfortable questions, and live with them.

review_content: Oh. What. Fun. is perfectly serviceable holiday streaming fare, but it never really rises above that label. The cast is noticeably stronger than the material they’re given, which makes the whole thing feel like a bit of a missed opportunity. There are moments that work and a few mild chuckles along the way, but the movie largely coasts on familiarity rather than doing anything memorable or inventive.  For a holiday movie you throw on without much expectation, it does the job. It’s fine, disposable, and easy to forget once the season passes—which, for this type of streaming release, is kind of the point.  Rating: 2/5 – 65% – Not Recommended to Everyone

review_content: Yet again, Edward Berger proves his masterclass in directing. I absolutely love the locations and landscapes. The almost organised chaos of it all between hollowed-out echo chambers of emotion. I don't know what they did, but even the colours on items like clothing seemed neon. The characters were all so full of colour too. Farrell and Swinton both always seem to give it their all. As for the story, I do think the tension and depth could've been stronger, but point to point, it was pretty good. I was glued the whole way through, eyes and ears. I'd watch it all again, just for the credits scene. It really was the cherry on top.

review_content: Director Edward Berger has already demonstrated his style with "All Quiet on the Western Front" and "Conclave." However, his latest film, "Ballad of a Small Player," lacks substance. Colin Farrell deliversa truly outstanding performance, and the film looks fantastic. But the story of a notorious gambler is far too superficial to be captivating. And all of the excess quickly becomes monotonous and, in some moments, appears pretentious. For me, there was never a sense of suspense. I believe the film is still worth seeing for its stunning visuals, but hope Berger chooses a more intriguing story next time.

review_content: You could already tell from the first installment—which was nothing more than a lackluster remake of the original—that the creators of the new “The Strangers” trilogy probably didn’t have a single idea that could justify three new films for the franchise. But while the first one at least had some semblance of structure, “Chapter 2” fails to impress on virtually every front. The world is completely implausible and empty, and not a single character behaves even remotely like a real person.     If I had to single anyone out for praise, it would be lead actress Madelaine Petsch, who at least gives it her all. But that’s simply not enough to save the film from boring strangers and a truly awful CGI wild boar. Particularly weak, however, is the attempt to give the eponymous Strangers a backstory. Not only does this approach reduce the entire concept of the film to absurdity, but the story is also really boring and dumb. Accordingly, I cannot recommend this “Strangers” film in any way.

cast_names: Madelaine Petsch, Froy Gutierrez, Gabriel Basso, Ema Horvath, Brooke Lena Johnson, Richard Brake, Pedro Leandro, Rachel Shenton, Janis Ahern, JR Esposito
review_content: The Strangers: Chapter 2 continues this new trilogy experiment — and I’m still not totally sold on the concept. The original worked because of its simplicity. Random terror. Isolated dread. No over-explanation. Stretching this into a single, continuous trilogy story inevitably chips away at that mystique.  It’s not awful by any means. There are still tense moments, solid atmosphere, and flashes of what made the series effective in the first place. But it feels like it’s losing some of the magic. The more mythology and connective tissue you add, the less raw and unpredictable it becomes.  That’s the frustrating part — the potential is massive. Instead, this feels like a serviceable horror sequel rather than something that elevates the franchise.  Rating: 2/5 – 65% – Not Recommended to Everyone

review_content: scream @ this riveting reveal of the backstory behind the Tamara of "is Tamara home?"     ---    so on the one hand this is pretty fucking bad and feels like 20 minutes of film stretched into nearly 100(?!) which is never a good sign. i checked the time duration at what felt like the end or near the end point as things felt like they were wrapping up and there was still another 40 minutes to go, oop.     having said that, this does have something of a sense of energy to it which was completely lacking in the first chapter, if only because of it moving away from straight redoing the original film into more new material. most of said material here is... clownish at best but the constant state of motion (this plays like an extended, movie length chase scene for the most part) makes a bit of a difference if nothing else.    the whole foolery with "so who are the Strangers" leading to every person that breathes in the movie being so severely repulsive it effectively turns any place with someone in it into an uninhabitable zone was hilarious. when it turns out the sleazy roommate isn't in fact the sack head bitch but just like That... and of course there's the bit with the boar which felt like it was a random excerpt of unhinged misery that unfolded endlessly in the 2013 Tomb Raider game (really sad to have had this spoiled for me because if i went into this not knowing i probably would've popped the fuck off lmao), where does one even begin with that.    typing this up the afternoon after watching it last night i feel like this sounds more fun than it actually is. like any bits of the above on their own (let alone all in one place as shown here) could've made for an all timer of a viewing experience in something that wasn't such a fucking slog? who knows maybe chapter 3 is going to just pull something out of its ass and click into place fully (probs not) when it gets dumped on streaming services out of the blue on a random thursday in 2026. i don't think this series has enough fanfare/popularity to have a passionate fan throwing together a fan-cut/edit collecting the highlights of the three parts into one non-bloated work but i would sit through that.

review_content: So close to being a solid 7/10. It kept me engaged and sort of informed the whole way through. Plus it acknowledged the simple fact of it being a dramatisation. My biggest criticism of most biopics, including "Swiped", is the level of idealisation given to certain characters. It feels more like publicity than authenticity, which, by the end, does more harm to the retelling as it takes away human flaws from reality. Then again, I can recount Whitney's story through bullet points, and it wouldn't really change much. Although the portrayals of the emotional turmoil through the acting and directing were probably the most memorable scenes. I'd swipe left on biopic and maybe right on filmmaking.

cast_names: Lily James, Ben Schnetzer, Myha'la, Jackson White, Dan Stevens, Ian Colletti, Mary Neely, Ana Yi Puig, Aidan Laprete, Pedro Correa
review_content: > *"In the algorithm of modern romance, she didn't just want to be a variable—she wanted to rewrite the code."*    Rachel Lee Goldenberg's incisive tech-world biopic transcends its conventional trappings to deliver a sharply observed portrait of ambition, betrayal, and resilience in the male-dominated startup ecosystem. *Swiped* chronicles the extraordinary journey of Whitney Wolfe Herd (Lily James) from eager entrepreneur to the visionary founder of Bumble, mapping her path through the toxic bro culture that both shaped and nearly destroyed her.    ### **Beyond the Girlboss Narrative: A Trojan Horse Critique**  Beneath its glossy surface and familiar biopic beats, *Swiped* operates as a sophisticated examination of corporate feminism's limitations. The film initially presents as a conventional empowerment story but gradually reveals its sharper edges, particularly in its unflinching depiction of how systemic misogyny operates in modern tech spaces.    - **The Performance of Empowerment**: Whitney's early success at Tinder comes at a cost the film doesn't shy from examining—[spoiler]her initial complicity in overlooking safety concerns affecting female users to maintain her hard-won position[/spoiler]. This moral complexity elevates the film beyond simple hagiography.  - **The Architecture of Exclusion**: The film meticulously documents how casual sexism becomes institutionalized, from [spoiler]the "Tinder" name's origins in frat house humor to the systematic devaluation of Whitney's contributions[/spoiler].    ### **Performance as Psychological Excavation**  Lily James delivers career-best work, transforming what could have been a one-dimensional girlboss archetype into a fully realized, flawed human being. Her performance finds particular power in the film's darker stretches:    - **The Descent**: James viscerally captures Whitney's psychological unraveling following her ouster from Tinder, with [spoiler]the harassment campaign rendered through disorienting cinematography that mirrors her fractured mental state[/spoiler].  - **Moral Ambiguity**: The actress excels in portraying Whitney's complicated relationship with power—both her desire for it and her discomfort with its exercise.    The supporting cast equally impresses, particularly Jackson White's terrifying portrayal of Justin Mateen's [spoiler]swift transformation from charming romantic interest to controlling abuser[/spoiler], and Dan Stevens' unexpectedly nuanced turn as Russian investor Andrey Andreev.    ### **Visual Language: The Aesthetics of Disruption**  Goldenberg and cinematographer Doug Emmett employ a sophisticated visual strategy that subtly comments on the narrative:    - **The Office as Character**: Production design perfectly captures the specific aesthetic of 2010s startup culture—the adult playgrounds that masked deeply traditional power structures.  - **Color Psychology**: The evolution from Tinder's fiery red palette to Bumble's sunny yellow visualizes the fundamental philosophical shift between the platforms.    ### **Structural Innovation and Thematic Depth**  The film's division into two distinct acts allows for expansive exploration of its central questions:    - **The Cost of Compromise**: The first act builds to [spoiler]Whitney's painful realization that her hard-won seat at the table required accepting a broken system[/spoiler].  - **Redefining Success**: The second act complicatedly explores whether Bumble represents genuine innovation or merely rebranding, particularly through [spoiler]Whitney's fraught partnership with Andreev, whose own scandal forces her to confront difficult ethical choices[/spoiler].    ### **Cultural Context and Lasting Relevance**  *Swiped* arrives at a moment of particular resonance as we reevaluate tech's social impact:    - **The Dating App Reckoning**: The film serves as a prehistory to our current moment of dating app fatigue and connection crisis.  - **Post-Girlboss Reflection**: Arriving after the decline of the "girlboss" era, the film benefits from historical perspective, examining both achievements and limitations.    ### **Technical Excellence and Narrative Craft**  - **Editing Rhythm**: The pacing masterfully balances corporate drama with psychological depth.  - **Sound Design**: The strategic use of smartphone notifications creates a constant low-level anxiety.  - **Production Design**: Meticulous recreation of startup culture's specific aesthetic evolution.    ### **Cultural Impact and Conversation**  The film contributes meaningfully to ongoing discussions about:  - Technology's role in shaping human connection  - The limits of leaning in versus burning down structures  - How good intentions can become compromised by growth imperatives    ### **Defining Sequences**  [spoiler]  - **The College Launch**: Whitney and Tisha's coordinated campus marketing demonstrates her genius for understanding social dynamics  - **The Harassment Montage**: A disorienting descent into online abuse following her Tinder ousting  - **The Bumble Pitch**: Whitney articulating her vision for an app where "women make the first move"  - **The Investor Reckoning**: [spoiler]Facing another scandal, Whitney must choose between loyalty and principle[/spoiler]  [/spoiler]    ### **Why It Deserves Your Attention**  Beyond its status as an engaging corporate drama, *Swiped* succeeds as a timely examination of the spaces where technology, gender, and power intersect. It understands that true disruption isn't just about building better apps but about challenging the systems that determine who gets to build them.    **Content Warnings**: Emotional abuse, workplace harassment, online bullying, psychological manipulation    **Perfect For**: Fans of *The Social Network* seeking a gendered perspective, viewers interested in business biopics with psychological depth, and anyone who appreciates complex female protagonists    > *"They told her to play the game. She changed the rules—then built a whole new playing field."*

cast_names: Lily James, Ben Schnetzer, Myha'la, Jackson White, Dan Stevens, Ian Colletti, Mary Neely, Ana Yi Puig, Aidan Laprete, Pedro Correa
review_content: A complete disaster. Every movie tries to sell an idea, but when that idea is delivered in a clumsy and heavy-handed way, it stops being a movie and becomes pure propaganda. In this film, men are portrayed as MONSTERS undeserving of compassion, as if all the problems in the world stem from human beings who happen to have a penis. And apparently, vaginas are the future.    This is a film that glorifies female supremacy. It's not a story about people—it's a story where men are evil and women are good. The movie is deceptive because it starts off great, but once the heavy-handed activism kicks in, everything that was built collapses into dust.    Here's an example: the woman whose job is to delete unsolicited penis pictures from Tinder accounts is portrayed as a victim, while men are shown as depraved, perverted, and morally bankrupt. But what about the women who post pictures of breasts or genitals? Or those who advertise prostitution services on Tinder? That part doesn't fit the narrative and is conveniently ignored by the film.    This isn't a movie. It's FEMINIST PROPAGANDA. :face_vomiting:

cast_names: Lily James, Ben Schnetzer, Myha'la, Jackson White, Dan Stevens, Ian Colletti, Mary Neely, Ana Yi Puig, Aidan Laprete, Pedro Correa
review_content: I usually enjoy founder stories, but Swiped falls flat with dull, unengaging characters—a failure of writing more than subject matter. Mark Zuckerberg, for example, isn’t inherently riveting, yet The Social Network made his story gripping. By contrast, Swiped feels more like a bland documentary than a film. Its timing doesn’t help either: we’ve all seen the damage social media has caused—corrosive effects on society and its use by governments to entrench surveillance—yet the movie offers no fresh insight against this backdrop.    As for Whitney Wolfe Herd, she didn’t create Tinder but helped market it successfully and was rewarded accordingly. Since then, she has leaned into a narrative of victimhood, often demonising colleagues, which overshadows her actual contributions. She started her life as a tree hugger and is now married to an Oil & Gas magnate whom she met during one of her ski trips to Aspen. 😂

cast_names: Lily James, Ben Schnetzer, Myha'la, Jackson White, Dan Stevens, Ian Colletti, Mary Neely, Ana Yi Puig, Aidan Laprete, Pedro Correa
review_content: Docudramas are a guilty pleasure of mine — a comfy, digestible info dump I could just as easily read on Wikipedia. Swiped delivers on that front, but the story of Tinder and Bumble is simply too uneventful to make for gripping drama. The film struggles to create any real tension or impact, not because of bad film making, but because the true story itself is surprisingly bland. Whether that's due to watertight NDAs or if the creation of these two applications is simply as mundane as portrayed, Swiped ultimately ends up feeling more like a recap than a narrative, leaving little to linger on once the credits roll.

review_content: Much better than I was expecting. If you like sword and sorcery/fantasy movies, this is a good one.  Now, it has been forever since I watched the 1985 version, and I never got into the comics, so I have nothing to say about those.  This movie feels like a mix of Game of Thrones, and _Gladiator_ (2000). The runtime does seem a bit long, though I can't think of what could actually be cut. The good news is the pacing moves well.  The story is as straight forward as you can get with good versus evil, however, our villain is actually kind of likable; he's still bad, but without feeling like a cliche.   As for the action: there are plenty of good sword fights, but I was surprised this was rated R; aside from one bloody stabbing, this could be PG-13. A few funny moments, but nothing that derailed the flow.  Overall, a fun watch. I recommend this to fans of the genre.

review_content: What an awesome surprise! I wasn't sure what to expect going into this film, but I loved every second of it. The action was thrilling, it's been ages since I last saw a decent action film that didn't take itself too seriously, and the humour was also spot-on. Seeing Jackie Chan's classic bloopers during the credits was such a fun throwback to his old films. It's rare to find a film that's both exciting and genuinely enjoyable, but this one delivers. I can't wait for the sequel!

cast_names: Jackie Chan, Zhang Zifeng, Tony Leung Ka-fai, Ci Sha, Jun, Wang Ziyi, Zac Wang, Melvin Wong, Liu Junxiao, Ma Cancan
review_content: Finally, after a bunch of very mediocre recent movies with Jackie full of CGI nonsense, we finally have a great old-school film packed with fighting and action. Fair to say, I suspect that for a good portion of the fight scenes, Jackie actually had a stunt double — but still, at his age, he’s great. And it’s always wonderful to see that classic Jackie Chan–style movie again.  I have to say, I’d probably give it a 6 instead of a 7 if it didn’t have Jackie in it. It was too long and dragged in many parts, the story itself isn’t new at all, and the “villain who doesn’t die or struggle after countless injuries” trope feels so cliché.

cast_names: Jackie Chan, Zhang Zifeng, Tony Leung Ka-fai, Ci Sha, Jun, Wang Ziyi, Zac Wang, Melvin Wong, Liu Junxiao, Ma Cancan
review_content: [IFFR '26] After a few disappointing titles Jackie Chan, at 71, returns to adult action cinema with his undiminished ability to generate tension. A sort of remake and sequel to "Eye in the Sky" (Yau Nai-Hai, 2007), it brings back the same antagonist to deliver a succession of perfectly crafted action scenes. From a story and character perspective, it's considerably more superficial than it intends, and it also ends up being longer than necessary, but as an entertaining action film that reflects, but as an entertaining action film that reflects, from both opposing sides, on the passage of time and generational replacement, it has some things to say.

cast_names: Piper Curda, Bobby Moynihan, Jon Hamm, Kathy Najimy, Dave Franco, Eduardo Franco, Aparna Nancherla, Sam Richardson, Melissa Villaseñor, Isiah Whitlock Jr.
review_content: Refreshing Departure from Sugar-Coated Nature Tropes  Recently, I've had the pleasure of watching Hoppers, and I must say, it is easily one of the most refreshing animations I have seen in a long time. What truly sets this film apart—and why I think it’s a must-watch—is its refusal to stick to the tired old trope where every creature in the forest is "best friends." We’ve been fed this "nature is a brotherhood" lie in countless other animations, but Hoppers takes a much more honest and grounded approach. It subtly but effectively shows children that the natural world has a food chain and that animals, quite frankly, sometimes eat each other. It doesn’t do this in a traumatizing way, but rather as a natural part of its world-building. Beyond its honesty, the movie boasts a fantastic plot and a seamless flow that keeps you hooked from start to finish. The pacing is spot on, and the visual storytelling is top-notch. If you’re tired of sugar-coated stories and want an animation that balances a great story with a touch of reality, this is it. A brilliant, well-paced journey that respects the intelligence of its audience, young and old alike.

review_content: We Bury the Dead surprised me more than I expected — it isn’t your typical zombie flick, and that’s part of what makes it worth watching. At first it took me a second to even recognize an older Daisy Ridley, who delivers one of her best performances since Star Wars as Ava, a grieving wife driven to join a body retrieval unit after a catastrophic disaster decimates Hobart. The undead are mostly a backdrop in this story about loss and the desperate drive to reconcile with a past that won’t stay buried, and Mark Coles Smith matches Ridley well in the film’s quieter, more emotional moments.  ￼  Director Zak Hilditch leans into atmosphere over jump scares, painting a bleak, beautifully composed landscape that feels as hollow and unsettled as the characters’ internal worlds — terrific cinematography that makes the devastation feel almost palpable. While longtime horror fans might go in expecting nonstop undead action and instead find something more melancholic, We Bury the Dead has several genuinely touching scenes and the thematic depth to stick with you long after the credits.

The makeup looks really good and the sound of teeth was an absolute nightmare, worse than nails on a chalkboard. It's very slow in parts with little action or suspence and wants to be a drama but it doesn't really stick in that department. If you're going to go there, I need to learn more about the characters and make them more compelling. Nice to hear a Metric needle drop at the end.

movie_title: The Hand That Rocks the Cradle
cast_names: Maika Monroe, Mary Elizabeth Winstead, Raúl Castillo, Martin Starr, Riki Lindhome, Shannon Cochran, Arabella Olivia Clark, Avery Tiiu Essex, Zale Morris, Brian Carpenter
review_content: Some remakes make sense, even if only to reinterpret an idea or offer a new perspective. This is not one of them. There’s no fresh angle here, no real reason to revisit the story. What we get instead is a hollow copy, a diluted version of something that already worked perfectly and never needed to be touched.    The main problem lies in the script. It lacks internal logic, fails to build genuine tension, and makes decisions that feel completely arbitrary. Character motivations shift whenever the plot needs them to, and twists don’t grow organically from the story but from a forced need to seem intense. Everything moves forward awkwardly, without emotional or narrative coherence, making it hard to stay engaged.    Comparisons with the original are unavoidable, and this version loses on every front. The earlier film worked because it understood suspense, ambiguity, and gradual unease. Here, there’s no real mystery, no lasting threat, none of that creeping discomfort that slowly got under your skin. What once was tension is now just noise.    Not even the performances, which in another context might have helped, manage to save the film. There is talent on screen, but it’s trapped in material that gives the actors nothing solid to build on. Characters never feel fully real, and the final act closes the story in a clumsy, empty way.    Visually, it offers nothing memorable. Flat, functional, and forgettable, it feels more like content made to fill a catalog than a film with something to say. An unnecessary, disappointing remake that adds nothing and diminishes what came before.

movie_title: The Hand That Rocks the Cradle

A forgettable buildup, with an underwhelming ending. An overexposed script, with weak directing. I can't find many positives, apart from maybe Winstead. Otherwise, it's just mediocrity and shallowness. I haven't watched the original, but I think I should have. Surely it can't be as bad as this.

review_content: I know this is a remake / reboot, but I don't think I ever saw the original.      This felt like a typical 2020's era movie where they have a story and then the "DEI Committee" comes in and does their recasting and rewriting.  The character of the husband was so weak, and really seemed shoe-horned in.  I want to watch the original and come back and update this thought.  [UPDATE]  After watching the original, 100% - the husband's role was so much more involved in the original.    The story seems to lag along.  Although I didn't feel it was boring, it was definitely like "OK, let's get to the plot already."    I did like the dramatic conclusion.  The accident scene [spoiler] was pretty gruesome, but conveyed a lot without any dialogue.  Although they were enemies in a sense, there was some empathy shown by how Caitlin was holding Polly's head.  And, at first I was thinking why would you let your daughter come over and see this horrific sight, but then I thought - this was the moment Emma stopped being a child.[/spoiler]    [UPDATE] The original was far superior.  Watch that instead.  This version definitely looked better - with the architecture and the focus on interior design (as pointed out by RG9400).  But everything else is better in the original.

review_content: Yet another instance where I wish Trakt had the half-star rating. This falls squarely between "Meh" and "Fair", as far as I'm concerned. Some say it was a bad movie but I found it interesting enough, and unique in its own way. Dakota Fanning's portrayal was absolutely brilliant, and the entire movie had a very cool "creepy" vibe to it that made it fun to watch. There were, of course, the jump scares but they were neither prevelant nor pervasive, and they didn't detract from the overall sense of foreboding through the movie. I suppose my biggest complaint is simply that they made the conclusion far too obvious far too soon. I mean, they could have cut quickly to the closing scene and shut it off with no warning but as it was, you knew what was coming, so it actually spoiled the entire finish, as far as I'm concerned. For a psychological thriller, I thought it fit the bill beautifully but it stopped short of being a real "horror" film (although there were a couple of horrific scenes in it). Not a bad way to spend an hour and a half, but don't expect anything mind-blowing or any "wow" moments here. Solid performance of a pretty shallow story.

review_content: 'Night Always Comes' delivered a good flick. I do tend to like these gritty crime dramas, so I'm pleased to say that I can list this one in that bracket. Vanessa Kirby comes in with a strong performance, I'm happy to belatedly see her act as the front and centre of a film.    There is plenty of action across the 108mins, atmospherically the movie is spot on. It can get quite frustrating but seemingly by design, Doreen is particuarly infuriating - gaslighter of the century, that one! Bar Kirby, this also minimally has Randall Park, Julia Fox, Michael Kelly and Eli Roth.    I will say some of the dialogue didn't quite do it for me, mainly for Kirby in fact. It just felt unnatural/ill-fitting to me here and there, but in fairness Kirby's acting prowess and the dramatic events are sturdy enough that I quickly forgot any issues I had with that. Worth noting, still.

review_content: I will watch anything Nick Offerman is in. And I can honestly say this is one of his best performances in a movie (not including the TV Show Devs). Movie aside, Nick Offerman is a very well-informed intelligent man who knows his shit about the same real world problems that are explored in this film which makes this film even more impactful. "Maybe aim more for the head. They wear bulletproof vests." I highly suggest everyone reads Offerman's latest reddit AMA.    After [spoiler]Jerry gets arrested and Joe goes to the "detention center" (I think that's what it is) we watch his attitude change [/spoiler] This movie takes a dark turn after these events. Jerry's speeches became more unstable as he talks about [spoiler] killing a man's sheep, babies and wives. To "conquer" not "wage war"[/spoiler] This film is more educational than entertaining, even though it is entertaining (in a dialogue heavy way).  This is an excellent film that explores the sovereign citizen movement which has not been put on the TV screen very much. Based on the real events of the 2010 Arkansas shootings. I highly recommend this film as an educational look into Sovereign Citizens. It's not a easy film to watch as we watch the son come to grips with his delusional father and just how far he will take his "conquering".

cast_names: Nick Offerman, Jacob Tremblay, Dennis Quaid, Bobby Gilchrist, Megan Mullally, Ruby Wolf, Buddy Campbell, Tommy Kramer, Jared Carter, Mike L. Thomas
review_content: Sovereign plays like a slow burning civil war between two men who could have been brothers if fate had not put them on opposite sides of the badge. At its core, it is not about good versus evil but belief versus belief, and what happens when neither side will bend.    Nick Offerman’s Jerry is a man fuelled by paranoia disguised as principle. His world is built on YouTube rhetoric: money is not real, the court has no power, sovereignty overrides state. It is the kind of fringe talk that has moved frighteningly close to the mainstream. His rejection of authority is not just personal, it is a performance, one that escalates every encounter until tragedy is inevitable. Offerman gives Jerry quiet charisma and unshakeable conviction, even as he drags his confused teenage son down with him.    On the flip side is Dennis Quaid’s John, a true believer in the rule of law, not the idealised version but the hard and uncompromising one. He is as much a zealot as Jerry, devoted to order at any cost. The final moments of his arc are genuinely moving, showing the toll of living life as a hammer that sees only nails.    The brilliance of Sovereign lies in how it refuses to caricature either man. Both are misguided. Both are trying to protect what they love. And both, ultimately, are victims of a system that breeds extremism. It is like watching Ron Swanson snap, shave the moustache, and go full militant.    This is not just a story about American paranoia. It is a sharp critique of how fear turns men into martyrs for causes they barely understand. The musket era logic of constitutional rights does not translate to a world of AR-15s and trigger happy checkpoints. When you treat every rule as oppression, you start manifesting your own worst fears.    Sovereign is bleak, thoughtful, and disturbingly plausible. A powerful film about a uniquely American disease that the rest of the developed world watches like a car crash, too surreal to look away.    If you liked… Leave No Trace, Shotgun Stories, Take Shelter.

cast_names: Ranveer Singh, Akshaye Khanna, R. Madhavan, Arjun Rampal, Sanjay Dutt, Sara Arjun, Rakesh Bedi, Gaurav Gera, Naveen Kaushik, Saumya Tandon
review_content: I have been fairly disillusioned by Indian movies for some time now, but RRR broke that for me, highlighting the power of mixing cinematic craft with masala to create magic. With the amount of buzz and hype around Dhurandhar, I thought they might have captured that lightning in a bottle again. Unfortunately, I think they did not in numerous ways. I'm going to start by putting aside the story and political aspects to just focus in on the technical areas.    This movie desperately wants to be stylish. There are 10 chapters, each with poetic subtitles. At times, the dialogue becomes flashy captions on the screen. There are numerous over the top gory deaths. In a lot of ways, more than RRR, it reminded me of John Wick. I think this movie was trying to emulate that, not just in terms of the macho hero or the testosterone-induced action sequences but also just the stylistic choices. The clear differentiator is the level of skill. John Wick is effortless, fluid and slick, cool in its unassumed set pieces. Dhurandar tries to go over the top everywhere. Edits are chaotic and way too fast, characters randomly enter slo-mo and at other times get sped up, colors look washed out, and the deaths are so gory that it is almost absurd and funny at times. It tries so hard that it crosses that border from cool to cheesy so many times. Like there is a sequence where in middle of a shootout, the movie does slo-mo to showcase Ranveer Singh flipping his hair back, something so silly that I actually couldn't stop laughing. In RRR, even if there were ridiculous moments, the movie always tried to look good. I can remember specific scenes where the camera would tilt or flip, where the colors would pop, or where the music was perfectly timed to the movements on the screen. Dhurandar has none of that. Outside of the shock factor in some of the violent scenes, I cannot recall any specific imagery or sequence. At one point, I swear I thought I was watching a car commercial with how saturated the green screen background became. The soundtrack is actually pretty good and one of the few technical highlights, but it comes in and out so chaotically in the movie. These are good songs to listen to outside of the movie, but they don't mesh with the actual scenes to make a full audiovisual impact, outside of one meme-worthy moment. I have to talk about the acting. It's also super cheesy. Akshaye Khanna's facial expression is more or less just constant twitching. At least he is able to emote. Ranveer Singh is a cardboard cutout, with his stilted dialogue often feeling like a table read. I think the standard in Bollywood is pretty low, where the movies are so driven by star power that they often feel centered around actors instead of characters. I think here the cast are not only playing their usual roles, so maybe people want to heap praise. However, this is not great acting.    In terms of the story, I honestly went into this really interested. Even the negative reviews surrounding this movie hyped up a geopolitical thriller that detailed a decade plus of Indian history, albeit from a very biased lens. This movie barely has any of that. The majority of this movie is similar to any other gangster movie that you might have seen. The interconnection to historical events and the real-life footage is minimal, often serving to drum up propaganda than to actually further the narrative or the character arcs. In fact, the story and its masquerading as a "Based on Real Events" story are so divorced from each other that by the end of the movie, without even Googling anything, I knew that 99% of this movie was made up. It's just so clearly not realistic. The idea that a spy would somehow become so entangled in gang politics or would be doing so many things that would gather way too much unwanted attention is ridiculous. There is maybe a 30-second window in which the character is actually worried he will be discovered. Beyond that, he just goes about doing crazy things that do not seem feasible at all. If you're looking for that type of high-tension cat and mouse cloak and dagger storyline, I would highly recommend The Departed or Infernal Affairs instead. Granted, the movie's gang storylines are actually kind of interesting at times. It's just dragged down by all this baggage surrounding the political propaganda and also the romance. I can't disparage the romance in this movie enough. Not only is it extremely icky, it also is completely unbelievable. The two leads have no chemistry (which makes sense since Ranveer Singh is twice the age of Sara Arjun), and how and why both characters choose to pursue this romance despite all the red flags not to is also mind-boggling. Finally, this movie is half a movie. Despite being an absurd 3.5 hours long, it still manages to end in an abrupt manner, with a final act that is just action sequences and no catharsis, failing to tie up any of the threads in satisfying ways. I think the middle of this movie was actually decent and engaging, so it's such a shame that they are trying to milk it with a sequel that was already ready to go by the time this one was released.     At the end of the day, Dhurandar is going to make a lot of money for a Bollywood movie. However, that's the problem. Bollywood is stuck inside a time capsule, an echo chamber where they keep repeating their formulas and templates without looking outside or learning from what others are doing with film. The argument is that is what their audiences want. Maybe that is true, but Bollywood movies, despite sometimes having record box offices, never actually make a dent in the global box office. RRR showcased the possibilities when a director looks beyond just what has been done in the past, when they actually focus and hone their technical craft. Dhurandar is not that, even if it might have had that lofty ambition initially. Instead, it is just a messy, overlong, gratuitously violent, cheesy masala movie propped up by political propaganda.

cast_names: Eva Victor, Naomi Ackie, Louis Cancelmi, Kelly McCormack, Lucas Hedges, John Carroll Lynch, Hettienne Park, E.R. Fightmaster, Jordan Mendoza, Anabel Graetz
review_content: Sorry, Baby is a movie I went into expecting something very different. I had heard it framed as funny, or at least humor-adjacent, and that expectation quickly dissolved. Whether it’s simply not my sense of humor or I’m not the intended audience for that framing, this isn’t a comedy in any traditional sense. What it is instead is heavy, intimate, and emotionally exhausting in a very deliberate way. The subject matter hits hard, and the film never really lets you off the hook once it settles into its tone.  Eva Victor’s performance is the anchor here, and it’s genuinely impressive. The direction and acting work together to create something that feels raw and deeply personal, even when it’s uncomfortable to sit with. This is not a movie I’d broadly recommend to everyone—it’s tough, it’s quiet, and it demands patience—but it’s undeniably powerful. It feels like the kind of film that will resonate strongly with the right audience and earn serious awards attention because of how confidently it commits to its voice.  Rating: 4/5 – 85% – Would Recommend

cast_names: Eva Victor, Naomi Ackie, Louis Cancelmi, Kelly McCormack, Lucas Hedges, John Carroll Lynch, Hettienne Park, E.R. Fightmaster, Jordan Mendoza, Anabel Graetz
review_content: Sorry, Baby is a tremendously sad film, yet it is not built around misery as a spectacle. It can be dryly funny, then quietly brutal, then oddly warm, often in the same stretch of time, without ever cheapening what it is about. It is sharp, specific, and unexpectedly moving, with a light touch that takes real confidence to pull off. Bravo, Eva Victor.

cast_names: Eva Victor, Naomi Ackie, Louis Cancelmi, Kelly McCormack, Lucas Hedges, John Carroll Lynch, Hettienne Park, E.R. Fightmaster, Jordan Mendoza, Anabel Graetz
review_content: I've seen quite a few movies this year, but I think this one has stuck in my mind the longest after watching it. Initially, I felt like this movie wasn't really for me, because the central experience of the movie (the "bad thing") is not something I can relate to at all. But the writing and performances by Eva Victor spoke to me. It managed to capture feelings I recognise in compelling ways, and paint a constellation of experiences so much more meaningful than any individual moment. And in that way it gives an insight into an experience outside of my own, which maybe make it a movie exactly for me.  Most of the humour comes from the contrast between Agnes' situation and her reactions (or the reactions of those around her), which I think speaks to the incomprehensible horribleness of the "bad thing". I think anyone that describes this movie as being about a "weak snowflake that doesn't know how to deal" is exposing themselves as all the worst stereotypes of a boomer, and utterly missing the point.

cast_names: Eva Victor, Naomi Ackie, Louis Cancelmi, Kelly McCormack, Lucas Hedges, John Carroll Lynch, Hettienne Park, E.R. Fightmaster, Jordan Mendoza, Anabel Graetz
review_content: "No... I know it's bad. But mainly because whenever I tell anyone about it, they look really scared for me... And I can feel, in my body, that it was really bad. But then sometimes I don't think about it... And I feel guilty when I don't think about it."    Body and memory fragmented, frozen. How can I Know anything if I don't know my body?    Pulling another body close. Any body, just to feel safe.    "No, no, it's just I, um, I haven't looked at my thesis since I published it. But I will. I can."    The deepest parts of me, the things I truly care about, contaminated, ruined. Stuck in time.    "Oh, don't worry. I never tell secrets, unless I feel like it's a secret I really want to tell."    Smiling, quipping, because I can't bear for you to see how I really feel. Anything but that.    Real empathy, understanding seems almost impossible to find. But how could anyone ever know, if I never show it?    "When you grow up, you can tell me whatever.... I'll never tell you it's scaring me. I'll just say, yeah, I know. It's just like that sometimes."

review_content: The story of an immature adult who has problems with herself, her sexuality and her relationships. She is sexually assaulted but recognises that she is in part to blame and is unable to cope with that situation. She just seems too weak to cope with the realities of life, namely the fact that life is sometimes hard and that one has to let go and get over the sh*t that happens. She needs to move on, but can’t and she does not seem to accept the help and advice she gets fro  her friends. Decent movie but also a movie not to be misunderstood as it can easily lead to depression for people who are weak too. The movie does not really show a way out, just the lingering in self pity.

review_content: Splitsville was simply wonderful. All four leads absolutely crush it, each bringing sharp timing and genuine chemistry that elevates the material far beyond what the premise alone suggests. The setup is immediately attention-grabbing, but it quickly becomes clear that it’s just the launchpad—not the point. What really makes this movie sing is the humor. It’s snappy, witty, and immaculately timed, almost like watching an expertly constructed stand-up set unfold in narrative form.  There are a few familiar, slightly cliché plot beats that keep it from reaching absolute top-tier status, but they’re minor nitpicks in an otherwise fantastic experience. I loved this one from start to finish.  Rating: 4.5/5 – 90% – Highly Recommend

review_content: The fact that everyone seemed to hate the film almost confirms its effectiveness. The marketing framed it as a story about MeToo going too far, but it's really just a mere pretext to dig into two, perhaps three, rotten generations of the American elite. There is close to zero demand for empathy here. The overall worldview and dialogue can feel a bit too far up their own asses, but I found that coherent, to the point that I wished it were even less accommodating.    On a side note, much credit goes to the direction: sharp and functional to the script, always adding nuances without ever going overboard.

review_content: Third installment of a saga that already gave everything it had to give in the first movie. This one exists because people like you and me are here watching them, and they work commercially, not because the story called for it.    Pretty cinematography, the soundtrack does its job of underlining the emotions, and there are enough scenes with romantic tension to keep the fans entertained. But as a trilogy conclusion it doesn't add anything new nor offer an ending that justifies the three movies... If you liked the previous ones, this will give you more of the same.

review_content: I was expecting a lot more from these little blue creatures…  But let me tell you what went down 👇    The story is about Papa Smurf going missing after being kidnapped by Gargamel and—plot twist—his brother Razzamel. (Yep, he has a brother now 👀)  Smurfette gathers the gang and sets off on an adventure into the real world to save him. 💥    The movie is animated—and I was genuinely excited for it.  But once I saw it? Major disappointment. 😐    I’ve been a fan since their 2011 and 2013 movies—nostalgic attachment since I was a kid.  The surprise? I thought Rihanna was the lead... but nope—it was James.  And I’ve got a personal rule:  If James shows up in a movie… he ruins it. 😂    The animation?  Not even worth half a dirham.  We’re in an era where visuals should make you say “wow”—not feel like you’re playing PlayStation 2. 😩    The plot? Slow.  The scenes? Lazy.  The movie overall? Pointless, unfortunately.    I tried to like it—not for me, but for my little brother. :child:  He enjoyed the story.  Me? Not at all.    🎬 My rating: 4.5/10  If you’ve got young kids, they might like it.  Otherwise? Save your time

cast_names: Rihanna, James Corden, Nick Offerman, JP Karliak, Dan Levy, Amy Sedaris, Natasha Lyonne, Sandra Oh, Jimmy Kimmel, Octavia Spencer
review_content: BOYCOTT PARAMOUNT: But… Damn You, Gargamel, You Magnificent Metaphor  So here we are. I broke the boycott. Or rather, the boycott broke me. I watched The Smurfs (2025), a release by the increasingly disgraced Paramount Animation—the very same corporate puppet theater that canceled The Late Show with Stephen Colbert for daring to have a spine. And I sat there, popcorn in hand, fully prepared to sneer.  And then Gargamel showed up.  Let me be perfectly clear: Gargamel is Steven Miller. Not “inspired by,” not “reminiscent of.” He is Steven Miller—the bony-cheeked architect of America’s most shameful immigration policies—reimagined here as a bumbling, big-nosed sorcerer whose evil vortex sweeps Smurfs from their homes and imprisons them without warning. Sound familiar? ICE raids, concentration camps, ghosted neighbors… Gargamel’s magical vortex might as well be titled “Operation Homeland Harmony.”  The resemblance is so uncanny, so grotesquely accurate, I nearly choked on a blue M&M. I even captured scenes, frame by frame, with my phone, documenting Miller’s every twitch reborn in animation. This was not subtle. This was seditious.  And so, a funny thing happened. What began as an act of resistance turned into an act of recognition—not for Paramount the Corporation (may its boardroom be forever haunted by bad Q2 reports), but for the hundreds of artists and technicians still working under its banner, who, in this quiet, beautiful, Smurfy rebellion, managed to get a few shots in.  Take the musical score. Henry Jackman, a composer I had shamefully overlooked until now, delivers a masterclass in modern scoring. Not content to coast on the limp hip-hop rework of the original Smurfs theme (a decision so aggressively off-brand it may have been sabotage), Jackman instead plants heartful orchestrations throughout, including a spine-tingling quotation of The Last Starfighter theme—yes, that one—from a time when heroes were earnest, and composers were gods.  It’s clear Jackman remembers. And his score gently, beautifully insists that we remember too.  The plot itself, centered on a lost Smurf who doesn’t yet know his name or place in the world, could easily have drifted into syrup. But it’s in the Smurfs’ climactic act of singing the original theme together, restoring harmony and memory, that the film finds its soul. Kindness becomes the spell that undoes tyranny. Music becomes the revolution.  Yes, it’s a kids’ film. But you know what? So was WALL·E. So was Zootopia. And now, The Smurfs (2025) joins the ranks of subversive bedtime stories.  So here’s the review in a nutshell: BOYCOTT PARAMOUNT—and while you’re at it, go see The Smurfs. Not because Paramount deserves your money. They don’t. But because the resistance is alive inside the machine. And sometimes the clearest cry against fascism comes not from the barricades, but from a village of blue idealists singing in harmony.  ⸻  Crafted with Hudson—an AI collaborator for creativity, innovation, and human potential. Explore what’s possible at OpenAI.

review_content: Technically, the movie implements everything provided by the source material - the Exit 8 game. At some points it even goes beyond, and I was thrilled in the moment. At the end of those 95 minutes though, I had to admit to myself that the game doesn't really provide a whole lot of content, and the movie suffers from this just as much. Nothing consequential happens, there is barely any story or development, and I spent most of my time trying to find anomalies before the main character does because nothing in the script was worth focussing on.

cast_names: Kazunari Ninomiya, Yamato Kochi, Nana Komatsu, Naru Asanuma, Kotone Hanase, Hirota Otsuka, HIKAKIN
review_content: If you see an anomaly, turn back immediately The rules are simple the same as the rules of life. But do we listen? We keep going and going seeing all the anomalies and we never turn back.   Exit 8 is not a horror in a conventional sense but it is nevertheless shocking. It doesn't tell a supernatural story it tells the story of our lives.   The most shocking part is the introduction to the walking man. All along we see the walking man, wondering who he is or why he is there, and then suddenly one time during the repetitions the camera casually follows him and we realize that he is also a lost character or was!   From this moment we realize the shocking truth. The fact that he was also once lost in exit 8 but lost to the game and even saw the anomaly (the open stairs to exit 8) he decided to go on. He didn't listen to the kid (who I will talk about later) he went on and then became an NPC—walking and walking in circles FOREVER. The way the story is told is also very intelligent. In the walking man narrative, we realize that there is also a walking girl, roaming around the exit 8. She was also once lost in the game and became also an NPC.   So, from the moment we start the movie and get trapped with the young man inside the station and see the walking man until we go to chapter 2 “The Walking Man” we go forward in time then by the beginning of chapter 2 we go for a flashback that introduces how the walking man became an NPC, and another important factor that is The Kid.   The kid is in the third chapter and we first see him on the path of the young guy, who thinks the kid is just an anomaly and loses progress. Then in the walking man progress, we realize that the kid was initially with the walking man and that the kid is super smart and tries to save the Walking Man and at the end even tries to save him from eternal perdition, but the man is so lost in the loop and so desperate that he rushes to his doom.   Then we continue where we left off in the story of the young man. He realizes how smart the kid actually is and with his help, they go through several rounds and get closer to Exit 8.  Here I will talk about one of the most brilliant scenes. In one of the loops, the anomaly becomes one of the doors being open. The man opens the adjured door looks into the darkness and sees himself on the train looking out the door (the movie begins with the exact shot but from the POV perspective). And we see him (the man inside the loop) with his eyes full of tears. It is as if even in the beginning when we saw the POV shot he was always looking not into his reflection in the darkness of the window but always to the trapped version. As if all along the trapped version was signalling him.  Then with the boy, they go on solving the loops until the per ultimatum loop becomes too dramatic. We suddenly see the flood coming from outside and fills the station. The man decides to save the child. He puts him on the station sign and he himself gets drowned by the massive flood.  Then there is this cutscene where we see him waking up on the beach. Sun shining, a shot from afar beautiful cinematography. Then we see his girlfriend standing by him and a boy playing on the sands. We cannot see the boy but we can guess that this boy and the abandoned boy from Exit 8 are the same person.   If you remember the movie begins with his GF calling him saying she’s at the doctors deciding whether to keep the child or not. And he is hesitant.  then in the limbo, the boy comes to him later, and it becomes clear in a monologue that the man was also abandoned as a child and never knew his father. Therefore, if we break this down we can understand that this loop has a literal sense for him.  He was abandoned, he won't be involved in his GF's decision to keep the baby and the baby will grow without a father, and so on…. So, this boy is both his own son and himself from the past and from the future. Yeah I know, the review just got a bit technical. Pay attention read again and you’ll get it.    And this boy is in fact his saviour. By saving him he is saving himself. And correctly we see that after he disappears in the flood first the boy and then he himself find their way out of Exit 8 and he finally exits the loop. But wait… The movie and its true horrors are not finished.   He starts coming down the stairs, and we see all these people going to work all in the same formal clothes and it appears that they are going up, yes, exactly… to their own Exit 8 loop. The movie is sending a message that all of us, we are the NPCs in this world, going around and around the same loop.   Then the boy calls his GF, saying that he will be there at the doctor and he gets on the train and guess what? It is the same scene with which the movie started.  The movie started with a man yelling at a woman whose baby was crying and no one did anything including the main character. He just put the earphones on and looked at his reflection in the dark window - which by now we know was the anomaly all along…in that scene the man puts the earphones on, plays a music (Bolero by Ravel, not going to explain why that specific choice is significant, search and learn for yourself- and he doesn't do anything to defend the woman… So, it seems that everything is destined to happen over and over again and he had never actually escaped the loop when exactly at the last moment we see him turning around and moving for a brief moment it seems towards the woman to help her. Then the credits roll and we ask ourselves the question over and over in a loop:  Did he actually escape the loop or was I hallucinating?

cast_names: Kazunari Ninomiya, Yamato Kochi, Nana Komatsu, Naru Asanuma, Kotone Hanase, Hirota Otsuka, HIKAKIN
review_content: Trapped in the same loop of subway passage. Sometimes a small detail changes and that means you have to go back. Otherwise complete the loop.  If it can work in a video game, the concept seemed hard to turn into a movie without getting really boring. Yet it kinda works. It's obviously still a bit repetitive.     The trick used is to switch the focus character in the middle, and it does the job. Switching perspective, it becomes a new experience, even if still the same passages.    However more than the repetition, the most frustrating part is the stupidity. Of course they can't just be "oh yeah, a change, going back" instantly, or the movie would be 20 minutes (not such a bad idea though). But come on ! Not only obvious anomalies but even imminent danger, and the characters are still hanging there like "hmmmn, am I sure ? Maybe I should wait a bit more before turning back." At some point there is a literal fucking alarm blaring, and the guy still waits there. This is very irritating.    The man's asthma breathing is also quite annoying, great it doesn't last the whole time.    It was not clear whether the boy was supposed to be [spoiler]him, his future son, or maybe both of them at the same time[/spoiler] since their experiences were very similar. This added a bit of depth on top of the simple concept, but it felt a bit undeveloped.

cast_names: Kazunari Ninomiya, Yamato Kochi, Nana Komatsu, Naru Asanuma, Kotone Hanase, Hirota Otsuka, HIKAKIN
review_content: **_This film is based on the adventure game The Exit 8, where you play as a character trapped inside a looping subway corridor.  Your only task is to walk forward and notice any strange or unsettling anomalies, if you overlook one or if one of them confronts you the loop resets and you are then back to square one or maybe 0 in that context._**    I found the film to be really interesting, the whole project aims to adapt a video game into a movie, but the very foundation of the film still relies on the gameplay mechanic of the game, therefore the real challenge is to create a coherent adaptation that also manages to tell a more concrete story. One might mistakenly think that because the game and its core mechanic are fairly simple, adapting it shouldn’t be that difficult but that’s a bad assumption. This is a feature length film and even if the core mechanic is simple, it becomes necessary to expand it into a story which is capable of keeping us engaged all the way through.    It’s worth noting that the movie is divided into three chapters, each focusing on a different character. This is a good idea because it allows the film to explore more while still keeping everything digestible. It creates natural pauses without breaking the overall intensity, and it simply works the movie has a nice rhythm and I didn’t feel the half hour pass. Watching it was genuinely pleasant.      The film is far from perfect, and it’s hard not to notice how it sometimes adds a dramatic layer that feels artificial and sort of random from a writing standpoint. At times it feels a bit too much, more like filler than something original and interesting, these writing choices for the main character are really regrettable, the character could have been written much better. It’s all the more unfortunate because the film’s opening setup is actually well shot and quite intriguing.    It is also unfortunate that the movie stays almost too faithful to the game in its horror aspect. There is plenty of room to do something truly wild with the core mechanic. Just because the protagonists return to square one when they make mistakes doesn’t mean they can’t suffer in more or less intense ways beforehand. Here, everything is rather restrained and basic and not very daring. The film leans more toward a mystery thriller than a horror movie even though it clearly borrows horror elements it ends up being a fairly faithful adaptation in the sense that it remains quite “soft” overall.        I found the adaptation quite brilliant as a whole, and I was repeatedly surprised by how much I enjoyed the camera work, the sound design, and the overall acting which is very nice.      There is the idea in the film that maybe we shouldn’t just go and enter a set path and that by doing so risking to sink deeper into it as if we were under some sort of hypnosis and being manipulated entirely cut off from everything around us following a basic pattern of life, a kind of endless and dizzying cycle, somewhat like Escher’s works, a cycle that suggests that life is reduced to our work and the subway hallways which although straight in most cases often take the form of labyrinths but instead the film seems to say that we should find the exit of all of this, the only way out, to return to something more essential and get closer to what really matters to us in the present moment such as our family, our loved ones, but also our dreams or at least what we really want to do, what is really important what has a real meaning.    Despite the fact that I would have liked the film to take more risks and aim a little higher **Exit 8** succeeds at being a good adaptation that is meaningful and good.    **7/10**

review_content: Minka Kelly stars in the Netflix holiday rom-com _Champagne Problems_. The story follows a businesswoman who’s sent to Paris to pitch a buyout offer to a prominent champagne company, but the owner first makes her and the other interested buyers prove themselves by taking them to his winery. Kelly gives a strong performance and has good chemistry with co-star Tom Wozniczka. And writer/director Mark Steven Johnson does a nice job at using the Paris and winery settings (given the limited budget). However, some of the characters are little more than broad stereotypes; used only for comedic effect. Yet while it has a few problems, _Champagne Problems_ is a charming holiday film.

cast_names: Rosamund Pike, Matthew Rhys, Megan McDonnell, Tadhg Murphy, Stephen Jones, Paul Tylak
review_content: This movie had interesting things to say about parenthood and the role and responsibilities taken on by parents. Rosamund Pike and Matthew Rhys, along with Megan McDonnell whose voice talent was on display, bring some weighty performances. But the bizarre cinematography and avant garde imagery, while admittedly unsettling, really ended up distracting from the truly great performances of this film. Cutting some of the weirdly long "vibe-setting" scenes could have turned this into an intense short film with an impactful message.

cast_names: Rosamund Pike, Matthew Rhys, Megan McDonnell, Tadhg Murphy, Stephen Jones, Paul Tylak
review_content: Tiny little film about two parents in a car on the phone. The actors do a great job making you feel their moral panic, and it opens up multiple perspective on the right thing to do, with layers of relationship history showing up in a realistic way to give depth to the conversations. It takes a hard turn to the supernatural in the third act, which was welcome, but maybe could have been executed better (the old lady sounded more silly than chilling, it wasn't quite clear what the stakes were, the last scene felt like a generic placeholder for an ending).     Moves quick and is easy to watch. Perfect for a low-commitment stream on a Sunday afternoon. Definitely do not pay money to see it on a big screen.

cast_names: Rosamund Pike, Matthew Rhys, Megan McDonnell, Tadhg Murphy, Stephen Jones, Paul Tylak
review_content: > What do you want?  >   > A better story.    The greatest accomplishment _Hallow Road (2025)_ achieves is trusting in the viewer's imagination. And to make that truly effective, there's one thing you absolutely must nail: the setup of a spectacular atmosphere.    Almost the entire film takes place inside a car. That could easily be a limitation elsewhere, but here it not only makes the film stand out and feel refreshingly different, it also heightens the confusion, uneasiness, and apprehension Babak Anvari clearly wants to evoke. Limiting us to only what the main couple can see amplifies everything tremendously.  The music and sound design -- especially those two sounds, you _know_ exactly which ones -- are deeply unsettling.    Every few minutes the film drops a new piece of information or an unexpected situation that injects fresh energy into the narrative and kept me constantly at the edge of my seat.  Rosamund Pike carries the film effortlessly and is brilliant throughout. One detail I only learned during the credits, and had no idea about while watching: she also plays the Strange/Kind Woman. You never see her face, yet she's absolutely _terrifying_.    I only really have two "complaints": Alice is pretty annoying, and the lack of urgency in the driving is maddening -- like, come on man, _are you seriously stopping at a red light right now?_  Other than that, I love how the movie starts off relatively "normal" and just keeps getting stranger and creepier as it goes. I get goosebumps just thinking about the moment things go off the rails. The little hints and details the film gives you so you can come up with your own interpretation to the story are pretty cool as well!

cast_names: Rosamund Pike, Matthew Rhys, Megan McDonnell, Tadhg Murphy, Stephen Jones, Paul Tylak
review_content: One of the better 'horror' films I've seen, probably all year. I love psychological thrillers, and this is exactly that. Almost the entirety of the rare 1 hr 20 m runtime is a car ride that the score, directing, and camera work make feel more like a rollercoaster ride. The story moves along implicitly, yet the intent of its metaphors grows explicitly. Parenting, responsibility, guilt, grief, and so on, told through surreal symbolism, harsh dialogue, and ineffable folklore. The voice acting and performances were intense. The format felt original, and the emotions were dialled up to 10. Overall, I'd probably give it a 7.5/10.

review_content: Yeah, this is an old trope, and Mrs. Doubtfire not even reimagined but simply Christmasified, but I love a holiday goof. Maybe it's the Christmas spirit. cast_names: Christelle Elwin, Ben Radcliffe, Fabian McCallum, Aggy K. Adams, Connor Crawford, Juliette Alexandra, Dolph Lundgren, Ben Robson, Sharlto Copley, Freya Allan
review_content: Behold, a prequel feature-length special (as its initial conception was a limited series) based on The Rats, a gang of misfits who are greatly despised by many people on social media. Among the intelligent-minded, there is utter confidence that the immediate reception upon its release (and since its release) was and is open-minded and civil and not at all a chaotic demonstration of mindless hatred, primarily revolved around the insane, deluded belief of "wokeness," an absolute misconception of what "woke" truly means. In an ideal world, perhaps, such open-mindedness and civility would prevail.    To nobody's surprise, the vitriol hurled at this film come from the same people who badmouthed the fourth season of _The Witcher_ and Liam Hemsworth (and continue to do so) because their deity Henry Cavill left the show, while claiming to care (and claiming that he cared) about the source material, ignoring that it was the most faithful season yet to the novels, as well as a return to form to an extent, having felt similar to the first season: the peak of the show so far, more or less, even if that doesn't say much from the perspective of some viewers who think the show wasn't that good from the start.    Naturally, I didn't feel like that at all toward the latest season or Hemsworth. However, it did take a little while for this to draw me in. By the halfway point, my interest was stirred, and I was fully engaged. That remained the case for the remainder of the film. The first half didn't inspire much confidence that the rest would be sufficiently enjoyable, so I was pleased when the second half came along as if a savior of Good Samaritan proportions. It was by far the better half.    Christelle Elwin (Mistle) delivered an excellent performance alongside Dolph Lundgren. Individually and collectively, they were among the best aspects of this film, not only in the acting sense. The chemistry between Brehen and the Rats, particularly Mistle, was surprisingly great. Their dynamic, bonding, and scenes together may have been the primary highlights for me. They were definitely reminiscent of Geralt and Ciri, with slightly more aggression from both of them.    Uncertainty occupied my mind regarding his performance as Brehen in his first scene at the beginning. He didn't come across as convincing as I'd have liked. I worried that his overall presence would be a negative component and undermine my enjoyment of the entire film. Thankfully, and somewhat to my surprise, he immediately "redeemed" himself.    While I didn't find them insufferable in the main show, let alone to the extent hate-watchers have, I will say that The Rats were more likable in this, recontextualizing and adding further emotion and sadness to their eventual demise at the hands of Leo Bonhart. You end up (or at least I did) wishing that never happened and that we got more of them, even on top of this film, which feels too little and like it wasn't enough.    They almost felt like different characters. Even the overall look of each character looked different, from their costume designs to their hairstyling. Higher quality and better. All of them looked excellent, "on-fleek," and "fetch." Yes, it's time to bring both terms back and make "fetch" happen. It creates a curious question, however: why did they look better here than in the show? More money to devote entirely to this and nothing else, as it's a stand-alone special? That seems likely.    As far as the subtle characterization differences are concerned, I suppose you could argue that the reason they didn't seem quite the same in Season 4 of _The Witcher_, compared to here, is because of what happened and what they went through at the end of this film.    I particularly liked Giz (the stereotypical cool, nonchalant character; it worked on me) and Asse. I liked Mistle well enough; Iskra, too. I was indifferent toward Kayleigh and Reef, particularly the latter. But I'll admit she looked the coolest out of all of them (Giz, a close second), with all the various accessories and jewelry, especially her hairstyle and hair color. The collective chemistry between everyone and the scenes with all of them overwhelmingly disregarded that minor criticism.    Visually, this looked great with multiple notable shots, scenery, and environments. The cinematographer (Trevor Michael Brown) did a good job. While there wasn't much action, the fight sequences we did get at the end (Brehen vs. the predictably identifiable Jalowick and Brehen vs. Leo Bonhart) had decent choreography. And, of course, Leo Bonhart's short role was another highlight.

review_content: There was a group of teenage bandits called Rats in the Witcher books. Thats the only thing this movie has in common with the books. Movie Rats are written as a stylized teen gang for light, fast-paced action, while the book Rats are traumatized, vicious war orphans whose violence, moral decay, and self-destruction are paramount. Film versions largely strip away their brutality, nihilism, so they stop reflecting the original thematic weight and corruption present in the novels. It's absolute disgrace of the original story.

review_content: I assume quite a few people will have noticed that a new season of “The Witcher” has been released on Netflix. However, I'm not sure if it's widely known that a special has also been released alongside it, which primarily focuses on the Rats, the group of outsiders that Ciri took into their ranks in season 4.    Like the series, the result isn't necessarily bad, but it's also not nearly as good as it should be given the source material. And the Rats aren't the most interesting group of characters in either the books or the series to begin with, so a prequel about them wasn't exactly the most exciting idea. But at least we get to see more of Leo Bonhart (Sharlto Copley), who was a clear highlight of the fourth season (and the books). And Dolph Lundgren is also quite entertaining as a washed-up Witcher.    Ultimately, however, “The Rats: A Witcher Tale” is little more than a fairly inconsequential supplement to the series. It’s definitely not a must-see.

review_content: With Hollywood pumping Christian films twisted in Hollywood manner to illustrate that Catholic is bad and scary, thise films kept falling from box office despite their fake praise from paid critics and influencers.  The Ritual, to my surprise, was an apologetic story how those seemingly kind-hearted act of liberals and faithless were in reality the acts of those seduced by the real evils and demons. Those twisted acts destroyed, if not worsened the situation. Returning to the traditional faith of Catholicism was, is and will always be the best course of stepping forward towards saving your own soul.  I needed to clarify the fact that Catholic church wasn't involved in the making or the funding of this film. However, even the actors, writers, director and producers went to this apologetic direction at the end.  The Ritual was also an amazing canvas for Al Pacino to recreate the character of exorcism priest Father Theophilus Resinger. Pacino truly embodied a wise fatherly old man, soft spoken with short yet clear selective wordings. Explosive Pacino, that's a regular act. This soft spoken, thoughtful and fatherly Pacino was a revelation of how powerful Pacino's acting ability truly was. Such a brilliant portrayal and acting prowess.   The talented Dan Stevens was also showcased his acting prowess. Father Joseph Steiger was a learned and serious character. Stevens maintained the creases on his forehead and frown smile throughout the film, making Steiger looking always had intellectual and humanity principles war within himself. The inner struggle that he ended up almost losing soul to demonic's twisted suggestions.  I am personally glad to see this kind of exorcism film because it is closer to exorcism process in Catholic church. Unlike the commercialized fake 2024's The Exorcism or 2023's The Pope's Exorcist with Russel Crowe.  The Ritual was a drama film first, and I don't even think it could enter the category of horror flick. The Ritual was also a low budget drama. It showed very little visual effects and no jump scare.  If you're Christian other than Catholic, you might want to contemplate: If God exists, then demons exist.  I personally love The Ritual and that 98 minutes went by like a breeze and then the film ended.  If you're Catholic or interested in Catholicism, I highly recommend The Ritual.
