cast_names: Susana Abaitua, Andrés Gertrúdix, Iraia Elias, Ariadna Gil, Raúl Arévalo, Anartz Zuazua, Jaime Chávarri, Cris Iglesias, Mikel Losada, Ander Lacalle
review_content: [Netflix] The thriller, as a genre for reconstructing historical memory, works by adding elements of suspense to the revelation of intimate stories that reflect dark periods. Agustín Díaz Yanes returns to the genre (and to cinema) to tell a story of infiltrators within the terrorist group ETA, but above all, of the psychological consequences of impersonation. With close-ups that express the oppressive atmosphere and a French noir pace, the film loses its balance in the development, but it builds characters of emotional depth.

cast_names: Susana Abaitua, Andrés Gertrúdix, Iraia Elias, Ariadna Gil, Raúl Arévalo, Anartz Zuazua, Jaime Chávarri, Cris Iglesias, Mikel Losada, Ander Lacalle
review_content: She Walks in Darkness is a story of silence, sacrifice, and quiet fear. Susana Abaitua shines in a restrained, human performance wrapped in elegant tension. It’s not about scares, but unease. Slow at times, yet deeply moving. Rating: 8/10.

cast_names: Susana Abaitua, Andrés Gertrúdix, Iraia Elias, Ariadna Gil, Raúl Arévalo, Anartz Zuazua, Jaime Chávarri, Cris Iglesias, Mikel Losada, Ander Lacalle
review_content: Agustín Díaz Yanes returns with a restrained yet gripping thriller that hooks you from the start. She walks in darkness balances the tension of espionage with the weight of recent history, and while it’s not flawless, it’s clear that Yanes knows exactly what story he wants to tell.    The pacing is steady, never dragging. There are moments of real suspense —the kind that make you hold your breath— and a contained tone that avoids overstatement, making each burst of action feel sharper. The cast is excellent: no one overacts, no one fades. Everything feels calibrated, believable, and tense.    Visually, it’s stunning. The cinematography has that raw, cold realism that fits the story perfectly, while Yanes’ direction walks a fine line between thriller and human drama. You can feel his effort to look back without falling into political preaching or empty heroism.    Could it have taken more risks? Sure. At times, it feels like it’s holding back a little too much. But even so, it’s a solid, mature piece of work —smart, ethical, and genuinely engaging.    She walks in darkness is cinema crafted with precision: tense, intelligent, and sincere. It doesn’t need noise to make an impact.

review_content: The Map That Leads to You is another entry in the long line of romantic dramas adapted from novels, and it hits nearly every cliché in the playbook. The story follows familiar beats, with predictable twists and well-worn character arcs, making it feel more generic than groundbreaking. That said, it’s competently made and does exactly what it sets out to do: deliver a heartfelt, dreamy romance aimed squarely at its target audience.    For viewers who enjoy these kinds of sweeping love stories, this film will likely land well. For others, it may come across as just another formulaic romance in an already crowded genre. Not bad by any means—just nothing new.    Rating: 2.5/5 – 70% – Worth Watching

review_content: “Love is what makes us dance in the face of death”  Everyone that knows me should know by now how much I eat up depressing romantic dramas. So naturally I was extremely excited when I saw a trailer for this movie. I like KJ Apa and Madelyn Cline quite a bit. So I figured this was the perfect choice for my weekly movie swap with Joe.  I was super into this movie from the jump. The helpless romantic in me was smiling and enjoying my time with it. I liked the characters a good amount. I appreciated how they developed the chemistry early on. I did think as the movie progressed it fizzled a tiny bit at moments. But I really enjoyed how they wrapped things up by the end. I cared enough for the characters that I was always invested in the movie. I also like the supporting characters. I don’t really have a ton to say about it. I found it to be enjoyable and pretty cliché but that was what I expected. It doesn’t really do anything different or bring anything new to the genre but that’s fine. I can watch movies like this all the time and enjoy them. I’m a complete sucker for these types of movies and I’m glad it was another one that I walked away enjoying  * Movie swapping with Joe

cast_names: Thomasin McKenzie, Ben Radcliffe, Katherine Waterston, Emma Laird, Tom Goodman-Hill, Ramon Tikaram, Tim McMullan, Anna Maxwell Martin, Sue Johnston, Tom Felton
review_content: Fackham Hall is what happens when Downton Abbey gets hit on the head and wakes up thinking it’s The Naked Gun. It has all the usual aristocratic nonsense—snobby nobles, long-suffering servants, and people dramatically announcing tea—but now everyone is also falling down stairs, misunderstanding basic words, or accidentally insulting the monarchy.    The humor swings between dumb and clever, often at the same time. There’s slapstick, sharp wordplay, and so many hidden background jokes that you’ll find yourself pausing and rewinding like you’re investigating a crime. Half the funniest moments aren’t even the focus of the scene.    The cast deserves credit for committing fully to the stupidity. Watching very serious actors behave like complete idiots—with absolute sincerity—is the real punchline.

cast_names: Thomasin McKenzie, Ben Radcliffe, Katherine Waterston, Emma Laird, Tom Goodman-Hill, Ramon Tikaram, Tim McMullan, Anna Maxwell Martin, Sue Johnston, Tom Felton
review_content: I'm loving the return of spoofs, even the not-so-great ones. This started hopeful, and certain gags worked magically. Unfortunately, 90 minutes felt like a decade, and the balance between sense and nonsense didn't work for me at all. I was in with the over-the-topness, but not so much when it resulted in making other jokes fall flat, like with the conclusion to the 'whodunit'. Otherwise, the production and casting were great. I'd maybe recommend it if you like royal period piece dramas.

review_content: A spoof comedy about mainly English aristocracy that uses unfortunately mainly blunt and not really funny humour. The basic idea opened up for sharp, witty and biting English humour à la Monty Python or Jimmy Carr, but no. They chose a continuous cascade of the same blunt jokes that are just sometimes funny. Though the pain of having to watch through it all in order to find the few gems is just too painful. There is better out there. The potential was there, but they failed to make anything out of it.

review_content: Scott Adkins is great as a martial-arts hero, but like pretty much all the martial arts "stars", his strong suit is ONLY his fight choreography; his acting skills suck balls and his _a la Steven Seagal_ continued grim-faced, impassive, "I'm only here to kill you in a thousand ways" schtick has really grown wearisome. Nonetheless, I decided to give this a try...and I can tell you right from the beginning that this Vincente character seriously needs to be dead. What a creepy pervert; he treats his daughter like he's dying to get in her panties and that's just disgusting, even in a movie. Nobody wants to see some sick pervert trying to seduce his own teenage daughter; that's just repulsive.     Fast-forward to _"Right now, you're a .... lunatic"_ (but pronounced "loon-ATTIC") This girl doesn't even know how to speak English, and they try to put her in a co-starring role; are you flippin' kidding me? A "loon-ATTIC".... I should stop right now. This movie is going to suck balls. I already know it.    He puts the gun on the ground...the ground in the WOODS that are surrounding them, the GROUND that is covered with pine needles, dirt, and leaves, and says _"I'm putting the gun on the floor..."_      "On the floor"... cast_names: Ciryl Gane, Alice Belaïdi, Foued Nabba, Maleaume Paquin, Anne Azoulay, Samuel Jouy, Virgile Bramly, Maéva El Aroussi, Affif Ben Badra, Emmanuel Bonami
review_content: K.O. is one of those films that doesn’t pretend to be anything else: straight-up action, violent and relentless. The plot is just an excuse to watch UFC star Cyril Gane unleash his physical power on screen. And while the story doesn’t break new ground, the frantic pace and fight sequences make sure there’s no time to get bored.    The main character carries a past marked by guilt and is forced back into violence when a new conflict arises. By his side, a police officer who doesn’t always follow the rules provides an interesting contrast, though the film barely explores her motivations. Everything moves fast, with the spotlight firmly on the punches and chases.    What really works here are the brutal action sequences. Gane may not be a versatile actor, but physically he delivers exactly what’s expected: strength, agility, and sheer presence. Some scenes, like the nightclub fight or the one in the police station, are shot with a raw energy that recalls the best of modern action cinema.    Anyone looking for depth or layered drama won’t find it here. K.O. keeps things simple: brutal fights, occasional comic relief, and a tight runtime that plays to its advantage.    In the end, it doesn’t reinvent the genre, but it does exactly what it promises: ninety minutes of stylized violence and fast-paced entertainment.

cast_names: Alex Roe, Maddie Hasson, Marco Pigossi, Andra Nechita
review_content: Bone Lake taps into a familiar thriller template—one that’s been done countless times—but it’s the kind of premise that never really gets old when executed well. While A Perfect Getaway still stands as the gold standard for this style of story, this film absolutely nails the tone and rhythm it’s aiming for. It’s tense, fun, and delivers exactly the kind of twisty entertainment you expect from this subgenre. Even knowing the general beats, the ride is still satisfying, and it’s the sort of movie I’ll happily revisit in a few years once the details fade. A rock-solid entry for fans of contained, paranoia-laced thrillers.  Rating: 3.5/5 – 80% – Would Recommend

cast_names: Alex Roe, Maddie Hasson, Marco Pigossi, Andra Nechita
review_content: 3 Thoughts After Watching ‘Bone Lake’:  1. Great slow burn. Great cast. Great twists. Great ending. What more could you really need?  2. I love these little horror-thrillers that I had never really heard of, with no mega celebs in sight, that end up surprising me. Did this re-invent the wheel? No. But I was thoroughly entertained from start to finish.  3. Kudos to the directing and the soundtrack. I thought the music choices were great and I thought there were some impressive shots.

cast_names: Scott Adkins, Peter Shinkoda, Michael Copon, Michael Rene Walton, Masanori Mimoto, Gary Cairns, Shane Kosugi, Kansuke Asano, Donald Cerrone, Fitim Krasniqi
review_content: I have to admit I have never been the world's #1 Scott Adkins fan. Not sure why but I've always had problems distinguishing him from the roles he plays, and normally I don't have that problem. In some (seems like most?) of his earlier roles, my biggest issue with Scott Adkins was his seeming arrogance and cockiness, as if his character was invincible and unbeatable in the movie, so Adkins somehow thought HE was himself. I know....I know.... and again, I rarely have a problem separating actors from their characters so I'm not sure why I've had such a problem with Scott Adkins. (Although I've never met him in real life; maybe he really _is_ like that! LOL) In any event, this was definitely one of the better Scott Adkins movies I've watched, and I've never had a problem with his martial arts skill; he's one of the best in American cinema. I was expecting this to be a really weak copy of Donnie Yen's _Ip Man_ (based on the life story of Bruce Lee's famous instructor in Wing Chun) but this was its own story, and a pretty good one, at that. There were parts of it, of course, that were exaggerated, and parts of it that slogged like a snail through a mud pit, but overall it was a decent martial-arts movie. I was a bit surprised and disappointed by parts of it (watch it and you'll probably understand) but it was still worth a watch. Admittedly, it's a bit long at almost 2 hours; I think there were numerous parts that could have been edited out, or at least shortened, but I would still recommend it for fans of martial arts or action flicks. It's not a copy or clone of anything; I think it stands on its own as a solid martial arts movie but admittedly, your mileage may vary.

I recommend it, but it's no more than a one-time watch.

review_content: I assumed this was based on a true story, so I was a bit disappointed when there wasn't a text crawl at the end with updates/details on what happened to these characters.  I think that highlights how invested I was.  The movie does an excellent job making these characters feel real, with credit due both to the writing and performances.  The documentary framing device allows for some excellent introspective moments, highlighting how much the faculty cares despite how insanely brutal the job can be.  We get a similar contrast for the students, seeing their extreme behavior, but also glimpsing the potential that drives the faculty to give everything to these kids.  A hard but worthwhile watch.

movie_title: The SpongeBob Movie: Search for SquarePants
cast_names: Tom Kenny, Clancy Brown, Rodger Bumpass, Bill Fagerbakke, Carolyn Lawrence, Mr. Lawrence, George López, Ice Spice, Arturo Castro, Sherry Cola
review_content: Watched this with my 8 year old who bloody loved it.   Having sat through (I think) all of the SpongeBob films with him, I have to say I appreciated this as a massive step up from most of the previous entries in the franchise (from the perspective of a 43 year old man with no history with or nostalgia for the original cartoon).  The humour is fairly base and in places it relies heavily on bums and bodily functions. But the simple story moves at a good pace; the jokes are frequent and some even landed for me - I did laugh out loud at Mr Krabs proclaiming that,  to celebrate SpongeBob's achievements, for the next half hour all Krabbie Patties would be full price.   Voice work across the whole film is good; Mark Hamil's voice performance was perhaps a little close to his Joker but that's not entirely a bad thing and for an actor I don't generally care for, i thought he did a very good job.   Not essential viewing at the cinema but if you have kids to entertain over the Christmas and New Year holidays then there are worse things to watch (Zootropolis 2).  Sensible run time too.

movie_title: The SpongeBob Movie: Search for SquarePants
cast_names: Tom Kenny, Clancy Brown, Rodger Bumpass, Bill Fagerbakke, Carolyn Lawrence, Mr. Lawrence, George López, Ice Spice, Arturo Castro, Sherry Cola
review_content: I’ll admit even though I’m a massive SpongeBob nerd who’s been a fan of SpongeBob ever since I learned my first ever word, I’ve never been blown away by any of the movies apart from the first SpongeBob movie of course which is one of my favourite animations ever but man I’m super happy to say that The Search For SquarePants really surprised me by how awesome it was and it was so much fun, as a lifelong goofy Gober member I definitely approve of this film.  I honestly didn’t expect this film to be this good but I had such a super fun time with this and it’s the best SpongeBob movie I’ve seen in freaking ages. I was pretty sceptical about the animation at first and although I would’ve preferred it a lot more if it had the classic 2D animation, it wasn’t too bad actually and yeah I know the live action part looked a little off but I actually loved it as it was absolutely hilarious to look at and come on SpongeBob is meant to be super wacky anyway so it didn’t bother me at all and to be honest it’s my favourite part of the film if anything.  The characters were amazing and after all these years they literally feel exactly the same, I mean SpongeBob’s still super annoying, Mr Krab’s still incredibly tight, Patrick is still…well thick as shit and Squidward is still a walking misery and is so relatable. I was a little surprised how they didn’t include Sandy or Plankton to much here though but I’m guessing that was due to their own films not going down too well.  The big standout in this movie for me was easily the Flying Dutchman as he was literally funny as hell and was such a amazing villain, Mark Hamill was absolutely perfect for this role especially as live action Dutchy which was hilarious, I literally loved that rollercoaster scene so much.  I am gonna give this movie a super solid 8.5 out of 10 and may even give it a 9 on a rewatch as it was such an awesome animation and if you love SpongeBob as much as me, I recommend this a lot.   8.5/10

review_content: this was great. it was a bit of a slow burn (which is not my thing), but I liked the payoff. the male lead is bland, but I'm pretty sure that's by design. this movie isn't about him, it's about her.     most of what's going on is a little obvious, but from reading comments/reviews on the internets, it looks like a lot of folks missed the twist/reveal. I read lots of incredulous takes about the female lead's actions, and yes, they don't make sense at face value. also lots of complaints about a particular ~5 minute piece where a character appears to lay out the whole conceit of the movie.    this is what I've seen most negative comments missing: [spoiler]when malcolm is describing what he's doing with the house, we initially believe him. the movie reveals to us, however, that he is wrong. he thinks he's taking advantage of the house/spirits, but he is the one being taken advantage of. the spirits are biding their time until they can get revenge. the box cake is made by the spirits, not the men. the spirits are looking for the soul of the woman malcolm killed 200 years ago. when liz eats the entire cake uncontrollably, they understand that after 200 years, they find the witch. and they get their revenge at the end - [/spoiler]it's all they ever wanted. malcolm and his cousin never had any idea [spoiler]that was what's happening. they thought they were in control. why do you think the house killed the obnoxious male cousin? he never had any power, and he had no more use at that point in the movie because the spirits knew they finally had the right person.[/spoiler] when you realize this, a lot of earlier activity in the movie is subverted and starts to make sense.    I am interested specifically in the comment about the other cabin nearby, where malcolm describes the residents as much worse. have to pay attention on second watch.    oh, and the horror designs are excellent. real nightmare stuff.

cast_names: Louis Theroux, HSTikkyTokky, Sneako, Justin Waller, Andrew Tate, Tristan Tate, Donald Trump, Keanu Reeves
review_content: Not sure Theroux's usual approach really worked here. His strategy has always been to ask uncomfortable questions and then keep quiet as the interviewees talk themselves into a hole.    With these guys, they'll happily spend all day talking without prompting. One of our first encounters with HSTikkyTokky sees Theroux having to ask for a normal conversation rather than leaving HS to yap directly into their cameras.    None of them care about digging holes either. They're fully aware they're in an attention economy where saying the most provocative shit is the name of the game. They know they're in the hole but they want to be there because there's money to be found at the bottom.    Throughout the documentary, we're supposed to see through the façade of these lifestyles. The relationship strains caused by excessive womanising and indulgence are meant to be cautionary tales. Except this ignores how many of their viewers will aspire to these lifestyles regardless. If you're watching Fresh and Fit Podcast as a below average guy making minimum wage, living at home and never getting laid then the leased supercars, rented condos and throwaway hook-ups with Instagram models are still going to look appealing.    Towards the end, we get a brief look into the crossover between the manosphere and conspiracy theories. This is played for laughs and it's almost like Theroux is taking inspiration from his interviewees. "The world isn't run by Sam Smith" is a funny rebuttal to be clipped and shared online. Except the full context is Satanist imagery used by celebrities and advertising. Earlier, we were shown how everyone in the manosphere copies Andrew Tate's hand gestures as symbolism. Is it really that hard to believe covering over one eye could be the same thing for another group? The reach, influence and depraved behaviour of the Epstein class is a proven fact by this point. We live in a post-conspiracy age and this seemed like a pointless detour.    In the end, Theroux is trying to shine a spotlight on cockroaches that don't mind the light. They're the reverse Bane: they were born in it, moulded by it. If you've never encountered these ideas before, it'll be shocking. Those who dislike the manosphere will continue to do so. But for guys already on board with it all I doubt this will change their minds.

cast_names: Louis Theroux, HSTikkyTokky, Sneako, Justin Waller, Andrew Tate, Tristan Tate, Donald Trump, Keanu Reeves
review_content: I watched Louis Theroux: Inside the Manosphere today and came away thinking the same thing for most of the documentary: *this is fascinating… but I’m not sure it’s having the effect it thinks it’s having.*    I wasn’t familiar with Louis Theroux before watching this, but his style is pretty easy to pick up quickly. He’s polite, slightly awkward, and asks questions in a calm, curious way that encourages people to keep talking. Sometimes a bit too much.    The documentary sets out to examine what’s often called the “manosphere”, the online world where people debate masculinity, dating and modern gender dynamics. Theroux spends time with some of the better known personalities in that space, including Andrew Tate, Myron Gaines and Pearl Davis.    The goal seems pretty clear. Put these figures under the microscope and expose the problems with their worldview.    But while watching it, I couldn’t shake the feeling that the documentary occasionally turns into the best marketing campaign the manosphere could ask for.    Instead of dismantling these personalities, the film often gives them plenty of time to calmly explain their views. If you had never heard of them before, this documentary might actually be the first thing that sends you to look them up on YouTube afterwards.    Theroux himself also adds an interesting dynamic. His gentle, slightly nerdy and very non-confrontational presence is clearly meant to contrast with the personalities he’s interviewing. The problem is that he unintentionally embodies the exact “beta male” stereotype that many manosphere commentators regularly criticise. At times it feels less like he’s challenging their worldview and more like he’s accidentally demonstrating it.    Even so, the documentary is still worth watching. You do get a glimpse into how this whole online ecosystem works. A lot of the outrage clearly runs on attention, algorithms and the basic internet rule that controversy equals clicks.    Where the film feels a little thin is in explaining why these ideas resonate with so many young men in the first place. Instead of digging into the social or cultural reasons, the focus stays mostly on the personalities themselves.    Personally I sit somewhere in the middle of this whole debate. I understand why critics on the far left are concerned about parts of the manosphere, but I also understand why many men are drawn to the conversations happening there. The real problem is that the loudest voices on both sides tend to turn everything into a shouting match.    In the end, *Inside the Manosphere* is still a very watchable documentary. It shines a light on a strange corner of the internet that clearly isn’t going away anytime soon.    I’m just not entirely convinced it dismantles the movement it set out to examine. If anything, it might accidentally send a few new recruits their way.

cast_names: Louis Theroux, HSTikkyTokky, Sneako, Justin Waller, Andrew Tate, Tristan Tate, Donald Trump, Keanu Reeves
review_content: Concerning.    These boys claim they're outside of the matrix and not doing what the powers at the top want them to do, but they don't realize that they're contributing to the matrix by creating a "men vs women" dichotomy/division. That's what the elite want, chaos, and for the people to be divided. They're doing the elite's bidding without even realizing it.    If they really wanted to go against the grain and do what the elites fear then they'd endeavor to empower & unite people. review_content: The Luckiest Man in America (2025) takes a true story that could have felt bone‑dry and turns it into something gripping. The film follows Michael Larson, a man who walked onto a game show as a supposed clown and left as a legend, outsmarting the system while everyone watched in disbelief.    Paul Walter Hauser gives a standout performance, playing Larson as a slightly awkward everyman whose odd manner masks a razor‑sharp mind. Walton Goggins is pitch‑perfect as the smarmy host, adding just the right amount of sleaze to the bright TV veneer.    Visually, it’s bold: high‑contrast lighting and tilt‑shift CGI give the sets an uncanny, dreamlike quality. The real tension comes from the clash between the gloss of the studio and the frantic production office, where the crew scrambles to figure out how Larson keeps winning.    As the stakes rise, the mood swings between exhilaration and dread. The producers try to contain a contestant they can’t predict, while Larson pushes his luck further than anyone expects. Strong sound design and immaculate set work turn what could have been a dry retelling into a taut, tragic character piece.    If you liked… Quiz, Uncut Gems, or Dumb Money, you’ll be right at home.

cast_names: Ethan Hawke, Bobby Cannavale, Andrew Scott, Margaret Qualley, Patrick Kennedy, Jonah Lees, Simon Delaney, Giles Surridge, Michael James Ford, David Rawle
review_content: It’s striking how some nights can feel small and ordinary when they begin, yet carry the weight of an entire lifetime inside them. All it takes is the right conversations, old resentments surfacing, or long-buried dreams finding one last chance to exist. “Blue Moon,” directed by Richard Linklater, builds its whole narrative around that kind of compressed moment, a few hours where the past, pride, frustration, and nostalgia collide in the same space. Instead of tracing the full trajectory of a historical figure, the film focuses on a single night in the life of lyricist Lorenz Hart, turning that evening into an emotional crossroads where memories, expectations, and a quiet sense of farewell pile up. The premise is fascinating because it suggests an intimate portrait of an artist facing a personal turning point. The problem is that the film quickly reveals how much that idea depends almost entirely on the strength of its dialogue and on the presence of its leading actor.    The story takes place on March 31, 1943, the opening night of “Oklahoma!,” the musical that marks Richard Rodgers’ new partnership with Oscar Hammerstein II after his creative split from Hart. Instead of following the show itself, “Blue Moon” stays almost entirely inside Sardi’s restaurant, where artists and collaborators gather to celebrate the premiere. That’s where we find Hart, played by Ethan Hawke, drifting from table to table, chatting with acquaintances, revisiting memories of his old partnership with Rodgers, and trying to deal with the uncomfortable feeling that he’s been left behind. The structure works almost like a chamber play on film, focusing on verbal exchanges and small social gestures. It’s a choice that fits Linklater’s tradition of prioritizing characters and dialogue, but it also exposes the film to an obvious risk. If the conversations don’t carry enough dramatic tension, time starts to feel heavy.    That’s exactly where the screenplay struggles the most. Written by Robert Kaplow, the script often leans on long monologues that try to reveal Hart’s emotional state but rarely move the story forward or deepen the relationships around him. There are moments when those speeches capture something compelling about the insecurity of an artist afraid of being overshadowed by a new creative era. Most of the time, though, they pile up without creating a real sense of dramatic progression. As a result, the film’s relatively short runtime starts to feel longer than it actually is. The dominant impression is of watching a character circle around the same anxieties over and over again, without the drama finding new ways to explore them.    Paradoxically, the element that keeps “Blue Moon” from becoming completely stagnant is also the one that highlights its weaknesses the most: Ethan Hawke’s performance. He builds Lorenz Hart with an engaging mix of insecurity and bitterness. His gestures, the rhythm of his speech, and the small behavioral tics all reveal careful character work. Even when the script falls into repetitive speeches, Hawke finds ways to make them expressive, as if he’s constantly trying to convince not just the people around him, but also himself, that he still has a place in that artistic world. That emotional commitment turns Hart into a figure who’s both fascinating and melancholic, someone who always seems one step behind his own story.    The characters orbiting around him function more like reflective surfaces than fully developed people. Andrew Scott appears as Richard Rodgers, carrying the quiet weight of a partnership that no longer exists in the same way. Margaret Qualley plays Elizabeth, the object of Hart’s affection and idealization, representing perhaps the last emotional redemption he imagines for himself. While these interactions offer moments of tension, they rarely develop deeply enough to break the narrative’s repetitive structure. The film often feels like it’s on the edge of a larger emotional revelation that never quite arrives.    That dynamic contributes to the sense that “Blue Moon” feels closer to a filmed stage play than to a film fully exploring cinematic possibilities. Linklater’s staging is elegant and respectful, but also deliberately restrained. The camera observes the characters quietly, favoring dialogue and performance over more expressive visual movement. That choice reinforces the story’s intimate tone, but it also limits the dramatic potential of the space. At several points, it feels like the material might have found an even stronger form if presented directly on stage, where its verbal density would have a more natural environment to thrive.    Overall, “Blue Moon” ends up as a curious study of the fragility of talent in the face of time. The idea of following Lorenz Hart through a decisive night in his life is conceptually strong, especially for viewers like me who weren’t familiar with the composer’s real-life story beforehand. The film hints at interesting reflections about artistic pride, creative collaboration, and the fear of becoming irrelevant. Still, those ideas rarely find a dramatic structure strong enough to fully support them. What ultimately remains is the magnetic presence of Ethan Hawke, whose performance turns an uneven project into something consistently watchable. “Blue Moon” may end up being remembered less for its overall achievements and more for that central performance, one that lights up a film that otherwise struggles to keep its own flame alive.

If you know almost nothing about Lorenz Hart, such as myself, you get such a good idea of a full depth characterization here. review_content: Ethan Hawke is superb in this role. Having watched the movie with little historical knowledge of Rodgers & Hart it was a the beginning of the tragedy of Hart's last year. His rambles, his yearning for love while feeling unworthy of it, his desire to stop or temper his drinking clashes with his desire for the drink itself. Most tragic is the separation from his professional partner and his partner's success contrasted to Hart realizing that the world and his industry is leaving him behind and the way he wants to express his talent no longer matches what audiences want. That this movie sets up his last musical where on the premiere night he shows up drunk, gets lost in the night and a few days later dies from exposure induced pneumonia. Very tragic but in that tragedy such wit, such poetry in the lines and the way they are delivered.

He was playing a man who was desperate to charm but in the end self-absorbed and hard to be with, and he did.. well... too good a job. I think we were meant to feel some sympathy for his fundamental sadness and admiration for his talent but as he was played here I couldn't get past his creepiness and narcissism.

cast_names: Helena Zengel, Willem Dafoe, Emily Watson, Finn Wolfhard, Stefan Burlacu, Gabriel Spahiu, Puiu-Mircea Lăscuș, Anna L. Coats
review_content: This movie is quite an experience. If for good or for bad, everyone has to decide for themselves.  Good: The cinematography is superb, foremost in still lives, in the nature, the play with light and shadow.   The locations, very rural, very 70s-80s Eastern Europe, no smartphones, no hightech, farm live. Willem Dafoe as Maxim wears an armour like a 17th-18th century knight. Makes the whole situation and scenery surreal and dreamy. Main colors are brown and green.   There is not that much talking. The movie tries to tell its story mostly through the images, the soundscape and atmosphere.   Bad: Street signs with english and English books in the library destroy the immersion of credible set design and locations.   The characters, foremost the group of children remains faceless and irrelevant. Maxim (Willem Dafoe) is a somehow sad man who has a daughter Yuri (Helena Zengel), wished a son, commands a group of children to protect the villagers against the Ochi and lives separated from his wife Dasha (Emily Watson) who left him and the daughter to live an eremitic life as a researcher of the Ochi.  The main character Yuri is a girl within a group of boys that doesn’t fit in, neither as daughter, neither as a group member within the children nor as a villager. She befriends with a baby Ochi and tries to bring it back to its family. The mother Dasha lives alone in a hut, takes care of some sheep and looks for the Ochi. At the end, she plays a melody on the flute to communicate with the Ochi. Well.   The Ochi, some variation of E.T. and chimpanseelike short nose apes, that seem to be ravishing beasts in the woods, but are hunted by Maxim and his children soldiers and communicate by singing and melodys. All this sounds quite interesting, but the story is so convoluted with different themes that the whole mixture doesn’t really fit together and everything is underdeveloped: the father-daughter-mother family drama, the coming-of-age theme of Yuri, the rural life between naturalism and superstition, the dynamics in the group of children, the poetic and surreal friendship between Yuri and the baby Ochi. The script forces all together, but doesn’t succeed in keeping the interest for the characters. The characters stay very distant and superficial.  For a one-time watch, it’s good, it’s unusual, it’s special and it’s interesting enough compared to all the mainstream flics. But in the end, it’s hollow and leaves you unsatisfied.

cast_names: John Candy, Tom Hanks, Steve Martin, Martin Short, Eugene Levy, Macaulay Culkin, Bill Murray, Catherine O'Hara, Conan O'Brien, Mel Brooks
review_content: I write very few comments or reviews, but I had to for this one. I am an 80s/90s kid and John Candy was the first celebrity death that really hurt. I was 9 years old when he died and I struggled to process it. I thought he'd always be there, and then he wasn't. I've thought of him countless times over the years, always with a tinge of sadness. When I learned last year that this documentary was on the way I waited excitedly for it to come out. Then, once it did, I took a few weeks to finally watch it because I wasn't sure how emotional it would be. Well, the answer is very, but I am glad I did.    Beautifully produced and the interviews are a dream team of incredible people in their own right. The family segments were especially poignant and ultimately what broke me. He is worth being remembered and loved, just as we all are. I am glad that this gives the world a chance to do that again.

cast_names: John Candy, Tom Hanks, Steve Martin, Martin Short, Eugene Levy, Macaulay Culkin, Bill Murray, Catherine O'Hara, Conan O'Brien, Mel Brooks
review_content: I Like Me is a heartfelt and well-crafted look into the life of John Candy, one of the most beloved comedic actors of his time. As someone who grew up watching his movies, I really appreciated how this documentary went beyond the laughs to show the man behind the humor. It gives an honest look at his kindness, humility, and the way he made everyone around him feel included, especially in his improv work.  What really stood out to me was how it handled his personal struggles, with his health, the pressures of fame, and the demands of the industry. It’s both inspiring and sad to see how deeply he cared about others, often at the expense of himself.  While it doesn’t break much new ground stylistically, I Like Me succeeds in what it sets out to do: remind us why John Candy mattered and how much heart he brought to everything he touched. A touching tribute to a man who made the world laugh, and feel, a little more.

cast_names: John Candy, Tom Hanks, Steve Martin, Martin Short, Eugene Levy, Macaulay Culkin, Bill Murray, Catherine O'Hara, Conan O'Brien, Mel Brooks
review_content: Adequate but not great documentary on John Candy. So many interesting and fun anecdotes could have been shared that I've seen in other media that was missing here. However, this did introduce a family context which has been glossed over in other sources, so I welcomed that. Overall worth watching.

cast_names: John Candy, Tom Hanks, Steve Martin, Martin Short, Eugene Levy, Macaulay Culkin, Bill Murray, Catherine O'Hara, Conan O'Brien, Mel Brooks
review_content: For longtime fans, it’s an affectionate reunion. For newcomers, it’s gateway to a remarkable talent and gentle soul. In sum: a warmly recommended tribute that honours Candy’s legacy with sincerity and heart.

cast_names: John Candy, Tom Hanks, Steve Martin, Martin Short, Eugene Levy, Macaulay Culkin, Bill Murray, Catherine O'Hara, Conan O'Brien, Mel Brooks

He was even better than you thought, end of story.

cast_names: Dylan O'Brien, James Sweeney, Aisling Franciosi, Lauren Graham, Chris Perfetti, François Arnaud, Arkira Chantaratananond, Tasha Smith, Cree Cicchino, Katie Findlay
review_content: I went into Twinless knowing absolutely nothing about what it was, and that lack of context actually worked in its favor. The opening stretch is a bit disorienting, almost intentionally so, but once the film finds its footing it reveals itself as something far more heartfelt than expected. What unfolds is a deeply sentimental and emotional story that quietly sneaks up on you, anchored by sincerity rather than gimmicks.  This is the kind of movie that won’t hit everyone the same way, but for the right audience it’s going to land hard. I can very easily see this becoming an all-time favorite for a lot of people, especially those who connect with its themes and tone.  Rating: 4/5 – 85% – Would Recommend

review_content: A fantastic premise that is heightened by the clever execution of its opening.  Combine that with the brilliant dual performance from Dylan O'Brien and it was enough for me to power through some of the more cringe comedy moments that I typically avoid.  And luckily, there are enough non-cringe (or at least less-cringe) comedy moments that landed for me to still enjoy that half of the dramedy package.  I also really enjoyed how distinct these characters felt, with Rocky, Roman, and Dennis all feeling memorably unique.

cast_names: Caleb McLaughlin, Gabrielle Union, Stephen Curry, Aaron Pierre, Nicola Coughlan, David Harbour, Nick Kroll, Jenifer Lewis, Patton Oswalt, Jelly Roll
review_content: This movie is the definition of aight. Good animation, charming, pretty funny, but I think it lacked the emotional depth that it was going for. The beginning set up this to be Will’s arc to prove the world wrong about smalls (or is he a medium? was a lil confused about that lol). But then it turned into a Jett movie, or at the very least a Thorns movie. I think the protagonist building up the arcs of those around him instead of himself is a totally workable concept, but that development felt a bit rushed for my taste. I can see why they had Will score the winning shot as early in the movie as they did cause it was to show Jett’s struggle through her development, but even that payoff fell a little flat for me. This movie’s “second act fight” I guess I’ll call it was the selling of the team getting to Jett and playing selfishly, washing out all her teammates in the process. But, as I said before, that conflict resolved too quickly, as most of the team just kinda join back with Jett like nothing happened. Overall, I can’t say I walked out of the theater unhappy, but certainly underwhelmed. Give it a shot when it goes to streaming.

cast_names: Caleb McLaughlin, Gabrielle Union, Stephen Curry, Aaron Pierre, Nicola Coughlan, David Harbour, Nick Kroll, Jenifer Lewis, Patton Oswalt, Jelly Roll
review_content: Well that was quite messy. Too many ideas and characters being stuffed into too short of a runtime, it ends up very chaotic. The pacing was off like crazy, leading to rushed tropes and undeserved character moments. I'm also a bit disappointed we didn't really get to see the animal's strengths in the game, they all kind of play the same, only rarely we see them use their specific traits to their advantage, if they'd all look the same you couldn't differenciate them by play style at all. Not even Will, who I thought would be better in difficult terrain than the others and that'd be his advantage, or this vision thing they kept bringing up but ended up not making any plot relevant difference.    The Mercedes product placement was also a bit much, like c h i l l, I get it, no need to ram it into my face all the time, ever heard of subtlety? They did it much better with the PS5 placement, no need to beat me over the head with it. Must have cost Mercedes a TON of money to pull that. Looked out of place in that universe too.

cast_names: Caleb McLaughlin, Gabrielle Union, Stephen Curry, Aaron Pierre, Nicola Coughlan, David Harbour, Nick Kroll, Jenifer Lewis, Patton Oswalt, Jelly Roll
review_content: Goat is exactly what you expect it to be and it lacks any real surprises. But I have to admit the kids in the theater cheering at climactic moments warmed even my crusty old cold heart and made me realize every movie can be original for someone. So while Goat can’t hang with some other more recent Sony Pictures Animation films (the Spider-Verse movies, The Mitchells vs. the Machines, & KPop Demon Hunters)  it’s still an enjoyable watch.   Would Recommend, Especially For Younger Basketball Fans

cast_names: Caleb McLaughlin, Gabrielle Union, Stephen Curry, Aaron Pierre, Nicola Coughlan, David Harbour, Nick Kroll, Jenifer Lewis, Patton Oswalt, Jelly Roll
review_content: Well this movie was indeed the GOAT.  Going into this movie, I didn't really know much about it other than it's made by the same studio as the Spider-verse films and that the animation is pretty similar to it, which did make me wanna check this out as l adore those movies and, well it's safe to say, l'm definitely happy I did as Goat was such a badass little animation and is easily one of my favourites of the year so far.  It’s basically a cross between Spider-Verse and Zootropolis and i absolutely love it for that because… WHAT A FREAKING COMBINATION. it was so awesome and don’t even get me started on the animation as it was absolutely insane.  The story was so much fun and had me hooked from start to finish. It was seriously epic and who doesn’t love a classic underdog story.   Will was a great character and who knew a Goat was such a Goat as Basketball. He was one heck of a player that’s for sure. It was really satisfying when he beat that Horse at the end because, as Horse’s go, he was such an asshole.  I loved every member of the Vineland Thorns as they were all so funny and if I ever wanted to be a Basketball player, I’m definitely gonna join this unstoppable team, I mean come on they have a freaking Giraffe for god sake, yeah I never in my life imagined a Giraffe playing Basketball either, but hey, why not right. It was insanely awesome by the way (he could rap too). Hell yeahhhh.  My favourite characters were easily those super adorable Rhino twins as they were so funny and were one hell of a chaotic duo. The runners up is the Monkey, who was pretty mental as well.  I’m giving Goat a Goated 9 out of 10 and I could easily change it to a 10 on a rewatch as I had so much fun with this film and, by the sound of my review you can probably tell that by now. It was so Goated man… okay I’ll stop now.   9/10

cast_names: Caleb McLaughlin, Gabrielle Union, Stephen Curry, Aaron Pierre, Nicola Coughlan, David Harbour, Nick Kroll, Jenifer Lewis, Patton Oswalt, Jelly Roll
review_content: My first animation of the year and it’s of to a great start with GOAT first of all I’m a big fan of this style of animation similar to the spiderverse films and the teenage mutant turtles animation movie, and then just by the characters and vibe of the film and world building that side of it things feels like Zootropolis.  I was hooked from start to finish I think they did an amazing job at the character development and storytelling I’m not a basketball fan either and they made that look like the funnest sport out there GOOOO THORNS!  The main characters especially had fantastic arcs with will harris voiced by Caleb McLaughlin awesome to see him already get a huge role I knew it was him just by the way he talks will’s story definitely was the most inspiring by not being not being the biggest guy out there and still having the talent to beat the bigger players   Jett was probably the most interesting character out of the group already a legend in the game but needed to win the CLAW to become thr “GOAT” her development throughout was amazing.  I loved all the group aswell the coach Dennis was pretty funny,then we have Lenny the Giraffe, Olivia the Ostrich and Archie the Rhino  more stranger things presentation with David Harbour love to see it, his two daughters were the funniest part of the movie for me along with Modo the Komodo Dragon.  Mane Attraction was a badass Horse with killer looking Dreadlocks he was a perfect villain for the film his rivalry with Will from start to finish was done very well seeing both embarrass each other leading up to the final battle.  Had a really good time watching GOAT animations are hardly ever letdowns always have fun but wholesome stories with amazing lifelong characters. 9/10 could rise to a 10 on another watch because there ain’t many things I think of that I didn’t like.

cast_names: Samantha Cochran, Natalia Montgomery Fernandez, Elena Musser, Adam Carr, María Romanillos, Ismael Martínez, Sonia Almarcha, Almudena Amor, Jenna Hogan, Michael J. Sielaff
review_content: This film had its moments, _Coochie Coochie Coo_ and _Kidprint_ were genuinely creepy and felt like they took themselves seriously, pretty much the only reason why I'm giving this a 5. But honestly, most of the other stories were so over-the-top ridiculous that it just killed the vibe for me. The second Fun Size started, I lost all interest and skipped ahead just for _Kidprint_ and couldn't even get through _Home Haunt_.    Read some reviews online, especially on Reddit, calling this the best in the franchise. Ever since I watched the original from 2012, I always expect good stories from the other V/H/S films, but we rarely get decent stories with good immersive horror and well written storytelling.

cast_names: Samantha Cochran, Natalia Montgomery Fernandez, Elena Musser, Adam Carr, María Romanillos, Ismael Martínez, Sonia Almarcha, Almudena Amor, Jenna Hogan, Michael J. Sielaff
review_content: this is, by a long margin, the worst entry in the franchise. I love found footage horror. compared to the highs and even the lows of all the previous VHS movies, this one isn't even on the charts. frankly, this is one of the worst movies I've ever seen. twice.    terrible practical effects. terrible digital effects. nominally an anthology, but by no means a cohesive film (even as an anthology, which says a lot because it needs so little).     the actors are beyond hammy. the writing is beyond belief. you can close your eyes and just listen to most of the movie because the characters incessantly describe everything they are seeing and doing, often in the most unrealistic way possible.    I'm just really disappointed that this was what they did with one of my favorite horror franchises. this one is so bad, they'll probably have to call the next one a 'reboot.'

cast_names: Samantha Cochran, Natalia Montgomery Fernandez, Elena Musser, Adam Carr, María Romanillos, Ismael Martínez, Sonia Almarcha, Almudena Amor, Jenna Hogan, Michael J. Sielaff
review_content: 1- **Diet Phantasma**⠀4/10  Diet is always healthier, right? Nice bloody kills and effects but otherwise it's a half-baked idea that serves as an excuse to have more gore in the movie. Very repetitive. The commercial at the end was better than everything else.    2- **Coochie Coochie Coo**⠀5/10  Just a *Barbarian* haunted house knockoff. I like how gross everything looked and it made sense that they kept holding the camera as it's the only source of light. Mommy was equally funny and disturbing, solid makeup and prosthetics. There's a backstory that we could kinda put together but it's not fully developed. The big pupils on everyone are so off-putting. The decision making towards the end made no sense.    3- **Ut Supra Sic Infra (As Above, So Below)**⠀4/10  A 'reconstruct what happened' mystery with back and forths between past and present. It feels stitched together and the editing is distracting. The reveal is whatever. Nothing is even remotely explained but we got one gross kill towards the end at least.    4- **Fun Size**⠀7/10  One per person! That was Fun and it's Size should be bigger because I didn't want it to end. I like the concept and laughed quite a bit. I wanna dress as Mr. Fun Size for Halloween now, sick costume. The packaging parts on the monitor and the butt candy kill are the best moments. Something was missing with the factory set, it should look otherworldly and fit with Mr. Fun Size's appearance. Can't help but wonder what this would look like with a bigger budget.    5- **Kidprint**⠀6/10  Ok, they went there. Not sure what they were originally selling? Backup 3D prints of kids to replace in case they died? Like robots or clones? It does what it can with the short runtime but anyway the twist was awesome. Didn't expect it to be that disturbing.      6- **Home Haunt**⠀5/10  That's what happens when you take a haunted object from the Warrens' basement. Except for maybe two badass kills it all looked so fake and staged. I liked the flying ghosts part and it's the most Halloween of all the segments.    Average Score: 5.1/10

cast_names: Samantha Cochran, Natalia Montgomery Fernandez, Elena Musser, Adam Carr, María Romanillos, Ismael Martínez, Sonia Almarcha, Almudena Amor, Jenna Hogan, Michael J. Sielaff
review_content: It’s Halloween season, which means a new V/H/S film. What a sweet, bloody treat. I’m surprised they're finally doing a holiday-themed movie, like a Halloween special. This opens up more possibilities for themed episodes. I mean, they could do a follow-up with a Volume 2 or something, because this entry only scratches the surface of its concept, yet it also leaves enough open to explore more Halloween-related themes.    While V/H/S/ Halloween might not be my favorite, all the segments were solid. The standout segments were ‘Kid Print’ and ‘Home Haunt.’ One was disturbing and violent, while the other was entertaining, creative, and very violent. Both involve kids in danger, and I was surprised at how far they went, especially in terms of killing kids. ‘Kid Print’ takes a more grounded approach and is the darkest entry I’ve seen in these movies. The main horror in that segment is every parent's worst nightmare for their child, combined with the presence of sick individuals out there who want to harm children. ‘Home Haunt’ is more fun, as it focuses on an amateur, homemade haunted house attraction that turns deadly and becomes very real.    The rest of the segments were also good. Every V/H/S film always has that weak or terrible segment, but in my opinion, this one didn’t. 'Coochie Coochie Coo' was gross and might make you avoid drinking milk for a while. 'Ut Supra Sic Infra' is a straightforward segment about demon possession. 'Fun Size' feels and looks like one of those YouTube horror shorts, but it's still fun to watch. 'Diet Phantasma' is a dark comedy about a soda company testing a new soda through trials and experiments, even resorting to supernatural means out of desperation to find a new flavor.    What more is there to say? It’s a bloody good time.

cast_names: Samantha Cochran, Natalia Montgomery Fernandez, Elena Musser, Adam Carr, María Romanillos, Ismael Martínez, Sonia Almarcha, Almudena Amor, Jenna Hogan, Michael J. Sielaff
review_content: Not scary, just gross and relentless. Frantic pacing, became ridiculous towards the end.

cast_names: Samantha Cochran, Natalia Montgomery Fernandez, Elena Musser, Adam Carr, María Romanillos, Ismael Martínez, Sonia Almarcha, Almudena Amor, Jenna Hogan, Michael J. Sielaff
review_content: V/H/S/Halloween might just be the best entry in the anthology series yet. It’s rare for a franchise this deep into its run to feel this fresh, but this installment manages to deliver the chaotic energy and shocking moments fans have come to expect—without a truly weak segment in the bunch. The creative variety across the shorts keeps things moving at a great pace, and the Halloween theme ties everything together in a satisfyingly twisted way.  It’s not going to convert anyone who isn’t already on board with the V/H/S formula, but it doesn’t need to. For fans of found footage horror, this is another impressive and gruesomely entertaining chapter that proves the series still has plenty of life left.  Rating: 4/5 – 85% – Would Recommend

cast_names: Patricia López Arnaiz, Juan Minujín, Mabel Rivera, Nagore Aranburu, Itziar Aizpuru, Víctor Sáinz, Miro Magariños
review_content: There are films that do not take an explicit stance, yet leave an unmistakable emotional imprint. Here, the decision of a young girl to enter a cloistered convent is not built from conflict or conviction, but from a disconcerting passivity. There is no visible process, no doubts, no articulated desire. Only silence. And that silence becomes the film’s true discourse.    Ambiguity is constant. At times it feels critical, at times contemplative. But when the family farewell is filmed with almost surgical coldness and the protagonist’s face conveys distance rather than fulfillment, what remains is not transcendence but loss. If the intention was to celebrate vocation, the staging works against it. If the intention was to question it, the script never fully commits to exploring that crack.    The monologue by Aunt Maite is probably the film’s strongest moment. She articulates rational objections clearly and without caricature. The niece’s reply —“I will pray for you”— is not aggressive, but it is revealing: it shifts the debate onto a plane where argument no longer matters. It is a sentence that closes the conversation rather than enriching it. And that tension, properly developed, could have sustained a far more incisive film.    The use of Into My Arms by Nick Cave in an almost liturgical context is particularly striking. The song begins by denying faith in an interventionist God and speaks, above all, of human vulnerability and love without guarantees. Turning it into an accompaniment to religious solemnity simplifies its meaning. Or perhaps not: perhaps that appropriation reveals an unintended irony, a distance between what is heard and what is actually being said. Who knows if that is where the film’s most subtle critique truly lies.    Ultimately, the film suggests more than it asserts, but it does not always manage to sustain what it hints at. What should be a profound exploration of vocation, freedom and loss ends up in an ambivalence that borders on indefinition. There are valuable moments, yes, but the whole never quite finds its own center.

cast_names: Hiftu Quasem, Natalie Mitson, Lauren Lyle, Gabriel Prevost
review_content: I know I’m a bit bias on shark movies and I definitely rate them a bit more leniently than others. I guess I kind of compare them to other shark movies. The thing is we get so many awful shark movies and when they don’t suck I consider that a huge win. I’ve always said I like when they don’t make a complete joke of themselves. This movie played it completely straight and had no interest of making a joke of itself.  I genuinely thought this was a really good movie. It’s very well directed and it’s well shot. I thought it looked really great. The performances aren’t half bad. Some of the actresses were better than others. The biggest thing for me was that I actually cared about some of the characters. I found myself invested and caring about what happened to them. It’s not littered with stupid choices that we see in so many of these movies. I thought they did a lot of great things here. There’s definitely a few things that I would’ve done differently but I’m not the one who made this. To be honest the director did an excellent job considering this is her first feature length film. I like that they utilized mostly practical effects. They didn’t show us the shark a lot but the presence was felt. The second half definitely becomes a bit stagnant at times but nothing that bad. I was never bored watching this and there’s plenty of suspenseful moments. I was on the edge of my seat the last few minutes. For all the shark movies that we get I thought this was definitely one of the better ones. I’ll have to buy the Blu-ray down the road and show my dad this one. If you’re a fan of shark movies I’d give this one a shot.  * Shark Week: Day 4 * Shark Week: Watch #5

cast_names: Hiftu Quasem, Natalie Mitson, Lauren Lyle, Gabriel Prevost
review_content: Shark movies follow a simple formula: throw some characters into open water, something goes wrong, and before they know it, they’re fighting not to become fish food. “Something in the Water” doesn’t stray from that structure, which isn’t necessarily a bad thing. Horror and suspense have always thrived on formulas—the difference between a great movie and a forgettable one is how well it executes its ideas. The problem here is that Hayley Easton Street’s directorial debut never manages to step out of the shadow of the countless similar films that came before it. The premise has potential, with a paradise-like setting contrasting the impending horror of the ocean, but the shallow script and generic characters keep the film from diving into truly tense waters.    The story follows a group of friends reuniting at a Caribbean resort for one of their weddings. The script tries to inject some emotional depth into the group, especially through the unresolved tension between Meg (Hiftu Quasem) and Kayla (Natalie Mitson), who drifted apart after a past traumatic event. But the character development never goes beyond the bare minimum, and it quickly becomes clear that they’re more about filling archetypes than being genuinely interesting people. You’ve got the energetic friend, the reserved one, the group leader, and, of course, the one who recklessly puts everyone in danger—roles that feel like they were pulled straight from a horror movie handbook. The attempt to build drama between them is well-intentioned, but it never becomes compelling or engaging enough for the audience to really care about what happens to each of them.    The turning point comes when they rent a rundown boat for a day trip to a remote island. Anyone who’s ever seen a survival movie knows this is a terrible idea, and sure enough, their fun quickly turns into a nightmare when a shark attacks Ruth (Ellouise Shakespeare-Hart), tearing up her leg. In a panic, the friends try to head back to shore but crash into a reef, damaging the boat. Now, they’re stranded in the middle of the ocean, vulnerable and surrounded by a predator that’s not giving up anytime soon. The idea of a defenseless group floating in shark-infested waters has massive potential for extreme tension, and in the early moments, the director does manage to build effective suspense, using tight shots and the vastness of the sea to create an overwhelming sense of isolation. The problem is, after that, the movie just doesn’t know how to maintain that momentum.    The shark—supposedly the heart of the suspense—gradually loses its impact as the story progresses. Part of this is due to the limited visual effects, which make the creature feel less present and not particularly intimidating. But the bigger issue is the lack of creativity in the danger sequences. “Something in the Water” doesn’t bring anything new to the table that we haven’t already seen in films like “The Shallows” (2016) or “47 Meters Down” (2017), and what it does attempt doesn’t have the same intensity as those better entries in the genre. The characters’ survival efforts follow a predictable pattern, and even when a scene manages to create a little tension, it’s quickly undermined by decisions that are either illogical or way too convenient.    The third act tries to throw in a surprise element, but the execution stumbles due to an inconsistent tone. The movie can’t decide whether it wants to be a serious thriller or embrace the absurdity of its premise, resulting in a climax that feels artificial and unnecessarily over-the-top. There’s an attempt to deliver a shocking ending, but instead of leaving the theater impressed, audiences are more likely to just shrug it off.    On the technical side, “Something in the Water” has some merits. The cinematography makes great use of the tropical scenery, creating an interesting contrast between the beauty of the setting and the horror of the situation. Hayley Easton Street’s direction is competent within the script’s limitations, managing to pull off a few solid moments of immersion and desperation. The performances are decent, even if the material doesn’t give the cast much to work with. Hiftu Quasem does her best to add some depth to Meg, but the script never really gives her much to sink her teeth into.    Overall, “Something in the Water” ends up being just another generic survival thriller, lacking the dramatic weight, creativity, or energy needed to stand out. There’s the outline of a better movie in there—maybe with stronger characters or a more inventive approach to the dangers they face. But as it is, it just joins the long list of forgettable aquatic thrillers—the kind you watch with low expectations and forget as soon as the credits roll.

review_content: This film was basically an allegory for boomers that sold out future generations for the sake of their own immediate comfort, with a smattering of adrenochrome torture thrown in for good measure.   People that are too dumb to make this connection are probably also the ones that came away disliking it; just look at all the illiterate retards shitting on it here and wherever else audience reviews exist.   Despite its many flaws, the film pays off nicely and with a surprisingly touching tone — brotherly love isn’t always pretty, but the intent behind even the most disgusting gestures remains pure.   The budget for this film was clearly on the smaller side, but you can tell there was lots of ambition with the project. That said, I think they went a bit overboard in many areas that simply didn’t add anything to the story — gross for the sake of gross, weird for the sake of weird, and so on.   Overall, I find this movie a refreshing change of pace from your standard horror fare. This is one bittersweet happy ending that felt earned.

review_content: I went into The Home completely blind, only giving it a shot after spotting it on sale on Vudu. The plot itself is fairly forgettable, and while the twist is welcome, it doesn’t land with much force.    Where the film succeeds is in its atmosphere. The visuals are creepy, off-kilter, and unsettling in the best way, with a commitment to disturbing imagery that keeps you on edge. It’s trashy, yes, but in the kind of entertaining way that makes these smaller horror projects worth watching when you’re in the mood.    Rating: 2/5 – 65% – Not Recommended to Everyone

cast_names: Jandino Asporaat, Werner Kolf, Mark Rietman, Florence Vos Weeda, Juliette van Ardenne, Ferdi Stofmeel, Teun Kuilboer, Victoria Koblenko, Romana Vrede, Yannick Jozefzoon
review_content: It has a few funny moments, I won't deny it. But if you're looking for a comedy that makes you laugh non-stop, you better look elsewhere. Almost Cops tries to be many things at once—action, drama, satire, social commentary—and ends up being none of them. The whole thing feels like a movie that doesn’t know who it’s for or what tone it wants to keep. One moment it’s trying to critique police corruption, and the next it’s making poop jokes. It doesn’t add up.    The main duo works... sometimes. The chemistry between Ramón and Jack could’ve been much better if the script weren’t filled with clichés and recycled twists. Most of the humor feels forced or straight-up childish. And worst of all: there are scenes that aim for seriousness, but neither the pacing nor the visual style supports it. The direction never finds the right tone, and the story lurches forward with choppy editing, flat dialogue, and ridiculous action scenes.    The Rotterdam setting is totally wasted. They could’ve leaned into the local context or real social dynamics, but instead, they chose to copy American formulas with zero personality. There's nothing here that makes it stand out or feel rooted in a specific place. This could’ve been shot anywhere and it wouldn’t have made a difference.    What’s really sad is that you can sense there was a message behind it all: some social critique, an attempt to show that the lowest-ranking officers are often the ones closest to the people. But it’s so poorly executed that it doesn’t move, doesn’t hit, doesn’t leave any mark. It’s a missed opportunity—a sloppy mix of genres and references that steps all over itself.    In short: there are a couple of moments that might make you smile, but this is a messy, aimless, and boring film. One of those comedies that simply isn’t funny, that promises thrills but delivers apathy. If Netflix is going to keep betting on international productions, they better back stronger ones. Because this one... this one’s a mess.

cast_names: Jandino Asporaat, Werner Kolf, Mark Rietman, Florence Vos Weeda, Juliette van Ardenne, Ferdi Stofmeel, Teun Kuilboer, Victoria Koblenko, Romana Vrede, Yannick Jozefzoon

cast_names: Jandino Asporaat, Werner Kolf, Mark Rietman, Florence Vos Weeda, Juliette van Ardenne, Ferdi Stofmeel, Teun Kuilboer, Victoria Koblenko, Romana Vrede, Yannick Jozefzoon
review_content: A comedy for the screen, a tragedy for public trust. BOAs need reform — not a movie deal.    This film is nothing more than a PR stunt to make the Dutch BOA system look good. BOAs (Special Enforcement Officers) were never meant to assist police — they were created to replace them because they’re cheaper and send fine money straight to local governments. They can’t use force, can’t make arrests, and mostly hand out petty fines for things like cycling in city centers or parking errors.    Almost Cops tries to paint BOAs as lovable underdogs, but it completely ignores the truth: BOAs are powerless, widely disrespected, and symbolize a broken system focused on revenue, not safety. Dressing that up in comedy doesn’t fix the problem.

review_content: Tin Soldier (2025) 6/10  Director: Brad Furman  Screenplay: Brad Furman, Jess Fuerst  Cast: Jamie Foxx, Robert De Niro, Scott Eastwood, John Leguizamo    John Leguizamo was good to see. The flashbacks are effective, but it’s hard to tell which are real and which are dream fragments—feels like trauma recollections.    I didn’t fully get how he first ends up inside the group—probably “lost people drawn to a savior.”    It’s also hard to understand why the FBI pushes so hard after them and finally catches them.    The return-to-places memories hit emotionally. The river explosion at the end confused me—who set it off and why? Why was Evoli there and not escaping, especially while pregnant? It seems like a religious-style sect, so she obeys the leader and the community.    Bokushi’s death felt odd to me; he barely seems to fight it—probably a martyr pose.    Overall, not my favorite. review_content: Sketch has been a real surprise and, to be honest, a real discovery. The film comes from the Ángel Community, and if you are not familiar with this collective, it is worth paying attention to: an interesting initiative for those who want to try something different.    The first thing that stands out is how original the proposal is. At a time when cinema repeats formulas ad nauseam, it's refreshing to find something fresh, very coherent and with an attention to detail that is evident in every shot. Visually and in terms of atmosphere, it is very reminiscent of those dark fantasy films of the 90s, but brought into the 21st century, with a modern tone and a much more mature background.    The child actors who carry the weight of the plot are excellent. Their naturalness lends credibility to a story that is not simple, and which in its final act becomes as exciting as it is moving. In addition, there is an interesting subtext about mental health and the way we deal with pain, treated with sensitivity and without resorting to artifice.    Sketch is an original, well-made and surprisingly polished film that deserves to be discovered. A little gem that proves that it is still possible to tell different stories with heart.

review_content: Sketch (2025) – Angel Studios’ Monster Manual for Mourning 	•	Studio baggage – Let’s not sugarcoat it: seeing Angel Studios flash across the screen triggers a reflexive cringe in many of us. This is the same outfit responsible for pious propaganda masquerading as cinema, delivering their “values” like Sunday School with a megaphone. So when Sketch opened with the usual sentiment-heavy tone—grieving children, somber narration, and the promise of moral revelation—it was tempting to flee the theater like it was an exorcism in progress. 	•	Tone shift: grief meets Goosebumps – And yet…something happened. Sketch took a hard left turn from mawkish into mischievous. The premise—children’s grief-made-manifest through drawings that spring to life—starts as emotional therapy and ends up veering into spooky fun. These aren’t just monsters under the bed; they’re monsters from within the bed, drawn with crayons and animated with enough charm to keep even a seasoned skeptic engaged. 	•	Narrative arc – At its core, this is a grief story wrapped in a children’s adventure. The climax builds to a father-son catharsis we’ve been emotionally baited for since act one, delivered in the genre’s familiar whisper-shout of absolution. Think Robin Williams’ “It’s not your fault” (Good Will Hunting, for those keeping score) or the unspoken ache at the end of Call Me By Your Name. Here it’s clean, expected, but earnestly handled. 	•	Gimmicks and apps – Because it’s still Angel Studios, there is a QR code moment. Kids are encouraged to scan it, download the app, and animate their own monsters at home—part gamified grief toolkit, part marketing ploy. It walks the line between well-meaning and opportunistic, but the tech tie-in at least stays on theme with the film’s idea: express your pain, don’t suppress it. 	•	Moral delivery – Yes, the moral is still there—grief is okay, and art can help—but it’s delivered with less sermonizing than usual. If Angel Studios is evolving into the 2020s version of The Value Tales (those Time-Life kids’ books that taught honesty, courage, and empathy through whimsical biography), this is one of their less sanctimonious chapters.  ⸻  Final verdict: 3 out of 5 stars Sketch won’t change cinema, and it won’t make Angel Studios palatable to everyone. But for 90 minutes, it sets aside the fire and brimstone to tell a gentle, quirky fable about how children process loss. It’s no Pixar—but it’s not Sound of Freedom either. Small mercies.  ⸻  Crafted with Hudson—an AI collaborator for creativity, innovation, and human potential. Explore what’s possible at OpenAI.

review_content: I would be lying if I said I decided to watch this film for any reason other than Alexander Skarsgård, but I ended up staying for a very sensitive commentary on self-discovery, exploration, communication, autonomy and limits.  As someone who isn't entirely familiar with the BDSM subculture, the little I know is that communication and consent are pillars to their dynamics, as well as boundaries. And this is what you will see here: A character (Colin) who has lived a life in the shadow of his parents, with no experience or experimentation that resulted from his own searching, but ends up not only discovering autonomy but learning the importance of verbalizing his needs and asserting his limits. In contrast, we have another character (Ray) who has lived a life in BDSM dynamics, doesn't seem to be interested in a sentimental relationship within the dom × sub configuration and flees the minute he finds himself infatuated by the very person he subjugated (in the BDSM notion) as his "slave", threatened by his newfound agency and lack of control.  Pillion cuts deep when we are shown that love isn't the purpose of everything, as Colin believed. We are reminded that romantic and sexual dynamics are different for everyone and left disappointed in not seeing the love story we were conditioned to fantasize about not materialize.  Aside from a few abrupt cuts that left to be desired about their story as a "couple" and a somewhat uninteresting soundtrack, this was genuinely a very interesting watch to say the least.

cast_names: Kang Ha-neul, Yeom Hye-ran, Seo Hyun-woo, Jeon Jin-oh, Park Seong-il, Yoon Jeong-il, Lee Jong-gu, Kang Ae-sim, Jo Han-joon, Ryu Sung-hyun
review_content: "Imagine finally achieving your dream—buying your forever apartment… only to unlock a door full of nightmares instead!"    Seung is a man who finally manages to buy the apartment of his dreams.  But instead of a fresh start, it turns into a complete nightmare—crushing loans, unbearable neighbors, and strange, disturbing noises.  Soon, he realizes there’s a terrifying secret hidden within those walls.    The direction was brilliant, especially in how the character was portrayed and how the camera captured his psychological journey—it really pulled you in.    Acting was top-notch across the board, especially from the lead and the antagonist.    The film really messes with your mind. You don’t know who to sympathize with, and the best part? You feel the pain and pressure right alongside the main character.    (Well… I didn’t personally relate to the pain—thankfully, my neighbors are great 😅)    Also, the soundtrack was excellent and matched the film’s tone perfectly.    My rating: 7/10

cast_names: Kang Ha-neul, Yeom Hye-ran, Seo Hyun-woo, Jeon Jin-oh, Park Seong-il, Yoon Jeong-il, Lee Jong-gu, Kang Ae-sim, Jo Han-joon, Ryu Sung-hyun
review_content: I wasn’t expecting much, and yet it hooked me from start to finish. It has that edge of madness I love, where you’re never sure if you’re watching dark satire, psychological thriller, or a sick comedy disguised as social drama. Twisted? Yes. But incredibly entertaining. It’s not subtle or elegant, but it’s not trying to be.    The movie kicks off strong: a man trapped in his apartment, his mortgage, his life. Constant noise, financial pressure, increasingly bizarre neighbors... everything adds to a suffocating paranoia. And the best part is you never quite know if it’s real or all in his head. Kang Ha-neul delivers a solid performance, and you stay with him even when his choices get absurd.    At times it reminded me of Parasite, though without its precision or depth. Here, everything is raw and direct. But the social critique is clear: the housing trap, urban isolation, and the middle-class illusion. And despite its over-the-top moments, it never loses momentum.    It’s not perfect. The final stretch goes off the rails, maybe too much. But that’s part of its charm. It doesn’t hit as hard emotionally as other Korean thrillers, but it leaves a weird aftertaste—and I like that.    I give it a 7/10. Not because it’s flawless, but because it dares. And these days, that’s worth something.
