cast_names: Kang Ha-neul, Yeom Hye-ran, Seo Hyun-woo, Jeon Jin-oh, Park Seong-il, Yoon Jeong-il, Lee Jong-gu, Kang Ae-sim, Jo Han-joon, Ryu Sung-hyun
review_content: In “Wall to Wall,” Kim Tae-joon builds an urban nightmare that starts off grounded in reality but ends up tripping over its own ambitions. The movie kicks off with a claustrophobic and disturbing premise: an ordinary man, Woo-sung (Kang Ha-neul), goes through a physical and mental breakdown inside his own apartment—a space that should symbolize safety and achievement but instead becomes a stage for oppression, paranoia, and noise. Tae-joon’s direction works really well in the beginning: the suffocating atmosphere, the relentless sound of knocking on the walls, and the neighbors’ quiet hostility create a very tangible kind of suspense, all rooted in a world anyone could live in. Ha-neul gives us a deeply human protagonist, torn between guilt, rage, and despair, in a performance that balances physical exhaustion, emotional collapse, and subtle moments of humor.    What makes the first half of “Wall to Wall” so gripping is how precisely and intensely it lays out Woo-sung’s everyday horror. His frustration with bureaucracy, the silent judgment from neighbors, and the helplessness in the face of a system that just doesn’t care all build a choking atmosphere. The social critique is sharp: the film touches on the housing market crash, the breakdown of community, and the gap between landlords and tenants—without ever feeling preachy. There’s even a nightmare within the nightmare, with a dream sequence that symbolically represents Woo-sung’s total psychological meltdown. And when he finally hits his breaking point—during a tense police station scene that mixes desperation and irony—the movie feels ready to explode. The problem is, instead of exploding, it starts to fall apart.    Trying to keep the energy up and throw in surprises, “Wall to Wall” shifts into a twist-filled mode that, while interesting in theory, ends up watering down everything it built so well. The sudden dive into a tech-based conspiracy—complete with surveillance, manipulation, and a shady web of hidden interests—feels more like an escape into exaggeration than a natural continuation of the story. Tae-joon clearly wants to raise the stakes, but the result is a messy mix of ideas and themes fighting for attention. The social commentary takes a back seat to a string of plot twists that don’t really land, mostly because the movie doesn’t give us time to digest them. It spirals into a sequence of reveals that feel like they belong in a different script, with a completely different tone. You end up thinking the movie either should’ve ended halfway through—or split itself into two totally separate parts.    Visually, “Wall to Wall” stays consistent and effective, even when the script starts to stumble. The cinematography really sells the sense of confinement and paranoia, and the score helps keep the tension dialed up throughout. The cast is another strong point: besides Kang Ha-neul, Yeom Hye-ran gives a pitch-perfect performance as the mysterious Eun-hwa, playing that kind of morally ambiguous authority figure who clearly has something to hide. But even strong acting can’t hold up the movie’s overload of themes. By the end, it feels like two different films were stitched together in a rush: one about urban isolation and invisible noise; the other about digital conspiracies and systemic control. Sadly, neither really gets a satisfying ending. “Wall to Wall” had everything it needed to be a hard-hitting psychological thriller—but it loses itself trying to pack in more than its walls can contain.

review_content: I didn’t think I would find something worse than The Bride, but here we are.   This movie had potential to be something great. However, the further along you get into the story, the more disappointed you become as you realize the ball has been dropped as it tries to step away from what they call a traditional “happy ending”.  My expectations going into the movie wasn’t all that high and I’ll admit the reason I decided to watch it was for Topher Grace, but I did enjoy watching most of it. That said, I cannot recommend this movie.

cast_names: Glen Powell, Margaret Qualley, Jessica Henwick, Ed Harris, Bill Camp, Topher Grace, Zach Woods, Bianca Amato, Raff Law, Alexander Hanson
review_content: Glen Powell Just About Saves a Middling Murder Romp.  I enjoyed this breezy black comedy but it doesn't really commit enough to be as compelling or dark as it could be.  Glen Powell carries pretty much the whole thing with an effortless charm; the guy is an absolute star. Margaret Qualley is the other standout as a nicely sly and alluring antagonist.  The film doesn't really flesh out the family members /victims beyond broad (if sometimes amusing) caricatures-each one is a quick sketch of greed, vapidity or entitlement. Some of the kills also feel quite rushed and glossed over too. Similarly Ed Harris's patriarch seems underwritten and never provides an explanation for the treatment of his daughter. This felt frustrating and like a missed opportunity.  The filmmaking is glossy and competent rather than being exceptional. Nice clean visuals, steady pacing, solid dark humour- just a bit safe and unadventurous, though the ending is nicely unexpected and eschews the usual neat hollywood closure. It otherwise coasts on Powell and the premise.  HTMAK is entertaining but ultimately forgettable. A solid update that promises more edge than it delivers.

review_content: I wanted to like this, I really did, so I'm disappointed that I felt nonplussed throughout and left feeling underwhelmed and frankly a little bewildered.    I'd seen the trailer numerous times before other films over the past few months; I like to go into films as blind as possible but seeing trailers in the cinema isn't easy to avoid, but the trailer was the extent of my preconceptions. I thought it might be a wry and slightly bombastic film.    Sadly that wasn't how I found it to be. And really, I'm not sure what I felt like it was, it seemed rather tonally inconsistent? Visually, there's at least nice lighting/shadows which feels uncommon nowadays, but the colour grading just felt cold and lifeless. Which yes, I appreciate that "cold and lifeless" might feel thematically relevant, but like I said, I found the film to be tonally inconsistent. Was it trying to be funny? Edgy? Dark? Thrilling? Heartfelt and with a message? Films can of course be more than one of those things, but this just felt like it wasn't really committing to any of them. What stood out the most to me was that it just felt like it was lacking a spark.    [spoiler]Framing the structure around the confession to the death row chaplain didn't really work for me, nor the rapid twists in the final act where Becket reveals that he was expecting Julia to save him with Lyle's note in exchange for Becket's inherited fortune, but time is running short so while he wasn't nervous at the start of confessing his story, he is now. But then he's saved with minutes to go before his scheduled execution. Then Ruth ditches him in the prison parking lot, but Julia's waiting there too so off he goes with Julia. Huh? Julia had blackmailed him with photos placing him with his relatives right before their deaths, her testimony over her husband's death got Becket handed a death sentence, and then she toyed with him in the prison meeting booth and blackmailed him again. While she may have come across as a sympathetic character or at the very least neutral early on, she's been increasingly clearly an absolutely horrible person, "coal wearing makeup", and I'm fairly sure we were initially supposed to assume that she'd killed Lyle? Even after the note I'm still not sure if she did or not, but I suppose she was in a salon or whatever so maybe not? But what are we supposed to assume, that Becket ends up living with the manipulative Julia who now controls the fortune he'd spent the plot trying to secure, with the photos linking him to several murders just constantly hanging over him as a threat that she can ruin him at any moment? I just don't see them living happily ever after, obviously. And with the way the film hurried through so many of the murders, introducing and then killing off some of them in what felt like less than a minute, with very little time allowed for the audience to actually take in the ever-updating family tree, it was a far cry from something like Kill Bill where the list is clear and the victims largely have time to be characterised. [/spoiler]    [spoiler] Were we supposed to feel sorry for Becket? Were we supposed to feel sorry for any of his victims? After his first murder, leading to him then getting nepo-hired by his uncle, it's made clear that he could have just stopped there and lived a comfortable life, if not for his mother's dying words haunting him in a frankly incredibly light manner, it's not like he wakes with a start in the night to her words compelling him to keep killing off his relatives, he just looks at the lock of her hair in a locket several times... [/spoiler] I don't know... the film just didn't work for me, there were a few giggles, [spoiler]one quick twist, a little bit of brief suspense,[/spoiler] but on the whole it just lacked the juice. Especially disappointing given the potential of the cast, this could have been really good but instead I just found it to be meh.

review_content: AMERICANA IS A NEW MOVIE. GRADE: B-. (STARZ).  #EricDane: now accepting bit parts before it's too late.  Several months ago, the actor told the world he had been diagnosed with ALS. Since then, I have seen him in a number of new acting rolls. His contributes to the new film currently streaming on #STARZ called #Americana. Solid actor #PaulWalterHouser (playing old #LeftyLedbetter) as well as frequent co-star #SydneySweeney (she has good jeans) help carry this movie which is billed as a "Modern Western Crime Thriller". That description alone had me sampling it. It incorporates several genres and serves to be a rather entertaining New Year's offering. Parts of it are rather condescending, but for a chilly January experience, it heats up real nice! #streamingbanter.

review_content: Americana is one of those movies I probably liked more than I can logically justify—and I’m okay with that. It’s a tough sell on paper: a multi-perspective narrative (which turns people off), an anxiety-inducing tone (which turns people off), and a Western backdrop (which definitely turns people off). And yet, it clicked for me. The shifting viewpoints kept things engaging, the tension was effective rather than exhausting, and the Western elements gave it a texture that felt fresh instead of dusty.  I genuinely had fun with it, even if I’m not sure who I’d confidently recommend it to. It’s niche, it’s uncomfortable by design, and it won’t be for most people—but it worked for me, and I already want to revisit it.  Rating: 3/5 – 75% – Worth Watching

review_content: Score: 3.75* / 5* or 7.5 / 10    I give it a very controversial 7.5. The things I liked, I really liked, but the things I found absurd were VERY absurd. Something worth mentioning is that, even though it may not seem like it, I think the film has good attention to detail. I think the reasons for many things can be explained simply by paying attention.    From here on down, proceed with caution, as I'm talking directly about scenes (Spoilers). Read only if you don't mind or have already seen the film.    I liked the world-building. That it's an apocalyptic world due to climate change, and that this whole "zombie" virus started because of melting ice and eating fish that were infected with something. Viruses and diseases preserved in glaciers are a real problem, and I found the fact that they used this as a starting point interesting. Also, the fact that the food was basically insects; that was the world's new protein.    I like that they show the chaos of the zombie pandemic. Sometimes they simply present a world of deserted cities or, on the contrary, full of zombies, but they don't always show you the process of how society collapses, which I always find interesting and I appreciate that they show.    I like that it takes place in a hospital and that there are all these different hospital zombies, like the one in a wheelchair, the ones hanging from therapy machines, zombie children in the children's area, the pregnant zombie whose water breaks, the zombie who eats babies... I think these are things that aren't usually shown in zombie movies because of the "crudeness" or sensitivity of the subject, but the truth is that these people exist, right? It's not like children cease to exist because of a zombie pandemic; there have to be zombie children somewhere, as well as zombies in wheelchairs, on machines, etc., etc. Since it's a "save yourself if you can" situation, those people who literally can't save themselves are free food for the zombies.    Another thing I liked was that the fact that the water caused those infected to enter some sort of "berserker" mode struck me as brilliant. Do those infected by a contaminated fish become worse upon contact with water? It's such an absurd thing that it seems brilliant to me. In fact, if you look closely at that point in the film, the infected looked more and more like the fish that infected them, with a large jaw and rows of sharp teeth.    Something I don't like. Well, let's see, I'm no zombie expert, but it seems to me that fighting them bare-knuckle isn't the most brilliant idea in the world. I know it's literally part of the movie's premise, but I think it's very funny. XD    I also didn't like the effects at times. I don't want to tear them apart based on that because, well, not all movies can be billion-dollar superproductions, right? But there were several moments where the effects were laughable.    The ending has two things I didn't like. The first thing I didn't like was that the protagonist survived all of that in a period of what? 30 minutes? A gunshot, a fragmentation grenade so close, AND EVEN THE C4 FROM THE HOSPITAL! Nah, bro, that's too much. Who the hell are you, Terminator? The second thing I didn't like was that at the end, when he reaches the roof, he's lying there on the floor leaning against the door while the helicopter is still there. So I'm like, "Oh, well, he must be really tired and can't take it anymore." The dude gets up and starts fighting the zombies again. ????? Dude, if you have the energy to dish out those punches you were dishing out at the end, you had the energy to make one last run for the helicopter, right?    Something that's neither good nor bad—it's up to everyone to decide how they take it—is the fact that everything is left open for a "Ziam 2." Beyond the fact that the protagonist remains alive, let's remember that he and his co-worker share a fish, a fish that we know was infected because in one scene they show a shot of the protagonist's house and you can see how his head moves, so it is correct to think that if his former co-worker ate his share of the fish there could be another source of infection.

cast_names: Prin Suparat, Johnny Anfone, Pimmada Boriruksuppakorn, Keerati Sivakua, Namo Tongkumnerd, Aroon Wanasbodeewong, Sornchai Chatwiriyachai, Kanrat Kaljareuk, Jirakan Kaljaruek, Panjai Sirisuwan
review_content: Sometimes you really have to wonder how certain movies even get released. Ziam is one of those cases. As a fan of the zombie genre, I always go in with some excitement. But this time, not even good intentions could save it. What was meant to be a tense, somewhat original apocalyptic thriller ends up as a complete disaster.    The script is beyond salvageable. It’s full of clichés, useless dialogue, characters that appear and vanish without explanation, and scenes that seem randomly stitched together. Whatever story might have existed is buried under sloppy, careless execution. And to make it worse, the actors don’t help at all — performances range from flat to overacted to just plain bad.    Visually, there’s little to praise. The zombie design is... interesting, maybe, but that’s all: contortions and screams. The hospital setting, which should create claustrophobia and tension, feels slow and repetitive. Some moments are so ridiculous they verge on parody — but without any irony or wit.    I give it a 2 just because it’s a zombie film, and that alone still sparks some interest in me. But even for this genre, which often embraces excess and absurdity, Ziam falls embarrassingly short.    In short, Ziam might be one of the worst zombie films I’ve ever seen. Terrible script, bad acting, and a story with no direction or tension. Not even recommended for genre lovers. Only the theme itself is worth the slightest bit of credit.

cast_names: Finn Wolfhard, Billy Bryk, Fred Hechinger, Abby Quinn, Krista Nazaire, Daniel Gravelle, Julia Lalonde, Matthew Finlan, D'Pharaoh Woon-A-Tai, Julia Doyle
review_content: The movie is painfully average. It doesn’t work as a comedy, isn’t absurd enough to qualify as dark comedy, and definitely isn’t scary enough to be horror. The murders have no real motivation, and the film seriously tries to shame a 24-year-old protagonist for still working at a summer camp for low pay. It’s not like he’s 50 — and even if he were, the attempt to joke about it just falls flat.    If this is the direction new films in these vague, mixed genres are going, I’m honestly not interested in watching them anymore.    I was hoping the cast would at least make it watchable — and to be fair, they do. The film isn’t terrible. It’s just empty. Killings for the sake of killings, genre elements that don’t reach any meaningful level, just existing without purpose.    And as for dark comedy — come on. Tucker & Dale vs. Evil was way more fun and at least had some soul. The rating is average, just like the film itself.

review_content: I really liked it because it’s one of those films that grabs you with something very simple (a situation at the limit) and, without making a big show of it, pulls you into a pretty dark place. It starts like a survival thriller, but what really tightens the screws is the emotional side.    The film plays very well with the idea of guilt: that sticky feeling of “if only I’d done this” or “if only I hadn’t said that.” And what’s most unsettling is how your mind can twist everything until you don’t know whether you’re watching facts, fear, or a mix of both.    The pacing worked for me. It doesn’t linger, it goes straight to the point, and it uses its time to keep tightening the tension, with scenes that feel small but leave you uneasy. It’s not a movie that shows off with twists; it’s more about keeping your stomach clenched.    Belén Cuesta carries the story with real honesty. She’s restrained, but you can feel all the damage underneath, and that makes you believe her even when the film gets more extreme. The rest of the cast supports well without stealing the spotlight, because what matters here is her pulse.    In terms of staging, the wildfire isn’t just “spectacle”: it works like constant pressure, like a ticking clock, like a physical and mental threat at the same time. There are really strong moments in how it blends what’s real with what the character projects.    Overall, it’s a very entertaining thriller, but with a pretty sad and human core. It leaves you thinking about how easily the mind can lie to you when you’re broken inside, and how quickly everything can end badly.

cast_names: Madelaine Petsch, Jacob Scipio, Katy O'Brian, Madison Bailey, Matteo Lane, Jim Gaffigan, Naomi J. Ogawa, Inanna Sarkis, Kerim Hassan, Rob Rausch
review_content: Let’s be honest… there are bad movies, and then there’s Maintenance Required. It’s not funny, it’s not moving, and it disappears from your mind before you even have time to get annoyed. It promised to be different, but nope. Same old story. Flat characters, recycled lines, elevator music… everything feels the same. Ten minutes in and you’re already checking your phone — or thinking about dinner.    Madelaine Petsch and Jacob Scipio try, they really do, but there’s just no way to save it. No soul, no rhythm, no spark. Nothing. It feels like a rom-com made with a template — the kind someone writes on autopilot: “throw in two attractive leads, a misunderstanding, a kiss at the end, roll credits.” And that’s exactly what it is.    Lacey Uhlemeyer’s direction doesn’t help either. No tone, no charm, no energy. The jokes don’t land, the romantic bits fall flat, and when the film tries to be serious, it’s just awkward. Everything looks rushed, careless — like someone said, “whatever, it’s done, just upload it.”    The only thing worth saving — and by far — is the song This Is the Day by The The. It plays twice, and for a moment you feel like you’re watching another movie. It’s got soul, it’s got life. Which makes it hurt even more, because it reminds you of what this story never manages to have.    And in the end… nothing. You’re the same as when you started, just more tired. Maybe even emptier. It leaves nothing, teaches nothing, provokes nothing. Some movies should just stay locked away and be forgotten. Honestly, this one should’ve never left the drawer.

I still decided to give it a shot this week and I figured Joe would appreciate it because he likes romantic movies and cars. I guess I’ll find out later if he liked it or not.  I mainly picked this because I like Madelaine Petsch. She’s absolutely stunning and honestly still looked great playing a mechanic. I’ve never been much of a car person myself but I’ve been having my fair share of car issues lately so I guess I watched this at the right time. I was into the first half of this for the most part but it dragged too much. The romance was lacking and kind of dull. I mean it had its moments but didn’t really hook me. It’s not very original or all that exciting. It never really brings anything new to the romantic comedy genre. I mean it’s fine enough but nothing that I’d ever be interested in rewatching again.  Side note: Be prepared for Madeline Petsch not wearing a bra half the movie. It really stood out and was quite the distraction. 👀  * Movie swapping with Joe

review_content: Stuckmann clearly has an eye for horror, and there are some genuinely standout moments in the opening act of Shelby Oaks that prove he’s more than just a YouTube film critic. The window cracking in the dead of night is one of my favourite scares in recent memory, it's simple, plays with your expectations and is more spine-tingling than any loud bang would have been. The reflection of the hellhounds eyes in the woods is also so simple yet very effective.     Chris is ultimately let down by a narrative that never quite meets the strength of those early moments. There’s an overriding sense of “is that it?” once the mystery is revealed, and that disappointment only grows as you realise that really is all Shelby Oaks has left to offer.    A decent debut, but nothing special. A great foundation to build from.

review_content: If you’ve ever watched a survival-at-sea movie and immediately started placing bets on who makes it out alive — and what desperate MacGyver-style stunt they’ll pull to do it — Beast of War delivers exactly that kind of salty suspense. The opening basic-training scenes with the grunts are unexpectedly fun, setting up the camaraderie before everything goes sideways, and I was especially glad to discover Mark Coles Smith, who brings plenty of charisma and quiet toughness to the chaos. The premise isn’t exactly reinventing the life raft, but the film knows what it is and rides the wave anyway, mixing tension with just enough swagger to keep things entertaining. Predictable? Sure. Enjoyable? Also yes — sometimes all you really need is open water, bad odds, and someone yelling survival ideas that definitely weren’t covered in training.

review_content: Beast of War is a strange mashup that never fully clicks for me. I’m always down for creature features and I have a soft spot for shark movies—even the bad ones—but the WW2 backdrop does very little to elevate the concept here. The pacing feels sluggish, and the movie spends too much time trudging through setup when it should be leaning harder into the absurdity or tension that makes this genre fun.  That said, it’s far from the bottom of the barrel. In the grand hierarchy of shark-related cinema, this clears the truly unwatchable stuff (Sharknado and its ilk still exist, after all). It’s a passable watch in the moment, but not one I expect to remember for very long once the credits roll.  Rating: 2/5 – 65% – Not Recommended to Everyone

review_content: The Penguin Lessons is a heartwarming and charming story, even if it’s not quite my usual style. The film delivers genuine emotion and a feel-good narrative that will resonate strongly with the right audience. While it’s easy to appreciate the craftsmanship and the story’s appeal, it’s not something I see myself revisiting often.  Rating: 3/5 – 75% – Worth Watching

cast_names: Steve Coogan, Jonathan Pryce, Vivian El Jaber, Björn Gustafsson, Micaela Breque, Tomás Pozzi, Ramiro Blas, Juan Barreiro, Aimar Miranda, Florencia Nocetti
review_content: "Penguins, Politics, and Emotional Potholes"  Steve Coogan plays a comically reprehensible and emotionally vacant man drifting through life. He lands in Argentina in 1976—just as the country edges into a military coup—and finds himself teaching English at a boys' school. Circumstances, both absurd and touching, lead him to reluctantly befriend a penguin.    This unlikely relationship sparks a journey of personal growth and rediscovery. Or at least, that’s the idea.    Coogan’s performance is both heartwarming and jarring, and I’m not sure if that tonal contradiction was intentional on director Peter Cattaneo’s part or just the result of tonal whiplash. I wanted to like his character. I tried to root for him. But for some reason, I couldn’t quite follow the arc of his redemption. While others in the film gradually warm to him, I remained stuck—still staring into the void of his character’s emptiness.    Then there’s the setting. Placing a penguin-centric personal redemption story in the middle of Argentina’s 1976 coup feels... off. Historically accurate, yes. But I couldn’t shake the sense that the filmmakers were using the backdrop of real political trauma as a kind of moral seasoning—maybe even a veiled commentary on today’s geopolitical climate. Not inherently wrong, but the juxtaposition of soft-and-fuzzy personal growth with state-sponsored terror left me disoriented. In the end, I think the film's warm intentions got diluted by its ambitions.

cast_names: Steve Coogan, Jonathan Pryce, Vivian El Jaber, Björn Gustafsson, Micaela Breque, Tomás Pozzi, Ramiro Blas, Juan Barreiro, Aimar Miranda, Florencia Nocetti
review_content: 'The Penguin Lessons' is hearty viewing. Steve Coogan plus penguin is a recipe for success so the films delivers in that respect, I think any movie with a cute little flipper is going to be impossible to dislike. Everything with the penguin is sweet, much with Coogan is funny.    It's a really interesting plot, one that is based upon a rather incredible true event. The backdrop of 1970s Argentina adds a fresh edge to it all, so it's not simply a flick that relies on its cute animal. As serious as the flick gets (still reeling from that gut punch tbh), it is amusing stuff too.    Coogan is a smart choice of lead, although I could also weirdly visualise Rowan Atkinson and someone else I've since forgotten (ffs) in the role too. Obviously I know who Coogan is and do like him, but aside from 'Night at the Museum' I always forget about him - not here.    Vivian El Jaber brings a praiseworthy performance as well, likewise Alfonsina Carrocio. Jonathan Pryce is terrific casting as the headmaster, that first scene with him is great. movie_title: In the Blink of an Eye
cast_names: Kate McKinnon, Rashida Jones, Daveed Diggs, Jorge Vargas, Tanaya Beatty, Skywalker Hughes, Rhona Rees, Tatyana Rose Baptiste, Nox Watkins, Tyson Night
review_content: In the Blink of an Eye has a really cool idea at its core, and that concept is easily the film’s biggest strength. You can tell the movie wants to be something more ambitious than the average thriller — which isn’t a bad thing — but that ambition also makes it easier to critique when the execution doesn’t fully land.  Despite a relatively short 94-minute runtime, it oddly feels slower than it should. The pacing drags in spots where tightening things up would have helped considerably. Shaving about ten minutes of fluff somewhere in the middle likely would have made the overall experience much sharper.  There’s still an engaging concept here and enough intrigue to keep things watchable, but it ultimately lands in that “good idea, middling results” category.  Rating: 2.5/5 – 70% – Worth Watching

review_content: “The Blink of an Eye” only works if you truly engage with it. Watch it casually and you might miss its strengths – watch it carefully and it reveals a finely crafted play with perception, time and perspective.    Not made for passive viewing.  But rewarding for those willing to pay attention.

review_content: Two areas that people will care to know about - is it a fair biopic and is Sydney Sweeney good in it?  Firstly as a biopic, it treads the familiar waters. Rise, pinnacle, adversity, comeback, happy ending text. However, it makes one major error that undermines a lot fo the film. Like a race car driver who doesn't wait for the apex before flooring the pedal, it suffers. In particular I'm referring to the relationship with Jim... There's no path. It isn't fleshed out enough to make sense to the viewer and the rest of the film sits on the shoulders of that relationship. More effort should have been made in that first 30 minutes. And some fat could have been cut out of the next 45 to make room for it.  Sydney Sweeney does well with what she has but not great. She shows decent range in her acting with situations that are everywhere from heaven to hell. She's not winning an Oscar for this unless it's for publicity reasons.

review_content: 2025 really did feel like Sydney Sweeney’s year, didn’t it? Christy stands out not just as a strong biopic, but as a clear showcase of her range as an actress. She brings a grounded intensity to the role that makes the character feel fully lived in rather than performed, which is especially impressive in a genre that can easily slip into imitation.    What I appreciated most is how restrained and emotionally honest her performance is. She does not rely on flashy moments. Instead, she lets the quieter scenes do the work, which makes the character’s struggles and growth feel authentic. For me, this kind of dramatic, character driven role suits her far more than the lighter romance or comedy parts she is often associated with. Christy feels like a turning point, less about star power and more about craft, and it makes a strong case for where her career could be headed next.    The real Christy who's story this movie is based on --> https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Christy_Martin

review_content: I came into this with no expectations because I have not seen any other indonesian movies that I know of. Being that it is a netflix movie the budget is good and the movie is shot beautifully, but what holds it back is the writing. In most zombie movies you'll see the characters struggle in the beginning as the panic sets in about the people around them trying to eat them, but then they learn to survive and those two parts are usually split in two.  In this movie the dynamic is the same but the learning to survive part comes soo late, like normally when a character killsba zombie for the first time they then learn to do it again and again, but these characters killed our saw a zombie get killed and yet they remain as incompetent as ever until near the end of the movie when they start to get more kills per scene and yet they remain complete idiots doing the dumbest stuff I've seen in any movie, the horror part of this movie refers to how frustrating it is to the point that I wanted to go and eat my whole family.  It is just a complete mess at times with no sign of coherence. I feel like I wasted my time watching this. I don't recommend it.

review_content: For those who are not familiar to Indonesia's film industry, the name Kimo Stamboel was not a familiar name. For those who are familiar, you might know him by other name, the half of The Mo Brother. Until 2016, Kimo Stamboel and Timo Tjahjanto have been directing films together, the last collaboration was Headshot (2016) starred Iko Uwais.  In 2025, both Timo and Kimo directed films in different genre. Timo directed Nobody 2 and Kimo directed this film, The Elixir, otherwise known as Abadi Nan Jaya (translated into English as Victorious Forever).  The Elixir was a zombie film, the Mo Brother's way. It's humorous, always using reference of current trend, very casual language, and more than needed action scenes.  The incident happened in a small village, where a traditional medicine factory was located. Their new innovation was called Abadi Nan Jaya, some kind of traditional approach to forever young concoction. Abadi Nan Jaya worked. The owner who injected himself with Abadi Nan Jaya regained aome of his youth, his hair became datk again, his eyes became clearer, his skin got younger, but there was an unexpected side effect. Abadi Nan Jaya made the owner to become the first zombie.  And all hell broke loose, chaos and desperation be ame the maim focus with drops humor here and there.  Is it good? It's just okay quality-wise. But The Elixir was well made.

cast_names: Will Arnett, Laura Dern, Andra Day, Bradley Cooper, Christine Ebersole, Ciarán Hinds, Amy Sedaris, Sean Hayes, Scott Icenogle, Calvin Knegten
review_content: Foremost, this movie addresses itself to people who have lived through a divorce or are close to it. They will best get the essence of it. The movie does have some good, strong scenes, namely the discussion between Dern and Arnette towards the end of the movie (around 1:36:00) that certainly is a discussion that many couples should have before it is too late. For the rest, I personally feel that there are way too many pointless scenes in the movie. I also find that the ending is too much geared towards couples who still have feelings for each other and had just not grown up in their relationship. For most couples that I know who divorced, that is not the case. It is a Hollywood-nice ending, but not really speaking to me (though good for them I guess ;-).

review_content: Is this thing on? Ain’t a bad movie whatsoever but it just isn’t my type of film, I thought going into it was going to focus more on the comedy but it’s mostly about Alex and Tess’s marriage and while I do like aspects and respect what they was trying to do I didn’t find it interesting.  For me the comedy style was very Dry and if you like that you will definitely love this movie, there was a few times that made me laugh still though.  I did struggle getting invested in the story as there were some pretty slow parts and with the long runtime aswell it made it harder for me to enjoy.  Will Arnett does a great job as Alex I liked him doing stand up nights even if they were awkward to watch at times, Laura Dern I already know is a very good actress with her roles in the Jurassic park films and the fault in our stars she does well here also but it’s not one of my favourite roles from her.  And finally this thing just wasn’t on for me but like I’ve said it’s a decent movie and I get why other people would be a fan of it. 6/10.

cast_names: Will Arnett, Laura Dern, Andra Day, Bradley Cooper, Christine Ebersole, Ciarán Hinds, Amy Sedaris, Sean Hayes, Scott Icenogle, Calvin Knegten
review_content: Is This Thing On  ? isn’t for everyone, but for the right audience I think it works, sadly I’m not one of those people who enjoys these kind of things so I’m not gonna be too harsh on this movie. It’s still a solid film though, even for me as the story is generally very nice, the acting is amazing and it has a fair share of comedy, it’s just a little long in my opinion and definitely drags at times.   I did find it quite hard to get into at first as I just wasn’t interested in what was going on, and felt a little bored, some parts were also kinda cringy in my opinion. That being said, there were still a few somewhat entertaining moments  Although it’s a tough movie to get into, I did like the story of Alex going through a rough divorce with Tess and using Stand-Up Comedy as a way to express all of his frustrations of their marriage, I thought it was  a nice story but again, It’s a little too long and boring at times.  Will Arnett is great here, and it’s nice to see him in a different kind of role. The only other movie I have seen him in is Blades Of Glory so compared to that, it was pretty weird seeing him in a more serious film and not going after Will Ferrell with ice skates. Laura Dern was superb here as well.  Look I’m not gonna totally crap on this movie as I feel like if this is your type of thing, you’ll most probably love it but for me it’s very average. I’m gonna give Is This Thing On ? a 6 out of 10.  6/10

review_content: Was surprised when Bradley Cooper popped up early in the film and even more surprised when he popped up again as the director - not sure how I missed that in the promotion. I would have been even more excited for the film had I known. I've been a fan of all of his work and I'm happy to report that this was no exception.  A simple story boosted by clever writing that still felt real/grounded and an excellent central performance by Will Arnett. It's always impressive when a small scale dramedy can create a moment that lands like a set piece, and I would say the big moment where [spoiler]Alex unknowingly performs a stand-up set in front of his wife[/spoiler] accomplished that for me.  I also appreciated the optimistic conclusion.

review_content: Good documentary but I can't shake the feeling there's more to the story. The girl shows zero emotions talking about it all (same as her father crying without tears). Could it be that she actually wanted to leave with them? They talked about how much into religion she was and the two weirdos were all about faith. She could have been brainwashed and all she talks about can be a good cover up story. After all she is from a rich family that desperately needed to fix their reputation after both her dad and uncle were being suspected... The last thing they would need would be for her to come clean about something like that. So a good story would fix it all. I know I'm most probably too paranoid but all these true crime documentaries taught me to question everything.

cast_names: Elizabeth Smart, Steevan Glover, Brian David Mitchell, Wanda Barzee, Soledad O'Brien, Katie Couric
review_content: This is a well filmed doco with okay pacing, but structurally is a bit messy. There are moments where further context feels warranted, but was glazed over.    Music was overly dramatic and did not lend itself well to the true story. Along with that, there are obvious fake moments here that were not necessary, and makes me feel this is just another Netflix doco where they care more for entertainment than truth. More so than other studios that is. Unfortunately, a lot of the reenactments also felt off, could be that they had the real life people involved do it instead of acting doubles where appropriate, but I appreciate their willingness to do so for the filmmakers.    Some awkward audio sync errors or overdubbing in the beginning I noticed too. But yeah main gripes here are the push of narrative over facts, but that's usually any true crime documentary nowadays... especially on Netflix.    **6/10**

cast_names: Elizabeth Smart, Steevan Glover, Brian David Mitchell, Wanda Barzee, Soledad O'Brien, Katie Couric
review_content: A clear, restrained documentary that offers a chronological account of Elizabeth Smart’s kidnapping and captivity, anchored by her own deeply affecting testimony. It may not break new ground for those familiar with the case, but it thoughtfully examines family trauma, flawed police work, and the lasting psychological impact on survivors, all without sensationalism.

review_content: Adulthood (2025) is one of those easy watches that goes down smooth while you’re watching it, but doesn’t necessarily linger afterward. It pulls from some pretty rehashed plot ideas — themes we’ve seen explored many times before — and at moments it feels like it’s reaching to be more profound than it ultimately lands.  That said, it’s a decent movie. The performances are solid, the pacing is clean, and there’s nothing here that actively drags it down. It’s competently made and emotionally serviceable, which is sometimes enough.  I don’t really have anything negative to say about it… but I also don’t see myself revisiting it anytime soon.  Rating: 2.5/5 – 70% – Worth Watching

cast_names: Josh Gad, Kaya Scodelario, Anthony Carrigan, Billie Lourd, Ingunn Omholt, Nck Name, Alessio Andrada, Alex Winter, Camille James, Jinny Wong
review_content: Adulthood kicks off with a sharp hook as two seemingly normal siblings discover a corpse in their mother’s home. What follows is a chain reaction of poor choices and accidental deaths that grow increasingly absurd. The dark humour lands often enough to keep it entertaining, even if it never fully commits to the madness.    Josh Gad is the highlight, gleefully breaking his Olaf image with a barrage of swearing and panic. Kaya Scodelario brings edge and charm to her role, once again showing she’s at her best when things get messy. The pair’s chemistry gives the film warmth amidst the bloodshed, even as the story veers into cartoonish territory.    It’s not a film that lingers after the credits roll, but it’s a perfectly serviceable watch for a quiet night. Smartly shot, briskly paced, and carried by a likeable cast, Adulthood entertains without ever hitting greatness.    If you liked… Very Bad Things, Game Night

review_content: i was a bit "meh" regarding the character art style, but honestly it's fine, and the backgrounds + animations are killer level, went blind into this movie and absolutely loved it    jay's song & his voice had such a smooth touch to it, i loved it instantly, and each time he sings it, (as well as Bon Voyage)  also some of the camera movements used in this movie were truly unique and amazing for an animated film, i get why people are loving it  great wide shots throughout the movie, + the transitions at the end  & that one quote;    **_"Somewhere in the starlight... there's someone who's forever and always... cheering you on."_**    **end me.**

cast_names: Kim Tae-ri, Hong Kyung, Yoon Ah-young
review_content: Let’s be real: the main reason to watch Lost In Starlight is the visuals. This is easily one of the most beautifully animated movies I’ve ever seen, pure eye candy. The lighting, the realism, the way every shot is composed… it’s honestly breathtaking, and the film knows it, constantly flexing what the animators can do. If you love movies that are visually pretty for their own sake, this is a treat.  But all that beauty comes at a cost. The story is about as generic as romance gets. Every plot beat is predictable, every character feels unmemorable, and the script never takes the big conceptual swings that the space aspect begs for. With a premise like this, there’s so much potential for deep, philosophical, or psychological exploration, but instead it settles for a pretty basic love story, and even the emotional beats feel more functional than genuinely moving. I cared a bit, but nothing truly stuck with me.  The pacing is uneven, the soundtrack is weirdly soulless (especially given the “musician” angle in the plot), and even though I watched the dub, I doubt the original cast could have done enough to save these characters from blandness. Still, the animation and visual design are on a whole different level, easily the movie’s saving grace.  If you want to feast your eyes on some of the best animation out there, Lost In Starlight delivers, just don’t expect much in terms of plot, character, or emotion. All style, almost no substance.

cast_names: Kim Tae-ri, Hong Kyung, Yoon Ah-young
review_content: This animation project is not a movie made by **Makoto Shinkai** !    South Korea is not globally recognized for its original animation projects, and Korea’s animation studios are rarely in the spotlight.  However this might change eventually, considering the fact that South Korea announced relatively recently that a huge amount of money more than **$1 billion USD** through **2029** will be invested in the animation industry.    The desire to boost this industry is definitely present. Not only that, streaming platforms such as Netflix also invest in projects from South Korea. Some animation projects are definitely on the way and there’s a chance for South Korea to create some really good projects in the near future.    That being said, I would have really liked **Lost in Starlight** to have more personality, to offer something different, something new.  Instead, what we get is an animated film that surely must have cost several million of dollars but relies on basic ideas, almost as if no mistakes were allowed under any circumstances.    **The Summer**, a movie directed by **Han Ji-won** in 2023 already had some similarities with certain projects created by **Makoto Shinkai**, but **Lost in Starlight** is **Han Ji-won’s** most Shinkai-esque film in every sense, and honestly, it’s a bit regrettable.  It feels less like a true expression from the director and more like she borrowed many classic patterns, ideas not only that but also Makoto Shinkai’s style that’s what makes it somewhat unsettling.    Many elements seem forced and cliché, particularly in the romance aspect of the movie, which makes it feel unnatural. The writing of our two main protagonists and the story development have major issues overall.    When it comes to the romance aspect, Nan-young represents the future, Jay the past, both different, not living in the same world, but ultimately two sides of the same coin, two opposites that naturally gravitate toward each other but this aspect is never really explored before, during, or after they begin their relationship while their past stories and traumas are actually portrayed in a nice way, in the end everything seems just so basic so simple.    **Lost in Starlight** is a romance movie that mainly explores themes of love, distance, perseverance, but also trauma. The film undeniably has great visuals.    I appreciated the way it depicts South Korea and Seoul, with a futuristic aspect that still remains faithful to the city’s image. It’s a romance and sci-fi film that preserves the architecture of the past in certain buildings, shops, and streets, which brings a natural feeling of nostalgia to the film, a nice touch of coherence to the story. Visually there’s very little to criticize  it really is a beautiful movie.      While I wasn’t entirely convinced, **Lost in Starlight** is the first major animated project from South Korea and a visually beautiful film.   Quite frankly, for both of these reasons it’s worth watching it, as it is from my perspective the foundation of what South Korea can bring to the animation industry.      **6/10.**

review_content: If you're up-to-date on the news, and I mean the news that covers objective reality (not Fox), this will be a rough watch. I see many reviewers praising the film with their comments, then rating it a 3.5. It's certainly not an enjoyable watch.  This is an intellectual thriller with overt tones of horror, mostly because of how spot on it mirrors reality. And if you're hoping it at least has a happy ending, unfortunately that part also mirrors our current reality.   From a technical standpoint, this was acted and directed well. Ok, maybe not the "NAME THE DOG!!!" scene, but the lead actress was phenomenal.   The Catch-22 to this film is, the people who understand it don't really need to see it, and the people who really need to see it wouldn't understand it. That's how we ended up with "The Change" (Project 2025) ruining our country.

review_content: I think the difficulty in making films like this is that you want to make it "safe" and generic enough so that viewers will walk in the door, and then you don't want to upset them before the end of the movie.  The first half of the movie tries to walk a "both sides have issues" line, and it does a decent job of doing so (although it does kind of insult our intelligence - we all know who belongs to which ideology).   The second half of the movie tries to prove a point, but the whole thing feels kind of empty.  It still feels like it is tiptoeing around what it really wants to say, even thought it clearly says something.  The final scene is powerful and probably the best scene in the movie - the ride to get their is fairly mediocre despite the presence of some fine actors.    follow me at https://IHATEBadMovies.com or facebook IHATEBadMovies

cast_names: Diane Lane, Kyle Chandler, Madeline Brewer, Zoey Deutch, Mckenna Grace, Phoebe Dynevor, Dylan O'Brien, Daryl McCormack, Rebecca O'Mara, Flavia Watson
review_content: Possibly one of the most unexpectedly bleak films I've seen all year, and the lack of marketing genuinely upsets me. Densely dystopian scripts like these aren't easy to convey, so credit to Jan Komasa, especially for making it as relatable and original as can be. Each character is crafted with meticulous and metaphorical intent, and the casting is flawless. Lane, O'Brien, Chandler, Brewer, Deutch, Dynevor, McCormack... the performances hit hard. The stark but gradual internal and external shift into its total antithesis is so disconcertingly 'familiar'. The lack of labelling might seem labyrinthine at first, but by the end, the messages of totalitarianism, extremism, propaganda, censorship, and repression feel like a stab in the gut.

It starts off well, but then it kind of goes nowhere. It’s just silly that two adults can’t seem to acknowledge how much they like each other until it’s too late, so she has an affair with her “best friend”.  Goldstein’s character is quite likable, actually. It’s she who is the actual player and manipulative person. And he dances to her every whim. I guess it’s good to see some roles subverted… for a start.   Ah, and I almost forgot. The time jumps between scenes were just too annoying.

review_content: This movie excels for two reasons. First, Poots and Goldstein have amazing chemistry.   I don't know why Imogene isn't a bigger star because she is a fantastic actress.  Second, the movie feels like a fresh entry into well-traveled themes.   This isn't a rom-com, and I think people got tripped up over that fact (and the ending, which is perfect). cast_names: Imogen Poots, Brett Goldstein, Zawe Ashton, Steven Cree, Jenna Coleman, Éva Magyar, Alara-Star Khan, Tariq Rasheed, Nadia Albina, Ieva Andrejevaitė
review_content: Its a great film. The entirety of the film hangs just on the shoulders of the 2 main leads, no other characters are taking the show - and they handle it amazingly. You never get tired of watching them. The film showcases a wide range of actions and emotions so everything is fresh. I deeply appreciated that it never fell into cheap clichés or overused tropes, and the film keeps an adult and mature level and trusts its viewers with quality materials.  Hand it all and trust Brett Goldstein to know what he's doing and how to write a well balanced story. Good Job.  Very recommended.    A note about comments that say "can't follow what's going on" -  As I said, here we have high quality writing and lots of trust in the audience. As a result, this film offers no american style in-your-face exposition. Meaning, you will NOT be fed with a tiny spoon about every event that happened, and will have to use your mind.  The viewer should take that into account before watching it, otherwise will result in a frown face and not understanding what's going on.    Also comments that rate with a low grade because they disagree with the character - consider these ratings as high values! Those mean that the film actually affected them enough to feel for the character!

review_content: I really like the actors. The scenery.   But this story and script? Seriously not very confrontational about the situation at all. And perhaps that's the point, right? People that and up in these situations want to remain blameless and they only end up on these situations with people who will never blame them.   She's a user. At the very end, I half expected her crocodile tears to dry up in the taxi and a smirk to roll across her face. Because that's what kind of person her character would be in real life. Just the female version would never be held to account like the male version. A male cheater carrying on for years would never leave his wife, and he would keep trying to pull those strings forever. Whereas the reverse is a man who will never blow the whole thing up the way a mistress would.   You feel sorry for him, but then you realize he was going to be one of her two suckers no matter what. Had he grown a pair, he would've been the husband she was cheating on. Instead he's the guy she kept in the wings for as long as she could.   4.8/10.   The fact it's close to true life for some is its only redeeming quality. It's also the reason I would say it's a waste of time other than for the analysis of female narcissists.

review_content: Literally just got out of the theater watching it and damn. That was really f*cking cool. I went in with zero expectations and no real knowledge other than when I first watched Mark play the game years ago.   I haven’t really watched Mark much in the past few years either so seeing him on the big screen is a wild experience. It was a little difficult at first to suspend my disbelief and not just see him as my OG Let’s Player but I think he did an amazing job.   Performance-wise, I was wanting just a little more of an emotional /push/, more of that broken desperation of a man left to die and hallucinating in the deep sea. But overall, it was still a great performance, especially from someone who is not an actor-by-trade. There are professional actors who I think would struggle with the same issue.  The practical set was STUNNING. Such a cool piece of artistry. I’ve seen a little bit of the behind-the-scenes so I’m aware of the way it’s rigged and as someone who’s dabbled in indie filmmaking, I think it’s magnificent.  I didn’t know the runtime until I bought my ticket and I thought, “There’s no way it’s only inside the entire time.” But they proved me wrong. And they did it so well.   Amazing cinematography, they really did so much with such a small space. Such clever work with the lighting and the blood, and the score was chilling, beautifully effective.   Honestly so much more than I was expecting and so inspiring what Mark and the whole team managed to do and create. I loved it.

review_content: The fourth installment of Zombies confirms what many suspected: the franchise is starting to show signs of exhaustion. The story feels repetitive, the resources seem limited, and the increasingly tight budget is evident in every frame. Still, against all odds, it’s not a total disaster. There are fun moments, and it maintains the upbeat spirit that made it a hit with younger audiences.    Narratively, the film once again emphasizes unity over hate and prejudice — a metaphor we’ve already seen throughout the series, but one that still holds value. This time, vampires and so-called daywalkers are caught in conflict, with Zed and Addison trying to bring them together, following the same structure used with zombies, werewolves, and aliens in previous entries.    Visually, it’s as colorful and sugary as ever, with cheerful musical numbers that do the job but no longer surprise. The chemistry between the leads still carries much of the story, while the new characters try to bring freshness without fully succeeding.    Perhaps the most redeeming aspect is its message: reinforcing the idea that unity, empathy, and diversity are stronger than division. It may sound naïve, but in today’s world it’s not such a bad thing for a teen movie to underline something so basic and necessary.    Zombies 4 won’t go down in history as great family cinema nor does it extend the saga’s magic much further, but for its target audience — especially those who’ve followed it from the beginning — it still has a certain charm. And if it gets new generations singing, dancing, and talking about accepting differences, maybe it fulfills its purpose better than it seems.

cast_names: Blanca Suárez, Daniel Grao, Aria Bedmar, Cosimo Fusco, Víctor Palmero, Daniel Horvath, Fernando Cayo, Pablo Rivero, Juan Manuel Cifuentes, Kandido Uranga
review_content: Some thrillers grab you from the first minute, while others collapse under their own weight. This one clearly falls into the latter. The setting has some appeal, with landscapes and locations that bring a different atmosphere, but the narrative drags on without a clear direction.    The plot tries to hold tension through twists that never quite fit, more designed to surprise than to make sense. The result is a story stretched beyond its limits, where every development feels like an exercise in patience for the viewer.    The highlight, without doubt, comes from the female cast. Blanca Suárez throws herself fully into a role that demands more than the script gives her, and Aria Bedmar adds a welcome spark in the middle of the monotony. They keep the interest alive, even if the rest doesn’t rise to their level.    The supporting cast does their job without standing out, and the police thread lacks the punch needed to sustain this kind of thriller. There was a good idea somewhere behind it, but it gets lost in clichés, forced dialogue, and a lack of rhythm that eventually takes its toll.    In the end, it’s a failed attempt, heavier than thrilling, wasting both its actors and its locations. A suspense story that ends up as little more than a forgettable anecdote.

cast_names: Vincent Cassel, Diane Kruger, Guy Pearce, Sandrine Holt, Elizabeth Saunders, Jennifer Dale, Eric Weinthal, Jeff Yung, Ingvar E. Sigurðsson, Vieslav Krystyan
review_content: I’m a big Cronenberg guy, and I still couldn’t believe how bad The Shrouds is. His dialogue is always stiff and clinical, but usually it’s intentional — a way of showing characters who are emotionally detached or alienated from their own bodies. Here, the characters are trying to connect, trying to share grief and trauma, and they still talk like malfunctioning AIs. Entire scenes play out as people dumping exposition at each other in shot-reverse-shot. Half the movie takes place on tablets. It honestly feels less like Cronenbergian coldness and more like watching a radio drama where the actors wandered onto a set by accident.    Visually, there’s nothing going on. No tension, no buildup, no sense of environment. Just flat rooms and people talking. Even the body-horror ideas — his wife’s mutilation, the bone growths, the “hip break” scene — feel disconnected from the film around them. And the AI character Honey? Absolute disaster. A Dora-the-Explorer cartoon who turns into a koala and later a naked cartoon version of his dead wife. It’s one of the lamest, most baffling images Cronenberg has ever put on screen. Add in Vincent Cassel giving one of the flattest performances of his career and an elderly sex scene that goes on forever, and the whole thing becomes unintentionally comedic.    And then there’s the story, which never actually starts. The “mystery” has no stakes, the twist is a shrug, and the movie just… ends. Cassel gets on a plane, hallucinates for five seconds, and credits roll. No climax, no resolution, nothing tying the themes together. I stuck with it because I’ve loved Cronenberg for decades, but this is the first time one of his films felt like a draft. I’m sympathetic to the grief behind it — his wife died, and this is clearly personal — but none of that emotion is on screen. It’s sterile in all the wrong ways, more lifeless than any of his hospital operating rooms. Easily his weakest film, and a rare Cronenberg I’d tell people to skip.

cast_names: Vincent Cassel, Diane Kruger, Guy Pearce, Sandrine Holt, Elizabeth Saunders, Jennifer Dale, Eric Weinthal, Jeff Yung, Ingvar E. Sigurðsson, Vieslav Krystyan
review_content: Didn't want to dislike this, but I think Cronenberg has flown well away from the compelling craftsmanship that was 'The Fly'. The subject matter is always unnerving and deep, so I'd get prepared since there's a lot of discussion around death, grief, mortality, and morality. The casting is great, as is the acting. The dialogue, not so much. I tried to understand what was going on, and I did for the first 30 or so minutes. Then I didn't. It's refreshing having original catalysts like the shrouds…I just wish the plot was structured more cohesively with less ambiguity.

cast_names: Vincent Cassel, Diane Kruger, Guy Pearce, Sandrine Holt, Elizabeth Saunders, Jennifer Dale, Eric Weinthal, Jeff Yung, Ingvar E. Sigurðsson, Vieslav Krystyan
review_content: [Filmin] Through the pain of loss, the film constructs a disturbing web of conspiracies that unfold within one another, yet persist in their mysterious nature. But above all, it's the story of an obsession with remaining connected to the loved one even after death, a kind of sentimental necrophilia that, on the surface, is represented through the protagonist's erotic impulses and his relationships with other women. It's as confusing as it is intriguing, a very peculiar vision of grief that is represented through a certain form of necrophilia.

review_content: 'Fixed' is, to barely any surprise (well, so I thought), utter tripe. It's somehow worse than 2023's 'Strays', which is the one impressive thing about this 2025 movie. These 'adult animated' films always fascinate me, like who the heck are they for? It's teenage humour for... grown adults?!    Grossed out humour can exist, but I wished they'd stop stooping to the bottom of the barrel every single time... poop and sex gags, that's all they're capable of apparently - even when dogs are involved, evidently. Every inch of this film is just mind-numbingly stupid.    Listen, I'm fully aware that I only have myself to blame. Despite knowing nothing major about it, the tiny knowledge I did hold made me know what I was about to watch. Yet I still chose to watch it, hoping one day one of these 'adult animated' releases will be worthwhile...    Zero creativity here, proper lazy stuff. I simply cannot compute that people actually sat around and wrote this... they live amongst us, which is a scary thought! Those voices onscreen aren't much better, Idris Elba is the only one who is minorly OK (hearing him f-bomb is minimally amusing).    You can have all this gross comedy, but at least surround it with a plot that's somewhat coherent and interesting; the gags should be part of the film, not it entirely. Lastly... gotta feel for Andrea Bocelli - they really be letting anyone use his music, huh?    I've not checked how this has been received yet... surely not any better than from yours truly? checks y'all too kind. If you like this then fair enough, I guess. One of the worst movies I've seen, personally.

cast_names: Motaz Malhees, Saja Kilani, Amer Hlehel, Clara Khoury, Nesbat Serhan, Ramy Brahem
review_content: “The Voice of Hind Rajab” is a film that refuses any possibility of comfortable viewing. From its very first minutes, Kaouther Ben Hania makes it clear she isn’t interested in guiding the audience through a carefully engineered emotional journey, with artificial dramatic peaks or strategically placed moments of relief. What takes over instead is a direct confrontation with violence, not the spectacular, overexposed violence of war imagery that’s been numbed by repetition, but a structural, bureaucratic, and psychological violence that reveals itself precisely through what isn’t shown. The film is born from a real act of extreme brutality, but its power lies in how it turns that event into a rigorous, ethical, and deeply disturbing cinematic device.    The decision to structure “The Voice of Hind Rajab” as a claustrophobic docudrama, largely confined to the space of a Palestinian Red Crescent office, is central to the film’s impact. Ben Hania understands that placing the camera inside the car, next to the child surrounded by the bodies of her family members, would be an easy, sensationalist, and ultimately exploitative move. Instead, she builds the narrative around listening. Violence enters the film not through explicit imagery, but through Hind’s fragile voice, transmitted via an unstable phone call, constantly breaking up, always on the verge of silence. This choice shifts the horror to a place that’s much harder to avoid: there are no images to look away from, no quick cuts to ease the tension. The viewer, like the emergency responders, is forced to stay there listening, waiting, powerless.    Formally, the film is cruelly precise. The use of an ultra-wide format creates a powerful visual irony: a vast frame for a situation with no escape, a generous visual field for characters trapped by protocols, authorizations, and chains of command that drain any sense of human urgency. The visualization of sound through a spectrogram is one of the film’s most striking choices, turning Hind’s voice into a physical, tactile presence that occupies the screen. It’s not just about hearing a child ask for help, but about seeing her voice exist, vibrate, falter. Sound stops being a supporting element to the image and becomes the film’s very body.    The performances of the actors playing Red Crescent workers are marked by remarkable restraint. There are no melodramatic outbursts or fiery speeches; desperation shows up in looks, in pauses, in the way each new bureaucratic demand is met with a mix of disbelief and resignation. The film understands that the true antagonist isn’t only the invisible military force on the other end of the line, but the system that normalizes waiting while a child bleeds alone. By following, almost in real time, the hours spent on absurd negotiations to clear a route just minutes away, “The Voice of Hind Rajab” gives devastating form to the idea of administrative violence. Protocol, here, isn’t neutral: it kills.    Narratively, the film functions like an echo chamber of powerlessness. Every attempt to move forward is followed by a setback, every moment of hope by a new obstacle. Ben Hania doesn’t arrange events to suggest a single mistake, one wrong decision that led to the tragic outcome. On the contrary, what the film exposes is that everything worked exactly as it was supposed to within that logic. And that’s what makes it so disturbing. By the end, there’s no comforting illusion that doing something differently would have changed the outcome. The system is designed to fail, and that failure isn’t accidental, but a direct result of its structure.    The constant use of real recordings from Hind’s phone calls with emergency services adds a crucial ethical layer to the film. This isn’t a reenactment loosely inspired by real events, but a work anchored in concrete traces of that child’s existence. These recordings function as archive, as evidence, and as memory. They don’t just document Hind’s final moments; they also capture, unmistakably, the workings of a dehumanizing machine that operates at a distance, shielded by procedures and official language. Watching the film becomes an act of witnessing, and Ben Hania makes it clear that this witnessing is neither optional nor comfortable.    There’s also a powerful metacinematic dimension in how the film deals with the circulation of these images and sounds in the contemporary world. When the narrative shows the team turning to social media as a last resort, hoping that international outrage might produce some tangible effect, the film points to a cruel paradox: the exposure of suffering becomes a form of currency, a desperate attempt to break through institutional blockade via the spectacle of pain. “The Voice of Hind Rajab” neither celebrates nor condemns this strategy. It presents it for what it is: a symptom of a world where a child’s life has to compete for attention within an endless stream of images.    In the end, the film refuses to offer any kind of comforting closure. There is no catharsis, no redemption, no simple moral takeaway. What remains is memory. Ben Hania builds the film as a cinematic memorial, not only for Hind Rajab, but for all those whose lives are crushed by systems that operate with total impunity. The question that lingers (how can someone fire on an ambulance, find a living child inside a bullet-riddled car, and execute her?) isn’t meant to be answered by the film, because there may be no answer that isn’t itself a declaration of barbarism.    “The Voice of Hind Rajab” isn’t a film you simply watch; it’s a film you carry with you. It demands that the viewer accept the responsibility to remember, to refuse turning that voice into just another statistic or another tragedy consumed and discarded. By choosing listening, restraint, and a rejection of spectacle, Kaouther Ben Hania delivers a political and cinematic gesture of rare force. The film doesn’t ask for abstract empathy or empty solidarity. It demands presence. And above all, it demands recognition that this physical, psychological, and administrative violence isn’t a deviation from the system, it’s one of its core features.

cast_names: Motaz Malhees, Saja Kilani, Amer Hlehel, Clara Khoury, Nesbat Serhan, Ramy Brahem
review_content: The Voice of Hind Rajab is a call to action. Throughout the movie, there is a very frustrating lack of action. It's predicated on doing things the right way, and the coordinator is not wrong that trying to rush dispatching the ambulance could put lives at risk. However, throughout, the literal voice of Hind Rajab reminds us that no reason matters if it results in the misery of a young girl. I hope this movie is able to reach a wide audience and it is able to inspire actual change. It is soul shattering devastation, never shying away from its mission to showcase a real story. The calls are all real. The voice is real. The dialogue is real. These things actually happened. Kaouther Ben Hania is able to seamlessly blend fictionalized reactions to the call and the decision-making process against that documentary-style backdrop. Motaz Malhees and Saja Kilani are phenomenal. Their facial reactions and vocal inflections are heartbreaking. Sometimes their counterparts dialogue will play as the camera just focuses on their faces, and in one scene, a character is filming the events on their phone, the screen on the phone displaying actual footage while the rest of the frame is the fictionalized footage. This movie then becomes something more, a rigorous and detail-oriented attempt at recreating the events faithfully while keeping the beat of authenticity throughout, never feeling overly sensationalized. I highlight these points because I want to be clear that while the movie is important because of the story it chooses to tell, how it chooses to tell that story makes it into a great movie. This movie has been seared into me and will haunt me. I hope it manages to do that to even more people because Hanood's story deserves to be heard around the world.

cast_names: Motaz Malhees, Saja Kilani, Amer Hlehel, Clara Khoury, Nesbat Serhan, Ramy Brahem
review_content: Kaouther Ben Hania’s The Voice of Hind Rajab is a brief but searing masterclass in restraint and moral urgency. In 1 hour 29 minutes she turns a single, harrowing emergency call into an unbearable, utterly human drama: five‑year‑old Hind Rajab trapped in a car under fire in Gaza, her voice the fragile tether that mobilizes volunteers and insists we not look away. The screenplay’s focus on that one desperate line of contact gives the film a crystalline intensity—every second counts, every silence roars.    The performances are raw and authentic, carried by an ensemble that never slips into melodrama. The volunteers’ quiet professionalism, fear and helplessness are portrayed with disarming naturalism; Hind’s presence—felt more than seen—makes the film’s emotional stakes explosive. Ben Hania’s direction is economical and humane, letting small details (the cadence of a voice, the static on a call, the bureaucratic obstacles) accumulate into crushing emotional truth.    Technically the film is economical but exact—editing and sound design do the heavy lifting, creating claustrophobic tension and a realism that keeps the viewer on edge throughout. The bilingual English/Arabic presentation and the Tunisian production perspective give it a necessary specificity and global relevance; it’s no surprise to see it listed among the international feature shortlists. This is cinema that insists on witnessing, on asking difficult questions about duty, trauma and the cost of attention.    This is urgent, humane filmmaking—devastating, unforgettable and essential. A profound 10/10.

cast_names: Ben Foster, Cobie Smulders, William Kosovic, Gavin Drea, Andrew Shaver, Mark A. Owen, Trina Corkum, Jonathan Watton, Reid Price, Allison Wilson-Forbes
review_content: There’s something beautifully bleak about Sharp Corner. One man, one bend in the road, and a string of car crashes—that’s it. And yet, somehow, it’s one of the most quietly gripping films of the year. Ben Foster plays a man so stripped of power in his own life that he finds purpose in disaster, scuttling out from his porch like a morbid caretaker of fate. He isn’t a hero. He just wants to feel like one.    The tone walks a razor’s edge: funny, but unnerving; grounded, but surreal. His interventions are oddly rehearsed, more performance than rescue, and there’s a strange tenderness in how he handles the injured—less about them, more about him. It’s an astonishing performance from Foster, who imbues his character with layers of internal contradiction—emasculation dressed as control, sympathy twisted by obsession. Every twitchy hand gesture and awkward smile tells you exactly what this man has lost—and what he’s clawing to reclaim.    This is Falling Down for the domesticated male: not raging at the world, but carving out meaning in the smallest, saddest way. A comment on the collapse of traditional masculinity—provider, protector, stoic rock—and what fills that void when none of those labels fit anymore. And somehow, with just one crash-prone corner of tarmac, the film captures all of that.    If you liked: Falling Down, The Assistant, Take Shelter, The Killing of a Sacred Deer.

cast_names: Alexandra Jiménez, Ernesto Sevilla, Aimar Miranda, Luis Callejo, Pepe Viyuela, Salva Reina, Carmen Ruiz, Joaquín Reyes, Germán Alcarazu, Petra Martínez
review_content: Some comedies rely on lightness to work; others simply give up and hide behind it. This one clearly falls into the second category. The initial idea could have led somewhere, maybe even allowed for a bit of bite, but from the moment it starts it’s obvious there’s no real intention to push anything. What follows is a string of weak situations, with no rhythm or surprise, moving forward more out of inertia than conviction.    The main problem is the script. Not because it’s aimed at kids — that wouldn’t be a flaw by itself — but because it’s lazy. Gags repeat themselves, dialogue lacks edge, and there’s a constant feeling that the film never really gets going. Whenever it seems about to pick up, it falls back into easy jokes and a flat, lifeless brand of comedy, as if no one bothered to refine anything beyond a first draft.    The direction doesn’t help either. The whole thing has a very TV-like feel, with functional but uninspired staging and not a single creative choice that stands out. Editing is uneven, and the digital effects, instead of adding anything, become distracting because of how poor they are. Everything feels rushed, careless, and oddly indifferent.    The cast does what it can. There are actors with experience and talent here, but they’re trapped in flat, broadly written characters. Ernesto Sevilla stays firmly in his comfort zone without bringing anything new, while the rest oscillate between overacting and quiet resignation. Only a few isolated elements — a veteran presence, a well-used musical cue — prevent the whole thing from completely collapsing.    As family entertainment, it might work for younger viewers, but even on that level it feels thin. There’s no imagination, no risk, and above all, no humor that truly connects. It’s the kind of film you watch quickly and forget even faster, leaving you with the feeling that you’ve wasted not just time, but a bit of patience too.
Why such a short run time, surely there must have been other people's perspectives/stories they could have got of what happened that night.
I would probably come too out of curiosity. ;)

movie_title: It's Worth It by Dani Rovira
cast_names: Dani Rovira
review_content: It's worth it, it's like that honest conversation that comforts you. Dani Rovira goes on stage and undresses emotionally – with humour, without posturing – to talk to us about sadness, grief and how to be honest with what we feel in the worst moments. It mixes everything up: you make jokes, you get excited, and you come out thinking that you are not alone in feeling sorry. It is not a light comedy; It is pure real comedy, alive, which respects the serious without giving it a life lesson. So, yes, you laugh, but you also laugh.

review_content: In the world of indie-filmmaking, one of the most important skills is stretching the budget, and that's perhaps this movie's greatest strength.  It could easily be mistaken for something with ten times the budget.  That translates into a visually impressive film with a surprisingly ambitious scope (i.e., car chases, shootouts, explosions, etc.).  Combine that technical proficiency with a central premise that puts a fun twist on the time-loop genre, and you end up with an enjoyable little revenge thriller.  Yes, there were some weak spots with respect to performances and writing (e.g., some on-the-nose monologues), but they weren't deal breakers for me.  On top of that, I'm a fan of Jim Cummings, and his part in the film was a highlight.
