movie_title: Spinal Tap II: The End Continues
cast_names: Christopher Guest, Michael McKean, Harry Shearer, Rob Reiner, Valerie Franco, CJ Vanston, Jean Cromie, Kerry Godliman, Garth Brooks, Trisha Yearwood
review_content: Hate to say it, but here’s more proof that father time is undefeated. It’s been forty years since _This is Spinal Tap_ so wickedly illustrated the less-glamorous aspects of arena rock stardom, and like the group’s throngs of jean-jacketed supporters, the magic has long since dissipated. The reality of Guest, McKean and Shearer’s screen reunion mirrors that of their dim-witted alter egos on the lighted stage. Three aging former sparkplugs, brought back together after decades apart, who can’t find the fire that once fueled their notoriety. They go through the motions, play the hits, try to fake it, but there’s no denying the truth. This ship sailed half a generation ago, for characters and comedians alike.    The long-awaited _Spinal Tap_ sequel isn’t completely devoid of wit. I found a few chuckles, but never once flirted with hysterics like I did (and still do) so steadily in the first film. As I feared, many of the best punchlines are in the trailer, and it wasn’t even a very good trailer. The scant plot is weighted down with cameos, including two distinguished members of rock royalty who look even older than the band does, but a vast majority of these guest shots are simple coast jobs. Famous somebodies who pop up and smile for the camera, confident that their mere presence will be enough to prop up an otherwise mediocre scene. Most returning members of the original supporting cast also fall into this category. It was nice to see Fran Drescher again, if only for a few moments, but couldn’t they have given her a laugh or two? I guess those are in short enough supply as it is.    This is mighty soft stuff. Soft, unnecessary and a little sad. Hobbled by age and complacency, the stars muster no energy at all, not even when they’ve reached the stage of their big comeback show and perform a medley of favorite old tunes. Not even in the shadow of a correctly-sized Stonehenge prop. Like most belated comedy sequels (see _Anchorman 2_ or _Zoolander 2_), I couldn’t find it in me to skip this one, but now kinda wish I had.

cast_names: Julio Peña, Alessandro Borghi, Miguel Rellán, Fernando Tejero, Luis Callejo, José Manuel Poga, Roberto Álamo, Albert Salazar, Juanma Muniagurria, César Saratxu
review_content: This is not the film many expect when approaching Cervantes’ captivity after Lepanto, and that becomes clear very early on. There is no academic reconstruction here, nor any real intention to stick to the version of events we’ve always been told. What Amenábar proposes is something else: a freer, more speculative approach that blends adventure, imprisonment, and reflections on identity, even at the risk of making viewers uncomfortable.    The main point of friction lies precisely there. The film dares to fill the silences of History with hypotheses that not everyone will be willing to accept. Some work as intellectual provocation, others feel more strained, but it’s clear there’s a conscious desire to challenge the official narrative. In a country still marked by latent homophobia, that choice is neither innocent nor safe, and its risk deserves acknowledgment.    As an adventure film, it works solidly. The staging is carefully crafted, the historical setting convincingly recreated, and there are moments of genuine tension and visual beauty. Emotionally, however, it struggles more. The story moves forward competently, but it doesn’t always make what’s at stake feel as heavy as it should. At times, it seems more invested in the idea than in the feeling.    It’s not a definitive or fully rounded work, but it is a distinctive entry within recent Spanish historical cinema. It may please or irritate—sometimes both at once. And perhaps that is its greatest strength: daring to touch a myth while fully aware that controversy would be the price.

cast_names: Julio Peña, Alessandro Borghi, Miguel Rellán, Fernando Tejero, Luis Callejo, José Manuel Poga, Roberto Álamo, Albert Salazar, Juanma Muniagurria, César Saratxu
review_content: [Veoasecan] An ambitious approach to the figure of Miguel de Cervantes that doesn't avoid the controversy, but above all, lacks emotion and depth. Its Orientalist portrayal of Algiers, the Western view of sensuality and the stereotypical depiction of a society that is not understood are more problematic than its exploration of sexual identity. But the story is told with a simplistic and superficial approach, structured thematically as if it were a history book, failing to convey even the slightest emotion, even in the prisoners' escape attempts.

cast_names: Julio Peña, Alessandro Borghi, Miguel Rellán, Fernando Tejero, Luis Callejo, José Manuel Poga, Roberto Álamo, Albert Salazar, Juanma Muniagurria, César Saratxu
review_content: Like a Spanish wine: lots of body but no heart.    Based on historical fact, Miguel de Cervantes is held prisoner for ransom in Algiers and seems to receive special treatment.     The film seems more like a sequence of scenes to detail Cervantes' inspiration for Don Quixote than an exploration of the man behind the book. Not to mention, the film's central premise seems flawed.    Still, at least I have a new guy crush (Alessandro Borghi 😍)!

review_content: [Movistar+] France has dedicated a series and a film this year to the evacuation of Kabul following the retreat of the United States and international forces. Both suffer from the same problem, focusing so much on depicting the difficult escape that they sometimes seem more interested in the message than the narrative. This film works well as a suffocating thriller that gradually engulfs its protagonists in an unimaginable nightmare. But in the end, it gives the impression that it doesn't have much to say about the consequences of the withdrawal, remaining instead focused on the spectacle of the masses trying to flee a country that will soon be crushed by religious extremism.

review_content: [Disney+] This could be the last great optimistic film from an octogenarian director in a country so deeply entrenched in polarization and pessimism. Set in the years when "we still liked each other," it has a nostalgic feel and is built with the rhythm of the 1940s comedies that Brooks has so skillfully referenced in his films. The idealism of movies like **Mr. Smith Goes to Washington** (Frank Capra, 1939) is not only found in the attitude of this young governor, but in the film itself, and the mess of the protagonist's family life is reflected in the charmingly chaotic structure of the movie.

cast_names: Jessie Buckley, Christian Bale, Peter Sarsgaard, Annette Bening, Jake Gyllenhaal, Penélope Cruz, John Magaro, Matthew Maher, Jeannie Berlin, Zlatko Burić
review_content: Man what a weird movie. There are times of pure excitement and incredible chemistry between two fantastic actors. Moments that are incredibly visually distinct from so many movies releasing today. All followed with insane ramblings and themes so in your face, that's the only thing you can focus on. This film wants to be so many different things. Some quite good. Others quite forgettable.     The two leads in The Bride/Ida and Frankenstein are undeniably amazing. I've always said Bale is one of a kind, the best actor working, and my favorite. I might be biased, but I'm still correct. His Frankenstein shows layers of pain, excitement, and anger that are amplified so well by his physical acting. This is my first Buckley movie, but it will not be my last. She absolutely shines and shows her ability to switch between 2 character in rapid succession, with ease. Going from someone so lost and confused, to being this free and rebellious spirit was a delight. Flashes of Harley Quinn inspired characteristics, which are good and bad for me.     The cinematography is at times absolutely stunning. All the while maintaining a consistent flashy visual style. Chicago is loud and brimming with opulence. All the while hiding it's grittier "freaky" secrets. Great lighting and costume design too. In terms of visuals this movie has no complaints from my end.     The major problems with this movie is the narrative, pacing, and general story beats/tonal shifts. There are so many jumps in ideas and subplots it feels so jarring. Going from B&F partying, to cops subplot, to dramatic scene, to another subplot, to monologue, to somehow another subplot. It's tiring and takes away from the good bits of story in this movie. The 2 cops aren't boring, but they aren't anywhere near as exciting as B&F. You would think they're the dead ones lol. The Bride at time feels like Harley Quinn. I'm not a fan of the character and her dominance of the last 10ish years has worn it's welcome. Especially when it jumps out after or before a slower scene.It can feel a bit like whiplash and can mess with the tone. That's not Buckley's fault, simply the writing and the direction of the movie. This movie wants to be a lot, with flashes of substances. It's just not enough though.    I think in 15-20 years this will be on of those movies people randomly find on HBO Max. Looking at the cast, wondering what this hidden gem is. After a watch and some review searching, they'll be shocked by the poor reviews when it released. Slowly becoming a cult classic type of film. It gives me a lot of feeling that I had when watching Babylon. Not the classic of today, but the underrated, hidden gem of the next decade or so. review_content: This movie is so lazy. It is campy as hell and does not take itself seriously, but fails to be entertaining enough that it needs editing tricks to make it feel like something is happening. All those slow-mo action shots? Ew. The slow-mo tries to add the cool factor in tandem with Renee's apparent omniscient ability to know where every bad guy is.Seriously, those slow-mo shots are the tip of the iceberg, the sped-up shots are the real annoying thing. They too happen so much, but outside of the action scenes and makes no sense in their placement. One was in the middle of an establishing shot... for no reason. Those establishing shots too are there to pad time, lots of lingering scenes. The exposition dump at the very beginning is suicide squad level bad, but much worse because it goes on for way too long as well.    The script is so cringe, it feels like the game Forspoken in terms of dialogue. Characters say things that should be obvious to them, but need to explain to the audience or have a little moment for us to like the one speaking. Lots of cliche lines too. "How am I going to do that?" "Use your imagination" type shtick trying to sound cool. There's also a really bad reference to Charlotte's Webb that makes no sense in general conversation lol. Lazy writing, generic plot, and another film with no research given to how things really work to suspend disbelief even a little bit. Like really, why does no one, in 2026 know how to either use a smart phone or know how they are supposed to look and behave?    Okay supporting actors wit h terrible material to work with, but the main "crew" is bad. Again, everything said or done is to build them up to be cool. It doesn't work hardly ever. The backstories they give are something I wish I saw visually. Because this feels like the Kill Bill squad in a way, but of course introductions are important as Tarantino showed. Clearly this film thinks they're overrated. These people are all meant to be highly trained and skilled, yet do and say things that go directly against that and their intel gathering isn't being gathered at all, its just falling into their lap rather than them doing reconnaissance. It's so frustrating.     I really liked the team concept they had going for this, much like The 365  movie that came out a few years back. It has the idea, and a few good executed moments. However, I would have liked if they used the budget they got here to actually make it good. Because there are some expensive looking shots and sets, but there are some lazy repeats of those and costumes. With a with a tie being the only thing changed.    This movie is horrible. Motivations are not given weight on screen, can't remember anyone's names, and the plot is told only through dialogue so it is at times hard to follow. Nothing felt built up enough for it to feel resolved by the end [spoiler](especially for that twist holy shit that was bad)[/spoiler] and therefore leave the film hollow.    **2/10**

review_content: ==India’s First Perfect Superhero Universe in the Making==    Didn’t expect it to be this good! The cinematography, VFX, and background music are all top-notch. The story itself is solid — though there’s definitely room for improvement — but what really stands out is how well it sets up the larger cinematic universe.    The connections built between characters and events feel natural and promising. Honestly, this could turn out to be India’s first truly well-structured superhero universe in the making. There are already a few superhero franchises in India, but this one feels more cohesive and timeline-accurate.    The post-credit and end-credit scenes did their job perfectly — I’m genuinely excited for what’s coming next. The lead character, Chandra, was a spot-on casting choice, and Kalyani looked absolutely stunning on screen.    A strong start to what could be an epic universe ahead.

cast_names: Kalyani Priyadarshan, Naslen K. Gafoor, Sandy, Chandu Salimkumar, Vijayaraghavan, Sarath Sabha, Tovino Thomas, Dulquer Salmaan, Nishanth Sagar, Raghunath Paleri
review_content: Set in to weave a remarkable superhero lokah (universe), the chapter Chandra is an incredible creation. As innovative as it is, some elements still felt unoriginal (cred: MCU and DCEU). The cinematography, particularly the fluidity in the transition of the two shots—salt on the table to the night sky full of stars and glasses to chandeliers—was beautiful. But the CGI was exaggerated. Is it too much to ask for a little bit more authenticity in the action graphics in this day and age? That being said, the cast nailed the action sequences, especially Durga C Vinod and Kalyani Priyadarshan as Chandra.     There were several unnecessary subplots in the story, though who knows, maybe those characters will return later on. The comedy in the first half made me cringe so hard. The chemistry felt forced to an extent, especially the history of it. But the tale, the build-up, the cameos, the suspense—worth the wait; worth the watch.     The villain has got such a weak arc. Most other characters, especially the protagonist has been built on solid ground. While what we know is a drop and what we don't is an ocean (or an entire lokah), it is too soon to judge this creative piece for its loopholes and loose ends. What we can reckon, though, is that regardless of what becomes of this lokah, it's a first in Mollywood, and shall forever be held in prestigious remembrance for the same, through decades, as fondly as we carry My Dear Kuttichathan.

cast_names: Eddie Murphy, Kevin Hart, Jamie Foxx, Pete Davidson, John Landis, Chris Rock, Jerry Bruckheimer, Jerry Seinfeld, Dave Chappelle, Kenan Thompson
review_content: It's a very upbeat documentary. That’s both the good and the bad.    I appreciated his insight into growing up and the way he spoke about his father and stepfather. I also liked that Charlie Murphy was featured so much, giving their relationship real presence in the film. Eddie talked about the drug use in his industry and admitted he didn’t try weed until his 30s, which made it even more impressive that he managed to stay grounded and succeed both professionally and personally. The documentary also touches on how Black actors and comedians were often pitted against each other with the mindset that "there can only be one." Eddie’s ability to expand his career into so many genres helped break that pattern and opened the door for future actors of color to do the same.    On the other hand, anything that wasn’t positive was mentioned very briefly, and Eddie didn’t offer much deeper reflection. He talks about seeing John Belushi doing drugs but never gives an opinion on it, and he doesn’t touch on Belushi’s overdose. The same thing happened when other comedians shared their own drug struggles. The documentary includes their comments, but Eddie never explains why he avoided those paths or whether he has any thoughts about seeing the struggles other comedians went through.    As mentioned above, Charlie is heavily featured for the first two-thirds of the film. There is one moment where Eddie says, “I miss my brother,” but he doesn’t expand on that loss or talk about how it affected him. I assumed that might have been too sensitive for him to get into.    The problem is, he doesn't seem to want to discuss anything negative about his career. He doesn’t go into how he handled his box office failures. He jokes about “never doing a movie where you turn into a spaceship,” a reference to Meet Dave, but this film doesn’t really examine that or any of his other poorly reviewed movies in any real way. This made the documentary feel more like "The Best Parts of Eddie’s Life" instead of a complete picture.    Additionally he talks a bit about struggling to find an authentic romantic connection. He even mentions breaking up with a girlfriend while on tour. Then the film suddenly jumps to him being married with multiple kids, without explaining how he got from one place to the other. There’s no sense of the journey, the missteps or sadness of failed relationships, or what he learned along the way. It becomes “look, I’m happy now,” without the story that makes that ending feel earned.    Bottom line, it’s a feel-good documentary about Eddie Murphy’s life that glosses over the tougher parts, but still leaves you with the message that you can succeed and avoid the pitfalls and vices around you.

cast_names: Eddie Murphy, Kevin Hart, Jamie Foxx, Pete Davidson, John Landis, Chris Rock, Jerry Bruckheimer, Jerry Seinfeld, Dave Chappelle, Kenan Thompson
review_content: Interesting peak behind the curtain.  Let's get the elephant in the room out of the way right now... His career was a supernova in the 80s, trailed into insignificance in the mid 90s and only brief flashes have been seen since.  And it's something of a time capsule because of that. The clips they show were genuinely funny at the time. In this, with all context stripped, they're hit and miss.  What the documentary really shows you is a man at peace with himself. At peace with his role as a father. Wealthy enough to not need 'ambition'. And clean-living enough to look deceptively younger than he is... Which was my takeaway from this - he's too old to expect him to shine brightly again. He doesn't need it and he clearly doesn't want it. He still thinks the whole world will recognise Bill Cosby and Richard Pryor impressions...!   Ironically he mentions Stallone. A man who has never lost the ambition and hunger to stay on top of his game. That's the kind of ambition Eddie would need and he clearly doesn't care for it.  I have the sneaky feeling that the recent activity (Coming 2 America, this docu) is about the most we'll get from Eddie now. I think it was just enough to slate his thirst for the old life - and enough to show him he doesn't need it.

cast_names: Yolanda Ramos, Berta Castañé, Sara Vidorreta, Claudia Roset, Martí Cordero, María Esteve, Roger Padilla, Pere Brasó, David Ramírez, Jordi Llovet
review_content: Honestly, I was expecting something much worse. With a title like that, and knowing it was a Prime Video production, the most logical thing was to fear a tasteless teen mess. But End-of-School Trip: Mallorca, while far from good, is at least watchable. That doesn’t mean it escapes all the typical flaws of this kind of film: weak script, poorly developed characters, uneven pacing, and a final message that tries to go deep but stays shallow.    The funny thing is that the movie is based on a real event that made headlines at the time: the COVID outbreak in Mallorca in 2021 that led to a hotel quarantine for a bunch of students. From there, Paco Caballero tries to mix raunchy comedy, social commentary, and generational drama, but he loses control of almost everything. At times, it wants to be Project X, other times Lord of the Flies, and then Good Morning Spain, with a parody that, to be fair, works quite well.    Yolanda Ramos, as always, is the highlight. Just seeing her elevates whatever she's in, although she appears far too little here. The rest of the cast is uneven. Berta Castañé has a decent moment or two, but her character takes so long to explode that by the time she does, we’ve already checked out. The rest of the kids seem pulled from a TikTok casting call, blending apathy and overacting in equal measure.    The worst part isn’t even that. It’s the editing, which makes everything feel disjointed, and that forced attempt at a deep message about generational divide and misunderstood youth. The problem isn’t the idea itself, but how it’s handled—clumsy and one-dimensional, as if everyone over 30 were a soulless boomer and the kids were helpless victims of a system crushing them as they pop pills by the pool. Subtle, it is not.    Still, there are a few redeemable moments. The critique of certain media outlets, the chaotic vibe that fits the post-pandemic anxiety, and a couple of scenes with a bit of spark. But it’s not enough. It feels like the film doesn’t know if it wants us to laugh, think, or relate to characters who just aren’t believable. If it had committed to one clear tone, it could’ve been better. Not a great film, but maybe a more honest one.

movie_title: Latin Blood – The Ballad of Ney Matogrosso
cast_names: Jesuíta Barbosa, Rômulo Braga, Hermila Guedes, Bruno Montaleone, Mauro Soares, Bruno Parmera, Jef Lyrio, Danilo Grangheia, Artur Volpi, Caroline Abras
review_content: [Netflix] A visceral biopic about achieving freedom by devouring life and remaining steadfastly true to one's ideals, it emerges as a celebration of perpetual rebellion, of being oneself, and of exploring the possibilities of reclaiming identity from a public platform. It's a beautiful story built upon paternal repression, but one that doesn't oversimplify personal relationships; quite the opposite. Ney Matogrosso is a moving force who had the privilege of also being a survivor in every sense, and the film adopts his visceral energy to craft his portrait.

movie_title: Latin Blood – The Ballad of Ney Matogrosso
cast_names: Jesuíta Barbosa, Rômulo Braga, Hermila Guedes, Bruno Montaleone, Mauro Soares, Bruno Parmera, Jef Lyrio, Danilo Grangheia, Artur Volpi, Caroline Abras
review_content: This film is what Bohemian Rhapsody should be.  An amazing trip on the life of one of Brazilian most popular singers, an LGBTQ Icon that show us that bee truly yourself sometimes is more difficult that it seems, the price of fame and a life that always feel very empty until you realized there’s more than just money.  A movie that resembles the importances of fighting for what you care, to show that love is everywhere and that life is full of choices, there’s never a correct path there’s always going to be difficulties but in the end, it will be worth it. Hard, cruel and with no intention of hiding all the bad and good decisions this artist make in life; a very impressive way to show the life and career of Ney Mattogrosso  Latin Blook is one of the best movies of the year.

review_content: I truly loved this film. Pereira doesn’t just play Ney — he becomes Ney. His performance is so intense that his gaze stays with you from beginning to end. It’s impossible not to get drawn in. Everyone in the film is absolutely gorgeous, and there’s a certain beauty in both the cast and the cinematography — it feels like every scene was crafted like a painting. It’s been a while since a biographical film moved me this much.

cast_names: Jessica Clement, Ryan Robbins, Summer H. Howell, Keegan Connor Tracy, Matty Finochio, Max Christensen, Ben Cockell, David Feehan, Bryn Samuel, Savannah Miller
review_content: Night of the Reaper was a really pleasant surprise—a random discovery on Shudder that ended up being far better than expected. The plot is clever and engaging, mixing classic slasher elements with a few creative twists that keep things interesting. It doesn’t shy away from brutality either, with some genuinely gnarly moments that horror fans will appreciate.    Sure, it has its flaws—some uneven pacing and a bit of camp that might not work for everyone—but that’s part of its charm. It feels like a throwback to old-school horror, the kind of movie you stumble upon late at night and end up loving for its imperfections.    Rating: 3/5 – 75% – Worth Watching

cast_names: Jessica Clement, Ryan Robbins, Summer H. Howell, Keegan Connor Tracy, Matty Finochio, Max Christensen, Ben Cockell, David Feehan, Bryn Samuel, Savannah Miller
review_content: There’s a distinct B-tier charm running through Night of the Reaper; a cozy, fall-themed thriller that’s as endearing in its limited scope and budget as it is impressive in execution. Familiar tropes, nods to legendary horror directors, consistent VHS static and a fun whodunit story thread all contribute to Night of the Reaper's comfy, cozy Halloween vibe that's perfect for a spooky season horror watch-list. Set your expectations accordingly and you’ll find much to enjoy in this surprisingly fun Shudder flick.

cast_names: Jessica Clement, Ryan Robbins, Summer H. Howell, Keegan Connor Tracy, Matty Finochio, Max Christensen, Ben Cockell, David Feehan, Bryn Samuel, Savannah Miller
review_content: It wears its influences on its sleeves, from its late 80s setting to the Reaper’s mask, but it never finds the cool or wit of the 90s slashers it worships. The film runs two tracks, a babysitter guarding a cop’s son and the cop himself chasing the masked killer responsible for his wife’s death. They feel like scenes from different films until the stories finally twist together.    Half Scream, half Seven, the movie’s ambition is clear and when it works, it works well. The atmosphere is moody, the lighting slick and the score brooding. You can feel the filmmakers’ love for the genre in every shot. But for all its polish, the pacing stumbles. The babysitter thread hits predictable beats while the cop’s detective arc reaches too far into noir. The final act does pull a few neat turns that subvert the usual formula, though it never quite sticks the landing.    It’s a respectable entry into the modern slasher revival, entertaining, stylish and confidently made, but not one that will linger long after the credits. Worth a watch, forgettable by next Halloween.    If you liked… The Black Phone, I Know What You Did Last Summer, Seven.

review_content: Marshmallow has a genuinely solid idea at its core: a slasher that knowingly wears an ’80s/early-’90s summer camp skin to lull you into familiar rhythms before pulling the rug out from under the genre entirely. I saw a twist coming, but not that twist, and I stayed engaged with the mystery the whole way. The production design does a lot of heavy lifting here, especially the contrast between the foggy, nostalgic camp aesthetic and the harsh, clinical tech spaces that arrive later. On a conceptual level, the fake-out works.    Unfortunately, the execution can’t quite keep up with the ambition. Acting is uneven (kid performances are tough, and it shows), the budget strain becomes obvious once the film shifts into high-tech territory, and the reliance on tasers over real danger drains tension fast. Characters are mostly tropes without much personality, and the dialogue is functional but forgettable, missing the wit and texture that made classic slashers fun to hang out in. There’s a better version of this movie buried in here, but it never fully escapes its limitations. Grade: C+.

cast_names: Pierson Fodé, Corbin Bernsen, Giorgia Whigham, Alysia Reiner, Paul Soter, Miya Cech, Kue Lawrence, Amanda Clayton, Jordyn Raya James, Kai Cech
review_content: Marshmallow pulls you into a classic summer camp setup: eerie woods, oddball counsellors, a kid hiding grief behind a quiet stare. It layers on the weird slowly. There’s a hazmat-suited figure, strange dreams, and a synth score humming underneath. It feels like it’s building to something big.    And then it just stops.    The young cast are solid, especially Kue Lawrence as Morgan, and the mood is nailed. It teases mystery, hints at sci-fi, and explores grief, memory, and being ignored. You’re in it. But when the reveal lands — the one it’s clearly been keeping up its sleeve — it feels like we’ve finally hit the good bit. Then the credits roll.    That’s the real letdown. The twist is smart, but it should have been the film, not just the ending. There was a brilliant movie hiding here. They just cut to black before letting it breathe.

A must watch. Appreciation for Gulshan Devaiah, so good to see him in a full fledged role after some time and he was fabulous, you hate him, you laugh at him and everything he was made for.

review_content: It plays like a fairly standard sequel for most of its runtime — bigger scale, bigger setup, but the story stays pretty average until those last 30 minutes hit. That shocking reveal changes the entire direction of the film and instantly lifts everything to a much higher level.    Rishabh Shetty absolutely carries the ending. It’s one of his strongest performances, and it gives the film the weight it needed.    Now the only hard part is waiting for the next installment. Hopefully they don’t make us wait as long as last time.

cast_names: Rishab Shetty, Rukmini Vasanth, Jayaram, Gulshan Devaiah, Achyuth Kumar
review_content: Strong BGM, Solid Performances, but Not the Same Magic  Kantara: Chapter 1 is a good film that looks and sounds incredible, but it doesn’t quite carry the same spark as the 2022 original. The background score is outstanding and adds so much to the atmosphere, while Rishab Shetty once again gives a brilliant performance that anchors the whole movie.  Where it falls a little short is in balance, the action sequences feel a bit overdone, and the divine element, while powerful, is used so frequently that it loses some of the awe it had in the first film. The villains also don’t hold much weight, which makes the conflict feel less intense than it should.  That said, it’s still an enjoyable watch with some fantastic moments, and I’ll be there for the next installment, even if I’m not as hyped as I was for this prequel. I'd give it a 7/10.

review_content: in James has made a career out of a bumbling everyman persona, the average Joe caught in the mercy of those more charismatic, intelligent, or malicious than he is. His newest, “Solo Mio,” a Kinnane Brothers production, is a departure in genre, but finds him in no more control, at least to start.  Matt Taylor (Kevin James) is an elementary school art instructor who has found The One in fellow teacher Heather (Julie Ann Emery). After a sweet in-class engagement, the couple jets off to Rome to tie the knot. But Heather leaves Matt stranded at the altar. Now, their wedding and honeymoon in disrepair, Matt must make the most of his unexpectedly solo trip. Booked on duo excursions, like tandem bicycling and romantic candlelit dinners, Matt’s lonesome attendance draws the eyes of fellow honeymooners.

review_content: I was already not liking how the plot was executed, and then they did that ending. Like what?? Having a character like Héctor who is both charming and an inadvertent sexist is super interesting, but the shifts were too abrupt and his behaviour in the future too extreme. He literally goes "women, right?" at one point, leaning into the exaggerated and comical. Also having him be completely isolated in the future while Nora has a bunch of family and several professional allies also felt too dramatic in my opinion. While I liked the broader social commentary and the performance of the lead actors was quite solid, all this would have been enough to give the film a low rating already. But then they decided to have Nora live out her professional career in the future and return to her husband in the more recent past??? All of the back and forth between them just made that sweet 60s era romance of theirs fade into the background, especially with at that point having them spend half their life apart. It just completely broke Nora's character arc as she literally goes backwards. Also things like paradoxes are never properly discussed. A cool idea but executed absolutely abysmally.

review_content: The Twits is as weird as it is brave —a grotesque, over-the-top fable that mixes dark humor, wild animation, and a hidden message about empathy. Visually, it’s a beautiful mess: dirty, loud, and chaotic, but somehow it works.  Still, it goes a bit too far at times. The pacing feels uneven, and the humor can get too gross even for those who enjoy the bizarre. It’s got heart, but it lacks balance.  In short: different, sometimes fun, and with more soul than it lets on, but it never quite shines as much as it could. My rating: 6/10.

review_content: Two boys meet at school when very young and start a lifelong competition in the 100 meters race.  Along the way they meet older runners that give them advice on running and the philosophy of life.  But the older runners are just as confused as the main protagonists, so their life advice can come across as incoherent or vapid, and is really nothing more than a temporary impression gained from what is going on in their own lives.    Surprisingly, those vapid, narcissistic, scatter-brained, and at times surreal pieces of advice from the older runners were the most interesting for me: older people trying to distill their life experience into pearls of meaning, and then trying to pass the wisdom that they glimpsed onto the younger generation.  But their wisdom really was mostly empty streams of consciousness --- except for the #1 runner who retired at the very end (and who may have thrown his last race): being constantly alone as the #1, without peer, is just as lonely as being alone in last place.  May The Lord give us all companionship in our worldly trials.

review_content: There is a scene halfway through 100 Meters that had my jaw on the floor. The beautiful animation, the rotoscoping, simulated camera movements that create the essence of a single take, the way the colors blend together, the epic score building the tension, and that rain in the aftermath. It's a staggering sequence. This movie has multiple moments where the animation is unique but deeply immersive. The running sequences are the real highlight, with the way that the styles change and shift, set against an incredible score. I love the way that the movie subverts sports stereotypes with what it chooses to focus on. There's a race in the movie where the camera is positioned just looking up at the character in the ready position, and after the start, it never shifts away, continuing to look up at the sky as the results of the race are filtered in through the audio. These are the best moments of the movie, showcasing a flair of creativity and style that creates a unique identity throughout. The problem are the moments in between these sequences. They are considerably slower, and I struggled to fully resonate with what they were trying to do. I like that this movie doesn't feel like the stereotypical sports story with lots of cliches. I like that it tries to focus on what motivates these characters to run. However, I could never get a good grasp on the characters. I felt like they did not have enough time to interact, and the movie jumps in time and has side characters come and go, giving them ample screentime but not giving us enough to care about their arcs. Throughout this movie, I was reminded of a non-anime live action movie called Rush. It was not about runners but still, I think it captured the unique dynamic between two different characters who were constantly pushing each other. I think that movie handled its characters and themes better than this one, and the comparison left me wanting more from this movie when it was not in one of those beautiful animation moments. It's worth watching, but once it ended, I was left thinking about what it could have been instead of what it was. Still, when that music starts and the animation shifts, the runners line up and the camera starts jerking around as if someone is actually holding it, it is astonishing.

movie_title: Aileen: Queen of the Serial Killers
cast_names: Aileen Wuornos, Michele Gillen
review_content: Watching this documentary honestly made my blood boil. It really shows how broken and cruel the system can be. Aileen Wuornos was clearly mentally unstable — anyone could see that — yet no one cared enough to help her or even notice. During her first murder trial, she was trembling so badly while trying to tell her story, and it was painful to watch.  What made it worse was John Tanner’s disgusting attitude — the way he talked as if it was okay for a prostitute to be raped because “that’s their job.” He didn’t even listen to her, not for a second. It was sickening.  And the part where they suddenly changed the chief judge halfway through the case? That was beyond suspicious. It honestly felt like they were determined to make sure Aileen ended up dead, no matter what.  The most heartbreaking thing is that later, they actually confirmed Richard Mallory — the man from her first murder — was a violent rapist. That alone proves her first charge, the one that got her sentenced to death, was never justified. It’s just tragic how the system failed her so completely.

cast_names: Jenna Ortega, Percy Hynes White, Adam Rodriguez, Marisol Nichols, Elias Kacavas, Corynn Treadwell, Alexis Zollicoffer, Bridget Oberlin, B Z Cullins, Larkin Bell
review_content: Coming right after a run of recent romantic dramas like The Map That Leads to You, it’s easy to see how Winter Spring Summer or Fall fits into that same category of formulaic, heartfelt storytelling. It carries the same tone and structure, with its mix of romance, sentimentality, and familiar beats, making it feel right at home alongside its contemporaries.    That said, it comes across as a little less engaging than The Map That Leads to You—not drastically, but just enough to notice. Still, it has its charm and will likely land well with fans of the genre. It does what it sets out to do, even if it doesn’t stand apart.    Rating: 2.5/5 – 70% – Worth Watching

movie_title: One Last Adventure: The Making of Stranger Things 5
cast_names: Ross Duffer, Matt Duffer, Paul Dichter, Kate Trefry, Caitlin Schneiderhan, Shawn Levy, Lampton Enochs, Chris Trujillo, Jess Royal, Amy Parris
review_content: Decided to check in on this to see how exactly they fumbled this season so bad. It's so funny that they immediately said that happens to _other_ shows. The lack of self-awareness is evident here and outside on the Duffer's press run they went on.    Hope, the teacher really said it best; the Duffers are not afraid of failing, and take big swings. I think they took too much of a swing in Season 5. They also state that they don't have time to write, but that would negate the whole year between the release of Season 4 and the production of Season 5.    The way they are shown to direct demonstrates their good intentions, but only half-thinking through on things. Like talking about how they don't kill anyone off to maintain the fun balance. But they forget that Harry Potter grew with the kids and worked excellent, this needed to happen here I think. Sure it got more gory, but it didn't raise the stakes any higher aside superficial ones.     Having said that, pretty much all the other content here in the documentary is interesting and is easily better than the season in terms of feeling rewarding. They dive well into the production and I found it very insightful, and even inspiring. Some wholesome moments, and some very obvious fake sappy stuff too. Shawn Levy I think is a true fan of the story, he always looks and sounds so happy to be there,    I think this doco is pretty truthful. It clearly shows that other members of the project expressed concerns over some of the main gripes us as audiences have, and even how they also note the lack of script direction because it was unfinished really did not help set designers and the like. But the Duffers stuck to their gut, like they said they do. The actors also show that they are more in-tune with the lore than the big Ds. Seemingly trying to ask if their theory of the backstory is correct, and to compliment it; only to be met with them saying "yeah i like that way of looking at it". It must have felt like when the fans got gut punched for their theories when they admitted irreverence on the press tour.    Well put-together and the only criticism with the documentary really is for a show of this scale, a 2 hour runtime is a bit of a let down. No post-production insight at all? It feels again like another oversight in planning.    **7/10**

movie_title: One Last Adventure: The Making of Stranger Things 5
cast_names: Ross Duffer, Matt Duffer, Paul Dichter, Kate Trefry, Caitlin Schneiderhan, Shawn Levy, Lampton Enochs, Chris Trujillo, Jess Royal, Amy Parris
review_content: Behind the scenes are always fun to watch. It’s interesting to see how the magic is made. For massive fans who have already seen behind the scenes over the years, this documentary may feel repetitive. It revisits core elements of the show and Easter eggs that have already been widely shared and known by the fandom.   That said, I wouldn’t call it a waste. Beyond watching the Duffer brothers contradict their recent interviews and unintentionally expose how unorganized their writing process was, the documentary does highlight important elements that shaped the final season. One of the most entertaining moments was watching the writers openly disagree with the Duffers… an interaction that made it clear the brothers were aware of the criticism coming their way, yet chose to ignore it anyways. They ultimately bit off more than they could chew and struggled to stick the landing with this ending when it comes to the storytelling and lore being consistent and coherent.   The real joy comes from the cast. Watching them bond behind the scenes, offer input on their characters, and read through scripts together was genuinely amazing. The cast is what made me fall in love with this show in the first place, and seeing everyone present again was easily the documentary’s best aspect.  Overall, it’s an arguably phenomenal documentary for viewers who have never seen behind the scenes content for the show. But for longtime fans who already know the lore, I’d probably skip it. There isn’t much that feels new, and the Duffer Brothers can be frustrating to watch for anyone invested in storytelling. I wish they had included more cast focused moments and extended the script reads, but at this point, I’ve come to expect the Duffers to fall short.

review_content: It's based on a book, and you can tell. The level of absurdity behind nearly every scene and metaphor... I can't tell if my interpretations are delusional, daft, or both. Don't watch this if you're expecting a surface-level story with explicit answers. As much as certain themes like identity, morality, race, culture, grief, and so on are picked up on, almost nothing is explicitly explained. I've not seen much of Hawkins, but I hope to see more of him, and Dafoe, as always, does it justice. The sound design and directing almost achieve 'horror', but I'd call it more thriller. All in all, it's alright but could've been better.

He kept going on about how he wanted Dafoe's character gone, but why? He was always just sitting there chilling, yet every day "Charles" got more and more agitated for absolutely no reason. At every passing moment "Charles" become increasingly annoying, and it kinda sours the whole movie. The pointless ending didn't help either.    [spoiler]I also became irrationally annoyed by the actors never pretending like the masks were heavy. I mean, weren't they supposedly made of gold??? Those big things should be insanely heavy, but they're just holding them between two fingers 🙃... And yes, like I said it is an _irrational_ annoyance, I know. It's a movie, who cares.[/spoiler]

Thought-provoking, yes—but not as gripping or cohesive as it could have been.

review_content: Killer Whale (2026) unfortunately falls far short of expectations, delivering a disappointing and lackluster experience. Despite the intriguing premise of a predator-themed thriller, the movie struggles with a weak and predictable plot that fails to engage or surprise. The characters are underdeveloped and lack depth, making it difficult to care about their fates.    The pacing is uneven, dragging in parts and rushing through crucial moments in others, which disrupts the overall flow. Visually, while there are occasional glimpses of effective cinematography, the film does not capitalize on its potential to create a truly immersive atmosphere.    Overall, Killer Whale feels like a missed opportunity, with uninspired storytelling and forgettable performances that leave little impact. It might only appeal to the most dedicated fans of the genre, but for most viewers, it’s a frustrating watch.

review_content: Forget that this is marketed as a horror movie. It is not. But what it is is an allegory for life. Essentially, four young people get on a road that seems to never end and where they cannot turn back. Also their car never seems to run out of gas. But when thy stop, they get attacked. What to do? Go on forever? Stop and give up? While this basic idea is kinda interesting if you watch it with those eyes, it also becomes repetitive and a bit boring after a while. Not a terrible movie, especially for a low budget movie. I just do not get why the market it to the wrong audience, thus drawing the right audience away from it.

review_content: I've been wanting to see this movie ever since I missed it at the Overlook Film Festival in April 2025. Today I walk away unsatisfied.     Describing the movie as "two-lane hellscape surrounded by untold horrors and cosmic forces" is completely misleading. There's nothing like that. It's not horror, either. There's one jump scare early on, but to be clear there are no other creatures or forces in the movie. It's just four people driving through the woods. That's the point. If the movie weren't billed as horror, it would have been a different experience. It's just an existential drama focused on four Gen Z characters immediately post-college. That's not me by about 20 years. I think it will speak differently to people based on where they are in their lives.

cast_names: Michael Herbig, Christian Tramitz, Rick Kavanian, Jasmin Schwiers, Merlin Sandmeyer, Friedrich Mücke, Jessica Schwarz, Sky du Mont, Tutty Tran, Daniel Zillmann
review_content: I went to see this movie mainly because of my wife. Personally, it didn’t really hit my sense of humor, although I did find myself chuckling here and there. That’s not because the jokes were “unacceptable” in today’s climate, but simply because this style of comedy has never truly been my thing — even Der Schuh des Manitu didn’t do much for me back in the day.  That being said, I have to give Bully credit where it’s due: he had the courage to tell his jokes in his own way and didn’t fall into the trap of watering everything down for fear of backlash. In a time where so many productions — looking at you, Disney — often feel the need to overcorrect and smooth out any edge, it was refreshing to see a filmmaker stand firmly by his comedic style.  So while the film didn’t fully work for me, I can still appreciate the boldness behind it, and I understand why it will resonate more strongly with audiences who are more in tune with Bully’s particular brand of humor.

cast_names: Michael Herbig, Christian Tramitz, Rick Kavanian, Jasmin Schwiers, Merlin Sandmeyer, Friedrich Mücke, Jessica Schwarz, Sky du Mont, Tutty Tran, Daniel Zillmann
review_content: "Manitou's Shoe" from 2001 is still one of the most successful German films of all time. And how I laughed as an 11-year-old at the movies back then. Michael “Bully” Herbig’s humor was still reasonably fresh, and as a parody of the Karl May film adaptations of the 1960s, the whole thing worked pretty well, too.     Nevertheless, the decision to release a sequel, “Das Kanu des Manitu,” 24 years later comes as something of a surprise. Bully's humor hasn't really evolved since then, and I seriously doubt that it will appeal to a younger generation. But I must admit that I did find myself chuckling a few times. Sure, the jokes still revolve mainly around unoriginal puns and “funny” accents and dialects, but I guess there wasn't much more to expect than this kind of nostalgia. And at least the film is well-crafted.    Overall, I didn't really need “Das Kanu des Manitu,” especially since the story isn't exactly great. But honestly, I had feared a much worse film. Fans of the first movie will probably not regret going to see it.

review_content: _Silent Night, Deadly Night_ is a mediocre and rather bizarre remake of the ‘80s cult horror film. The story follows a serial killer named Billy who hears a voice directing him to kill people who he senses are evil.  It’s clearly ripping off _Dexter_; with the whole serial killer who kills killers thing and the Dark Passenger that guides him in his kills. However, the writer is weak; offering little as to who or what the voice is and how it knowns what it knows. And there’s no setup to Billy’s kills, which makes them rather empty and meaningless. While it delivers a few thrills and tries to do some interesting things with the material, overall _Silent Night, Deadly Night_ is a poorly made film that doesn’t work.

cast_names: Rohan Campbell, Ruby Modine, Mark Acheson, David Lawrence Brown, David Tomlinson, Erik Athavale, Rick Skene, James Durham, Sharon Bajer, Tom Young
review_content: The remake of the 1984 film “Silent Night, Deadly Night” does not achieve the anarchic quality of the original movie. Although some characters and story elements have been retained, the new version is a completely different kind of film. This starts with the main character, Billy. In the original, he was a deeply disturbed guy who freaks out at the sight of Santa Claus and sees only naughty people everywhere. The new Billy seems a bit more collected. He is actually more of a mixture of Dexter and Venom (!?) - a kind of antihero who sends really evil people to hell.    You could certainly accuse the reboot of taking itself a little too seriously, which detracts from the fun factor. And especially at the beginning, the film takes a little too long to get going. But once Billy gets started, it somehow works. In terms of violence, it does have its moments, but it is also not extremely violent. Anyway, by the time the song “Santa's Watching” from the original was reused, I was even having some fun.     All in all, the new “Silent Night, Deadly Night” is a solid Christmas horror flick that's worth giving a chance. Above all, it's an interesting approach to a remake, even if it doesn't reinvent the wheel. I give it a mild recommendation.

review_content: I always appreciate a rom-com that isn’t afraid to throw a few twists into the usual formula, and The Threesome manages to do exactly that. The setup feels familiar at first, but the story finds ways to stay funny and surprisingly unpredictable compared to many of its genre counterparts.  The humor lands more often than not, and the pacing keeps things moving in a way that makes it an easy, enjoyable watch. It doesn’t reinvent the rom-com genre, but it adds just enough variation to keep things fresh.  And once again, Zoey Deutch proves why she’s so easy to root for on screen. She continues to shine in roles like this, bringing charm and comedic timing that elevate the material.  Rating: 3.5/5 – 80% – Would Recommend

cast_names: Zoey Deutch, Jonah Hauer-King, Ruby Cruz, Jaboukie Young-White, Josh Segarra, Robert Longstreet, Arden Myrin, Kristin Slaysman, Allan McLeod, Julia Sweeney
review_content: _The Threesome_ is one of those films that’s surprisingly deeper than its playful title suggests. While on the surface it revolves around three people trying to navigate romantic and emotional boundaries, the story digs into something more meaningful — identity, connection, and the tricky, messy space between friendship and desire.    The chemistry between the leads is what truly holds the movie together. Every glance, every awkward pause, every moment of tension feels real. You can feel the push and pull between attraction and self-preservation. It captures that strange moment in life where you’re still trying to figure out who you are, while also trying not to lose the relationships that make you feel grounded.    The film balances humor with heartfelt vulnerability. There are some genuinely funny, relatable moments, but also scenes that hit harder than expected — the kind that leave you thinking long after the credits. The writing is thoughtful, and the emotional beats feel honest rather than forced.    If you’ve ever found yourself tangled in feelings you don’t have a simple name for, The Threesome will resonate. It’s bold, a little messy, and very human — just like real relationships tend to be.

review_content: This was definitely not the movie I was sitting down to watch but it was good nonetheless. One big reason this isn't rated higher for me (despite one of my fave actresses being the female lead) is because the whole self-sabotage thing is such a writing cop-out. It just excuses away shitty behavior as a way to emotionally move the plot along. It's the same reason I don't like movies/shows where the main characters have diseases like multiple personality disorder or other psychoses (looking at you Mr. Robot). It's just too easy to bail the plot out with "oh we will just write this in as he was having an episode" rather than finding a creative way to actually move the plot. For as good as this movie was, it could've been so much better without her constant self-sabotage despite the fact that it's clear she is in love with Connor from the 1st minute of the movie. There was enough drama with his predicament without the need for her to kick him emotionally so many times. But the acting was extremely good and the actress who played Jenny did an excellent job.

review_content: Tinsel Town aims for a glittery, meta‑Hollywood satire but lands closer to a disjointed scrapbook of half‑formed ideas. It’s the kind of movie that clearly wants to say something about fame, image, and the machinery of celebrity culture, yet never finds the narrative discipline or tonal confidence to make any of it resonate.      🎭 What Works (Occasionally)        A few clever visual gags: The film sprinkles in moments of stylistic flair—neon‑washed sets, exaggerated costume design, and playful transitions—that hint at a sharper, more self‑aware movie beneath the surface.        Committed performances: Some actors genuinely try to elevate the material, injecting energy into scenes that otherwise feel flat.    🎬 Where It Falls Apart        A muddled script: The story jumps between satire, melodrama, and quirky comedy without ever choosing a lane. Scenes feel stitched together rather than organically connected.        Shallow commentary: The film gestures at critiques of Hollywood excess, influencer culture, and media obsession, but never digs deeper than surface‑level clichés.        Tonally inconsistent: Emotional beats arrive without setup, comedic moments undercut dramatic ones, and the pacing swings wildly from sluggish to frantic.        Lack of character depth: Most characters feel like archetypes rather than people. Their motivations shift scene to scene, making it hard to invest in their journeys.    🎞 Overall    Tinsel Town is a glossy but hollow experience—ambitious in concept, clumsy in execution. It’s the cinematic equivalent of a flashy billboard: eye‑catching at first glance, but ultimately empty once you get close. A few bright spots keep it from being a total misfire, but the film never finds its voice or purpose.    A scattered, unfocused satire that sparkles briefly but fades fast.

review_content: Bullying amongst children is certainly an issue worthy to be addressed … but not like this. Apart from one or two scenes where the issue is reasonably discussed, the rest of the movie is mostly clichés and unfinished ideas and too long filler scenes that seem to be inserted in order to meet the agreed runtime. To me the most striking aspect of the movie is the apparently complete lack of education of the kids by the parents and the teachers about bullying, such that only one and a half children do show understanding and opposition to it. Then there is also apparently near to no adult supervision and awareness in American children’s camps. They go out on their own as they please and there are no consequences when caught. But most strikingly, the supervising persons do apparently not send a child with an obviously highly contagious skin condition home, they do not see the bulling or decide (mostly) not to intervene and the children can do whatever they please in the dormitory or in the changing rooms without anyone noticing. To me, the writer an director does not really know what he is talking about or how to put it into pictures. Good subject, bad implementation.

review_content: Enemies starts from a powerful idea: two lives at odds that, forced by circumstances, must reconsider their hatred. David Valero builds a drama that aims to move and make you reflect, with a neighborhood setting that feels close and recognizable.    The film has strong moments, especially when it lets silence, gestures, and those looks full of resentment and pain speak for themselves. That’s where the honesty of the story shows, when it doesn’t need to underline everything. However, at times it falls back on devices clearly designed to force an emotional response, losing some of the naturalness found in the best films of this type.    It doesn’t reach the rawness or authenticity of Daniel Guzmán’s works, which have elevated neighborhood cinema to another level. Here the story works, but it lacks that punch of truth that stays with you long after leaving the theater.    Even so, it’s an interesting film, with a strong soundtrack and direction that shows promise. Perhaps uneven, but sincere enough to make it worth watching.

review_content: Rosario (2025), directed by Felipe Vargas, is a chilling yet uneven horror debut that thrives on Emeraude Toubia’s commanding performance. Toubia plays Rosario Fuentes, a Wall Street stockbroker forced to confront her Mexican heritage when her estranged grandmother’s death traps her in a grimy Brooklyn apartment during a snowstorm. As supernatural forces tied to the Palo Mayombe religion unleash grotesque terrors, Rosario uncovers dark family secrets and sacrifices. Toubia’s raw, vulnerable portrayal anchors the film, balancing fear, guilt, and determination as she navigates a claustrophobic nightmare. The practical effects—rotting corpses, maggots, and eerie rituals—are nauseatingly effective, and the production design crafts a suffocating atmosphere. However, Alan Trezza’s script falters with repetitive jump scares and an overstuffed climax that undermines the film’s emotional depth. While it explores compelling themes of immigration, generational trauma, and cultural identity, the execution feels rushed, and the horror often leans on familiar tropes. David Dastmalchian’s quirky neighbor role adds intrigue but is underused. At 88 minutes, Rosario is a brisk, unsettling ride that doesn’t fully realize its potential but signals Vargas and Toubia as talents to watch.

cast_names: Maddie Ziegler, Lana Condor, Iris Apatow, Millicent Simmonds, Avantika, Michael Culkin, Lydia Leonard, Uma Thurman, Tamás Hagyó, Miklós Béres
review_content: Pretty Lethal is excellent at some things and absolutely terrible at others. It has some incredibly stylish action sequences with some excellent choreography. The way it blends ballet seamlessly with the fights is really fantastic, and the result is some really cool imagery. I also thought the lighting was pretty good, lending to the stylized cinematography. The problem is that all the effort of the filmmakers went towards the action, and not towards the script. The script is bad. Bad is an understatement. It is absolutely atrocious. There are multiple moments in this movie that completely defy belief. Not in the sense of film logic or just being annoyed at characters doing dumb things. As in, I cannot believe the filmmakers expect me to believe that this could be actual reality. An example is a character manages to get caught sneaking around and gets out of it by saying she was having trouble with the Wi-Fi. Sheer stupidity. It's a shame because you don't need to have the deepest plot or characters for a movie like this -- there are plenty of examples like Ready or Not that aren't exactly renowned for their story. The problem is that when there are moments like this, it completely takes you out of the movie and makes it so cringey until the next action sequence rolls around. I also think the girls don't have the type of gravitas to fully carry this movie. Maddie Ziegler as Bones is actually decent, but the rest range from okay (Lana Condor) to annoying (Avintika). I want to be able to recommend this movie because there are certain scenes that are legitimately very good, but I can't because the rest of the movie is a pain to get through.

cast_names: Maddie Ziegler, Lana Condor, Iris Apatow, Millicent Simmonds, Avantika, Michael Culkin, Lydia Leonard, Uma Thurman, Tamás Hagyó, Miklós Béres
review_content: it’s a great movie that i hope opens the door for so many women-led and women-centered action thrillers. that said:  i wish it was less centered completely on bones (and princess). i get that there needs to be a “protagonist” but i would’ve enjoyed it more if it focused on each of the 5 girls equally  i hate chloe’s clueless deaf girl personality, i just feel like seeing her sister show up covered in blood should have done SOMETHING more  it feels soooo low stakes since somehow none of their injuries affect them, especially zoe and princess’ deep gashes? i wouldve preferred even seeing them patch up wounds quickly between fighting scenes  with those critiques, i really did enjoy the movie and i’ve been waiting for it to come out. the cinematography is great and it kind of has an unsettling feel without being creepy or horrifying, really beautiful fight scenes, a nice backstory to what is supposed to be the villain and i really really love grace’s concept and execution, it’s probably the best writing they did across the whole movie and avantika really brought her to life

review_content: One could argue that the animated film “Little Amélie or the Character of Rain” tries to pack a little too many philosophical questions into what is actually a very simple story. It certainly has only an outside chance at the Oscars, but it is definitely welcome that films like this are also considered at the major award ceremonies.

review_content: ==Original French Version Watched==    I don't think I've been moved as much as this movie made me feel throughout its runtime. What an accomplishment of film-making and animation.     Beautiful story and non-stop heart in every scene.    The trials and tribulations of a 3 year old aren't something we usually think back on, as evident of the older siblings in the film. But this movie shows how important those years truly are. How integral the bonds we have early on shape us as people. How the world shapes its own people.    I have no complaints here. Only thing i think is that the length could have been slightly longer to spend on the mother and daughter dynamic a bit more. Other than that, it's breathtaking.    **9/10**

review_content: It's presumably a dark comedy  but there's nothing to laugh about.  The whole movie feels like it was written by a psychopath whoenjoys making his audience uncomfortable, especially through violence against women. If this were marketed as a trashy horror movie, it wouldn't have been so bad,but the attempt to make even the darkest and most senseless acts of cruel violence funny is really disgusting. There's no character in the movie who feels real or sympathetic; everyone is just a caricature of a bad, mentally ill person The main characters are just very annoying and frustrating.There is nothing thoughtful, interesting, or resolving in this piece of cheesy and cynical dreck.

cast_names: Mads Mikkelsen, Nikolaj Lie Kaas, Sofie Gråbøl, Søren Malling, Bodil Jørgensen, Lars Brygmann, Nicolas Bro, Lars Ranthe, Rikke Louise Andersson, Anette Støvelbæk
review_content: You'd think by this time you'd be used to how incredible Mads Mikelsen is. You'd be wrong. Even without the rest of the movie (which is very good), his performance alone is worth the watch.     He plays this guy with dissociative identity disorder. Not 100% sure it's the actual term, also not sure this is what he actually is. In the beginning he's probably just autistic (or whatever the current term is), I don't think a kid thinking he is a viking is an actual identity. Not sure people thinking they are actual famous dead people fits this either, but whatever.  When he plays it, you don't see the insanity in the character, you actually see the trauma that caused it, you see the defence mechanism, it's incredible. You never actually laugh AT Manfred, tough it would be easy, only at the situation.     Around him is a host of just as crazy characters. The brown guy obsessed by the Holocaust, in one way or the other, and who thinks he is two Beatles, Bjorn from ABBA and Himmler, is definitely second in comic power.  Even the less important new owners of their childhood house are developed and interesting. Though they're definitely weird. Who get implicated this much in other people's lives on a whim ?    The recurring gags are awesome. Manfred stealing dogs, and him jumping out of cars or windows, as soon as he's called Manfred.    The story, based on uncovering the hidden money and the brothers' childhood traumas, is also engaging. Most the trauma is being started by Anker absence, rekindling stuff repressed after the father's disappearance (though he was definitely not completely sane even before that, reason why the father was always angry at them). This explains his obsession with stealing dogs, by the death of their dog as kid. Quite the traumatic event with [spoiler]their father forcing them to get him killed to punish them[/spoiler].    The biggest reveal is not so surprising, as soon as Manfred says that Anker didn't remember the dog, it was clear [spoiler]the father was the same[/spoiler].     The side plot of bringing the Beatles back is cute but not that interesting, however this brings huge comedic power.    It is great cinema. Both moving and hilarious, engaging story and characters, trauma, drama, all wrapped up in an original idea.

cast_names: Mads Mikkelsen, Nikolaj Lie Kaas, Sofie Gråbøl, Søren Malling, Bodil Jørgensen, Lars Brygmann, Nicolas Bro, Lars Ranthe, Rikke Louise Andersson, Anette Støvelbæk
review_content: Another excellent movie from Anders Thomas Jensen. From the trailer you might get the impression that this movie is only comedy and crazy stuff - but it has a quite deep and emotional story line as well. The cast is perfect for the movie and I think it is one of the directors best ones yet - and perhaps also one that speaks to the zeitgeist.

cast_names: Stephen Graham, Andrea Riseborough, Anson Boon, Kit Rakusen, Monika Frajczyk, Savannah Steyn, Mila Jankowska, Callum Booth-Ford, Noah Manzoor, Austin Haynes
review_content: I didn't know what to expect from this movie. From beginning to end, I wasn't sure if Tommy would just murder them all, discover a series of kidnappings by the same family and save the other victims, or if he was just being indoctrinated by cult leaders of some sort. And that uncertainty kept me guessing and made the movie thrilling for me.   Still, Good Boy (originally Heel) is a film about trauma, grief, psychological abuse, literal brainwashing (or indoctrination, if you will) that resulted in Stockholm Syndrome, which made Tommy go back to the very family that abused him. Not because they were good people, but because they made him believe anything other than what they had wasn't good and wouldn't make him happy. So, when Tommy freed himself and went back home, he felt disenfranched, forgotten, and figured he'd rather have what he was given in literal captivity, where he was kept locked in chains, violently disciplined, but "cared" for to some extent.   Did I love the movie? No, but I liked it quite a lot. Aside from a few plot holes, like Rina's family having no reaction whatsoever after seeing a boy chained in the house she was working in, this was much better than I expected. And Stephen Graham was phenomenal, as usual.  Finally, jokingly, I thought: Jamie (Adolescence,  2025) wouldn't have turned out to be a red pill incel had his father raised him like this, would he?!

review_content: [Movistar+] Social cinema that at times feels like a collection of anecdotes from an immigrant, somewhat laden with stereotypes, yet manages to develop complex characters. The portrayal of different types of Spanish immigrants (Manel's cynicism) is more effective than the depiction of clichés about racism. The search for personal identity is more powerful than the portrayal of social integration in a country described with broad strokes: seriousness, rain, xenophobia... The syndrome of the Spanish immigrant who feels comfortable anywhere but also doesn't want to return to Spain. There are good moments of expressive silences, but they are embedded within the context of stereotypical social cinema.

cast_names: Parks Stephenson, Yasmin Khan
review_content: The idea behind “Titanic: The Digital Resurrection” is, on paper, pretty fascinating: to use the most advanced underwater scanning tech ever applied to a shipwreck to recreate, with near-perfect precision, the wreck of the Titanic and the massive debris field surrounding it. And honestly, on a technical level, the documentary is a milestone. The visual detail is jaw-dropping—from the first descent of the remotely operated cameras to the stunning digital flyovers of the wreckage, everything is captured with a level of realism you rarely see in this kind of production. The team at Magellan, who processed over 700,000 images across two years to build a 3D model of the ship, deserves serious props. Visually, it really does feel like we’re walking across the Titanic for the first time—not through some Hollywood dramatization, but through the real, mangled, ghostly remains sitting 3,800 meters below the surface.    But while the technical precision is impressive, the storytelling doesn’t always keep up. The documentary jumps between genuinely interesting discoveries and pretty flimsy conclusions, like it’s trying to stretch some of its findings into something groundbreaking when, really, they just confirm what we already figured out decades ago. For example, the assumption that a certain mast’s position means the crew was trying to launch another lifeboat as the deck was going under—it comes off as a bit obvious, even naive. Sure, it’s cool to see the steam valve that kept the lights on during the final moments, but that’s not new, and it doesn’t really change our understanding of anything. What’s missing is a sense of true investigative purpose. Instead of breaking new ground, the film often leans on speculative interpretations and old myths dressed up as fresh revelations.    Maybe the documentary’s biggest win—aside from the tech—is how it shows the disaster for what it really was: brutal, violent, and far from the polished, almost theatrical image we’ve gotten from films like James Cameron’s “Titanic.” “Titanic: The Digital Resurrection” gives us a shattered scene, where the ship was literally ripped in half with staggering force. The break-up theory is explored here with surgical precision, backed by solid explanations about accumulated structural stress and the cascading failure of key components. That kind of analysis, supported by modern simulations and scan data, actually contributes something meaningful to how we understand the tragedy. This is where the film really shines—not when it tries to reconstruct a crew member’s final steps based on a single detail, but when it lets the Titanic’s physical remains tell the story more than a century later.    Another strong point is the respect the film tries to maintain for the site as a mass grave. The Titanic-as-tomb idea is handled seriously, though the documentary does run into some contradictions. It wrestles with the ethical stance of “look but don’t touch” versus the archaeological urgency of preserving, studying, and maybe even retrieving artifacts before the wreck decays completely. The comparison to Pompeii is a powerful one and raises legitimate questions about what’s actually being done—or blocked—in the name of honoring the dead. The criticism that banning access to the ship’s interior is keeping important discoveries out of reach is valid and presents a moral dilemma that any future Titanic project will have to reckon with.    That said, there’s definitely something a little forced in how the film tries to tie tiny pieces of evidence to specific historical figures. Efforts to dramatize things like a bracelet or a pair of binoculars by identifying their supposed owners often feel out of place—random curiosities in a film that should be leaning harder into scientific precision than emotional pull. And at times, it tips into full-on sensationalism, like when it tries to turn every twisted piece of metal into some major clue, even when there’s just not enough to support the theory. That kind of thing distracts from the genuinely significant findings and waters down the impact of the real contributions.    All in all, “Titanic: The Digital Resurrection” is a stunning technological achievement that unfortunately stumbles when it comes to narrative structure. The quality of the 3D images is beyond question—they’re historical records that’ll stick around, especially since the Titanic itself is being eaten away by iron-eating bacteria at a terrifying pace and may vanish entirely in a few decades. But while the film dives deep into the ocean, it rarely dives as deep into the analysis. You get the feeling there was a more powerful, more insightful documentary hiding somewhere in the shadows of the wreckage and the silence of history, but the script, maybe too eager for answers or mass appeal, decided to keep things on the surface.

cast_names: Parks Stephenson, Yasmin Khan
review_content: This doc is heavy.  They used cutting-edge tech to create a full-scale 3D digital twin of the Titanic, capturing over 715,000 images to reveal unprecedented details  . It's like time-traveling to 1912 without leaving your couch.   The revelations are mind-blowing.  Turns out, the iceberg caused six small tears across watertight compartments—two more than the ship could handle—leading to its demise  . And the engineering crew?  They kept the ship powered to aid evacuation, sacrificing themselves in the process.  True heroes.   Also, First Officer William Murdoch gets his redemption.  New evidence suggests he was helping passengers until the end, not the villain portrayed in that 1997 movie  .  7/10. Not perfect, but a monumental step in preserving Titanic's legacy.

cast_names: Parks Stephenson, Yasmin Khan
review_content: Technically, it's a good documentary that goes over the key moments of the sinking, but as someone who has seen a lot of documentaries about the ship, almost everything feels redundant. The only thing this documentary really has going for it is “look at this 3D scan of the ship”, but since you can't sustain 1 hour of just that, the rest of what they show feels like extras, ones that can't hide the fact that, on its own, this documentary doesn't reveal anything that wasn't already known.    But leaving that aside, I liked it, being a good review of one of the most remembered tragedies of the 20th century, and worthwhile for anyone who wants to know more about the Titanic (although there are others that do reveal genuinely more interesting things).
