cast_names: Oscar Isaac, Kenneth Branagh, Uma Thurman, Pierce Brosnan, Roman Griffin Davis, Forest Whitaker, Ben Kingsley, Mark Hamill, Jim Cummings, Dee Bradley Baker
review_content: I approach this film in a three ways.  1)  I view it as a fan of film:  In this regard, the animation in this film is very good.  It is animation that is on par with much that is produced by Illumination studios (Despicable Me).  The voice cast is incredible.  It rivals many Disney films.  And as one would expect, the voice acting is very good.  The film score by Kim Tae-seong is also very strong.  It is quite clear that the Angel Studios is getting richer and richer.  This isn't like the films put out by Pure Flix and other similar studios.  These films actually have good quality.  Framing the film around Charles Dickens (will get to this later) telling the story to his kids is a nice creative move.  It does give one thought, "If you had an hour and a half to tell your children the story of Jesus, would you be ready?  What would you say?"  Good thought and premise.    2)  I view it as a Pastor, theologically:  It doesn't take long for this movie to have problems.  Much of the chronology and details around Jesus' birth were off.  King Herod, though voiced by Mark Hamill (didn't realize at the time), is too healthy and vibrant.  Granted, most of this, I could let go since they are product of common myths surrounding the nativity.  Jesus in the temple at the age of 12 is off.  Jesus wasn't preaching to the temple, Luke's gospel says he was asking questions.  This is different.  Couldn't help but notice his response to Mary was not what is recorded in scripture.  This wouldn't be the last time they changed scripture.  At the baptism of Jesus, the reason for John needing him to be baptized, is changed as well.  The movie is very much seeking to emphatically reject Jesus being God.  This comes out very much so after the healing of the paralytic.    The Pharisees & High Priest are portrayed like they the Dark Lords of the Sith.  Honestly, Caiaphas at times looks like Count Dooku from Star Wars: Clone Wars.  In the pivotal decision to have Jesus' crucified, why not include Caiaphas words, "It is better for one man to die for the nation, than the nation for one man."  This would not make Caiaphas into such a cartoonish villain, and would in fact echo why Jesus went to the cross.  It also loses why they wanted Jesus crucified.  This gets echoed half-way through the film where Jesus said that he gave his life as a benefit for a multitude of people.  In the scriptures, Jesus says, "Ransom" not benefit.  This is a very different statement.  The resurrection was short-cut, and it seemed like they were trying not to say he rose from the dead.  Instead emphasized more that he was alive.  And they show him walking off into the sunrise, like an old western.  This would go in line with the many so-called Christians who deny that Jesus rose from the dead, but instead believe his just alive in their hearts.    3)  I view it in comparison to Charles Dickens' work, "The Life of Our Lord"  I actually listened to this on Audible while drove home from the movie theater.  It isn't too long of a listen.  I wanted to see how it compared with the film.  Do my criticisms fall at the feet of the film-makers or Charles Dickens?  Answer:  95% at the feet of the film-makers.  5% at the feet of Charles Dickens.  There were a few of my criticisms that came straight from Charles Dickens one writing.  And much like the film starting premise, it was indeed written for his children.  Keeping in mind that Charles Dickens' the amongst the greatest authors in the history of the world, is no theologian.  Which is why I expect there to be mis-steps hear and there.  But it is also encouragement for every parent to ponder, "What would I tell me children?"  The book is better than the film and very different in spirit and tone.  The film has a strong emphasis upon Jesus as king, so much so it is even in the title of the film.  This stands in contrast to the actual title of Charles Dickens' writing.  He seemed much more fond of referring to Jesus as "Our Savior".  He had a very strong emphasis upon Jesus' works of compassion and kindness to the down-trodden.  This would be quite consistent with Dickens most famous works "A Christmas Carol" and "Tale of Two Cities".  This emphasis while somewhat present in "King of Kings" film, it isn't nearly as strong as it is in Dickens' writing for his children.  From what I can tell, Dickens' was a confessing Anglican, and therefore held to the belief that Jesus is God.  But admittedly, this isn't overly clear in his writing.  Dickens spends a good amount of time on the resurrection of Jesus, in contrast to the film which spends only a few minutes.  In Dickens' account, there is no way anyone would come away uncertain if he believed in a bodily resurrection of Jesus.  I can't say the same about the film "King of Kings".  He even writes some detail about the life of the Apostles and the events in the book of Acts.    In Conclusion, do I recommend this film:  As a fan of cinema:  Not really, good quality but when dealing with such a well-known writings such as the Bible and Charles Dickens writings, it should strive for better accuracy.    As a pastor:  Definitely not.  Because it can be somewhat engaging, and it professes a Jesus different from scripture and a different Gospel, it is actually a dangerous film.  I can not recommend this film.

cast_names: Ma Shih-Yuan, Janel Tsai, Nina Ye, Brando Huang, Akio Chen, Hsia Teng-hung, Alvin Lin, Blaire Chang, Trần Thu Liễu, Franco Chiang
review_content: Left-Handed Girl (2025) is a tender and beautifully observed film about family bonds and the daily struggles of people running small stalls in Taipei’s night markets. It quietly captures the resilience of working-class families trying to survive with dignity and love.  What begins as a warm family drama gradually unfolds into a deeper, more layered story rooted in traditional Chinese beliefs—where the left hand is considered impure or associated with the devil, a superstition that still lingers among older generations. Through this idea, the film explores how tradition and fear can shape identity and emotional wounds.  The child actor delivers a remarkably moving performance, expressing complex emotions with subtlety and grace. Paired with calm, soulful cinematography and intimate storytelling, the film creates a gentle yet lasting emotional impact.  Left-Handed Girl is the kind of film that soothes the soul—quiet, compassionate, and deeply human.

cast_names: Ma Shih-Yuan, Janel Tsai, Nina Ye, Brando Huang, Akio Chen, Hsia Teng-hung, Alvin Lin, Blaire Chang, Trần Thu Liễu, Franco Chiang
review_content: _Left-Handed Girl_ is a moody, slow-burn psychological drama that sneaks up on you instead of grabbing you by the collar. What starts as a simple character study turns into a quiet unraveling, part mystery, part emotional excavation, and the movie earns its biggest punches by whispering instead of shouting.    Atmospheric, beautifully acted, and just mysterious enough to keep your mind spinning, this film is a quiet little knockout that earns it's stars!    Ultimately, _Left-Handed Girl_ is a thoughtful, tender portrait of identity and resilience. I highly recommend this!

cast_names: Ma Shih-Yuan, Janel Tsai, Nina Ye, Brando Huang, Akio Chen, Hsia Teng-hung, Alvin Lin, Blaire Chang, Trần Thu Liễu, Franco Chiang
review_content: Left-Handed Girl snuck up on me. Throughout the movie, you see how different external forces such as tradition, social expectations, gender roles, superstitious beliefs, and economic struggles create pressure on this mother and two daughter family unit. At times, it is genuinely heartbreaking. Those three characters are far from perfect either, with all of them making mistakes throughout. Shih-Ching Tsou is constantly splicing two different scenes together against a percussive score to create tension, building this feeling that something is about to go wrong.  In some ways, this could be a very depressing movie. In fact, the movie's climax never fully feels resolved and has a sharp edge that cuts everyone. Despite all of this, I actually found the movie surprisingly beautiful. There are sequences throughout where young I-Jing weaves in and out of a bustling and glittering night market in Taipei or where I-Ann zips across streets on her moped that made me smile. There is one scene in particular where I-Ann is trying to teach I-Jing a lesson that felt so wholesome and emotional. Then, the ending completely recontextualizes everything. I think it would have been easy to have the twists and turns in this movie feel cheap, but I never felt that way. Even though we don't get a ton of time to process that ending, I think it added so much depth to what I had watched. This is a movie about so much working against this family. But in my opinion, where it really shines, and what it really is about, is how these characters are able to help each other through moments of small kindness. I think this is an understated but really thoughtful movie.

cast_names: Ma Shih-Yuan, Janel Tsai, Nina Ye, Brando Huang, Akio Chen, Hsia Teng-hung, Alvin Lin, Blaire Chang, Trần Thu Liễu, Franco Chiang
review_content: Like Romeo and Juliet with no romance: a family drama.    A mother and two daughters (one in her early 20s and a preschooler) move to Taipei, Taiwan, where the mother runs a noodle stand in the night market and everyone confronts issues, family, and each other.     Produced and co-written by Sean Baker, the movie was filmed in Taipei on an iPhone, which makes the viewer feel that they are there, in the city and on the street with these characters.    While the film is character driven with no driver, the drama, the characters, and the smell of Taipei linger after the movie has ended.

review_content: "Uh, semen. Not that I know how that tastes. But if I were to guess, I would say semen."    I was looking forward to Stans. I was hoping to hear a bit more from Eminem himself but I do like how they went with it. But let me warn you, some of these people are like Stanley from the videoclip Stan. Damn, they are cringe. Like why do you have 22 tattoos of Eminem and with a lot of them are like the face of Eminem and then you are offended that people find that weird?     And the cringiest of them all the French guy that goes by Zolt Shady. I never heard of him before but I had to google him. He knows Eminem's car. He went to Detroit to see all of the places that mean something to Eminem. He freaking stayed home for three months when Proof died, because he was in so much pain. I was so weirded out by some of these "Stans"    Anyway, Eminem is one of my favorite artists (the others being; Ozzy Osbourne, Luke Combs and Chris Stapleton) and the one I listened to the most growing up. Sadly I've only seen him once and I doubt he'll ever tour or even strike down in the Netherlands again. But seeing this documentary I'm happy I'm not that into any artists. I do love the stories that his songs helped people through tough times and I think that is the magic of music but mimicking everything about an artist goes a bit too far.    Still I enjoyed watching this and if you want to cringe out or enjoy Eminem, this is worth checking out.

review_content: The trilogy might have been ok on its own, but it's like trying to spread the Mona Lisa into three separate paintings. cast_names: Madelaine Petsch, Richard Brake, Ema Horvath, Gabriel Basso, Pedro Leandro, Rachel Shenton, George Young, Kyle Breitkopf, Stevee Davies, Hannah Galway
review_content: If you’re looking for a silver lining to the “Strangers” trilogy, you could say that the three films managed to maintain their quality. The problem is that the quality was pretty lousy to begin with. By the third installment at the latest, even the most reluctant viewer should realize that there wasn’t a single halfway interesting idea behind this trilogy. Consequently, this finale feels aimless, especially since none of the characters behave in a way that makes sense and the whole small-town setting remains completely implausible. Ultimately, there is still only one “Strangers” movie that’s truly worth watching, and that’s the first one. In my opinion, you can safely skip all the ones that came after it.

review_content: Sweeping statements of content consumption, content moderation, online censorship and the effect this never ceasing feed of doom-and-gloom-scrolling has on our own mental health are scattershot and plentiful in American Sweatshop, they just unfortunately fall a little flat when explored at this brief, surface level. That's not to deride American Sweatshop's techno-drama chops; it's still an intriguing contemporary thriller which shines a light on the guilty pleasures we all have hiding in the apps on our phone. I feel Red Rooms explored the voyeuristic nature of content consumption better and with a much more cutting tone, but American Sweatshop manages to ask more broad questions around a more grounded, relatable topic which is probably more approachable to the average viewer.

cast_names: Lili Reinhart, Daniela Melchior, Jeremy Ang Jones, Josh Whitehouse, Tim Plester, Christiane Paul, Joel Fry, Faith Delaney, Mark Ryder, Max Croes
review_content: As Lili Reinhart, portraying Daisy in *American Sweatshop*, I found the role both haunting and exhilarating. Daisy’s job as a content moderator dragged her into a psychological abyss, mirroring the real toll of sifting through digital darkness. Her obsession with a violent video pushed her to dark places—both mentally and physically—as she chased justice. The film’s climax felt like a beacon of hope, suggesting Daisy might find peace. Yet, the ambiguous ending hints she’s slipped into an even bleaker abyss, leaving me chilled. This raw, gripping story showcases the unseen scars of our online world. A must-watch!

cast_names: Lili Reinhart, Daniela Melchior, Jeremy Ang Jones, Josh Whitehouse, Tim Plester, Christiane Paul, Joel Fry, Faith Delaney, Mark Ryder, Max Croes
review_content: American Sweatshop follows Daisy Moriarty (Lili Reinhart), a content moderator at Paladin tasked with watching flagged videos and deciding what stays online. Violence, abuse, hate, it is endless, and she must endure at least 20 seconds of every clip. The job comes with “comfort rooms” and pep talks, but breakdowns are routine and therapy feels like a box ticking exercise.    The story shifts when Daisy sees a clip that feels too real: a woman screaming in possible danger. Convinced it is not staged, she launches her own investigation. What begins as scrolling at her desk spirals into late-night sleuthing, confrontations and risky choices that strain her already fragile life. The film blurs whether she is chasing justice or losing her grip.    The strongest moments come from Reinhart’s performance, her exhaustion flickering between bitter humour and hollow stares. The film captures the toxic mechanics of moderation, the ambiguity of policy, the desensitisation, the constant threat of collapse, yet it pulls back from the deepest consequences. Instead of descending fully into the darkness, it mixes mystery, satire and noir flourishes. That keeps it watchable but leaves you wishing it had dug further.    If you liked…    Severance, The Conversation, Black Mirror

cast_names: Cassandra Naud, Emily Tennant, Georgina Campbell, Lisa Delamar, Jonathan Whitesell, Veronica Long, Dylan Playfair, Osric Chau, Lewis Raymond Taylor, Nalani Wakita
review_content: I loved the first movie. The script, the pacing, CW's charm, the cast, the music-pretty much everything was great. I'm less enthusiastic about this one because I couldn't understand what the director was really trying to show.    [spoiler]  I don't like time jumps, and there are quite a few here-probably too many for my taste. CW's monologue to Jacob was exactly what I wanted to hear. Who knows why she does what she does-does it even matter? In the end, she knows she's been caught, so she just kills because she can and because she wants to. [/spoiler]    [spoiler] But rushing this part into 10-15 minutes just to "shock" the audience? I'm not convinced. I think the director did it on purpose, but it felt rushed. CW left everything wide open-her place, her computer, even her own AI turned on on her. It doesn't make sense. The framing also felt rushed: we don't really see what they did, CW gets angry and confronts them immediately, even before the "stabbing" mistake. And who accidentally stabs someone that easily? [/spoiler]    [spoiler] I really hope the director will get it right in the third movie, if there is one-which I hope there will be. These movies have a soul, but the second one is somewhat undermined by flaws and mistakes (at least in my opinion) that could have been easily fixed with an extra 15-20 minutes of footage. [/spoiler]

review_content: Having just watched the original, I knew almost exactly what I was getting with Influencers, and for the most part, that’s what this delivers—for better and for worse. It leans into the same structure and beats, but the execution is still sharp enough to stay engaging. The cinematography is genuinely gorgeous, elevating the familiar setup, and the twists and turns are effective even when you sense where things might be headed.  It doesn’t necessarily outdo its predecessor, but it doesn’t disappoint either. Gun to my head, I’m not sure which one I prefer, which is probably the best compliment I can give it. If you liked the first, this is an easy recommendation.  Rating: 3.5/5 – 80% – Would Recommend

cast_names: Louis Theroux, Itamar Ben Gvir
review_content: To make a highly inappropriate comparison: it gave me major _Borat_ vibes. It’s basically an hour of a bunch of sociopathic Zionists saying the most insane shit, with the occasional cutaway to Louis Theroux giving blasé facial expressions. It just goes to show that sometimes you don’t need to try very hard to make a compelling documentary, just let the imbeciles do the work for you. It’s deeply disturbing and unintentionally funny, I honestly respect Theroux for keeping it together in the way he did. My blood was boiling by the end, so I can’t even imagine doing those interviews on the spot. Technically it’s nothing remarkable, so I can’t quite call the overall end result great, but I’ll certainly be thinking about this one for a while. It also makes for a pretty neat companion piece to _No Other Land_.     7/10

cast_names: Louis Theroux, Itamar Ben Gvir

review_content: the movie is a survival thriller that disappoints largely due to its weak plot and reliance on genre clichés. The story revolves around a famous actress trapped in remote isolation, but her actions often feel contrived—she only displays real survival instincts when she's cornered, leaving the viewer to wonder why she didn't use her advantages earlier, when it mattered most.  While Sophie Turner delivers a strong performance, the film fails to give her character depth or agency; most of her decisions just reinforce the damsel-in-distress trope, and the narrative is filled with predictable twists and poorly written villains. Ultimately, the film squanders its premise and its talented lead with a lackluster execution that never manages to truly engage or surprise.

cast_names: Theresa May
review_content: [Sheffield '25] An impeccable piece of journalism that, while offering clear answers about the shared responsibilities in the Grenfell Tower fire, shows special compassion for the survivors and victims, constantly returning, in the midst of the journalistic account, to the testimonies of those who experienced the fire from within their homes. It clearly establishes the political, corporate, and security responsibilities that led to the greater severity of the disaster, denouncing the lack of rigor of a justice system that has yet to criminally prosecute anyone. It is a portrait of a system in which capitalism has come to pose a threat, even to personal safety.

cast_names: Judah Kelly, Michael Keyes, Graham Mackie, Robert Broski, Jim Donnelly
review_content: Some shorts rely on a twist. This one doesn’t. It rests on something far more fragile: a voice — and the effect it has on those listening.    The setup is simple, almost minimal. A singing duel that slowly stops being a duel. Winning doesn’t matter; what matters is who breaks through first. The camera stays still when it should, allowing the music to carry the emotional weight.    It works because it feels honest. It doesn’t try to impress — it aims to move. And when the performance lands, it truly lands. In just a few minutes, it achieves the kind of quiet intensity many full-length films struggle to reach.    Not grand, not flashy — just intimate and human.

cast_names: M. Sasikumar, Simran, Mithun Jai Sankar, Yogi Babu, Bagavathi Perumal, M. S. Bhaskar, Elango Kumaravel, Yogalakshmi, Ramesh Thilak, Abishan Jeevinth
review_content: Engaging • Interesting • Amazing  It is a warm comedy-drama that blends situational humor with heartfelt emotion. Everyday family chaos turns into laugh-out-loud comedy, while moments of tenderness remind you why love and connection matter.  The comedy works every single time, situational comedy, how can you turn a heartbreak into fun, this movie does that. And the emotions lands too. It touches on seriousness and goes deep and you feel it.  M. Sasikumar and Simran ground the film with sincerity as loving parents, while I've Mithun Jai Sankar previously in avesham and bar is high but he's brilliant here too. His dynamism with family and Yogalakshmi is amazing and sweet. A little tease little love. He had a brilliant serious scene too. Kamalesh jegan is th heart for comedy, however his role is less in second half but he is one to remember. and the ensemble cast (Yogi Babu, M. S. Bhaskar, Ramesh Thilak, Elango Kumaravel, Bagavathi Perumal) ensures no character feels wasted.  The movie is damn too fun and sweet. The heartbreak scene in which the marriage video is shown to family was too damn good, i laughed instantly.  Sean Roldan’s score and Aravind Viswanathan’s cinematography elevate the film, making it as vibrant visually as it is emotionally. A slight dip in pacing during the second half is noticeable, but never enough to derail the experience.  Yes, its optimism may feel idealistic, but that’s part of its charm, a hopeful lens that uplifts rather than critiques.

cast_names: Rajinikanth, Nagarjuna Akkineni, Soubin Shahir, Upendra, Shruti Haasan, Sathyaraj, Aamir Khan, Rachita Ram, Kanna Ravi, Lollu Sabha Maaran
review_content: “Stylish, nostalgic… but messy.” That’s basically Coolie.  Lokesh wanted this to be a big Rajinikanth celebration—nostalgia, swag, fan-service, all of it. And yeah, the first half actually works. But then the second half happens… and the movie just collapses under its own weight.  The Good Stuff  Rajinikanth still owns the screen. At 74, the charisma is unmatched. The de-aged flashbacks? Pure gold. You can almost feel Baasha vibes again. Nagarjuna looks 🔥 as Simon, though he barely gets proper screentime. Criminal waste. Dayal (Soubin Shahir) is one of the most interesting parts of the movie—menacing, intense, and well-acted.  Anirudh’s BGM? Honestly the real hero. He carries entire scenes on his shoulders. Even filler moments feel big because of him.  Sathyaraj and Upendra shine in their short appearances.  The Problems  The story is all over the place. Starts solid but gets convoluted with endless subplots, random flashbacks, and filler scenes. Shruti Haasan’s character? Practically irrelevant. Feels like she’s only there to scream or be rescued. Nagarjuna deserved way more depth—stylish villain, but hollow at the core. Songs are fun (Monica rocks), but the placement? 🏽‍♂ Total momentum killer. Action looks stylish but feels repetitive after a point. The ending hour is just stretched spectacle with logic tossed aside.  Style Over Substance  Lokesh’s direction looks slick - the neon-lit port, the mansion fights, all shot with flair. But it often feels like he’s more obsessed with style than storytelling. It’s like he tried to make a “festival film” instead of a tight, engaging story.  Coolie isn’t a bad movie ; it’s just a messy one. For hardcore Rajini fans, it’s a celebration. You’ll whistle, you’ll cheer, you’ll enjoy the nostalgia. But if you’re someone expecting a coherent, emotional, well-written film… this one will test your patience.  High on swag. High on music. Low on story.

review_content: I was roaming around without purpose until I watched Saiyaara. It changed something in me. I don't know any film has loved me or even dared to understand me at this level. Even my entire bloodline never really cared about who I am but this film did. I stopped wasting time and I started living every single nanosecond counting my every blessings. The acting of Chonky Pandey jr and his perfect hair has made me all wet. Aneeta is the best actress I have ever seen. She might be my new favourite actress after Urvashi firsteverwoman. The music in this film has single handedly defeated Aashiqui 2 and Rockstar mediocre albums. Ever since I laid my eyes on this film, I started believing in God. I don't know how can I thank Sir Mohit Suri for transforming our lives and every Korean films possible. Now, I just watch his film so that I can avoid the trouble of reading subtitles and watching BTS on screen. I don't think any ratings can justify this masterpiece so here I am. Fully sober and calculative declaring this film as the most perfect film ever made in the history of Cinema. This is bigger than any Nolan, Martin Scorsese, Akira Kurosawa films combined. I don't remember any film has made this huge cultural impact on the civilisation. I personally feel every aspiring director should study this film's optics. I understand people just want to be different by hating this masterpiece but deep inside, even you know this is the greatest thing ever imagined. Currently writing this review from hospital. Kinda fainted while watching this for the 69th time. Saiyaara forever. Saiyaara ever and ever. I wish I can make a tattoo of Chonky Pandey jr on my chest.

cast_names: Ahaan Panday, Aneet Padda, Alam Khan, Rajesh Kumar, Geeta Agrawal Sharma, Varun Badola, Shaad Randhawa
review_content: It's been a while since I saw a good bollywood romantic piece. Saiyaara is an emotional, melodious journey that feels both familiar and refreshingly raw even if it's served with a side of familiar Bollywood tropes. The film's emotional core is its absolute strength. It gets to you and pulls you right into its world.  The music... Oh wow! Don't even get me started. It's a character in itself, one of those rare albums that will be remembered for years. Truly outstanding.  Ahaan Panday makes a solid debut, and his raw energy is considerable in some scenes, but the real soul of the movie is Aneet Padda. She is simply amazing, gorgeous and innocent, with a performance so pure it's the highlight of the entire film. If she keeps this up, she could be the next big thing.  Now, for the things that could have been better. The story relies on a classic Bollywood trope, the angry, messed-up guy and the sweet, sober girl who fixes him. It's a formula we’ve seen countless times. Also, the ending could've taken more time and tell us more, and the side characters aren't developed, which is a missed opportunity. I was really hoping Krish would give some love to Vaani’s caring mom as he don't have that type of parents.  Overall, Saiyaara is a touching romantic drama with star-making performances. The emotional honesty and brilliant music overcomes the little flaws.  It's a shame that some of the irrational audience behavior divided viewers, but the fans need to behave especially in public or they will do their movie no good. A film like this, with so much heart, deserves to be judged on its own merits, not on the antics of a few.

cast_names: Ahaan Panday, Aneet Padda, Alam Khan, Rajesh Kumar, Geeta Agrawal Sharma, Varun Badola, Shaad Randhawa
review_content: It’s been a while since I shared an Indian movie recommendation—so here’s one for all the romance lovers!    Let me tell you about it:    The story follows a guy who dreams of becoming a singer and a girl who’s a writer. They meet by chance, and their love story begins...  But then something happens—something that shouldn’t happen.    It’s a romantic film, and for the first time ever, I’ll say it: the emotions were heavy.    The direction was flawless—every scene felt beautifully crafted.  Acting? Outstanding—especially Ahaan, which really surprised me since this is his first film!  The female lead, Anette, was also fantastic—she brought grace and emotion to the screen.    The music is lovely and really adds to the film’s atmosphere.    I highly recommend this movie—it’s a must-watch if you enjoy heartfelt, emotional romances.    My rating: 8/10

cast_names: Daniel Bernhardt, Christina Orjalo, Paul Lazenby, Nicholas Rice, Patton Oswalt, Nina Bergman, John Clifford Talbot, Jon Ambrose, Conor Sweeney, Robert Hyland
review_content: Deathstalker looks like it was unearthed from a dusty VHS bargain bin labeled “Definitely Not Conan,” and I mean that as the highest compliment. It proudly recreates the entire early-80s fantasy aesthetic—glorious rubber masks with frozen expressions, claymation creatures that move like they’re reconsidering their life choices, and plastic puppets that deserve their own agent. The whole thing feels like a live-action Dungeons & Dragons campaign run by a dungeon master who’s had three beers and zero budget. It’s packed with tongue-in-cheek humor, absurdly macho action, and just enough self-awareness to let you know the filmmakers are in on the joke. Deathstalker isn’t for everyone, but if you’ve ever wanted to watch a barbarian punch a rubber monster while synth music cheers him on, this is basically cinema.

review_content: Plainclothes is thrilling, heartbreaking and heartwarming all at once. It suffocates you from beginning to end with a coming out story that ends with an alleviating, approving smile, but that supports itself on a love story that was never meant to be.   The cruising storyline almost fell into a cliché for me, but the director managed to transform what was once the only option for gay men to interact sexually into a rush of anxiety and panic.   Russell Tovey is an amazing actor I knew since the series And Years. Tom Blyth, however, I discovered through this film and he gave Lucas/"Gus" such depth I wasn't expecting with phenomenally heart-racing acting!  Watch this movie expecting to sit with it and reflect for yourself, and not for a given social commentary. That way you'll enjoy it thoroughly.

cast_names: Tom Blyth, Russell Tovey, Amy Forsyth, Maria Dizzia, Christian Cooke, Gabe Fazio, John Bedford Lloyd, Darius Fraser, Alessandra Ford Balazs, Marlene Mancini
review_content: “Plainclothes” opens with a quiet, intimate gesture: two men touching each other, timidly, in the dim light of an old movie theater, and it becomes clear right away that all the tenderness of that moment is about to be swallowed by a world that has no idea what to do with men like them. In her first feature, Carmen Emmi builds this story with an uneasy visual energy, full of fragments, memories, and contradictory impulses, like the film’s own form is being eaten away by the protagonist’s anxiety. And it’s exactly this collision between possible tenderness and everyday terror that turns “Plainclothes” into a cruel identity drama, sometimes suffocating, always emotionally honest.    The narrative structure embraces Lucas’s inner chaos (played by Tom Blyth) with frantic montages that jump between textures, from dirty VHS to clean digital, in a rhythm that feels like interrupted thoughts, traumatic memories, and fears that spill over without warning. At first, this style works like a sensory dive into the mind of a man constantly on the edge of collapse, but Emmi and editor Erik Vogt-Nilsen stretch this device so far that it starts to lose strength; the sheer amount of visual and musical noise can be more draining than immersive. Even so, the intention is crystal clear: Lucas lives in a permanent state of alert, and the film wants the viewer to feel every twitch of that paranoia. It’s a risky choice, but totally aligned with the emotional brutality Emmi is shaping.    At the center of this spiral sits the story’s cruelest contradiction: Lucas works as an undercover cop arresting gay men who look for discreet encounters in public bathrooms. He seduces with a glance, lures them in, turns them over to his partner, and every arrest is like a tiny emotional execution. The film doesn’t treat this as metaphor; it treats it as real torment. Lucas recognizes in the men he destroys the same desire that consumes him, and this cycle of self-destruction is filmed with deliberate discomfort. When Andrew (Russell Tovey) enters the story (quiet, reserved, with the face of a 90s “family man”) the spark between them isn’t just sexual: it’s a glimpse of who Lucas could become if he had the courage to exist. Andrew is the mirror of a possible future, but also a reminder of everything that gets lost when you live a lie.    The film grows strongest in the dynamic between these two men. Even though Emmi tackles social and family issues with force, it’s in the moments where Lucas and Andrew let themselves breathe (whether in the empty theater, the quiet greenhouse, or in those awkward touches) that “Plainclothes” finds its heart. Russell Tovey plays Andrew with compassion and a kind of contained sadness, a man already tired of surviving in the shadows; but it’s Tom Blyth who carries the film on his shoulders. His performance is devastating and physical: he makes you feel a body shrinking in on itself to fit inside an identity that was never his. His eyes always look like they’re asking for help, like he’s about to confess everything but knows he can’t open his mouth. It’s a relentless performance, driven by pain, anxiety, and a yearning for liberation that can’t exist without cost. When the fear of recognizing yourself takes over, it gives birth to an affection so powerful, and so unreachable, that it almost restores something inside you, and Blyth nails every nuance of that, from terror to ecstasy.    The family setting reinforces the weight of expectations shaping Lucas. The loving presence of his late father only deepens his silence; his grandfather, the inspiration behind his career, represents the traditional masculinity he tries so hard to replicate; and the grotesque figure of Uncle Paul (Gabe Fazio) becomes the voice of the kind of hatred Lucas fears waking up if he ever reveals who he is. Fazio plays Paul with a disgusting, almost cartoonish aggression, but that just highlights the point: men like him aren’t exceptions, they’re part of the social fabric that traps Lucas in a maze with no exit. In contrast, Andrew becomes the one safe place Lucas manages to find in all that noise.    Technically, “Plainclothes” is a film of extremes: sometimes suffocating and hysterical, sometimes intimate and filled with silence. This back-and-forth can be tiring, but it’s right in this excess that Emmi finds her voice. The film is imperfect, but intentionally imperfect, like a body trying to stay upright while something inside it collapses. Its fragmented aesthetic echoes a time when living openly meant risking not only affection, but your social survival too, and the film recreates that climate with real accuracy.    Overall, “Plainclothes” is less a forbidden romance and more a warm (and painful) portrait of a man trying to exist in a world that forces him to choose between loving and surviving. When Lucas and Andrew find in each other the possibility of breathing for the first time, the film reaches its brightest point. And it’s exactly that contrast (between the violence of repression and the gentleness of desire) that turns Emmi’s film into a visceral study of identity, shame, and the courage needed to finally look in the mirror without looking away.

cast_names: Tom Blyth, Russell Tovey, Amy Forsyth, Maria Dizzia, Christian Cooke, Gabe Fazio, John Bedford Lloyd, Darius Fraser, Alessandra Ford Balazs, Marlene Mancini
review_content: This movie surprised me. It wasn't fast paced, it wasnt chock full of action. And yet it held my fixation throughout. The cinematography was incredible, it felt at place within the setting and invoked emotion constantly. Tom Blyth has a standout performance. This left me feeling changed, though I'm not sure whats changed. Im already out in every way i can be. My queerness is never quiet or in shadow. Maybe i just need to reflect more to know what it was motivating me towards. The entire film was phenomenally shot, beautifully written and incredibly acted. There are a variety of really impactful moments throughout the film that have really stuck with me. The most prominent are the ending, of course, which was fantastic, and the sex scenes. I think well done sex scenes are really impactful in film and the ones in Plainclothes really had emotional weight behind them. I saw this in a theater with a completely packed audience, almost entirely older. It was a nice experience to see them react positively.

cast_names: Tom Blyth, Russell Tovey, Amy Forsyth, Maria Dizzia, Christian Cooke, Gabe Fazio, John Bedford Lloyd, Darius Fraser, Alessandra Ford Balazs, Marlene Mancini
review_content: The ending was really tear-jerking—I couldn’t hold it in… Yet another suffocating story—embracing your true self sounds so simple, but in reality, it’s incredibly hard…  Maria’s performance in those final few shots was truly excellent—she portrayed the shift from a mother’s heartbreak to acceptance with such raw depth. Why is life so hard? No one is really at fault, so why must we be forced into such painful corners?  People say it’s the times to blame, but what about us now? How many of us can actually be ourselves? The editing in the first half was a bit choppy, but the suffocating atmosphere in the last 30 minutes was portrayed so well.  May we all one day stand in the sunlight and have the courage to be our true selves… And may we all someday find our own San Francisco.

"The Alabama Solution" is one of the obligatory prison films at the Oscars. But as long as the disastrous conditions in the US penitentiary system do not improve, there will be enough material for new films of this kind every year. This documentary deals specifically with the inhumane, indeed criminal, treatment of prisoners in Alabama. And the main stylistic device, besides the usual talking head sequences, is footage shot by the inmates themselves. The film gets its message across clearly, and one can only hope that those responsible will see it and come up with better ideas than “more prisons” to tackle the problem in the future.
I like this. It was difficult to watch with the way it was filmed, btu the message was really intense.

Engaging documentary about abuse of power, highlighting the systemic problems and human rights abuses. If you don't leave this doc infuriated, I'm not sure what it would take to trigger a reaction.    2025 (98th) Academy Award nominations: Best Animated Feature

"An Alabama problem deserves an Alabama solution."    I just had take one side step from watching horror movies this month to check out the Alabama Solution and damn, this was a tough watch. In my line of work we sometimes get "loaned out" to help out in prisons over here in the Netherlands. And I have seen every single one of them and no, they don't even compare to the shit I saw in this harrowing documentary.     How the hell is this shit possible? I get that you can be understaffed. But having the prisons be overcrowded at the same time? And have your staff being so abusive, corrupted and taking advantage of their position? Beating people to death? Yes, people. Not prisoners or criminals. And even getting promoted? The American Department of Justice must clean-out the whole system over there.     I was also shocked by the fact that almost nobody has a chance on parole. Those people in jail over there don't even have a light at there end of the tunnel to give them hope. Horrible.    The Alabama Solution is worth a watch but be prepared it is shocking.

Black men have came up. They’re now allowing black men to assault, beat and kill white men. In the eighties and nineties, it was crack cocaine; today, it’s guns. Everything bad starts from the top, the same thang they're doing in prison they are doing in the streets.

review_content: [Mubi] It maintains a consistently unsettling atmosphere around Matthew, intriguingly portrayed by Canadian actor Théodore Pellerin. However, it can sometimes feel overly ambiguous, which may hinder the story's development, as if the characters never quite find their precise place within the narrative. But it culminates in a particularly clever ending, skillfully constructing throughout the story a deconstruction of the world of fame and its toxic environments, with elements of a psychological thriller, though it never fully embraces the genre's characteristics.

cast_names: Théodore Pellerin, Archie Madekwe, Havana Rose Liu, Sunny Suljic, Daniel Zolghadri, Zack Fox, Chaize Macklin, Sean Wang, Myra Turley, Mikaela Poon
review_content: What I liked about the film:    ++ The story is great. How Matthew comes to dominate Oliver is awful, but captivating.   ++ Both Matthew and Oliver's characters are acted brilliantly. Scenes where Matthew is in an internal panic over losing Oliver's affections as well as moments where Oliver has been completely broken down by Matthew's control over him are done so well.  ++ I enjoyed the music. I couldn't name a Nile Rodgers song beforehand, but My Love Song to You is stellar. The score is also very well-done and scenes are elevated as a result of it.  ++ I liked how the film didn't spell everything out . You see Matthew obviously setting Oliver up with some underage girls... and then he's back in the inner-circle. No time is wasted on scenes and dialogue between them where the demands are made by Matthew.     What I didn't like about the film:    -- I suppose some somewhat key moments are unbelievable? Matthew plays a song that Oliver likes and then BOOM he's part of the gang. I'm not sure how Matthew's blackmail plot would really fair in the real world. For some reason he had access to Oliver's house still, access backstage, etc. Surely Oliver would have blacklisted him?   -- I'm not sure whether Matthew's character is very consistent. Initially, he seems a besotted fan for whom Oliver is his favourite person (to use a BPD term, see below XD). He seems to crave validation from him and needs his affections. By the end, he does not seem to need Oliver's love. He just wants to be in control and I'm not sure that is consistent with the Matthew we meet at the start.     Considering the word of the year (in the UK, at least) is parasocial, this is a movie that is very relevant. I'm not sure how I feel about Oliver's predictament. I suppose I feel bad for him as Matthew is clearly a horrible person, but Oliver was also an unpleasant person who treated people like t-shirts and was more than happy to cast-off others at a moment's notice. Maybe that's why I like this movie; it's hard to find someone to root for. Also, with movies like this where a character is clearly mentally unwell, I like to try and diagnose their afflictions. My absolutely unprofessional understanding of psychology leads me to diagnose Matthew with BPD at the start, and then grandiose NPD by the end.

review_content: A movie about old age and managing grief about the loss of a loved one. Generally sensitive and excellently played by June Squibb.  Personal note: I though did not like too much that they try a bit too hard to grab sympathy for the Jewish culture and Jews in general. Those parts, while it is certainly still important to point out the horrors of the Holocaust as well as to show that not all Jews are like some war mongers in Israel, feel a lot like trying to grab sympathy in the light of the doubtful action of Israel in Gaza. A more general and more subtile handling of this subject would have been better for the movie. There were also many non-jewish people who died innocently in WW2 and in the holocaust. Anyway, it is still a good movie that can help people handling grief and see that they are not alone.

Elizabeth Moss is great as always and I really liked her character she's relatable and has a good vibe. Kate Hudson steals the show whenever she's on-screen as this shady mysterious company owner who says the weirdest shit with a straight face. The scenes they have together are gold. There's something comforting about the vibe of this movie and it's hard to put a finger on what it is exactly but probably a mix of everything. Digged the score, the sets and totally my kind of comedy. The body horror isn't as effective as it should be and it's too bad they don't show the transformation but those moles were pretty disgusting. Very straightforward movie with nothing really surprising by the end if you look at the clues but I still loved seeing it unfold. That third act was so much fun!

cast_names: Mohanlal, Shobana, Prakash Varma, Maniyanpilla Raju, Farhaan Faasil, Binu Pappu, Arsha Baiju, Irshad, Thomas Mathew, Krishna Prabha
review_content: This one came to the OTT platform quite fast. The making of Thudarum was unnecessarily lengthy, with fillers that served no purpose than to extend the run-time. The film crawled at a slow pace until the intermission, after which the trajectory becomes too predictable as it turns into nothing more than a classic revenge story. All the build-up? Meh. I barely felt connected to any of the characters, or cared for them at all.       However, the direction, the cinematography and the soundtrack are commendable. Mohanlal as Benz, Prakash Varma as CI George, and Binu Pappu as SI Benny are some of the best performances in recent Mollywood. This one is worth a watch for sure—but I reckon just once.

cast_names: Mohanlal, Shobana, Prakash Varma, Maniyanpilla Raju, Farhaan Faasil, Binu Pappu, Arsha Baiju, Irshad, Thomas Mathew, Krishna Prabha
review_content: Thudarum delivers a powerful emotional drama, grounded in strong performances and tight storytelling. Mohanlal, the complete actor, is in top form—subtle, restrained, and deeply affecting as Shanmughan. This is one of his most honest performances in years, carrying the film with quiet intensity.  Prakash Varma is a surprise package. As CI George Mathen, he brings a menacing calm that makes the film’s conflict feel real and chilling. Binu Pappu as SI Benny also deserves special mention—his portrayal of a morally torn officer adds heart and humanity to the police angle.  Director Tharun Moorthy builds the story patiently, and it pays off. The emotional payoff is strong, and the cinematography captures both Kerala’s beauty and the film’s undercurrent of tension. A must-watch if you’re into performance-driven Malayalam cinema.

cast_names: Mohanlal, Shobana, Prakash Varma, Maniyanpilla Raju, Farhaan Faasil, Binu Pappu, Arsha Baiju, Irshad, Thomas Mathew, Krishna Prabha

cast_names: Gilles Lellouche, Adèle Exarchopoulos, Louis Garrel, Romain Duris, Valeria Bruni Tedeschi, Stéphane Bak, Hugo Dillon, Artus, Daphné Patakia, Agathe Mougin
review_content: There’s something about French sci-fi that makes even a slightly formulaic buddy-cop plot feel like it showed up wearing a better coat than everybody else. Dog 51 may follow some familiar beats, but it’s still a slick, moody, very enjoyable ride: part mystery, part romance, part “oh great, this future looks way too possible.” The divided Paris of “zones” feels less like wild fantasy and more like a warning label, with echoes of today’s border politics and an especially uncomfortable Israel-Gaza vibe, while the glimpses of grimy, desperate life in Zone 3 give the movie real texture beyond the central whodunit. The male lead gets extra points for looking like an actual worn-down human instead of a shampoo-commercial action hero, and he’s all the better for it; meanwhile, I’d never seen the female lead before, and even if some viewers think her range runs on one facial setting, I thought she was terrific here—tough, vulnerable, and believable enough to sell both the case and the chemistry. In the end, this is less a bang-bang future-cop movie than a smart, melancholy detective story that sneaks in a love story and leaves you wondering how long before we all get our own ALMA judging us from the cloud.

review_content: American Boy ended up being a pleasant surprise. Going in, I was a bit hesitant since Marcello Hernandez’s SNL sketches aren’t usually my favorite, but the stand-up format works much better for him here. The material feels more confident and personal, and his delivery is sharp enough to keep things consistently engaging even when the jokes aren’t totally in my wheelhouse.  This isn’t a special that’s going to convert non-fans or land among the upper tier of modern stand-up, but it’s an easy, enjoyable watch. Solid pacing, a few good laughs, and a clear sense of who he is as a comic made this a fun way to pass the time.  Rating: 3/5 – 75% – Worth Watching

That is not to cancel the guy! That’s not what I’m doing, it’s three stars because he is charming and affable and it will get you smiling if not bowling over in laughter. It just didn’t all hit for me, and I wouldn’t call it a revelatory or super insightful set. But I’m glad some love it

review_content: When a director is truly convinced of the subject matter for their film, this usually rubs off on the audience. That was certainly the case for me with “Nouvelle Vague.” Richard Linklater's love letter to the historical French cinema movement of the same name and Jean-Luc Godard's film “Breathless” at least managed to spark a certain fascination with the subject in me.    Stylistically, Linklater clearly draws on the black-and-white visuals of “Breathless,” which gives the film a very fitting atmosphere. Guillaume Marbeck and Zoey Deutch are thoroughly convincing in the leading roles. Deutch manages to convey the radiance of Jean Seberg, while Marbeck masters the numerous monologues about defying all possible film conventions.    However, the latter is also a certain weakness of “Nouvelle Vague.” For while it celebrates Jean-Luc Godard's contempt for cinematic conventions, the film itself is largely very traditional. This makes it much more accessible to the masses but also wastes some potential. Either way, I had a decent time with the film. It definitely helps to watch “Breathless” beforehand. Then you can appreciate Linklater's film even more.

movie_title: Ready or Not: Here I Come
cast_names: Samara Weaving, Kathryn Newton, Elijah Wood, Sarah Michelle Gellar, Shawn Hatosy, David Cronenberg, Dan Beirne, Olivia Cheng, Varun Saranga, Nadeem Umar-Khitab
review_content: Ready or Not 2, like its predecessor, is pretty fun. It takes the formula from the first one and basically reruns it, just with a bigger scale. To be honest, I was disappointed that they didn't try to use a different game or a different framework for the setup. I think there is a lot of opportunity to take this concept and go beyond simple Hide and Seek in terms of the underlying death game. It's hard to ignore that nagging feeling that you've seen it all before as the movie starts to kick in. However, once it starts rolling, I ended up enjoying it for what it was. The new characters add plenty of personality. Kathryn Newton is a perfect addition to the franchise, though I wish we got more from her as she is was still very much playing second fiddle to the excellent Samara Weaving. Speaking of Samara, she kicks it up a notch in this movie, with her oscillating between deadpan delivery and that stellar scream. The other characters are where the movie is able to shine, with the Pitt's Shawn Hatosy being the real standout. Despite the familiarity, the movie does shock in its scene-by-scene moments, and there are plenty of extremely fun set pieces. I also appreciate that the movie doesn't spend too much time before kicking things into action or high gear, with the body count starting and piling up relatively quickly. I do think the rules feel fairly arbitrary. Not to say that there should be a lot of systematic rules or anything, but it felt a bit random how characters would do things that were or were not against the rules. Finally, I have to compliment the costume design. The bridal dress in the first movie is iconic, and I think the costume design of the two sisters at the end of this movie rivals it. Overall, if you enjoyed the first movie and don't mind 110 minutes of more of the same but just scaled up, you should watch this movie.

movie_title: Ready or Not: Here I Come
cast_names: Samara Weaving, Kathryn Newton, Elijah Wood, Sarah Michelle Gellar, Shawn Hatosy, David Cronenberg, Dan Beirne, Olivia Cheng, Varun Saranga, Nadeem Umar-Khitab
review_content: They managed to do the impossible. A sequel that wasn't necessary was actually good!!!  Ready or Not ended perfectly and could have been a standalone, without a doubt.  Ready or Not: Here I Come picks up immediately after the first movie, although this one comes out !7! years later. I am so impressed by just that fact alone, and them pulling that off.  I wasn't sure if they would be able to come up with a plot strong enough to justify another movie, but they really kept it interesting and believable (in a realm where people explode, that is).   I'm also glad with the addition of the sister, I think that was a great relationship dynamic to add to the mix. Elijah Woods was fun as ever and a great addition to the cast as wel.   We don't need another movie and I can't even imagine what it would look like; but if there were to be one, it would have to have Elijah in it!!!

review_content: An overall good movie and an alright successor to Raid, with grander scale, more twists and turns and a more charismatic corrupt politician. Creators gave a sad back story to Dada Bhai so you feel for him. And the problems were solved way too easily in the end. Little comfortable writing. And the characters that were changed, just swap Ileana for Vani? And also not many characters returned from first movie, however it was explained due to transfers saga. Tauji was the charm of it, whenever he came, movie got attractive Dada Bhai was soul of it, the charm, the attitude. Ajay Devgan was good as he is. Vaani didn't had much to do. Lallan & Amma were two well written and acted characters. Overall a good movie.  It was good that they didn't go on to repeat the first movie, they went bigger and it turned out alright. The reunion in jail looked forced though. And if you consider reality which we should not, the movie is highly fictional.

review_content: [IFFR '26] Writer Ben Shattuck has adapted one of the short stories from his anthology _The History of Sound: Stories_ (2024), which focuses solely on the relationship between two characters during their journey through Maine capturing recordings of traditional songs. Perhaps this is why one part of the story may struggle to find its balance with the rest of the narrative. Regarding the lack of sensuality or emotion in the relationship between Lionel and David, it's part of the narrative restraint adopted by the director, a grammatical bareness that is exposed through conflictual absence and expressed from the melancholy of absence rather than the physicality of presence. But this doesn't hinder the film's conveying of the story's understated, romantic depth.

cast_names: Duncan Lacroix, Hannah Khalique-Brown
review_content: Year 10 (2024) wastes no time on pleasantries. With almost no dialogue, the film drops you headfirst into a filthy, brutal post-apocalyptic world and simply expects you to keep up. It’s an intensely suspenseful experience that pulls no punches, thriving on atmosphere rather than exposition. The grit feels earned, not performative, and the filmmakers clearly squeezed every ounce of value from a limited budget.  At its core, this is a story about desperation and survival — a world where only the strongest endure, and the characters wear that reality like a second skin. The tension builds beautifully toward the ending, though the film stumbles slightly by never convincingly explaining why cannibalism becomes the go-to option when wolves are apparently plentiful. Still, the rawness works far more often than it doesn’t, making Year 10 a harsh but compelling watch.

Sew Torn might be the strangest film I’ve watched during this recent streak of oddities, but that’s part of what makes it intriguing. Its unconventional storytelling structure and the surprisingly stylish use of yarn as a narrative device give it a distinct identity, even when the execution wobbles. It’s offbeat, experimental, and absolutely not for everyone—but for viewers who appreciate bold swings and weird cinematic texture, there’s something to latch onto here. For everyone else, it may simply feel too strange to fully embrace.  A flawed but memorable little oddity that stands out purely because of how unique it is.  Rating: 2/5 – 65% – Not Recommended to Everyone

cast_names: Ajay Singhal, Anshul Sharma
review_content: Indian animation has always been seen as “for kids.” Cartoons, cheap stuff, not real cinema. And then Mahavatar Narsimha came out. Directed by Ashwin Kumar, this doesn’t feel like just another animated film, it feels like the start of something big, maybe even a revolution. Ashwin himself said in his podcast with Men Of Culture that he felt he had to make this movie because nobody else was doing it. He’s a gamer, an artist, and someone curious about spirituality, so he imagined the story of Narasimha in animation. Honestly, that choice makes the film stand out immediately; it’s one of those rare animated films in India that adults can actually sit through and enjoy.  The Story It’s the story of Prahlad, Hiranyakashipu, and Narasimha, a tale we all know. But the film tells it across three generations, starting even before Hiranyakashipu’s birth. That gives the story a bigger scale, like an actual epic instead of a one-note legend. It’s based on scriptures like the Vishnu Purana and Bhagavata Purana, and yeah, some details feel different, but then again, every scripture has its own variation.  The Visuals & Action For a ₹20 crore budget, this movie looks way better than expected. The animation is smooth, the designs are clean, and while not always “world-class,” it works. Some stiffness is there, but you kind of ignore it because of how ambitious the film is. The action choreography is sick. Slow-motion hits, detailed battle moves, and that climax fight where Narasimha shows up, absolute goosebumps. This is one of those “you need to see it in theaters” sequences.  Voices & Music I watched it in Hindi, and the dubbing is top-notch. • Hiranyakashipu’s arrogance? Perfectly voiced. • Prahlad’s innocence? Nicely done, though at times you don’t fully feel for him. • Narasimha? Pure power in the voice. Kudos to Aditya Raj Sharma, Haripriya Matta, Harjeet Walia, Sanket Jaiswal, Priyanka Bhandari, Sanchit Wartak, Dinesh Varma, Uplaksh Kochhar, Vasundhra Bose, Abhishek Sharma and others   And then there’s Sam C.S.’s score, devotional, epic, spine-chilling at times. It lifts so many scenes.  What Worked / What Didn’t Worked:  * Fast pacing, never felt draggy  * Three-generation storytelling adds weight  * Nicely choreographed action & slow-mo shots  * Smooth animation considering the budget  * Great research and scripture-inspired writing  * Music & voice acting carry huge emotional weight  * Finally, an Indian animated movie that isn’t just for kids Didn’t Work (but small stuff):  * Emotional connect with Prahlad could’ve been stronger  * Some tiny script contradictions (like Holika killing Prahlad instead of Hiranyakashipu)  * A few animation hiccups, but forgivable  * Violence in the climax, depends on the viewer, but I didn’t find it an issue  For me, Mahavatar Narsimha is more than just a film. It’s proof that Indian animation can do big, bold, spiritual stories and not just silly cartoons. It’s researched, respectful of mythology, and at the same time entertaining with action and music. It’s not flawless, but honestly? It doesn’t need to be. This is a milestone for Indian animated cinema, and hopefully just the beginning of the Mahavatar Cinematic Universe.

review_content: There it is again: my general aversion to films that feel very stagy. To be fair, "Hedda" does possess some cinematic qualities. Above all, despite its limited setting, it is visually diverse and has a certain grandeur. But the star of the show is clearly Tessa Thompson, who is thoroughly convincing as the scheming main character. And she also has solid chemistry with Nina Hoss as her counterpart. Personally, however, the story never really grabbed me, which brings us back to the staginess. Because the story is probably better suited to the stage. But for anyone who doesn't mind this film structure, "Hedda" is definitely worth a recommendation.

cast_names: James Marsden, Vince Vaughn, Eiza González, Keith David, Jimmy Tatro, Stephen Root, Lewis Tan, Ben Schwartz, Emily Hampshire, Arturo Castro
review_content: This movie starts out well in my opinion. It's got an interesting concept and a charming cast, and the jokes are dumb but funny. I thought that it had all the makings of a solid action-comedy. However, as the movie progresses, it suddenly starts to veer into trying to develop emotional character moments. Now, don't get me wrong, that isn't a bad thing on its own. The problem is that the movie isn't doing anything particularly great in regards to the character dynamics. It tries to build sentimentality but doesn't succeed. However, by spending time doing that, the jokes start to thin out, and the ones that are present don't always land. The zippy pace starts to grind to a halt, and I think it becomes a lot less fun because of it. This movie should have embraced its silliness and just stayed the course as an over-the-top action comedy with a high joke per minute count. The balance is also weirdly stilted, with the more serious storytelling being entirely in the second half and ending. I had fun with the comedic moments, but I just don't think the emotional moments are that great. Quick note: this movie has this weird stylistic quirk where it will suddenly start doing this juddery slo-mo. It does it a lot in the second half, and a lot of times at really random moments like where there isn't even any action. It looks terrible, and I have no idea why the director and cinematographer thought it was a good idea. I disliked it so much that I am docking a point because of it.

review_content: What We Hide was a deeply satisfying watch. Ever since I first saw Mckenna Grace as a young actor in Gifted, I had a feeling she would one day deliver a performance this impeccable. I just didn’t expect it to come so early in her career—what an overachiever. Not only is her acting powerful here, but she also feels perfectly cast as the strong, survivalist big sister. Watching her in this role felt almost like watching a child in my own family grow up and graduate from college.    If you enjoyed this film, I recommend Where the Crawdads Sing (2022), which shares similar themes. Coincidentally, Jojo Regina, who plays the little sister in What We Hide, also stars in that movie.

review_content: Odd. Lazy writing, but kinda fun.    This is another one of those modern movies that fall under "I don't understand who the the target audience is".    Don't get me wrong, there are maaaany older silly movies like this, but at least the writing always made some kind of sense, especially when they spend the extra money on having a few recognizable actors.    The town meeting went way over the top and straight into slapstick town, seemed like the writers were more focused on social commentary. I'm not sure they even remembered they were doing a movie.    I can't imagine this is what teens want, and the whole moral of the story seemed to be "get out of small towns, move to the big city!" - but is that what people want these days? Who knows though.

cast_names: Brandon Routh, Malina Weissman, Taia Sophia, Harrison Cone, Mena Suvari, Jack Seavor McDonald, Diya Rao, Jeff Fahey, Mariann Gavelo, Debra Wilson
review_content: Director said he intended it as an horror movie for the whole family. I can see that. So I'm a bit old for that, and I would have much preferred a_real_ horror movie, but I can definitely see this become cult for a younger generation (if it gets the chance to be distributed enough). Honestly, great work on that.    I had a bit of a hard time to get into it, since a long part of the beginning is a series of fast cuts into the protagonist life. A weird choice that didn't agree too much with me. But once the story really starts it's kinda nice to follow.    One part of effort fortyhe family friendly restriction, apart on stopping real horror, is also that there is absolutely no emotional impact of character's deaths. I mean at some point [spoiler]both of Grace's parents[/spoiler] die, it's barely shown, the moment she learns of it is skipped, and there is absolutely no impact on this later. In the whole movie, absolutely nobody cares that anybody dies. And this is weird. This feels wrong. I don't know whether it was intentional, but I feel it really removes a lot of what could make it memotable.     The concept itself is great, this somethitg there that everybody ignores (which now would remind us of CoViD except it was already written before that) until it's too late. The effects are actually extremely good for a movie that didn't have that much of a budget.    Something rare, humour is non partisan. So there is some anti-woke humour that panders to a more conservative audience while also making fun on how their view of the world is ridiculously incompatible with common sense and basic survival (mostly references to real life events I think), in a _Don't look up_ way.    I got a bit of an issue with the climax scene. They manage to stop the Ick using [spoiler]the stadium's lights[/spoiler], just to discover that it actually can [spoiler]just attack them underground to make them fail[/spoiler], and so they just decide to... [spoiler]go up the higher structure available to get in the sun[/spoiler] like it could not do the same thing and make the whole thing fall...

Unfortunately, the decision to make it as a film weakened auch exploitation and ended up with more survival games with RPG aspects film compared to superhero film.  With too much story development packed into a single full feature film, the story became imbalanced as if there were parts of the story left untold. Consciously or unconsciously, the audience will feel it and could give the best appreciation due to the overwhelming untold stories. It's a box office hit, clearly, but it would not reach iconic status.  It's entertaining enough to watch, but don't expect to see a masterpiece film.

cast_names: Michael Cera, Michael Angarano, Maya Erskine, Kristen Stewart, Rosalind Chao, Bunny Levine, Amber Riat, Iman Karram, April Jeanette Mendez, Daniel Edward Mora
review_content: As a big fan of Michael Cera, Sacramento was an easy win for me. This isn’t the raunchy, over-the-top Cera from comedies like Superbad—instead, it’s more in line with the quieter, thoughtful energy he brought to Juno, just with the perspective of an older character. The movie itself is simple and straightforward, focusing on character and story over spectacle, but that’s part of its charm.    It doesn’t try to be bigger than it is, and that makes it all the more endearing. With its grounded storytelling and heartfelt moments, this one is an easy recommendation, especially for anyone who already enjoys Cera’s particular brand of understated humor.    Rating: 4/5 – 85% – Would Recommend

cast_names: Michael Cera, Michael Angarano, Maya Erskine, Kristen Stewart, Rosalind Chao, Bunny Levine, Amber Riat, Iman Karram, April Jeanette Mendez, Daniel Edward Mora

cast_names: Michael Cera, Michael Angarano, Maya Erskine, Kristen Stewart, Rosalind Chao, Bunny Levine, Amber Riat, Iman Karram, April Jeanette Mendez, Daniel Edward Mora
review_content: I chose this movie for my movie swap week with Joe. I kind of regret picking this after watching it. This just did nothing for me. I was kind of bored throughout. I didn’t like any of the characters. I honestly found them to be very awkward and unlikable. I couldn’t relate to them in the slightest. In a movie like this you have to buy into the characters and I never once did. I never cared about anything happening around them. I’ve never been a huge Michael Cera fan to begin with. I’m not saying he’s a bad actor, I just don’t care for his humor or style. Plus he might’ve had the world’s worst beard in this. They should’ve wrote more into the script for Maya Erskine because she’s hilarious. Unfortunately she’s a wasted character here. I suppose this was supposed to be a comedy/drama but I didn’t laugh once or feel any emotion. This was definitely a bummer for me as I had hopes of enjoying it. I watched it once and that’ll be good enough for me.  * Movie swapping with Joe

cast_names: Michael Cera, Michael Angarano, Maya Erskine, Kristen Stewart, Rosalind Chao, Bunny Levine, Amber Riat, Iman Karram, April Jeanette Mendez, Daniel Edward Mora
review_content: I know most of the cast to be comedic, but this wasn't anywhere close to funny. There wasn't much overall enjoyment either, as I giggled maybe once and lost interest after I realised there was no actual plot. Maybe if I were a first-time parent, I would relate more, but I don't plan on ever having kids. It's a way too simple story about friendship and parenthood that I found hard to connect with. Otherwise, it's a quiet flick that starts at a high 6 or 7 and gradually drops to a low 3 or 4.

cast_names: Michael Cera, Michael Angarano, Maya Erskine, Kristen Stewart, Rosalind Chao, Bunny Levine, Amber Riat, Iman Karram, April Jeanette Mendez, Daniel Edward Mora
review_content: Riddled with early 30s doubt, anxiety and dread, Sacramento is a mumblecore comedy drama that will either leave you bored silly or tip the scales on your existential crisis. Some areas feel a little surface level and underbaked, but there is an unmistakable sense of heart throughout Sacramento that will connect with those that can relate with Glenn and Rickey's wobbly grapple with adulthood and it's unrelenting feeling of fear and self doubt. The more I reflect on this movie, the more I appreciate the maturity it displays when broaching this often mocked millennial topic, as well as the performances put in by Cera and Angarano. They're understated for sure, but I think that's what makes them so effective. Good movie, if these understated modern dramas are your thing.

cast_names: Michael Cera, Michael Angarano, Maya Erskine, Kristen Stewart, Rosalind Chao, Bunny Levine, Amber Riat, Iman Karram, April Jeanette Mendez, Daniel Edward Mora
review_content: Some of the reviews here... ugh.  This isn't a "road trip" movie, it is a movie about two men struggling to move into adulthood.  Another review says Kristen Stewart can't act?  Um.... moving along....    At the core of this movie is a really decent story: two men are struggling to move into the world adulthood, and they don't quite know where to start (or even that they aren't there yet).   While I enjoyed the movie and then message (I think any well-adjusted adult male can understand the struggle), I still felt like the film was missing something.  It dragged a bit in the middle and some of the scenes were uneven.   That said, I thought Angarano is a revelation in this role.     follow me at https://IHATEBadMovies.com or facebook IHATEBadMovies

cast_names: N.T. Rama Rao Jr., Hrithik Roshan, Kiara Advani, Ashutosh Rana, Anil Kapoor, Bobby Deol, K.C. Shankar, Varun Badola, Hearty Singh, Soni Razdan
review_content: Hollow. Flashy. Forgettable  That’s how I’d sum up War 2.  They thought they were building something huge… a Spy Universe flex, the “Hrithik vs NTR” dream clash, big sets, big visuals. But in the middle of all this, they forgot the story. And man, without that, everything else just crumbles.  The Good Stuff (Yes, there is some)  Hrithik Roshan? Still a king. The guy can make even trash dialogue feel heroic. Jr. NTR? Dude walks in and matches Hrithik’s screen presence blow for blow. His hand-to-hand combat scenes are fire. Anil Kapoor & Ashutosh Rana? Absolute powerhouses. Their scenes had actual weight. The sad part is one of them gets wasted in a completely unnecessary way. A few action set pieces (snow mountain brawl, some close combat shots) are genuinely thrilling.  And Then… The Bad News  The writing is a total mess. Aditya Chopra and Shridhar Raghavan have honestly killed whatever momentum the Spy Universe had. Predictable twists, unnecessary flashbacks, and side-stories that serve no purpose.  Kiara Advani? Completely wasted. Feels like the script only remembered her when they needed a “convenience button.” The VFX? Bruh. Straight-up video game cutscene energy. Some shots look like memes from Twitter. The action? Half-borrowed. There’s literally a car chase that looks like a bootleg Mission Impossible: Dead Reckoning Part One. The tonal shifts are insane. One second it’s “life and death sacrifice talk,” the next second “Janabe Ali” song bursts out of nowhere. Logic? Dead.  Direction – Ayan Mukerji Some moments work, you can feel the flair in isolated scenes. But overall? It’s a letdown. The film is almost 3 hours long, and instead of tightening it, they keep piling on unnecessary drama and fake gravitas. It’s spectacle without soul.  War 2 isn’t a disaster, but it’s a huge disappointment. A film with Hrithik, Jr. NTR, Anil Kapoor, and Ashutosh Rana should’ve been an event. Instead, it feels like a flashy empty shell—beautiful at times, hollow at its core.  They aimed for epic. What they delivered? “High budget despair.”

review_content: Este documental homenajea a una mujer extraordinaria y rescata una historia increíble que merecía ser contada. Además, nos lleva de vuelta a una época en la que ese deporte tenía mucha más presencia en los medios y muestra cómo fueron cambiando la tecnología y la estética con el paso del tiempo. ♟

cast_names: Judit Polgar, Garry Kasparov
review_content: This is an excellent documentary about a woman, Judit Polgar, whom I never heard about, but who is by far the best female chess player to this day. The movie also gives a deep and interesting insight in to the world of professional chess. One takeaway of the movie to me is that, despite Polgar being the best female chess player ever, she mostly lost against Garry Kasparov, the best male player and many other male players. She was at best in the top ten of best players, though mostly even “only” in the top 30. I do not really get why, but it seems that the chauvinistic remarks by many players that women just do not have the mental and emotional capacities to match men in chess seem to hold some truth. The objective results seem to confirm that. Go figure why.

cast_names: Seymour Hersh, Jeff Gerth, David Obst, Bob Woodward, Max Friedman, Amy Davidson Sorkin, Antonio Taguba, Camille Lo Sapio, Salvador Allende, Carl Bernstein
review_content: Unfortunately, investigative journalism is becoming less and less relevant. This is mainly due to the changing media landscape, where the focus is now on chasing one breaking news story after another. And, of course, the fact that political scandals nowadays seem to have hardly any consequences also plays a role.    “Cover-Up” examines the career of journalist Seymour Hersh, who has uncovered numerous scandals since the 1960s. And it is precisely this look back at the past that is the most interesting aspect of the documentary, with original footage cleverly incorporated, making it almost a shame that the individual stories are not given more time.    For my taste, however, the film is too uncritical of Hersh, who has lost the support of all the major media outlets, especially with his more recent publications. In this sense, the journalist himself is a good example of the decline of investigative journalism, and I think this should have been emphasized more. Apart from that, though, the film is definitely worth seeing, especially for history buffs.

movie_title: Taylor Swift | The Eras Tour | The Final Show
cast_names: Taylor Swift, Mike Meadows, Max Bernstein, Paul Sidoti, Amos Heller, Matt Billingslea, Karina DePiano, Kamilah Marshall, Jeslyn Gorman, Amanda Balen
review_content: it’s hard to say which version of the eras tour i prefer. the ttpd set feels so essential to me, but i’ll forever mourn what was cut to make room for it. despite that, i think this is the superior concert movie. the transitions between eras feels much more natural without the title cards, which was a big pet peeve of mine from the first eras concert film. a few of the cuts to the band/audience feel a little jarring, but otherwise the camerawork and editing is very well done and captures all the right moments. there were times during this when i forgot about the audience entirely and i felt almost like this was a show just for me. it’s a very intimate experience of the eras tour, and a beautiful capture of the emotions of the very last show and everyone at the top of their game. i look forward to rewatching it for years to come. thank you taylor and crew for everything you have given us <3

cast_names: David Osit, Chris Hansen, Dani Jayden

I like that they run up against the true crime genre as a whole but doesn’t amount to much.

cast_names: Ozzy Osbourne, Sharon Osbourne, Jack Osbourne, Kelly Osbourne, Tony Iommi, Duff McKagan, Robert Trujillo, Billy Idol, Maynard James Keenan, Chad Smith
review_content: First of all, this was an excellent documentary that brought us into the last few years of Ozzy's life. We got to see the ups and down without the clown show that the Osbourne's show portrayed back in 2002. Ozzy's music was a big part of many of us growing up (myself included) and we know he had a lot of struggles along the way. What I can't help ignoring is the fact that Ozzy took partying to the extreme for many decades. I don't think he ever cared that someday it would get him in the end, but somehow in this documentary we are expected to feel sorry for him. Watching someone go downhill with health issues is never easy but I think a lot of the truth behind this was not mentioned here in full. All in all however, the filming brought out all the passion, strength and honesty behind his drive for his music and fans. Ozzy you did it all, and that is a life well lived!

cast_names: Ozzy Osbourne, Sharon Osbourne, Jack Osbourne, Kelly Osbourne, Tony Iommi, Duff McKagan, Robert Trujillo, Billy Idol, Maynard James Keenan, Chad Smith
