review_content: This movie was recommended by friend, and I went to watch it knowing nothing about them.   If you are planning on watching it, I would suggest you do the same. This is a psychological thriller which is well written— something that you can now routinely expect from Mollywood films, has stunning locations and great performances to boot.   From the beginning, we see Peeyoos make certain observations that normally other people wouldn’t, which at the time would’ve been just a throwaway detail to pad time, but every line in this movie is intentional.   The shots although looking beautiful, were confusing at certain times and were jarring especially for action scenes. However, that was made up for with the story.   This reminds of Coherence with a higher budget, and that’s a compliment!

movie_title: Even If This Love Disappears Tonight
cast_names: Choo Young-woo, Shin Sia, Jin Ho-eun, Cho Han-cheul, Lee Chae-kyung, Jang Yoo-sang, Ryu Sun-gyu
review_content: This movie took me by surprise. Its premise is almost exactly the same as 50 First Dates, but there's such tenderness to the story that makes it easy to overlook some of the similarities. The cast all do a fantastic job in selling their budding relationships with each other, and I like that the movie involves side characters and a group of friends instead of simply being about a boy and a girl. Shin Sia is the standout in my opinion, but I also was impressed with how Cho Yu-jung and how she showcases her vulnerability. Before I knew it, this movie had snuck up on me, and I had fully become invested in the characters. I do think it can be slightly manipulative at times, but it never feels sappy. Its sentimentality is earned and earnest. I was very impressed with the second half of the movie and how it used its familiar setup to surprising results. Honestly, the movie made me emotional, very emotional at times, and that's the biggest compliment I can give it.

review_content: Twisted (2026) is a fun thriller that hits many of the same story beats as Don’t Breathe, leaning into tension, confined settings, and a few uncomfortable twists along the way. It’s not doing anything revolutionary for the genre, but it understands the assignment and executes it well enough.  The movie moves along at a good pace and delivers the type of suspense you’d expect from a modern streaming-era thriller. It’s familiar territory, but it’s still engaging while it lasts, which is sometimes all a movie like this really needs.  More than anything, I hope this leads to more horror roles for Lauren LaVera. Between her, Mia Goth, and Jenna Ortega, we’re in a pretty great era for modern scream queens.  It doesn’t reinvent the wheel, but it gave me exactly what I was hoping for — and that’s all I can ask for.  Rating: 2.5/5 – 70% – Worth Watching

movie_title: Kesari Chapter 2: The Untold Story of Jallianwala Bagh
cast_names: Akshay Kumar, R. Madhavan, Ananya Panday, Mark Bennington, Alexx O'Nell, Regina Cassandra, Simon Paisley Day, Amit Sial, Vicky Kaushal
review_content: Everyone in our country knows about the Jallianwala Bagh massacre but only few would've known about the fight of C. Sankaran Nair against the British to prove that it even happened. A good movie to represent that. Based on the book- The Case That Shook The Empire by Pushpa & Raghu Palat, it is a gripping courtroom drama. The film aims to expose the truth & seek justice, offering a powerful and emotionally charged narrative. The music was superb. Downsides - historical inaccuracies, more drama. In the end when original pepl images are shown, Akshay looked too young for the role. He did his best though. Other actors were also good. Madhavan was on fire. Absolutely loved that they showed a picture about Sardar Udham Singh killing Michael O'Dwyer, immediately reminded me of the movie Sardar Udham which in itself is a great movie. Recommended.

movie_title: Kesari Chapter 2: The Untold Story of Jallianwala Bagh
cast_names: Akshay Kumar, R. Madhavan, Ananya Panday, Mark Bennington, Alexx O'Nell, Regina Cassandra, Simon Paisley Day, Amit Sial, Vicky Kaushal
review_content: #KesariChapter2 - A top-notch courtroom drama with a hard-hitting story, brilliant Making, and superb performances — pure goosebumps material! 💯  Highly recommend movie  Worth!

cast_names: Elizabeth Cullen, John Harlan Kim, Mia Challis, Genevieve Mooy, Robin Goldsworthy
review_content: Diabolic had all the ingredients for something decent on paper. A moody atmosphere, a solid cast, and a premise that promised psychological tension mixed with supernatural dread. Unfortunately, it never quite cooks.  The first act actually builds some intrigue—creepy visuals, unsettling sound design, and a lead performance that feels genuinely haunted. I was invested enough to think this could go somewhere interesting. But once the plot kicks into gear, it just collapses under its own weight.  Visually it’s competent (some genuinely striking shots), and the acting is better than the material deserves, but that’s not enough to save a story this sloppy and hollow. It wants to be profound and terrifying but ends up feeling like a tired retread of better films, without anything fresh or memorable to say.  Worth a watch if it’s on streaming and you’re bored, but don’t go out of your way. Disappointing.

cast_names: Steve Hartman, Meryl Hartman, Bryan Muehlberger, Gloria Cazares, Javier Cazares, Lou Bopp
review_content: Violence is usually told from the moment it happens, rarely from what’s left behind afterward. “All the Empty Rooms” flips that logic by building its entire experience around the absolute aftermath: spaces waiting for someone who will never return.    The short follows a long-term project led by Steve Hartman and photographer Lou Bopp, documenting the bedrooms of children killed in school shootings across the United States. The idea is simple, and precisely because of that, unbearably powerful: entering these untouched spaces, preserved like time capsules, and letting objects speak for those who no longer can. Unfinished drawings, clothes never worn, toys carefully arranged, all of it remains as silent proof of a life cut short before it could fully begin. The film understands that there’s no need to reconstruct the violent act itself; the tragedy is already fully contained in what remains.    The narrative structure revolves around a handful of these final bedrooms yet to be visited, but the focus never drifts. “All the Empty Rooms” has no interest in assigning blame, presenting cold statistics, or fueling heated debates. The attackers are never mentioned. That choice is crucial: by completely removing the aggressor from the frame, the documentary shifts our attention to where it almost never stays long enough, the victims and the void they left behind. The result is a film that refuses to turn death into spectacle or talking point, insisting instead on the humanity behind numbers that have become terrifyingly routine.    Formally, the short embraces restraint, defined by static framing, a slow rhythm, and, above all, a deliberate use of silence. The absence of constant narration or emotionally manipulative music creates space for viewers to project their own thoughts about grief, mortality, and injustice. There are no graphic images, no footage of violence itself, yet the emotional weight is crushing. Precisely because it doesn’t show the violence, the film forces us to confront its consequences in a deeper, more lasting way.    The parents’ testimonies are treated with immense care. They aren’t guided toward neat conclusions or pushed to turn their pain into an explicit political message. They speak of their children as ordinary kids, full of quirks, interests, and dreams, and it’s in that ordinariness that the film lands its hardest blow. Each room reinforces the idea that the world is objectively worse without those people in it, even if the viewer never knew them. Grief here isn’t abstract; it occupies physical space, it has color, texture, and memory.    Overall, “All the Empty Rooms” stands out as one of the most devastating documentaries precisely because it refuses to be loud or comprehensive in its discourse. Its power lies in suggesting everything without explaining anything, trusting that silence can hold complex conversations about death, political negligence, the normalization of violence, and a collective failure to protect children. It’s not a film that seeks catharsis or offers comfort, it simply exposes the human scale of an ongoing tragedy. And maybe that’s exactly why it lingers so heavily after it ends: because it reminds us that every empty room represents not just a life lost, but all the possibilities that will never exist.

cast_names: Steve Hartman, Meryl Hartman, Bryan Muehlberger, Gloria Cazares, Javier Cazares, Lou Bopp
review_content: There are films that are tough to watch, and the short documentary “All the Empty Rooms” is definitely one of them because of its subject matter. It centers on a project by journalist Steve Hartman and photographer Lou Bopp, who photograph the rooms of children killed in school shootings. The aim is to shift public focus away from the perpetrators and onto the victims, which it does quite successfully. I found the whole thing very moving. It's just a shame that we don't get to see enough of the finalized project, which makes the film seem a bit unfinished. But it's definitely worth watching.

review_content: This isn’t peak Matt Rife by any stretch, but Unwrapped was still a really enjoyable watch. The crowd work keeps things loose and spontaneous, and Rife’s natural charm and charisma carry the entire special with ease. Even when the jokes don’t hit at a high level, his presence alone makes it entertaining, and it’s easy to see how well this skill set would translate to something like a future talk show role.  It’s not trying to be a definitive stand-up showcase—it’s just a fun, festive hour with a comedian who knows how to work a room, and that’s more than enough.  Rating: 3/5 – 75% – Worth Watching

movie_title: Matt Rife: Unwrapped - A Christmas Crowd Work Special
cast_names: Matt Rife
review_content: Another hit-and-miss from Matt Rife. I got into stand-up in the past few years and his social crowd work clips pulled me in. Those two minute reels were sharp and funny and they pushed him into the spotlight. He kept saying he is a stand-up first and that crowd work is only a small part of his set, yet his whole online presence relies on those clips.    His first stand-up special proved why. It ran for more than an hour and the jokes fell flat. The ending felt like he snapped at the room then walked off with a line about how he only does crowd work. The whole thing bombed and the reaction showed it.    Then he tried a full crowd work special. That also bombed. No real laughs and no real spark.    Now we get this. A “crowd work” Christmas special with an audience that feels staged. The jokes are weak and he repeats the same bit across shows. The questions and answers feel stiff. The laughter sounds short and forced. The faces in the crowd look like they got cues from a producer. The timing gives away how scripted it is. The bit about masturbation shows it clear as day. He sets it up like it’s spontaneous but both he and the camera hit their marks too clean.    He also opens with twenty minutes of plain stand-up and none of it lands. It feels dull and awkward with jokes that sound childish.    I said this back in his first special and I see more people saying the same now. He is sliding fast. I hope the water sponsor pays well because the comedy is not doing it anymore.

cast_names: Sophie Turner, Kit Harington, Marcia Gay Harden, Laurence O'Fuarain, Jonathan Howard, Catherine McDonough
review_content: This movie is almost impressive in how thoroughly it wastes its own potential. It looks fantastic — beautifully shot, atmospheric, and dripping with the kind of grim, moody cinematography that makes you think you’re about to watch something genuinely haunting. The cast is strong, the acting is strong, and Marcia Gay Harden is, as always, terrific. For a while, it really feels like the movie is building toward something memorable.    And then it just... doesn’t.    The story is an absolute mess. You spend the entire movie waiting for the pieces to come together, waiting for the tension to pay off, waiting for the weirdness to mean something — and none of it really happens in a satisfying way. It flirts with being a psychological thriller, then a folk horror film, then maybe some kind of grief-induced hallucination, but never commits to any one idea long enough to make it work. Instead, it just leaves the audience stranded in a swamp of ambiguity.    Normally, I’m fine with a movie refusing to explain everything. But there’s a difference between mysterious and undercooked. This felt undercooked. If it wanted to be a bleak period piece about a widow and her monstrous mother-in-law trying to survive during wartime, that could have been interesting. If it wanted to go fully supernatural, that also could have worked. Instead, it dabbles in both and lands nowhere.    So yes, I understand why people have punished this movie with low scores. It’s long, offers no real payoff, and ends in a way that feels less provocative than simply unfinished. In the end, The Dreadful is a beautiful film wrapped around a completely unsatisfying story. Gorgeous to look at, but a hard pass.

review_content: This one was a pleasant surprise. It’s not some crazy, over-the-top blockbuster, but a very well-made movie that just works. The screenplay is super tight and never gets boring, which is a big win in my book. The writers had a simple, genius idea with the whole ancient board and its rules, and they followed them perfectly. It’s that kind of disciplined storytelling that makes you feel like you're in good hands.  The main characters - Shaji, Jithin, and Ranjith, are all fantastic and have some real depth to them. They are well written and we'll played. Their performances feel genuine, and I loved how their journeys played out. While the supporting characters could have been more fleshed out, their limited screen time doesn't detract from the main narrative's strength.  There’s a great line in the movie where one of them says, It’s a game, you know? Just with slightly higher stakes. That pretty much sums up the whole vibe. The comedy lands almost every single time, which is a rare feat. You just find yourself laughing along with their ridiculous situation. The director and writer deserve major props for creating something so original and fun. This is the kind of movie that shouldn't go under the radar. It's a testament to the fact that you don't need a huge budget to tell a great story. A fun, smart, and totally worth-it watch.

review_content: Barron’s Cove hit me hard. It’s dark, tense, and painfully human. Hedlund’s grief feels raw and real, pulling you into a story where every decision feels impossible. It’s not a perfect movie because some moments feel over the top, but if you’ve ever felt loss or wrestled with doing the “right” thing when the world feels broken, this one sticks with you long after the credits.

Compared to Shooting Guards, this one was both more enjoyable and worse as the same time. The story still feels fake, but I was engrossed throughout.    Rating: 2.5/5 - 70% - Worth Watching

Internet phenomenon Brian Johnson, aka The Liver King, takes center stage in a documentary that is visually well-produced, thanks to director Joe Pearlman, but still stumbles in substance.    At its core, the film feels like a stylized PR maneuver, giving Johnson a platform to softly backpedal rather than take true accountability.    The man, the myth – he built an empire on the idea of "ancestral tenets", a back-to-basics philosophy of eating and living as our primitive ancestors once did. It sounded revolutionary. Bold. A wellness movement no one else had supposedly thought of.    The money poured in through clever marketing and shrewd entrepreneurship (with plenty of help behind the scenes). But then came the lie – or more accurately, the revelation that shattered his ancestral empire: a leaked list of advanced anabolic steroid orders. Johnson offered a muted apology, and this film largely continues in that same tone of passive regret.    Let’s be clear:  When a bodybuilder orders Omnitrope (prescription HGH), IGF-1 LR3 (insulin-like growth factor), and CJC-1295 (a growth hormone releasing hormone), he’s not new to the game. This isn’t someone dabbling. This is seasoned, long-term steroid use, executed with intent and experience.    So thank you for the show, Brian.  But whether on camera or off, you’re still a compulsive liar.  That said, I’ll give you this – you know how to build a brand and sell a story.    The documentary doesn’t offer much that wasn’t already known. It's more of a deep dive into Johnson’s emotional turmoil, paired with some light reflections on food, fatherhood, and identity.    And perhaps that’s the one question it leaves us with:  How much chronic illness does the modern food industry truly create?  It’s hard not to think that, if Brian’s children hadn’t gotten sick, he may never have taken his philosophy to such radical extremes.    Rating:  ⭐ 6/10  Production: 7/10  Cinematography: 7/10  Authenticity: 6/10
Untold is such a hit or miss. This is just a PR campaign for the Liver King. Not a documentary at all.

cast_names: Elizete Malafaia, Jair Bolsonaro, Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva, Petra Costa, Sérgio Moro
review_content: I like Petra Costa's approach to her documentaries. This essay-like style provides a clear structure, and the filmmaker simply has an eye for the right footage at the right time. Furthermore, like its predecessor, "The Edge of Democracy," her new film, "Apocalypse in the Tropics," benefits from the obviously extensive access Costa has gained to numerous high-ranking politicians. In terms of content, this time the focus is on the growing influence of Protestant evangelical preachers on politics in Brazil—a topic that can certainly be applied to other countries as well. As an atheist, you can only shake your head at these people. Above all, however, it is frightening to realize how easily democracy can be undermined. Anyone who is even slightly interested in politics and the background to it should definitely watch "Apocalypse in the Tropics."

Did one of the druid/rock troll people dropped a hint that the princesses were adopted.    I'm really glad Disney decided to release this proshot. The theatre tickets were too expensive, and I wasn't a big enough fan of the film to go see the musical in theatre.

cast_names: Ayushmann Khurrana, Rashmika Mandanna, Nawazuddin Siddiqui, Paresh Rawal, Faisal Malik, Geeta Agrawal Sharma, Varun Dhawan, Sathyaraj, Abhishek Banerjee, Nora Fatehi
review_content: Fits well into the universe, but missed the horror part.  Watched this in a crowded theatre, and I can confirm,  The movie is good, you enjoy, the whole theatre was enjoying. However this is much more of an Adventure-Comedy than a horror-comedy.  The real strength is how well it connects to the existing Maddock Horror-Comedy Universe (MHCU). This isn't just fan service, the cameos aren't just there as cameos, they are there for the storytelling. Every link feels earned and organic, proving this franchise truly has a plan. And the Bhaski Showdown was the maniacal highlight. Seriously, that sequence was peak cinematic fun.  However, the film isn't without its stumbles. My main issue was the pacing, first half felt a bit long, and yeah, there were definitely a few jokes that also don't land sometimes. The set-up takes too much time, even if the second half pays it off brilliantly. Also the writing in the ending felt like low effort.  Technically, it's brilliant. The music and colour grading works perfectly, the dark, kinda gritty colours fitted the tone. And the background score works perfectly. But I have to ask, I don't know why they marketed the movie with the item numbers when the movie had substantial things to show. The core of the film is a beautiful love story, and it deserved more emotional tracks.   Ayushmann Khurrana and Rashmika Mandanna were fantastic. Their chemistry works very well, and Rashmika, especially, shines as Tadaka. Unfortunately, the writing for Nawazuddin Siddiqui's character was a letdown. I felt he could've been better written and performed both.  Overall, the movie has fun within it and you feel it. It successfully set up future projects very well, and despite its pacing and dialogue inconsistencies, it’s a necessary and nice addition to the universe. A good Diwali watch with friends and family.

cast_names: Hugo Stiglitz, Laura Trotter, Maria Rosaria Omaggio, Francisco Rabal, Sonia Viviani, Eduardo Fajardo, Stefania D'Amario, Ugo Bologna, Sara Franchetti, Manuel Zarzo
review_content: I made it almost to the 23-minute mark and after that, I couldn't take anymore. I love zombie flicks, and I love Italian flicks, and I especially love Italian zombie flicks that are beautifully gruesome and gory but this... Wow. And that ain't a good "wow", either. This was just bad all the way around. The SFX are very 1980's-esque (cheap red paint and glue) but what gets me is these aren't "zombies" in your typical zombie fashion. These are zombies armed with weapons who want to KILL people (not eat their brains) and then want to drink the blood. That's not a zombie; it's some sort of hybrid vampire/serial killer or whatever, with some REALLY BAD MAKEUP on. I mean, this might have at least been interesting enough to finish (cheesy or not) if the FX had been good but this was just really really awful in every imaginable way. The acting was terrible, the script was awful, the story was utterly stupid, the dialogue... I almost turned it off when the guy actually said, _"Open up in the name of the law!"_  (Yes, it's that bad.) You're welcome to give it a try but believe me when I say it's just completely bad. REALLY bad.

cast_names: Melania Trump, Donald Trump, Barron Trump, Hervé Pierre, Queen Rania of Jordan, Brigitte Macron, Peter Sohn
review_content: A beautiful portrayal of the classiest First Lady the country has produced. The documentary captures Melania not only as a public figure but as a woman whose presence has always been defined by composure, restraint, and an unmistakable sense of elegance. Throughout the film, there is a quiet confidence in the way she carries herself, never loud, never overstated, yet always commanding attention.    What makes the documentary particularly compelling is how it frames Melania’s style as more than fashion. Her sartorial choices become a language of their own: structured silhouettes, refined tailoring, and an eye for timeless design that places her firmly within the tradition of iconic First Ladies who understood the power of visual diplomacy. The film highlights how clothing can communicate grace, authority, and individuality without ever needing words.    For that reason, I am adding this documentary to my personal Fashion and Sartorial movie collection. It deserves a place among films that celebrate the intersection of style, identity, and public life. In the same way certain movies immortalise designers, models, and fashion houses, this documentary preserves the image of a First Lady whose aesthetic sensibility became part of her legacy.    Among the many fashion-centered documentaries and style-driven films out there, this one stands comfortably alongside the best. It is not just a political portrait. It is a study in elegance.

cast_names: Abhishek Banerjee, Shubham Vardhan, Mia Maelzer, Harish Khanna, Kritika Avasthi
review_content: One of the best movie from bollywood in recent times. Stolen: A Raw Look at Reality It acts as a ray of hope in challenging times. It's sad that it's based on real events. If you want a film that's more than just a thriller. Gripping, Intense & Moving. Stolen isn't just a movie, it's a gut punch that perfectly mirrors the harsh realities of our society. It digs deep into that common choice we often make in times of chaos and injustice: "Let's not get into this, mind our work" But this film screams, "kindness can often backfire," taking you on an anxiety-inducing road thriller that's realistically intense, emotionally gripping, and truly thought-provoking. The social commentary in Stolen is layered in a powerful way, raising strong, introspective moral questions. It tackles everything from the tough lives of marginalized people and the unintended effects of social media to the scary power of mob mentality, chaotic lynchings, and even illegal surrogacy. The story follows two brothers from a high-class background who get unexpectedly caught up in a child abduction case. The film constantly tests their guilt, their morals, and their humanity as they try to help a helpless, poor mother who's lost her kid. These two brothers are like mirrors, reflecting two very different sides of our society. One just wants to go to his home to a wedding, not caring what happens to the child. The other wants to do the right thing, to help the mother and stand against injustice. This ongoing fight of morals between them creates such an engaging drama that keeps you hooked. The movie brilliantly highlights the real power play in our society. It shows how the broken and corrupt system just exists without truly acting, where money talks, and those below the poverty line hardly get a chance to fight for their rights. Yet, Stolen also makes you believe that selfless people still exist, and that human goodness isn't entirely gone, even if it feels rare these days. You'll be absolutely glued to the screen from beginning to end. It's incredibly gripping, intense, and constantly moving in every single frame. This isn't your usual thriller; it's something more profound. The film is truly elevated by the brilliant performances from the entire cast. Abhishek Banerjee is particularly phenomenal, and his transformation is unreal. At the start, you see him in a blazer and turtleneck, stepping out of a fancy car. By the end, he's stripped down, beaten, bruised, and completely lost, only to find himself fighting for justice. It perfectly shows how someone can be stripped of their facade, their arrogance of class and privilege, to finally see reality and seek true justice, which brings that huge smile to his face at the end. He absolutely nailed playing that rich, narcissistic character who couldn't care less about others – so glad he got that role! Mia Maelzer also gives one hell of a performance, and Shubham Vardhan is truly the heart of the film. That specific scene where Shubham's character gets shot was masterfully filmed and edited, and everyone's reaction in that moment was just top-notch.  A Look at the Harsh Truths Stolen never shies away from showing the brutal reality. Take that powerful mob lynching scene, for instance. There's also a moment where the camera pans to those three boys realizing they almost beat an innocent person to death because of false rumors. Nothing was said, but those 2-3 seconds meant a whole lot. Brutal, right? And speaking of that, it's a chilling reflection of what's happening. In 2025 alone, in just these past five months, there have been 9 reported fatalities due to mob lynching based on rumors, government propaganda, or just pure hatred. And God knows how many more unreported incidents. Most of these victims were from minority groups. Looking back, from 2014 to 2024, there have been over 500 reports of mob lynching that resulted in casualties. The number of victims from minority and lower castes within that count is staggering. This is the sad truth of our "nation" right now. This movie, in these seemingly dictatorial times, truly stands tall as a ray of hope. It's an exceptional directorial debut by Karan Tejpal; honestly, you wouldn't guess it's by a newbie director. The same goes for the cinematographer, Isshaan Ghosh, who is exceptionally talented. The camera work was so wonderfully done; it made you feel like you were right there with the characters, constantly following, observing, and trying to survive with them. Brilliant job by all three leads as well. So, yeah, the film was good. Go watch it now, or maybe don't, I don't know, lol. But it's definitely a must-see for a real dose of reality and some incredible filmmaking.

cast_names: Miriam Margolyes, Alistair Nwachukwu, Stephen Fry, Oscar Lloyd, Charlie Clemmow, Lee Knight
review_content: Not every friendship needs conflict to be meaningful. Sometimes, all it takes is the coincidence of two people at different moments of vulnerability, willing to share time and silence. “A Friend of Dorothy” builds on that idea to develop a bond shaped more by care than by radical transformation.    The film follows the meeting between JJ (Alistair Nwachukwu), a teenager still trying to figure himself out, and Dorothy (Miriam Margolyes), an elderly widow living among memories, books, and long stretches of quiet. The script wisely avoids forcing artificial conflict: the relationship grows out of small gestures (a ball lost in the yard, a stubborn can that won’t open, shared readings) and develops organically, grounded in affinity and mutual respect. Dorothy doesn’t “save” JJ, nor does he become a cure for her loneliness; what exists between them is a balanced exchange, clearly temporary, and the film never tries to pretend otherwise.    There’s an evident tenderness in how “A Friend of Dorothy” positions itself as a love letter to the arts, especially theater. JJ’s readings work both as acting practice and as metaphor: by giving voice to characters on the page, he learns to give voice to himself. Dorothy, in turn, finds in these visits a symbolic continuation of a past devoted to nurturing young artistic talent. Cinema here aligns itself with theater as a space of care and self-discovery, even if this approach is handled quite safely, without major narrative risks.    The way the film handles JJ’s sexuality is another positive point, treated with sensitivity and without heavy-handed messaging. The expression “friend of Dorothy,” reclaimed from its historical use as a coded phrase within the LGBTQIA+ community, takes on a new meaning within the story, becoming a bridge between generations. The introduction of a play that confronts homophobic persecution expands this arc of acceptance, but does so in a predictable way, following an emotional progression that’s unlikely to surprise more attentive viewers.    Much of the film’s emotional impact rests on Miriam Margolyes’s performance, which gives us a Dorothy who is warm, witty, and quietly melancholic. Her character carries the very human fear of growing old alone, intensified by the neglect of distant, disengaged family members. The performance avoids caricature, even when the script flirts with broader humor, such as her playful observations about the birds that frequent her garden. Alistair Nwachukwu matches her naturally, shaping a JJ who is shy, curious, and emotionally open, even if written within fairly recognizable archetypes.    Narratively, the film doesn’t try to hide its predictability. From early on, it’s clear that this friendship has an expiration date and that its impact will be more emotional than transformative. Supporting appearances, like the brief role played by Stephen Fry, reinforce this comforting, almost overly gentle tone, as if the film were actively avoiding any edge that might cause real discomfort.    “A Friend of Dorothy” is a beautiful, sensitive, and well-intentioned film, but one that rarely strays from the expected path. Its strength lies in the immediate empathy and sincere affection it builds between its leads, while its weakness comes from that same lack of risk. It’s a story that comforts more than it challenges, moving the audience through recognition rather than surprise. In the end, it leaves a bittersweet feeling: the sense of having watched something genuinely touching, if also a little too safe in its choices.

In terms of music, Alexandre Astier offers us again something very clean, but without relying enough on the leitmotifs to my liking, except for _L'Aube des Départs_ which goes through them all a bit too quickly. review_content: Como buen argentino, amo a Francella, pero el título de esta película es un chiste de mal gusto, que no tiene mucho sentido. Es difícil verse reflejado en alguna de las "representaciones"; esto no me enseña a un Argentino. Ni siquiera podría decir que en parte representa a alguien de Buenos Aires; es más bien la vida de un palermitano que rara vez salio de Capital Federal y probablemente ni siquiera conozca Córdoba. Es triste ver que este proyecto se redujo a eso mismo, sumando la frase del inicio que parece atacar el mensaje de "El Eternauta" y lo que Darín logró en su serie. Además, ni siquiera el póster es original, ya que se copia a si mismo (En "El Encargado"), y la idea de hacer referencias a esos cortos homónimos italianos que buscaban contar varias viñetas satiricas de la vida cotidiana (I mostri de Dino Risi en 1963) quedó reducida a la vida de un mediocre porteño que nunca le faltará nada y que no le importa del todo el resto. Esto jamás será la realidad del argentino, y me alegro mucho por eso.

cast_names: Paul McCartney, Linda McCartney, Denny Seiwell, John Lennon, George Harrison, Ringo Starr, Mick Jagger, Elton John, Mary McCartney, Stella McCartney
review_content: Really enjoyable watch but it’s also just essentially Paul McCartney quietly litigating a series of arguments that about four people on earth still care about. Unfortunately one of those four people is Paul McCartney, which is funny because he is literally Paul McCartney – a man who has spent roughly sixty years being adored in every room he walks into by basically every human being alive. And yet the emotional engine of the film is still him going “just to be clear, I did NOT break up The Beatles”, which, fair enough, he probably didn’t! But it is quite amazing watching an 83-year-old man with the most successful songwriting career in history still doing a bit of shadow-boxing with Rolling Stone reviews from the 70s. It’s oddly sweet but also slightly surreal. You want to gently tap him on the shoulder and say: mate, you wrote Let It Be, you’re fine

cast_names: Konkona Sen Sharma, Pratibha Ranta, Aditya Nanda, Sukant Goel, Barbara Blum, Sanjeeta Bhattacharya
review_content: Bad, bad, bad. Very bad. It’s the kind of film that seems to want to tackle a delicate and timely subject, but ends up as a clumsy mix of thriller, workplace drama, and a preachy final message. For a while it looks as if something interesting might come out of it, because the premise has some pull and the initial uncertainty works, but it falls apart very quickly.    The biggest problem is that it has no pulse. The story moves as if dragging its feet, repeating situations, stretching conflicts, and circling around an idea it doesn’t know how to develop. Instead of creating tension or unease, it just becomes tiring. And when a film like this can’t even sustain intrigue, it is basically dead.    It also doesn’t help that the characters are so poorly built. They never really feel like people, just pieces of the script arranged to push the next twist forward. The professional conflict, the marriage crisis, and the investigation all lack the force needed to make you care. Everything feels far more artificial than it should.    Worst of all, it seems to want to say important things about power, abuse, social judgment, and queer relationships, but it does so in a very shallow way and, at times, an outright silly one. Instead of going deeper, it simplifies. Instead of unsettling, it lectures. In the end, it lands in a kind of nowhere zone: neither a solid thriller, nor a convincing human drama, nor a serious reflection on anything.    The lead actress tries to hold it together, and there are moments when you can glimpse the film it might have been if it had a much stronger script. But that is not enough. Everything around her is so weak, so mechanical, and so uninspired that not even a strong screen presence can save it.    Overall, it felt like a mess. A film without tension, without soul, and without the intelligence it needed to handle a subject like this. I didn’t like it at all, and I really don’t understand why some people rate it positively.

movie_title: Come See Me in the Good Light
cast_names: Andrea Gibson, Megan Falley, Tig Notaro
review_content: Stories that show seriously ill people nearing death are always difficult to watch. In "Come See Me in the Good Light," poet Andrea Gibson's ultimately lost battle with cancer plays a major role, but the film is fundamentally more interested in Gibson as a person and their life and work than in being overly emotionally manipulative. Even though I'm not particularly into poetry slams myself, Gibson's perspective on life impressed me. The documentary is really heavy-going in places, but at the same time it's also life-affirming, which makes for an effective contrast. In any case, it quickly becomes clear in the documentary that Gibson was a fascinating and creative personality. And this film certainly creates a worthy memorial to that person.

review_content: Finally got a chance to see this a few weeks ago when it made to Austin and it surpassed even my wildest expectations. The entire time I was completely rapt. The fact this was only nominated in the makeup and hairstyling category at the Oscars is a real shame because every facet of this movie is exquisite. I was not at all surprised to find out the two leads had spent 18 months in training for their kabuki performances because everything about them felt authentic. All of the actors truly shined and I was particularly impressed by the younger versions of the leads as well. Ken Watanabe absolutely flawless as usual.    I was blown by both the cinematography and especially the score which was so perfectly handled. For me, this is one of the best films I've ever seen.

cast_names: Ryo Yoshizawa, Ryusei Yokohama, Mitsuki Takahata, Shinobu Terajima, Soya Kurokawa, Keitatsu Koshiyama, Min Tanaka, Ken Watanabe, Nana Mori, Takahiro Miura
review_content: "Kokuho" offers a fascinating insight into Japanese culture and the subculture of Kabuki. Spanning several decades, the story follows the rise of a Kabuki actor with a troubled background and an obsession with perfection. It raises issues such as class society, but also the pursuit of excellence and its consequences.    The structure of the story is certainly not new and is also melodramatic in places, especially when it comes to the personal relationships of the main characters. But the film is strong in its technical execution. The Kabuki performances are brought to life, particularly through the makeup and the score. And the actors are more than up to their difficult task.    I thoroughly enjoyed "Kokuho." It requires a bit of patience, but the film is definitely worth it.

cast_names: Ryo Yoshizawa, Ryusei Yokohama, Mitsuki Takahata, Shinobu Terajima, Soya Kurokawa, Keitatsu Koshiyama, Min Tanaka, Ken Watanabe, Nana Mori, Takahiro Miura

review_content: Not wanting to give away the story, but this movie has every right to relate to Hitchcock. You'll meet a supposedly average-joe tennis coach Tom, around 40 and spending nights drinking, dancing and copulating, and teaching holidaymakers, not very enthusiastically, during the day. Along comes a couple with a seven-year old son, and all changes. Tom becomes a character.   The story involves a supposed crime with the husband going missing, but what really makes the movie special are the things remaining unsaid, you're lucky if the camera gives you a glimpse of an emotion, or a motion being made. There is so much tension, so much desire in so many layers, you're constantly filling in gaps with your own imagination. A beauty of a film, hadn't expected that from the description.

cast_names: Manolo Solo, Maria de Medeiros, Branka Katić, Rita Cabaço, Xavi Mira, Morgan Blasco, Rui Morisson, Ivan Barnev, Luísa Cruz, Pedro Almendra
review_content: [Filmin] A story about homes lost and found, with a serene pace that sometimes feels too slow, and understated acting that can also be a bit too restrained. There's a reliance on coincidences that seem contrived and unnatural, but the characters are complex enough to be engaging. The writing itself also seems overly polished, with dialogues that feel artificial and a forced naturalness that prevents a complete emotional connection with the story. An emotional and at times moving short documentary about long-term inmates at a high-security prison in Missouri, USA. It offers a glimpse into a rare sewing project where prisoners create quilts for vulnerable children – including foster kids and children with disabilities.    The initiative reminds me of similar rehabilitative programs in Europe, like ASV (alternative supportive services). At its core, it’s a fantastic idea: giving convicted individuals a chance to give back in a meaningful and constructive way.    Yet despite the heartfelt theme, I found the film somewhat emotionally flat. Maybe I simply set the bar a bit higher, but the lack of character development makes it hard to fully connect. We never truly get to know these men – their stories, their growth – and that weakens the impact.    That said, it was interesting to learn how complex quilting can be, especially on a larger scale. It demands patience, structure, and time, and that craft element adds unexpected depth.    In the end, the film serves as a gentle counterimage to the often harsh and dehumanizing view of the U.S. prison system. There's a thread of healing here – though not fully explored.    ⭐ 5/10 (The lack of character development unfortunately lowers the score)  Production: 6/10  Cinematography: 6/10  Authenticity: 8/10

I generally like movies with heroes that are too old for this shit, and this is a good one on that level.     The fights are dynamic and well filmed. The story in between is ok, could have been a bit shorter.  The last fight is dragging a bit though, and I'm not a fan of the whole [spoiler]he was just a little boy crying inside[/spoiler] thing, but that's just the ending.    A bit more flashbacks from her beginnings would have been nice. The concept of their agency is original and elaborating more on it could have been interesting: where does that come from, how do they validate/approve their customers, what's the price, how do the customers know of them and find them, etc.    Also the guy seeing his kid while doing his job, that was such an original concept. Not sure it would have fit into the movie, this was not about that and this would have distracted from the main storyline, but it was also weird to come up with [spoiler]it wasn't real[/spoiler], because it was not just one sight, he pursued her until he got caught on camera. cast_names: Ane Gabarain, Elena Irureta, Iñake Irastorza, Laura Iturregi
review_content: Gaua is, above all, a beautiful tribute to the folklore of northern Spain. It is one of those films where reality and myth come together without asking permission. Through several intertwining stories, the plot takes us into the life of a village, a woman, and three elderly women who gather at night—supposedly to do laundry—while, in reality, they weave tales, superstitions, and memories that seem to come straight out of medieval oral tradition.    The attention to detail is simply spectacular. The sets, costumes, and every element that appears on screen are treated with enormous care, achieving a very authentic, dark, and symbolism-laden atmosphere. Everything breathes truth, damp earth, dark night, and that ancestral fear that needs no monsters to strike deep.    However, in its final act, the film decides to take a more dreamlike and surreal turn. It becomes almost a feverish dream, losing some of the terror and obscurantism that it had built so well from the beginning. It can be a little disconcerting, especially if you were expecting it to maintain that more disturbing tone until the end.    And yet, it's hard to completely fault it, because that ending also works as an absolute tribute to medieval Spain, its imagery, its myths, and that very particular way of understanding the world where the magical and the everyday coexisted without clear boundaries.    Overall, Gaua is an atmospheric film, meticulously crafted and deeply rooted in our tradition. It may not end in the most terrifying way, but it does so in a manner consistent with what it wants to convey and honor.

cast_names: James Hyndman, Colm Feore
review_content: Some stories form like shells at the bottom of the sea, slowly building layers of pain until something precious emerges almost by accident. Others shine so brightly on the outside that only over time do they reveal the weight they carry within. It’s in this tension between beauty and suffering that “The Girl Who Cried Pearls” unfolds its dark and delicate fable, using enchantment as bait for a moral discomfort that deepens with every scene.    Directed by Chris Lavis and Maciek Szczerbowski, the short film leans on the classic structure of a grandfather telling a story to his granddaughter, but it quickly makes clear that this is anything but a comforting tale. The man’s account of his miserable youth in Montreal, marked by hunger, abandonment, and survival on the city’s margins, sets a harsh emotional foundation, where fantasy doesn’t function as escape, but as a poetic distortion of reality. The idea of tears turning into pearls is introduced with cruel naturalness, as if suffering could be monetized without consequence, and that’s precisely where the film begins to unsettle the viewer.    The strength of “The Girl Who Cried Pearls” lies in how it turns this fantastical concept into an ethical dilemma. The protagonist realizes he can profit from the sadness of a girl abused by her stepmother, but to do so, he must ensure she remains unhappy, a conflict that grows even heavier once genuine affection begins to develop between them. The script never spells this out in a didactic way; instead, it lets the horror emerge from the simple logic of the situation. Loving someone while simultaneously depending on their pain to survive becomes the film’s tragic core, elevating the story far beyond a straightforward moral tale.    Visually, the stop-motion work is remarkably sophisticated. The puppets carry a deliberate sense of strangeness, with faces that resemble imitation pearls (beautiful, yet artificial) and skin tones that communicate character and emotional states even before any movement. The hands, meticulously animated, are especially striking, whether in moments of gentle touch, the exchange of money, or the offering of a gift, functioning as physical extensions of guilt, desire, and compassion. The sets, built from found objects and mixed materials, create a tactile, richly detailed world that invites repeated viewing.    The lighting and Patrick Watson’s score complete this atmosphere of melancholy. The music doesn’t steer emotion in obvious ways; instead, it seeps into the images, shifting between softness and unease as the narrative layers reveal themselves. There’s a constant sense that a larger story lies beneath the surface, one about exploitation, poverty, memory, and the way we turn other people’s pain into symbolic or material value.    In the end, “The Girl Who Cried Pearls” stands out precisely because of its moral ambiguity and its ability to linger long after the final frame. It’s a short that understands the power of storytelling not as comfort, but as revelation, exposing uncomfortable truths beneath a seductive surface. Among so many animated films eager to charm quickly, this one asserts itself as something rarer: a jewel that does shine, but cuts deep if you hold onto it for too long.

cast_names: James Hyndman, Colm Feore
review_content: I have a soft spot for stop-motion, so the animated short film "The Girl Who Cried Pearls" is right up my alley. The style is amazing and, above all, extremely rich in detail. If you have the opportunity, you should definitely invest the 17 minutes it takes to watch it, because this film is certainly worth seeing and deserves to be labeled a "work of art."

review_content: [Annecy '25] The directors abandon the surrealist atmosphere of their previous short film to create a more traditional story, with a moral that speaks about the sacrifices and actions provoked by greed, inspired by the texture of Hans Christian Andersen's tales and taking as a reference some iconic images such as Man Ray's photograph "Glass Tears" (1932). By expanding beyond the semi-abandoned house where the young woman lives, it allows the story to breathe in environments that simulate the streets of Paris and the Old Port of Montreal in the 20th century, incorporating vintage trams that give the story a special atmosphere, which may also be reminiscent of the classic stories of Charles Dickens.

review_content: My thoughts are all over the place with this one, but let me try to make some sense of the madness. The movie is ambitious, I'll give it that. They tried to do a slapstick whodunnit on a massive cruise ship, and for a second, I was almost intrigued. Almost.  The A and B ending thing is a total gimmick, a cheap trick. Option B is even less logical than the rest of the movie, and that's saying something because the whole film is a Swiss cheese of plot holes. An Interpol officer believing a person can die in seconds from a stab wound? Please. The whole thing feels like they threw ideas at a wall and filmed what stuck.  The pop culture references and meme usage were pretty sharp, I'll admit. And the movie’s intro with the writing of the number 5 was actually innovative. The little callbacks to the older films, the old songs, and the parrot and the monkeys, gave me a hit of nostalgia, but they were completely disconnected from the plot, making the logic go out the window entirely.  As for the cast, it's a mixed bag. Akshay Kumar tries really hard, and his comedy lands sometimes, but it's far from his best work. Riteish Deshmukh and Sonam Bajwa, however, were superb, felt natural, nailing their scenes with ease. Shreyas Talpade was an absolute waste; he looked great at the start and then just disappeared. Nargis Fakhri's attempt at a Nepali accent was so bad it was distracting. Why cast her? On the other hand, Jacqueline Fernandez and Chitrangada Singh were just there for looks and they looked good.  The songs are mostly forgettable; only Lal Pari sticks, and the Phugadi thing was just a cringy, unnecessary mess. Also, the blatant objectification of women, especially with Soundarya Sharma's character, felt like they were pushing a borderline adult comedy vibe that was just not it. And don't even get me started on the lip-syncing issues – it was genuinely bad and pulled me out of the movie more than once.  They had a grand vision for this one, but it's a classic case of trying too hard and failing. It's not the worst thing you'll ever see, especially if you watch it on a free streaming service like Prime Video, but it's a chaotic mess that could have been so much better. It's not good. SIMPLE.

WTF? He was basically like a father to him and he killed him. So sad, but it had a good message in a way.

A forest holds stories the same way an old record holds music: not in words, but in vibrations, layers, and repetitions that travel through time. When someone is willing to listen closely, what emerges isn’t just sound, but memory, warning, and permanence. Starting from this act of attentive listening, “Forevergreen” presents itself as an experience closer to a contemplative ritual than a traditional narrative, using animation as an emotional and spiritual language.    The short film directed by Nathan Engelhardt and Jeremy Spears abandons any classic structure of human conflict and turns nature itself into the protagonist. The forest isn’t a backdrop or a passive metaphor: it breathes, reacts, resists, and reorganizes in response to human intrusion. The absence of dialogue reinforces this approach, shifting understanding away from rational explanation and toward a sensory reading, where sound, rhythm, and image carry the narrative weight. What might seem abstract at first proves remarkably coherent in practice, because the film understands that the relationship between humanity and the environment is rarely expressed through words, it shows up in impacts, scars, and silence.    Visually, “Forevergreen” stands out for its delicacy and aesthetic cohesion. The painterly animation style makes each frame feel like a living canvas, with organic textures, pulsing colors, and movements that resemble a ritual dance. Greens dominate the palette, but never in a uniform way: they shift according to the forest’s emotional state, moving between lush vitality, tension, and melancholy. The fluid motion of the trees and roots, in particular, reinforces the idea of a conscious ecosystem, where everything is interconnected and reacts collectively to external threat.    The soundtrack plays a central role in the experience, functioning as the film’s voice and heartbeat. Through the music, the audience senses the initial harmony, the rupture caused by human presence, and later, the slow process of regeneration. The pacing is deliberately contemplative, which may challenge more immediate expectations, but this choice proves essential to the short’s impact. Engelhardt and Spears seem less interested in shock value and more focused on encouraging reflection, inviting the viewer to slow down and observe, something increasingly rare both inside and outside the movie theater.    Thematically, “Forevergreen” engages with urgent environmental issues without resorting to obvious messaging or overt guilt. The film doesn’t demonize humanity in a simplistic way, but highlights the imbalance created by exploitation and the failure to listen. Its strength lies precisely in its refusal to preach: the message emerges from the aesthetic experience itself, from the contrast between before and after, from the forest’s persistence in adapting even after being wounded. By the end, the feeling isn’t one of total comfort, but of responsibility, a quiet reminder that nature can resist, but it isn’t infinite.    Taken as a whole, “Forevergreen” is a deeply sensitive short film that understands the potential of animation as both a poetic and political tool. In just over ten minutes, it creates a space for listening, contemplation, and necessary discomfort, leaving the sense that we haven’t just watched a story, but witnessed an ancient song that insists on surviving. It’s a work that continues to echo after the final frame, like the distant sound of leaves in the wind, asking not for applause, but for attention.

“Forevergreen” is one of this year's Oscar shortlist entries for Best Animated Short Film, which are supposed to be cute above all else. And with a hungry bear as the main character, who is raised by a tree (!), it's not that difficult to make it charming. At the same time, the film has a neat environmental message, namely that it's terrible to litter nature with our human waste. The icing on the cake is that the animation style is also very appealing. And since the ending is compelling, I give it a solid recommendation. If you have children, you should definitely invest the 13 minutes.

[Annecy '25] The parable of the prodigal son, translated into a story between a bear cub and a tree that acts as an adoptive father, manages to create a universal message beyond its religious background. The artistic direction is notable for its use of computer-generated images to create a carved wooden texture that represents the landscape and the characters. Despite these seemingly rigid designs, the movements of the animated beings are agile and tactile, achieving surprising effects on elements such as the pine branches of the parent tree.

As atuações são muito fracas, com diálogos pouco naturais e direção irregular. review_content: I went into this not really knowing much about it. It was a bit different than I had expected it to be. I wasn’t super into the entire lore and backstory of it all. It’s at least something different but I wasn’t super into all of that stuff here. Despite not being into the backstory I still really enjoyed this movie. It’s a ton of fun and unique enough in its own way. It’s plenty bloody, violent and action packed throughout. The camera work was extremely awesome. I loved the feel of the way it’s filmed and shot. Some of the slow motion shots were a thing of beauty. I would’ve liked this a ton more if it wasn’t so strange and unorthodox. They went full on batshit crazy by the final act. Even with all of the weird things I wasn’t into I’d still recommend checking this out for any fans of horror.  * AMC Crestwood 18

cast_names: Zazie Beetz, Myha'la, Patricia Arquette, Paterson Joseph, Tom Felton, Heather Graham, Gabe Gabriel, Chris van Rensburg, Darron Meyer, Angus Sampson
review_content: Based on the trailer for “They Will Kill You,” I would have expected a relatively shameless rip-off of “Ready or Not.” And while the film definitely heads in that direction, it ultimately finds its own voice, so it doesn't feel completely redundant.    The film’s biggest selling point is the always captivating Zazie Beetz, who gives it her all here. Her co-stars are also well-cast, and I was particularly happy to see Heather Graham again. The filmmaking is also commendable. The action is mostly well-staged, and there’s no shortage of over-the-top bloodshed.    Unfortunately, the film becomes a bit too repetitive in places, which is certainly also due to the fact that the supernatural twist hardly leads to any consequences and the conflicts tend to repeat themselves. The film still has a solid flow, but by the time the finale rolled around, I’d had enough. All in all, “They Will Kill You” isn’t exactly unforgettable, but it’s quite entertaining for 90 minutes and certainly worth a look.

review_content: [Prime] A necessary story to understand how conservative perspectives not only restrict personal freedoms but also use them to justify hate speech. As a narrative, it's markedly inferior to other works that delve into Argentina's recent social history, but it works best in the realm of intimacy, in the personal encounters between women who don't understand what's happening to them or who are fighting to regain their lost dignity. A bit more depth would have been more interesting, but within its superficiality, it works effectively.

cast_names: Priyanka Arul Mohan, Park Hye-jin, Baek Si-hun
review_content: This was such a sloppy movie. The plot was ridiculous. Here comes a Tamil girl who dreams of going to Korea, is shown learning their language, but cannot get a single word across. And how does our dear protagonist get there? Duped by her so-called love interest of nine years and sent off alone on a plane (which she voluntarily boards without waiting for him to even make it to the airport—serves her right). Whoosh! The opening was utterly bad—just what was the point of showing her stand atop an elephant? Nothing makes sense and there is nothing but loopholes. More shenbafoolery and then she ends up working as Yeon-Ok's caretaker—the granny who is actually not in a coma (you would have never anticipated or discovered the truth). Yeonfoolery and a new restaurant and money issues and K-friends—blah, blah, blah. Landslides force Shenba to rush back home to come to her parents' aid if they are alive (does she even speak to them anymore?), where her K-friends immediately follow. Apparently, also, granny dies and the closing scene is an ultimate emotional showdown with tear-jerking edits of the granny being shown as seated in their little restaurant and slowly losing opacity... until she vanishes from the delulu as she has from trululu too. Adobe Premiere Pro Max Ultimate.  Empty characters, empty arcs, flat and hollow redemption (disgusting), but Priyanka did act well. I hated most of the tracks too. I've not felt more numb watching anything else.

review_content: Classic prisoner situation. This limits a lot the possibilities of actions, and there is nothing really original going on for the genre on this aspect. However there are a few frustrating ones, because they're not bad luck but bad action on his part. All the rest suggests that [spoiler]she is Cesar. Whether it is an actual other personality or an entity possessing her in the case the nightmare is real.[/spoiler]    The final scene, with [spoiler]calmly going back to sleep after her grandmother's death, the fact that she knows Cesar, that she has the same nightmare, and Alicia's final words to not be like her, suggests that Elena is indeed like her, and they're both aware of it.[/spoiler] That's a nice finishing touch. However wtf with the people in cars seeing [spoiler]a woman getting on the tracks and doing nothing, and just honking after she's been hit[/spoiler] ?    Forgettable, but not that bad in the genre.

This is my favortie of the Untold series that I have seen. Both main players are dumb as hell and it makes it very entertaining to see from the outside. Gilbert is a known goof still getting in trouble to this day and the documentary allowed him to just let loose and talk his brand of entertainingly stupid. The other guy, Scorpio Sky looking fool, he was dumb too but in way more stereotypical ways and completely shit his career away. Just a hilarious documentary about a comedy of errors by people who then didn't lerrn from those errors. He prayed to God asking if he should bring his gun to the arena and I guess God was feeling like a troll that day and told him yes. If that don't sum up this doc I don't know what would.
Feels so grim ‘rating’ this but thankful for learning of this story and getting the opportunity to reflect. Thoughts and prayers, love and light to all.
If you have seen the Untold films before, this one is par for the course. Not my favorite ever, but a compelling story nonetheless.    Rating: 3/5 - 75% - Worth Watching

review_content: As a sucker for zombie movies—good or bad—Queens of the Dead landed squarely in the middle for me. I’ve absolutely seen much worse entries in the genre, but this one also didn’t give me much reason to ever come back to it. It’s competent enough, has a few decent moments, and understands the basics of what makes zombie content work, but it never elevates itself beyond “fine.”  This ultimately feels like standard AMC+ content: watchable in the moment, easily forgotten shortly after. Not offensive, not memorable—just there.  Rating: 1.5/5 – 60% – Not Recommended to Everyone

review_content: Definitely fun. I loved Dawn of the Dead as a kid (I know) and I could feel the essence of those movies in Tina's work but with a modern, very queer lens. We have the similar aspect of the consumerism driven zombies in the mall with the phone addictions here.  I can see thi films being too much for your average straight horror fan but we loved it. (but also very queer so...)  Amongst the campy fun there were some real moments and some decent performances.  Definitely a slay.

review_content: A very American view of a very British part of WWII. Most of the contemporary audio is by Ed Murrow and his colleagues. Stories from British survivors contribute to the whole tone of the presentation, which is off-hand, somewhat detached from the events being shown. On the plus side, the footage has been sympathetically restored and looks very good. It's just a shame that the opportunity that that footage offered has been rather wasted.

cast_names: Vinayakan, Mammootty, Shruti Ramachandran, Kunchan, Gibin Gopinath, Biju Pappan, Megha Thomas, Malavika Menon, Gayatri Arun, Azees Nedumangad
review_content: A decent thriller, though it follows a fairly predictable formula. The character arcs feel underdeveloped, and the investigation loses momentum through repetition. The clues and details lack the depth needed to keep audiences truly engaged. The cat-and-mouse dynamic between the police and the killer fails to build meaningful tension, and the climax falls flat, capped off by an all-too-familiar cliffhanger ending.    On a brighter note, the film deserves credit for resisting the urge to justify the serial killer's actions through a contrived backstory. The use of classic songs and background score also stands out as a genuine highlight.

cast_names: Anupam Kher, Neena Gupta, Pankaj Tripathi, Konkona Sen Sharma, Aditya Roy Kapur, Sara Ali Khan, Ali Fazal, Fatima Sana Shaikh, Saswata Chatterjee, Darshana Banik
review_content: Marital and love affairs are no longer a crime when it was dealt by Bollywood...  Anurag Basu does it again with his signature hyperlink storytelling. :clap_tone1::ok_hand_tone1:He makes us fall in love with love. A light-hearted, comfort film that make you smile, laugh and cry.

cast_names: Anupam Kher, Neena Gupta, Pankaj Tripathi, Konkona Sen Sharma, Aditya Roy Kapur, Sara Ali Khan, Ali Fazal, Fatima Sana Shaikh, Saswata Chatterjee, Darshana Banik
review_content: Okay, it just came out on Netflix, just waw it and it isn’t perfect, but damn, it kept me hooked, it came out to be better than what I expected. Anurag Basu’s got this knack for weaving messy, real love stories, and this one’s got four of ‘em, all urban and raw. It’s a musical, which is rare for Bollywood, so props for that alone. Pritam’s music? Absolute soul, those songs and the background score hit me right in the feels.   Pankaj Tripathi and Konkona Sen Sharma are comedy gold as Monty and Kajol, though their story gets a bit chaotic at the end, like it’s trying to say two wrongs make a right before fixing itself. Anupam Kher and Neena Gupta’s arc is super sweet, proper heart-melter. Sara Ali Khan and Aditya Roy Kapur’s story has dope character arcs, showing life’s ups and downs in a cool way. But Ali Fazal and Fatima Sana Shaikh’s bit? Ugh, so annoying and kinda unrealistic, glad real life isn’t always like that.  I love how it talks about stuff people usually avoid, bold, “woke” vibes done pretty well, even if it stumbles sometimes. Some folks online are whining it’s all about sex or too explicit, but nah, it’s about feelings, not just physical stuff. It shows life as it is. Also, big win: it doesn’t try to justify cheating like some other films (Aap Jaisa Koi). The second half is a bit slow, and some stories don’t wrap up neatly, but I was never bored. It’s got this magnetic vibe that keeps you watching.

review_content: A tycoon—with his son being a bitter, lost cause and his daughter betrothed to our idli-shop owner Sivanesan's only son—is stuck in an acid puddle.      And the remedy? Punches, kicks, gunshots, fire, and a poetic justice of a batter. Except for the below-par songs, which failed to interest me with either the music or the visuals, it actually managed to keep me thoroughly engaged for over two hours straight.      There was nothing extraordinary about the shots; no commendable artistry in the making. The writing is very linear and predictable. The screen is crammed with cringe jokes and emotional scenes, which lets it be an entertainer. I was absolutely enthralled by Nithya Menon's delivery, by the way.       Quite quaint a story of legacy, love, life and loss. This could be watched once.

review_content: ==Greed, Gore, and Consequences==    A solid slasher clearly inspired by the Saw franchise. It shows how one selfish and greedy decision can destroy another person’s entire world — a simple but powerful message: **don’t be greedy.**    Despite all the negative reviews, as a long-time Saw fan, this film delivered exactly what I hoped for — the same brutal, disturbing, gory atmosphere I felt during Saw 1 through 6. It’s not perfect, but it scratches that classic torture-horror itch, and I would genuinely love to see this continuation as a full franchise.

review_content: Li Shande's success is a grimmer absurdity than Sisyphus's struggle; he achieved his goal, but at a devastating price for others. There’s a fundamental ceiling to this kind of 'logistical fiction,' and this film adaptation has likely reached it.

review_content: The movie is literally the **aura farming** of Pawan Kalyan. It’s enjoyable not because of the storyline, but because of the presentation. The storyline is a basic gangster fight — the same old stuff — but ==the action choreography and background music make it super enjoyable==. Thaman sir’s soundtracks were on point, especially _“Hungry Cheetah”_ and _“Trance of OMI.”_    However, the supporting actors are underutilized, especially Arjun Das. When I saw him in the movie, I got hyped, but it turned out to be for nothing. Hopefully, they use him better in the next part. Also, Emraan Hashmi’s character felt like it could have been developed a little better.

cast_names: Hugo Silva, Megan Montaner, Miguel Rellán, Eva Ugarte, Ismael Martínez, Paqui Horcajo, Álvaro Rico, Chani Martín, Josean Bengoetxea, Itziar Aizpuru
review_content: [Movistar+] Gracia Querejeta's characteristic restraint works against a story where everything is so austere that it becomes tedious, starting with the main character. This is the problem with an adaptation that takes the novel's main plot to reconstruct the events, but loses along the way the reflective undercurrent about life and old age. Only the skeleton of a story remains, in which secondary but essential characters like Felipe are underutilized. The suspense of the first part works, but it heads toward a disappointing development when the secrets are revealed.

cast_names: R. Madhavan, Fatima Sana Shaikh, Namit Das, Manish Chaudhary, Ayesha Raza Mishra, Karan Wahi, Shashie Vermaa, Saheb Chatterjee, Sanjeev Wilson, Beena Banerjee
review_content: Patriarchal extremism is the real villain here, not Tinder or wokeness.  If a man loves his wife, people call him ‘nalla’… if a woman joins a dating app, she’s suddenly characterless.  That one line sums up why this film hit me so hard.  Yes, it’s predictable at times, yes it borrows from the usual social drama template, but honestly? It’s still a very good movie, one that society actually needs.  Directed by Vivek Soni and produced by Karan Johar’s Dharmatic Entertainment, Aap Jaisa Koi blends old-school romance with modern chaos. R. Madhavan as Shrirenu Tripathi is pure gold, shy, sweet, a Sanskrit teacher with the heart of an old-school romantic brought up infused with that patriarchy mindset. Fatima Sana Shaikh as Madhu Bose is fire, vibrant, confident, and magnetic. Together-  They spark, stumble, and shine. Their chemistry don't always work, but when it does....  The film looks gorgeous thanks to Debojeet Ray’s cinematography, switching between the calm of Jamshedpur and the buzz of Kolkata. Scenes like the restaurant confrontation are masterfully planned and hit deep. The music by Rochak Kohli & Justin Prabhakaran, with lyrics from Gurpreet Saini and Raj Shekhar, sticks with you long after the credits roll.  But the real punch here is how it handles patriarchy. Women being judged for dating apps. Women being told they “can’t play cards” or “talk politics.” Families policing every move in the name of “sanskar.” It’s not just cinema - I see this in my own neighborhood, my society, even my family. And maybe you do too. That’s why watching it feels raw and validating.  "Good sanskar means doing household chores… how does that even make sense?"  Now, my biggest problem: the cheating angle. The writing tries to justify it by running it parallel to the dating app theme. But cheating and being on a dating app? Not the same thing. It weakens the story. A stronger choice would’ve been confrontation, divorce, moving forward, not justification. That’s where the film slips.  Still, performances carry it. Madhavan is top-class, Fatima is strong, and the supporting cast (Ayesha Raza especially) add so much warmth and wit.  At the end, Aap Jaisa Koi isn’t perfect. It’s predictable, flawed, yet deeply important. Because cinema isn’t just about entertainment, it’s about holding a mirror to society, and this film does exactly that.

review_content: “It’s not about winning. It’s about finishing what you start.”  I knew I was going to eat this up when I watched the trailer and I sure did. I absolutely loved everything about this movie. It’s a powerful true story about a 59 year old man who goes back to college to play football for his senior year. It sounds pretty ridiculous but it’s a real story.  This delivered on everything I look for in movies. This touched on so many different topics and themes. I was a huge fan of Michael Chiklis here. I’m not on the age he was or trying to go back to school but I found myself relating to his character in a lot of ways. I’ve always loved football but especially loved the way they coupled that with family, romance, fatherhood, regrets and hope. It is technically faith-based but doesn’t go down the preachy route at all. As someone who loves training montages and things like, that this delivered several times on that.  This is really the type of that is made for someone like myself. Do you ever watch a movie and just think to yourself that was made for you? I love walking out of a movie theater and feeling inspired and challenged. This movie challenged me in a lot of ways. It’s a great start to the marathon of movies I’ll be watching at the theater today.  * Triple Feature at AMC Crestwood 18

The new main character kinda stalls the Jester on his Halloween night and I really don't like what they did with her in the third act. Her motivations and decision making made no sense, pick a lane girl.    The Jester is kinda like Art the Clown meets the Djinn in *Wishmaster* with all his tricks, and the tricks here are way better and bloodier than the first. The card kill is the best. I hate it when he kills without doing a trick though, ruins the fun. His appearance gets an upgrade with the mask, hair and dirty suit... I was dying to see what was underneath that mask. It's nice that we learn about his origins, I liked everything about that and the mechanics of his magic. Weird twist at the end that leaves the franchise in a weird spot.

review_content: I'm really surprised that a short film like “Jane Austen's Period Drama” managed to get an Oscar nomination. cast_names: Julia Aks, Ta'imua, Samantha Smart, Nicole Alyse Nelson, Hugo Armstrong, Marilyn Brett, Dustin Ingram, Shirin Enayati
review_content: Laughter is often one of the most effective ways to destabilize traditions that rely on silence to survive. When satire looks back at the past, it exposes not only the absurdity of certain conventions, but also how their echoes still linger in the present. “Jane Austen’s Period Drama” works from that logic, turning historical decorum into raw material for sharp mockery.    The premise is as simple as it is irresistible: right in the middle of a marriage proposal, Estrogenia (Julia Aks) gets her period, an event treated as a full-blown civilizational collapse by Mr. Dickley (Ta’imua), a man who has apparently never heard of menstrual cycles. The over-the-top reaction isn’t random; it serves as a perfect summary of the institutionalized ignorance surrounding the female body during the Georgian era. The protagonist’s escape and the subsequent attempt to cover up what happened become both the narrative engine and a biting commentary on how society chose to invent absurd fictions rather than deal honestly with female biology.    The film nails its approach by fully embracing the aesthetic inspired by classic Jane Austen adaptations. Impeccable costumes, formalized language, and idyllic settings create a world that feels lifted straight out of a 19th-century novel, and it’s precisely that visual fidelity that supercharges the humor. The production design stands out as one of the short’s greatest strengths: bucolic landscapes, richly decorated interiors, and carefully composed frames create a delicious contrast with the scatological content of the dialogue. The almost excessive beauty of the imagery works as bait, keeping the viewer engaged while the satire quietly sharpens its claws.    The humor itself holds nothing back. The jokes are blunt, often crude, and revolve around linguistic misunderstandings, absurd metaphors, and male characters’ total inability to grasp something basic. Still, “Jane Austen’s Period Drama” shows real intelligence by not relying solely on isolated punchlines. There’s a continuous, structural comic build, where each situation reinforces the larger commentary on repression, misinformation, and control over women’s bodies. Even the deliberately caricatured character names function as extensions of that irony, amplifying the satirical effect without breaking the film’s internal logic.    The short’s greatest achievement lies in how it balances entertainment with historical commentary. Even wrapped in broad, unapologetic humor, the film never loses sight of its goal: exposing the backwardness, fear, and misogyny that shaped centuries of silence around menstruation. The approach isn’t aggressive or preachy; it trusts laughter as a pedagogical tool. By revisiting the past with a critical eye, the narrative draws clear connections to the present, reminding us that many of these taboos still exist, just dressed up in new packaging.    In the end, “Jane Austen’s Period Drama” delivers exactly what it promises: it’s funny, visually charming, and surprisingly sharp. It doesn’t aim for extreme subtlety or academic depth, but it knows exactly where it wants to go and how to provoke its audience. It’s a period comedy that uses exaggeration as strategy, turning a historically silenced subject into a satirical spectacle, laughing at the past while precisely pointing out how much of it still refuses to go away.

review_content: Very cute and sweet with pretty animation. I think this is elevated by Folds’ songs that have an honest melancholic tinge that make you feel something even if the short length makes some things like Sally’s turnaround, her unnamed friends, and the happy ending feel a tad unearned. But it’s a children’s special and in an age of eroding institutions a happy ending like that is appreciated, even if a part of me feels like it’d be more of a Peanuts special if it ended with the win being a fantasy of Charlie Brown’s like I thought for an instant it was.  It should’ve been Peppermint Patty as the lead- if you want an aspirational headstrong determined figure who can be surprisingly wistful and melancholic and who’d save camps she is right there, don’t warp Charlie Brown into it. The more I think about it the more the shift of Charlie Brown into a moderately cool older brother talking about ‘Susie’s generation’ and saving the day rankles. For me this special ends as that fantasy.
