cast_names: Luise Heyer, Lena Urzendowsky, Claudia Geisler-Bading, Lea Drinda, Andreas Anke, Susanne Wuest, Gode Benedix, Bärbel Schwarz, Lucas Prisor, Luzia Oppermann
review_content: The German entry for the 2026 Oscars is a bold choice by the German jury. After all, "Sound of Falling" is much more arthouse-oriented than the German entries of previous years. The drama, told across four time periods, is set on a farm in the Altmark region of my home state of Saxony-Anhalt. Specifically, it follows women/girls in the 1910s, 1940s, and 1980s, as well as in the present day, in a non-linear fashion. And the lives of the protagonists are connected in a certain way.    What you shouldn't expect, however, is a classically structured plot. Instead, the narrative is pieced together like a puzzle, regularly jumping back and forth between the different time periods. The focus is primarily on themes that affect the lives of the adolescent protagonists, such as dealing with death and parental expectations, but also topics such as sexual abuse and depression. Those who need lighthearted moments in their films will rarely find them here.    In terms of the production, director Mascha Schilinski has done a remarkable job. The camera work provides variety in the limited setting, and the different eras are also sufficiently distinct from a visual perspective. However, the most powerful element is the use of sound. The sound design is an essential link between the individual stories and allows the viewer to participate in the inner lives of the characters.    Overall, I really liked "Sound of Falling," even though I usually prefer a classic narrative structure. However, I found the decision to adopt a non-linear approach to be quite effective; the acting is strong, and despite the film's runtime of 149 minutes, which is not particularly short, the film flew by. I am certainly curious to see if it will be enough for an Oscar nomination in the end. I think the film probably deserves it.

review_content: 3 Thoughts After Completing ‘undertone’:  1. I was gonna rate this a little lower, but my brain has been dissecting it ever since leaving the theater, and I ultimately felt it deserved more. And I think that’s because I found it to be incredibly aware of all that unnerves us… playing with our senses… knowing that we’ll be looking — and waiting — for something to appear in the vast, unfocused spaces behind our main character. The ending was a testament to that. I was uncomfortable.  2. I found the pacing in the beginning to be almost unbearable. If I had to watch her wash dishes, stare at wall art, or have her postpone her podcast recording ONE more time… I WOULD have rated this lower.  3. I wish the plot could have honed in on a bit more purpose. I don’t mind ambiguous endings, but I don’t want creepy nonsense for creepy sake. And I felt like there was a little bit of that.

review_content: 96 Minutes tries to inject new energy into the familiar “bomb on a train” thriller by upping the stakes with not just one, but two high-speed trains hurtling toward disaster, and a villain who literally texts every passenger to ratchet up the tension. While there are a few genuinely touching moments and imaginative uses of phones that keep the suspense lively, the film often moves too fast (or too fuzzily) to clearly track who’s who among its crowded cast, and the Chinese names can get lost in the shuffle. CGI efforts look especially rough at times, which undercuts the intensity of the set pieces critics say should be the movie’s strong suit. Performances vary from compelling to uneven — some actors ground the emotional backstory better than others — but overall 96 Minutes is more ambitious in concept than it is polished in execution.

movie_title: Eloá the Hostage: Live on TV
review_content: No matter how hard you try to watch it with some kind of critical distance, “Eloá the Hostage: Live on TV” inevitably hits you in very personal places, especially if you lived through those days in October 2008 glued to the TV, following that tragedy like some kind of morbid spectacle happening in real time. The documentary directed by Cris Ghattas faces one of the most shameful and traumatic episodes in Brazil’s recent history head-on, but it does so with an aesthetic and narrative awareness that sets it apart from so many other true-crime titles. There’s no fetishizing the violence and no glorifying the criminal here: the film is built as an act of recovery. Recovery of Eloá Cristina Pimentel’s memory, recovery of what the country lost inside that suffocating apartment, recovery of everything the media, the police, and a portion of society chose to ignore while consuming the tragedy as entertainment.    Narratively, “Eloá the Hostage: Live on TV” operates on two fronts at the same time. The first reconstructs the kidnapping with documentary rigor, using never-before-seen footage, bits of news broadcasts, and testimonies that highlight the absolute absurdity of that coverage. The second and more powerful front gives Eloá her place back as the protagonist, the 15-year-old girl who had her privacy violated, her story distorted, and her humanity reduced to a few minutes of ratings. The film completely rejects the idea that the media was just a “spectator”: it was an accomplice, an active participant, a full-on character, something that becomes even clearer when we rewatch reporters trying to negotiate with the kidnapper live on TV, or when we remember that the young girl Nayara was allowed by the police to go back into the apartment, a total breach of any national or international protocol. The documentary highlights these wounds with absolute clarity, but without turning those mistakes into a spectacle. Every narrative choice feels guided by careful ethics: don’t feed into sensationalism while calling out exactly that.    On a technical level, the film strikes a very precise balance between archival footage, interviews, and emotional reconstruction. Ghattas knows the film’s power doesn’t lie in reenacting the horror (after all, it was already broadcast live to the entire country, and that collective trauma isn’t going anywhere). The editing stitches together the media chaos of 2008 with the intimate voice found in Eloá’s diary, creating a painful collision between the real girl and the distorted version of her that Brazil helped manufacture. The cinematography and score are discreet, respectful, carrying a silent weight that never tries to manipulate the audience’s emoticons, because the impact is already in the reality itself. And it’s exactly this restraint that makes the film even more devastating: there’s no relief, no catharsis, no attempts to soften what happened. Just the raw realization of how badly we failed.    Beyond the factual reconstruction, “Eloá the Hostage: Live on TV” becomes a powerful portrait of issues that still haunt the country. The film lays bare, cruelly but necessarily, how gender-based violence becomes normalized early on (Eloá was a victim of pedophilia starting at age 12, when she began dating a man seven years older, something many people at the time disturbingly romanticized as “young love”). It also exposes how structural misogyny allowed parts of the public to see the aggressor as “a lovesick boy,” while the victim was erased in her suffering. And above all, the film exposes the police incompetence that, mixed with the media’s hunger for spectacle, pushed the situation toward a fatal outcome. Revisiting all this today hits like a punch not only because the story is deeply sad, but because nothing about it feels like the past: everything feels like repetition.    Personally, watching the documentary felt like reopening a wound I didn’t realize hadn’t healed. I remember following those broadcasts so clearly, hours and hours nonstop, like watching a reality show. I was 14 at the time, and there was this collective naivety, this feeling that we were just “watching the events unfold,” when in reality we were participating in a circus that turned pain into entertainment and allowed a kidnapper to become a temporary celebrity. The film forces us to confront that complicity. It reminds us that, in 2008, the entire country hoped for a peaceful ending, but at the same time stayed glued to the screen, reinforcing the logic that turned a brutal crime into TV commodity. And it reminds us of something even more disturbing: we live in a country where, many times, criminals end up receiving more human treatment than their victims.    That’s why “Eloá the Hostage: Live on TV” stands as one of the most necessary documentaries on Netflix. Not just because it revisits one of the most shocking events in recent memory, but because it puts the viewer face-to-face with their own responsibility. It’s a film that moves you, that angers you, that saddens you, and above all, that unsettles you. And that discomfort is exactly what makes it important. It gives Eloá her narrative back (her dignity, her humanity) without turning her into a product again. At the same time, it lays bare everything we still haven’t learned: that tragedies like this don’t just happen out of nowhere; they’re the direct result of institutional, cultural, and social failures that we keep repeating.    Revisiting this crime is easy; what’s hard is accepting how much we helped turn it into part of the national imagination as spectacle. Hard is admitting that things haven’t changed nearly as fast as they should have. And even harder is recognizing that, in some way, we’re still watching it (not live anymore, but edited, organized, available in the catalogue) hoping to understand something that, deep down, Brazil still insists on ignoring.

Despite the acting being questionable at moments I did like several of the actors. I’ve always been a fan of Masey McLain in particular, so I was happy to see her appear in this. I thought the way they structured the story was part of the problem. It just needed some better editing choices. I felt like they kind of rushed through the ending and John’s adult years. With all of that being said I did really like this movie. There’s some great lessons to be learned here.  * AMC Crestwood 18

movie_title: Armed Only with a Camera: The Life and Death of Brent Renaud
cast_names: Brent Renaud, Jon Alpert, Volodymyr Zelenskyy, Anderson Cooper
review_content: The death of American journalist Brent Renaud while reporting on the war in Ukraine is an event that was widely covered in the media. The short documentary “Armed Only with a Camera: The Life and Death of Brent Renaud” serves to provide some context for Renaud's life and death, which it certainly achieves. But the selection of clips is too chaotic to really make for a focused film. I also think that the short format is not the best way to tackle this subject. There should be enough footage of Renaud to justify a feature-length film. Such a film could certainly be much better structured than this short film. It's not bad, but it could have been done much better.

movie_title: Armed Only with a Camera: The Life and Death of Brent Renaud
cast_names: Brent Renaud, Jon Alpert, Volodymyr Zelenskyy, Anderson Cooper
review_content: Documenting war isn’t just an act of courage, it’s an ethical choice that carries irreversible consequences. When a camera insists on staying where everyone else wants to leave, documentation turns into testimony. It’s from that awareness that “Armed Only with a Camera: The Life and Death of Brent Renaud” takes shape, rejecting sensationalism in favor of memory.    The documentary builds its narrative backbone around Brent Renaud’s own life and work, stitching together footage from different conflict zones (Central America, the Middle East, Haiti, Afghanistan, and finally Ukraine) without ever turning horror into spectacle. The structure is simple and straightforward, almost austere, mirroring the same journalistic approach that guided Brent’s work: observe, listen, and record without interfering. By choosing this path, the film steps away from geopolitical analysis or lengthy contextual explanations about the war in Ukraine, focusing instead on the human experience of those who live (and die) in the middle of chaos.    The decision to include images of Brent’s body after the Russian attack is one of the film’s most delicate moments. At first glance, the footage feels invasive, even cruel, but it gains meaning once the camera passes into the hands of his brother, Craig Renaud. By documenting his own brother’s death, Craig applies the same ethical principle that guided Brent: refusing to hide the reality of violence, no matter how painful it is. This gesture turns the film into more than a tribute, it becomes a continuation of Brent’s work, now focused on his absence.    Formally, “Armed Only with a Camera” rejects any kind of excessive stylization. There’s no manipulative score, no sensational editing. The impact comes from the accumulation of testimonies (people who lost children, homes, bodies, futures) and from the way Brent positioned himself in front of them, always with empathy, never with urgency. The documentary understands that the true horror of war isn’t just in explosions, but in what comes after: empty stares, open hands unsure of what to do, sentences spoken with the resignation of those who’ve already lost everything.    The film also creates an intimate portrait of Brent outside conflict zones, showing his relationship with his family, with his brother, and with his own way of being in the world. When it mentions his autism, the documentary avoids easy explanations or romanticization, instead offering a quiet key to understanding why that extreme environment was, paradoxically, where he felt most grounded. In contrast to the superficiality of everyday social life, war offered a brutal kind of truth, and it was that truth he felt compelled to record.    The greatest strength of “Armed Only with a Camera” lies precisely in what it refuses to do. The film doesn’t turn Brent into a martyr, doesn’t point fingers in a preachy way, and doesn’t use his death as an ideological banner. Its goal is more human, and therefore more devastating: to show that he was a real person, with a clear calling, who deeply believed in the importance of giving voice to those trapped between political decisions and weapons they never chose to wield. When Craig says that what Brent valued most were “the people caught in the middle,” the documentary reaches its moral core.    In the end, “Armed Only with a Camera” works like a final speech at a funeral, not to close a story, but to make sure it isn’t forgotten. It’s a restrained, respectful, and deeply painful film that reaffirms a simple, unavoidable truth: there is nothing worse than war, and documenting its consequences isn’t heroism, but necessity. By applying the same ethical rigor to both the life and death of Brent Renaud, the documentary makes one thing clear — even in the face of absurdity, nothing that was done was in vain.

review_content: The Family McMullen is the sequel to 1995's The Brothers McMullen, although you don't need to have seen the original to enjoy this one. Edward Burns does the hat-trick again — starring, writing and directing — and his stamp is evident throughout: a simple, relatable and very down-to-earth story.    The film works as a light-hearted family comedy about love, second chances and the little dynamics that arise around couples, marriage and, of course, the omnipresent influence of the church in the daily life of the McMullen clan. It is suitable for all audiences and maintains a friendly tone that does not seek great revelations, just to keep you company for a while.    Visually, it doesn't complicate things: the editing is very television-like, with simple, no-frills shots, just enough to let the story flow without distractions. Nor does it pretend to be a drama, despite being classified as such; in fact, it is light years away from that label. It is rather an endearing, comfortable and uneventful story.    It doesn't take any risks, but it doesn't disappoint either. It is exactly what it promises to be: an ideal film for a Sunday afternoon, especially during the Christmas season, when you feel like something gentle, cosy and uncomplicated.

As an atheist, the whole abortion debate is something I will probably never fully understand. Of course, there has to be a limit somewhere as to when a pregnancy can be terminated, but a “higher power” should certainly not play any role in setting this limit. The short documentary “The Devil Is Busy” shows how bad the situation has become in the US for women who want or need to have an abortion. Not only do they have to travel extremely long distances, but once they arrive, they also have to put up with being harassed by a bunch of fanatics. However, the documentary doesn’t really reveal anything new. The problems are well known, and it's not exactly a topic that's under the radar when it comes to public debate. In that sense, the film is not particularly groundbreaking.
The editing is OK. Choosing that lady who contradicts herself was not. I was glad seeing those disciples of Christ preaching. God bless them.
This made me sad. Crazy to think that they are just trying to help people and are getting so much crap for it.

When conflict drags on for too long, it stops feeling like a crisis and starts to look like routine. That quiet shift is exactly what “The Devil Is Busy” sets out to capture, observing how a space meant for care ends up operating under a constant state of alert, until no one seems to question it anymore.    The short follows a single day at the Feminist Women’s Health Center in Atlanta and structures its narrative around the exhausting repetition of procedures, interruptions, and confrontations. There are no didactic introductions or long historical explanations: the camera simply watches. From the very first scenes, it’s clear this isn’t an exceptional environment, but a place where stress is part of the daily routine. The constant presence of protesters outside, the policing of the parking lot, the precautions taken to protect patients’ privacy, and the strict time limits imposed by restrictive laws turn medical care into an emotional and bureaucratic minefield.    The film’s central figure is Tracy, the clinic’s head of security, whose presence completely reshapes the documentary’s perspective. An openly Christian woman, she occupies a position that dismantles any simplistic reading of the conflict: someone deeply religious who spends her days protecting women in a space attacked precisely in the name of faith. “The Devil Is Busy” finds its ethical and emotional core in her, not because she offers answers, but because she embodies contradiction. Her work is physical, psychological, and spiritual, and the film makes a point of showing both her professional efficiency and the exhaustion that sets in by the end of the day.    Geeta Gandbhir and Christalyn Hampton’s direction relies on a strict observational approach, avoiding formal interviews or external commentary. Dialogue emerges organically, in the middle of work, short breaks, and restrained moments of venting. This choice is crucial in keeping the film from turning into a manifesto. Abortion is an unavoidable topic, but never the sole focus: what’s really at stake is women’s health as a whole, even as public debate insists on reducing it to a single procedure. When a nurse expresses her hope that one day women’s health won’t be defined solely by abortion, the documentary reaches one of its most revealing moments, not as a slogan, but as accumulated fatigue.    One of the film’s greatest strengths is its refusal to soften the medical experience or turn it into an abstract gesture. “The Devil Is Busy” shows the clinic’s efforts to treat patients with care and respect, from attention to identity and language to attempts at preserving privacy, while also acknowledging the discomfort of the procedure itself, with loud machines and an environment far removed from any kind of idealization. That honesty prevents both romanticization and demonization, reinforcing the complexity of what patients actually experience.    The protesters, always present, function less as characters and more as constant noise, an aggressive soundtrack of judgment and condemnation. The way Tracy talks about some of them, especially the hypocrisy of those who preach selective redemption, adds an unsettling layer to the film. This isn’t just ideological disagreement, but an ongoing exercise of moral power, where certain bodies are deemed worthy of forgiveness while others are pushed into symbolic damnation without ever being heard.    In the end, “The Devil Is Busy” is most striking because of its restraint. It doesn’t try to persuade, organize arguments, or offer neat conclusions. By simply observing a system in motion, the documentary exposes a country frozen by moral, legal, and religious contradictions. Its impact doesn’t come from shock, but from the quiet realization that this routine (absurd, cruel, and exhausting) has already been normalized. And it’s in that normalization that the film becomes truly disturbing, suggesting that as long as women’s health is treated as an ideological battlefield, no one walks away unscathed.

cast_names: Fabio Testi, Yannick Renier, Koen De Bouw, Maria de Medeiros, Thi-Mai Nguyen, Céline Camara, Sylvia Camarda, Sophie Mousel, Hervé Sogne, Barbara Hellemans
review_content: Reflection in a Dead Diamond was such a whirlwind compared to whatever expectations I had walking in. It plays like James Bond on acid — stylized, surreal, pulpy — and then filters that through a distinctly French arthouse lens. It’s chaotic in a controlled way, flashy without feeling empty, and constantly dancing between spy thriller homage and psychedelic fever dream.  I’m not the biggest James Bond fan to begin with, which honestly may have helped. I wasn’t comparing it beat-for-beat to anything — I just got to enjoy the ride. The editing is punchy, the tone is bold, and even when it drifts into indulgence, it never becomes boring.  It’s weird. It’s stylish. It’s definitely not for everyone. But it was a fun ride.  Rating: 2.5/5 – 70% – Worth Watching

cast_names: Zelda Adams, Toby Poser, John Adams
review_content: Mother of Flies is an incredibly unnerving piece of folk horror that fully commits to its witchy atmosphere. The imagery is the real standout here—grotesque, unsettling, and the kind of stuff that lingers in your head long after the movie ends, sometimes in ways you wish it wouldn’t. It leans hard into mood and discomfort rather than jump scares, which makes it feel more oppressive than outright shocking. When it’s firing on all cylinders, this is genuinely effective horror that understands how to make the viewer uneasy.  That said, the pacing does drag at times, and it may test the patience of viewers looking for a more traditionally structured horror film. This is very much a vibes-first experience, and that won’t work for everyone. But for those who enjoy slow-burn witch horror with striking visuals and a deeply unsettling tone, this one absolutely delivers.  Rating: 3/5 – 75% – Worth Watching

cast_names: Zelda Adams, Toby Poser, John Adams
review_content: Wtf, that was an awful movie. The scenary was beautiful, but the acting was atrocious from everyone. The story was boring and dialog was literally cringe inducing. cast_names: Zelda Adams, Toby Poser, John Adams
review_content: This feels like it crawled out of a damp notebook full of unsettling ideas and half finished thoughts. When it’s working, it really works. The atmosphere is thick, grimy, and uncomfortable in a way that horror fans will appreciate. The imagery sticks to your brain like, well… flies.     That said, the movie never fully commits to being great. It hints at deeper themes about guilt, grief, and transformation, but kind of circles them instead of landing the punch. A few scenes feel like they’re there just to vibe rather than move things forward, and the pacing drags in the middle like it forgot where it was headed.    Still, there’s enough here to make it worth a watch if you’re into slow-burn, gross-but-artsy horror. Not a total mess, not a masterpiece. Just a weird little movie that buzzes around your head for a bit after the credits roll.

cast_names: Zelda Adams, Toby Poser, John Adams
review_content: Folk-horror fairytale from the Adams family. Witchcraft, grief, and a cure that costs too much. Simple, eerie, beautifully made.    A father and daughter, cornered by cancer, reach for the woods and meet a healer whose remedies feel older than medicine. The Adams family keep it intimate and tactile: practical effects, crisp sound, images that stick. It echoes their earlier Hellbender work while finding its own spell, balancing tenderness and rot. FrightFest positioned it as their "spiritual sequel" to Hellbender, drawn from personal brushes with illness, and that honesty shows in the way love curdles into ritual. It is small in scope, sharp in feeling, and the witchcraft looks properly lived in.    If you liked… Hellbender, The Witch, The Autopsy of Jane Doe, Gretel & Hansel

cast_names: Zelda Adams, Toby Poser, John Adams
review_content: [Fantasia '25] The best Adams Family film achieves a more convincing character development than in other cases, perhaps due to its emotional connection with reality. It knows how to use elements of witchcraft and necromancy to build terror without being gimmicky, and offers an interesting reflection on the contradictory nature of aggressive treatments to cure illnesses, the need for pain to achieve healing. In a nature that also contains ambiguous connections, where death is only another form of initiation. The directors manage to be reflective at the same time as disturbing, in a film that explores elements of popular horror, folk horror and body horror.

review_content: I continue to enjoy the random horror releases that pop up on AMC+, and Bodycam (2026) fits right into that lane. It’s not revolutionary and it’s certainly not the best horror movie you’ll see, but it scratches that itch for quick, experimental genre content.  The biggest comparison point is something like V/H/S. The concept feels very much like a short film that’s been stretched into a full-length movie. At times that works in its favor — the bodycam perspective keeps things uncomfortable and unpredictable, giving the film a distinct style that helps it stand out.  Even when the execution is uneven, that unpredictability gives the movie a bit of personality. It’s not particularly great, but it’s memorable enough to justify the watch.  Rating: 2/5 – 65% – Not Recommended to Everyone

cast_names: Teja Sajja, Manchu Manoj, Ritika Nayak, Raj Zutshi, Rana Daggubati, Shriya Saran, Jayaram, Jagapati Babu, Raghu Ram, Kishore Tirumala
review_content: Had the ingredients, missed the recipe.  A frustrating rollercoaster of ambition and missed potential. The vision for a grand, myth-infused action fantasy is visible, but the execution is very uneven.  The Good: Some naturally shot scenes are genuinely stunning and cinematic, you can feel the directorial vision what they wanted to make, CGI and action in some scenes is good when considered the budget.  The Bad: Inconsistent visuals, frames suddenly cut to fake-looking AI shots that shatter immersion. Major script flaws, starting with revealing the mother, son, sacrifice emotional core way too early. That reveal should've hit when the protagonist learned it, not upfront. Plot holes everywhere, convenience writing where events happen because the script needs them to, not because they make sense.  The Ugly: Jarring tonal shifts. The unnecessary comedy with the friend gang and cops completely disturbs the serious momets. Cut that forced humor, trim the runtime, let the action breathe.  The Lord Ram connection felt forced and disconnected from the established lore, an odd inclusion that didn't land that we'll.

cast_names: Teja Sajja, Manchu Manoj, Ritika Nayak, Raj Zutshi, Rana Daggubati, Shriya Saran, Jayaram, Jagapati Babu, Raghu Ram, Kishore Tirumala
review_content: Nothing but wasted potential of a great premise. [spoiler]The storytelling is really poor and completely fails to create the magic of _**Hanu-Man**_. All the Puranic connections feel out of place, and ==the movie should have focused more on the adventure genre.== And what is Lord Ram's bow even doing in the story? You’re using one of the most powerful archery weapons without a single day of archery training. For everything, the only explanation given is, *“He is born to do it.”* Come on—even the antagonist goes through a training period under one of the protectors of the nine scriptures. They could have easily shown the protagonist training under **Agasthya Muni**, but no—he doesn’t need to train, he’s just born ready.[/spoiler] What could have been a great sci-fi adventure movie ended up as wasted potential.

review_content: Tried too much and made a mess. Very few positives and disappointment all around. Even it felt like it was rushed in the pressure to release it at Eid. They've taken time to complete it properly. The story starts alright and has an alright first half but the second half is way too long and slow. The subplots are introduced and dragged too much. The screenplay has pacing issues, was pacy where it should've explained and vice versa. The only good thing was cinematography and camera work, it caught an eye. It made the action look better. Some shots looked fabulous the way they were shot. Kajal Aggarwal and Sharman Joshi didn't have much screentime. Salman Khan looked like he always does, however his efforts showed but he should stop doing big boss and all. Sathyaraj was a typical unimpressive villain and the rivalry felt flat. Rashmika had a small but impactful role. The makers tried to give all, love, emotions, social messages, action but all those don't land that way. Emotions feel but not in a perfect way. The action was good though. BGM was alright but the CGI and VFX was bad, even face swapping was evident. However it wasn't that bad like KKBKKJ and Radhey but it is also not one of his good works. He needs better scripts and also execution. I noticed around me everyone says he always looks the same because they see him too much, stop bigboss please.

movie_title: Jordan Jensen: Take Me With You
review_content: I haven't written about her on any other platforms so I guess I'll just leave this here.    I started getting into her material a few years back. She reminded me of Maria Bamford with the whole mental health schtick and all of the funny voices. That, and she's extremely close to my age, I'm pretty sure we're both '91 babies. Generational commiseration matters more than I like to admit as I hit my mid-30's. I sort of became obsessed with her because of how she handled her gender and her views on society, always exasperated about how she was *actually* straight despite her presentation and how shitty liberals are. I used to say that she was like a dudebro with a woman's body and heterosexuality.    Since then Jensen has become somewhat of a guilty pleasure for me. I've come to find her politics to be pretty inscrutable which some would argue comes with the standup territory, especially for women. Finding out that her disdain for small L liberals comes from the right and not the left was disheartening. She's claimed that she's "spiritually Republican" which doesn't surprise me but it does turn me off ever recommending her to friends or admitting that I find her really funny.  In truth she's probably just a small l liberal but that inevitably also shits me off.    The controversy surrounding her has only increased as she's blurted out more and more problematic things on stage and while podcasting. Most recently on Stavvy's World she was caught out just blatantly saying transphobic slurs while claiming ignorance, with Stavvy himself repeatedly having to correct her. Jordan, it's 2025 and you're in the entertainment industry, there is no way you don't know what the slurs for trans people are. It's not the first time she's been in hot water over statements like this, and from my many hours of Jensen content consumption I've deduced that she's sort of post-cancellation, in the sense that she's been viral online for years now and has probably developed mechanisms to insulate herself from the public vitriol. Jensen's a new homeowner in upstate New York while renting an apartment in Brooklyn, she's got what she needs for life whatever the left (or the right) think about her.    All that being said, I still find her hilarious. As I laugh at her many different character voices and facial contortions, the charity I give her goes as far as, "She still has a lot of stuff to work out" and "I think she's gonna end up coming to the same political conclusions I have". But even here on this Netflix special, she brings up Hamas in a derogatory tone, lumping them in stereotypically with ISIS. In no way does she have to condone Hamas but to bring them up while the IDF is murdering children (and not mentioning that fact) is pretty yucky.    I'm rooting for her to reflect on her success especially in the wake of this Netflix special, and not succumb to the right-wing grift (absolutely a possibility).    A weak 7 out of 10, jordanlolstop    - written 10th September 2025

cast_names: John Abraham, Neeru Bajwa, Manushi Chhillar, Hadi Khanjanpour, Madhurima Tuli, Alyy Khan, Qaushiq Mukherjee, Elnaaz Norouzi, Adam Karst, Derek Horsham
review_content: Tries to keep it real with espionage vibes, but it’s a mixed bag. It’s not amazing, but it kept me hooked, so here’s the deal.  John Abraham looks pretty good as Rajeev Kumar, doing his usual intense-but-cool thing. He’s solid, though not mind-blowing. The problem? The rest of the cast, like Manushi Chhillar and Neeru Bajwa, barely get anything to do. Manushi’s on the poster, but her role’s super thin, such a letdown.  The movie looks good visually. Tehran’s gritty streets and Delhi’s vibe pop off the screen, thanks to some slick cinematography. The action’s raw and real, not overdone, which I dug. But the little CGI they use? It's little and it's a good thing but that little also is kinda lame.  The score by Ketan Sodha keeps things tense, and the film never gets boring, even if it drags a bit.  The story’s okay, smart, with some geopolitical stuff, but it feels half-cooked. It’s your typical spy flick, nothing new, and some parts, like John finding allies too easily, feel off. The mix of Hindi, English, and Farsi is cool for authenticity, but it messes with the flow. Also, why put a song in a car chase? A sick score would’ve been way better. And honestly, I expected more from Maddock, they usually nail it.  All in all, Tehran's a decent watch if you like spy stuff. It’s engaging and looks great, but the slow bits, weak side characters, and so-so story hold it back.

review_content: I really got drawn into Avignon. The atmosphere pulled me in right away, the way the city is filmed feels almost like a waking dream, with meaningful silences, gentle direction, and characters that feel real and relatable. There's a softness to the storytelling, a calm, floating rhythm that gives space for reflection without ever forcing anything. I found it beautiful, simple, and sincere.    The film also made me reflect on theatre, the acting world, how others judge you, self-doubt, and what it takes to hold on to your dreams. There’s something very human about it that resonated with me quietly, but deeply.    I was a bit disappointed by the ending though. It felt too quick, a little abrupt. I wish it had taken a bit more time to settle, to land gently. But still, I had a really lovely time watching it. It didn’t shake me to my core, but it touched me in its own quiet way and that already means a lot.

cast_names: Miley Cyrus, Naomi Campbell, Jonathan Rado
review_content: “Miley Cyrus: Something Beautiful” isn’t just a visual album—it’s a full-on sensory manifesto. Alongside the record of the same name, this film, directed by Cyrus herself in collaboration with Jacob Bixenman and Brendan Walter, offers more than just an audiovisual translation of its tracks. It’s a deep dive into the creative psyche of an artist who, almost two decades after bursting onto the scene, has never seemed so comfortable in her own skin. With her ever-changing aesthetic, raw energy, and magnetic presence, Cyrus pulls the viewer through a maze of images, sounds, and emotions that swing between the sublime and the strangely intimate—cementing her place not just as a singer, but as a performer with a truly unique artistic vision.    The two-act structure lets the project shift through different moods and atmospheres, balancing dreamy, introspective moments with bursts of pop grandeur and visual fireworks. From the get-go, it’s clear this isn’t about linear storytelling—it’s about feeling. The hazy opening, full of ambient noise and almost ghostly whispers, sets the tone for something that plays more like a dream than a narrative. With producers Sean Everett, Ian Gold, and Piece Eatah by her side, Cyrus builds sonic landscapes where acoustic instruments, synths, and natural sounds blend into one emotional ecosystem. And at the center of it all is her voice: raspy, raw, vulnerable—equally capable of summoning desperation and liberation.    Visually, the film is all about texture. With diffused filters, layered images, projections, and psychedelic backdrops, the look calls back to experimental ‘90s music videos while staying rooted in current visual trends. Each track comes to life through luxurious costumes and carefully chosen symbolism—like the green fringed dress in “End of the World” or the retro-glam energy of the dancers in “Easy Lover.” None of it feels random; it all connects to the visual language Miley’s been building for years, now with a new level of stage maturity but without losing that spark of mischief. Naomi Campbell’s appearance, with her hypnotic presence and near-mystical delivery, gives the whole thing a futuristic fairytale vibe—and let’s be real: if Naomi’s out here orbiting Miley’s world, then that world clearly has its own gravity.    If “Something Beautiful” doesn’t follow a traditional story arc, that’s not a flaw—it’s part of the point. It plays like an open letter—or maybe a holographic diary—where memories, desires, and past wounds overlap like pieces of a broken mirror. Scenes like “Pretend You’re God,” with its strobe light storm, or the liberating “Golden Burning Sun,” with Cyrus riding a motorcycle through gusts of wind, capture that spirit of visual and sonic catharsis. And even though the film leans into spectacle, it also knows when to pull back. There are silences, still moments, glances that say more than any lyric. In a time when visual excess is everywhere, that kind of restraint feels powerful.    Benoît Debie’s cinematography meets its perfect match in Miley, who moves through his sensual, provocative lens with ease. The camera doesn’t worship her from afar—it keeps her close, almost tangible. That closeness, that raw emotional exposure, is what makes the film feel like a genuine extension of her music. Tracks like “Every Girl You’ve Ever Loved” and “Walk of Fame” blend glamour and pain with real finesse, and the overarching idea of healing a sick culture through art—something Miley talked about in interviews—comes off not as pretentious, but as a sincere attempt to connect something deeply personal with something universal.    “Miley Cyrus: Something Beautiful” isn’t easy to pin down. It’s not just a long-form music video, and it’s definitely not a disguised biopic. It’s a ritual. A trip. A love letter to art pop with a rock ‘n’ roll soul—channeling Bowie and Björk while boldly stamping the name “Miley” in its own loud, unapologetic ink. Honestly, it wouldn’t be surprising if this project ends up being looked back on as a landmark in visual music—one that didn’t need to explain itself, because it knew how to feel. And for long-time fans like me, it’s a gift: intimate, daring, and yes, something truly beautiful.

cast_names: Guillermo del Toro, Oscar Isaac, J. Miles Dale, Scott Stuber, Jacob Elordi, Felix Kammerer, Mia Goth, Christoph Waltz, Kate Hawley, Mike Hill
review_content: A gothic fever dream stitched together with elegance and madness. Frankenstein: The Anatomy Lesson isn’t just a retelling — it’s a dissection of creation itself. Every frame feels like an autopsy of the human soul, soaked in candlelight and guilt. The creature isn’t just a monster here; he’s a mirror held up to the arrogance of ambition and the fragility of empathy. It’s tragic, grotesque, and strangely beautiful — like poetry written in formaldehyde. Mary Shelley would’ve gasped

cast_names: Lacey Chabert, Andrew W. Walker, Cadence Compton, Steve Bacic, Louriza Tronco, BJ Harrison, Alessandro Miro, Dax Belanger, Francisco Trujillo Avalos-Davidson, Kurt Long
review_content: Lacey Chabert is on the case in _She’s Making a List_. When a Naughty-or-Nice inspector gets involved in the life of a young girl that she’s assigned to evaluate, she starts to question the whole naughty or nice system. Chabert gives a strong performance and has good chemistry with co-star Andrew Walker. And the film uses a clever narrative device of having Chabert break the fourth wall to explain to the audience how she does her job and how the naughty or nice system works. However, the plot kind of falls apart in the last act and doesn’t tie up the various subplots very well. Still, the comedy’s rather fun and delivers some good laughs. _She’s Making a List_ is entertaining, but it has some problems that hold it back.

cast_names: Vini Jr., Antonio Rüdiger, Carlo Ancelotti, Gustavo Hofman, Karim Benzema, Neymar, Roberto Carlos, Thibaut Courtois, Toni Kroos
review_content: Some films are born with a sentence already written. Baila, Vini is not just a portrait of Vinícius Jr.’s meteoric rise at Real Madrid. It is, above all, a direct and brave statement against the racism that continues to plague Spanish football stadiums. And that’s exactly where many choose to cover their ears.    The documentary starts strong, revisiting his childhood in Brazil, his arrival in Madrid at just 18, and his evolution into one of the world’s brightest stars. But soon it shifts toward something far more uncomfortable and courageous: his blunt and unapologetic denunciation of the racism he has endured in Spain, culminating in that infamous match at Mestalla, now etched in recent football history.    The images of the crowd shouting “monkey, monkey” are painful—at least for those willing to feel that pain. Sadly, many don’t. Worse still, they deny what’s obvious, justify the unjustifiable, or attack the messenger. So it comes as no surprise that the documentary was bombarded with negative ratings on platforms like FilmAffinity or IMDb, often orchestrated by fans who felt targeted. Some have even threatened to sue the producers over the subtitles, as if that were the real problem.    Truth be told, Baila, Vini doesn’t reinvent the documentary format. It’s straightforward: interviews, archive footage, narration. But it doesn’t need more. Its power lies in the message—one of a young star who, despite being one of the best players in the world, dares to stare racism in the face and speak up. And in a country where some still believe that racially abusing a Black player is “just part of football,” that takes courage.    Those who accuse Vinícius of provoking, exaggerating, or acting should ask themselves why their anger isn’t directed at those who hurl racist abuse at him week after week. And why defending “Spain’s image” seems to mean hiding racism instead of fighting it.    Baila, Vini will be ignored, mocked, or dismissed by many. But it will be remembered. Because beyond football, it stands as a powerful testimony to a truth that too many still refuse to accept. And that’s exactly what makes it so essential.

review_content: Autumn Reeser and Niall Matter star in _We Met in December_; a Hallmark take on _Serendipity_.  During a layover two strangers have a magical night together, but when they try to find each other again a series of mishaps keeps them apart. Both Reeser and Matter give strong performances and have good chemistry together. Also, the script does a nice job at balancing the A story of the two lovers searching for each other with the B story flashback of their night together; making them feel like a couple and getting the audience invested in their romance. And the film has a nice message about living in the now and the roles people play in our lives. Charming and fun, _We Met in December_ is a lovely holiday treat.

I could care less about Lyra as a NA champion. She is still too wooden for me, and you can see cogs spinning in her head thinking about her next move. No wonder she botched a cover and went for the top rope instead.  Becky can always be relied on to put a newbie over though. I know people generally enjoyed Gunther/Pat McAfee atrocity, but it did nothing for Gunther's credibility, and reminded us once again that we were watching a scripted show. These "for entertainment" matches with non-wrestlers have no place in WWE, in my opinion. The main event was RKO central; is the ref okay? Let's go Cena!

cast_names: John Cena, Randy Orton, Pat McAfee, Walter Hahn, Dominik Gutiérrez, Pentagón Jr., Rebecca Quin, Jacob Fatu, Andrew Galloway, Shaun Ricker
review_content: Good show. I thought the 4-way was the best match of the night. Jacob Fatu is a can’t miss superstar. It’s him and Bron.   Very good match between Lyra and Becky also. They went from duelling chants to 100% Let’s Go Lyra by the end, which is an achievement. Becky Lynch continues to be ever reliable in these spots.   Penta/Dom was great.   McAfee/Gunther was cute but Pat got too much in the match and they could’ve achieved the same thing in half the time. I don’t have an issue with the match itself. It’s a detour for a bully heel who’s taking his frustrations out on a broadcaster and the crowd were into it, but I didn’t need Pat Mac fighting spirit. Pat’s a better wrestler than he is a commentator though.   The main event exceeded my pretty low expectations and I had fun watching it. The RKO spam on the producers earned a pop. That said, there’s been a real lack of clean finishes in WWE recently and it’s causing some fatigue when watching now. Four of the five matches had outside interference/ref bumps. The main event had three ref bumps. That’s nuts.   John Cena’s big main events don’t need the overbooked shlock. The people in St Louis were with the match he and Randy were working and reacting to it like they were tearing the house down. It was structured like Cena couldn’t be trusted to have a match that would work for the live audience without hats on top of hats to create drama, not realising it’s Cena’s retirement tour. The fans are gonna be into it no matter what. And the match was trending in a respectable direction and the crowd was in love with it.   Hilariously, the overbooking didn’t kill the crowd like it did at WrestleMania, they stayed with it and changed This Is Awesome, but it was all excessive and unnecessary.   The match was never boring, which is a funny evaluation for this pairing, as most of their previous work is. I’m just disappointed because this was an opportunity to reset this increasingly alarming WWE PPV main event trend and just have a classy wrestling match. They appeared capable but then couldn’t help themselves.   Overall, I found Backlash to be an easy watch with good undercard matches and appreciated how unrelentingly loud St Louis were.

review_content: It’s my first go at this story since I haven’t seen Pariyerum Perumal, and it hits. Shazia Iqbal crafted this love story wrapped in caste issues, and, it starts strong. They tried to show both sides, and there were some good arguments and some debatable as usual but then they try to juggle way too much, poverty, language barriers, every kind of discrimination. It drags, sometimes, not much but it does. Could’ve cut that extra stuff and kept it tight on the love story and caste angle.  The cast pulled out great performancs. Triptii Dimri was amazing, after being typecasted in some of her latest flicks, she showed why she's a great actor. You’re not just a pretty face, girl! Siddhant Chaturvedi is solid as well, but that Bhopali accent, naah, Slipping in and out like they forgot halfway through. Kinda annoying. Anubha Fatehpuria as Neelesh’s mom stole the show for me, she was so strong in every scene. And Sourabh Sachdeva as the villain was terrifying. This dude’s a straight-up psycho who thinks caste hate is his duty.  The music is perfect. The songs and background score just fit, lifting every moment without overdoing it. But yeah, the overstuffed story and those accent fumbles held it back from being a banger. Also the ending could've been better. Still, it’s bold, and I respect it.

review_content: I don't usually comment (because anything I have to say has been said many times before by others), but I feel the need to comment here. -- The location is beautiful, the setting and concept sold me, and while I wasn't sure about Meghan Carrasquillo at the start, her character and performance very quickly grew on me. I was intrigued by the Firewatch profession, lifestyle and routine, how she spent her time, her background and reasons for being there, choosing that career path, etc. And believe me, the sci-fi/horror (even mystery) aspect had me excited, anticipating and expecting much more at that point.    Unfortunately, that's exactly where the movie fell flat for me. A sudden freefall for that whole part of the movie. I _truly_ wanted to give this a much better rating for most of it (I'm not a critic, just some average, open-minded movie-lover) -- but, sadly, the last quarter of the movie just took a bad turn for the worst and left me feeling so disappointed....

The lead doesn't quite have the tools to carry what is essentially a one-hander for 40 minutes, but the cinematography of the forest is beautiful and sets the atmosphere well. Once the blue goo shows up, though, the Goosebumps-quality effects, laugh-out-loud bad acting, and nonsensical story beats tank the film. Skip it.

cast_names: Italia Ricci, Scott Mechlowicz, Zachary Golinger, Terence Rosemore, Justin Matthew Smith, Allie McCulloch, Jay DeVon Johnson, Troy Rudeseal, Vince Eisenson, Craig Kolkebeck
review_content: The Confession has a promising premise — a mother (Italia Ricci) returns to her childhood home, discovers a recording of her dead father confessing to a grisly act, and watches her son slide into increasingly unsettling behavior — but the film never turns that premise into anything memorable. Director Will Canon leans heavily on familiar horror shorthand and mood pieces without developing the emotional core or building real tension. What should feel ominous often plays as predictable, and the mystery at the center is both telegraphed and underexplored.    Positives    Ricci gives a committed lead performance and tries to sell the film’s stakes.    A few isolated scenes have effective atmosphere and a pleasingly concise runtime (1h 27m) keeps things from overstaying their welcome.    Negatives    The script is thin on character development; motivations and relationships feel sketchy.    Pacing is uneven — moments that should breathe are rushed, while quieter scenes drag.    Scares rely on clichés and jumpy beats rather than genuine dread.    The resolution is unsatisfying, leaving more questions about execution than about story.  Overall, The Confession is an earnest indie horror with flashes of potential but too many missed opportunities to recommend. Worth a look only if you’re curious about Ricci’s turn or collecting recent low-budget horror — otherwise it’s easy to skip.

review_content: It's definitely been a while since we've had a Depeche film. I really enjoyed it and am so glad it exists. My only complaint is that it only briefly touched on the topics and themes of the album as well as the current era of technology. However, it is a very intentionally thoughtful film. It encourages introspection and human connection, falling in line with Depeche Mode's international legacy (or rather the core of art and music in general). It is a Depeche Mode film first and foremost, so the majority of the film is the concert, but the tidbits in between left me wanting more. Ironic?  Can't wait to watch it again!

review_content: Was expecting worse, not that it's awesome, but was still entertaining, always leaving you guessing who lives and who dies (I won't spoil it). Before watching, I've read a few reviews on IMDB that heavily (viciously) criticized its CGI, and all I have to say is: it's just fine, in fact I was encouraged to watch it because they came across as extremely biased reviews; I've seen US movies with bigger budget and worse CGI.    The plot itself is not particularly original, although the setting (submarine) and the predator (Kraken) is something I don't remember having seen combined in this way. Usually I think of submarine movies as these West vs East (or Nazi Germany) war thrillers. The movie is at least a 5/10. The acting isn't great but also isn't the bottom of the barrel, and the same could be said for the script. The cinematography is probably the better part, even though the main themes are ice/water, tight spaces.    The soundtrack is lacking, at best. It's very repetitive, at some points it almost doesn't sound like it's trying to match what you're seeing on screen.    The biggest problem for me is the duration though, this was stretched to 2:10 hours or something like that. I get that there was necessity to create at least a couple backstories and tension scenes, but this movie could have done with 1:45 hours at most.     Anyway, you're not losing some hidden gem. The overarching theme (submarine movie), is probably the most you'll get out of it if you're into the genre.

review_content: Caveat - This is a Hallmark movie     Hallmark movie rating 8.5/10  Regular rating 7.5/0    From the website  https://www.hallmarkchannel.com/christmas-at-the-catnip-cafe/about-christmas-at-the-catnip-cafe    Olivia Pierce (Cahill) is a marketing executive from Oakland, California who is this close to realizing her dream of purchasing a condo, but she’s a bit short on the down payment. When she learns that her late great aunt left her half of a cat café in upstate New York she thinks it’s the Christmas miracle she needed – as long as she can sell and close the deal before the holidays. Olivia’s plan hits a bump in the road when she meets Dr. Ben Kane (Campbell), the veterinarian who owns the other half. The café serves an important role in the town – helping homeless cats find forever homes – and he has no intention of closing up shop. Christmas is the most important time of the year as they plan many activities to help raise funds and get them into the black. Olivia and Ben strike a deal – she agrees to help him plan the fundraising events at the café over the next three weeks in exchange for Ben promising not to drag his heels on selling at the end of that time. Unless, Ben notes, she ends up changing her mind – a lot can happen in three weeks, after all. As they work together and a spark between them grows, Olivia becomes part of the neighborhood, gains an appreciation for the café’s adorable feline inhabitants and what it means to Ben, as well as to the entire community. And when she learns the surprising reason her great aunt opened the café in the first place, that just might be the cat-alyst for change that will give them all a Merry Christmas. Starring Erin Cahill and Paul Campbell.    Definitely a show for cat lovers!    Nicely done - the usual Hallmark tropes with some nice twists    I have seen Paul Williams in a lot of shows but I have to say that he really seemed to enjoy his role in this one.

cast_names: Minami Takayama, Kappei Yamaguchi, Wakana Yamazaki, Rikiya Koyama, Yuji Takada, Ami Koshimizu, Show Hayami, Megumi Hayashibara, Takayuki Yamada, Mizuki Yamashita
review_content: The story revolves around a mysterious case connected to the past of both Detective Kogoro Mouri and Detective Yamato—famously known for his single eye.  It all begins 10 months ago, when Yamato was shot during a mission in the icy mountains, and everyone thought he had died.  But fast forward 10 months, someone connected to Kogoro’s past is murdered in Tokyo.  This leads Kogoro and Conan to Nagano to investigate—and what they uncover is far beyond what they expected. The case might even be tied to an old national security incident.    As for the mystery and thrill? They were top-notch 🔥  As always, Conan is the smartest one in the room.  But this time, the story is deeper and more challenging—I genuinely enjoyed every moment of it.    My advice? Pay close attention while watching! There are moments where you'll find yourself going, "Wait, what just happened?!" 😅    And if you really want to grasp everything, I highly recommend watching the related episodes before seeing the film:  516–517 / 557–561 / 653–654 / 754–756 / 808–812 / 983–984 / 1123–1124    Once again, huge thanks to Spacetoon – The Youth of the Future for making this premiere experience possible.    My rating: 8/10

cast_names: Kajol, Ronit Roy, Indraneil Sengupta, Jitin Gulati, Gopal Singh, Dibyendu Bhattacharya
review_content: First of all, that opening title card was Beautiful. The word “Maa” written in Hindi, simple but powerful, set the mood perfectly. For a few minutes I thought this was going to be something special.  The first half builds good tension, especially around the rituals and atmosphere, but the moment the tree demon shows up, everything collapses. The CGI is laughably bad, and instead of horror I felt like I was watching a rejected superhero-fantasy villain. Honestly, if they had kept the demon in the shadows, it could’ve worked. Show less, scare more.  Acting is mixed. Ronit Roy is outstanding, easily the best performer here. He plays menace with real weight, even though the visuals around him ruin half the effect. Kajol is fine, good in bursts, but never fully commanding. The rest of the cast drifts between okay and forgettable. Oh, and the forced Bengali accents on some actors felt odd.  There are some interesting elements: motherhood, mythology, patriarchy. But they are buried under weak execution . Horror becomes fantasy, fantasy becomes superhero action, and by the climax it’s so over-the-top that it doesn’t even feel like the same movie anymore. Add confusing subplots (is girl pregnant? why Kajol was gonna kill her?), and it just turns messy.  Music is a saving grace, Usha Uthup’s “Kali Shakti” slaps. The Kali Puja scenes and cultural visuals look gorgeous. But pacing drags, editing falters, and scares are basically absent.  And compared to Shaitaan? Not even close. That film was sharp, scary, consistent. Maa feels like a confused cousin. Maa had ambition and Kajol’s star power, but ends up more of a fantasy-drama with clumsy CGI than the myth-horror it promised.

cast_names: John Cena, Alexis Cabrera, Walter Hahn, Carla Gonzalez, Rhea Ripley, Cody Rhodes, Joshua Fatu, Logan Paul, Shaun Ricker, Pentagón Jr.
review_content: WWE’s Money in the Bank 2025 turned out to be a solid, albeit slightly underwhelming, night of wrestling. The two Money in the Bank ladder matches were intense and packed with surprises—Seth Rollins clinched the men’s briefcase amid chaos, and Naomi pulled off a stunning win in the women’s match. However, neither MITB victory particularly excited me, as neither winner felt like a compelling future world champion as they both could get there without the briefcase.  I also noticed that the absence of a world title match on the card made the evening feel a bit less momentous than usual. Still, with WWE’s Premium Live Event format, it’s tough to have a truly bad show—lasting intrigue, decent match quality, and that surprise return of R-Truth (aka Ron Killings) during the main event all helped lift the card.  Overall, a solid but forgettable night—few standout moments, but no real letdowns either.  Best Match: Men’s Ladder Match  Rating: 3/5 – 75% – Worth Watching

cast_names: Vijay Deverakonda, Satyadev Kancharana, Venkitesh V P, Bhagyashri Borse, Baburaj, Ayyappa P. Sharma, Manish Chaudhary, Rajkumar Kasireddy, Keshav Deepak, Navya Swamy
review_content: Opened Netflix found Kingdom, and Vijay Deverakonda is straight-up fire but the movie ain't. Alright, picture this: a slick Telugu spy thriller with Vijay as Soori, a cop on a mission to find his brother Siva (Satyadev, who’s amazing). Their chemistry was good, when they both were on screen, the screen presence is felt. Vijay’s a beast, time and again he's proven his versatility and Satyadev matches him. Venkitesh V.P.’s villain is evil, you can feel it but Bhagyashri Borse, yes she was undeniably gorgeous, but her role’s so small and motivations unclear. And her dynamic with Vijay, so fast and so forced.  Anirudh’s music rocks, theme’s stuck in my head, and it’s not his usual style. But it's a banger. Action’s tight, with Soori outsmarting everyone, not just punching. Gowtam Tinnanuri directs with style, and the visuals were also okay. Big win: no cringey masala tropes - no item songs, no cheesy lines, no explicit romantic scenes.  But, oof, the screenplay’s shaky. Start feels slow, then it’s zoom, no time to feel the emotions. Story’s predictable, ripping off KGF and Salaar vibes. Cartel drama, chosen one stuff, and a Part 1 ending? Ugh, really? Also, why do guys with guns run in with knives? Just shoot, man! The brother conflict could’ve been epic but resolves too fast.  Still, Vijay carries it, Anirudh’s score is chef’s kiss, and both brothers get solid screen time. It’s Vijay’s ambitious project, and it shows, he gave his all.

review_content: Royal Rumble 2026 was ultimately a fine event, but it once again reinforced why this style of show just isn’t for me. Four hours for four total matches leaves very little to actually sink your teeth into, and the pacing always feels dictated by spectacle rather than substance. The two Rumble matches themselves were perfectly serviceable, but both winners left me cold, which is a tough hurdle for an event where those outcomes are the selling point. Add in a surprisingly thin list of surprises, and the novelty wears off quicker than it should for WWE’s most concept-driven PLE.  Nothing here was outright bad, but very little felt special either. When the most notable surprises are the Bella Twins and Powerhouse Hobbs, it’s hard not to feel underwhelmed. Especially coming just a week after a stronger Saturday Night’s Main Event, this felt like a reminder that the Royal Rumble concept carries the show more than the execution itself.  Best Match: Gunther vs Styles  Rating: 2.5/5 – 70% – Worth Watching

cast_names: Emma Corrin, Nicholas Galitzine, Maika Monroe, Amir El-Masry, Charli xcx, Richard E. Grant, Felicity Jones, Safia Oakley-Green, Josh Cowdery, Markella Kavenagh
review_content: 100 Nights of Hero has the candy pop aesthetic of an Emerald Fennell movie, but its writing lets everything else down. Conceptually, this is an interesting premise that has stories within stories and tries to showcase how misogyny seeps into these women's lives. The problem is that everything feels too superficial. Amir El-Masry's character is not even present for most of the movie, so while he obviously mistreats Cherry, it's never really explored beyond the very low-hanging fruit that would make even modern misogynists claim he was in the wrong. That's part of the problem. The movie has women being unable to read and write, and they are treated purely as vessels to birth heirs. However, the movie portrays this type of social structure in an almost cartoonish way that prevents it from really feeling insightful or sharp. Similarly, the story within the story, a fairy tale about a character named Rosa, or even the framing story about the background of Hero, are all similarly given such surface level plots that it almost works against the themes of the movie, creating almost a caricature of men versus an honest portrayal. I'm not trying to say that the "all men are evil" concept is flawed. I'm saying that by not showcasing the more realistic and insidious ways that sexism can rule these women's lives, the movie almost plays into this narrative that since women can read now, men are no longer guilty of being horrible. That basic writing is present in Hero and Cherry's characterization and arcs as well. Their relationship and dynamic never feels genuine because it is never actually properly developed. Manfred is also just kind of there, his presence in the movie never being the actual threat that the movie seems to think it is. It also has weird pacing, with the nights literally going by at random. If the characters also have no understanding about the passage of time, neither will the audience. It just ends up making the "100 nights" a pointless conceit other than trying to link it to the fable One Thousand and One nights. All that said, I do think the sets, the costumes, this quirky style to the narration, the neon-bathed visuals, and the quick editing set to glitzy needledrops is all great. It has good cinematography and style. It also has a strong cast that is letdown by the weak script. I really think there was a lot of potential here, but they should have put a lot more effort into the story and writing because it really feels like an afterthought compared to the aesthetic.

cast_names: Rebekah Kennedy, AJ Bowen, Emily Goss, William Gabriel Grier, Sean O'Bryan, Sean Whalen, Raquel Rojas, Susan Gayle Watts
review_content: Traumatika opens with a haunting past that refuses to stay buried. A cursed artifact, an abused family, and a demon named Volpaazu intertwine across decades, from a child’s horrific home life to a grown-up survivor’s descent back into evil. What begins as an intimate tale of trauma and possession spirals into a chaotic collision of timelines and tones.    The first act is gripping: a young boy trapped with his unhinged, possibly possessed mother, trying to survive as reality blurs around them. It’s claustrophobic, emotional, and genuinely frightening. But as the story expands into the aftermath and a talk-show-style reckoning years later, it fragments. The film starts feeling like several different horrors stitched together, part The Exorcist, part Evil Dead Rise, part slasher sequel.    When Traumatika focuses on grief and inherited pain, it’s powerful. The performances, especially Rebekah Kennedy’s chilling turn, carry real weight, and the sound design keeps your nerves raw. Yet the constant narrative jumps and tonal pivots undercut the emotional core. You can sense a sharper film hidden beneath the noise, one that might have been genuinely devastating.    It’s ambitious, bold, and sometimes brilliant, but too often feels like chaos pretending to be depth.    If you liked The Dark and the Wicked, Evil Dead Rise, or When Evil Lurks, you’ll find some memorable horror moments of buried in the mess.

review_content: I love these types of documentaries, as we all want to know what happened to Cleopatra, and people like Kathleen Martinez have brought us so much closer... Having things actual prints of Her is so rare, so the more that exist, are more validation that Kathleen and team are on the right track, as all they have done is uncover objects that have historically been "proof" of existence.    I very much appreciate their approach, they being Kathleen and Bob, as Bob joined Kathleen, and they both seem to have shared a scientific approach, where they are happy to have ideas, but they want proof, and they put the time in to prove it, real time.   No sensationalism, they aren't promising anything, all they have done is point out what they have done, what they found, what they are doing, why, and what is next.  It is exactly what you want to see from a Scientific endeavor of any sort.

review_content: Rachel Boston stars in the schmaltzy Hallmark film _The More the Merrier_. When a small town is hit by a snow storm on Christmas, the hospital gets overwhelmed but tries to brighten things up for its patients so that they can still enjoy the holidays. Boston is charming as always and has chemistry with co-star Brendan Penny. But the writing is bad; just about ever character’s a saint, doing good and paying it forward. It’s just heavy melodrama after melodrama; premature births, battles with serious medical conditions, etc., etc. It’s too much. _The More the Merrier_ is an overstuffed and taxing drama that, while it has its moments, doesn’t work.

cast_names: Rachel Boston, Brendan Penny, Lucia Walters, Aadila Dosani, Carmel Amit, Iris Quinn, Donna Benedicto, Jerry Wasserman, Marlie Collins, Cai Holm
review_content: Caveat - It's a Hallmark movie    Hallmark rating: 8/10   Normal rating: 7/10         Here's the summary from the website:  Alice (Boston), an emergency room doctor at a small, rural hospital, always volunteers to work the Christmas Eve overnight shift and goes the extra mile to make the holiday merry and bright for patients and staff. When the hospital gets snowed in, Alice and Brian (Penny), the newly hired top-rated cardiologist, find themselves in the middle of a bona fide baby boom when three women – including the only OBGYN for miles around – go into labor on the rarest birthday of the year. As they team up to bring these bundles of joy into the world as the night wears on, Alice, Brian and the rest of the staff also work hard to make the holiday feel festive for all the patients. Though they may have different approaches to medicine – and to life – there’s a spark between them and by the end of their shift it’s clear that this may be the Christmas Eve that changes them both forever. Starring Rachel Boston and Brendan Penny.    https://www.hallmarkchannel.com/the-more-the-merrier/about-the-more-the-merrier    Two of the top Hallmark actors with a few cameos from others    Holiday romance set in a hospital - wow wouldn't it be nice to go to a community hospital like this ...no long waiting lines with happy rested  staff.     Rachel and Brendan work well together, especially with their verbal sparring. The story was as little different which was nice.   Felt warm and fuzzy and connected to the character stories by the end - which is why I watch these movies at this time of the year.     Hallmark fans won't be disappointed.

The animated short film “Snow Bear,” about a lonely polar bear searching for companionship, is pretty cute. The animation style is kept quite classic, which is certainly effective. And, of course, there is a climate change message that everyone will easily understand. I wouldn't necessarily say that the film is overflowing with creative energy, but it efficiently conveys what it sets out to achieve. It's definitely worth watching.
A beautiful story with an important message.

review_content: Review Singkat: My Daughter is a Zombie (2025) My Daughter is a Zombie mencoba menawarkan ramuan unik antara genre zombie comedy dengan drama keluarga yang hangat. Film ini menceritakan perjuangan seorang ayah, Jung Hwan (Jo Jung Suk), untuk menyembunyikan dan "melatih" putrinya, Soo Ah (Choi Yu Ri), yang merupakan satu-satunya zombie tersisa setelah wabah berakhir. Namun, terlepas dari premis yang menjanjikan dan penampilan memukau dari Jo Jung Suk sebagai ayah tunggal yang penuh kasih, film ini terasa terseok-seok dalam beberapa aspek teknis dan naratif. Pacing dan Penempatan Komedi yang Mengganggu Kritik utama tertuju pada pacing yang terlalu pelan di beberapa bagian, terutama di paruh tengah. Film ini seolah terlalu asyik dengan "interludes slice-of-life yang bertele-tele" (seperti disebut salah satu ulasan), yang membuat momentum cerita sering kali menghilang. Masalah pacing ini diperparah dengan penempatan jokes yang terasa memaksa dan membuat sequence kacau. Film ini sering melakukan transisi nada yang terlalu tiba-tiba, beralih dari adegan drama yang menyentuh menjadi slapstick yang terkesan konyol tanpa ritme yang pas. Alih-alih menyatu, momen-momen komedi tersebut justru terasa disjointed dan merusak ketegangan yang sudah dibangun, meninggalkan kesan yang tidak konsisten. Aspek Visual dan Produksi Dari sisi produksi, terdapat indikasi terlihat sekali budget pas-pasan yang memengaruhi aspek visual dan sinematografi. Beberapa adegan aksi zombie dan latar belakang futuristik Neo-Tokyo (walaupun hanya disinggung sebagai kota yang ditinggalkan) terasa kurang maksimal. Walaupun demikian, patut diakui bahwa film ini berhasil menutupi kekurangan budget tersebut dengan chemistry yang kuat antar pemain, menjadikan heart atau emosi keluarga sebagai fokus utama, bukan horor atau efek visual gore yang mahal. Meskipun film ini meraih sukses besar di box office Korea karena keberaniannya mencampurkan tragedi dan komedi keluarga dengan tema penerimaan (menurut beberapa ulasan daring), bagi penonton yang mengharapkan ketegangan dan narasi yang mulus, My Daughter is a Zombie mungkin terasa seperti zom-com yang masih terlalu lambat dan sedikit canggung dalam eksekusi humornya.

But in the first part, it achieves some particularly successful moments regarding the use of sound as a metaphor for personal traumas.

review_content: The story's intentions seem clear in its exploration of the fullness of old age, despite difficulties and ungrateful children. However, the script's lack of substance—unable to avoid clichés, resorting to emphatic dialogue, and succumbing to mediocre sentimentality and cloying do-goodism—ultimately undermines the film's intentions, leaving it superficial and affected. It shines in its sensory appeal, but falters in its emotional impact.

review_content: The plot twist was quite predictable, but the action scenes were solid. The narrative pacing falters — cutting from high-tension fights to oddly placed lighter moments, like a cake-testing scene. As with many Malaysia films, the background music often overpowers the dialogue, especially in the beginning of this movie.   A nod to The Godfather is evident when Ghaz assumes leadership and his wife witnesses the traditional hug (instead of hand-kissing) gesture.   But I have to say, the dialogue between Wan Hanafi Su and Zamarul Hisham had a different kind of weight to it — very cinematic.  You can tell the difference when veteran actors deliver their lines. 🔥  Syafiq Yusof should really give these veterans, and others like them – a full lead in his next action flick.

review_content: Like making a soufflé with a toolkit: a lot of devices go underused.    A writer in residence begins to suspect the communal AI of bad intentions.    Dalloway wants to make The Voyage Out To The Lighthouse but gets stuck in Jacob's Room.     The intriguing premise, nice cast (including a tragically underused Anna Mouglalis) and competent directing are comprised by plot points that are introduced and then ignored (medication, a daughter), as well as a hit-or-miss plot that grossly exaggerates how hard it is for AI to steal content.     Dalloway is based on a book (_Flowers of Darkness_) by a writer in residence about a writer in residence but then heavily edited by writer / director Yann Gozlan to make it a clone of his 2021 film _Black Box_. Ironic, because the movie is about AI robbing artists when the director basically plagiarizes himself.

review_content: WWE Crown Jewel 2025 managed to overcome many of the usual frustrations that plague modern WWE events—yes, it still had the typical five-match structure and far too many ads and promos—but the in-ring product itself was thoroughly enjoyable. Every match felt purposeful, and while the pacing could’ve been tighter, the overall presentation delivered a big-event feel that lived up to its hype.  The clear standout was Cena vs. Styles—a true love letter to professional wrestling. It wasn’t just a nostalgia act; it was a perfectly scripted, technically sharp showcase between two veterans who know exactly how to tell a story in the ring. That match alone elevated the entire card, making Crown Jewel 2025 one of WWE’s stronger offerings of the year.  Best Match: Cena v Styles  Rating: 4/5 – 85% – Would Recommend

review_content: Imagine your uncle leaves you an inheritance worth 15 million...  But it turns out—it's not money.    Let me tell you what happens:    A guy and his cousin find out that their late uncle left behind a will, saying they’re inheriting something worth 15 million!  Naturally, they get excited.  But when they go to claim it… plot twist! 😳  The 15 million isn’t cash—it’s goods.  And not just any goods… weed—a massive stash of hashish! 💀    Now they have to smuggle it without getting caught—and in the most clever way possible.    The direction was well done, and the cinematography? Honestly, amazing.  The movie is fun, lighthearted, and genuinely enjoyable.    It’s a comedy, and that works completely in its favor.  Sure, there are a few unrealistic moments—but the humor makes up for it.    Personally, I wasn’t expecting much—  especially since most Egyptian comedies nowadays aren’t that funny.  But this movie? Different story.  I was laughing out loud—  my brother even told me, “Chill out! Keep it down!” 😂    And truly, whoever said “acting is half the film” was right.  Essam Omar and Taha El Dessouky absolutely nailed it.  Their chemistry is effortless, and they have a natural charisma that lights up the screen.    Bottom line?  It’s a fun, hilarious movie that’s totally worth watching ✨    My rating: 8/10

review_content: Goosebumps After Dark (now with a Pumpkin-Necronomicon!)    It never tries to be scary scary, and that’s its charm. The movie keeps everything pure PG no T&A to speak of, not even a cheeky wink and the blood never really flows. The gore’s so mild you could eat dinner through it.    But what it lacks in shock, it makes up for in soul: every frame drips with 1990s Goosebumps nostalgia, and the soundtrack absolutely nails the mood with synths and chimes that feel pulled from an old Nickelodeon bumper. By the time the “pumpkin-bound spellbook” shows up, you’re either grinning or groaning but either way, you’re in the right kind of Halloween trance.    Verdict: A cozy, corny little scare that trades blood for autumn color and heart. Feels like curling up under a blanket after trick-or-treating and flipping on SNICK.
