cast_names: Luke Baines, Gwen Van Dam, Tatjana Marjanovic, Audrey Neal, Erik Fellows, Princess Elmore, Gary Kasper, Kathy Butler Sandvoss, Michelle Pokopac, Mark Wilson
review_content: I've watched my fair share of disturbing movies before but I found this absolutely sickening! Maybe the fact that it's based on the true story of The Siberian Ripper made it even worse.    Luke Baines is terrifying in the role and the things he does, there's no word for. Strong gore and nice effects even though the camera doesn't always show what's happening probably due to budget constraints. I got nauseous in the second half especially and had to put it on pause and it's because of the gore, sure, but the mindset of these characters is what was really getting to me. The mother is somehow even more disturbing at times and the relationship they have is sorta fascinating.    My biggest complaint is it focuses on the acts more than the psychology of it all, we barely scratch the surface with these people and I wanted more characterization. It gets repetitive too and the acting in the first half was a lot worse than in the second half for some reason. Very unpleasant watch overall but that's clearly the intention. Don't watch it while or after eating!

review_content: The music alone makes this worth watching, but the story is emotionally resonant and the animation is actually good (especially for a studio who hasn't dipped their toes into anything larger than an OVA). It's a shame that it's not longer, though, and you can tell that the creator wanted to do more, so they only do what absolutely needs to be done to tell an interesting one-off story. If there would ever be a chance to do so, I'd love to see more.

cast_names: Haruna Mikawa, Shoya Ishige, Hitomi Nabatame, Wataru Tsuyuzaki, Riho Sugiyama, Haruka Minami, Shinya Takahashi, Yuji Kameyama, Naoto Kobayashi, Kouichi Souma
review_content: Surprisingly, I’m the target audience for this. There is emotional sincerity and warmth behind this story of two people leaving their home to find a new life somewhere else. As a 20-year-old student, in some ways this did hit home for me, to the point I slightly teared up in the end. Maybe I was a little envious too because I don’t really relate to the feeling of “home” like our main character does; also why the conclusion of her finding her way back home didn’t resonate fully. And while the story is sincere, the execution is stereotypical with traditional norms, which isn’t necessarily bad, but it’s ultimately not that interesting, and it feels like something you’ve already seen.    [spoiler]Even the twist is like Your Name.[/spoiler]    This criticism, however, is mostly reserved for the main narrative. The narrative didn’t delve much into the world-building and the imagery (possibly rooted in Thai culture?), and I don’t have any knowledge on this subject, but I think that’s where a lot of personal stuff for Garnt probably came into play too. Visually this anime is quite polished; I like the animation a lot (although there is the tonal inconsistency of the guy having more realistic/measured expressions while the girl is a lot more moe). But I think the backgrounds are very flat and the colors are dull, a more vibrant aesthetic could’ve helped to sell the sense of wonder.    With all that in mind, an original anime is an accomplishment and also something this industry needs more of. I also hope Garnt ensured this project had a healthy schedule, and this could be a spark moving forward: community-led or influencer-led passion projects with proper funding and planning that would provide an alternative to the bleak industry landscape we have right now.    6.5/10

cast_names: Stephen Malkmus, Scott Kannberg, Bob Nastanovich, Mark Ibold, Joe Keery, Nat Wolff, Fred Hechinger, Logan Miller, Griffin Newman, Tim Heidecker
review_content: Back in  ’90s  there were bands you discovered and bands you *lived with*. Pavement never asked for devotion, but somehow they collected it anyway    Catching them in ’91, supporting Sonic Youth on the *Dirty* tour, felt like stumbling into a secret. Two drummers in glorious disarray, Malkmus tossing off lines like he’d rather be everywhere else, and yet the whole room leaning forward as if decoding scripture. They weren’t polished; they weren’t trying to be. That was the point.    By the time Brixton ’99 rolled around, the end was already baked into the atmosphere. Those final shows had the energy of a band both exhausted and mythic—songs collapsing into feedback s, a crowd singing along like they knew they were witnessing the last page of a magazine that would never be reprinted.    And then the reunions, years later—less resurrection, more time capsule. The same crooked melodies, the same shrug‑and‑smile delivery, the same reminder that some bands don’t soundtrack a moment; they soundtrack whole eras of your life.    Cult band? Maybe. Just a band? Sure. But for those who were there, they were a compass—pointing somewhere between irony and sincerity, noise and beauty, youth and whatever came after.

cast_names: Stephen Malkmus, Scott Kannberg, Bob Nastanovich, Mark Ibold, Joe Keery, Nat Wolff, Fred Hechinger, Logan Miller, Griffin Newman, Tim Heidecker
review_content: [Mubi] A biopic that confuses and interests in with the ability to invent and reinvent the story of a cult band from the 90s. It works in the ironic look at traditional biopics, jukebox musicals, actors who play great singers and the diverse ways in which an indie band can sell itself to the market, grumbling while being part of what it claims to hate. Without the line between fiction and reality being clear, the feeling that remains is that this simultaneously rebellious and flattering film seems to reflect the contradictions and the personality of a band allergic to success.

cast_names: Stephen Malkmus, Scott Kannberg, Bob Nastanovich, Mark Ibold, Joe Keery, Nat Wolff, Fred Hechinger, Logan Miller, Griffin Newman, Tim Heidecker
review_content: There are movies you enjoy and then there are movies that completely overwhelm you. Pavements did just that to me. I’ve been crying from the first second to the last, carried away by emotion, memory, and music. This is not just a documentary; it’s a strange and beautiful hallucination about my all-time favorite band, Pavement, and about Stephen Joseph Malkmus, who’s long been something between a musical idol and an emotional reference point for me.    The film is not made to convince new fans. It doesn’t explain much, nor does it follow a clear linear narrative. It’s messy, meta, ironic, sometimes even absurd—but deeply heartfelt. Some will adore it, others will detest it or simply not understand a thing. But for those of us who have carried Pavement in our veins since the ‘90s, it feels like a gift. A rare, chaotic, and unpredictable gift, just like the band itself.    I found it bold, innovative, and refreshingly different from typical rock docs. There’s a museum (both real and surreal), there's a jukebox musical, and there’s even a fake biopic that hilariously hits every cliché of the genre while still conveying the real drama behind the band’s history. And in between, the music—always the music—guiding every emotional beat, every twist of nostalgia. When Circa 1762 started playing, I broke down. It’s one of my favorite, most overlooked songs from their catalog, and hearing it in this context felt like a private message.    If I miss something, it’s that Carrot Rope doesn’t close the film. That song, that farewell disguised as a joke, would have been the perfect ending. But maybe that’s also part of the spirit of Pavement: never giving the expected, always choosing the offbeat road.    Would I love for them to reunite for real, to tour again, to make a movie, to turn the museum into something permanent? Of course. But for now, this film is enough to make me cry like the first time I heard Gold Soundz. A kaleidoscopic, loving tribute to a band that changed my life—and continues to do so.

cast_names: Andrew Santino
review_content: Andrew Santino: White Noise is a consistently funny, easy-to-enjoy stand-up special that delivers exactly what fans of his expect—quick wit, confident delivery, and plenty of laughs. It’s not necessarily groundbreaking, but it’s a sharp, well-rounded set filled with clever observations and that trademark Santino charm. Some jokes land harder than others, but the pacing and energy keep things engaging from start to finish.  It’s the kind of special that feels effortlessly watchable—funny enough to recommend, but not one that demands rewatching. A solid entry in Santino’s growing comedy catalog and a reminder that consistency can be just as rewarding as brilliance.  Rating: 3.5/5 – 80% – Would Recommend

cast_names: Masami Nagasawa, Koji Seto, Tetsushi Tanaka, Naomi Nishida, Hiroki Konno, Tōru Shinagawa, Ken Yasuda, Jun Fubuki, Hirotaro Honda, Kintarô Hara
review_content: Dolls are one  of the basic themes in horror. It's been done over and over. And yet Dollhouse manages to stay engaging, mostly by switching the focus along the way.    In the first part the daughter dies. This is weird. As a mother, who would leave 4-5 really young girls running around in the house and leave for groceries ? To a place so far she needs her bike. Not for one urgent thing but for a whole basket. Not even warning anyone when we've seen all the other mothers are just next door and are here. Who does that ?? This is crazy.  But anyway, she finds a doll and treats it like her daughter. Probably would have been boring to stay at that.    Second part, she gets pregnant and they have a new baby. The doll is forgotten for years until their daughter picks it up. Now we're in the classic living doll phase. It's a bit weird too. Why does Mai accept it and keeps the doll when it hurts her and gets her in trouble. When the other girl is bitten by the doll couldn't she have said something ? And as time passes more people witness the living doll, and they're still all pretty cool about it. The mother in law has the doll actually walk to her and climb on her back. Why doesn't she freak the fuck out a bit more than that ??    Really cool scene when the mother sees the doll with a paper bag on her head and beats it up, only to realize it was her daughter. This would have made another great focus switch, but it was clearly happening too soon in the movie to be real. I think later in the movie I kept waiting for a moment where an attempt to destroy the doll would turn out to be the daughter, but they never used it again.    Probably very japanese, the girl has marks all over her body. Not only the father does not know, but it doesn't surprise anyone that he doesn't. Though the marks are not all new, and it doesn't seem like the mother noticed either.    By this point most people are convinced. Third part is the exorcism. Several attempts, with successive experts. Change of pace, we don't see the daughter anymore, and not the doll too much either in the beginning. This is what made the movie the most interesting I think. It didn't get stuck in the potential too cliché of the first two parts.    Loved it when the temple calls back to say "yeah, we'll make an exception for your doll, and even send someone to pick it up". Or when the cop just discarded the polaroid. Had the whole cinema burst out laughing.    The youtube video thing was really great. Fun and useless on the first watch. Then half an hour later the exorcist watches and is like: [spoiler]Dude, there's an extra person there[/spoiler] !    But this also has a weird part. Despite being injured, given the acknowledged level of danger, how can the exorcist [spoiler]just abandon and let them deal with it on their own[/spoiler] ?? This is just insane.    Not so sure about the ending. Suddenly out of nowhere the doll has the power to [spoiler]show illusion to people[/spoiler] ?? She never exhibited that before. Not sure how the scene where he's hitting with the shovel really fits. He was not hitting his wife, so what ? He was just hitting thin air ?  Nice that it mirrors the [spoiler]youtube video, they're coming back with an extra person[/spoiler] but it's not like in the beginning where [spoiler]the doll is supposed to replace the daughter[/spoiler], it should not make them forget [spoiler]they have another one[/spoiler].    Despite some weird inconsistencies in each of them, the breakdown in different phases with different focus and using different aspects of classic doll horror gives it good pacing and keeps interest up over time. All that with relatively small need for effects. Great job.

movie_title: Soyut Dışavurumcu Bir Dostluğun Anatomisi Veyahut Yan Yana
cast_names: Haluk Bilginer, Feyyaz Yiğit, Hatice Aslan, Bige Önal, Şevval Sam, Murat Onuk, Kubilay Penbeklioğlu
review_content: The Missing Link of Contrast: Yan Yana  While Yan Yana is a successful adaptation overall, it suffers from a notable casting mismatch. Haluk Bilginer proves to be an excellent choice, portraying his character’s depth and dignity with his signature mastery. However, Feyyaz Yiğit’s presence fails to truly convey the "ghetto" spirit central to the story.    Instead of Yiğit’s unique absurd style, someone like Hasan Can Kaya, who naturally embodies street culture and the class divide, would have made the contrast far more impactful for the audience. Although this casting choice slightly clouds the story's sincerity, Bilginer’s towering performance still makes the film well worth watching.

review_content: AEW Revolution 2026 was a superb event and a perfect example of why AEW continues to dominate the PPV space. The in-ring product delivered exactly what you expect — incredibly high highs, standout moments, and a diverse mix of match styles across a long but rewarding card.  What really elevated this show, though, was everything surrounding the wrestling. The returns, the surprises, and — most importantly — the storytelling. This didn’t feel like just another event; it felt like a true culmination of months of build. Multiple arcs paid off in satisfying ways while simultaneously laying the groundwork for what’s coming next.  That balance is hard to strike, and AEW nailed it here. Big moments with purpose, not just spectacle.  Best Match: Takeshita v Moxley  Rating: 5/5 – 95% – Highly Recommend

review_content: Wrestlepalooza ended up being a tale of two very different stories. On one hand, it was a major spectacle—WWE’s first grand PLE on the new (and pricey) ESPN deal. That alone gave the show an air of importance, bolstered by the highly anticipated returns of Brock Lesnar and AJ Lee. A couple of the setup matches early on were strong, setting the stage for what could have been an all-timer.    On the other hand, the execution didn’t quite meet the sky-high expectations. The world title match landed as average, the mixed tag was fun but didn’t elevate beyond novelty, and the squash match felt flat—made worse by airing opposite the buzzworthy Hangman/Fletcher showdown elsewhere. The women’s title match ended up being the standout of the night, which says a lot about the overall card. In the end, it was fine, but not nearly the groundbreaking event it wanted to be.    Best Match: Vaquer vs Sky    Rating: 3/5 – 75% – Worth Watching

review_content: Fight Another Day (2024) - ❤x5    The concept isn't bad, but the execution is not.  It looks and feels like a bad, cheap, 80's B-movie - not in a good way. review_content: Clash in Paris delivered a solid card with good results across the board. One of the small but appreciated changes was extending beyond the standard five-match PLE structure—while adding only one more bout isn’t a huge jump, it still made the event feel fuller and better paced. The wrestling itself was strong, and the Paris crowd gave it an added atmosphere that made the show stand out.    What takes a bit of the shine off is the context—it’s bittersweet knowing this marks the end of the Peacock era for WWE PLE events. Still, judged on its own, Clash in Paris was a fun, worthwhile show and a fitting way to close out this chapter of WWE’s streaming history.    Best Match: Cena vs Paul    Rating: 3.5/5 – 80% – Would Recommend

review_content: An almost 2.5 hours long snoozefest where the first hour kinda interesting with how russia turned to capitalism and democracy at the end of 80s, early 90s. But other than that the interesting things horribly slowly and painfully leave the building.     We are left with Paul Dano's soft, but sometimes firm Badanov. Who is everything, but interesting or captivating. When Putin finally comes around you strongly grab Jude Law's shirt, because he's the only one who can save you from falling asleep. He doesn't brings his A-game, but still better than anything else. Vikander and everyone else is a side note character.     A terribly boring recount of how they grab the power and formed Russia from seemingly democratic state to this not so seemingly democratic dictatorship. It barely shows the mechanics at play here. The movie claims the beginning that at the end of the day it still a fictional work. Yet they don't dare to take a stance on the character's and their motivations. Just a surface level characterization and implying their motivations.    Sure, some of the machinations and power moves could have been interesting, but they were written so dry that you just want it to get over it. A great fucking miss of a movie that didn't dare to take a strong stance on what they think of their own characters. I wish I could get back my time...

review_content: Blind dates and personal ads have long been fertile soil for thriller movies, and the proliferation of dating apps has only accelerated the trend. Match, a Tubi original directed by Danishka Esterhazy (best known for the clever and entertaining Slumber Party Massacre requel in 2021), attempts to stand out among the plentiful fish in this sea not by subverting the genre or saying something profound; but through its batshit enthusiasm for throwback grindhouse shock.     Match wants you to squirm, and never cuts away from the disturbing. There’s a certain levity to the absurdity, the “they really went there?” distancing the audience from the horror. Not Psycho Goreman levels of insanity, not Martyrs levels of self-seriousness, but something more palatable and fun in between.    Read more here: https://substack.com/home/post/p-179006958

review_content: WWE Elimination Chamber 2026 was a perfectly average event. Like most gimmick match PPVs, the structure always frustrates me a bit — the match card gets shortened, but the overall runtime of the show doesn’t really shrink with it. That often makes the event feel padded despite having fewer matches.  That said, the Elimination Chamber itself is still one of my favorite gimmick matches in wrestling. The structure naturally creates tension and storytelling opportunities that many other stipulations don’t, and it remains one of the few gimmicks that consistently feels meaningful.  The only real negative here was the “surprise” being Danhausen. He’s entertaining and has a fun personality, but it felt like another small step away from the wrestling itself being the main focus of the product — something WWE occasionally leans too hard into.  Nothing about the show was bad, but nothing truly elevated it either. Just a solid, middle-of-the-road event.  Best Match: Women’s Chamber  Rating: 3/5 – 75% – Worth Watching

cast_names: Omar Sameer, Rona Toledano, Meyer Levinson-Blount, Sara Raed, Oron Caspi
review_content: For a student project, “Butcher's Stain” is quite impressive. The short film about an Arab-Israeli butcher who is wrongly accused of tearing down a poster showing the hostages abducted by Hamas in his supermarket is effective in showing you the paranoia and numerous prejudices that spread in a brutal conflict such as the Gaza War. However, the film could have benefited from more focus. In particular, the subplot about the butcher's child seems completely superfluous. Without this part, the film would certainly have been much more effective.

cast_names: Omar Sameer, Rona Toledano, Meyer Levinson-Blount, Sara Raed, Oron Caspi
review_content: In times of collective trauma, the urge to point fingers often speaks louder than any real commitment to justice or nuance. Mistakes (real or imagined) become nothing more than excuses to confirm prejudices that were already in place. It’s from this corrosive dynamic that “Butcher’s Stain” builds its tension.    The short follows Samir (Omar Sameer), an Arab butcher working at a supermarket in Tel Aviv, who suddenly finds himself accused of tearing down posters of Israeli hostages after the October 7 attacks. The accusation is never proven, but that hardly matters: the film makes it clear early on that factual truth carries far less weight than the identity of the accused. The narrative focuses on that cruel space between “maybe” and “we already know,” showing how collective fear quickly turns into institutionalized discrimination, disguised as moral concern or patriotism.    Formally, “Butcher’s Stain” is extremely aware of its setting. By confining much of the action to enclosed spaces, especially the supermarket’s break room, the film heightens the sense of claustrophobia and constant surveillance. Every sideways glance, every uncomfortable pause in conversation, every slightly tight framing contributes to a kind of suspense that doesn’t rely on loud music or plot twists, but on the steady accumulation of microaggressions. The direction favors restraint, allowing violence to surface more through subtext than through direct action.    Omar Sameer’s performance is the film’s emotional core. His Samir is an ordinary, exhausted man, worried about survival and about the child who depends on his paycheck. The performance avoids heroics or dramatic outbursts, opting instead for a quiet dignity that makes the injustice even more infuriating. The script is smart in showing that Samir isn’t politically militant within the story, he simply exists, works, and tries to move forward. Even so, that alone is enough to make him a suspect.    Thematically, the film exposes a deeply perverse logic: the idea that condemning the death of children or questioning state violence can be interpreted as support for terrorism, depending on who is speaking. “Butcher’s Stain” reveals this moral inversion without fiery speeches, letting it emerge through coworkers’ behavior, the management’s bureaucratic language, and the ease with which an identity-based accusation overrides any presumption of innocence. It’s a harsh portrait of how fragile coexistence becomes when fear starts setting the rules.    Still, despite the strength of its premise, the film struggles to reach its full dramatic potential. The narrative build is powerful, but the ending feels more abrupt than transformative, as if the film stops at the very moment it could push its consequences further. The impact is immediate and sharp, but it leaves the sense that the short peers into the abyss without taking the next step. That doesn’t invalidate its critique, but it does limit its emotional and reflective reach.    Overall, “Butcher’s Stain” is a necessary, unsettling, and brave film, especially coming from such a young filmmaker. Its strength lies in its ability to condense a historical, political, and moral conflict into a single, everyday act of unjust accusation. Even if it doesn’t fully exhaust the potential of its central idea, the short remains a disturbing portrait of how prejudice operates not through grand speeches, but through small, fast, and devastating decisions, the kind that, in the end, leave the hardest stains to remove.

Donkeys and telescopes, what's not to love?    2025 (98th) Academy Award nominations: Best Documentary Short Film

It's a strange film with as a friend said some classic shots. I'm not sure what to make  of it. I'd like to read an intent document or something. Not gonna get my vote for doc but it's an interesting film none-the-less.    The Documentary Short category must be lacking if this is among the nominations. That said I don't think it'll beat the abortion clinic one.  THe
At first it didn’t click for me, but once it did I really liked it. Not my pick for the Oscar.

There's something about it that I'm starting to like. But my question is, what are we documenting here? I mean, it's a short documentary nominated for an Oscar. I don't know if we're documenting the beauty of the desert, the observatory, the donkeys (?). On the positive side, the cinematography is great; you can tell they did a really good color grading in post-production. I'd love to know what camera they used to film it. Anyway, as I saw in a comment below, I think it's the most questionable Oscar nomination in recent years.

It’s striking how some cinematic experiences feel like staring out the window of a bus that isn’t moving: beautiful, quiet, but without any sense of displacement. At times, they also resemble ambient music that plays endlessly: pleasant to the ear, yet incapable of leaving any real trace once it ends. That’s exactly the ambiguous zone where “Perfectly a Strangeness” settles, relying almost entirely on atmosphere while giving up any commitment to a more solid narrative foundation.    Alison McAlpine’s short sets out to explore “what a story can be” without dialogue, sustained only by sound and music. Set at the La Silla Observatory in Chile’s Atacama Desert, the film follows the daily routine of three donkeys (named Palaye, Ruperto, and Palomo) as they wander among the scientific structures used to observe the universe. The camera lingers on their movements, their gazes, the flick of their ears, creating visual parallels between animal behavior and the idea of galaxies constantly orienting themselves in space. The conceptual intention is clear, but the execution remains overly thin.    From a technical standpoint, “Perfectly a Strangeness” is flawless. The cinematography sensitively explores the contrast between the desert’s ancient vastness and the almost alien presence of the telescopes, while the camera finds beauty both in wide shots and in the smallest details. Ben Grossman’s score deepens this sensory immersion, shifting between daytime delicacy and nighttime grandeur as the domes open toward the sky. Everything works in perfect formal harmony, like a carefully composed audiovisual installation.    The problem arises when you try to identify what the film wants to communicate beyond this aesthetic contemplation. There’s no dramatic progression, no conflict, no articulated reflection that goes beyond vague suggestions about time, transience, and the coexistence of the natural and the technological. The absence of dialogue isn’t an issue in itself (silent cinema has proven its power countless times) but here it comes paired with an absence of structure. The short observes, but doesn’t investigate; it hints, but doesn’t develop.    The implied comparison between the donkeys and the machines, between humanity’s forgotten past and contemporary scientific ambition, remains stuck at a surface level of abstraction. “Perfectly a Strangeness” seems to believe that simply placing these elements side by side is enough to generate meaning. Without a stronger guiding thread, though, the film becomes a series of beautiful images that never coalesce into a thought. Watching the animals walk around, as charming as it may be, doesn’t automatically turn into a statement.    This conceptual fragility raises an unavoidable question: what is the documentary’s purpose? If there’s no story to tell and no idea to truly explore, what justifies its length and, more importantly, its circulation in spaces traditionally reserved for works with greater reflective density? The feeling is that the short confuses minimalism with a lack of content, as if refusing to say anything explicitly were, by itself, a sufficient artistic gesture.    “Perfectly a Strangeness” holds up as a pleasant sensory experience, marked by gorgeous cinematography and careful sound design, but it fails to offer any substance beyond the immediate impact of its images. It’s a film you watch with momentary interest, but one that doesn’t provoke, doesn’t question, and doesn’t linger. In that light, its presence in a competition for recognition as a documentary feels less like a celebration of the form and more like a symptom of increasingly blurry criteria around what, exactly, is considered worthy of being told.

It may well be that "Perfectly a Strangeness" is a charming little experimental short film in which a couple of donkeys explore an abandoned astronomical observatory. Above all, the film certainly looks good. But as a short documentary, I can't take it seriously. I honestly have no idea what is supposed to be documented here. You simply observe a few donkeys being donkeys. To see more than that in it strikes me as pretentious, not profound. That's why I find it a questionable Oscar nomination that I can't get behind.

review_content: A deliberate, austere film that rewards patient viewers. The beautiful wide shots of the desolate landscapes and wreckage coupled with the oppressive, industrial sound design set a tone that has you peering into every shadow, questioning everyone's motives. I can't fault you if you feel bored; very little actually happens until the first act ends with a punctuation mark. Then we get another slow-burn segment of a disgraced detective hunting down our protagonist; once again, it's light on plot beats and dialogue. It does pay off in the third act and the epilogue has my gears turning hours later.

review_content: It's called Lesbian Space Princess. Ah ah funny. And it is. Well it is exactly the kind of humour you imagine is in a movie called Lesbian Space Princess. So mostly dumb low level jokes, on the queer/woke/men are dumb register. Actually I'm not sure it can really be called jokes. It's more like a sequence of feminist sarcastic tweet replies to a white male.   It's quite funny, at least for the first twenty minutes. Afterwards it feels a bit repetitive and very predictable.    The story is still cool and well paced. Saira's self loathing and lack of confidence is well depicted.    One thing I wonder, while Willow is listing her flaws, she says something like she's not observant. Which could match her not finding the ladder to get out of the hole earlier. However, this comes literally a minute after she's the only one who notices the moon crystal they needed next to the ship. Also later, when Saira just sees the button in the ship, Willow says she saw it ages ago. So, was it just a stupid mistake from the film makers ? Is it Willow being nice by describing imaginary flaws ? Is it Willow lying to get laid ?    Also at the end, the Maliens literally just **decide** to be gay. Which is a really really weird message to send for a queer production.

cast_names: Claire Foy, Brendan Gleeson, Sam Spruell, Josh Dylan, Denise Gough, Lindsay Duncan, Arty Froushan, Emma Cunniffe, Katy Carmichael
review_content: H is for Half an Hour Too Long    A very well acted meditative drama with some excellent cinematography. It's stretched thinner than it can bear at 2 hours, which is unfortunate as it's otherwise a very likeable film that eschews the tidy redemptive ending because grief and people don't really work like that.     All of the actors are very good but Foy is the standout and an absolute star. I say that as someone who has never really cared for her much in anything else too. She's brilliant here; there's no flashy melodrama to her performance as a daughter struggling with profound grief and sorrow. She somehow manages to make her character stoic, sad, funny, maddening and sympathetic in such a naturalistic way that it has made me a fan.     A good, well made film with a great central performance and some amazing cinematography that captures mesmerising bird sequences and the wildness of the English countryside.     It's overlong for the story and themes it wants to explore but I'm glad that I watched it.

cast_names: Kevin Lima, Bill Farmer, Jason Marsden, Dan Rounds, Brian Pimental, Gregory Perler, David Z, Jeffrey Katzenberg, Gaëtan Brizzi, Paul Brizzi
review_content: I didn’t really grow up as a Disney kid. I mostly grew up with Don Bluth’s animated movies than I did with Disney animated movies. The most Disney I experienced as a kid was some of the live action movies, some of the animated movies, but mostly the Disney Afternoon. I remember being as excited about A Goofy Movie the same way I was excited about DuckTales: The Movie because I always liked watching Goof Troop and DuckTales on the Disney Afternoon. I never got to see either in theaters but I did get to watch them on home video and A Goofy Movie was definitely the Disney animated movie I watched the most and might be my favorite Disney animated movie (sorry, DuckTales: The Movie).  Not Just a Goof is a wonderful love letter to A Goofy Movie and an underdog story (see what I did there?) about a movie that was made with a lot of love but didn’t succeed when it was released and managed to find an audience later and became a cult classic. There’s a lot of great behind the scenes footage and some fun stories from the making of the movie. I especially liked the portion about the production of the movie’s I2I dance sequence. I wish there could have been more recent interviews with Rob Paulsen, Dante Basco, Kellie Martin, and Tevin Campbell, though. This was a really fun watch and if you’re a fan of A Goofy Movie you’ll really enjoy this documentary.

cast_names: Asif Ali, Aparna Balamurali, Sampath Raj, Hakkim Shajahan, Hannah Reji Koshy, Arjun Gopan, Saravanan, Deepak Parambol, Madhan Kumar Dhakshinamoorthy
review_content: How many twists can you expect from a single movie — one, two, maybe three? What about six, seven, or eight? This movie throws even more than that at you. On paper, it sounds exciting. And yes, the twists are everywhere… but when a film relies on big reveals, the writing and storytelling must be equally strong. That’s where this movie collapses.    The film is packed with twist after twist, yet not a single one truly lands. Instead of shock, you feel nothing — because the execution is weak, the buildup is lazy, and the emotional impact is missing. A twist only works when the story earns it.    It’s ironic that the director doesn’t take inspiration from his own masterpiece Drishyam — a film that proved he knows how to deliver a twist that shakes you to the core. Here, he throws surprises like confetti, without the craft, patience, or tension needed to make them memorable.

review_content: This year’s Survivor Series: War Games landed squarely in WWE’s frustrating “good enough” zone—competent, watchable, and ultimately uninspired. For one of the Big 4, it should feel special, but instead it played like a night of missed opportunities. Match 1 featured a spot that actively detracted from the wrestling itself. Match 2 leaned on a fake betrayal and a cheap finish. Match 3 was perfectly serviceable but indistinguishable from something you’d see on a random Raw. Match 4 turned into a finisher parade capped by yet another weak ending. None of it was outright bad, but almost all of it felt like WWE settling rather than striving. There’s always room to elevate this event, but this year they simply didn’t.  Best Match: None, really (but Cena v Mysterio if I had to choose)  Rating: 2/5 – 65% – Not Recommended to Everyone

review_content: Starts by a kinda awkward relationship. She's extremely casual, like she's been there a hundred times, while it looks like he barely talks to people let alone be in a relationship. This is just weird and boring.    Enter the fantastical element: a new disease where people seem to fuse with objects, that can be transmitted by a look. Follows a lockdown with limited external news and occasional communication with neighbours. Until she gets contaminated...    The effects and make up look pretty good. However when it gets weirder (and would require more effects), image becomes black and white, very dark and blurry, and it definitely felt more like a lack of means than a stylistic choice.    The idea is goof. But the "transmitted by look" thing has an issue: very early, objects start fusing together (it becomes impossible to open doors or move things around for instance), so this definitely indicates something non biological, not a disease. (Could be that they are actually [spoiler]fusing with someone at a large scale like we see later[/spoiler], but it happens very early and to separate objects.    Sadly too little is known. There are no news. Only time we see someone else, it's the army catching her on the way to his place, and they're never seen again, no instruction from authorities ever. There's no real questioning or experiment on people fusing. Do they feel their extensions (it seems yes later), can they move them at will like a part of their body ? Do they keep a conscience ? Do they have bodily functions ? Do they need to eat ? I would have liked to know more.    Not clear what happens to him in the end. Is he [spoiler]merging with her, or is she eating/consuming him[/spoiler] ?    The final idea, that it's all just a new [spoiler]evolution to another form of life on Earth[/spoiler] is a nice choice for conclusion, and at least on that they didn't cheapen out on the visuals. Also means that it was useless to [spoiler]cut out children's eyes, and that no, she was not gonna learn to live outside of water[/spoiler].    Same as knowing more on the process, the adaptation of mankind during an intermediary phase, like we can briefly see when he's outside, would have been a interesting topic.

review_content: [Venice '24] Replacing the documentary genre with fiction, Brazilian director Marianna Brennand has the support of Walter Salles and Jean-Pierre and Luc Dardenne as associate producers in her debut, which is already a statement about the cinema that has had the most influence on her film. Sexual abuse is not addressed explicitly in the story and is not even referred to verbally, but it is always hovering in the air. In a secondary but very moving layer, the mother becomes a tragic figure that represents the paradox of a forest area that is subject to deforestation and abandonment just as the feminine is subject to abuse and violence.

cast_names: D.Y. Sao, Andy Le, Brian Le, Craig Ng, Geoffrey Giuliano, Ron Smoorenburg, Sahajak Boonthanakit, Byron Bishop
review_content: This movie was absolutely awful. LOL  So why did I rate it a "9"? Well, you have to be in the mood for an "absolutely awful" movie, and I was. This took me back to those Friday nights in Army barracks when all my buddies were out bar-hopping or clubbing or trying to get laid, and I stayed holed up in the barracks watching USA's "Up All Night" B-movie films and eating Ritz crackers with squirt cheese...and loving it. Listen, you don't watch a movie like this expecting to be absolutely awestruck by the screenplay, the acting, the dialogue, or the cinematography; you watch a movie like this because you're bored, you're eating, you just want something to entertain you for an hour and a half while you eat Ritz crackers with squirt cheese, or corn dogs, or popcorn...just _"Let me disengage my brain and not have to THINK for a little while..."_ and that's exactly what this gives you. There are some incredibly choreographed fight scenes in here - from start to finish - and overall, the whole movie has the vibe of _Bloodsport_ meets _Lionheart_ meets my handwritten self-absorbed novels from when I was a sophomore in high school. The acting is atrocious, the dialogue is diabolical, the story... there isn't one. It's so incredibly lame that you wonder how something like this ever got produced...and yet somehow you sit through the entire thing, do a facepalm at the closing scene, and then laugh when the end credits start rolling because you actually sat through the entire thing. But it's okay because this isn't meant to be taken seriously, or for anything more than what it is: an adrenaline-rush martial-arts tournament movie with a lot of cool fight scenes. The "Sifu" (what we used to call "sensei" in martial arts movies) side-story is laugably bad, but gives the movie just that extra cheese factor (not that it was lacking to begin with) to make it even more laughable. Yes, I gave it a "9" but you'll be doing good to give it a "5", honestly....probably something closer to a "2" or a "3" because it's just a terrible movie. Tonight, however, it just hit the right nerve, and I'm still laughing about it so yes, it hit just the right spot for me and earned a very high rating. I'll probably watch it again some time and still laugh at how bad it is. Don't even THINK about watching this if you're looking for a "good martial arts movie"; this isn't one. But it's highly entertaining, and high-octane action and hilariously bad...and those ought to count for something.

review_content: Laura Vandervoort and Stephen Huszar star in the Hallmark holiday film _Melt My Heart This Christmas_. The story follows a glass artist who works her way into a Christmas craft fair by volunteering to be the assistant to their featured artist who’s come out of retirement and is hoping to make a comeback. Vandervoort gives a strong performance and has good chemistry with Huszar; though their relationship is left kind of vague (with hints of a past relationship). Also, the script avoids some of the more common clichés and makes some interesting choices. And there are a couple of heartwarming Christmassy moments. It’s not without its problems, but _Melt My Heart This Christmas_ is entertaining and a bit of fun.

I would love to get it a more in depth review, but I just honestly don't have the energy and I've already started to forget things from watching it yesterday, lol.     I knew it would be bad. We expected to joke through it. Instead we just kept being irritating and offended. Not worth the watch in my opinion, unless you enjoy critiquing bad movies.    Edit: also, I don't take issue with the idea that many people in the queer community can also be shit. We aint all saints. It's the way it's done that makes it so awful.

cast_names: Arturo Mercado Jr., Roy Ambriz, Arturo Ambriz, Assira Abbate, Blas García
review_content: Contrary to what the pitch was selling, there is little happening in the "real" world, and Francisca's life is anecdotal. Which is great because the real world is not really interesting, and the **character design of the Spooks is absolutely gorgeous**. Even more when you see the actual figurines used for the stop motion in the behind the scenes during the credits.    The story is classical, a version of real and dream worlds being intertwined. Appearing a bit childish in the beginning but with mostly adult themes.    Maybe two hours is a bit too much and personally I could have done without the songs.    Script wise there was two weird things. One, there is a time jump. Just before we see Procustes trying and failing a coup. And since Ceimut was there, everyone, including the royal family, is aware. Yet years pass, and nothing is done. In the meantime he is supposed to write great stuff. Yet years pass, he doesn't deliver, and no one questions it. And most importantly, everyone is aware that the prince can go to the other world, which was thought impossible. Yet years pass, and no one even mentions it. This is literally the most important event in the realm for centuries, and no one cares.    Second, the whole story holds on the fact that Procustes manages to deceive both Frankelda and the Prince. But this whole time, even if the Prince is the one that got her there, that she knows him since childhood, etc, she just doesn't want to trust him. And yet she instantly accepts whatever Procustes says. So maybe there's the adult interpretation that she is insecure and he is the only one reinforcing her insecurities, so she buys it. But anyway, how the well didn't the Prince explain first "well we have this guy, with an important official position, that is supposed to write stories, and he sucks, and the whole point of bringing you here is to replace him" ?? Then maybe that would have put his word into perspective.    And, it seems that no one cares what happens to her body. First he brought her, leaving her body in the cemetery like that. Only the poor guy that is in love with her since childhood cares for her and takes her in. But... her body is never mentioned again, nor is this poor guy that no one cares about.    A bit disappointed that the 7 clans were basically reduced to their leaders, I would have loved to see armies.    There was several short blank scenes, with sound and no images, so not sure whether it was a projection issue or if the copy was incomplete.

cast_names: Arturo Mercado Jr., Roy Ambriz, Arturo Ambriz, Assira Abbate, Blas García
review_content: [Fantasia '25] With an aesthetic inspired by 19th-century Mexican Baroque, this film develops a simple story that is sometimes unbalanced by a pace that seems too accelerated, adapting the narrative of an episodic show to a nearly two-hour feature film that requires a more gradual pace. There is a beautiful and colorful, dark and gothic artistic design that is sometimes too overloaded but creates a very personal world. The film has a childlike approach but with contemporary and psychological themes that bring it closer to a more adult viewer. But all the visual richness is too overwhelming and the script has trouble conveying the characters' emotions.

cast_names: Travis "Taco" Bennett, Jimmy Tatro, Victoria Justice, Joel McHale, Briana Price, Dan Davidson, Lehi Makisi Falepapalangi, Melanie Haynes, Brock McKinney, Terence Goodman
review_content: California King snuck up on me, and with no expectations going in, it turned out to be a pleasant surprise. The movie is clearly built for streaming—light, breezy, and never aiming for perfection—but it got the job done by providing a handful of genuine laughs. Jimmy Tatro, as always, has a natural comedic presence that makes even the simplest gags land, and he’s the reason this worked as well as it did.    It’s far from a great film, but with the right mindset, it’s a fun, disposable comedy that gives you a few chuckles and doesn’t overstay its welcome.    Rating: 3/5 – 75% – Worth Watching

cast_names: Travis "Taco" Bennett, Jimmy Tatro, Victoria Justice, Joel McHale, Briana Price, Dan Davidson, Lehi Makisi Falepapalangi, Melanie Haynes, Brock McKinney, Terence Goodman

The runtime felt too long, and the sound design was headache-inducingly loud. At least the cinematography was good. Otherwise, I probably wouldn't recommend it, even for Victoria Justice.

cast_names: Sem Ben Yakar, Bart Oomen, Emma Josten
review_content: Easily one of the best slashers of the 2020s. The last time a slasher made me feel this satisfied was The Hunt (2020). The themes and stories are different, but the tone feels strikingly similar.    The underlying message is especially strong: bad people are bad, but people with “good” intentions can be even worse deep inside. The most innocent-looking person can become the most dangerous when family is involved. Associating with the wrong people can get you killed even if you haven’t done anything wrong yourself.     In this world, there’s no single true predator. Every predator can become prey to someone stronger. Power keeps shifting, and no one is ever truly safe.

review_content: All Out 2025 reinforced once again why AEW’s pay-per-views continue to set the standard for professional wrestling in 2025. The event packed an immense amount of value into one card, offering a blend of styles that ensured variety and pacing rarely faltered. From technical showcases to hard-hitting brawls, the night was filled with genuinely superb moments that reminded fans why AEW’s big shows feel special.    Yes, the runtime will always draw criticism, but that’s an easy tradeoff when the alternative is WWE events cluttered with ads and fluff segments. While this particular card may not have had the single, defining “all-time great” match that pushes a PPV into legendary territory, it came very close, with a couple of bouts that were absolutely outstanding. AEW continues to prove why their pay-per-views are must-watch for wrestling fans.    Best Match: Hangman vs Fletcher    Rating: 4/5 – 85% – Would Recommend

review_content: Jessy Schram stars in Hallmark’s _A Suite Holiday Romance_. While staying at a luxury hotel to ghostwrite a memoir, Sabrina befriends a fellow hotel guest that she suspects could be an elusive English lord. Schram gives a strong performance and has good chemistry with co-star Dominic Sherwood. However, the script’s a bit disjointed; trying to juggle several different subplots and bring them together. And there are some leaps in logic and plot contrivances that hurt the film. Still, the characters are likable, and there’s a nice message about following one’s heart. It has its problems, but overall _A Suite Holiday Romance_ a fun and enjoyable film.

cast_names: Jessy Schram, Dominic Sherwood, Reena Jolly, Al Sapienza, Lauren Cochrane, Adam Hurtig, Gino Anania, Megan Best, Lisa Marie DiGiacinto, Marina Stephenson Kerr
review_content: Caveat - this is a Hallmark movie     Hallmark rating: 8/10  Regular rating 7/10    A fun solid xmas movie. Good acting and an okay script but the visuals steal the show.   Lots of warm glowing lights set in a hotel - really pretty        From the website:   It’s Christmas week when Sabrina Post, arrives at the iconic Grand Fairbanks Hotel in New York City, ready to accept the ghostwriting position for the memoir of Grayson Westcott – a famous art dealer. As a struggling writer, Sabrina can't believe her luck: a paycheck and a stay in her own suite at the hotel. She feels like Cordelia, the heroine from her favorite series of children’s books written by Hazel Holley. What promises to be a perfect week is complicated when Sabrina meets Ian Turner, a handsome British visitor, at the hotel bar. When Ian assumes Sabrina is another wealthy guest at the hotel, she doesn’t correct him – a decision she doesn’t regret after learning that Ian is a member of the British aristocracy. Or so she thinks. The truth is that Ian is not a wealthy lord but is the personal secretary of Lord Spencer Braxton. Ian is in town for the holidays to oversee the installation of an exhibit featuring the Braxton Royal Jewels at the Avalon Museum. As the week unfolds, Sabrina shows Ian Christmas in New York and the two start to fall for each other. Starring Jessy Schram and Dominic Sherwood. https://www.hallmarkchannel.com/a-suite-holiday-romance/about-a-suite-holiday-romance

review_content: The first 20 minutes dragged, when it moves to the following scene I was thrown for a few minutes because I didn’t fully listen to the drone of the two people in the first scene. Enter Shia (what I was here for), a further 30 minutes of droning back and fourth, I had really zoned out by the time an alarm was going off in the prison, this woke me up.   The last act sees Henry (Evan Jonigkeit) barricaded in the library with a gun, with the viewer to assume he’s shot someone as the prison goes into lockdown. Then follows another 20 minutes of drone between a guard and Henry.   The last 5 minutes was the best part of the film, blanks were filled in, we had a purpose, a potential outcome on our hands. I was disappointed to learn in the credits this was an adapted stage play, it was certainly acted that way.  It’s up there with the rest of the 1/10 films I’ve watched this year.

review_content: Good, solid, well-made film that I liked when it was on but immediately started picking at on the way home. It’s tight, funny in places, and absolutely whizzes by but my main issue is how weak it makes Mick look, when I’m not convinced that really was the dynamic. Like, Coogan is good but also a bit of a problem. Sometimes he’s excellent and restrained and sometimes he drifts into full Partridge mode which slightly undercuts what should be a more evenly matched power struggle. Him being noticeably smaller and much older than Keane doesn’t help either. It starts to feel less like two stubborn professionals at war and more like one man bullying another, which flattens the ambiguity the film otherwise wants. “It’d be better if we had footballs” is a perfectly delivered line though. And Hardwicke is an eerily good Keane. Also worth saying: the soundtrack is tremendous. Pogues, Stone Roses, Dylan, Fontaines. Me likey

cast_names: Gianni DeCenzo, Jan Luis Castellanos, Elizabeth Yu, Vanessa Rubio, Nicole Elizabeth Berger, Kevin Porter, Tiana Le, Casey Mills, Gilbert Owuor, Gavin J. Konop
review_content: Black Spines has a solid, creepy premise: a sleepy small town, a series of grisly murders, and an outcast high school photographer who finds mysterious VHS tapes that might hold the answers. Director Jordan Foss leans into mood and atmosphere, and the film does a good job of building an unsettling, retro-tinged vibe that horror fans who like analog mystery elements will appreciate.    Pros:    The concept is engaging and ripe with potential; the VHS-tape angle gives the film a tangible, eerie texture.    Jan Luis Castellanos (Cameron) anchors the movie with a sincere, likable lead performance, and the supporting cast mostly do what’s needed with limited material.    The setting—Page Hollow—feels lived-in, and the film makes effective use of quiet, slow-burn tension in several scenes.    Cons:    Pacing is uneven. The middle section drags at times, stretching a fairly straightforward mystery into a longer runtime without adding much depth.    Character development is thin; several supporting figures remain underwritten, which weakens emotional stakes when revelations land.    The plot relies on familiar horror tropes and predictable twists, so the payoff feels underwhelming rather than surprising.    Technical choices (editing and some sound-mix moments) occasionally pull you out of the story rather than immerse you.    Verdict: Black Spines is watchable and has moments of genuine chill, but it never fully capitalizes on its premise. Fans of small-town horror and retro-mystery aesthetics may get more out of it than general viewers. Worth a rental or streaming watch, but not essential.

cast_names: Hayden Panettiere, Justin Chatwin, Beverly D'Angelo, Mischa Barton, Lori Tan Chinn, Eric Lutes, Kea Ho, Cathy Salvodon, Gene Dante, Chris Everett
review_content: Very unnatural conversations and bad opening. Boring and unnecessarily long establishing shots in the beginning, was weird. POV camera movements do not sync with actors. Exposition is delivered through dialogue-heavy scenes when it was not needed with flashbacks happening right after. Feels like scenes are missing, we are left out on why characters have certain information and the film just assumes we don't care. Some scenes feel shoehorned in to add tension, but are later tossed aside when the tension isn't needed. It's very much going for 60's era horror structure and it basically admits it by its poster and films seen within this one. But does not try hard enough to convey its style or make itself standout from even its modern day horror contemporaries.    Sarah and Michael are just terrible parents. I get that Sarah is going through grief and abuse, but even in flashbacks we see, she is incompetent in her own right. She is so dumb and idiotic it's honestly hard to root for her even in obviously horribly unfair scenes. Even with the abuse, it's quite unrealistic and over-the-top for movie drama. Her artwork, while disturbing is genuinely good. Though, I think that some pieces look like they are from different artists entirely. The child actors are pretty bad, the main girl is way too over-the-top to make you feel bad, but it doesn't work. Hayden Panettiere is okay in this role and is really the only standout. The film is not researched enough, as even other characters don't seem to know what they are talking about... even if they are "professionals".    The horror aspect of this film did not need to be here at all and fails miserably. I would have preferred it was just a drama with more work put in that department. Cheap jump-scares only made possible by screeching strings with no build-up. As is, i hate it's inclusion and implementation. Only time I think it really worked was the fear in one scene from Sarah's POV when someone walks past her window, that I think worked well. Until she attempts to reach said person... with no effort put in on her behalf... again, incompetency. No one actually thinks of Holden's best interests here. I got to say though, Bai's scene is actually a surprise because I did not expect the spirit medium to actually you know, do their job and further the plot because of it.    I will say however, that I actually like the metaphor for split families they went for here and how damaging it is to the children involved. Plus the ending I think was solid in concept and what the film needed. Though, there are still things left unresolved, and it's no excuse for having Sarah be so overly bad to Holden throughout to "foreshadow" the reveal. Should have tamed it down to make the ending more impactful.    Not a fan of the film, but the underlining themes are at least decent.    **3/10**

cast_names: Sree Vishnu, Ketika Sharma, Ivana, Vennela Kishore, VTV Ganesh, Kalpalatha, Reba Monica John, Narne Nithin, Rajendra Prasad, Kirrak Seetha
review_content: Single is exactly what it promises to be: a fun, goofy rom-com that doesn’t take itself seriously and honestly doesn’t want you to either. The story isn’t groundbreaking - it’s the same formula you’ve seen a hundred times but it works because it feels relatable. The comedy lands more often than not, the situations are silly in the best way, and the film is self-aware enough to joke about itself.  What I loved most is how the movie keeps breaking the bubble, it drops meme jokes, meta references, and little nods that connect straight to the audience. It’s not pretending to be some deep emotional journey. It’s just here to make you laugh and remind you of those chaotic, exaggerated moments we all see around us.  Performances are the highlight. Sree Vishnu is effortless and super watchable, carrying the film with his timing. Vennela Kishore is gold, every reaction of his adds another layer of humor. Ketika Sharma and Ivana both do exactly what’s needed, bringing charm and energy even if their characters don’t get much depth. Together, they make sure you’re entertained even when the story feels too familiar.  Technically, it’s colorful, snappy, and designed for light entertainment. The songs? Forgettable. The background score? Fits the mood. Editing is tight in the first half and shaky later, but it never derails the fun.  At the end of the day, Single isn’t trying to be a classic. It’s just a breezy, meta-heavy comedy that knows its limits and plays within them. I never felt bored, I laughed more than I expected, and that’s exactly what this kind of film should deliver.  Perfect weekend chill watch when you just want to relax and laugh without thinking too hard.

review_content: This movie, although a war film, carries a strong antiwar message. It clearly conveys that war impacts both sides of the conflict, affecting soldiers and their families on both sides. Additionally, it serves as a tribute to Dharmendra, as it was his last film. The story is well-crafted, though there are a few weak points in its execution. The dual timeline narrative may confuse some viewers. Overall, it’s a good movie.

cast_names: Dharmendra, Jaideep Ahlawat, Agastya Nanda, Simar Bhatia, Suhasini Mulay, Rahul Dev, Sikandar Kher, Ekavali Khanna, Vivaan Shah, Saurabh Dubey
review_content: It's about the 1971 war hero Lt Arun Khetrapal, MVC. The story shows his father, Brig Madan Lal, travelling to Pakistan years later and meeting Brig Naser, the Pakistani officer who had allegedly killed Lt Khetrapal in the Battle of Basantar (1971). The film portrays this meeting as warm and emotionally overwhelming because of the respect and care shown to him.  Logically speaking, this film should not have been made if the makers had any sense of respect for their own soldiers, because isolating one courteous exchange within a brutal war distorts historical reality.  This being an Indian film shows the more of the Pakistani point of view, equating the brave Indian army with the rapist Pakistani army. It also diminishes Arun Khetarpal's chivalry by adding a fake character in the Pakistani army who is shown at the same age as Arun and is equally fearless.

cast_names: Dharmendra, Jaideep Ahlawat, Agastya Nanda, Simar Bhatia, Suhasini Mulay, Rahul Dev, Sikandar Kher, Ekavali Khanna, Vivaan Shah, Saurabh Dubey
review_content: What a banger of a film to start the year! I honestly didn’t expect it to be this good, especially since the actors are relatively new to the screen. However, they absolutely blew me away.  ​The cinematography was incredible—the war sequences, particularly the night scenes, were a standout. It didn’t even feel like a typical Bollywood film; it felt like witnessing a Call of Duty cutscene come to life. It gave off heavy All Quiet on the Western Front vibes, which is my favorite war movie of all time.  ​Rest in peace, Dharmendra Ji. This was a great portrayal of the Army and retired soldiers, capturing the reality of their lives on duty. I loved how the film highlighted that soldiers often don't care about politics—they simply fight with honor.  ​This is a must-watch on the big screen. The atmosphere and scale just wouldn't feel the same anywhere else.

Give me happy and sad stories, fun facts, funny clips, lifesavers and rescues, adoption centres and sanctuaries, care and talent. They show some of it, but nowhere near enough. cast_names: Joe Begos, Matt Mercer, James Russo, Riley Dandy, Josh Ethier
review_content: Jimmy and Stiggs is absolutely bonkers — in the best possible way. This is fundamental filmmaking stripped down to its raw core. No bloated budget. No studio polish. Just pure creativity, practical ingenuity, and an infectious “let’s make a movie” energy that jumps off the screen.  What really elevates it is the behind-the-scenes footage at the end. Seeing that this is essentially where the director lives — surrounded by the set pieces, props, and DIY effects — makes the entire experience feel even more authentic. The tricks, the scrappiness, the resourcefulness… it’s filmmaking as a passion project, not a product.  Is it perfect? Of course not. But it’s as good as it can possibly be given what it is — and that’s what makes it special. Movie lovers, especially those who appreciate the craft, should absolutely check this out.  Rating: 3/5 – 75% – Worth Watching

cast_names: Joe Begos, Matt Mercer, James Russo, Riley Dandy, Josh Ethier
review_content: An incredible movie - I am basing this review on what Joe Begos ("Jimmy" / screenwriter / director) was able to accomplish with almost non-existent budget, limited space, being a one-man tour de force able to write / act / direct the movie at the same time. It's basically Movie Making 101 taken to the extreme.    The film is funny, crass, and over the top. The fake previews were great, so was the first person POV at the beginning. Now I have to go check out Begos' other movies!

cast_names: Joe Begos, Matt Mercer, James Russo, Riley Dandy, Josh Ethier
review_content: More like *Fuck and Gooey*. First of all, the "The Piano Killer" intro was hilarious, I rewatched it four times, we need to get a full-length movie please! The "Don't Go In That House, Bitch!" segment was a little less funny but still enjoyable because of Snoop. Then we get to the main event and I barely laughed even though it was trying really hard to be funny.    The characters are so annoying and loud and I don't think I've ever heard the word "fuck" that many times in 60 minutes (I'm tempted to rewatch it just to count)... it's so irritating after a while. The story is stretched out to death, it's like a 90 minute version of two pages of script. After 30 minutes we already hit the maximum and they keep recycling the same stuff, so repetitive.    Impressive practical effects though, really enjoyed all the goo blood and the aliens are kinda cute. The leds make it so colorful it's like you're watching animation. The first fight scene with an alien was the best, probably my favorite moment but there's also a scene where the camera keeps rotating in opposite directions that was trippy. The snail thing shows up and gets the hell out of the movie for some reason, so disappointing. Didn't really care for anything after that.

cast_names: Joe Begos, Matt Mercer, James Russo, Riley Dandy, Josh Ethier
review_content: An out‑of‑work filmmaker claims aliens abducted him, ropes his mate into prepping for round two, and the night erupts: drugs, UFO panic, splattery mayhem. It’s home‑wrecking mode, painting the flat with day‑glo blood and riffing on Evil Dead/Bad Taste energy. The scrappy bravado and practical effects sing; the hangout stretches, though. Trim 20‑30 minutes and you’d have a riot. As is, the buzz dips between glorious splats.    If you liked… Bliss, Evil Dead 2, Bad Taste.

cast_names: Joe Begos, Matt Mercer, James Russo, Riley Dandy, Josh Ethier
review_content: My opinion of this movie varied wildly from this is dog$#!+ to this a masterpiece of "it's so bad its good" filmmaking. It a total mashup of Evil dead, Fear and loathing, and any found footage movie where the camera moves to fast too often, and all that made it a fun if uneven film.

cast_names: Joe Begos, Matt Mercer, James Russo, Riley Dandy, Josh Ethier
review_content: This was basically a neon, blood-soaked, cocaine-fueled, home invasion, alien abduction, adrenaline rush. It was all shot in Joe Begos actual apartment. He acted, directed and pretty much did everything else as well. When you pop on a Begos movie you better know what you’re getting into. His dialogue is littered in F-bombs, it’s filled with drugs and always has a ton of practical effects and great gore. I personally loved his last movie ‘Christmas Bloody Christmas’ but never found myself enjoying this. The practical effects are absolutely gnarly. The colors are incredible and the movie looks exactly like you’d expect if you’ve ever seen another Begos movie. I personally like his cinematography and think it looks excellent. He has a very distinct way of doing things. I just never got into this one. I thought it started pretty cool and unique but just goes off the rails and never drew me in much. It’s absolutely chaotic, extremely fast paced and completely bonkers if that’s what you’re into. That tends be hit or miss for me but in this case was a complete miss. I respect what Begos did here and I think it had a lot of good going for it but it just wasn’t my thing.  * Triple Feature at AMC Crestwood 18

This one felt driven by storytelling as much as in-ring work, with matches that carried real emotional weight and payoff. It was a refreshing change of pace—less about nonstop chaos, more about narrative and long-term vision. The wrestling itself was, as always, top-tier, filled with surprises and memorable spots that made the show feel alive from start to finish.    The main event was pure insanity—a divisive match that will have fans arguing over whether it was brutal brilliance or too far over the edge. But that’s exactly what made it special. The “pass the bat” moment was the kind of cinematic wrestling storytelling AEW has mastered, and it’ll go down as one of those unforgettable images in wrestling history. review_content: Oh wow, what a movie.   Imagine Leaving Las Vegas, but darkly funny and a fair bit hopeful.   I know that some won't be able to get past the monkey suit (shame on you), but it actually helps the story.  As the movie progresses, we get to see the purpose of the suit.   In a weird kind of way, the suit reminds me of Teller of Penn and Teller fame.  When someone is partially hidden (in Teller's case, silent), the audience has a bit of sympathy for the character.  When you couple that with an expressionless face (not unlike Vader at the end of Return of the Jedi), the result is powerful.  But beyond all of that, it is a hell of a story.   This is easily one of my favorite movies of the year.      follow me at https://IHATEBadMovies.com or facebook IHATEBadMovies

cast_names: Nina Conti, Shenoah Allen, Bill Wise, Melissa Chambers, Rachel Kylian, William Sterchi, Asha Bee, Bourke Floyd
review_content: Sunlight is bizarre, tender, hilarious, dark and unapologetically horny. Nina Conti, already known for turning ventriloquism on its head, takes it a step further by embodying her infamous potty mouthed monkey puppet in a full suit, creating a surreal character that moves and breathes as a living, unsettling presence.    The film throws you straight in. A suicidal man, Roy, meets a woman in a full monkey costume speaking in a gruff male voice. Together they set out on a twisted quest to dig up Roy’s dead father to retrieve a watch. The strangeness is never questioned by Roy, which normalises it for us and sharpens the film’s off kilter humour. His morbid boredom with life is pierced by fascination with this creature, and that spark pulls him back from the brink.    As the story unfolds, fragments of their pasts are revealed. Both are wounded, both are desperate, yet in their chaos they find acceptance. While the world around them tries to contain or correct them, they find solace and an unlikely intimacy in each other.    It’s a film that won’t land with everyone. Some will never get past the image of a woman in a monkey suit. But those who do will discover a strange, daring, and beautiful piece of work, proof that cinema still has room for wild ideas and aching hearts.    If you liked…Anomalisa, Swiss Army Man, or Frank, you’ll be at home here.

cast_names: Adriane McLean, Sarah French, Gigi Gustin, Dazelle Yvette, Adam Bucci, Mark Justice, Jed Rowen, Ben Kaplan, Bishop Stevens, Alex Dundas
review_content: *Brute* (1976) delivers a gritty, unsettling vibe reminiscent of the Manson Family’s Spahn Ranch, with a twisted family dynamic akin to Rob Zombie’s *House of 1000 Corpses*. The film’s visual imagery and camera angles are striking, showcasing a raw, almost hypnotic aesthetic that elevates its low-budget charm. The desolate setting and eerie atmosphere pull you into its dark world, making every frame feel alive with tension.  However, the version I watched was censored, with blocks obscuring some violent scenes, which dulled the impact and left me frustrated. I’m eager to seek out an uncensored cut to experience the film’s full intensity. Going in with B-movie expectations, *Brute* delivers on schlocky thrills but falls short on story. The plot feels thin, lacking the depth to match its bold visuals, and the narrative momentum stumbles at times.  Despite these flaws, the film’s creepy cult-like family and stylistic flair make it a compelling watch for fans of gritty horror. It’s not a masterpiece, but it nails the B-movie vibe with enough raw energy to keep you hooked. If you’re into unsettling, low-budget horror with a side of depravity, *Brute* is worth a look—preferably uncensored.

cast_names: Shane Boris, Talal Derki, Ibrahim Nash'at
review_content: “Hollywoodgate” is a documentary that captures the Taliban’s shift from insurgent force to governing regime, while peeling back the complex layers of propaganda, power, and fragility underpinning that transformation. Directed by Ibrahim Nash’at, the film is as unsettling as it is fascinating, placing us in the middle of a historic moment with rare access and a narrative that deeply unsettles.    From the outset, there’s a palpable tension in how Nash’at positions himself within the space he’s documenting. The former American base, known as Hollywood Gate, serves as a microcosm of the Taliban’s new order. It quickly becomes clear that the camera isn’t just a recording tool—it’s part of the power dynamics. Taliban members often seem to perform for the camera, fully aware of the impact of their words and actions. This is especially evident in Mawlawi Mansour, the Commander of the Air Force, whose commanding presence borders on theatrical. Mansour is portrayed as both ambitious and cynical, determined to refurbish the military equipment left behind by the Americans, turning decommissioned helicopters into symbols of renewed strength.    The film skillfully balances the banality of daily life on the base with moments of profound symbolic weight. Soldiers goofing around with forgotten cans of Red Bull from the freezer transition into scenes of war machines being painstakingly restored—a shift that encapsulates the transformation from chaos to control, at least on the surface. This oscillation between the mundane and the apocalyptic amplifies the viewer’s discomfort, constantly reminding us that what appears to be routine is, in fact, preparation for something far bigger—and far more dangerous.    Nash’at’s choice to document not only the military rebuilding but also the social and cultural dynamics of the new regime is a quiet stroke of brilliance. One of the most striking moments shows Taliban members discussing changes to women’s rights laws. As they watch the news, the contrast between their words and the devastating reality of these policies is glaring. The cruelty embedded in their indifference hits just as hard as any scene of destruction or armed conflict.    The documentary also stands out for its narrative restraint—for what it chooses to show or leave out. Over 92 minutes, Nash’at offers a concise yet sweeping view of a constantly shifting landscape. But he resists the temptation to fill in every gap, leaving viewers with unease and unanswered questions. This isn’t a flaw—it’s a strength, reinforcing the idea that Afghanistan’s story under the Taliban is far from a conclusion.    The film’s climax is a chilling reminder of the extent of this power transition. In a nearly choreographed display of strength, helicopters and weapons left behind by the Americans are showcased as trophies of symbolic victory. It’s a moment of calculated terror, designed not only to intimidate external enemies but to solidify internal control. Nash’at doesn’t need to underline these scenes with heavy commentary; the starkness of the images speaks volumes.    Yet, “Hollywoodgate” never loses sight of the human context. While the camera often focuses on symbols of power—the leaders, the weapons, the helicopters—there’s an undercurrent of a much larger tragedy. The film hints, without spelling it out, at the long-term consequences for Afghanistan’s population, particularly women and children, who continue to face a worsening humanitarian crisis compounded by hunger and oppression.    “Hollywoodgate” is a remarkable piece of journalism but also a film that challenges its audience to grapple with the nuances of what it reveals. It’s both a window into the present and a warning about the future, a reminder that what’s being rebuilt at Hollywood Gate may have global implications. The discomfort the film provokes comes not only from what it shows but from what it deliberately leaves for the viewer to interpret. This ambiguity is its greatest strength. In the end, Nash’at delivers a work that’s as much a document as it is a warning—a reflection of a moment where chaos continues to shape the fate of a nation.

review_content: *The Way to You* is a heartfelt and charming Hallmark film that beautifully captures the magic of unexpected connections and second chances. From the very beginning, the movie wraps the viewer in a warm atmosphere filled with sincerity, hope, and the kind of emotional storytelling that Hallmark does so well.    [spoiler]The story follows two people who meet during a time of transition in their lives. What begins as a chance encounter gradually turns into a meaningful journey as they spend time together and discover how much they can learn from one another. Along the way, both characters are confronted with past choices, personal fears, and the question of whether they are ready to open their hearts again. As their bond grows stronger, they begin to realize that sometimes the most important paths in life appear when we least expect them.[/spoiler]    What truly stands out is the chemistry between the leads. Their performances feel natural and genuine, making the romantic journey believable and engaging. As their relationship unfolds, the story gently reminds us that sometimes the most meaningful paths in life are the ones we never planned.    The film’s pacing is comfortable and relaxing, allowing the emotional moments to breathe without ever feeling rushed. Combined with lovely cinematography and a cozy setting, it creates the perfect escape for anyone looking for a feel-good romantic story.    Another highlight is the film’s uplifting message: love often finds us when we least expect it, and sometimes the road that leads us to happiness is not the one we initially choose.    Overall, *The Way to You* is a delightful and uplifting movie that fans of romantic dramas will truly appreciate. It’s a story about trust, vulnerability, and the courage to follow your heart — leaving viewers with a warm smile long after the credits roll.
