movie_title: Children No More: Were and Are Gone
review_content: The short documentary “Children No More: Were and Are Gone” focuses on a group of protesters in Tel Aviv who silently hold photos commemorating Palestinian children killed in the Israeli offensive in Gaza. This certainly shows that there is still room for freedom of expression and protest in Israel, even if the activists have to endure a fair amount of backlash. As a documentary, however, the film is a little too repetitive to be completely convincing. But the intention is certainly one to be welcomed.

movie_title: Children No More: Were and Are Gone
review_content: Silence is rarely neutral. Depending on the context, it can function as denial, complicity, or absolute accusation. By choosing to remain silent and simply show, “Children No More: Were and are Gone” occupies an uncomfortable space where the image stops being mere documentation and becomes evidence of a moral collapse that can no longer hide behind noise.    The documentary follows a vigil held in Tel Aviv by a small group of Israeli activists who stand in a public square holding photographs of Palestinian children killed in Gaza. Each image is accompanied by minimal information (name, age, date of death) and the phrase “were and are no more,” which works less as a caption and more as a sentence. The film’s proposal is rigorously simple: to observe. There is no explanatory narration, no direct interviews, no attempt at broad historical context. What exists is the repetition of the gesture and the reactions it provokes, both from passersby and from those who feel personally attacked by the mere presence of these images.    The power of “Children No More” lies precisely in its refusal to argue over versions or justify positions. The film does not discuss military strategy, list political arguments, or relativize civilian deaths. By focusing exclusively on murdered children, it dismantles any attempt to abstract the conflict. The silent vigil exposes an absolute ethical boundary: there is no narrative that can make the death of children acceptable. And yet, what the documentary reveals is how even this basic truth sparks anger, hostility, and accusations of betrayal.    Formally, the direction relies on long, observational takes that capture both the stillness of the activists and the verbal aggression of those who confront them. The contrast is brutal. On one side, people who say nothing; on the other, shouting, insults, and attempts to silence. The film makes it clear that what unsettles people isn’t speech  (since there is none) but the image itself. Photographs of dead children seem to cross an invisible line, exposing a truth many would rather keep out of sight. Silence, far from calming things down, intensifies the discomfort.    There is something deeply disturbing in how the documentary exposes the normalization of horror. Opposition to the vigil isn’t built on denying death, but on ranking grief, as if some lives deserve to be mourned and others don’t. “Children No More” doesn’t need to spell out this logic; it’s laid bare in the reactions caught on camera. The discomfort isn’t caused by the images themselves, but by what they demand: empathy, recognition, and moral responsibility.    By avoiding any attempt at emotional mediation, the film becomes even more forceful. There is no score guiding feelings, no manipulative editing. The viewer is placed in the same position as those who walk through that square: to look, or to look away. And unlike many documentaries about armed conflict, this one doesn’t aim to inform, but to confront. Its central question isn’t “who’s right?,” but “how did we reach a point where photos of dead children provoke more outrage than their deaths themselves?”    In the end, “Children No More” stands as one of the most painful and necessary records of the present precisely because of its restraint. It doesn’t ask for agreement or offer comfort. It simply insists on something that should be obvious, but no longer is: no cause, no ideology, no narrative justifies the murder of innocent civilians. In a world that treats genocide as routine and reacts with fury to the mere reminder of its most defenseless victims, the film turns silence into accusation, and the image into a non-negotiable moral line.

cast_names: Klara Castanho, Karine Teles, Indira Nascimento, Alana Cabral, Cláudio Mendes, Juliana Guimarães, Isley Clare
review_content: “#SalveRosa” starts from a very contemporary premise, centered on how lives shaped by social media create artificial realities, distort relationships, and turn intimate experiences into consumable products. The film clearly understands this territory, pointing out how the internet has stopped being just a space for connection and turned into a commodified stage where identity and visibility walk hand in hand. It’s a relevant, necessary, deeply modern discussion, especially when it involves childhood and adolescence. The problem is that the thematic strength behind the idea doesn’t translate into the execution, and it’s exactly in that gap between intention and realization that the film starts to weaken.    The script struggles to structure dramatic progression and organize character arcs, which hurts the narrative’s solidity early on. Many supporting characters show up as functional figures rather than fully developed people, limiting the emotional weight of the relationships around the protagonist. There’s a recurring sense that several storylines could’ve been explored with more care but remain stuck on the surface, as if the film introduces conflicts without really wanting to mature them. That lack of depth weakens the dramatic universe and makes certain events feel predictable or emotionally irrelevant.    This limitation becomes even clearer in the construction of Dora. Karine Teles brings intensity to the role, but the script doesn’t give her the same support, resulting in an antagonist built more on schematic traits than real human contradictions. The character has a manipulative aura that could’ve led to something complex, but the writing keeps her stuck in a one-dimensional space, never digging into her motivations or offering concrete clues about her choices. Key questions are left hanging. Why does she lie? What drives her unstable relationships? What actually moves her internally? That absence of depth weakens the central conflict, because a thin antagonist shrinks the emotional reach of the story.    In contrast, Rosa receives much more careful treatment and ends up as the film’s emotional anchor, largely thanks to Klara Castanho. She builds a believable character by balancing fragility and strength in a natural way, avoiding caricature and leaning into emotional nuance. The performance carries moments where the film gets closer to something stronger, especially when it slows down to observe the protagonist’s subjective experience under the invisible but constant pressure of digital exposure. Castanho manages to convey innocence, fear, and a deep desire to belong all at once, and that layered work gives the movie its most grounded emotional beats.    Still, a strong protagonist isn’t enough to sustain a film with recurring structural gaps. The issue grows more visible when you look at the narrative environment surrounding Rosa. Several subplots are introduced without consistent development, creating a sense of fragmentation that prevents a solid emotional progression. Some elements feel promising but are dropped before gaining real dramatic relevance, contributing to a story that oscillates between strong intentions and scattered results. The film never quite establishes a clear axis of tension.    The direction also fails to compensate for these structural weaknesses. Formally, it plays things too safe for a theme that would allow far more aesthetic boldness, especially considering the story directly deals with image, performance, and identity construction in digital environments. The staging is functional but rarely expressive, and the shot design relies on conventional solutions that don’t fully explore the visual potential of the world it wants to portray. Instead of translating the sensory impact of social media into cinematic language, the film sticks to a more neutral style, which creates a growing sense of distance.    That distance deepens because several narrative elements are mentioned without receiving proportional development. Personal backstories are hinted at and then abandoned. Relationships appear without clear follow-through. It all reinforces the feeling that the film accumulates ideas without fully integrating them. The lack of cohesion between these dramatic lines prevents a consistent emotional curve, causing the narrative to dilute instead of intensify as it moves forward. By the time the story reaches the third act, these structural issues become even more evident. The pacing suddenly speeds up and starts compressing resolutions that would’ve needed more emotional groundwork. The ending feels rushed, like it’s trying to tie together conflicts that never had the time to mature, and that compression weakens the dramatic resonance.    That said, the film isn’t without merit. Its critique of child exploitation in digital spaces remains sharp and relevant, showing an attentive eye for urgent issues, especially when it touches on how algorithms and adult interests shape young experiences. Whenever the narrative leans into that more intimate dimension, you can see flashes of a stronger film, one capable of balancing reflection and emotion more effectively. The problem is that those moments never solidify into a cohesive aesthetic and narrative whole. The result is a movie that constantly hovers between what it could’ve been and what it ultimately becomes, as if the power of the subject never finds a cinematic form strong enough to carry it. There’s a lack of depth where there was latent conflict, a lack of development where there was potential tension, and a lack of formal boldness where the theme demanded invention.    “#SalveRosa” sparks reflection and raises important questions, but it also leaves a lingering sense of unrealized potential. It feels like a film that understands the urgency of what it wants to discuss but can’t translate that urgency into equivalent dramatic density and narrative rigor. The intention is relevant, but the construction doesn’t fully support the impact it aims for, and that mismatch ends up being its biggest weakness.

cast_names: Damon Hill
review_content: This is more of a psychological study of Damon Hill's character than a biography. There are also elements that have an odd tone; almost a creepy feel at times, especially in the use of overlays in some of the contemporary race footage. The problem I have is that, as a whole, it doesn't really work.  The psychology is somewhat cod and laid on too thick with next-to-no analysis & the racing coverage, despite obviously being well researched, is just a little too selective.       If you're a long-time F1 fan, like me, then you should watch it, as the careers of both Damon and his father are part of motor-racing's history and this documentary offers a different perspective to them. But don't expect too much in the way of new revelations about either. For the general viewer, its subject may be too niche.

movie_title: Sarah Squirm: Live + in the Flesh
cast_names: Sarah Sherman, John Waters, Jack Bensinger
review_content: Sarah Squirm: Live + in the Flesh is a strange, intentionally uncomfortable stand-up special that leans heavily into presentation and production. From a visual and conceptual standpoint, it’s creative and engaging, clearly trying to do something different from a traditional comedy set. That aspect works—the atmosphere and commitment to the bit are impressive.  Unfortunately, the actual stand-up material never quite matches that ambition. While some jokes are clearly designed to make the audience “squirm,” and that part lands, the humor itself rarely hits hard. It’s more interesting than it is funny, and that imbalance ultimately holds it back.  Rating: 2/5 – 65% – Not Recommended to Everyone

The character and the choices she makes are interesting but its so familiar and it gets even more familiar past the first act. There's nothing new here unfortunately and it's drowning in movie references leaving it with little identity.    My biggest problem with it was the decision making and plot points, I don't mind a little bit of suspension of disbelief but the whole situation feels annoyingly forced and unlikely. So unbelievable that I laughed a lot watching it and I don't think that was meant to be since it's fairly grounded. The needle drops are so cringe and I swear this guy can't sing. The third act reminded me of one of my favorite Valentine's Day horror movie.    Did the dad ever find his boxers?

review_content: **My Score: 8.1**  This excellent documentary delves into the drama surrounding climate action in American politics during the 80s and 90s. The format, featuring no narrators and relying solely on archival footage, is particularly interesting. It shifts the entire focus to the actual events, rather than the narrative. Prior to watching this documentary, I had no idea that there was ever a point when climate change was an actual political issue, and they came so close to solving it. The slow pacing might put some viewers off, but I thoroughly enjoyed it.

cast_names: Shirley Chen, Mckenna Grace, Amelie Zilber, Vivian Wu, Maitreyi Ramakrishnan, Fang Du, Elaine Hendrix, R. Keith Harris, Megan Hayes, Kristen Cui
review_content: Funny, sad, dramatic, and culturally important. At its core, Slanted is a movie about belonging and trying to fit in within modern America. It’s Mean Girls + The Substance + Get Out, all wrapped in one movie.  It is not afraid to say what needs to be said out loud. The uncomfortable truths about America are on full display with no held punches. It’s also surprisingly really funny. The jokes land very well, and are supplemented by the outrageous things some of the characters say. It’s honestly impressive how Slanted weaves the comedy and absurdism with the deeper emotional and dramatic parts.  I absolutely loved all the little details in the background. The performances were absolutely amazing. The speech delivered by Roger (Fang Du) about wanting to fit in but discovering his own definition of what it means to be American made me tear up a little. In a time so device and full of hate to people of color and of different ethnicity, hearing the truth actually said was powerful.  I think that Slanted is an incredibly powerful movie. The themes of belonging and culture as well as the struggles of being an immigrant and dealing with modern America are so incredibly important right now. I really think that different people will be able to connect to this movie and feel like it understands them. I really hope that this movie gets seen by so many people because it deserves so much praise and deserves to have its message heard.

It addresses important issues, but does so by focusing more on the solidarity born of a guilty conscience than on reflecting on the futility of borders.

It explores the recovery of historical memory while delving into the complex relationship of two brothers who have found themselves on opposite sides of the law. The documentary about Casa Verdi—a retirement home for opera singers and musicians—is quite charming, but that's about it. In any case, it didn't manage to hold my attention for the entire runtime. That's certainly also because opera isn't really my thing. But I'm convinced that the subject would have been better suited to a short documentary. Then, however, the song "Sweet Dreams of Joy" wouldn't have been eligible for an Oscar nomination. In that sense, everything was probably done right, even if I wasn't completely won over.

review_content: [Cannes '25] The boldest and most original film at this year's Cannes Film Festival is the feature debut of director Ratchapoom Boonbunchachoke, which is at once a surreal comedy, a ghost story, and a sociopolitical reflection on his country's historical past. The story's queer spirit constructs a look at diversity, as diverse as the manifestation of ghosts, some with voices without visible bodies, others translucent or completely imperceptible. Their inner suffering is expressed through murmurs and the movement of objects, occupying the space of a country like Thailand, full of ghosts that emerge from the uncertainty of their deaths, caused by forced disappearances and massacres. Their presence reflects a connection with the past that is not closed.

review_content: Like a guy with only one testicle: someone really dropped the ball.    Franz Fanon was a psychiatrist, intellectual, political radical, Pan-Africanist, Marxist humanist who wrote about the psychopathology of colonization and the human, social, and cultural consequences of decolonization. This man deserves a biopic of epic proportions. Which, regrettably, is not what he receives here.    The script and directing take the story of this monumental man and turn it into a kind of telenovela soap opera SNL skit. That the writer / director admires the man is clear, everyone should, but to portray Fanon in such made-for-TV light is as tragic as how under-appreciated Fanon is.

A harrowing but necessary watch.   Israel’s systematic targeting of Gaza’s healthcare infrastructure and personnel is undeniable. From invading hospitals and leaving NICU babies to decompose in their incubators, to abducting surgeons and doctors and torturing them to death.  The film follows the stories of a few Doctors in Gaza. How they were forced to evacuate from one hospital to another, how they lost their families and patients, how they survived Israeli forces' kidnapping, and how some were abducted again by the end of the film.   The film also details the kinds of torture and violence inflicted on medical staff, starting with Israel's massacre of 15 paramedics, and burying their bodies and ambulance cars. The IDF denied that the event ever happened until footage found on one of the deceased medics’ phone proved otherwise.  Palestinian Doctors were tortured, sometimes to death, in Israel’s Sde Teiman facility. Where footage of Israeli soldiers sodomising a detainee to death was leaked. The accounts of the Israeli guards and doctors validate those claims. Yet you’ll have people downvoting and calling this film a propaganda piece for documenting Israeli war crimes. The use of a virtual set to present the changes in setting and time does not work in its favor, because it ends up conveying an artificiality, with the characters always in close proximity and a somewhat poor quality in the integration of the images. Thus, the distance that a character like Jordi Pujol could provoke is increased by the technique used.

review_content: Watched #MadrasMatinee last night!   Absolutely loved it! I think this film portrayed the closest to what middle class families are in real. All the emotions felt so real, actors were top notch especially Kaali Venkat.  He was fantastic! ♥  A typical middle class parent who would do anything in their capacity to give the best kind of education to their children and the best part is the struggles are not being romanticised here!! cast_names: Rahul Bhat, Sunny Leone, Mohit Takalkar, Megha Burman, Shrikant Yadav, Kurush Deboo, Jeniffer Piccinato, Johnny Cockfill, Murari Kumar, Kundan Roy
review_content: A very dark story with no good guy anywhere. I'm not sure about the "looking for redemption" part in the description. He's looking for his personal revenge, while still doing very dirty work (killing lots of people) for corrupt police leaders and their racketeering business.  At first it seems that he's doing the jobs he's given and has his side quest trying to get to Rasheed, and it's not clear which part is the job and which part is personal.    Probably supposed to be a twist but it was almost evident since the beginning that [spoiler]his _roommate_ was not real[/spoiler].    The real twist is when we're shown he is actually [spoiler]a total psycho, that enjoys murdering people[/spoiler] and the whole thing with Rasheed is just his personal revenge for [spoiler]his son's death[/spoiler], except this was a retaliation for his own actions. And this turns the whole thing even darker.    Rahul Bhat is great through the whole thing.    I'm not quite sure what the point of the Charlie character was. We see her repeatedly, she could have been used for something but in the end she only serves to have a place for the final confrontation (that could have been done anywhere any other way).  The confrontation itself is pretty short and a bit of a letdown.    The final part is a bit out of place. I suppose it's meant to be emotional and show that even a guy like this has feelings ? But he is a [spoiler]real piece of shit. He's supposedly looking for revenge for his son, but he's the first one that murdered an innocent family member in the past, and he does it again afterwards. When sent to kill a guy, he murders the whole family. I don't buy that he has genuine feelings for his daughter. Specially as even his subconscious tells him that talking to her will scar her for life, he's aware of it, and still does it. As for his confession, if it could have helped getting justice... but he already killed all the people involved.[/spoiler]

review_content: The sad part is that they didn't even show the worst things these animals did to those kids. How they through grenades into shelters by the road where these kids were hiding, how these kids had to hide under piles of dead bodies hoping to survive, how they had to put on blody clothes pretending to be already shot etc. And I will never forget the pictures in news after it happened where Palestinians were celebrating in the streets when these terrorists brought raped young girls covered in blood on the back of their trucks. People were running to them, hitting them, spitting on them, dancing and singing. And some people are still wondering why Israelis have no mercy with them. If that happened in my country to my kids, I would want the entire nation to be wiped up from this planet. That doesn't mean that it's right. It's not. I can just understand how when something like that happens to you and your kids, you don't really think rationally about it anymore. Emotions will take over and you will want revenge no matter what. cast_names: Marion Cotillard, Clara Pacini, August Diehl, Marine Gesbert, Lila-Rose Gilberti, Gaspar Noé, Dounia Sichov, Valentina Vezzoso, Cassandre Louis Urbain, Aurélia Petit
review_content: You can see a lot of work has been put into the light (and dark) and some scenes look good. But it's nothing extraordinary, and it is so slow and sluggish and boring.    Everything is too long and too slow. Nothing really happens.    The concept of reality, dream and movie melding together is nothing new, and this one brings nothing original to the table.    When finally something happens and Cristina starts talking to Jeanne, it's still way too slow with no clear direction. In the end, it's not clear whether Cristina expected [spoiler]her to jump and kill herself for her[/spoiler] ? Or if trying [spoiler]to abuse her[/spoiler], like shown afterwards was the initial goal. But anyway, by the time it happens, I had already lost interest. And both scenes are just weird.    I guess after the ending there's a possible full reinterpretation of all the interactions, we can assume Cristina is [spoiler]grooming her from the beginning[/spoiler]. Did she choose her because she identified with her, saw herself when she was younger ? Or is it just bullshit ?  Probably she picked her because she saw she was vulnerable, without a mother figure, without protecting adults, without anyone to miss her. And when she talks about fleeing from an orphanage, she's just inventing something to create a link.  In this case, the mountain scene is just a test of her hold on Jeanne and she wouldn't have let her go through. Or maybe it's meant to fail, and instill a sense that she doesn't want to disappoint her ever again ?    There are probably other interactions to reinterpret with this prism. But adding a deeper meaning after the fact does not prevent the scenes from being boring in the first place. And it's hard to keep interest until then.

cast_names: Vishnu Manchu, Preity Mukhundhan, Akshay Kumar, Mohan Babu, Mohanlal, Prabhas, Kajal Aggarwal, R. Sarathkumar, Brahmanandam, Madhoo
review_content: Devotion is pure… but cinema should respect it.  Kannappa wants to be a mythological epic, but it ends up as a lavishly-produced TV serial. The ambition is there, but ambition without execution is just noise.  The biggest problem hits you straight away: the CGI is embarrassingly bad. Whole environments look fake, sets look half-rendered, and instead of grandeur you get a cheap video game feel.  And the costumes? Confusing. Men wear period attire, women are in oddly modern, revealing outfits that break immersion. Instead of authenticity, the movie feels like a cosplay event that went wrong.  Instead of focusing on the core story, the devotion of Kannappa to Lord Shiva, the film spends half its time on an unnecessary love track. The romance is dull, misplaced, and strips the narrative of seriousness. Add in forced comedy characters and the screenplay has no discipline.  Performances don’t save it. Many actors overact like they’re in a stage play. Even veterans like Mohanlal leave no impact. Prabhas manages to look good, Kannappa’s father works, but beyond that the characters feel hollow, stripped of depth and sincerity. The leads look embarassingly bad.  But here’s the painful part: the ending was almost good. The scene where Kannappa is ready to sacrifice both his eyes… finally, a spark of emotion. For one brief moment, you see the film it could have been. A story of faith, sacrifice, and divine love. Yet it only reminds you of the wasted opportunity.  And here’s the irony, if this exact movie was made in Bollywood, people would have torn it apart. But because it’s from the South, many ignore the flaws or stay silent, since they only expect blockbusters from the South. Everything that South makes is good, it isn't like that as people only watch their good famous movies and don't criticize absolute embarassments like these. The bias is real. Respect shouldn’t depend on geography.  A devotee gives everything. A filmmaker should too.  Kannappa doesn’t give us devotion, or grandeur, or sincerity. It gives us weak CGI, incoherent costumes, a misguided romance, and acting that fails to connect. It disrespects its own source material and misunderstands its cultural responsibility.

What should’ve been a tense, unsettling psychological horror instead feels oddly flat and predictable. The atmosphere is there, and the setup promises something chilling, but the film never capitalizes on its own potential.    It’s not a total disaster, but it’s one of those movies that leaves you frustrated because you can see how good it could have been. Ultimately, it ends up being more bland than bad, which somehow makes it even more disappointing.    Rating: 1/5 – 55% – Would Not Recommend

movie_title: Who Wants to Marry an Astronaut?
cast_names: Raúl Tejón, Alejandro Nones, Raúl Fernández de Pablo, Sabrina Praga, Francisco Andrade, Sofia Nemirovsky
review_content: There’s something mildly charming about it. The premise is light, the road-trip energy is pleasant, and the romantic tension is set up clearly. The issue is that you know exactly where it’s heading from the very first minutes. When a romantic comedy offers no real surprises, it starts to drag.    Its normalization of a queer relationship without heavy drama is refreshing in itself. There’s no exaggerated identity conflict, just a familiar love story structure. That choice has value, but beyond that, the film stays firmly within the safest clichés of the genre.    At times it hints at parody, as if it’s aware of the conventions it’s using. Yet it never pushes far enough. It lands somewhere between homage and soft satire, without fully committing to either.    Technically solid, performances decent, but the chemistry never quite ignites and the emotional stakes remain shallow. It’s the kind of harmless weekly rom-com you watch… and quickly forget.    Pleasant, but far from memorable.

review_content: Written, produced, directed, costumes, casting and acted by Alessi Wasser. Everytime I saw such multi roles, I understood that it's an independent low budget film, hence the multi role by 1 person. I know the risk but how else would you find new movie makers.   Is Messy good? It's very subjective. Neo feminists would love Messy. The real feminists would say it's an unrealistic wishful thinking. No, worries, conservatives and religious people would hate it, except for the extremist muslims who wished they met such a messy liberal neo feminist to abuse.  Anyhow, if you are a film fans, you would know 1977's Looking for Mr. Goodbar with Diane Keaton as leading actress and a slew of new comers who would becime legendary actors and actress.  This film, whether admitted or not, took the idea from Looking for Mr. Goodbar. Bored of her life, she roamed the city for one night stands. The main difference is the ending. While the much more superior in writing quality, Looking for Mr  Goodbar dealt with consequences, Messy didn't.   Messy just continued on with the next 'adventure' which made this film comedically ironic and also damaging. There is always consequences, it doesn't matter if you don't like it. Face and explote the consequences, and the film will get a lot better.  As for now, let's just say Messy should be retitled as The Adventure of Non-Attractive Horny Girl. And for me personally, it's very bad, I wished Wasser reduce nudity a lot and explore the tragedies that came out of this wishful fantasy   Skip it.

review_content: The story itself is weak, and the character arcs are a complete failure. The tropes of communicating with ghosts, demonic possession, and the protagonist defying his grandparent's teachings are all clichés that Tamil cinema has already exhausted. The duality between the corrupt cop and the vigilante was far below expectations. The villain never feels like a credible threat, as the hero overcomes every obstacle with little effort. The placement of a romance song in the pre-climax was a poor creative choice, made worse by the heroine dressing as a retro movie character simply because the hero pretends to be one. The film is riddled with further flaws, and I would not recommend watching it.

[VdR '25] Following a pseudo-nutritionist connected to conspiracy theories and far-right groups in Germany, it paints a portrait of a society whose fragility compels it to indulge in the darkest theories and the most unnerving discourse. However, it manages to avoid being a platform for conspiracy messages by offering the perspective of one of its victims, a young man suffering from schizophrenia whom they claim can be cured through a healthy diet. The first part of the documentary shows the alternative reality created by the gurus of toxic masculinity, while the second looks at the true reality of their victims.

I definitely cried a tear or two at the end  I could’ve watched six more hours just diving into minutiae of the show with memories and commentary and analysis, but Voyager is my favourite Trek by far and one of my favourite shows all-round. To the Journey does the show and its legacy justice and I hope many more fans of the show get to watch it soon.

review_content: Like making chili as you become a member of the mile-high club in an airplane lavatory in economy on a turbulent flight: messy, tasty, and it goes well over the place.    To say the film is a thriller about three friends with more self-assurance than brains who get into trouble while vacationing in the Caribbean (The Dominican Republic, to be more specific) doesn't do this piece of cinematic spin-art justice.    It's sloppy in every good way and every bad way, too, with it's ultra-violence and non-stop action (though it does start of with a flash forward and has a dream sequence -- which is lazy filmmaking, imo). It all goes so fast you don't have time to judge it, you just survive it and end up grateful for the ride.

cast_names: Trine Dyrholm, David Dencik, Johanne Louise Schmidt, Christine Gjerulff, Kurt Dreyer, Hanne Uldal
review_content: [Berlinale '25] A film that is more relevant to what lies beneath than to what it shows, a latent tension that is multiplied through the frustration of the characters. And it skillfully avoids dramatic underlining, even in a very sober soundtrack by the American composer Nathan Larson. This foray into a relationship that is shaken by an unexpected event to transform itself, reflects on the nature of commitment and the stability of a loving connection that is sometimes sustained by feelings such as guilt or remorse. So, although it aims to be optimistic, it ends up leaving a bittersweet taste.

cast_names: Sanae Loutsis, Chelsea Edmundson, Meredith Binder, Risa Mei, Jason Brooks, Lydia Pearl Pentz, Tabitha Bastien, Jonathan Holbrook, Jason Reynolds, Brandon Knapik
review_content: A very low budget and at times amateurish production, but that is not always a bad thing. The Death of Snow White leans into its limitations in a way that gives it a strange charm. I actually enjoyed the dark fantasy take, and the theatre like vibe made it feel more like a twisted stage play than a typical glossy fairy tale movie. It felt scrappy and weird in a way that was at least interesting, even when the acting or effects did not quite land.    You can tell a lot of it was made with passion rather than money, and that goes a long way here. Some scenes really worked because they were willing to be moody, grim, and a little unhinged instead of trying to look slick. It was not always polished, but it had a personality, which is more than you can say for a lot of bigger productions.    And honestly, it was still a better Snow White movie than that Rachel Zegler and Gal Gadot flop. At least this one tried something different instead of feeling like a bland studio product. 😂

cast_names: Patrick Hivon, Piper Perabo, Gilles Renaud, Eric K. Boulianne, Connor Jessup, Gord Rand, Patrick Garrow, Denis Houle, Jean-Carl Boucher, Benoît Mauffette
review_content: [Cannes '25] The director uses elements of comedy, based on sexual encounters, which reflect the chaos experienced by a protagonist suffering from eco-anxiety in a world where human contact is increasingly distant. The film becomes more serious at times, when fantasies don't correspond with reality, but it never abandons an optimistic outlook. And when what seems like the beginning of the catastrophic outcome of our world erupts, it regains its lively tone. Its virtue lies in its ability to move between different genres without seeming to be moving from a pre-apocalyptic story to a romantic comedy.

cast_names: Georgina Campbell, James Preston Rogers, Malcolm McDowell, Logan Miller, Grace Dove, Aaron Merke, Cassandra Ebner, Sydney Sabiston, Nigel Shawn Williams, Terry Ray
review_content: Pretty fun with several high moments, but also definitely has some weaker moments. The action and plot were pretty fun. I loved the creepiness of the masked man. Honestly, I was more rooting for him than Jane.  It did feel like the movie was holding itself back a lot, trying to appeal to a general audience. I think that if the film leaned more into the horror and terror of the slasher, it could have felt a lot more thrilling.  It really was not as scary as I was hoping it to be. The crime scenes and work with the blood was really cool, but other than that it is hard to find some of the spookier parts.  Definitely not something amazing, but worth a watch if it seems interesting. I thought it was fun, but also this movie was right up my rally so I can see why people wouldn’t like it as much as I did.

cast_names: Georgina Campbell, James Preston Rogers, Malcolm McDowell, Logan Miller, Grace Dove, Aaron Merke, Cassandra Ebner, Sydney Sabiston, Nigel Shawn Williams, Terry Ray
review_content: I didn’t really know what to expect with this going into it. I thought it was a slasher but maybe with a satanic twist. The entire satanic plot and cult stuff didn’t really work for me but the slasher element was excellent. This movie started so strong with excellent suspense and pacing. I thought things began to fizzle a tad bit in the middle half. Things definitely picked back up in the final act. The ending helped bring things full circle. The performances were very strong. I thought the script was really smart and the cinematography looked excellent. I love the way they blended the colors to make it really fit the style of the movie. It’s not extremely gory but the moments there was gore were effective. Despite the second half not reaching the heights of the first half I still thought this was very good.  * Triple Feature at AMC Crestwood 18

cast_names: Jon Gries, Johannes Grenzfurthner, Roland Gratzer, Jasmin Hagendorfer, Galen Howard, Sky Elobar, Tim League, Jason Scott, Michael J. Epstein, Jason Tostevin
review_content: Ambitious, yes. Watchable? Barely.    Set in a crumbling Austrian farmhouse once owned by the director’s family, Solvent follows Gunner Holbrook, a weary American investigator searching for Nazi-era documents. What he finds instead is a corrosive force that eats through flesh and ideology alike. The film mixes found-footage horror with pseudo-academic narration, lurching between art experiment and endurance test.    Johannes Grenzfurthner clearly aims for a statement on guilt, corruption, and the toxicity of belief, but subtlety is not in his toolbox. Characters do not speak so much as declaim, hammering their points in monologues that sound like rejected TED Talks. Every idea is shouted, then explained again, then repeated just in case you missed the metaphor oozing down the walls.    There is some craft here: the claustrophobic camera work, the unnerving sound design, and a few sequences that genuinely crawl under your skin. But the constant self-importance drowns it all. Instead of horror, we get a lecture soaked in goo.    Solvent could have been a smart descent into obsession and ideology. Instead, it is a self-congratulatory swamp of ideas, style, and acid-green bile.     If you liked: Masking Threshold, Skinamarink, The Outwaters.

review_content: The worst Malayalam film I have ever seen. I watched it solely because of Mohanlal, never imagining he would be part of something like this. The story is weak, meandering through random, disconnected plotlines. The characters, their development, the narration, and the attempts to connect other films all fall flat. Even as I write this, I find myself unsure whether the film was intended to be taken seriously or as a satirical spoof. If this was meant to be the actor's comeback, it stands as one of the most disappointing returns in cinema history. The most glaring flaw, however, is the setup for a sequel. Do yourself a favour and skip this one entirely.

review_content: Sleek, confident chiller. It rides mood and craft instead of cheap jumps, then spends its gore in sharp little bursts that actually sting. The villa setting and art direction do loads of work: glass, stone, reflections, all judging the characters while the tension tightens. You can feel the lineage, guilt and control games, secrets under calm water, but it still feels its own thing. The ghost design is simple and horrible in the right way, the sort of wrong that prickles your neck. The ending lands hard and will haunt you. If you liked… Psycho, What Lies Beneath or Rebecca this is for you!

review_content: NXT Vengeance Day 2026 ended up being another perfectly average NXT event. The biggest drawback is the now-familiar WWE structure: a short five-match card where none of the matches are given much time to truly breathe. It keeps the pacing brisk, but it also limits how memorable anything can become.  On the positive side, the match stipulations added some variety, and importantly, nothing on the card came close to being a bad match. Everything ranged from solid to good, which made the show easy to watch even if it never reached a standout moment.  More than anything, the event clearly functioned as a setup show for Stand & Deliver, and in that regard it did its job well. Storylines were pushed forward and the groundwork for the bigger event was laid effectively.  Best Match: Hendry vs Saints  Rating: 3/5 – 75% – Worth Watching

It was really nice to see Sean William Scott back on the big screen. I totally dug how they filmed this in 16mm. It really gave it that old school, grimy, slasher feel to it. The movie had some promising ideas but it never fully executed everything. The practical effects and make up design were excellent. This had a few incredible looking gory moments. It just moves a bit slow and slightly drags but it was decent enough.  * Dolly Early Access at AMC Crestwood 18

cast_names: Max Lindsey, Fabianne Therese, Seann William Scott, Ethan Suplee, Michalina Scorzelli, Kate Cobb
review_content: A classic horror slasher monster. Even a bit forced as it reuses everything it can.      A gimmick: she's playing with dolls, has dolls all around, uses her victims as dolls  A creepy mask: big doll head.   Family trauma: clearly an abusive father, may have killed her own mother.  Near invulnerable: whatever happens she always gets up  Inhuman strength: she seems to be able to lift all the other characters.  Dolls: dolls everywhere    Pretty classic slasher stuff otherwise, with a bit of prisoner movie.    The first reveal is good though. We only see a woman, that may have killed her daughter, and when she turns around the first time: this huge doll face. Really effective.  At the beginning we could think she is a mother who killed her daughter. When it is revealed she is in fact the daughter, I thought then she might have been burying her mother, but then the ranger at the end says Macy is not the first one.  This is a really weird statement, as it means that:  1) she might have been burying just yet another victim  2) there were several others before  3) that escaped (assuming lot more that didn't)  4) the cops did nothing? Didn't investigate, didn't go to the house?    As for the slasher part, the shovel in the leg, then in the face at the beginning, combined with ripping out the jaw was afterwards is probably the only original, or at least at out of the basics, slasher move.  Oh yeah, and the punching through the father was good too.  All the rest stays pretty basic, nothing fancy, nothing gory.    Dolly's attitudes are fun, and she doesn't seem to be evil. Just probably mentally undeveloped. She doesn't really want to hut her prisoner, and she seems genuinely distressed when they don't want to play. She's also trying to help against her father.    The director is apparently looking to make both a sequel and a prequel. Would it be worth it ? Maybe. It ticks all the boxes, but it's not really novel, so it may be missing a little something to have its own appeal.

review_content: [Berlinale '25] Death is revealed in this story as a kind of transformation of love, but not as a complete disappearance, wrapped in a magical character that is played out in this small town surrounded by a mystical aura. It is a profoundly feminine film, in which men have a very secondary presence, even though songs by Milton Nascimento and Fernando Mendes are included. For the girl protagonists and their mothers, the cycle of death and rebirth has a deep meaning that takes on different forms and realities. And in this story of friendship that deals with issues such as gender identity in a very beautiful way, it shows them with remarkable sensitivity and an astonishing playful tone.

Through a story in which desire becomes relevant in the relationships between the protagonists, it presents a less pleasant perspective on the work of NGOs, establishing their relationships with dark powers, in an epic narrative about the intimacy of its characters.

review_content: It's a good watch and my biggest respect to everyone involved on all sides. To me personally the pacing was a bit off though. How much of a struggle was the sand (both in their faces or under their feet), how much of a problem was the sun, how did the food intake go, how much of a help were the people involved. Strong performance though from both of them and everyone else.  Again in summary, it is a good and interesting watch. Very inspiring performance.

cast_names: Average Rob
review_content: _"If I quit, it is because I went way too hard, and not because I felt sorry for myself."_  Every Average Rob video makes you think you're about to be blown away, and after watching, it's even better than you expected. The Iron Man video was already emotional, but this one goes even further. I especially appreciated how the different people who accompanied Rob and Arno on this journey were included. A special thought for Veerle, I absolutely broke down in tears during her part.    Arno, in particular, was truly an inspiration in this documentary. The quote comes from him (and it's honestly crazy to come up with something like that after walking 100 kilometers in constant 40°C heat and while being sick, he's just an absolute beast).

review_content: I really don't know what to make of this movie. Halfway though I figured it is maybe less of the weird wave thingy it gives impression to be and better watched a free form art flick like the experimental dance - which made sense for a while. But then it gave up on both of them and kept meandering in a socially uncomfortable valley and wrapped up with, idk... a pretentious film student piece? Maybe it makes sense after the director's next movie what he was going for, because this feels like it is attempting to encompass way too much and ends up inconsistent and messy. Or rather, maybe the director gets the experience to pick and choose what to leave out, because there are multiple directions with potential but keeping all of them in makes a mess. Kill your darlings and all that.

cast_names: Mackenzie Davis, Christopher Abbott, Ariane Labed, Freya Hannan-Mills, Nadine Labaki
review_content: A family with already pretty weird relationship dynamics gets even weirder with the presence of a stranger in their vacation home.    It's a bit of an home invasion situation, though she is officially invited to stay, where she is clearly weird, enigmatic, and with an hidden agenda.    Good point: it doesn't take the very easy and expected path, of tormented couple issues and nobody sleeps with her ([spoiler]though maybe the daughter ?[/spoiler]).    However it tries a bit too hard to be weird, to the point it is really not natural. Most conversations are not dialogues but exchanges of rhetorical or weird questions with no answers. Isabel acts weird all the time, ok there is her difficult job and disconnection from normal life, but that really does not justify most of her behaviour, starting by her extreme insistence that Kitti stays and how she suddenly decides she must go. She also doesn't seem to mind her weird attitude towards their underage daughter.    Similarly, the weird dance scenes in the middle could have been avoided, really not my thing and I really don't see what they bring here. Less strange but pretty much useless too, the scenes at the beach with the weirdos.    In the end, turns out Kitti is actually simply [spoiler]Death[/spoiler]. Why not. That's a good idea and it fits some of her previous behaviour. Makes you kinda wonder whether it takes that much work and time each time, why Vito "knew her", and whether Isabel actually [spoiler]killed him by telling her to go[/spoiler].

But far from being isolated murders, they reflect the structural failures of a German society facing the advance of extremist policies. The emotional impact conveyed by the film is as profound as its accurate reflection on how extremisms rise from the passivity of societies.

review_content: [Berlinale '25] The debuting directors achieve a solid film that poses disturbing dilemmas to the viewer, without offering answers or a closed ending, but rather reflecting the fragility of a judicial system that is often unable to adequately protect child victims. The balance between the presumption of innocence and the need to establish a system of protection for the most vulnerable is shown in a restrained but at the same time deeply moving way, driven by a long central section that uses statements before a custody judge to reconstruct the context of the story.

cast_names: Jhon Narváez
review_content: Pure art cinema with the high concepts you'd expect like colonialism, patriarchy and violence, told in a mystical and philosophical way (from the point of view of a dead hippo). Even if you don't care about that though the visuals are remarkable and the hippo scenes, filmed by the director himself, are fascinating and adorable in spite of the danger posed to himself.    "Carlos de Los Santos is interested in moving away from the “Americanisation” of cinema which has been the “visual brain of the imperial world, influencing children from a very young age to read images and symbols in a particular way.” Instead, he is interested in creating a Caribbean cinema which places imagination in a political context: “I mean, how does a hippo speak? It is something that is full of imagination. I believe we need imagination in the political arena to imagine a world that does not yet exist – not just fantastical themes, but new forms of love, of feelings.” "    - Nelson Carlos de Los Santos Arias

cast_names: Roschdy Zem, Marina Foïs, Thierry de Peretti, Vincent Colombe, Xavier Robic
review_content: Like a star: cold, distant, and commonplace.    This biopsy biopic of a toxic relationship proves the marriage between Simone Signoret and Yves Montand was malignant.     Turns out the stars **are** just like us normies: dull, petty, and bitter. Speaking of stars, I found the casting of _The One I Loved_ questionable. Even though I'm a big fan of both leads, it looked more like Yves Montand playing Roschdy Zem and Simone Signoret playing Marina Foïs than the contrary.    [Personal note you can ignore: This film touches on one of Signoret's later films _La Vie Devant Soi_ (_Madame Rosa_), based on the novel _La Vie Devant Soi_, (_The Life Before Us_). Even though that film won the Oscar for Best Foreign Film (1977) and a Best Actress Cesar for Signoret, I won't to see it because the book is my absolute favorite novel of all time and I don't want someone else's vision to interfere with my perceptions. I can't speak to the English translation of the work as I read it in French, but it is a timeless, poetic and profoundly moving novel. If you can read French, it's a must read.]

cast_names: Kya-Celina Barucki, Julia Novohradsky, Nhung Hong, Zoë Pastelle Holthuizen, Jason Klare, Yoran Leicher, Henning Baum, Victor Kadam, Annette Frier, Mai Phương Kollath
review_content: Fun!  I was very skeptical when I heard they were remaking this. As someone who grew up with the original, even though I haven't seen it for 20 years, I was worried if the concept of the movie would still work in this day and age, with today's youth, who are apparently much cooler and more open-minded than we were back then. The original jumped on the American Pie bandwagon back then - a film, that, in my opinion, has also gathered some dust.  So I was quite surprised when I read all the positive reviews of the remake.  And yes, the concept still works today! Although it borrows many scenes and elements from the original, it still feels fresh and independent.  The girls have good chemistrynand are super likeable. Many scenes have real heart, despite the naturally high cringe factor.  Unfortunately, the acting often is very "German" and wooden in places, but that was somehow to be expected.   The final message is also very textbook and preachy. This could have been handled more elegantly.    Nevertheless, a nice, shallow, entertaining summer movie.

review_content: [Locarno '25] Christian symbolism related to sin and guilt is present throughout the film around the character of Teresa, but the title suggests a representation of a God who is not present when His help is needed, even when the character is facing self-inflicted punishment. There is a peculiar way of using the language barrier, avoiding easy resources and using this lack of communication to bring the characters closer together rather than tear them apart. The rugged, rocky, cold landscape, characterized by the constantly whispering wind, forms part of the visual solidity of a film that crafts a rural drama that explores family dynamics in an environment shrouded in religious beliefs and patriarchal structures.

review_content: A young girl emigrates from Poland to Belgium in the 1990s. On her journey by bus, she escapes into a fantasy world, which naturally works well for an animated film. The style of animation is appealing, with the other characters being depicted as anthropomorphic animals being a particularly striking feature. This provides variety on a visual level, while the plot deals with the topic of migration and the experiences associated with it in a way that is suitable for children. In this regard, the film is a complete success, as the target audience is likely to be children (in addition to award season enthusiasts). "Autokar" is certainly worth watching.

cast_names: Vicky Krieps, Antoine Reinartz, Monia Chokri, Aurélia Petit, Park Ji-min, Julien de Saint Jean, Malou Khebizi, Féodor Atkine, Manuel Vallade, Anna Sigalevitch
review_content: [Cannes '25] From the opening of the film, which shows the rubbing of skin, there is a sensuality in a furtive sexual encounter that expresses the transformation the protagonist is undergoing. Her resistance to surrender as a mother is drawn in the shots of Clémence cycling through the streets of Belleville, the effect of the changing seasons, and the physical transformation that reproduces her rebellion, expressed by a very forceful physical representation of the character by Vicky Krieps, who takes over space and time throughout the film. A beautiful portrait of motherhood, sexuality, and freedom that confronts established norms.

One of the interesting aspects of the relationship between Stephan and ChaO is that she represents an exuberant vitality, while he reflects a certain shyness and feeling overwhelmed. The physical treatment of the mermaid, which falls into classical standards, can be debatable, as opposed to the more imaginative design of the secondary characters, all endowed with some amphibious aspect. It is an overwhelming film in its visual aspect and optimistic in its ecological message, but above all, very entertaining in its chaotic development.

Still, the matches themselves delivered, and the pacing never dipped enough to lose engagement entirely. What stood out most here was the amount of young talent featured on the card. Many of these wrestlers clearly have the tools to become stars in AEW down the line, but for now, it’s fun to see them shine on this stage and build their names.    While it may not have been the most streamlined PPV of the year, the mix of established names and up-and-coming talent made this a worthwhile watch and a promising glimpse at the future.    Best Match: Bandido vs Hechicero    Rating: 3.5/5 – 80% – Would Recommend

movie_title: Jim Gaffigan: Live from Old Forester — The Bourbon Set
cast_names: Jim Gaffigan
review_content: The existence of this show surprised me.  While watching the show, I wondered if Jim pulled a Ryan Reynolds or [insert celebrity name here], but with bourbon?  This is one of the funniest generic ads for a specific product type I've seen.    As Jim does state that he has not partnered with a bourbon company (which may or may not be true, although he doesn't shill a specific brand), my mind moved on to wondering if the show was commissioned.  I'm still trying to wrap my mind around the fact it exists.    As an aside, there's a complementary video via Sotheby's (the auction house) in which Jim allegedly drinks rare bourbon while offering insightful commentary such as, "this is good" and "yummy".  This video features clips from The Bourbon Set.    This show may draw a 2026 parallel of sorts to learning of weird commercials celebrities did in other countries back in the day when the world wasn't so small.

cast_names: Empar Ferrer
review_content: [Movistar+] The short film finds its greatest strength in the coexistence between personal narrative and collective memory, offering a look at women's struggles from the 1930s to the outbreak of the Civil War, and the often-forgotten involvement of women on the Republican front. It succeeds in blending gentle, almost fairytale-like animation with the stark reality of the themes it addresses: the violence of war and the cruelty of reprisals. The protagonist's epistolary exchange with a mother who may never receive her letters becomes her driving force, but the story demonstrates that there is always an addressee who ultimately inherits the powerful strength of resistance.

cast_names: Mónica del Carmen, Raúl Villegas, Andrea Jiménez Camacho
review_content: [Berlinale '26] Despite tackling a complex theme, this is not a pessimistic film. It underscores the solidarity that can be found in friendship, even when that friendship requires making difficult decisions. It is a beautiful film about friendship, in which the director places the entire weight on the splendid performances of the two young protagonists, but also defines their relationship through framing, sometimes placing them in different spaces, between the close-up and a reflection in a mirror, to emphasize the distance between them. The environment of the two teenagers in a sport that requires a certain degree of physical exposure allows for a clearer confrontation with the uncertainty of revealing their secret.

movie_title: My Undesirable Friends: Part I - Last Air in Moscow
review_content: [VdR '25] An urgent chronicle of attacks on independent journalism in the Russian Federation, offering an inside look at the escalating persecution of dissident voices by Vladimir Putin's regime, alongside the growing tension of the recent war in Ukraine, which could only be described as a "special military operation." The isolation of opponents as a totalitarian strategy paints a picture of the Russia of its citizens who have been forced to leave after being threatened and persecuted. It is a powerful story of the resistance of independent journalism within an environment of oppression and the criminalization of freedom of expression.

review_content: Flat. I would try and think of a more inventive critical topical pun, but my lack of effort reflects the film. A paint by numbers screenplay with not one single surprising twist in the plot and incredibly trite dialogue. Frankly painfully unoriginal, even Moorcroft himself has basically made this film already (on more than one occasion). When deciding what to rate it, I thought _"is 'bad' too harsh, perhaps just 'poor' would be fitting?"_ but no, given how dreadfully unambitious this was, along with the utterly predictable plot points and wooden dialogue, I think this is deserving of a 3/10 'bad'.

cast_names: Sachin Khedekar, Amey Wagh, Siddharth Chandekar, Prajakta Koli, Kshitee Jog, Harish Dudhade, Kadambari Kadam
review_content: **Krantijyoti Vidyalay Marathi Madhyam** is a warm, nostalgic, and emotionally powerful film that beautifully captures the soul of Marathi-medium education. The movie blends humor, sentiment, and social commentary in a way that feels both relatable and impactful. The story of a struggling Marathi school, and the people who come together to save it, is told with sincerity and deep affection.    The film’s biggest strength is its ability to remind viewers of their own school days, the classrooms, the teachers, the friendships, and the sense of belonging that only a Marathi school can create. The narrative never feels forced; instead, it flows naturally, making you laugh, reflect, and even tear up in moments.    The performances are strong across the board, especially the chemistry between the cast.  A special mention must go to Sachin Khedekar his heart-touching performance adds immense depth to the story and stays with you long after the movie ends.

cast_names: Klaus Fried, Erich Fried, Astrid Proll, Petra Fried
review_content: [DOK.fest '25] Through the career of the poet Erich Fried, this film, directed by his son Klaus, delves into the history of one of the most prominent figures in German literature. It also addresses political issues that connect with current events, while also representing a personal quest by a son who barely knew his father. With excellent editing that captures the chaos and dynamism of the turbulent decades of the 1960s and 1970s, it offers a multifaceted portrait of a complex personality, but also a personal quest by a son who tries to understand his father's contradictions. It evokes his father's most controversial poems to provoke debate among viewers, ultimately serving as a fitting tribute to Erich Fried.

review_content: A film based on Raim Laode's song with the same title, **_"Komang."_**    Honestly, I haven't followed the development of music and film for a long time, but when I watched this film, it felt like I was being treated to something fresh. Telling a love story between Raim Laode and Komang Ade that is depicted so close to everyday life, making the audience (especially me) feel like they are being invited into it. Not complicated, not long-winded, simple but sweet.    Although there are some parts that I personally find a bit funny during the scenes of the two lovebirds drunk on love that are full of sweet talk. But the rest is very, very interesting.    Not to mention Aurora here is super beautiful. Beautiful and sweet to the point that I myself can't seem to find the right words. Her performance as Komang Ade also deserves a thumbs up. The Balinese accent that was created and the local Sulawesi accent (if I'm not mistaken) that is also present through Kiesha Alvaro are really an extraordinary attraction for me.

Or did they plan it together once they figured it was an accident… I wonder…

**Pros:**  17 Pages [spoiler]uses a very interesting storytelling technique where it tells a story from two different viewpoints. That alone isn't what makes it unique though. It's the fact that you don't know about it until you've finished the first part. You are randomly shown one of the parts and can only view the second one after finishing the first. It highlights how a story can be told in multiple ways and how that can influence the opinions of those that see it. By putting a focus on that aspect, it allows the viewer to think more critically and come to their own conclusions about which viewpoint they think is the correct one.[/spoiler]    **Neutral:**  I didn't find the scientific case covered in 17 Pages to be quite as interesting as those in some of the other videos made by BobbyBrocolli (Kevan Mackay's username). Maybe because the end result wasn't as impactful as I was hoping for or maybe because it was a little too deep into the details.

review_content: With a little more background on Slovakia's communist regime, it would be a bit clearer what is going on, from the bizarre tradition of publicly throwing water at women to the reason why Perla was in danger of being arrested in her native country, but I wasn't too bothered by the lack of this information. Not everything always has to be spelled out. The fact that Perla doesn't always make smart choices can be frustrating for the viewer, but it is realistic. Perla is just a human being and makes wrong choices. I found it all quite moving, and well acted. The daughter in the story was very touching and played her part convincingly.

review_content: [IFFR '25] There are two interesting elements in the story of a woman torn between the stability of her life in Austria and her return to communist Czechoslovakia: on the one hand, it establishes the historical context without placing it at the centre of the story. So the characters don't talk about politics, but are swept along by the influence of the political environment. And the protagonist is not portrayed as a victim despite the attitudes of a patriarchal and sexist society, in which humiliating traditions remain, but rather she has the strength to face these situations with dignity.

[Venice '25] The most realistic fiction film possible about the migration routes of refugee families heading to Malaysia. There is a constant realism throughout a story that makes no concessions, being as harsh as possible, without falling into the romanticization of the journey, but also without falling into the melodrama of survival. Akio Fujimoto manages to construct a story that successfully balances the dramatic reality of the Rohingya refugees who, although aware of the dangers of the journey, seem to have no other option but to try to reach Malaysia, with the eyes of two brothers who, despite facing difficult situations, keep their innocence and hope alive.

Unfortunately, the director's presence in the first half is so loud that it often drowns out the message. Still, the incredible cast and brave position taken by the filmmaker make this a worthwhile film.

review_content: [Venice '25] A journey along the roads of northern Mexico that reflects the character of a country where the persistence of toxic masculinity contrasts with the emergence of new forms of diversity, embodied by a generation that no longer hides its true self. The film creates complex characters who confront normalized violence, portraying the raw sexuality that unfolds in clandestine meeting places such as truck stops and public toilets at roadside restaurants. The explicit sexual scenes reflect not only the characters' physical desires, but also their loneliness and emotional emptiness.

[Sheffield '25] Through this brief odyssey of a dreamer who loves cinema, Fernando's relationship with his son Dylan reflects the idea of ​​transmitting ancestral knowledge, such as language, which is so important in indigenous communities where language is the vehicle through which traditions and knowledge are passed on. In a sort of David-trying-to-get-Goliath's attention tale, this film focuses on the transmission of endangered cultural elements, but it's also a heartfelt love letter to cinema and how some films form part of the cultural education of several generations.

[Venice '24] Some images in this documentary about the consequences of the war in Ukraine may seem familiar, but the power of these images remains a prominent driving force for this documentary that wants to portray the drastic transformation of a society that must face a much more inhumane way of survival. Less propagandistic than other films, it reveals great humanity through a montage that contrasts moments of chaos with the silence of what has been left behind. When a group of students proposes a new utopian Ukraine (without corruption, without borders, without social division...), it is also reflecting the weaknesses of what Ukraine was before the war.

[Venice '25] The film, which was shot in South Africa because the Swiss government banned the protagonists from entering, raises the need to listen to the personal experiences of survivors, in contrast to the usual generic representations made by the media or social media, through cold statistics of victims or striking photographs, but without context. Nine survivors of the first year of bombing, who managed to flee to Egypt and Oman in 2024, describe their experiences and their losses. But the silences, pauses, and gestures resonate more emotionally than the verbal descriptions.

cast_names: Valeria Golino, Saul Nanni, Jasmine Trinca, Francesco Colella, Gianni Bissacca

And there's a poetic perspective that transforms the story into a dark yet luminous tale, the tale of a prey in the midst of a world of predators.

[VdR '25] A beautifully subtle and emotionally powerful film that doesn't even need many words to define a family that never speaks of their father, who disappeared after he was deported from the United States to Mexico. movie_title: The Boulet Brothers' Holiday of Horrors
cast_names: Bonnie Aarons, Aaron Abrams, Briana Venskus, David Dastmalchian, Lauren LaVera, Jennifer Polania, Leah Kilpatrick, Tracie Thoms, Tiffany Smith, Abhora
review_content: This was a real disappointment. On paper, Holiday of Horrors should have been a slam dunk—I love Tales from the Crypt, I love Christmas-themed horror, and I love short-form anthology storytelling. Somehow, this manages to miss on all fronts. The segments are shorter, weirder, and far less satisfying than comparable anthology horror, rarely giving any idea enough room to breathe or land with impact.  It’s not totally bad, but it’s shockingly close to the floor of what still qualifies as mainstream horror content. The creativity is there in spirit, but the execution is thin, underwhelming, and ultimately forgettable.  Rating: 0.5/5 – 35% – Hard Skip

movie_title: The Boulet Brothers' Holiday of Horrors

cast_names: Paige Hurd, Amber Stevens West, Cosmo de La Fuente, Jhone Y. Lucas, Andre McCoy, Nicole O'Neill, Kossim Osseni, Branden Wellington
review_content: Together is not a comfortable or conventional film. I didn’t experience it as a story meant to entertain or explain things in a traditional way, but rather as an immersive emotional experience; at times even exhausting. I understand why it might be hard to watch, but that discomfort is part of its value.       The monstrous elements didn’t feel gratuitous to me. They work as a physical metaphor for something deeply intimate and recognizable: the fear of losing oneself in another person, and at the same time, the panic of being left alone. The entire film moves within that tension between the desire for fusion and the need to remain an individual. When love crosses a certain threshold, it stops being a meeting point and starts to resemble absorption.       Isolation accelerates this process. Cut off from the outside world, the couple is forced to look at each other without distractions, confronting not only the other person but also what they can no longer sustain within themselves. Adaptation stops being romantic and becomes almost violent, shaped by attachment to the past and the deceptive feeling of moving forward while actually remaining stuck.       While watching, I couldn’t stop thinking about the Platonic myth of the Androgyne, popularized in The Origin of Love from Hedwig and the Angry Inch: that original body split in two, condemned to endlessly search for its other half. Together seems to ask what happens when that desire to become one again turns literal and physical. The answer is uncomfortable but clear: absolute fusion doesn’t save: it deforms.       In the end, rather than asking whether union is possible, the film asks how to unite without disappearing. If loving means erasing oneself, the result is monstrous; if it means transforming without self-annihilation, perhaps something more human can emerge. That’s why I liked it: because it unsettles, because it refuses to idealize love, and because it leaves you with a question that lingers long after the credits roll. "How much of yourself are you willing to share while still remaining yourself?"

As such, the film's effectiveness rests solely on the shoulders / voice of the lead, Saadet Işıl Aksoy, who rises to the challenge with brio.    Directed by the same duo (Çağla Zencirci & Guillaume Giovanetti) who brought us the impressive _Sibel_, this mid-length feature (at 76 minutes) captivates.

With animation of simple lines and detailed backgrounds, it has interesting aspects are raised about the complex coexistence with the Chinese cultural tradition, the ambition of youth and uncertainty of the future in the world of art. A kind of vibe reminiscent of Richard Linklater but far less exciting among the musings of the Art School students.

[Sheffield '24] Although it takes place in an old family mansion where a mysterious death occurred in 1650, the film doesn't talk about ghosts but about fantasies, those that accompanied the children who are now forced to clear the memory impregnated in the walls, in the dragon figures found in ornaments and tableware, or in the fairy rings in the garden. This mixture of childhood fantasies, which are represented in the traditional texture of the stone walls and the environment that surrounds the house, is used by the director to create a beautiful magical and poetic atmosphere that refers to childhood with a melancholic tone.

cast_names: Christine Hakim, José Rizal Manua, Kaan Lativan, Lukman Sardi, Happy Salma, Nai Djenar Maesa Ayu, Arswendy Bening Swara, Iswadi Pratama
review_content: I could be considered a bit late to watching this movie.    One thing I want to say is, no wonder why this movie won so many awards at BIFF. From the first minute, the film presents an engaging visual, depicting the Pantura region (I'm guessing), which I've known and heard from my parents.    Resting places for truck drivers are often associated with "massage" locations.    From here, we're introduced to Sartika, a woman who (whether willingly or not) leaves her village to find any work she can find and ends up meeting Ibu.    The first conflict also begins with Sartika's concern about the job that she had to choose. Honestly, this is where I felt sad. So many people do jobs not because they want to, but because they're forced to for the sake of their children and family.    Until she finally meets Hadi. Honestly, at this point, I was already suspicious about the conflict that would follow because their meeting was too perfect, it made me have trust issues. I thought Hadi would die or have an accident, or something else. It turns out I was too positive about a man like Hadi. At this point, I felt cheated because I'd forgotten Fedi Nuril's usual role. :)    More important, what can i get from this movie is the struggle of a mother, a woman, and a human being in living a hard life. I felt empty, sad, wanting to cry (which I did), and upset all at once after watching this movie.    Furthermore, hats off to Reza Rahardian and Felix K. Nesi as director and writer for successfully creating a truly excellent work.    The movie, all of the crews, actors and actresses deserve a 10 out of 10.

cast_names: Ken Kirby, Carlos Carrasco, Diane Robin, Earl Baylon, Marc Forget, Mitch Narito
review_content: I have to be honest, I don’t normally watch movies like this. Slow burn coming of age dramas with long, quiet scenes and a lot of unspoken emotion usually aren’t my go to. I tend to gravitate toward faster paced stuff—thrillers, sci-fi, or something with a clear plot engine that keeps things moving. So when I put this one on, I was fully prepared to be polite but ultimately unmoved.  Instead, I was completely hooked.  This Time is one of those rare films that sneaks up on you. The story follows two people in their early twenties reconnecting after years apart, navigating the messy space between who they used to be and who they’re trying to become. There’s no big dramatic twist, no life altering event that forces everything to explode, just two people, real conversations, and the kind of small, aching moments that actually feel lived in.  The performances are outstanding. The leads have this natural, lived in chemistry that never feels forced. The cinematography is beautiful—soft, golden hour light and long takes that let you breathe with the characters. The soundtrack is subtle but perfect, never overpowering the emotion on screen.  I walked away from this one thinking about it for hours before writing my review. It’s quiet, it’s tender, and it’s surprisingly powerful. If you’re someone who usually skips these kinds of movies like I do… give This Time a chance anyway. You might surprise yourself.

[Berlinale '26] An entertaining blend of fictions created by its protagonists, realities described by their families, and a reconstruction of a world that perhaps one day can be better. This optimistic and joyful outlook is contagious, vital, and empowering, presented from a female perspective that refuses to be relegated to a secondary role, even in Brazilian communities bordering on poverty, like Guaribas. Within its simplicity and playful nature, this documentary, brimming with joy, manages to delve into issues such as the social structures imposed in the most disadvantaged areas of Brazil, which still needs to build bridges to help many families escape poverty and illiteracy.

An AI announces that there's a killer among them... The premise may be good, but "good with a twist" is a bit much, right?
