cast_names: Chris Mayers, Haley Leary, Tordy Clark, Hamid-Reza Benjamin Thompson
review_content: A great base idea. The whole pre battle part, where everyone is casually discussing counter measures to hellish entities, with the new neighbours believing it is a joke, is real fun.    When it starts, well, I would have liked a bit more seriousness, instead of the [spoiler]everybody but them dies[/spoiler] scenario for comic effect. Like, more people, real defence, maybe some heroic actions. I mean, maybe Lucas fucks it up early, but even before that,someone dies in the first seconds, at the first attack of the first monster. This highly contrasts with the "nobody died for years" we're being sold.    Would have like more monsters, and more counter measures. They surely take their sweet time between waves.    It takes a bit too long for Lucas to step up., this really slows the action down.    But otherwise, yep, stupid jokes, funny gore, and the movie doesn't drag it too much, great entertainment value.

cast_names: Chris Mayers, Haley Leary, Tordy Clark, Hamid-Reza Benjamin Thompson
review_content: Hold the Fort rockets through a HOA party where a portal to hell opens and rubber monsters spill out. New homeowners at an equinox shindig join forces with a pint‑sized folk hero, guns and gore fly, and the film keeps the jokes and carnage coming. At just 74 minutes it never overstays, leaning into creature‑feature fun with charm and pace. Cheap in the right ways and cut to the bone, it’s the perfect midnight palate cleanser.    If you liked… Tremors, PG: Psycho Goreman, From Dusk Till Dawn.

cast_names: Chris Mayers, Haley Leary, Tordy Clark, Hamid-Reza Benjamin Thompson
review_content: [Fantasia '25] Following the tradition of horror films set in suburban neighborhoods, this genre mashup moves from dark comedy to gory nonsense with considerable skill and without hiding its references, especially the films of Sam Raimi. Using traditional elements of horror comedies, from singular demons to imaginative artifacts, it manages to entertain while having as its undercurrent a certain ironic look at the middle class, which has been losing ground in American society due to growing inequality. It's funny enough to forgive some of the poor effects, while still maintaining its B-movie tone.

[Annecy '25] The narrative structure of the story blends the most emotional aspect with a space adventure that involves dangers and challenges. Following a trend in recent years in animated films, the absence of dialogue doesn't pose a problem for the narrative, but in this case, songs are introduced to help connect with the story, almost all of them performed in a calm tone that almost lulls the characters to sleep. This film is a short story, with some philosophical undertones surrounding the end of existence in the third act, told with sensitivity and with an ability to connect with the viewer through a simple story that may not take any creative risks, but conveys humanity even when depicting a non-human character.

So I have not listened (besides the music he did for movies) to any music by Kid Koala and neither did I read the graphic novel this is based on, but this certainly was delightful. Who knew we needed another animated movie lacking any dialogue with a robotic protagonist already! This one probably is a bit more for the children but I think everyone can take something away from this. Oh and the best about Berlinala are certainly the Q&As after the movies. Knowing that it is without any dialogue was mostly because it is a story about loss, in this case Kid Koala/Eric San processed the loss of his grandma in the graphic novel/movie and because she didnt speak any english when they were growing up in Canada, one of their favourite things to watch was old silent Charlie Chaplin movies....

movie_title: How to Be Normal and the Oddness of the Other World
cast_names: Luisa-Céline Gaffron, Elke Winkens, Cornelius Obonya, Oliver Rosskopf, Martina Poel, Nancy Mensah-Offei, Harald Krassnitzer, Jutta Fastian, Fanny Altenburger, Christian Holzmann
review_content: Great visual appearance. Liked the mixture of different shot styles. They blended together well.   Story was very thick and had (maybe a bit to) many layers. Most of the psychological conditions were well presented, but towards the end it got weaker, because the story needed different acting that you would expect from a person in those states.

movie_title: How to Be Normal and the Oddness of the Other World
cast_names: Luisa-Céline Gaffron, Elke Winkens, Cornelius Obonya, Oliver Rosskopf, Martina Poel, Nancy Mensah-Offei, Harald Krassnitzer, Jutta Fastian, Fanny Altenburger, Christian Holzmann
review_content: [Berlinale '25] A mix of visual and narrative ideas that are attractive, but that give the impression that they express more on the surface than in the substance. Translating the perspective of a character who feels misunderstood to reflect that in the world around her the concept of normality is also very relative, is an interesting idea, but in this case it is so lost in the aesthetics that, although it is tinged with a certain sense of humor and there is a clear empathy for the protagonist, it ends up being more intentional than effective.

**A Testament to Unbreakable Spirit**    "Kherson: Human Safari" stands as an extraordinary witness to human endurance in the face of unimaginable brutality. Director Zarina Zabrisky has crafted more than a documentary-she has created a vital historical document that captures the raw truth of civilian life under Russian aggression.    The film's five-part structure chronicles the devastating journey of Kherson's residents: from the shock of invasion through the suffocating months of occupation, the bittersweet liberation, the catastrophic flooding after the Kakhovka Dam's destruction, and the ongoing nightmare of drone warfare that turns human beings into targets for sport.    What makes this work exceptional is its intimacy. These aren't statistics or headlines-these are neighbors, teachers, doctors, and artists who risk everything simply by speaking their truth on camera. Their voices carry the weight of lived experience: the underground hospitals operating by flashlight, the schools teaching in basements, the artists who continue creating amid ruins. One unforgettable scene shows a woman dancing in her destroyed school-an act of defiance that speaks louder than any protest.    The film's power lies not in sensationalism but in its quiet accumulation of testimony. When someone whispers, "While the world is asleep, we are dying here," the words land with devastating precision. The documented drone attacks-where civilians are stalked, filmed, and murdered for online propaganda-reveal a new dimension of warfare's cruelty.    Yet this is fundamentally a story of resistance. Despite nine months of occupation, despite daily terror, Kherson's people refuse to be erased. They protest, they rebuild, they create art, they keep their Christmas trees up for hope. Their mantra "Vse bude dobre" (Everything will be fine) isn't naive optimism-it's steel-willed determination.    Zabrisky's brave filmmaking has already drawn backlash from Russian forces, with increased drone strikes following the film's release. This only underscores its importance as both artistic achievement and urgent call to action. Available free since June 27, 2025, this film demands to be seen-not as entertainment, but as essential testimony.    "Kherson: Human Safari" achieves what great documentary filmmaking aspires to: it makes the distant immediate, the abstract concrete, and the unbearable unforgettable. It's a portrait of a city that refuses to disappear, told by people who insist on being heard.

**Kherson: Human Safari is available to watch FREE online!**    This powerful 72-minute documentary by journalist Zarina Zabrisky captures the reality of life in Kherson, Ukraine - from the Russian invasion and occupation to liberation and the ongoing attacks. The film has been made freely available (to the public since June 27, 2025, as a way to raise awareness about what's happening in Kherson: https://khersonhumansafari.com/

cast_names: Steve Oram, Sahajak Boonthanakit, Ting Sue, Jonathan Samson, Thaweesak Alexander Thananan
review_content: A clever film that terrifies it's audience with the threat of seeing images they wouldn't want to see. This film deals with real-life horrors that make your blood curdle but also various more personal dilemmas. The performances are great, especially Steve Oram who manages to inject humour into a very dark tale, as he is so good at doing. Really well made original film.

cast_names: Steve Oram, Sahajak Boonthanakit, Ting Sue, Jonathan Samson, Thaweesak Alexander Thananan
review_content: A vicar in Bangkok and his wife reel after an animal cruelty video rattles their daughter; when she vanishes, grief mutates into obsession. The search drags them through sex tourism, graft and moral rot while his belief buckles under Old Testament fury. Rumley threads theological anguish through exploitation textures, pushing questions of forgiveness versus vengeance until they cut. Performances feel lived in, the city sweats, and the film refuses easy catharsis. Miserable by design but piercing.    If you liked... First Reformed, Red White & Blue, Hardcore (1979).

cast_names: Brandon Yates
review_content: Stunning animation with some cute story moments. Very generic overall plot, but a sweet and fun short watch.

cast_names: Baraa Alem
review_content: [Venice '25] A film that speaks about the freedom of three generations of women who have carried their stories in silence and the different ways of accepting it: those who understand it as resistance, those who seek it in flight, and those who claim it within Saudi society itself. The reflection of cultural diversity in the desert is manifested through the languages ​​spoken throughout the film: Arabic, Urdu, Turkish... and the interdependent relationships that the characters must endure, which is one of the fundamental elements that allows this film to distance itself from others that also address female perspectives in Arab societies.

cast_names: Anne Alexander Sieder, John Keogh, Kai Helm
review_content: I had no expectations, but I wanted to give it a chance. I've been watching it for maybe a week and it's only just finished. It was very boring for me. The production was passable, but the effects looked very artificial. The acting was mediocre. I don't know if it's watchable. If you can't find anything else to watch, this movie might be worth it. Otherwise, it's not really a watchable production. Still, thanks for your efforts.

cast_names: Anne Alexander Sieder, John Keogh, Kai Helm
review_content: Crazy intro, to say the least, violence begets violence in the end. It's a rather simple story up front, but I feel that's exactly what they want us to think.  So much intrigue, and so many unanswered questions as to the finality of the clues given.  Now for some back story, and it's substantial and one that I can really get into. What a finale, and you must see for yourself to truly appreciate it.

cast_names: Praveen, Harsha Chemudu, Ramachandra Raju
review_content: #BakasuraRestaurant: Unexpectedly watched this film and honestly had a good time. #Praveen and #VivaHarsha are so good. A fun blend of comedy and thrills that work, and #ShiningPhani nailed it as Bakasura. A Decent Watch.

cast_names: Justin Bartha, Anna Camp, Lucy Punch, George Young, Tawny Newsome, Ignacio Serricchio, Maulik Pancholy, Stephen Guarino, Natasha Leggero, Cole Stockel
review_content: The irony of the title being "Nuked"... The whole thing feels radioactive. It begins with several unique booms in the worst way possible and ends with a groan almost identical to that of a brain-dead zombie. The performances are irritating, like a fly buzzing around you constantly, and the casting, in general, is just bizarre. I couldn't latch onto any character, and the story almost got there but failed miserably.

movie_title: TMZ Presents: The Real Hulk Hogan
cast_names: Harvey Levin, Hulk Hogan, Sylvester Stallone, Vince McMahon, Bill Goldberg, Mark Henry, Ashley Fliehr, James Ray Hart, Mick Foley, Mark LoMonaco
review_content: If you're really into pro wrestling background, history, etc then you'll probably enjoy this. For me, the biggest plus was that it was only 43 minutes long. As a "Hulk Hogan" biopic, it was incredibly shallow, seemingly thrown together as quickly as the headline that TMZ threw out only moments after discovering that Terry Bollea (Hulk Hogan) had passed away. There was virtually nothing "in-depth" or revealing about this, and in the latter part of the film, it came across as more of an _"oopsie, Hogan really wasn't a racist; he just made a mistake"_ type of thing. I mean, I was a Hulk Hogan fan back in the mid- to late-80's, mid 90's, when he was still "Hulk Hogan" but there have been too many other stories that have come out, and if you followed professional sports entertainment like I have, you don't have to follow social media or online pulp rags to know that Terry Bollea cashed in on his fame and walked across the backs of many of his fellow entertainers. He was a tremendous entertainer, and yes, he was probably the greatest to ever lace up a pair of wrestling boots, when it comes to the entertainment aspect of it, but he was no saint, and he made a lot of enemies. This film tends to whitewash that particular side of Terry Bollea, probably all in the idea of preserving the positive aspect of his legacy. But a real biopic is not afraid to talk about the faults and flaws their subject has, and _The Real Hulk Hogan_ effectively avoids that aspect. So, in a sense, this is far, far short of telling the whole story of "The Real Hulk Hogan" because they refuse to tell everything. Interesting in its own way, yes, but definitely slanted to protect Hogan.

The first one was hard to follow because of the lack of understandable communication. This one is easier, at first at least. Humans still exist on the planet, there has been a war with mulligans, the artificial humanoids created for labor who rebelled, but peace came hundreds of years ago.  A strange phenomenon is discovered and humans and mulligans prepare a common mission to explore it when terrorists from a mulligan sect attack. We follow Lady Triss and Robin, her customized robot assistant, accompanied by a human Ambassador and his assistant, and a mulligan warrior.  It gets harder to follow when they encounter the phenomenon and it's a portal to time/parallel worlds travel.    Several acts, that show the same events with new information each time.   First one is straightforward, but we don't understand some weird details and the origin of a warrior robot ally. A civilization based on the mulligan warrior is beyond the portal.   This is explained after Robin enters the portal and builds his own civilization over centuries to be able to come back and save Triss in the second part.  We also see the mysterious warrior that helped them, secretly in the beginning, directly afterwards, sent from Robin's civilization.  Third one is more confusing, as a civilization based on the assistant, probably having enslaved the ambassador, exits the portal and they kill/eat them.  Not really sure why it happens, what they do, where the civilization came from, since before that it was consequences of someone entering the portal.  Then one based on Robin.    The more it goes, the more confusing it is as it seems less and less causality bound. It's still pretty fascinating.      Like the previous one, incredible animation work, a very original world, great decor and character design. Nothing more to add on that level, it is just excellent. Top level creativity at all over.

cast_names: Thomas Mraz, Marlene Morreis, Maria Hofstätter, Johannes Silberschneider, Thomas Stipsits, Heinz Marecek, Roland Düringer, Adele Neuhauser, Erika Mottl, Stefano Bernardin
review_content: Super funny, best idea to watch this during Christmas in an Austrian cinema

cast_names: Nas-T, Remy Ishak, Rosyam Nor, Anwaar Beg Moghal, Sweta Sekhon, Halim Othman, Amelia Henderson, A. Galak, Azlee Senario, Wahid Senario
review_content: Casting Nas T as main character cam tak kena. He cannot bring out that keluang man feel. Story lak suka simply throw in random meme here and there, end up jadi cringe and not relevant to the plot at all. Can see they try to tarik Gen Z yang maybe tak kenal keluang man, at the same time want to jaga OG fans yang now millennial, nampak agak forced. Overall solid but not that remarkable

cast_names: Nas-T, Remy Ishak, Rosyam Nor, Anwaar Beg Moghal, Sweta Sekhon, Halim Othman, Amelia Henderson, A. Galak, Azlee Senario, Wahid Senario
review_content: I watched this with super low expectations, but I ended up being genuinely surprised by how hilarious it was. The humor totally gave off that Mamat Khalid vibe which was sharp, random, and unapologetically local. I was laughing non-stop from the start because so many of the funny scenes came out of nowhere.  That Insp Sahab scene with the Karan Arjun reference? Damn too funny I just couldn’t handle it hahaha. And when Keluang Man suddenly started speaking in Hindi?? I totally was not ready for that 😂  Honestly, the whole thing feels like a Stephen Chow-style comedy, but with a Malaysian twist. Super chaotic, super fun. As a millennial, I appreciated all the little pop culture throwbacks — from Maria Mariana to old-school Bollywood. Solid nostalgia + comedy combo ❤

cast_names: Sam Riley, Alexandra Maria Lara, Noah Taylor, Mark Monero, Bill Nighy
review_content: Multi dimension, space and time loops, repeating characters, non chronological narration, experimental science. I just love this kind of stuff.    The way it was pitched I was expecting a super brain fuck, but it's absolutely followable and understandable.    If we assume the last flashbacks we see are real, it makes sense: [spoiler]the experiment goes wrong, probably intentionally, so they retreat into the House that is a kind of pocket dimension, to survive the explosion. However only them 4 survive, the other die and are heard in the Valley of Screams. Somehow the heavy metal injected allowed Harlan to exit[/spoiler]. But is it real, we saw different versions of flashbacks before.    We can assume, as it it said in the beginning, that once you get through it, you (probably unconsciously) retain the important information, allowing each loop to progress slightly differently. But there's not much Harlan actually does though, he is mostly passive. It mostly looks like he's only here to open access for Aclima.    It's very entertaining to go through the steps and plunge into the weirdness, gaining some knowledge of the real events bit by bit.    Probably only a consequence of small budget, but I found all the vehicles scenes, with animations at 20$ stop motion film level, completely endearing. They definitely exaggerated that for fun, specially the helicopter crash. Great touch.    A lot of open questions though. Where does the whole billionaire/military/journalist/scientist scenario comes from ?  I love the facts that replica inside formed factions, but what happened inside to create these ? What are they fighting for ?  How come not all copies react the same ? They're not all part of the faction. Kesler seems to have several very different roles, more like NPCs, while Aclima has several active roles. Including the House operator ones for both of them.  Why does it look like there is only one Chambers when the others are multiple ?  We don't really see other Harlans inside, but there's this whole thing about ensuring he's the real one. So where do the fake come from, and who sends them ? What's their storyline ?  If [spoiler]killing Chambers[/spoiler] is not the end, what is ? Convince him ?     Not that they need to be answered, just show the depth and possibilities of this world.

cast_names: Teresa Palmer, Joe Dempsie, Eamon Farren, Adrienne Pickering, Zahra Newman, Sarah Peirse, Lois Baxter
review_content: Theresa Palmer and Joe Dempsie had great chemistry in the movie. Loved the anxiety/OCD elements throughout the movie and how they merge this with the romance events of the film. Great movie to catch in cinemas with your group of gals.

cast_names: Karry Wang, Zhou Shen, Huang Bo, Jia Bing, Ma Dong, Jolin Jin, Yan Peilun, Zhang Xiaowan, Jerry Liu
review_content: [Annecy '25] It can sometimes seem overly confusing in its revelations and at the same time somewhat simplistic in the conception of time control, especially in one half of the story that isn't as spectacular as it claims to be. But it features well-crafted animation and detailed backgrounds of early 20th-century Shanghai, even if the narrative can be unbalanced. It's a story of empowerment for its female protagonist that doesn't quite develop its more complex ideas, such as cinema and its power to retain the past or predestination, but it builds a narrative that's entertaining in its first half and melancholic in the second, establishing a more reflective structure: "Not to be forgotten. That is the meaning of time."

cast_names: Vishwak Sen, Kayadu Lohar, Naresh, Easwari Rao, Muralidhar Goud, Pammi Sai, Raghu Babu, Sampath Raj
review_content: Dont know why its has below par review.. I admit the movie has no story, choppy dubbing and patched cinematography... Only good thing is i watched with zero expectation and its only 2hrs.. I passed my time with one liner punches and cute heroine.  better than top tier heros with outdated shit boring action movies

cast_names: Simone Bucio
review_content: [Venice '25] A look full of ambiguities that focuses on the relationship between the two protagonists, and which has the courage to leave unclear for most of the story which path it will follow, adopting an intimate approach that is interested in bodies and faces, rather than words. When the story unravels its own narrative, it does so not to place us in a more comfortable place, but quite the opposite. It builds a relationship that transcends some social boundaries and delves into a metaphorical and symbolic perspective that is equally bold and ambitious.

cast_names: Sean Gunn, Jermaine Fowler, Scarlett DeMeo, Michael Abbott Jr., Harley Bronwyn, David Pressman, Sean Wing, Aaron Holliday, Kellan Rhude, Paige Searcy
review_content: Classic narrative of multiple character stories crossing over, all turning to carnage, mostly because of a single asshole loser using deep fake videos. A lot of characters, high in colors, almost all interesting.    The intro is a bit apart from the rest, not sure whether it was intended to highlight that underneath the comic gore there is a serious warning. A guy just kills his girlfriend because of a deepfake video. No gore, just really dramatic. Most of the rest is a lot more fun.    The whole girl afraid of black people think is real funny, even if predictable and already done.  The husband that "just watches" is great.  And the realistic cosplay weapon powered gore fest at the end is a delight.  Not sure the final attackers were seen before ? Since most of the rest were related, it seems odd they'd come out of nowhere.  High quality entertainment (and gore).    There's still a truth inside. We're probably not that far of this happening. Not sure who the warning is about as the real villain here though ? AI that will allow chaos ? Nihilistic entitled assholes that would use it ? The real criminal that would weaponize like at the end ?    For my part, I'd bet on the assholes. Yes criminals would (and already are) abusing AI to scam people. But to quote George Carlin, "Think about how stupid the average person is, and then realize that half of them are stupider than that". But the same goes with assholes. Just look at random comments on social media. Here Duke might be a bit exaggerated, but is he really ? Crazy entitlement level: "What ? The world famous cosplay creator does not want to work for me on short delay and without money ?". He can't even process this rejection. Nihilism: he just goes and ruins a random girl's life (I don't think he thought of having her killed, not that he would mind), just because he found her posts (not directed at him) mildly annoying. How prevalent is this kind of thinking in today's (mostly american) culture ? Think of the damage these people can do.

cast_names: Sean Gunn, Jermaine Fowler, Scarlett DeMeo, Michael Abbott Jr., Harley Bronwyn, David Pressman, Sean Wing, Aaron Holliday, Kellan Rhude, Paige Searcy
review_content: Appofeniacs is Tarantino-pulp meets Black Mirror dread. Deepfakes, crossed wires, and a bloody punchline.    Multiple vignettes collide as Duke, an aggrieved incel with a deepfake app, wages petty war on strangers for sport. A hot-tub fiasco snowballs. A cosplaying arms dealer, Clinto Binto, sells anime swords. A wildcard Uber driver, Texas Tim, keeps reappearing at the worst moments. The intersecting threads riff on confirmation bias and weaponised fakery, building to splattery consequences when screen and reality finally fuse. It’s brazen about the Tarantino worship, but the techno-panic satire and gallows humour land.    If you liked… Pulp Fiction, Black Mirror, Lowlife.

[Dok.fest '25] There are some pacing issues when family conversations are shifted, or when Najiba Noori asks her mother questions to get her to share her personal experience. Because although she describes herself as her mother's best friend, Hawa isn't very expressive, and the intimacy that supposedly exists between them doesn't quite translate to the screen. It's a story of emancipation and broken dreams, but one of hope nonetheless, which notes in the end credits that Afghanistan is the only country in the world that prevents women from accessing education, despite being funded by Qatar.

[Locarno '25] An ambitious proposal to reflect a new generation of Irish musicians who recover traditions, but eliminate conservative and religious aspects to build a vision of the future that does not renounce its past. Giving sufficient space to the music, some concerns are raised about the weight of division and violence, but from a postcolonial perspective, that of young people who reclaim the offensive nature of folk music, the discomfort of lyrics that aspire to upset religious and political pulpits in order to find their own identity. It represents the contradictions and uncertainties, but also the hope conveyed by these new musicians who no longer speak of confrontations, but of coexistence.

cast_names: Jean Chevalier, François Cluzet, Karin Viard, Alex Lutz, Émilie Gavois-Kahn, Olivier Broche, Marc Zinga, Nathalie Richard, Vincent Grass, Francis Leplay
review_content: Like a broken leg: unfunny and elevated by a strong cast.    The behind-the-scenes shenanigans of appointing a French Prime Minister featuring a father / son relationship no one cares about and a bf / gf relationship no one believes in.    Alex Lutz (Guy!) and François Cluzet (ah, 'Round Midnight ) are incredible but the story is predictable, the jokes are cliché, and -- like an election -- the outcome is disappointing.

cast_names: Maribel Verdú
review_content: To be honest, initially, the style of the animated short film “The Quinta's Ghost” reminded me a little of a cutscene from an indie video game. Later, however, the film drifts more and more into the surreal, which appealed to me much more. And since the story about the last years of the painter Francisco de Goya also has quite a few horror elements, it was right up my alley anyway. It's not necessarily a typical Oscar short film, which makes it stand out from the crowd. I wasn't entirely convinced by the style, but the idea alone gets a thumbs up from me.

cast_names: James Oliver Wheatley, Fotini Papatheodorou, Anthony Barclay, Colin Mace, Skye Little Wing Dimova-Saw, Priya Blackburn, Noush Skaugen, Katrin Vankova, Jonas Talkington
review_content: Bad performance from even the worse actors. The storyline feels empty, the pacing is slow, and the characters lack any real depth or development. Overall,it's a disappointing experience that doesn't offer anything memorable or worth watching.

cast_names: Prithviraj Sukumaran, Kajol, Ibrahim Ali Khan, Jitendra Joshi, K.C. Shankar, Mihir Ahuja, Boman Irani, Rohed Khan, Anuraag Arora
review_content: I wanted to love this one, but it left me feeling kinda meh. It’s got this big idea of mixing a father-son drama with some heavy-duty patriotism in Kashmir, but it doesn’t quite hit the mark.  So, the story follows Colonel Vijay Menon and his son Harman, who gets mixed up with some bad guys. Kajol plays Meher, the mom trying to hold it all together. Sounds intense, right? But here’s the thing: it’s so predictable. The trailer basically spills everything, Harman’s alive, he’s not a good kid anymore, and yeah, he’s got a suicide mission vibe. No surprises there. The one twist with Kajol’s character being tied to the terrorists? It just flops because they don’t drop any hints. And if she switched sides ages ago, how’s she still feeding info to the cops? Makes no sense.  The emotions? They’re just not there most of the time. I didn’t feel completely what they were trying to show. They’re going for this deep father-son thing mixed with love for the country, and it kinda works, but it’s half-baked. Like, Vijay lets his innocent kid die at the start but then risks it all to save him when he’s a terrorist? “What’s the logic, bro?” And don’t get me started on the time jump, eight years pass, Harman’s all grown up, but Kajol and Prithviraj look the same. Oh, and the movie keeps explaining stuff over and over. Like, I get it, you don’t need to spoon-feed me every five minutes! The ending’s a mess too, Kajol gets stabbed and dies, but Vijay and Harman take bullets and just keep going? “Are they superheroes now?”  But it’s not all bad. The music’s pretty dope. The songs, even if some are remade, fit the mood and got me a little emotional. Kashmir looks stunning, those visuals are straight-up gorgeous, props to the cinematographer. And the acting? Kajol was good as the mom,  she’s got these moments where you really feel her pain. Prithviraj is solid too, all intense and army-guy vibes. Ibrahim’s okay, does some cool fight scenes but doesn’t say much, so hard to judge. Now, Mihir Ahuja? Dude, he’s the real deal. His villain vibes are so good, you can see the evil in his eyes.  It tries hard but feels like a missed shot. Great cast, awesome visuals, nice music, but the story’s too predictable, and the emotions don’t land. It can be a one time watch but don’t expect to be blown away.

cast_names: Kue Lawrence, Christina Brucato, Oscar Nuñez, Jamad Mays, Eugenie Bondurant, Clayton Royal Johnson, Michael Sean Tighe, Thomas Philip O'Neill, Rashaan Rondo, Hudson Meek
review_content: [Fantasia '25] The film presents a scenario that could almost be considered a warning rather than a dystopian one, a consideration of where a society that has normalized violence and focuses defense through more violence, rather than analyzing its origins, might be heading. At times, it can be overly expository in its approach, as if it were more interested in warning of the dangers than in presenting a scenario that is already a warning in itself. And perhaps it wasn't necessary to construct such stereotypical characters. The format and the B/W cinematography seem to represent a throwback to the past, even though we are talking about a hypothetical future, which provides a feeling of unease at the idea of repeating the mistakes made.

[VdR '25] The directors are more interested in the human impact of the relocation of Old Hasankeyf on a Kurdish population that has already suffered continuous relocations, up to 350,000 people in Northern Kurdistan. Amidst the bulldozers that lift dirt roads and demolish the old stone houses to build a dam, the small Özkan hair salon remains as the last bastion of resistance, amidst a skeleton of empty shops. The images of Hasankeyf submerged under the waters offer a melancholic vision of the disappearance of historical heritage under a supposed progress that always affects the same people.

genres: animation, action, adventure, superhero, fantasy, thriller
cast_names: Denny Sumargo, Donny Damara, Aghniny Haque, Cok Simbara, Donny Alamsyah, Tanta Ginting, Revaldo
review_content: Good action and plot but the sometimes flat characters, poor animation and lackluster art style hold it back.

[Sheffield '25] The  structure proposed by filmmaker Amber Fares returns to Noam Shuster's stand-up comedy, alternating with the comedian's personal experience, perhaps to demonstrate that the panoramic perspective she seeks to offer of an ongoing conflict is only possible from a personal perspective, reflecting on the identity of its protagonist as a projection of the identity of an entire country. But it's not as interesting when Noam Shuster expresses solidarity with the occupied Palestinian territories, but rather when she confronts the ambiguous or racist opinions of her fellow Israelis.

cast_names: Adam Kinzinger, Steve Pink
review_content: [DOK.fest '25] The intention is to show Adam Kinzinger as a likable and relatable Republican, thanks to his sense of humor and the few incursions the director makes into his opinions on controversial issues. There is a slight reference to his pro-life stance on abortion and a friendly discussion about his admired Ronald Reagan. But it effectively shows the consequences of a politician responding to personal convictions and constitutional precepts over the narratives of his own party, compared to those who modify their positions out of fear or self-interest.

cast_names: Vino G. Bastian, Acha Septriasa, Donny Alamsyah, Della Dartyan, Whani Darmawan, Ridwan Roull Rohaz, Marsha Timothy, Randy Pangalila, Maudy Effrosina, Cecep Arif Rahman
review_content: Big fan of the first movie, but not the sequel. Expected more and the movie ended up being boring AF.     Cant believe they already teased a third movie, if it goes ahead. Really hope, its better than the sequel.

cast_names: Jonathan Lambert, Élodie Navarre, Elliot Jenicot, Rémy Adriaens
review_content: A fine mix of funny, disgusting and tense.     There are some hard and tense moments, it's a bit like 127 hours, but instead of his arm in a rock, his head is stuck inside the toilets. He struggles a lot, is hurt and ends up hurting himself even more as it goes.    Obviously, since it's in the toilets, some of the hard moments involve some not so appetizing stuff (cf title).    And yet it's funny. A lot. Both on the tense stuff ([spoiler]getting his ear cut and then having to fish it with his headphones[/spoiler]), the disgusting stuff ([spoiler]having to pull on a tampon string with his mouth underwater to unclog the toilets so he doesn't drown[/spoiler], but also other just genuinely funny scenes. The whole "he and the rat met in rehab" bit is priceless.    Can you keep it interesting for one hour in a single toilet stall with a guy that can't move his head ? Apparently you can.

cast_names: Jonathan Lambert, Élodie Navarre, Elliot Jenicot, Rémy Adriaens
review_content: [Fantasia '25] There's no denying that it's an entertaining and appropriately grotesque film, if the viewer doesn't mind scatological humor or black comedy about dead animals, although it has the virtue of being less savage than it could have been. But at the same time, it feels rather empty, a diversion that works on the surface but doesn't seem to have much depth. It's the story of a man pushed to the limit by his own decisions, but there's also a redemption through suffering, a sacrifice that requires going all the way to the bottom, literally, to find a way out. Being such a seemingly petty character from the start, he prevents the protagonist from falling into simple caricature in order to acquire glimmers of humanity.

cast_names: David Tiefen, Ronald Blanton, Dorothy Hadley Joly
review_content: So damn bad!!! So bad acting.

cast_names: David Tiefen, Ronald Blanton, Dorothy Hadley Joly
review_content: This really wasn't a bad movie. It was extremely interesting and very different from anything I've seen before however it wasn't great but definitely worth a watch.

cast_names: David Tiefen, Ronald Blanton, Dorothy Hadley Joly
review_content: the idea is interessting... but thats it. bad actors, Bad lines, Bad backgroundstorys.  even the rooms, the buildings look strange and not planed.  the police Departement is a living room, edwards home looks like he just moved in.  low budget is ok,  but this is low low low budget...

cast_names: David Tiefen, Ronald Blanton, Dorothy Hadley Joly
review_content: The premise to this movie is interesting but it quickly falls flat due to the atrocious acting that makes almost every scene feel like something it's not supposed to feel like. On top of that the dialogue is often ridiculous and comes off as unrealistic. People simply don't talk or act like that... And what the hell was that monologue about fairies not existing but then turning into monsters saying "hi".  If that wasn't enough they decided to put too much in there with overlapping stories and background stories I frankly didn't give a shit about.     There's too much lousy talking as well for a "horror movie" and I personally don't even want to call it this as there are no scares at all.     This is clearly an indie movie made by and with people who needed a lot more practice... And keep those boom mics out of view, this isn't the eighties anymore.    All in all: boring, badly made and stupid on many levels. Not worth the 72% it has on here as I'm writing this.

movie_title: Tamala 2030: A Punk Cat in Dark
review_content: This is a real experience. The pitch was just talking about looking into the cat disappearances, in a thriller, maybe noir kind of way. But this is not that at all.    This is so japanese, so what the fuck. There are successions of scenes and characters that are hard to make sense of in the beginning. A lot of dialogues are nonsensical too, specially with Tamala.    The way she speaks, always calmly and cutely, but sometimes with swear words in the middle, is a great idea. The whole world and puns on cat with everything is great, though I guess it depends on the translation you get.    Beyond the detective work (which is basically useless), there's this whole piercing the veil of the reality of the world thing, realizing the world is not what it seems and trying to find its meaning. And all that linked with conspiracies, an all powerful conglomerate, some mysterious monsters, etc. It goes into esoteric and metaphysical stuff pretty fast.    To me there is a really huge End of Evangelion vibe, but cute and with cats.    A lot of stuff makes little sense, but maybe there's a meaning behind it that could be understood on another viewing. I also did not see the first one, but I'm not sure it would have helped.    The drawing style changing drastically along the way, depending on the scenes. It can be black and white, in color, almost barren of decor, or with detailed paintings and grafitis. It can also be pretty abstract. It is really flowing with the mood, which works pretty well.    Maybe one defect: everything is repeated many times (by fear that the viewers would not understand other wise ?). So the existential questions, the clues, and the explanations are done many times. For instance, several character, separately, sometimes almost one after another, will understand something, and basically just repeat what the previous character explained 2 minutes before. And it is not just a little, there are some heavy repetitions in there.

[CPH:DOX '24] For seven years, the Spanish artist Aura Satz has been recording different types of sirens in such representative spaces as a school near the former Fukushima nuclear power plant or an assembly line for warning systems in the United States, composing a mosaic of ideas and reflections on past and present emergencies. The film is a fascinating journey that unfolds through different layers: the sound one from the recreation of new sounds by experimental artists, the visual one with images taken in long overhead shots and the textual one through reflections by different experts and activists. At the beginning of the film, the Lebanese trumpeter Mazen Kerbaj performs his own interpretation of a siren, while the actor and activist Khalid Abdalla, a Scot of Egyptian descent, creates a story about the siren as the emblematic sound of resistance, oppression and futures lost during the Arab Spring, "_a fork in the road that may show an alternative path to the future._"

[Sheffield '24] Through his approach to the history of the colonial occupation of Mapache territories, he addresses the need to shed the history that has been told from interested perspectives and confront the historical reality, establishing a dialogue between the present and the past. But the film also subtly introduces in this journey a look at the current situation of the Mapache people, facing in recent years conflicts that demand the recovery of lands and the right to self-determination.

cast_names: Caitlin Taylor, Vivian Vanderpuss
review_content: [Fantasia '25] Visually, it unfolds between format changes, animation elements, creative artistic design, and hallucinatory passages, so that at times it can feel too overloaded. But in this exploration of Mia's past and present, it raises a reflection on art and what is expected of it, creating an ambiguity between what is real and what seems the product of hallucinations. It's not exactly body horror, but it has some similarities, and it's not exactly a coming-of-age story, but there is some inner demon that needs to come out. In this pseudo-experimental journey, there is an outstanding musical work that effectively reflects the aesthetics of the nineties, achieving an almost surrealist exploration of the consequences of trauma, while examining the fragility of creation when it exposes internal fractures.

cast_names: Kim Min-ha, Kim Joo-ryoung, An Seo-hyun
review_content: [Reinvent] Through the eyes of a North Korean refugee, a story of displacement unfolds, in which fleeing political oppression becomes another kind of oppression—labor and social. It is a highly relevant depiction of the challenges faced by those who flee but cannot find a place where they feel integrated, the drama of a forced migration that fails to achieve the longed-for freedom. It has the capacity to offer such a powerful portrait that it transcends a local story, resonating beyond the relationship between the two Koreas to speak to the frustrations of emigration.

cast_names: Hadis Nazari, Pirouz Nemati
review_content: [Venice '25] The vertigo that comes with the decision to emigrate to another country is reflected in this story, told with a calm gaze that captures the city of Tehran like a poetic dedication. Although it doesn't aim to provoke reflections on emigration or propose political positions in a country like Iran where it's so easy to do so, it certainly paints a picture of Amir's surroundings through the group of friends he meets, something that is unusual in Iranian stories. It's a film that seeks to look more into the humanity of its characters, describing the uncertainty of a young man who feels the melancholy of his city even before he's left.

cast_names: Rodolphe Burger
review_content: [IFFR '26] The director uses two secondary characters from a previous film to create a kind of spin-off that unfolds as a cozy crime story, in which the object of the investigation is less important than the relationship between the two friends and their interactions with other townspeople. This archetypal character serves to describe them with their own distinct traits, slightly aged, almost as if they were from another era. The premise is to confront these two inseparable friends with other characters and observe how they behave and relate to one another, with references to Hervé's comics to depict an anti-narrative in which atmosphere and characters matter more than plot.

[Cannes '25] The images of a poetic documentary show the transformed everyday life of a Ukraine that nevertheless tries to return to a certain normality. The resistance to redefinition by Ukrainian citizens is outlined through those who refuse to leave their homes during evacuations, as a fruitless search to maintain a certain normality despite the constant presence of air raid sirens. The film, however, remains on an observational level that does not address political aspects, although it has a political texture as it moves from the opening long shots to the close-up portraits of the soldiers bidding farewell at the end. This human being, born of devastation, is capable of coexisting with destruction, demonstrating his capacity for resilience.
I can no longer find the words to describe this filthy genocide. Hind was one of many.

cast_names: Jon Rudnitsky, Karan Soni, Cazzie David, Jason Alexander, Taylor Hill, Oliver Cooper, Seager Tennis, Skip Howland, Elin Hampton, Alex Crawford
review_content: In a weird kind of way this film works as a spoof of a Tarantino movie.  After all, it is somewhat stylish, it has a lot of the same themes and locales.   There was clearly an effort to pay an homage.   The problem is that once you get past all of that, the move isn't compelling in any way.  The 80 minute run time felt like twice that by the end of the movie.  Side note:  Cazzie David needs to stop taking these kinds of roles.    follow me at https://IHATEBadMovies.com or facebook IHATEBadMovies

cast_names: Jon Rudnitsky, Karan Soni, Cazzie David, Jason Alexander, Taylor Hill, Oliver Cooper, Seager Tennis, Skip Howland, Elin Hampton, Alex Crawford
review_content: I am very disappointed with this film. The best part was Cazzie David painting her toes. The jaw on the fake Tarantino was very exaggerated.

movie_title: Music Box: Happy and You Know It
cast_names: Laurie Berkner, Anthony Field, Chris Ballew
review_content: A nice documentary into the lives of children's music creators. Not AI, but the real guys. And what PinkFong did was not so good. About all I wanna say.

cast_names: Lal, Sujith Sankar, Mansoor Ali Khan, Munishkanth, Padine Kumar, Arul D. Shankar
review_content: #Surrender - 3.5/5]  - No Herione, No Songs, A Quality cop thriller🔥 - Good First half Followed by a Decent second half👌 - Engaging screenplay throughout the film  - Tharshan, Lal & Sujith Shankar delivered their characters well🌟  Recommended for Cop Thriller fans💯

cast_names: Cirillo Luna, Henrique Barreira, Ivo Müller, Gabriela Greco
review_content: [Berlinale '25] More balanced when it reflects on exhibitionism and the power of representation, which in the characters ends up crossing the limits of the stage to infect their private lives. The search for exhibitionist sex practiced in public places also requires the presence of a third gaze, that of the spectator, which places this desire to be seen on a level parallel to that of acting in theatre or cinema. The camera thus becomes a voyeur but also a participant in the encounters between the two protagonists. And the contrast between the exhibition sheltered by night and the dissimulation that occurs during the day is a way of breaking down the margins between reality and artifice.

cast_names: Tamannaah Bhatia, Hebah Patel, Murali Sharma, Shafi
review_content: Avoid at all cost. They tried to cook hard it burned out

movie_title: Elon Musk Unveiled – The Tesla Experiment
cast_names: Elon Musk, Donald Trump
review_content: I hope this doc reaches as wide an audience as possible. As I watched this at a movie festival, I could tell from the audience’s reactions that many people remain unaware of just how dangerous Elon Musk is.    The documentary is thorough. While its primary focus is Tesla and the company’s autopilot technology,  it still manages to address nearly all the significant aspects of Musk’s trajectory, from Tesla’s early days through late 2025.  The only thing I would ask is for a more extensive segment on Musk and DOGE. It was brought up briefly; but it didn’t fully convey the extent of his corrupt and far overreaching involvement in the US government.    Andreas Pichler, who directed this film, explained after the screening that the main reason the documentary took so long to produce was because they wanted to ensure that the topics they wanted to cover had reached a concrete conclusion.    I would caution sensitive viewers for graphic and violent content, including real life footage of deaths. They do obscure any close-up shots of bodies etc, but it's definitely enough to warrant a warning, which was not present at any point of the film, and should have been included at the start.

movie_title: The Son of Revenge – The Story of Kalevala
cast_names: Elias Salonen, Eero Aho, Ilkka Koivula, Ronja Kuoppamäki, Olli Rahkonen, Krista Kosonen, Johannes Holopainen, Janne Hyytiäinen, Seela Sella, Jesse Gyllenbögel
review_content: [Berlinale '26] More than an epic saga, it's constructed as a personal story and an internal process of recognizing one's origins, and in this sense, perhaps the geographical possibilities of the Karelian forests aren't fully exploited visually. But a sense of internal epic works well as a vehicle for entertainment with a certain philosophical depth surrounding origins and a sense of belonging, somewhat in the manner of Shakespearean historical dramas, and for those unfamiliar with Kullervo's story, it's a film that never loses its balance.

[Sheffield '25] The optimism and friendliness of two Ukrainian paramedics contrasts with the chaotic and violent situation surrounding them, shown by the director without avoiding the crudeness of some images, especially when they care for the wounded. The drama of war, however, takes hold of the story as it develops, especially when the tone changes in the second act, after one of them suffers serious injuries that force them to separate. Despite their joviality, a touch of bitterness remains in the visits to relatives who now live in Spain, and in the feeling of irrecoverable lives in a country where the impact of the war is so great that it is impossible to go back, even if the conflict ends.

cast_names: Miyu Yamashita, Nattie Neidhart-Wilson, Seth Lesser, Timothy Moura, Jonathan Gresham, Luke James Uggles Eatwell, Gabriel McMenamin, Peter England, Shayna Baszler, Josh Barnett
review_content: As a newcomer to this event, I really dig this. Second event in a row I have been blown away by. Several great matches on the night, but I will give the nod to one of the final two as my two favorites.     Best Match: Sabre vs Gresham    Rating: 3.5/5 - 80% - Would Recommend

cast_names: Kerri Lynn Miller, Alex Hernandez, Margaret Colin, Ian Lyons, Kelly Deadmon, Kate Miller, Ryan Cooper, Estella Gabriel
review_content: Very good acting . Also directing and everything. Very balanced movie.

cast_names: Lilly Singh, Jessica Clement, Christian Martyn, Samantha Helt, Sonia Dhillon Tully, Jeff Geddis, Jaeden Noel, James Goldman, Amanda Barker, Stephanie Beatriz
review_content: This is so bad on so many levels. This movie has been done before and better plenty of times.

cast_names: Ajai Rao, Archana Jois, Supritha Sathyanarayana
review_content: Even though the climax felt abit too dramatic, this was a decent courtroom drama. Since this is not a sequel, stop looking for chapter 1 ☺

movie_title: Leibniz – Chronicle of a Lost Painting
cast_names: Edgar Selge, Aenne Schwarz, Lars Eidinger, Michael Kranz, Antonia Bill, Barbara Sukowa
review_content: [Berlinale '25] The impossibility of carrying out a more ambitious project around the biography of the philosopher Gottfried Leibniz has ended up transforming an epic narrative into a chamber piece in the style of Manoel de Oliveira's cinema. The film thus reveals itself as a minimalist piece with simple staging, which attempts to reflect on the idea of ​​the image and its meaning, establishing parallels between painting and cinema, and transferring some of the German author's reasoning to create an entertaining dialogue between philosophical thought and artistic thought.

cast_names: Sheila McCarthy, Natasha Mumba, Joanne Boland, Arnold Pinnock, Shailyn Pierre-Dixon, Noah Lamanna, Steven McCarthy, Idrissa Sanogo, Cameron Nicoll
review_content: [Fantasia '25] A post-apocalyptic story that focuses more on the relationships between people than on describing the consequences of a virus affecting the water supply. Throughout the film, an interesting question is raised about the need to rebalance societies when extreme situations arise, faced with the maintenance of certain privileges. Forgetting the past to restart the present, referring to the river of oblivion that the souls of the dead drank from to forget their earthly lives. But the idea is not fully developed enough to drive a narrative that ends up drowning in dialogue and performances that are too languid.

movie_title: Mouth of the Wolf: Amanda Knox Returns to Italy
cast_names: Amanda Knox
review_content: Have just finished #InTheMouthOfTheWolf, the latest @amandaknox.com doc & I highly recommend it. Though, crazy as this might sound, I had my heart in my throat all the way. The jeopardy as she pushed through to her remarkable redemption of Avvocato Mignini felt excruciating. Remarkable. Top lady 💐

cast_names: Zhou Xun, Yang Haoyu
review_content: A animated chinese movie with a monkey as main protagonist and it is NOT based on Journey to the West for once, color me surprised! Certainly an interesting oral folktale and the artstyle is also something you dont see too often these days, let alone from china. The director/writer Li Wenyu apparently also animated the whole movie himself?! At least thats what he said after a Berlinale screening and that is just insane...

cast_names: Cynthia Rothrock, Richard Norton, Don Wilson, Patrick Kilpatrick, Keith Cooke, Forbes Riley, Brandy Futch, Tracy Melchior, Jody Nolan, R. Marcos Taylor
review_content: I wanted to see Cynthia Rothrock kick ass and get revenge. It delivered on what I wanted. Bonus points for casting both the legendary Don 'the Dragon' Wilson and Richard Norton. Restitution.

cast_names: Edoardo Pesce, Valentina Lodovini, Lino Musella, Thony, Donatella Finocchiaro, Paola Tiziana Cruciani, Michela Andreozzi
review_content: I went to see a movie about how to balance a child's free expression and the toxic society around them.  The movie is actually about how the dad cures himself from toxic masculinity.  There is little to none consideration on how to face the fucked up society we're in.    Ignoring the plot misdirection, I think it does a very good job between drama and comedy to convey its message.   I liked this movie a lot.

cast_names: Amy Keum, Kevin Woo, Vana Kim, Joseph Lim Kim, Eliza Shin, Jas Wang, Jason Her, Jess Matney, Desiree Mee Jung, John J. Shim
review_content: This isn’t completely awful, considering it’s a Tubi original that’s actually high praise.

cast_names: Sherman Augustus, Karole Foreman, Ramsay Midwood, Keylor Leigh, Arianna Ngnomire, Diana Stevan
review_content: Very boring movie. Don’t recommend watching it.

[Cannes '25] The success of the proposal lies in not using the usual resources for humanizing dandelions, which are nevertheless given personalities through micro-movements or mimicry reminiscent of silent films. But the journey feels too long and repetitive, with traditional devices such as the danger posed to seeds by entering fast-moving waters. More interesting is the visual treatment of the environments filmed in real images and how they fit into the superimposition of layers, changing perspectives to offer a different representation: a tree fungus filmed from a low angle becomes an architectural monument, the mold filmed in a time-lapse manner resembles a field of flowers.

cast_names: Blanche Gardin, Philippe Katerine, Bastien Bouillon, Ole Eliassen, Laurent Papot, Clémentine Baert
review_content: [Berlinale '25] Sébastien Betbeder's latest film constantly straddles the line between genres and different worlds, highlighting contrasts. It is a comedy-drama that feels comfortable in the middle, starring a nonconformist character who has made his way outside his family, but who needs to return for a final farewell. In the Jura Mountains, creating a first part that reflects maladjustment, the director uses formal resources that are close to the humanist westerns of the early seventies, while adopting a more documentary tone in the part in Greenland, the search for absolute serenity that has been yearned for throughout a lifetime.

cast_names: Jelena Đokić, Sergej Trifunović, Milutin 'Mima' Karadžić, Milica Stefanović, Branislav Jevtić, Miloš Lolić, Miloš Timotijević, Petar Strugar, Jovo Maksić, Dušanka Stojanović Glid
review_content: Intriguing concept. Buddha blackmails a pregnant woman into killing people. As it goes on it keeps adding layers upon layers of weird and original ideas.    The first part, when we don't know what's going on, more like a thriller, is really exciting.    This Buddha that seems to hate humans, having no issue with hurting kids, cops, or collateral damage. This random woman taken hostage, will she do it or not ? The display of his powers, leaving no doubt that, even if he is not who he says he is, he is definitely supernatural. And her, reluctant, talking to people and realizing that maybe, they all do deserve to die.  A bit of a red flag on the bathroom scene with the woman wanting to get rich by accusing the writer. This really is a victim blaming false flag, no woman has ever gotten rich or famous by accusing a powerful man, even when it's real.    The whole scene with the father is even better. Very Tarantino like.      Then comes a bit more explanation on what the final objective is, and the whole absurdity of such a scenario is grandiose. Buddha sent [spoiler]his son - well, his second son since apparently the first one was Kurt Cobain - but he was put into an asylum and used in a reality TV show[/spoiler]. That's just the kind of what the fuck idea I like.      The ending part is a complete mess. A bit of forced gore, and complete mayhem. I think it was a bit too long. I'm usually not a fan of long scenes where everyone is running around and fighting all over the place. WHen you've seen 2 minutes of it, you've seen it all. If it lasts 10+, what's the point ?  The gore autopsy part was fun though.    As for the ending, I don't know when I realized that he was probably [spoiler]her baby[/spoiler], but it didn't really come as a twist.    There are a few plot holes, but it's not too much of a bother. How did she get in the club with a gun though.

movie_title: Thus Spoke Kishibe Rohan: At a Confessional
cast_names: Issey Takahashi, Marie Iitoyo, Arata Iura, Tina Tamashiro, Shigeyuki Totsugi, Shunsuke Daitoh, Alessandro Bressanello
review_content: Interesting story and another good addition to the Rohan series. But it has the same issues as the other Rohan movie. Needlessly long and needlessly taking place outside of Japan. Venice is mostly just a backdrop with no effect on the story. It reminded me of when American movies take place in Tokyo, and all actors are American. Makes the whole location feel fake.

movie_title: Vitrival – The Most Beautiful Village in the World
review_content: [IFFR '25] The everyday life of a small, quiet town, which seems to have been taken from a Claire Simon documentary, contrasts with the less everyday situations that recall the films of Bruno Dumont, so that this opposition establishes a humour that remains throughout a film that doesn't seek comedy directly, but is permanently imbued with it. The directors use long shots in front of the characters, which facilitates the interaction of amateur actors, but also transmits a certain sense of authenticity. The film acquires a broader perspective than the simple portrait of a local community.

movie_title: What Does That Nature Say to You
cast_names: Ha Seong-guk, Kwon Hae-hyo
review_content: The funniest Hong Sangsoo film in years. It's still HSS though - it's either love or hate.

Speed Faster (2026) is a slog masquerading as an action thriller. The promising one-line premise — Ace and his crew pulled into a deadly high-stakes heist — never develops into anything interesting; characters remain cardboard, the stakes feel manufactured, and the film’s 1h27 runtime somehow drags. Jason Park’s direction fails to drum up tension or style, the action looks cheap and choppy, and leads Christopher Deon, Luke Dingess and Maria Mae Lopez are given nothing to elevate beyond rote line-reading. By the time the tagline “THE CHAOS WAS NOT OVER” appears, you’ll likely be praying it is. Unimaginative, forgettable and frustratingly shallow — 1/10.

[Sheffield '25] With a narrative close to fiction, avoiding interviews and general analysis, the film addresses the story of a young woman who faces her mother's alcoholism and an important decision for her stepsister. Although it doesn't deal with it directly, the background reflects the problem of alcoholism in Belarus, the country with the highest consumption in the world. It can be very painful at times, in the serious reflections Katya makes to her friend Tanya, but it is a very moving story that reflects, through personal observation, the impact of a national problem.

cast_names: Chiara Caselli, Christian Bégin, Nikolay Mutafchiev, Svetlana Yancheva, Sylvie Lemay, Alexia Roc, Raphaël Fournier
review_content: [Berlinale '26] Centered on the protagonist's inner quest, which is reflected in an external search for the truth surrounding the talent of a precocious artist, the film is expressed through the silences and journeys Mihail undertakes through a Bulgaria that now seems unfamiliar to him. The film effectively conveys this feeling of estrangement, of being a stranger in one's own land, although the persistent melancholic tone of the second half can make it somewhat monotonous. It strikes a delicate balance between a reflective story about origins and a desire to show the connection between art and its environment.

[SSIFF '25] Through a traditional homecoming story, the director brings a female perspective to a family made up of three generations of women living alone in a Himalayan community. But there are no easy speeches, but rather a rich and complex description of characters, revealing the weaknesses of each of them. One of the greatest successes is finding a way to reflect contradictory perspectives, such as the making of momos, a connection with the warmth of family tradition, but also a representation of the role of women in society.

cast_names: Anna Geislerová, Juliána Brutovská, David Vodstrčil, Jana Plodková, Stefano Cenci
review_content: [Cannes '25] Few films offer a normalized view of the emotional needs of neurodivergent individuals, and this one does so with subtlety and the complexity of diverse relationships that can sometimes be confusing. However, the film lacks a greater drive to allow its characters to transcend the screen beyond a sketchy representation lacking in emotional depth. In its visual approach, it attempts to offer a nuanced depiction of personal relationships and relies on the camera's closeness to bodies, but ultimately remains a discreet representation that reflects on how the bond between mother and child remains intact.

movie_title: An Evening with Elton John and Brandi Carlile
cast_names: Elton John, Brandi Carlile, Dan Levy, Andrew Watt, Chad Smith, Dua Lipa, Ed Sheeran, Chappell Roan, Sam Smith, Jason Momoa
review_content: Oh wow! The energy, the love, THE...MUSIC!!! Should NOT be missed!

[Annecy '25] One of the most interesting elements is the way the short film depicts the rebellion and frustrated anger experienced by a child, powerless in the face of the circumstances he experiences at home. Benci begins to destroy his surroundings as a way of channeling these frustrations, but he also takes them out on a dog that only returns loyalty and affection, which ultimately becomes a form of healing for the child. With simple animation and perfectly designed planning, the short film has the power to represent a situation that describes the reality of many homes.

[VdR '25] Through the road trip taken by a Vietnam War veteran belonging to the Yurok nation, the directors trace the consequences of PTSD, but also the traumas of a sense of belonging through an identity that faces the uncertainties of the future. They skillfully manage the balance between a docudrama and a spiritual documentary essay that confronts the anxieties of its protagonist, but by also incorporating characters he meets along the way, it offers a portrait of a country that still has many unfinished business with its past.

cast_names: Ryan Hollyman, Anna Douglas
review_content: Intro: A man has a bike accident and scratches his whole face on the road. A great intro, hilarious to watch.    His young daughter draws it with her new crayons. When her mother gets home the girl says the crayons come from Mr Meat Crayon, a monster that is here with her. The colors actually come from the [spoiler]blood from people[/spoiler].      Well we don't get much info on other colors except red yellow and orange, too bad.  Cheaply done I guess but the monster looks cool. Creepy child is creepy.    The crayon making squishy meaty horror movie sounds when used is a nice touch.
This was so sinister! I love it! I’d watch a full feature of this!

cast_names: Ntobeko Sishi, Siyabonga Melongisi Shibe
review_content: [TIFF '25] A solid drama that follows a traditional narrative path, but builds an intelligent look at the fissures caused by racism, reflecting survival through music as a tool of resistance, because it has not been able to be taken away by the contagion of so-called Western civilization. With a credible staging set in 1968 in the midst of apartheid, it tells a story of personal growth with all its consequences, offering an interesting look at how the shadow of corruption and betrayal caused by totalitarian systems like apartheid ends up contaminating even its victims.

[VdR '25] A look at the war in Ukraine from a different perspective that reveals itself as a moving portrait of resilience and dedication to abandoned animals. Anna Kurkurina's story conveys a profound love from a permanent solitude, shown with an invisible camera that nonetheless feels close. It also focuses on the eyes of the animals, those that reveal the depression and fear caused by war, the physical wounds that may heal, and the psychological ones that may never heal. "A war is not just the destruction of the environment or death. War destroys from within."

movie_title: De Grote Sinterklaasfilm en de verdwenen verlanglijstjes
cast_names: Robert ten Brink, Chris Tates
review_content: Leuke film, iets trager dan de voorgaande films. Maar weer leuk voor dit jaar.

[Sheffield '25] More than a music documentary, it ends up being a quite moving portrait of a musician imprisoned by the success of a song (the worst song in history) and so dedicated to his performances that he even receives comments from his partner about her frustration at the fact that he is less committed to her and their son than he is to music. Kim Hunter's film is not interested in remembering the trajectory of Black Lace, but in showing the last survivor doing just that, surviving, performing in clubs and pubs in Benidorm and looking after his beloved mother.

cast_names: Ina Marija Bartaitė, Šarūnas Bartas
review_content: [Venice '25] It's always surprising when a film director decides to express their most personal emotions, such as grief and loss, through a film. But it's the convergence that this painful and deeply personal experience finds, its true meaning. There is so much life in a film that addresses loss and death, expressed in a place that shows nature returning to the cycle of existence. The expression of grief and loss is reflected in the life that flows around us, giving us a sense of hope that it is possible to move forward even when the pain hits with the force of a hurricane.

cast_names: Asia Ortega, Emma Suárez, José Luis García Pérez, Manuel Vega
review_content: [Veoasecan] It constructs a mirror reflecting two seemingly different couples: a younger one in a constant state of crisis, on the verge of breaking up, and a more mature one in a stable comfort that reveals its vulnerabilities. It works with a certain sense of drama thanks to a well-crafted script and complex characters who gradually unveil their fears and personal circumstances in dialogue that becomes increasingly raw, yet also increasingly sincere. While it may have some pacing issues, it unfolds with a very effective naturalness.

[VdR '25] Christian Frei's latest documentary takes a subjective view to portray a story of friendship between three scientists who end up embroiled in fake news and false narratives constructed around the origin of the coronavirus. Between conspiracy books by John F. Kennedy, Jr. and the defunding of Donald Trump, it insists that the laboratory virus theory is baseless. But above all, it raises the dangers of political interference in science through self-serving narratives and leaves a worrying message. If we thought that, after facing a global pandemic, we are better prepared for when another arrives, the reality is the opposite.

cast_names: Estéban, Pascal Tagnati, Céline Fuhrer, Eva Rami, Sébastien Chassagne, Alma Jodorowsky, Fred Tousch, Pascal Légitimus, Arielle Dombasle, Philippe Duquesne
review_content: Norbert is a 350 year old vampire, who has never bitten anyone yet. His parents aren't happy and send him to Japan to find a pure blooded, good family woman to bite and marry. But his ship crashes on a small island. Only he and the family servant survive.    This is an absurd comedy. There is a bit of gore too, specially in the end. In a funny way, but still gore (removed entrails, cut fingers, pierced eyes, and lots of blood spraying).    Lots of jokes about stuff that people would qualify as woke this days, often by pushing it to absurd extremes. The funny gore is great, if you like this kind of thing. And lots more to enjoy if you're into absurd humor.    Can't say much about actor performances, their dialogues often sound more like theater than cinema. Except Pascal Legitimus that offers a grandiose performance, culminating in his torture scene.    The professor's monologues are also a highlight of the movie, as well as the cult leaders.    In the end, still wondering if the girl is pure blooded enough (and so whether [spoiler]Norbert's family dies as it is said in the intro[/spoiler]).  I also might have missed something. At some point they're bringing back Marie to put her in his coffin, that is the goal, it is said. But then they seem to just put her there, and remove him to bring him back to the clinic. I don't think there's any explanation on that.

cast_names: Polly Maberly, Mikael Persbrandt, Rebecca Calder, Guy Burnet, Charley Palmer Rothwell, Sarah Beck Mather, Leon Annor, Sallieu Sesay, Adam Lawrence, Kellie Shirley
review_content: There is an explosive fun scene at the end. That's it.    All the rest is the life of a real estate agent that borrowed too much money. And it's boring as fuck. You keep waiting to see where it's going and when it's gonna start. It doesn't.    Even the main event, which is so long to come, when she's out of nowhere forcibly involved in a kidnapping, doesn't manage to make things interesting.    Weird: [spoiler]Why did she call the wife ? And free the guy at the end. Does she really believe the Viking will not kill him ?[/spoiler]  Weirder. The post resolution scene where she [spoiler]goes to kill a woman, probably a friend, she owes money to[/spoiler]. Is this real ? If yes, what the fuck.  If it's a dream it might match with the next scene where we can see her unbelievable success.  Anyway, both feel out of place.

cast_names: Polly Maberly, Mikael Persbrandt, Rebecca Calder, Guy Burnet, Charley Palmer Rothwell, Sarah Beck Mather, Leon Annor, Sallieu Sesay, Adam Lawrence, Kellie Shirley
review_content: Neon crime caper about a coked-up London estate agent in over her head. Strange, try-hard, and kind of boring. If you’re doing pitch-black comedy, you’ve got to land the bite.     Natasha, an estate agent with debts, drugs and delusions of control, gets leaned on by loan sharks to stash a kidnapped rival while she hunts an old underworld ally called The Viking. It plays as a week from hell through London's seedier corners. The cast is stacked, and you can see the intent, but the tone wobbles between grim and glib, and the "isn’t she awful" gag wears thin. The dark-comedy bite never really arrives, so the chaos just feels loud. FrightFest billed it as a neon-soaked gangster spiral with Gerard Johnson at the wheel; you feel the swagger, not the soul.    If you liked… Bronson, You Were Never Really Here, Villain, In Bruges

cast_names: Walter Hahn, Bill Goldberg, Colby Lopez, Shaun Ricker, Randy Orton, Andrew Galloway, Jonathan Solofa Fatu, Jeffrey Cobb, Tevita Fifita, Jelly Roll
review_content: Thankfully, WWE’s product alone keeps the entertainment value afloat, because this card wasn’t particularly good. Nothing really happened—and that’s fine, because it didn’t feel like the show was trying to do much in the first place. It’s watchable, but forgettable.  Best Match: Orton vs McIntyre  Rating: 2/5 – 65% – Not Recommended to Everyone

cast_names: Walter Hahn, Bill Goldberg, Colby Lopez, Shaun Ricker, Randy Orton, Andrew Galloway, Jonathan Solofa Fatu, Jeffrey Cobb, Tevita Fifita, Jelly Roll
review_content: I would rather see Bastion Booger than Jelly Roll

cast_names: Jack McCafferty, Lauren LaVera, Liz Priestley
review_content: As I am doom scrolling through movies, looking for a romantic comedy to help me forget for a little while that I hate being alive, I find...  What's this, Lauren LaVera? Sienna Shaw herself - the best final girl ever - in an indie romcom?  Yeah, turns out she took some time off from kicking demon-clown ass to make this.  Okay, so this is a romcom, it's very by the numbers; that is both good and bad.  We follow Miles, and we get the whole meet cute between he and Ren - that's Lauren.  The relationship develops, there's an obstacle late in the second act, followed by the third act redemption. Unless this is your first movie, you have seen this all before. Anyway, crazy predictable, but still very sweet, and I am a sucker for a love story, especially when it's with LaVera, because who wouldn't fall in love with her?  Beyond that, what keeps the movie afloat are the animated dream sequences that take up half the runtime, and Carter. Who is Carter? He's Miles'... friend? Roommate? Something. Look, he's basically Silent Bob, only without the silence and just the moments of wisdom.  The movie is sweet, fun, ambitious for what the budget must have been, painfully predictable, but I'm a total romantic, so I can't really hate this.  If you're a fan of LaVera, I recommend this. She's in it the entire time, and she's absolutely adorable, and it's so nice to see her shine outside of horror.

[VdR '25] Once again using the night as a political resource, in which the protagonists take over the city to escape from invisibility, the last part of the trilogy "Nuit obscure" is set in Paris, around the monuments that attract tourists such as the Eiffel Tower or the Palais de Chaillot where the Universal Declaration of Human Rights was signed in 1948, to reflect the other side of society. The young people who in the previous films dreamed of reaching Europe from Melilla, now travel around Europe looking for the country that will not mistreat them or expel them.

[DOK.fest '25] Maintaining an observant perspective, Weronika Mliczewsa's debut film is a melancholic look at the reality of a reunion far from a happy ending. It proves to be a moving approach to the sense of belonging, the past of abuse and discrimination for being Vietnamese children of American soldiers, and the hope of recovering one's identity. A moving story supported by a subtle and delicate soundtrack by Joaquín García, an Argentine composer based in Sweden.

cast_names: Rainey Qualley, Shane West, Shiloh Fernandez, Isabella Brenza, R.A. Mihailoff
review_content: It's watchable in terms of horror and gore. The script is slow; I got bored in some scenes. I watched it by fast-forwarding. The acting is passable. I wanted to give it a chance. I think it's neither very good nor very bad. I'd rate it a 5 out of 10. Otherwise, if you want to watch it, I'd say it should be a last resort.

cast_names: Rainey Qualley, Shane West, Shiloh Fernandez, Isabella Brenza, R.A. Mihailoff
review_content: Was at the world premiere of this. The director introduced the film by explaining that this is based on the folklore of a small town in America. For me, this added to my anticipation as the concept is downright freaky and a great premise for a horror film. Sadly, as the story unfolds, the more it strays from the myth and it turns into a much more traditional modern horror. It's not a bad film at all and there is plenty for the horror fan to enjoy but I just feel they could have done so much more with the story. The most interesting part remains as a distant backdrop by the end.

cast_names: Sarah-Jane Potts, David Ganly, Helen Grady, Sharon Duce, Simon Shorten
review_content: Could have been so much better, too many  unanswered questions.

cast_names: Elaiza Ikeda, Kei Adachi, Sayu Kubota, Yuki Kura, Ai Hashimoto, Hikari Ishida, Toshinori Omi, Oshirō Maeda, Kasumi Yamaya, Reika Oozeki
review_content: [Fantasia '25] This tribute to Nobuhiko Ôbayashi, filmed in his hometown of Onomichi, also the setting for films by Yasujirō Ozu and Kaneto Shindô, is a curious mix of romantic drama and science fiction, which ends up working better in the first concept than in the second, creating a nostalgic tone that asks questions about the events of our lives: "If we could change the past, what would happen?" But it ends up being a melancholic film that is more interested in how fantastic events affect personal relationships than in the construction of these events themselves.

cast_names: Alexandra Swarens, Lynne Burnett
review_content: I went into the movie with no knowledge of the creator/star/subject matter, and I came out of the movie really impressed.   I love how the movie followed a rather unconventional love story.  It was obvious that there was a bit of a connection, but it was just such a joy to watch the two leads interact and grow together as the movie went on.  I really enjoyed it.      follow me at https://IHATEBadMovies.com or facebook IHATEBadMovies

[TIFF '25] To show a harrowing reality within Iraqi society, the director establishes a personal connection that leaves room for intimacy, but also uses shadows and blurred reflections in glass to metaphorically portray the figures of nameless women, erased from society. While she doesn't offer figures or broader perspectives, preferring instead to focus on personal testimonies, she brings a more reflective approach to this intimate tone, seeking to understand the psychological effects of feeling rejected as an integral part of Iraqi society.

cast_names: Nam Jin-bok, Nam Yeon-woo
