review_content: [Fantasia '25] A more cryptic proposal than usual from the director, who delves into suspense to reflect on conspiracy theories and the contagion of fake news. The film adopts a disturbing perspective that uses a slow pace and shots that hover halfway between hallucination and reality to convey a sense of suspense surrounding the protagonist, always placing her in an ambiguous perspective. There are no closed plots or total certainties in this interesting proposal, but that is the most attractive aspect of a story that speaks precisely about assumptions and conjectures.

[Sheffield '25] Not really a documentary about the coronavirus, it uses it to depict the way Chinese sweatshops cope with a crisis, and how the autonomy built around Lenovo's Wuhan factory isn't fully effective within a global economy. Zhou Hao's disturbing documentary conveys the repetitive nature of assembly lines, with the camera sometimes picking up distant conversations as if a spy. Its footage reflects how both workers and managers are part of a machine that seeks constant growth at the expense of mental health, substituting emotional stability for economic stability.

[VdR '25] Through three Cambodians deported from the United States after being sentenced to prison, it is not so much a political denunciation as a reflection of lives that have to start over and the difficulty of adapting to a society they don't really know, because their parents fled with them when they were just children. The background of the receptions in the United States comes from the bombing of Cambodia during the Vietnam War, describing how those who have been returned find themselves displaced, raised in a different culture that isolates them.

[VdR '25] The film gradually reveals that the selection of several territories in the 1980s for nuclear waste disposal was tinged with racist and colonialist elements, primarily targeting communities already suffering the consequences of contamination or used as a political tool to seize lands belonging to First Nations. The consequence of the choice of these six locations has become a kind of permanent stigma for their inhabitants, which has not only marked their own lives but has also led to a profound economic decline in their communities.

cast_names: Milutin Dapčević, Jessica Piccolo Valerani, Cristina Donadio, Matteo Martari
review_content: [Locarno '25] Adopting the girls' point of view, in the most literal sense of the word, with the camera always placed in their perspective, and an aspect ratio that reflects the limitations of their world, it has a narrative structure that holds the mysteries for the viewer to discover at the same time as the protagonists. The directors overload the images with references, sounds and music that evoke the nineties, building a friendship that has a certain fantastical tone. But this overloaded style becomes a narrative loop in which the description of this friendship is not enough to maintain the dramatic tension, weakening the structure.

cast_names: Andrea Bræin Hovig, Pål Sverre Hagen, Vivild Falk Berg, Torill Kove, Kåre Conradi
review_content: Torill Kove, known for her sensitivity in exploring the complexities of family relationships and the nuances of memory, presents us with “Maybe Elephants,” a short film that revisits themes seen in her previous work while offering a fresh perspective on human nature. Set in the 1970s, the film follows a Norwegian family on a trip to Kenya, seeking new experiences, only to encounter the personal and collective challenges that arise during the journey.    The film’s plot is a sensitive and ironic dive into family dynamics. Much like in “Me and My Moulton” (2014), Kove introduces us to a family that, although seemingly ordinary, carries peculiar elements in its daily life that make it unique. This time, the focus is on the mother, a strong, creative, and contradictory character. Her personality deviates from the expected “perfect mother” mold, especially when her struggle with depression begins to affect family relationships. This portrayal is not melodramatic but rather infused with subtle irony, characteristic of Kove’s style, allowing the audience to reflect on what it means to be “normal” within the confines of a dysfunctional family.    The narrative delicately explores the growth process and transformation of each character, especially the sisters. As they enter adolescence, they begin to notice the differences between themselves and the adults around them. Kove not only questions the concepts of social conformity but also illustrates the tension between the need for belonging and the desire for individuality. The interactions between the sisters, especially as they experience this journey in Kenya, serve as a reflection of the uncertainties typical of this stage in life, while the parental figures reveal their own flaws and vulnerabilities.    One of the most impactful moments in the film occurs when the mother, in an impulsive act of courage, drives a herd of elephants away from their campsite. This scene is loaded with symbolism and is remembered in very different ways by the sisters as time passes. This simple but dramatic event becomes a landmark in the characters’ memories and illustrates the complexity of emotional memory. The way time distorts memories, transforming them into something almost mythical, is one of the film’s greatest contributions: a mother facing imminent danger with an unshaken posture, while in reality, her own fragility remains hidden.    Kove, unlike many filmmakers who approach family dysfunction with a heavy and accusatory tone, adopts a more introspective and ironic stance. In her works, irony doesn’t minimize the seriousness of the situations but offers a broader perspective on human conflicts. This touch of melancholic humor adds depth to the characters and sets the film apart from other stories about complicated families. The relationship between the family members is, at once, marked by emotional disconnection but also by a deep and genuine love, even if misaligned.    Visually, “Maybe Elephants” is a treat. The film’s aesthetic, blending simplicity and sophistication, perfectly complements the narrative. The colors, scene composition, and the way Kove uses space to tell the story visually and intimately are clear examples of her mastery as a filmmaker. The elephant scene, in particular, is emblematic not just for the action but for how nature, with its contrasts and vastness, reflects the characters’ conflicting emotions.    Each family member’s voice is skillfully explored throughout the film, and the voice acting is moving. The emotions of the characters overflow through the subtlety of the dialogues and the careful interpretation of feelings, often unsaid but visible in gestures and expressions. The way the animator manages to convey tension and tenderness with so little is a perfect example of how animation can be deeper than it first appears.    At its core, “Maybe Elephants” is about trying to understand the past, but also about how it transforms into something we can carry with us or even reinvent over time. The film’s handling of memory, trauma, and the evolution of family relationships is both delicate and radical. The mother, with her strength and vulnerability, serves as a mirror for the viewer’s own uncertainties, while the sisters, with their different perspectives on the same story, show us how time can alter even the most significant memories.    Overall, “Maybe Elephants” is a work of rare sensitivity, exploring the complexities of memory, family, and the human experience with impressive softness and depth. Kove once again proves herself to be a unique filmmaker, capable of addressing universal themes with a lightness that doesn’t diminish the power of the issues she raises. The combination of irony, delicacy, and introspection makes this film a masterpiece of animation, speaking to the heart of anyone who has ever gotten lost and found themselves again in their own memories.

cast_names: Sohum Shah, Nushrratt Bharuccha, Nora Fatehi, Sharib Hashmi, Vipin Katyal
review_content: A mute movie, not so often seen so credits for that.  A dumb, mad ride full of chaos and drama. Comedy is a mixed bag. Can a simple, one time fun watch.

[Visions du Réel '24] The B/W cinematography and the absence of external elements such as a soundtrack, reinforce the condition of the film's internal journey, a confessional approach towards the protagonist that functions as a reflection of her past from her own narration, while showing the structured monastic life, giving it a certain timeless air, like a time stopped between the snowy paths outside and the chiaroscuros of the interior of the convent. The film contrasts a spiritual perspective with the reflection of the most earthly reality, the world of crime and poverty with the search for redemption, which is expressed in the repetitive nature of the images and the slow pace of their development. But somehow it manages to convey that inner journey of Vera who, however, doesn't seem to have a very deep religious conviction.

cast_names: Anabel Lopez, Gessica Généus, Bruno Mourral, Patrick Jeudy, Lionel Messi
review_content: At its heart it is a very classic case of "criminal activity goes wrong and keeps getting wronger" comedy type mixed with the double-triple-quadruple crossing trope where everyone lies and betrays everyone else. What makes it a bit more original is the Haitian vibe, and the underlying very serious issues in real life. (Apparently 3 of the crew were kidnapped on the set, several others had family kidnapped and some were also killed after the shooting)     It is mostly funny, though can't say there were any original gag that haven't already been done in the genre. Not being a soccer fan the constant references to spanish teams rivalry was extremely annoying. But the comedy part was good. The multi crossing part a bit less, since it was mostly corruption without much intelligence and it mostly tied into the drama part.    One of the most annoying character ever with Laura. How did they not just kill her 10 times over ? When the story switched from the kidnapping to just dealing with Laura and Patrick, it seemed the movie had a little lost its way and it kept going until the end. Since it's an extension of a shorter movie, maybe that's the added part they packed in to add some length ?    The drama part where it mixes in with the political campaign and elections is kinda good too. So maybe switching to more drama would have been a better choice than keeping them in that car.

cast_names: Anabel Lopez, Gessica Généus, Bruno Mourral, Patrick Jeudy, Lionel Messi
review_content: [Fantasia '24] Based on the wave of kidnappings that Haiti has experienced in recent years, shrouded in unpunished violence, the film builds an action story with elements of black humor that does not lack social criticism about the corruption of the police and politicians. At times very absurd, it is above all too chaotic, surrounding the main plot with characters that contribute to making it more anarchic. But the two protagonists allow it to function as a nice buddy movie that is sometimes so crazy and exaggerated that it ends up being funny, although the real background of violence can freeze the smile.

cast_names: Karelle Tremblay, Barbara Ulrich, Félix Dufour-Laperrière
review_content: Like holding mass in a coffee shop: preaching in poetry is still preaching and makes the message all that more dubious.     A woman is part of a violent protest at a rich old folks home but then things get weird... and maybe capitalism apologist?     The animation is so beautiful I'm giving this a 'like' even though the story and message of the film rate only a 6/10 for me.

cast_names: Karelle Tremblay, Barbara Ulrich, Félix Dufour-Laperrière
review_content: [Cannes '25] A story about radicalism, with its contradictions, represented in a film with political components regarding class imbalances. The ideological and philosophical proposals can be somewhat simplistic, or at least they don't seem too profound or very different from those of any progressive discourse. But although it sometimes seems a bit vague and superficial in its approach, it is a visually attractive proposal that takes risks by incorporating political and social themes against an ethereal backdrop of fatalism.

cast_names: Shana Mathai, Staffan Göthe, Jan Gunnar Røise, Ingvild Holthe Bygdnes
review_content: Go see this one in teathers. I cried at least 3 times. It really hit me. It is slow but in a good way, don't rush it, enjoy the story.

cast_names: Mickey O'Sullivan, Sophia Echendu, Jack Ball, Dan Waller, Caroline Hendricks
review_content: Short indie thriller that should appeal to fans of indie thrillers. Got a bit of a bad rap over on IMDB due to some astroturfed reviews, but it’s worth checking out if you’re a fan of the genre.

cast_names: Samir Guesmi, Solène Rigot, Olivier Rabourdin, Pierre Lottin, Patrick Bouchitey, Harpo Guit, Gérard Boucaron, Jonas Dinal, Xavier Mathieu, Miss Ming
review_content: Really original, intriguing, and entertaining. It's a real fun movie, also mysterious for the most part, with beautiful countryside shots.    For a long time we only see this guy, Darius, walking around from the airport with his suitcase. The screen ratio is ultra wide (7/2), giving a lot of scenes where we just see him crossing the screen. He's following roads, crossing fields and forests, only following his compass. You wonder for a long time where he is going, he has a meeting, which turns out to be in Paris business district (so that's 80km away from the airport).    He briefly interacts with people, mostly speaking in weird maxims. When he does more than just briefly talking, we discover that he has superhuman abilities. Whether pool, archery, fighting, he is godly.    Along the way, he encounters several animals, that he also has a lot of knowledge on, and a lot of scenes are filmed from the point of view of these animals, either for scenic or comedy effect.    A lot of very funny moments (the pool, the archery, the ricocheting bullet, the girl, etc).    Not everyone will, but I also appreciated the ultimate goal of his trip.

cast_names: Mercedes Cabral, Alexis Manenti, Anna Luna, Irma Adlawan, Madeleine Nicolas, Ruby Ruiz, Yun Huang, Nico Antonio, Jun Nayra, Elora Españo
review_content: [Berlinale '26] One of the strengths of this proposal is its portrayal of a system of constant invisibility that allows Switzerland, as a representative of Western countries, to maintain a façade of wealth while permitting blatant labor exploitation. Although it may seem somewhat simplistic in its narrative, establishing the traditional confrontation between rich exploiters and poor exploited, it lucidly captures how the very construction of an endemic capitalist system, dependent on cheap labor to survive, is what establishes these kinds of power relations, this kind of perpetual slave labor environment.

cast_names: Laura Bach, Carla Eleonora Feigenberg, Marie Askehave
review_content: Slow to get going, and that pace persists throughout the film. It's worth sticking with it, as the protagonist and plot are intriguing.

cast_names: Ginés García Millán
review_content: [Annecy '24] An emotional story about the relationship between a father and son as he prepares to leave his home in the Murcian countryside. By choosing the stop-motion technique, the story achieves a three-dimensional texture that offers a realistic envelope, with careful decoration of the interior of the house, which meticulously reproduces the director's family home. The cinematography provides pale toned colors, contrasting the darkness of the interior of the house with the luminosity of the exterior, the field in which father and son go hunting for the last time before separation.

[VdR'22] There is a subtle structure in the film that manifests between the memories, the morning mist of Mâcon, the B/W footage of the protests against the mandatory deportation project BUMIDOM, and the home recordings of the father, reflecting a stabilized life. But the trauma of uprooting that has been buried for so long also reflects the consequences of the frustration of his disconnection with his origins.

cast_names: Khrystyna Deilyk, Vasyl Kukharskyi
review_content: Oxygen Station is, above all else, a visual triumph. Directed by Ukrainian filmmaker Ivan Tymchenko, the film transforms the remote Siberian village of Zyryanka—some 5,400 kilometers east of Moscow—into a stark, painterly landscape. Credit must go to cinematographer Thomas Stokowski and editors Ivan Bannikov and Karolina Maciejewska, whose work is nothing short of extraordinary. Their lens captures not only the icy desolation of exile, but also the political undertones buried beneath the surface.    Into this frozen exile is sent Mustafa Dzhemilev, the Crimean Tatar dissident arrested in 1968 for so-called “anti-Soviet activities.” After enduring a brutal 303-day hunger strike, he is banished to this edge of the empire in classic Soviet fashion: out of sight, out of mind. And yet—strangely—the film barely touches upon this extreme act of resistance. The omission is puzzling, bordering on negligent, especially given the gravity of Dzhemilev’s protest.    Instead, the film slows to a near-halt, unfolding with a methodical pace that borders on glacial. The storytelling is often frustrating, and one’s attention wavers easily. Characters like the Prosecutor, Safinar, and Kolya drift in and out with limited development. One figure—Hani—remains so ill-defined it’s unclear what purpose he serves at all. Even the mention of Crimean Tatars and their right to return home is left in vague abstraction.    And yet, Oxygen Station is never boring to look at. One scene in particular—a quiet moment on a train with an overweight family eating boiled eggs instead of buying food—says more about the working-class ethos of the Soviet era than pages of dialogue ever could. It’s a fleeting, almost throwaway moment, but it resonates. That’s where the film’s strength lies: in the visual, not the verbal.    Borys Orlov, who plays Dzhemilev, delivers a grounded, stoic performance. But his presence often feels secondary to the film’s true protagonist: the camera itself. This is not a biopic. It is, more accurately, a political and aesthetic statement—a haunting portrait of Soviet exile made even more poignant by the director’s likely intent to draw a parallel with today’s Ukrainian struggle.    In the end, Oxygen Station is difficult to judge. It’s a film where the story falters, but the image endures. Without its stunning cinematography and razor-sharp editing, the rating would be far lower. As it stands, the visuals elevate the film from mediocrity to something nearly memorable.    Rating: ⭐ 6/10  Production: 6/10  Cinematography & Editing: 10/10  Authenticity: 8/10

[VdR '25] With B/W cinematography reminiscent of 1950s science fiction films, this look at the space race reflects the environmental consequences of deploying satellites in the atmosphere and the slow change taking place in places like Boca Chica in the Gulf of Mexico, home to SpaceX's spaceport. It has a hypnotic visual texture that also uses sound design to create a sense of uncanniness, as tourists marvel at rocket launches exploding in the air and scientists warn of the danger of saturating our planetary environment with space junk.

cast_names: Terri Merritt Bennett, Ryan Bijan, George Hardy, Judd Lormand
review_content: I’ve always said found footage is one of the best genres out there—you can never go wrong with it! This was a solid watch that delivered on the suspense and drama. It’s a little slow-paced at times, but it leans straight into the unknown in a way that kept me hooked. Definitely a decent pick for FF fans.

cast_names: Jordan Stephens
review_content: 7 out of 10  Emetophobia Friendly  Watched on Channel 4 app.

cast_names: Will Brill, Gillian Jacobs, Rob Lowe, Melanie Herrera
review_content: The modern day Travis Bickle is a middle school drama teacher and we are all the better for it.

cast_names: Shelby Allison Brown, Callie Bussell, June Tuss, Karen M. Chan, Stacey Lee Powell, Kendahl Landreth, Jordan Myrick, Caitlin McWethy
review_content: Surprisingly sad at the start and for a bit too long tbh! Definitely lingered on the setup for the writer being out the spirit. But when it warms up it was super sweet. Acting and production is par for the course of this kind of movie but it was pretty well done all in all. Good chemistry between the leads and some memorable characters. A nice addition to the wlw Christmas movie list that's building up nicely each year now!

cast_names: Jenny Schily, Ursula Werner, Manfred Zapatka, Markus Schleinzer, Johannes Zeiler, Kirsten Block, Markus Lerch, Karina Plachetka
review_content: [IFFR '25] Through a story that connects with a real experience, the director creates an interesting study of the family dynamics that are established in the face of dependency processes and the care of members who need special attention. Showing schizophrenia from an approach that avoids psychotic symptoms to focus more on negative symptoms, a certain parallel is established between the different forms of dependency in family environments: the one that Sven has due to his illness, but also the emotional dependency that is perceived in the protagonist. The most interesting thing about the film is its ability to raise questions that connect directly with family relationships with which we can identify.

cast_names: Lo Yi-shan
review_content: [Visions du Réel '24] From the first images that show footsteps in the snow, the film adopts a dreamy cadence, a melancholic tone that is sustained in Lo Yi-Shan's reflections on the words of her friend Chun, or in her attempt to get Yueh to come out of his state of emotional denial. The director creates a journey that begins in the trauma of loss to build a story of growth in which she assumes the responsibility of the survivor who must share their story. There is an omnipresence to that mountainous environment of the Himalayas that despite its beauty cannot help but convey a feeling of isolation. But the journey that the director has built ends in a silent encounter that at the same time gives way to the slow process of healing the trauma of loss and reconciliation with memory.

cast_names: Yosuke Kubozuka, Ryuhei Matsuda, Junior Chihara, Haruka Imou, Kiyohiko Shibukawa, Masahiro Higashide, Itsuji Itao, Kilala Inori, Mame Yamada
review_content: Transcending Dimensions is less a story than a trance. Monks, sorcery, a starship to Kelman, and Toyoda’s meditative fury. Hypnotic.    A missing monk, Rosuke, is split across planes. His lover Nonoka escorts assassin Shinno to a secluded religious site, where the sorcerer Ajari waits. Elsewhere, Rosuke rides a spacecraft toward the planet Kelman and meets a strange old man, as a ritualised battle of belief and magic brews. Toyoda treats images like prayer beads: repetition, rupture, release. It plays like a visual poem more than plot, but once you tune in, it hums.    If you liked… Koyaanisqatsi, The Tree of Life.

cast_names: Yosuke Kubozuka, Ryuhei Matsuda, Junior Chihara, Haruka Imou, Kiyohiko Shibukawa, Masahiro Higashide, Itsuji Itao, Kilala Inori, Mame Yamada
review_content: [Fantasia '25] In the vein of spiritual cinema that makes up the Mt. Resurrection Wolf short films, but with comments on false religions, the director proposes a mix of genres to show the search for an answer to the spiritual void, which the main characters approach in different ways. It is an experimental proposal that surprises in its long scenes underlined by the music of Sons of Kemet which, despite not being as consistent as other works by Toshiaki Toyoda, maintains that chaotic mysticism that makes it a fascinating journey for viewers who want to transcend beyond traditional narratives.

cast_names: Atul Gawande, Marco Rubio
review_content: It goes without saying that massive cuts in foreign aid will have equally massive consequences. The short documentary “Rovina's Choice” focuses on the recent cuts at USAID and the corresponding impact on refugees from South Sudan in Kenya. The issue is presented in a clear and concise manner, allowing those who are unfamiliar with the topic to learn a little bit about it. However, there is not enough time to provide a broader overview, such as the domino effect that other nations are also massively reducing their foreign aid. Nevertheless, “Rovina's Choice” is a useful introduction to the topic.

Like waking in the forest at night: if not scary, a little creepy and the opossums were over my head.    This fun found footage (sorry for the tautology) movie centers on an alt couple in Adelaide, Australia who fall prey to conspiracy theories while making a documentary about the local urban legend of the mysterious Household's alleged victims.     The production value is amazing for its indie scale, the central actors are totally believable, and while the movie raises many points it never fully addresses (the trove of discovered x-rays and the fuckling opossums, for example), the film is not necessarily the worse off for the enigmas.

cast_names: Taapsee Pannu, Kani Kusruti, Mohammed Zeeshan Ayyub, Manoj Pahwa, Kumud Mishra, Revathi, Naseeruddin Shah, Supriya Pathak, Rajendra Sethi, Satyajit Sharma
review_content: The movie tries to touch upon too many issues in a single narrative leaving the overall story a bit idealistic and incoherent. This causes the film to lose its grip and its potential impact. The umbrella man plotline is only there to make the statement "an eye for an eye will turn the whole world blind" - Tapsee 's stance alone was enough for it and the focus could instead be on making the courtroom drama more gripping because now it feels nothing like the real life justice system. I'm a lawyer myself and I'm yet to see a woman judge so biased in favor of the victim. They have to play by the book without letting emotions get in their way.

cast_names: Taapsee Pannu, Kani Kusruti, Mohammed Zeeshan Ayyub, Manoj Pahwa, Kumud Mishra, Revathi, Naseeruddin Shah, Supriya Pathak, Rajendra Sethi, Satyajit Sharma
review_content: Frustrating watch that starts strong but collapses under heavy-handed messaging. "Assi" tackles a brutal gang-rape case in India: a teacher viciously attacked in a car, abandoned on railway tracks, then betrayed by a corrupt system. A grieving ex-spy (Kumud Mishra) becomes the vigilante "Umbrella Man," targeting the privileged perpetrators. Taapsee Pannu plays the advocate pushing for legal justice.Strong points: urgent topic, solid performances from Mishra, Naseeruddin Shah, and Manoj Pahwa (especially the father-son pistol scene), and red screens highlighting rape statistics every 20 minutes.Weaknesses: Taapsee's theatrical acting feels forced, pacing suffers from repetitive interruptions, the spy angle feels unnecessary, and the film lectures more about ideology ("leftist hypocrisy") than evoking real anguish over the crime. It criticizes vigilantism while pushing its own agenda.Worth a look if you're into socially charged Indian cinema and can handle preachiness; skip for deeper emotional impact, better options exist in the genre.

cast_names: Camille Cottin, Zar Amir Ebrahimi, Shant Hovhannisyan, Aleksandr Khachatryan, Babken Chobanyan, Denis Lavant
review_content: [Locarno '25] There is a profound connection between reality and fiction throughout the journey taken by the film's protagonist, as she tries to reconstruct her image of her husband and come to terms with the consequences of her actions. Throughout this road movie, the director's gaze lingers on the landscapes of present-day Armenia, with its ruins, its dead, and its ghosts, but also its capacity for constant rebirth. Two of Céline's journeys are punctuated by music, reflecting pride and the hope of a return after the ethnic cleansing of Nagorno-Karabakh. The film reflects on memory and trauma and lingers on Céline's face, who, although a foreigner, represents part of the pain of the Armenian diaspora.

cast_names: Nicholas Pinnock, Jordan Bolger, Osy Ikhile, Jason Isaacs, Rob Malone, Joplin Sibtain, Blake Harrison, Nabil Elouahabi, Jamie Bamber, Barry Aird
review_content: [Raindance '25] A boxing film without actually showing the boxing match, because the real confrontation takes place inside the locker room, establishing a dynamic relationship between those who accompany Derek and reflecting that for a boxer, not only physical training is important but also psychological preparation. The protagonist is trained enough to be able to face the world champion, but it seems unclear if he has the adequate psychological stability. For a film that takes place in a single space, it maintains the tension and moves around the characters as if they were the movements of the boxers in the ring.

cast_names: Sylwia K. Dudzinska
review_content: **“Noseeums”** just had its premiere at FrightFest, and the marketing hook says it’s **“Get Out meets Candyman.”** That’s a bold claim, but the comparison is skin-deep. Sure, it shares themes of racial injustice and swaps **Candyman’s** bees for tiny swarms of biting insects, but that’s about where it ends. Those classics worked because they had ideas that haunted you long after the credits rolled. Here, the intent is admirable—tackling generational trauma through a horror lens—but the execution feels heavy-handed and obvious, like being spoon-fed the message instead of discovering it through the story. Add to that a lead performance weighed down by direction that pushes panic too early, and the impact dulls instead of sharpens.    Visually, the film’s fine until the bugs show up, and then it slips into the realm of distraction. The noseeums never look convincing, especially in the climax, and that hurts the immersion. It’s not a bad movie—the ambition is there, and the themes matter—but the uneven acting, on-the-nose writing, and shaky effects keep it from reaching the heights it’s aiming for. For me, that lands it at a D, 47 out of 100 stars. Admirable effort, not memorable execution.

cast_names: Sylwia K. Dudzinska
review_content: A college student joins rich friends at a country house, finds a grave in the woods, and the swarming no-see-ums start biting. Premonitions, flashbacks and land-back lore point to buried crimes and vengeful spirits. I get what it's going for, but the didactic streak overwhelms the horror. When a film keeps lecturing rather than letting dread accumulate, the scares deflate. It's earnest, it's angry, and it'll work for some, but the sermon tramples the story for me.    If you liked… Get Out, Candyman (2021), Master.

cast_names: Andrzej Jakimowski
review_content: [Berlinale '25] Partly to counteract the feeling of still being considered a foreigner despite having lived in Poland since 2010, Delhi-born director Arjun Talwar decided to film the neighbours on the street where he lives with the intention of promoting his integration. This documentary, with a humanistic and humorous perspective, offers a more heterogeneous view of Poland, which portrays a diverse environment, not always friendly to foreigners but less stereotyped than usual. It continually moves in contrasts to reflect a society that misunderstands the concept of multiculturalism.

cast_names: Regnārs Vaivars
review_content: LIFF39 2025 #4    Dog of God... was definitely an experience, especially in the cinema. It's not the usual kind of movie I see in cinemas, but each year this film festival offers something different to local cinemas that would otherwise never get attention. There's a lot to appreciate here despite having mixed feelings about other aspects.    Ah, screw it, I'm just gonna say—it's a very daring film that doesn't shy away from its content.    I liked the animation and the score. It looked very unique, like a painting come to life, and was even more impressive because it was filmed with actors on a green-screen set and then turned into animation using computer technology. It also kept going to places I didn't expect and kept doing so for an impressively long time. The creators behind this weren’t on drugs; instead, they have a creative imagination, albeit somewhat odd.    Although I wasn't always fully engaged with it, it’s never dull, because that would be impossible.    It's hard to recommend this to anyone or watch it with someone who doesn't know what to expect. If it interests you, you can watch it. If not, then you can just skip it. It’s definitely an experience I won’t forget, I’ll tell you that.

cast_names: Regnārs Vaivars
review_content: [Fantasia '25] A horror fantasy that has something of a psychedelic trip to it, directly connecting with werewolf lore, but not in the Western European sense that was eventually adopted by North American cinema and literature, but rather in the pagan mythologies of Central Europe that have a certain shamanic quality. Proving that Latvia's small audiovisual industry has something more to say in the animation genre, now with an adult focus, it is a sordid film that recognizes the influence of Ralph Bakshi and addresses themes such as belief-based paranoia, fears, witchcraft and lycanthropy, but above all it talks about religion as a plague more poisonous than disease.

cast_names: Geetha Kailasam, Saran Shakthi, Bharani, Ashand Raju
review_content: Good film. Liked it. Simple storyline presented neatly without any boring moments..

review_content: Fair, not overly informative, doc regarding the early formation of the Beatles. Thankfully it keeps the subject from expansion and comes in under an hour for a quick viewing.

[VdR '25] In an observational format, the camera is positioned at a distance, focusing on faces but allowing for a certain intimacy between the doctor and his patients. Numerous short stories are shown throughout the film, not intended to delve into any of them but rather to offer the diverse facets that medical practices dedicated to identities can have. Sex reassignment consultations are presented within the context of the other consultations, reflecting the normality of autonomy and belonging of the body.

movie_title: Sanatorium Under the Sign of the Hourglass
cast_names: Andrzej Kłak
review_content: That was hard to watch. The visual style is not particularly pleasant, and too often blurry and dark.    There probably is a kind of story, seeing it's cut in different parts with titles that suggest something is happening, but it was impossible to discern any specific action or progression during the whole thing. The experimental effects, like looping the same 3 seconds 10 times in a raw, (that happened a lot), make sit even more unbearable.    Some sequences are filmed, most are animation, and they're separate. It's pretty hard to see a link between the characters in the two worlds.    One thing, it's clear that this required a lot of work, and it seems the base material is already weird and probably very hard to adapt. But the result is boring to death.

cast_names: Jermaine Fowler, James Morosini, Pauline Chalamet, Violett Beane, Edy Modica, Rob Yang, Brendan Hunt, Harrison Cone
review_content: The film is still a comedy like the rest of Steve Pink's filmography, but it's a dark comedy that incorporates a layer of low-budget science fiction into the texture of a thriller about extraordinary situations, from which it becomes increasingly difficult to escape. It may be somewhat excessive in its final part, but it has the ability to maintain interest because we never know where it will go next, whether it will opt for satirical humor about the achieved or frustrated aspirations revealed in a reunion of old college friends, or whether it will prefer to head towards the darker side of the paranoid thriller.

“Last Days on Lake Trinity” is a competently filmed short documentary about the housing crisis, benefiting from its three likeable protagonists. Through these three elderly women, we learn what the closure of a trailer park in Florida means for the entire community. The impending loss of their homes causes great uncertainty and is an enormous burden for those affected, which is also clearly conveyed in the documentary. Overall, however, I would have preferred a little more focus. In places, the documentary meanders a little. It is worth watching overall, but it certainly could have had a little more substance.

cast_names: Daniel Baldwin, Bryan Scamman, Danielle Titus, E.R. Ruiz, Omar Cook, William Castrogiovanni
review_content: The worst Jurassic movie I've ever seen. Truly horribly fake, after 5 minutes I could already tell it was terrible. Definitely not worth watching.

[VdR '25] A foray into the family past of a married couple who lived through National Socialism, the Federal Republic of Germany, and reunification, tracing a personal historical memory within a context of political significance. Structured around fragments of a book by Christa Wolf, based on her grandfather's memories, the director explores personal responsibilities in authoritarian regimes and builds on the layers of sediment provided by memories, which contrast with the traces left by time. In a family history that becomes more relevant within the current context.

cast_names: Anna Starchenko, Sanzhar Madi
review_content: The first three episodes out of 10 of an upcoming TV show.    Based on Kazakh folk monsters that we probably never heard about, so that's great. Alway a good thing to add new monsters to the global folklore.    But there's also an underlying story, the hidden secrets of the city and the different characters involved. Plus another subplot about the cop [spoiler]becoming a monster[/spoiler].    Definitely Yerzhanov's style. On the camera work and landscapes. No mute character this time (yet) but definitely a weird one to come with the cop's son. The usual corrupt cops.    The fortune teller character is very cool, though the exorcist like scenes look a bit ridiculous.    At the end of these episodes it looks like the team is formed, several sub plots have been launched, and it looks very promising.    I don't know whether it was initially intended to be shown this way, but the three episodes form a full arc and can be seen as a whole story.

[Sheffield '25] The use of drone footage showing the vast expanse of territory the migrants must cross somehow establishes the reduced and intimate nature of the small stories told. The region is marked by intense weather conditions that are difficult to endure, especially for families. Although the directors try to offer the perspective of the inhabitants of the Chilean border town, there are only a few hints about political responsibilities. The film is more interested in the human stories, but a more in-depth analysis would have been more interesting.

[Hot Docs '25] There's a slow pace to the film's images that conveys a sense of calm in the daily lives of its protagonists, but this contrasts with some archival footage in mediocre quality that nevertheless shows the tension of crossing the desert. The coyotes of the title refer to the immigrant traffickers who theoretically help immigrants cross the border but often become their worst traveling companions. The documentary ends in a border zone that was once a binational space where one could walk freely between the United States and Mexico, showing the profound transformation of immigration policies that condemn emigrants to cross a deadly desert.

[VdR '25] Amidst the breathtaking scenery surrounding the city of Lausanne, the French director uses an external perspective to reflect the imperfections of a Switzerland that always seems to strive to maintain that idyllic image of a balanced and civilized country. Noah doesn't suggest a risk of social exclusion, but he has an explosive character that ends in calls to the police. The film raises reflections on the extent to which the constant passage between reformatories and foster homes also becomes a vicious circle that prevents him from escaping the spiral of an unstable life.

[Annecy '25] A beautiful fable about old age and the changes that occur when one must accept that the rest of one's life will be spent in a senior center. Shrouded in a certain melancholy as Piet comes to terms with his own aging and believes he's turning into a bird, it opens up different interpretations that make it more interesting: it could refer to Alzheimer's and the way memories are allowed to fly away, but also to the way of accepting the end of life. Although the intention is to create a friendly story, the different interpretations give the story a moving complexity.

[VdR '25] There's a powerful quality to the images in this black-and-white film, reflecting the loneliness of the clandestine hunter. The first part of the story has a Western tone, conveying Landi's isolation in contrast to the global experience of the pandemic. The second part, focusing on his wife and son, is more poignant, but also reflects how diving into the wetlands into hand-to-hand combat with crocodiles is never enough to sustain a family. The repeated departure and return thus becomes a cycle of life and survival.

[TIFF '25] The documentary's greatest strength, but also its harshest moments, lies in the personal testimonies of victims of sexual abuse within the Catholic Church. But it is very clear in its denunciation of a hidden complicity of the ecclesiastical hierarchy, with excommunications lifted and promises of investigations that drag on. The Church prefers to air its dirty laundry internally, but with the intention of turning abusers into sinners rather than criminals. The testimonies are a reflection of the lack of interest in the addressing allegations of abuse.

cast_names: Valentyn Vasyanovych, Vladlen Odudenko, Marianna Novikova, Volodymyr Yatsenko, Mykhailo Liubarskyi, Serhii Stepanskyi
review_content: [TIFF '25] A film that talks about the war in Ukraine without showing it, set in an imagined postwar period where the scars remain. Although it plays with the metanarrative within itself, which can sometimes be confusing, it manages to distance itself enough to reflect on the possibility of using cinema as a tool for survival, both mental and physical. The title refers to a toast during World War II that has now returned to form, once again, a part of the future of a country with wounds that go beyond material destruction.

[Venice '25] From the perspective of a young Taliban soldier, his daily life is framed by religious indoctrination, praying and surrendering to the possibility of martyrdom, like a suicide who achieves enlightenment through sacrifice. But it is above all his relationship with his younger brother, Rafi, age 14, that presents a perverse reflection of childhood brainwashing. We can understand Samim's radicalization through the description of Rafi's daily life, constantly subjected to narratives of violence and a religious doctrine based on destruction and sacrifice. The film explores the essence of fanaticism.

cast_names: Jeff Yung, Adam Cawley, Justin Bott, Julius Cho
review_content: [Fantasia '25] Through an absurd and wacky comedy in which a shrimp achieves the best recipe for Chinese fried rice, a mockumentary is proposed that simultaneously parodies culinary documentaries and television cooking shows. But far from being just a superficial comedy, it addresses relevant themes, such as one's own identity and the culinary culture that has been passed down from parents to children, and a certain search for identity that is represented in the inner conflict seen in the short film through the contrast between the enterprising and foul-mouthed shrimp, and the human who needs to keep a low profile for fear of deportation.

cast_names: Amel Baccouche, Tim Bülow
review_content: [Locarno '25] The directors utilize spaces where masculinity is traditionally represented, conquering the places where lesbian gazes also practice cruising and furtive sex takes place in public restrooms. This occupation of male space sets the tone for a story that reclaims freedom, however imperfect. In this joyfully queer and somewhat chaotic road movie, which ends with La Gata, the oldest lesbian bar in the world since it opened in 1971, the three protagonists reclaim their identities by confronting the secrets and memories of a distant past while addressing the uncertainties of a future that is all too close.

movie_title: The Hole, 309 Days to the Bloodiest Tragedy
cast_names: Carissa Perusset, Khiva Rayanka, Anya Zen
review_content: [IFFR '26] From a genre perspective, the film reaches its full potential through the appearances of a supernatural figure known as the Hollow Ghost. While there are gory scenes and apparitions that may satisfy fans of Indonesian horror, it is the structural violence that stands out within a story that references significant events. It might be an exaggeration to call it a political thriller in its deepest sense; however, the reflective elements about corruption that obstructs the truth—a concept treated as an object of manipulation—fuel the story, making it far more profound.

House of Hope, a school in Palestine where children learn to treat each other with respect and to fight the Israeli occupation with words rather than violence. The documentary is mainly set before the Hamas attack on 7 October 2023. When the attack is reported on the news halfway through, you notice that the tension and fear among the population is increasing. It is intense to watch, and it is a sad thought now to know that the children in the film might probably no longer be alive.

cast_names: Cristian Mercado, Luis Bredow, Juan Carlos Aduviri
review_content: [Fantasia '25] There's a mystical quality to the film's opening that leads into the main plot, in which a young girl searches for a way to free her mother from the earthly misery she faces. There's a strong feminine energy to the film, from the journey Santa shares with her mother's body to the encounter with a group of indigenous fighters who call themselves "cholitas," and a somewhat stereotypical view of the uniqueness of the Bolivian environment. What's disappointing is that, in such a sensitively told story, the magical elements don't quite break the traditional narrative and create a conclusion that's sympathetic to the characters, but also feels overly complacent.

[DOK.fest '25] Focusing her gaze on a group of domestic animals in a small village in the Pirin Mountains, Bulgaria, the director balances and equalizes the relationship between them and humans by placing the camera at their level. She seems to be reflecting on the traditional superior position of humankind over nature and the living beings in their care, who in reality evaluate human actions. The representation of animals is presented as a projection of human fears and anxieties, and this mystical nature best defines a film that walks between superstition and reality, constructing a poetic vision that can sometimes seem too imprecise.

cast_names: Go Ayano, Ko Shibasaki, Kazuya Kamenashi, Yukiyoshi Ozawa, Masahiro Takashima, Fumino Kimura, Ken Mitsuishi, Kazuki Kitamura, Rie Minemura, Ayaka Higashino
review_content: [Fantasia '25] Tackling the courtroom drama for the first time, Takashi Miike presents a true story from a clear point of view that initially reflects the media-reported perspective of a teacher who allegedly harassed one of his students for being a "halfu," a Japanese man of mixed blood. While more interested in analyzing the parallel public trial than the actual court case, he adopts a less nuanced perspective that undermines credibility, with a portrayal of some characters that seems somewhat manipulative. The complexity of the case is blurred into a perspective that ultimately feels too flat in terms of the development of the events.
