review_content: Started good and won't lie, mid was the best but then they dragged the topic for no reason. Could have worked a little more on the topic.

cast_names: Jordi Ballester
review_content: [Movistar+] Starting with a premonition during a tarot reading, the story unfolds as a thriller, maintaining tension until a finale that reveals the darkest side of our society. It's an intriguing proposition that uses the genre to gradually delve into social commentary, beginning with an initial premise that progressively leads to the reality it aims to reflect. It avoids clichés to construct a suspenseful narrative, complete with excellent sound design, that captivates the viewer and draws them toward an unexpected conclusion.

cast_names: Iván Zulueta, Jaime Chávarri, Eusebio Poncela, Cecilia Roth, Marta Fernández Muro, Carlos F. Heredero
review_content: [Movistar+] A journey through the passions and anxieties of one of Spain's most controversial filmmakers, who attempts to reclaim his legacy by making him a secondary character in his own story. The formal construction of the vampirism of cinema, which extends to the very making of the documentary-fiction, becomes its main problem, because it constantly gives the impression that there are more interesting things to tell in the background of the past than in the reproduction of the present. However daring it may be, it feels too artificial and egocentric.

A couple arrives at a BnB where the host is exceedingly present for them. Soon becomes creepy. Then a bit scary. They want to leave but he doesn't let them. Finally basically [spoiler]stabs himself[/spoiler].    Easy story, but flirting with a mix of comedy and horror, you're never really sure until where it's gonna go. Real fun but with a mastery of suspense and tension.

cast_names: Clara Schwinning, Voodoo Jürgens, Simon Steinhorst, Dolunay Gördüm
review_content: [Locarno FF '23] Through a rural environment surrounded by a certain apocalyptic dystopia, the director creates a disturbing and at the same time placid atmosphere, which is seductive and attractive. Although in general it's a somewhat cryptic film in which many elements (disappearances, illness, secrets...) are not fully explained, the intention of transmitting feelings rather than elaborating a precise narrative achieves its objective, maintaining a capacity for attention towards the protagonists and their desire to abandon a village of immovable everyday life that is doomed to failure.

cast_names: Bára Anna Stejskalová
review_content: [Annecy '25] Without dialogue, the short film opens with a musical number starring a family of crabs, and continues to develop through a primarily musical tone, although it addresses the relationship between a peaceful blind fish and a predatory shrimp that observes the world with its ability to detect more shades of color than any living being. This contrast works well in a story that is perhaps not as moving as it claims to be, because it uses less conventional characters. But it reflects on friendship and the sacrifices that come with accepting who we are.

Every year at the Oscars, there seem to be a few films that deal with the prison system in the US. This year, that's the case with the short documentary "Classroom 4," which is about a course taught in a prison that both regular and incarcerated students take part in. In keeping with this very specific subgenre, the film manages to humanize the prisoners and thereby draw attention to important projects aimed at their rehabilitation.    However, the cinematic implementation in "Classroom 4" is rather unsuccessful. The subject matter is presented in a dry manner. The discussions between the students are certainly interesting, but we learn little about them, so that it is only in a few moments that any emotional connection with them is forged. Ultimately, "Classroom 4" is quite effective in drawing attention to the "Inside Out" program. But it also squanders a lot of potential.

cast_names: Amanda Peet, Matthew Shear, Alessandro Nivola, Judd Hirsch, Bob Balaban, Andrea Martin, Zosia Mamet, Jessica Harper, Holland Taylor, Sheng Wang
review_content: Like watching a plane taxi for 91 minutes, this movie promised a journeuly but never took off.  Waste of some talented actors.

cast_names: Denis Podalydès, Olivier Rabourdin, Micha Lescot, Laurent Poitrenaux, Irène Jacob, Manuel Guillot, Anne Alvaro, Julia Faure, Arcadi Radeff, Dominique Reymond
review_content: It's a daring gamble to attempt a Maigret set in our era, especially one who is less physically imposing than Cremer or Gabin. But after the first few minutes, the impressive acting and meticulously crafted dialogue carried me along without a hitch. The 1 hour and 20 minutes flew by, and I came out feeling satisfied. Here's hoping that others are in the works.

cast_names: Giulia Maenza, Aomi Muyock, Vinicio Marchioni, Giulia Gonella
review_content: Clearly meant to be beautiful oneiric stuff. But I don't know, it probably is influenced by your mood and the viewing condition, but to me it never really **felt** oneiric. It just looked like a movie that tries really hard to be. Most of it just feels forced.      It has got all the clichés: music, lighting and shadows, weird things, people speaking poetic nonsense in near ASMR mode. And the Italian. Being in Italian really helps, it would have completely failed if it was in English.    It's kinda weird how she "disappeared" in that moment. He's looking for her, did he even know she died ? Did she just die here on the spot ? Or did she flee after that night knowing she was dying ?    The devil, well, the boss, but it looks l like he's just some kind of lower middle management, not THE devil, is a half missed shot. The fact that it's just an empty vest, that looks suspiciously like his own shirt, is not that bad an idea. But it's also visually lazy.    There are some animation parts, and sometimes (not really often), the transition between real images and animation is really blurry. Those are real good. Maybe visually the movie should have more focused on these.

cast_names: Lili Reinhart, Lola Tung, Victoria Pedretti, Alexandra Shipp, Emma Chamberlain, Gabrielle Union, Hailey Summer, Jordan Duarte, David Pinard, R Austin Ball
review_content: This was a good time and reliably solid movie. It’s a slow burn but the humor through out kept me engaged. The cast did a great job embodying their characters and the soundtrack fit the movie perfectly.   Would Recommend

cast_names: Vasanth Ravi, Mehreen Pirzada, Sunil Varma, Anikha Surendran, Kalyan Kumar, Sumesh Moor, Rajkumar, Jeeva Ravi, Gajaraj
review_content: One of the better performances of #VasanthRavi, though his overacting acts as a spoilsport at places, he shines with respect to action and when he is subtle. #Indra cinematography was good. Twists are not that good but okay it satisfied.

cast_names: Galatea Bellugi, Matthieu Lucci, Samuel Kircher, Sharif Andoura
review_content: I'll have to admit this movie confused me quite a lot and I'm not sure what I got from it. On one hand, I felt that the vision of the teacher from the city who comes to "civilize" the "uneducated" villagers was worth being questioned- and it wasn't. On the other hand, this depiction somehow didn't feel cliche.. And most of it was deeply captivating and the shots of the snowy mountains were breathtaking.

cast_names: Evgenia Dodina, Arkadiusz Jakubik, Dominika Bednarczyk, Jowita Budnik, Artur Dziurman
review_content: [TIFF '25] Using handheld close-up shots, the director depicts Mila's sense of strangeness when she discovers that life has continued without taking her into account. She realizes that she is a figure as indispensable as she is easily dispensable. Despite a certain predictability, she manages to construct a character who captures the dissatisfaction of her reunion with what seemed like her true home. The film feels more secure in its visual calligraphy than in the narrative of an uneven script, but it raises interesting reflections on the concept of home and social differences.

cast_names: Mara Bestelli, Marcelo Subiotto, Betania Cappato
review_content: [Berlinale '25] In today's Argentina, governed by a president who claims to talk to his dead dog, this approach could be read ironically, although the story began to develop long before Javier Milei became president. The director sets a calm pace for a story that may have some problems in becoming more complex due to the absence of conflicts, but which is built through an atmosphere of magical realism that becomes more and more present. And although it may be too contemplative, there are moments of a very notable poetic and almost philosophical quality.

I had the chance to watch the movie and was not aware of Theosophy (also had a quick read about the author Helena Blavatsky). The old skeptic in me, from way back, could be turned off just for the sheer fact that the whole Theosophy seems mostly philosophy-based and has traces of having no empirical data support for some claims (e.g., hidden "Masters"), accusations of plagiarism (to be fair, to be strongly proven) and outdated race theories (due to the time in which it was written), but the new embracing and open-minded me sees Theosophy for the worth it has: the right blend of New Age spirituality, healing culture, Eastern philosophy and expression of many ways of belief (each religion expresses the truth in its own way). While I might not incorporate many of the esoteric practices mentioned in the movie, I do respect this way of life and do believe (in similar ways explained in other movements) that life has its chapters. If it is a reincarnation or not, I don't know. But it seems so that life has its cycles... or maybe that's just a confirmation bias of mine. Either way, very good documentary and recommend people to watch it.

cast_names: Catherine Corcoran, Lloyd Kaufman, Zac Amico, Doug Sakmann, Asta Paredes, Clay von Carlowitz, Bjarni Gautur, Lisbeth Kaufman, Liam Regan, Nico Praz
review_content: [Fantasia '25] At a time of resurgence for the Troma brand, this documentary reflects the independent nature of the production company and its founder, Lloyd Kaufman. But it also reflects that it remains a primarily family business, supported by unpaid volunteers and with a frankly questionable marketing strategy. Filmed at the Cannes Film Festival between 2013 and 2017, the displays of made-up monsters contrast with the snobbish atmosphere of La Croisette, heavily patrolled by the police. But the documentary prefers to remain anecdotal, without delving too deeply into the production company's true independent spirit and without presenting a genuine analysis beyond its contrast with the superficial world of the red carpet.

cast_names: Jessica Erin Martin
review_content: A woman finds an bottomless hole in a closet. Lots of intermediate family drama about dementia father. Maybe a bit too much. Then something finally hits the bottom and there is a reaction.    The letter saying "[spoiler]he loves marbles[/spoiler]" is a good idea, at least the father remembers that. Though they are selling the house, so who cares ?    Loved the parallel between the dog laying in the beginning and the monster at the end.    Could have cut the bit in the family drama, I guess it's to distract and make think that the hole is an analogy of something ?

cast_names: Dakota Gorman, Todd Terry, James Austin Johnson, Liz Atwater, Jordan Mullins
review_content: [Fantasia '25] It begins as a survival horror film within a space where the director takes advantage of the opportunity to incorporate flashback scenes in an original way, but it unfolds as a psychological drama with touches of classic horror that speaks to identity and family connections. It's not about surprising with an intriguing mystery, but rather about building a psychological connection between the characters that, in a way, runs along parallel paths in their psyches, just as the van crosses secondary roads to avoid being discovered. It's a story that also speaks to the transformation that stems from a painful event, and the acceptance of a new destiny even if it leads to unexpected places.

A film that celebrates Eleanor Ambos' 92-year-old passion for life. She was constantly organizing projects, we heard her speak German, English, French, and Spanish with her collaborators, and had a near-romantic friendship with a young waiter. Refusing to accept old age and write a will, with no family and a property holdings in New York, she is one of those characters who build a film from her vitality. It is an intimate look that also explores some emotional traumas from a past in Nazi Germany and a strict father who nullified her as a person.

cast_names: Genaro Vasquez Nevares, Julian Moriarty, Iban Dido Gutierrez, Adrienne Palmer, Melissa Cervantes
review_content: ROH/CMLL Global Wars Mexico was a short and sweet wrestling card. While it didn’t offer a ton of standout moments, the matches it did provide were enjoyable and showed some solid in-ring work. The smaller scale made it easy to watch, but it left me wanting just a bit more from the overall event.  Best Match: Bryan Danielson Return  Rating: 2.5/5 – 70% – Worth Watching

cast_names: Will Sterling, Edward Hong, Aisha Holden, Molly Gibson, P.J. Marino, Tavish Grade, Courtney Perdue, Mark Jacobson, Montelle Harvey, Zachary J. Luna
review_content: [Raindance '25] The main character is an unemployed actor who decides to make his own movie about an unemployed actor who decides to make his own movie. This approach, which moves in the comedy with cinematic references, offers an ironic reflection on cinema from the point of view of those who see the Hollywood Sign as an almost unattainable goal, but without losing hope. The film handles the springs of the metanarrative by incorporating it in an increasingly elaborate way, such that the difference between the recording of the fictional film and Will's own personal story becomes blurred.

cast_names: Jake Daniels, Benjamin Mayer, Gabriela Fuentes
review_content: This is a free [YouTube Fan Film](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fETXFxBI4Z0), and I thought they did a great job bringing us into that world.  Gabriela Fuentes was a standout as Naomi, but no one was bad, and I hope to see more from the studio.

[DOK.fest '25] It tells the story of Lebanon's recent years of crisis, but from the perspective of the diaspora, those young Lebanese who had the opportunity to leave and form a community in New York that maintains, from afar, the protests against the different governments that have passed through the country, even if only at the doors of the Embassy in the United States. The film seems to have the need to demonstrate that the link with the country remains strong despite living far from it, but in the physical transformation of the protagonists themselves, it is perceived that they feel more integrated into the streets of New York than in those of Beirut.

cast_names: Aslat Mahtte Gaup, Nikolaj Coster-Waldau, Sverre Porsanger, Mikkel Gaup, Mary Sarre, Gard Emil Elvenes
review_content: Feels like they made a movie about my family😭

cast_names: Marie Bloching, Anton Weil, Proschat Madani, Jane Chirwa, Aram Tafreshian, David Vormweg
review_content: [Berlinale '25] The script co-written by Sarah Miro Fischer and Agnes Maagaard Petersen uses a moral dilemma, without moralizing, to reflect on family dynamics and the relationships of trust, especially when this is shattered by a situation that turns one of them into a possible sexual aggressor. The film is not so interested in showing the reality of the facts, but in reflecting the subjectivity of the complicated personal crossroads in which Rose finds herself, increasingly confined between window and door frames. That is why it loses some strength when it decides to abandon ambiguity.

cast_names: Tânia Maria, Alli Willow, Valmir do Côco, Severino Dadá
review_content: [IFFR '26] The film has a distinctly dystopian, almost horror-like tone, functioning as a satire on Brazil's relationship with world powers, through the presence of American scientists who intend to eliminate the threat of a mosquito using nuclear weapons. Within its blend of genres, it possesses the tone and visual style of pulp science fiction films, but also adopts a certain atmospheric quality that seems more interested in conveying sensations than developing a plot, opening itself to the fantasy of the normalization of the infestation and proposing a story marked by ambiguities.

cast_names: Rio Vega, Marie-Anne Guilbert
review_content: A guy who seem addicted to cheese. May be an analogy for drugs, maybe not. When he's out of cheese, his friend invites him for a raclette... But there's no raclette, it's a surprise birthday party. He goes crazy and kills them all.    Really little effects so the death are not that good.  But fun discussions when he does not understand how they could lie to him about the cheese.    Fun side part when the friends get extorted to buy some cheese in the middle.

movie_title: Damned If You Do, Damned If You Don't
cast_names: Gianni Di Gregorio, Greta Scarano, Tom Wlaschiha, Iaia Forte
review_content: [Venice '25] Although it's unbalanced by some absurd subplot, it feels more comfortable in the everyday setting of a grandfather spending time with his grandchildren, and in this sensitive portrait of old age in which there's still time for romantic play, even for provoked jealousy, or for sneaking off in the middle of a family dinner to have a quick cigarette. Although it's not as funny or perhaps as vibrant as other films by Gianni Di Gregorio, this gentle and melancholic comedy leaves a good taste thanks to its insightful perspective on the role of parents when they become grandparents.

cast_names: Ariel Lin, Wu Ke-xi, Jimmy Liu, Frederick Lee, Moon Lee, Wang Yu-xuan, Li Xing
review_content: [IFFR '25] The interplay of relationships established between the three protagonists is a thoughtful look at sexual freedom and fluid identities, but also creates stereotypical characters that function as unnecessary antagonists. There is a direction that is structured in static shots, giving the sensation of composing the kind of photographs that are very present in the film. Although there are somewhat forced resources, it offers a look at bodies and sexual roles that is very captivating, aesthetically careful and erotically seductive. And it is successful in the sensitivity that it manages to bring to each of the protagonists.

cast_names: Cláudio da Silva, Gerti Drassl, Raphael von Bargen
review_content: [Locarno '25] Somewhere between an archival film, an ethnological observation, a poetic essay, and a representation of fables and tales expressed within the format of fiction, this proposal is a blend of formal and narrative styles that is as heterogeneous as it is free from structural constraints. It presents as a documentary that showcases the work of archaeologists Georg and Vera Leisner, but it also aims to document the imaginary of a region where past and present, reality and legend, intermingle. Different eras, fictions and realities, are juxtaposed, ultimately forming an internal relationship, as in the excavations where fragments from different historical periods are discovered. This can make the film feel less cohesive than it should, but it creates a magical and timeless quality around its images.

[Berlinale '24] The three protagonists of the film offer different visions about Lebanon and the way to achieve a change that frees them from corruption and fraud. A revolution by arms, political or popular, is the approach offered by each of them in the Myriam El Hajj's second documentary. However, is not only a pessimistic view, but also offers a vision of resistance through its protagonists, and the editing by Chilean Anita Pérez structures this fragmented stories in a way that unifies the three characters. with a common objective of introducing a change in Lebanese society.

movie_title: The Srebrenica Tape – From Dad, for Alisa
review_content: [DOK.fest '25] In these kinds of stories that delve into a war-torn past, there's a confrontation between the need of a later generation to recover family memories and the memories that many survivors find traumatic. This raises some questions about the re-victimization of victims that this kind of personal investigation can provoke. The film is more interested in Alisa's need to learn more about her father, though it doesn't really offer many answers. The interest in passing this knowledge on to her daughter feels honest and necessary, though the path she takes seems to have little empathy for the wounds it reopens.

movie_title: Kat Crime: Tales of the Occult
cast_names: Brady Reiter, Cameron Meyer, Trevor Stines, Scott Hamm Duenas, Kipp Tribble, Trevor Penick
review_content: I was the VFX Supervisor for this film.    Overall, I was really proud of the end result here. When I worked on the film, I only saw fragments at a time. Seconds. A texting graphic here, an email notification there, a muzzle flash there. I made the opening and end credits as well - but I had never gotten the ability to see the film as a whole...and finally, I got there. It's a whole new world when you see your work alongside everyone else's. It's something else.    The movie itself is a low budget, indie horror fare - and you mostly get what you expect. I do like some elements of the cinematography, the simple structure and the true crime YouTuber idea - however, it does have a problem with too much exposition and far too short of a 3rd act/climax. Overall, I had a good time with it.

cast_names: Yutaka Kyan, Tammy Lin, Mimi Shao, Ray Fan, Mondo Otani, Wan-Hao Chen, Yuhei Shirakawa, Reika Oozeki, Hironobu Nomura, Yasukaze Motomiya
review_content: Definitely one of the better J-horrors I've seen in recent years, mainly because there are some elements of originality and it isn't completely bonkers. The ending is worth waiting for.

cast_names: Niall Ferguson
review_content: All the usual faces on the for side and all the usual faces on the against side.  Giving time to people like Schiff (a notorious anti-bitcoiner who requested btc donations on his twitter and kept hundreds of thousands of dollars worth of them... Along with admitting to sleeping with a minor when he was in college. Yes really) as well as relying on someone like Mike Green (who has missed every opportunity to ride a bitcoin wave from trough to peak and therfore belittle anyone who invests in it as low intelligence) shows you that the list of folks being interviewed is problematic.  If you're curious about bitcoin, this won't help you. It'll dredge up a load of nonsense from 15 years ago and pretend it can predict the future. And represent bitcoin as being for fringe society members only.   They'd have been better spending all the time on how btc mining offsets energy usage and just leave it at that. After all, energy is a worldwide issue and we don't have enough of it.   For every person making a valid point about the use and legitimacy of Bitcoin is offset by somebody who is of dubious character.

movie_title: Once Upon a Time in Gaza
cast_names: Majd Eid, Hussein Nakhleh
review_content: [Cannes '25] There are elements of dark humor and B-movie action genre clichés in the final part, but the overall tone is more serious than it seems, and perhaps for that reason it comes across as somewhat unbalanced, with a curious absence of female characters, who only appear as representations of a longed-for absence. Focusing on character portrayals, the first part addresses smuggling and corruption, to later establish an uneven comedic tone within the meta-narrative that contrasts the representation of heroism with great dramatic effects, with the silent hero imbued with patience and resilience.

cast_names: Daniela Ramírez, Néstor Cantillana, Mariana Loyola, Clemente Rodríguez, Erto Pantoja
review_content: [SSIFF '25] The first part of the film unfolds in a familiar setting surrounded by the sound of waves, conveying a corporeality that lingers on glances and sensations. Perhaps there is a bit too much subtlety at times, demanding greater attention to detail, but it cleverly reflects Celeste's transformation between her early puberty, when she asked questions without expecting answers, and her transition to adulthood, when she questions in order to obtain them. Awareness dawns in the protagonist as the hidden traces of her past are unearthed from the sand.

cast_names: Bishop Black, Jorge Benavides
review_content: [VdR '25] Through a group of people of different identities and sexualities who meet to have group sex, Maja Classen offers a look at the interaction of bodies in which sensations predominate over traditional sexual practices. Almost as if it were an ethical porn film, it is explicit in some of the encounters but above all it shows mutual respect as the basis for bodily pleasure. It can't help but be as repetitive as documentaries that talk about sex tend to be, but it invites a sensorial experience that goes beyond superficial pleasure.

cast_names: Luka Albarrán, Esther Luna, Ana Rodríguez
review_content: [Movistar+] This story tackles adolescence through a slice-of-life comedy about a young man who seems to have the ability to perform miracles. When invisibility becomes a reflection of superstitions in a small town, even his own family sees a teenager with no future prospects in a new light. Conversations about infinity, skepticism about medicine, and faith in the Virgin Mary's ability to heal the spirit and body envelop a story that lacks the final push to become truly captivating.

[VdR '25] Following in the footsteps of the women of his family, a journey through memory and the spirits of the past, the director constructs an essay about his queer identity and his relationship with his family. Most interesting is the way he speaks of the violence and tragedy that has surrounded the women, which some attribute to a curse, but which in reality reflects a dynamic of permanent sexist violence. He sometimes struggles to reconcile his personal reflection with that of his family, but it addresses relevant themes that also reflect the influences forcibly transplanted to Brazil.

[Sheffield '25] In the mid-1970s, as a result of the Women's Liberation Movement (WLM), groups of women in the United States decided to establish communities without the presence of men, following what they considered a necessary continuation of those movements. The short film addresses a reunion of former members of one of these communities, but as part of a future feature film, it feels like it skims over some important aspects. This lesbian separatism, predominantly formed by cisgender white women, offers interesting perspectives that deserve to be explored in more depth.

[VdR '25] Director Tomasz Wolski composes one of his archival montages to portray a controversial figure, the Jewish spy Leopold Trepper, who is suspected of having collaborated with the Nazis after his arrest in 1942. Using images from the 1960s and 1970s, which are the director's main source, the character's contradictions are reflected. But at times, the documentary's perspective seems to lean toward a hagiography of the spy, allowing him the last word and fueling doubts about the accusations against him. In the end, there remains the feeling that the mystery surrounding Leopold Trepper will remain unsolved.

[VdR '25] A traumatic coming-of-age story set against a backdrop that reflects the fragility of peace, as tensions surrounding the Yazidis begin to rise again, recalling the genocide in which the Islamic State murdered and kidnapped many of its inhabitants. The young Rashid becomes the spokesperson for a family that has suffered murders and the kidnapping of a sister who is still missing. The enduring ruins of Sinjar reflect an immutable situation that faces a long reconstruction, an uncertain future that ultimately leads to emigration as a painful but inevitable solution.

movie_title: PARA VIVIR: The Implacable Times of Pablo Milanés
cast_names: Pablo Milanés
review_content: [Sheffield '25] A beautiful portrait of the final years of Pablo Milanés' life, estranged from his native Cuba due to an illness whose treatment forced him to live in Spain. But the most relevant part is the depiction of his changing relationship with the hope of the Cuban revolution and his disappointment in the face of repression. This disillusionment, felt by some who embraced a possible future, is dispersed throughout Pablo Milanés' musical career, from his incorporation into the Nueva Trova movement to his break with it in the 1990s. It is also a story that reflects the eternity of his music.

[VdR '25] The directors take inspiration from a novel by B. Traven to show the similarities between the exploitation of Mexican carters in the 19th century and the oppression of capitalism, which also exploits its workers, in this case truck drivers from a small transport company, who adopt the name of the book's protagonist in the documentary. The drivers, under this pseudonym, end up being the anonymous cogs in a system that turns them into invisible work tools that circulate on the roads competing against time.

[VdR '25] The film offers a collective portrait that reflects the isolation of a place that not only seeks to separate from civilization for fear of a misunderstood disease like leprosy, which in the past showed very evident physical scars, but also attempts to create an environment of tranquility that would promote the healing process of patients. The isolation of the San Francisco de Borja Sanatorium, founded by a Jesuit in 1909, was not so much for the protection of the healthy world, but for the protection of the other world of sick people, but at the same time it becomes a kind of prison.

movie_title: National Pride: From Jericho to Gaza
review_content: [VdR '25] The film is structured as a journey that makes several stops to trace the development of the recent history of Palestine and the conflict with Israel, establishing the route of the relationship between Yasser Arafat and Yitzhak Rabin, the two leaders who made a real effort to achieve peace. The journey of Palestinian diplomat Hassan Al Balawi in 2019 traces some of the background to the struggle for self-determination and offers an interesting note on the internal threats of disunity and betrayal that have prevented peace from being achieved even when they were willing to discuss it.

[VdR '25] Three brothers continue the ancient practice of herding sheep through the mountains of North Macedonia during the summer, living alongside twenty sheepdogs in an environment where even domestic animals face the untamed wilderness. As the film's title suggests, the mountain remains unmoved, as it does every year and every summer of transhumance. But the city seen in the distance from the highest peak reveals itself as a yearning full of opportunities. Brand Ferro's cinematography manages to convey the beauty but also the wild spirit of the nature that surrounds the brothers.

[TIFF '25] The minimalist direction reinforces the naturalness of the scenes and provides the necessary space for the actors to develop their characters. The look at reverse immigration portrays what is perceived as a failure from the local perspective and represents an alienation and a sense of suffocation for someone who has lived for years in another society. Perhaps the portrayal of the characters lacks some emotional depth, with metaphors that may seem simplistic; however, set against the backdrop of the desert landscape, the film offers an unusual perspective on the pain of emigration.

cast_names: Olivier Gourmet, Kim Higelin, Bruno Lochet, Marie Kremer, Michaël Erpelding, Francis Renaud, Anne Richard, Jean-Baptiste Lafarge, Daouda Keita, Rod Paradot
review_content: It starts so-so and there is never anything particularly good happening, neither in story or actor performance.     However for what it is, a chase movie, there is a real mastery of the main ingredients: suspense and tension. After the first encounter, there is never a pause, it's a constant chase keeping you on the edge.    And something even better, the "who chases who" is fluctuating all along. Hunting, hiding, defending, counter attacking, and changing loyalties as the situation escalates. This gives a real dynamism to the story.    The boss' monologue being the exact same fake bullshit as real life answers of big corporation was a nice touch.    One stupid thing though, why the hell does the boss even show his face there ?? How could there be any peaceful issue once he directly showed himself to the activists ?  In the same vein, they definitely knew the guys would come to the house, and they didn't even warn the others ?    I was hoping that one of the activists at the house would be an expert, and it would turn into a deadly Home Alone at this point. It went a more realistic and gruesome way.

[Sheffield '25] The director establishes a connection between the personal and the collective narrative in this short film that connects Spanish emigration to Switzerland with the struggle to keep rural areas of Galicia protected from the fires that occur frequently, mainly due to the establishment of non-native species. The two territories establish a dialogue between the personal and the political, which speaks of those who left and those who remain, of the species and humans who have settled in other territories, of solidarity and the collective spirit.

cast_names: Jonny Phillips, Bella Ramsey
review_content: This was a great concept but the movie failed to deliver.

movie_title: An Intimate Evening with Adam Pally
cast_names: Adam Pally, Dave Hill, Max Ash, David Krumholtz
review_content: Alright look. I’ve only seen Pally in the Sonic stuff and apparently IM3. I haven’t seen Happy Endings yet. I didn’t particularly enjoy him in the Sonic stuff, or remember him in Iron Man 3. This was my chance to get him, and I still. Don’t. He still reminds me of Andy era Chris Pratt, with a touch of Fallon in his voice. Like I appreciate all angles of creating to create, of the anti humor, of the deconstruction of performance art especially in the presumption of honesty in stand up acts and intimate evenings. I see those. None of them hit though. He does none of them all that well. He’s cruising on being a loud kinda conventionally attractive mid white guy and it’s supposed to be deep, actually, he’s a trickster. I still don’t get Adam Pally’s appeal. I’m starting to think there’s nothing to get.

movie_title: An Intimate Evening with Adam Pally
cast_names: Adam Pally, Dave Hill, Max Ash, David Krumholtz
review_content: An Intimate Evening with Adam Pally is a fascinatingly offbeat special that straddles the line between stand-up, documentary, and performance art. It’s not your typical laugh-a-minute comedy show—instead, it feels more like an experience. Pally mixes personal reflection, self-aware humor, and a surprising amount of emotional honesty to create something that feels refreshingly different from the usual comedy formula.  If you go in expecting punchlines and crowd work, you might come away underwhelmed. But if you’re open to something more introspective and experimental, this lands as a thoughtful, funny-in-its-own-way exploration of fame, creativity, and self-image. It’s a strange but worthwhile watch.  Rating: 3/5 – 75% – Worth Watching

cast_names: Emmanuelle Lussier Martinez, Bill Rowat, Mark Antony Krupa, Dawn Ford
review_content: This is the middle part of a trilogy yet to be made, so the director explained at the world premiere in Glasgow.  He also claimed that the film was shot in one shot; I'm not convinced but won't dwell on that as I don't really understand his reasons to make this a one-shot film. It came across as a gimmick or a  bit of showing off.  In fact, I thought it worked really well. It made you feel like you were in the house with them;  made especially effective by the sounds of the house.  The story is a good one but, due to some tape recordings that were not so easy to hear, there were a few holes for me.  I guess the prequel will clear that up for me. This was a pretty good horror which, if you have experience with dementia, also has the ability to pull on the heartstrings.  I noticed a couple of camera lights reflecting in the many wall pictures in the house, I suppose there is still time before general release to get rid of those.

cast_names: Rubén Barbosa, Antonello Novellino, Julen Robles, Cristina Gallego
review_content: [Movistar+] An interesting premise that blends different animation styles, but a story that runs out of steam so quickly that the 23 minutes feel long, as if the joke never quite ends. In the development of the story of a director considering making a short film, there are some good ideas that satirize production processes and the hypocrisy of the industry, but they remain mere flashes that never truly stand out. Questions remain about the processes in which AI was used and how it contributes anything beyond mere anecdote to the story.

cast_names: Betsey Brown, Chloe Cherry, Dasha Nekrasova, Ron Farrar Brown, Jimmy Noonan, Ben Tripp, John Borras, Rina Mejia, Liam Sullivan, Preston Christopher Lawrence
review_content: I couldn't finish this.  It's only  80 minutes long, and despite being strange and weird, it was just dull.    Was it original?  The combination, yeah sure.  But there didn't seem to be much I hand not seen before

cast_names: Ashton James, Charlie Ebbs, Shomari Downer, Sharon Forrester, Parveen Kaur, Faizan Khan, Nayo, Angie Reid, Andrea Grant, Neema Nazeri
review_content: Felt like Köln 75 (2025) but without running lol.

cast_names: Joel Torre, Yassi Pressman, Candy Pangilinan, Yayo Aguila, Rafa Siguion-Reyna, Denise Esteban, Gwen Garci, Liz Gonzales, Jonica Lazo
review_content: The beginning of the story is too long and the main story is told very briefly even though it could be told at much longer length.

cast_names: Joel Torre, Yassi Pressman, Candy Pangilinan, Yayo Aguila, Rafa Siguion-Reyna, Denise Esteban, Gwen Garci, Liz Gonzales, Jonica Lazo
review_content: a very good philipino movie I recommended

cast_names: Margaret Cho, Tura Satana, John Waters, David Henry Hwang, Kitten Natividad, Dennis Busch, Pamela Des Barres, Ted V. Mikels, Joshua Grannell, Tara Moss
review_content: A documentary about Tura Satana - what a name - best known for her lead role in _Faster, Pussycat! Kill! Kill!_ (1965). Yeah, there was no way I wasn't going to watch this.  But, man, this is a dark ride, like right from the jump. Tura did not have a happy life, and the doc does not shy away from talking about the abhorrent trauma she faced at a young age.   For someone who is best known for her exploitation work, her life reads like the plot of a rape/revenge story.  That being said, this isn't all misfortune. The doc is well made, very informative, and the tone becomes triumphant toward the end.  "I never try anything, I just do it!" Recommended to fans of cult and exploitation films.

cast_names: Robert Rusler, Monique Parent, Sarah French, Sarah Polednak, Gigi Gustin, Sarah Nicklin, Dazelle Yvette, Ben Stobber, Adam Bucci, Alex Dundas
review_content: Everything about this is bad: The acting, the writing, the camera work, the music, the lighting. All bad.  This has the production quality of a softcore porn... only without the porn. That being said, we do get to see two cocks, one real, one fake.  From the beginning, the story is very confusing. After that, it looks like this is going to be _Eyes Wide Shut_ (1999) meets _Hostel_ (2005). Then it switches gears to be about revenge. Look, the movie doesn't make sense.   If a story is to be found, it centers around a "family," the Edens. How they are related is partially unclear. Are they more of a cult? Well, they're Christian, but it hardly matters. I'm a big fan of religious horror, but this one just failed to do anything with it. Anyway! The family is rich, they live in the desert, and they own a restaurant. Okay, why? Like every other part of this, it doesn't matter.  What's really at the center of this is the Edens have masquerade parties - why do people come to these? Who gets invited? How would you arrange all of this? It all gets pretty absurd the more you think about it. Anyway! We only get the backstory to two of these and it's all very ridicules.   I'm only going to talk about two of the kill scenes: one because of how dumb it was, the other for how weird it was. 1. Well, I'll put it this way, I've never seen a fishing pole used in a horror movie, as the murder weapon. 2. Strap in! This was where the movie took a turn into Alejandro Jodorowsky level of bad filmmaking for me. The Edens lead a guy into the desert, with the hope of beating a "fag" (his words) with a baseball bat. They take him to a tent, and a total leather daddy comes out with a giant LGBTQIA+5678LMNOP%$@ flag - I'm talking the one with all the colors. It was at this point that I was thinking "movie, are you trying to say something? Because you really lack the depth to make any kind of point."  Anyway! This could have been a simple story about a crazy religious cult. This could have been a revenge torture porn story, like the Saw series. This has no business being over two hours. One of the worst movies I have ever seen.

cast_names: Delores Churchill, David Suzuki
review_content: [Sheffield '25] Using only archival footage to give a narrative structure to the events, the film introduces an animated character who provides contextual information, demonstrating its educational intent. Above all, thanks to the excellent editing and outstanding soundtrack, it manages to unfold an interesting reflection on the territorial disputes that remain a structural problem stemming from the consequences of colonialism. At the same time, it demonstrates mutual respect (and perhaps some understanding) between workers and indigenous members, but marked by a deep conflict of interests, which makes it all the more complex.

cast_names: Diana Danielle, Anand Rajaram, Pushpa Narayan
review_content: This is a good film. However, the one major thumbsdown moment was the scene where Dr. Jegha showed the CT scan. Oh my god. First, the scan was upside down. Second, it clearly showed intracranial bleeding, not features of Alzheimer’s at all. This is something Malay films unfortunately keep getting wrong, and it really breaks immersion.  That said, aside from that flaw, the film is solid. I really enjoyed Diana Danielle’s performance. She’s naturally soft-spoken, and that gentleness actually fits the role very well.

cast_names: Mahmoud Hemida, Sherine, Hana Shiha, Tamer Nabil, Ahmed Abdelhamid
review_content: [IFFR '26] With a visual concept of static shots and unique sets that has a certain sitcom feel, this bittersweet comedy about the nightmare of repairing a refrigerator offers a sharp insight into the planned obsolescence that human beings have imposed upon themselves through retirement. The refrigerator that refuses to continue functioning is like a country that is progressively subjected to decay and permanent crisis, maintaining its activity as a form of resistance. It reflects the idea of ​​"Al Qahr," that expression that evokes the feeling of profound powerlessness caused by the historical humiliation experienced by Arab countries.

cast_names: Rosana Matecki
review_content: [Sheffield '25] Through the description of different "dead houses," the film offers a look at the Venezuelan migration crisis, not from the perspective of those who leave but from those who stay: the loss of a son in the 2017 protests without justice, the care of the houses left behind, or the old woman who returns to a village submerged under water by the construction of a hydroelectric plant. The church that could not be demolished is a symbol of the resistance of a people who face the literal erasure of the images of their martyrs from the streets, tattooing their faces on their skin.

cast_names: Jake Weber, Landry Bender, Madison Lintz, Amy Hargreaves, Erik Bloomquist, Carol Cadby, Blaque Fowler, Nikolay Moss, Annette Saunders, Ira Carmichael
review_content: Two teens audit a secretive weekend self-help program; one girl's mom is dating the group's silver tongued leader. Masks, mantras and curated breakthroughs hide nastier business. There are sharp moments and a few punchy gore bits, including an eye watering set piece, but the protagonists are undercooked and the narrative loses shape. It gestures at trauma and control without digging deep, then idles until the next reveal. Fine on a plane, not much to chew after.    If you liked... The Invitation, Faults, Sound of My Voice.

cast_names: Bren Foster, Plutarco Haza, Tania Raymonde, Kris Van Damme, Fabrice Uzan, Louis Mandylor, Roman Phillips
review_content: Ben Foster has the juice. Charismatic, affable, and an absolutely incredible martial artist.   The action is very, very well done, and the characters are fine to fun. However, I felt some of the connecting tissue in-between the action was awkward and stretched thin, with weird stops and starts.   Still worth it to seek out. The first few fights are amazing.

cast_names: Bren Foster, Plutarco Haza, Tania Raymonde, Kris Van Damme, Fabrice Uzan, Louis Mandylor, Roman Phillips
review_content: I was hoping for more continuous action sequences given the premise. The first 40 minutes felt a bit slow, and the buildup probably could’ve been trimmed to help the pacing. Things do start picking up around the 45-minute mark though.

cast_names: Jonathan Boynton-Lee, Noxolo Dlamini, Jack Devnarain, Bouwer Bosch, Thulani Mtsweni, Nthati Moshesh, Stevel Marc, Kaseran Pillay, Clayton Boyd, Sihle Ndaba
review_content: Seriously… it's bad, really bad… bad writing, bad acting, & absolute trash procedurally, but all the compounded stupidity makes for a pretty good time and probably more laugh-out-loud funny than anything that's come out of Hollywood in years.

cast_names: Camille Cottin, Romain Duris, Denis Podalydès, Céleste Brunnquell, Bruno Podalydès, Kester Lovelace, François Orsoni, Fabrice Cals, Stéphanie Pasquet, Myriem Akheddiou
review_content: Like a tequila sunrise made with ouzo: a lot of different elements that don't mix, never gel, and leave you with a bad taste in your mouth.     A woman (the splendid Camille Cottin) sees a Rembrandt painting in a museum so worries about climate change in her nuclear power plant job.     The film means well and nothing, really, because the meaning is lost in all the philosophical posturing. Rembrandt makes as much sense as an existentialist teaching calculus in art class when he embraces the blackboard and weeps.

cast_names: Jessica Rothe, Joseph Cross, Julianna Layne
review_content: Interesting, but not that original. You are kept guessing for most of the film despite a very early reveal. Not all questions were answered for me. The director told us, in Q&A, that he designed the film to be watched several times as there are clues to be found. I'm not sure I will do that anytime soon; I found it quite slow.

cast_names: Tasnia Farin, Mosharraf Karim, Misha Sawdagor, Fazlur Rahman Babu, Arfan Mredha Shiblu, Chanchal Chowdhury
review_content: Poor story, Poor execution. Same shit over & over again

cast_names: Lukman Avaran, Dhyan Sreenivasan, Raveena Ravi, Sheethal Joseph, Navas Vallikkunnu, Vijayaraghavan, Arjun Radhakrishnan, Shanthi Krishna, Abu Salim, Govindh Vasantha
review_content: Starts simple but picks up with solid twists! Decent music & BGM throughout. One-time watch for sure 👍

cast_names: Mana Alkoy, Gönül Ürer
review_content: It's watchable, I guess. The series has low ratings on IMDB, and I'm surprised they're still continuing it. It's the same scenario, always starting from the beginning. If you're going to make a series, there should be sequels. I don't know what they're doing, but I'm most curious about their audience. I think it deserves a 4 out of 10. If you can't find anything else to watch, maybe watch this for horror. I don't recommend it.

review_content: The screenplay and writing by Michela Andreozzi reveal a film of poor quality with an extremely trivial plot. The film is directed in a lackadaisical manner and with absolutely no skill or talent by Stefano Cipani, who completely fails to make the most out of a cast that seems to have been chosen at random and without any logic whatsoever among Italian actors by the casting director (Anna Pennella).

review_content: Boring as fuck. nothing good in this thing.

cast_names: Julia Franz Richter, Reinout Scholten van Aschat, Gerti Drassl, Maria Hofstätter, Erika Mottl, Gerhard Liebmann, Beatrix Brunschko, Inge Maux, Carmen Gratl, Linde Prelog
review_content: Another take on the sect-like village folk horror. It does the job well, having all the right ingredients but it's nothing original either, and a bit boring in the execution.    It looks great and the atmosphere in general is on point. The village women are perfectly creepy. There's a family secret, an isolated village, some weird events and dreams.     However even if not everything needs to be explained in this kind of movie, but a bit of discovery to make the story progress is nice and necessary. And here, there's pretty much nothing.    She goes through these [spoiler]time skips[/spoiler], ok. But then what happens in between ? Is she under control ? Is it another personality ?  And what actually happens when she finds herself in the middle of the forest like this ?  Suddenly the husband is a [spoiler]good little zombie[/spoiler]. How ? Why ?    I think I fell asleep during the [spoiler]childbirth[/spoiler] scene, which is supposed to be the climax, but even that was too long and generic without any information. Or maybe I slept through the important revelation ? Because I don't understand how [spoiler]she just leaves afterwards[/spoiler]. Weren't they after [spoiler]the baby ? They didn't want to kill it ? Or keep it ? They didn't want to keep her there[/spoiler] ?  It seemed to be important since at the very least we see they could kill for it.    In the end, is this a criticism of countryside medical desert ? A metaphor on the alienation that is pregnancy ? Not clear.

cast_names: Julia Franz Richter, Reinout Scholten van Aschat, Gerti Drassl, Maria Hofstätter, Erika Mottl, Gerhard Liebmann, Beatrix Brunschko, Inge Maux, Carmen Gratl, Linde Prelog
review_content: Gave me a "Austrian Midsommar" kinda vibe in the end. Sure, not nearly on that level and left many questions open for answers but in the end I came out mostly satisfied. Some shots are amazing, the sound design is disturbing and some characters, like Tante Paula, are down right scary. The Bene Gesserit cosplays in the end were also pretty damn cool. ;)

cast_names: Kelly Marie Tran, Benedict Wong, Jimmy O. Yang, Aria Kim, Fiona Fu, Ricky He, Cardi Wong, Mark Krysko, Dimitry Tsoy, Tanja Dixon-Warren
review_content: Ambitious and has a noble directive but doesn’t really work at all. It seems like someone wanting to make a horror film because it’s hot right now.

cast_names: Prit Kamani, Ashutosh Rana, Gulshan Grover, Sanjay Mishra, Meghna Malik, Sarah Lockett, Ben Walton-Jones
review_content: Easily the most terrible Hindi movie I've watched in the recent times. Even "Soldier" was better than this cringefest. The heroine is cute, that's about it. Nothing else is a positive.  Outdated 80s plot coupled with some cringey acting by so-called veterans. Awful bgm, forgettable songs, ultimate suckfest of action scenes. I could go on and on, but to save you some time - DON'T WATCH IT, even if you get a free ticket (like me). Time is money.

cast_names: Mari Oliveira, Ravel Andrade, David Santos, Pally Siqueira
review_content: The film didn't turn out at all what I expected. The acting is passable, the effects are good, and the script is classic but too simplistic. I've seen many films and series made in this style. At least the plot is similar or close. Is it watchable? Yes, but only if you can't find anything else to watch and are interested in this type of production. Otherwise, it's not very good. It's only about a 4 out of 10.

[Sheffield '25] The camera pans around the family home from a position on the roof and lingers on some old curtains that block out any light from outside, conveying the feeling of a haunted place, burdened by the weight of the ghosts that inhabit it. It is this ability to convey sensations that distances it from a simple portrait of the self-destructive attitude of an alcoholic mother and becomes a clarifying vision of how addictions affect their environment. It speaks with sensitivity and intensity of the helplessness, responsibilities, and guilt in a family facing illness.

[DOK.fest '25] The film has a somewhat television-like feel, with a simple narrative structure, many shots of the protagonists looking at the horizon, reflecting the lack of activity in the small German town, and a certain melancholic tone that shows a certain parallel between the economic difficulties of a German with a neo-Nazi past and those of young immigrants, all of them trapped in difficult future prospects. The perspectives of the three protagonists reflect the difficulties of integration from different perspectives, but also solidarity as a necessary path.

cast_names: Rose Byrne, Dominic Sessa, Octavia Spencer, Ariana DeBose, Demi Lovato, Simon Rex, Corbin Bernsen, Elsie Fisher, Katie Lee Hill, Joseph Lyle Taylor
review_content: Just like Amanda Ogle’s real-life dilemma, this movie drags on and feels far longer than its one hour and 45 minute runtime. The pacing is off, and the way it’s put together never quite works. While Rose Byrne does her best to hold it together, the script and supporting cast don’t do her any favors.

cast_names: Maya Hawke
review_content: I’m surprised by the low scores here. While taste is subjective, this is objectively a top-tier deep dive. It’s a well-constructed look at the cultural impact of home video, and any fan of the medium should find it incredibly thorough.

cast_names: Maya Hawke
review_content: Interesting run through of video rental shops in pop/film culture, how they're portrayed and how that's changed over time. It was well put together, and generally interesting, but wouldn't consider it overly informative or interesting.    Was it bad? No   Would I recommend it? Probably not
Low production value, but moving story. Not the best acting.

cast_names: Kris Sidberry, Michael McLendon
review_content: I'm not gonna lie, this is eh. Just showed up on YouTube for me but it felt way too long and drawn out for not much payoff. They could've condensed it and cut it at the town scene with maybe a neater ending but like most short films, if you cut this down to keyframes the whole thing would only be a couple minutes long so that's why I gave it a 5.

cast_names: Bakhitzhan Alpeisov, Dulyga Akmolda
review_content: [Fantasia '25] Beyond the more obvious comparisons, the film manages to create solid characters that bring humanity, even in the case of a scatological-looking alien trying to understand the strange society he's arrived in. But it also adds some political touches regarding corruption and authoritarianism that place it in line with other Kazakh films, although in this case addressing a genre uncommon in that small industry. In its modesty as a simple story with some social notes, it ends up working very well within its genre because it manages to compose an absolutely charming story.

movie_title: The Man Who Saw the Bear
cast_names: Pierre Richard, Timi-Joy Marbot, Gustave Kervern, Anny Duperey, Louis-Do de Lencquesaing, Vanessa Liautey
review_content: Like your grandad doing stand-up: endearing, weird and not very funny.     A collection of anecdotes and non sequiturs about old men and a younger man with autism spectrum disorder (played by a neurotypical actor) living in French Occitania (southwest coast).     Look, Pierre Richard (known for his 70's comedies like The Tall Blond Man with One Black Shoe) is 91 years old and hasn't written / directed / starred in a film since 1997. So, if he took the trouble to make this movie at this stage in his life, it had to have some meaning for him. While I didn't necessarily see the same things he saw in this project, it does have a quirky, offbeat quality that made the experience kind of charming.

cast_names: Youssef Akdim, Samy Naceri, Sarah Perles, Nadia Niazi, Mourade Zeguendi, Doudou Masta, Brice Bexter, Younes Benzakour, Jalil Naciri
review_content: [English + French]    🇬🇧🇺🇸 The film opens with a long CGI shot focused on a suit of armor, used only at that moment and disappearing completely from the story afterward: why show it to us? We follow characters discovering a “hidden” room marked by an inscription surprisingly recent for a cave that is surprisingly old. A few meters away, an iron staircase, clearly installed recently, highlights the incompetence of the archaeologists who had, until then, noticed nothing about this discovery.    The protagonist is presented as a hacker, but at no point do we actually see him using his skills: everything happens between two scenes and is summarized in a few rushed lines, awkwardly and without conviction. The story’s technological side is ultimately limited to a few holographic screens during a brief briefing, where we are told there is nothing to fear from Ukrainian computer experts since they now work with organic viruses.    The first use of the hero’s power has no impact whatsoever: in the middle of the street, he flies off leaving behind a faint shockwave that absolutely no one notices, not even the driver who almost hits him. The world seems to continue without any awareness of what is happening.    The dialogue feels straight out of an old PS1 or PS2 game, with forced, artificial voices. The explanatory 3D segments, meant to provide lore about the city of Atlantis, are dated and clumsy. One even attempts a semi-ambitious long take, moving through an entire temple with a completely free-moving camera, but it fails to convince and rather gives the impression that the camera is trying to escape the 3D rendering.    The approximate color grading varies from shot to shot within the same scene, suddenly shifting from a cool tone to a warm one, without any explanation.    One scene shows a clock that could have helped situate the action in time, but the cinematographer forgot to freeze it, giving the impression that a simple few-second scene was filmed over an hour and a half. The film resembles a true minefield of continuity errors: every object that moves or disappears drives another nail into the already well-begun coffin of the story’s coherence.    The few fight scenes barely two and a half for a superhero movie are overacted. Every punch seems trivial, but the opponents’ reactions are exaggerated, giving the illusion of controlled and powerful force. In the end, one has to wonder if this is the first superhero movie where neither the protagonist nor the antagonist exudes any sense of power.    The ending is rushed abruptly, as if the film never found the time to properly present its stakes. One might even fear the arrival of a possible sequel, given how tightly the nearly nonexistent plot is wrapped up—but fortunately, that is not the case. The film concludes with a duel where the cuts between shots are missing and the acting is mediocre, making the fight completely messy.    The film leaves us with a sense of frustration: neither the plot, the characters, nor even the "action scenes" manage to convince, and the movie seems to sink into oblivion as soon as the end credits roll, raising the fear of a post-credits scene hinting at a sequel. Yes, that scene exists, and I’m looking forward to seeing what comes next.    🇫🇷 Le film s’ouvre sur un long plan en CGI centré sur une armure, utilisée uniquement à cet instant et disparaissant totalement du récit par la suite : Pourquoi nous l’avoir montré ? Nous suivonsdes personnages découvrant une salle « cachée » indiqué par une inscription étonnamment récente qui orne le mur d’une grotte étonnamment vieille. À quelques mètres, un escalier en fer, visiblement installé récemment, souligne l’incompétence des archéologues qui n’avaient, jusque-là, rien remarqué de cette découverte.    Le protagoniste est présenté comme un hacker, mais à aucun moment on ne le voit réellement utiliser ses compétences : tout se déroule entre deux scènes et se résume en quelques phrases expédiées, maladroitement et sans conviction. Le côté informatique du récit se limite finalement à quelques écrans holographiques durant un briefing expédié, où l’on nous explique qu’il n’y a rien à craindre des informaticiens ukrainiens puisqu’ils travaillent désormais avec des virus organiques.    La première utilisation du pouvoir du héros se fait sans le moindre impact : en plein milieu de la rue, il s’envole en laissant derrière lui une légère onde de choc qu’absolument personne ne remarque, pas même le conducteur qui manque de le percuter. Le monde semble évoluer sans avoir conscience de ce qui se passe.    Les dialogues semblent tout droit sortis d’un vieux jeu PS1 ou PS2, avec des voix forcées et artificielles. Les passages explicatifs en 3D, censés apporter du lore autour de la cité d’Atlantide, sont datés et maladroits. L’un d’eux tente même un plan-séquence semi-ambitieux, parcourant un temple entier grâce à une caméra totalement libre de ses mouvements, mais il a du mal à convaincre et donne plutôt l’impression que la caméra cherche à fuir ce rendu 3D.    La colorimétrie approximative varie d’un plan à l’autre au sein d’une même scène, passant soudainement d’une teinte froide à une teinte chaude, sans aucune explication.      Une scène présente une horloge, qui aurait pu situer l’action dans le temps mais le chef opérateur a oublié de la bloquer, donnant l’impression qu’une simple scène de quelques secondes a été filmée pendant 1h30. Le film ressemble à un véritable champ de mines de faux raccords : chaque objet qui bouge ou disparaît enfonce un nouveau clou dans le cercueil déjà bien entamé de la cohérence du récit.    Les rares scènes de combat à peine deux et demi pour un film de super-héros sont sur jouées. Chaque coup paraît anodin, mais la réaction des opposants est exagérée, donnant l’illusion d’une force maîtrisée et puissante. À la fin, une question se pose si nous ne sommes pas face au premier film de super-héros où ni le protagoniste ni l’antagoniste ne dégagent la moindre sensation de puissance ?    La fin est expédiée de manière abrupte, comme si le film n’avait jamais trouvé le temps d’exposer correctement ses enjeux. On pourrait même craindre l’arrivée d’une éventuelle partie 2 tant le timing pour conclure l’intrigue, presque inexistante, est serré, mais heureusement, il n’en est rien. Le film se conclut sur un duel où les raccords entre plans sont manquant et le jeu d’acteur médiocre, rendant le combat totalement brouillon.    Le film nous laisse avec un sentiment de frustration : ni l’intrigue, ni les personnages, ni même "les scènes" d’action ne parviennent à convaincre, et le film semble sombrer dans l’oubli dès son générique de fin qui nous amène la crainte d'une scène post générique qui annoncerait une suite. Oui, cette scène existe, et j’ai hâte de voir la suite.
