review_content: Not bad doc about the many different aspects of clowning...many far from the Big Top.

movie_title: Bat Out of Hell: The Musical
cast_names: Sharon Sexton, Luke Street
review_content: Hot garbage! As a fan of Jim Steinman, musical theatre and bad taste camp, this should be for me but it was unforgivably bad, especially the guy playing Strat, just awful.

cast_names: Aiko Beemsterboer, Minne Koole, Roman Derwig, Hannah van Lunteren, Edward Stelder, Leonoor Koster
review_content: [IFFR '25] The approach to the need to adapt to blindness of a teenager who has suffered an accident is approached from a perspective that always places the viewer in the point of view of the protagonist. Sometimes reproducing the world as she sees it, only formed by blurred and diffuse lights, an image similar to that of a windshield wiper placed in front of the lens, and generally without showing the faces of the secondary characters, who are always focused in shots that cut off their physical appearance. Also marked by a kaleidoscopic sound design that reflects the gradual increase in auditory awareness of the protagonist. It is an optimistic story that uses the loss of vision as a metaphor for the futility of our need to be perceived from a gaze of false perfection.

cast_names: Levi Heaton, Isabella Percival
review_content: Was at the world premiere of this and was lucky enough to hear from the director and cast. They explained that they are all mates and their difficulties in making the film on a low budget. Some of the dialogue and performances suggested that this was a debut film but the story is engaging, exciting and pretty well told. I liked it.

[Raindance '25] Dealing with the more than 20,000 Ukrainian children who have been forcibly deported by Russia for re-education is an interesting subject, but the director's insistence on being more emotional than expository means it never fully develops. As in his Oscar-nominated documentary, Evgeny Afineevsky uses animated sequences to show what cannot be shown in images, especially through the testimonies of the young people who participate in the film, and who narrate their experiences, often terrifying. It's a documentary that wants to be inspiring, but falls short in developing a relevant subject, dwelling too much on the emotional shock of the experiences that have traumatized a generation of Ukrainians.

movie_title: I Have Already Died Three Times
cast_names: Jacques Nolot
review_content: [ArteKino '25] It could be seen as a kind of continuation of the film "Avant que j'oublie" (Jacques Nolot, 2007), shot in the real apartment where the director lives, now reflecting on his refusal to sharing his thoughts through cinema. He seems more interested in recounting his personal experiences with the young street children he invited into his home or his memories of cruising nights on the outskirts of Paris. But through his anecdotes, there emerges an interesting portrait of the artist who refuses to be one, of the filmmaker who once longed to be provocative and to reflect on his life.

[IFFR '25] The first cinematic proposal by photographer and experimental artist Sammy Baloji may have difficulty holding attention due to its excessively methodical conception, relying on three narrators and an analytical look at the colonial past and its influence on the present of African populations. But it opens a very suggestive dialogue that unfolds in different narrative layers about the transmission of a capitalist concept that maintains Western dominance over the permanence of extractivism as the global economic base. While it offers a more anthropocentric view in the first two parts, the third is shown in a more poetic way, and transmits a deeper tone through surround sound.

cast_names: Raquel Castro, Paula Só, Sara de Castro, Américo Silva, Isabel Costa, Nuno Nunes, João Miller Guerra, João Rosas, Isabel Abreu, Margarida Bento
review_content: [ArteKino '25] Drawing on a personal experience, it speaks in a way of loneliness amidst a crowd, through the story of a production manager who receives news of her mother's death while filming. In a grieving process that is generally silent, fueled by alcohol and a somewhat extroverted attempt to fit in, Helena's journey nevertheless feels very solitary. The film feels too detached, emotionally weighed down by its desire to approach reality, and lacks the impact of the director's previous film.

cast_names: Prithviraj Sukumaran, Shammi Thilakan, Anu Mohan, Teejay, Suraj Venjaramoodu, Dhruvan, Pramod Veliyanad, Santhosh Damodharan, Kiran Peethambaran
review_content: The movie made me go from 'Ooo, I like this' to 'Okay?' to 'Cool' to 'Wtf am I watching?' to 'What is even happening?' to 'Ok. Ig.'

cast_names: Jim Lujan, Maureen McElheron, Ana Sophia Colón, Daniel Kaufman
review_content: A bit crazy animation western story. Really fast paced, with an approximative drawing style. Lots of small funny ideas here and there.

cast_names: Alyssa Marvin, Patrick Wilson, Margaret Cho, Elizabeth Marvel, Molly Ringwald, Yul Vazquez, Bill Camp, Nuha Jes Izman, Grace Reiter, Jesus Del Orden
review_content: This has a lot of good going for it. It tackles some emotional beats really well and poses some interesting points of view. The ambition kind of crushes the movie in the end because it’s an impossible subject to tackle in a satisfying way but that’s also kind of admirable.

cast_names: Maryam Naghiyeva, Elshan Abbasov
review_content: [Venice '25] In his latest film, the director embraces what could be considered experimental cinema, no longer interested in presenting clearly defined characters or representing reality as it is, instead adopting a mannerist, abstract perspective. While the metaphorical approach that Hilal Baydarov employs works effectively as a visual dialogue with the viewer, it falters due to its imprecise and superficial writing. Although the film is rich in images that create fascinating representations of a symbolic world, it ultimately falls short of its intended impact, becoming repetitive and superficial.

[Cannes '24] In her fable set in an imaginary world, director Hala Elkoussy creates a timeless story with a markedly anachronistic design, reminiscent of Italian films from the 1950s, especially through the use of an analog texture of 16 mm. So the film ends up addressing themes such as abuse, authoritarianism, violence or hopelessness after the Arab Spring, but surrounding them with a visual exuberance that is enriched by the texture of black and white cinematography. This inventiveness takes over the story too much, so that on a narrative level it pales between the metaphors and symbologies that it intends to represent.

cast_names: Domhnall Gleeson, Gayle Rankin, Grant O'Rourke, Emun Elliott, Michelle Gomez, John Hannah, Paddy Kondracki, Annabella Forbes, Dave Flanagan
review_content: Really fun and thoughtful film. A very well done balance of tone between the humor and the deeper drama at play. The power of storytelling.

For anyone interested in music, interested in history, or connected to the worlds greatest atrocity, this is a good watch.    There's some weirdly enthusiastic young musicians, tempered with the some comments from survivors, and some beautiful music, all coming together to personalise stories of ordinary people in an extraordinary situation    But if none of this appeals, this is probably going to be a hard watch

cast_names: Pedro Izquierdo
review_content: [Visions du Réel '24] Over four years, the director has accompanied Sara in a moment of transformation between carefree youth and adulthood, placing in a hybrid perspective in which the metanarrative emerges showing the the rehearsals of a project in which Sara plays herself. What the film does well is convey that feeling of anxiety of a youth that has to give up carefreeness to face adulthood, a coming-of-age in which Sara is swept away by a profound transformation almost without realizing it, and that adopts the concept of social class change from authors like Annie Ernaux. Although Sara's journey is sometimes too noisy, the film manages to maintain the balance between fiction and documentary, even when the protagonist herself questions the naturalness of certain scenes, but above all it leaves a trace of melancholy around the inevitable loss of youth.

cast_names: Katia Pascariu, Cătălin Dordea
review_content: [Locarno '25] The director draws on a sense of punk humor already present in the script that inspired it to tell the story of a woman obsessed with an old singer who actually only performs in seedy dives. Despite the complex themes it addresses, both from a political perspective and from a more personal perspective, what's interesting is that it does so from a playful and fun concept that plays with absurd moments and dialogues full of direct and imperfect humor. At times, it seems as if this narrative structure gets lost in its own concept, but the film also has a very distinct personality.

[Venice '25] There's a careful use of color, and planning that frames with meaning and significance, which for a debuting director is a good way to demonstrate her skills. But the film struggles with a narrative and characters that never become as transcendent as they claim, failing to sustain reflection for long. It offers a look inside an apartment where two women with different pasts and surely equally different futures find a way to understand each other and to understand that, despite everything, they are similar.

movie_title: The Legend of the Happy Worker
cast_names: Josh Whitehouse, Thomas Haden Church, Colm Meaney, Meagan Holder, Amy Shiels, Rhys Mitchell, Nathan Stevens, Diane D. Griffith, Althea Sam, Jason K. Wixom
review_content: [Locarno '25] It's not easy to find David Lynch's mark on that film, even though it used some of the codes of popular genres, surrounding them with darkness and surrealism. Presented as one of those colorful and cheerful musicals set in the West, it benefits from a visual aspect that plays with anachronisms and silly humor to ironize idealism, colonization, capitalism, and labor. There are carefully crafted elements within a fable that never ends up being too relevant, no matter how much it tries to stimulate parallels between the past and the present. And the suggestive beginning of that first scene, in which the reason for the action is questioned, ends up developing into a series of sequences that are too light to have a deterrent effect on the ease with which its premises are quickly forgotten.

movie_title: Ha Gom: The Darkness of the Soul
cast_names: Jirawat Puttamma, Yasaka Chaisorn
review_content: - Ha Gom tries to be a social commentary film, but it ends up using old ideas that we’ve seen many times before.  - This movie is supposed to be horror, but it’s not scary at all.  - The guys in this movie are so good-looking!

cast_names: Kabby Borders, Chantey Colet, Wynne VanBuskirk, Kara Vasseur, Rachel Petsiavas, Stephanie Hong, Don Worley, Skylan Kimbrell
review_content: If you want a movie you can roast heavily the entire time, then have I got the movie for you…

cast_names: Ashley Hays Wright, David Owen Wright
review_content: Nothing to say here.It was just horrible!

cast_names: Logan Wallace, Andrew Thomas, Stuart Maxheimer, Cashmere Moniqué, Dylan DeVane, Austin J. Rhodes, Kellan Rudnicki
review_content: For a low budget film it did have a lot of scares

cast_names: Danny Trejo, Veronica Falcón, Suleka Mathew, Andrew Gray, Taylor Treadwell, Omi Vaidya, Ranjita Chakravarty, Jaspal Binning
review_content: I’m basically a sucker for these super cliché and unoriginal sports movies. This pretty much has every standard trope you can think of. The acting is pretty average at best. The lead actor felt very wooden but that’s fine for a movie like this. The heart is obviously in the right place and it was kind of relatable for me. I enjoyed the story enough, I liked the relationships and the training. The fight choreography left a lot to be desired and the final fight is not great. I’m probably a bit high with my score but because I like these kinds of movies I’ll give it a slight passing grade. I still don’t think I’ll be running out to rewatch it anytime soon.

cast_names: Penelope Sangiorgi, Matteo Pasquini, Francesca Vettori, Donatella Bartoli
review_content: The script progresses incredibly slowly. I got bored while watching it and even fast-forwarded through it many times. The acting isn't very good. The script is heavy, and the effects are just passable. I actually thought this movie would be good when I started watching it. I generally think movies starting with "the" are usually good. Is it watchable? I think it's a waste of time. You can watch it if you can't find anything else to watch. Still, thanks for your effort.

cast_names: Ondi Timoner, Aloe Blacc
review_content: The wildfires in Southern California in January 2025 definitely warrant a documentary. However, the execution in “All the Walls Came Down” is not very effective. The short doc feels weirdly unfocused. Instead of zeroing in on one angle or at least following a clear narrative thread, it jumps back and forth. As a result, hardly any of the questions viewers might have while watching the doc get answered. Therefore, I'd say the film is mostly a missed chance to say something meaningful about the disaster.

cast_names: Sarah French, Jed Rowen, Devanny Pinn, Heather Grace Hancock, Jake Red, Fritzi Marth, Sarah Nicklin, Ben Stobber, Tyler Gallant, Dave Sheridan
review_content: It was bad, had potential, but the acting, etc. was lacking. However, I did watch until the end. By the way, it was not the worst movie I have seen; I have seen much worse that I turned off after 15 minutes.

cast_names: Josh Fadem, James Urbaniak, Barbara Crampton, Tipper Newton, Vera Drew, John Ennis, Ginger Gilmartin, Mary Buss, Ben Hall, Chris Freihofer
review_content: I liked this; it's an unusual film. The casting is daring, but it works. The best elements of this are the visual and sound effects. It is clearly meant to look like a 70s film with plenty of psychedelic influence; shame they didn't go all-in as smartphones on a grainy film irked me a bit. It's an odd choice for Halloween Frightfest, but it's a good film, so I'm not complaining.

cast_names: Josh Fadem, James Urbaniak, Barbara Crampton, Tipper Newton, Vera Drew, John Ennis, Ginger Gilmartin, Mary Buss, Ben Hall, Chris Freihofer
review_content: [Fantasia '25] Evoking the serial killer films of the 1980s, with references to Brian DePalma and David Lynch, the director constructs a hybrid film less interested in the actual crime itself than in the psychological aspects of guilt and mental manipulation that ultimately give rise to extremist gender ideologies that respond to repressed sexualities. But it does so by incorporating new technologies as instruments of control, in an unorthodox blend of comedy and techno-thriller that always remains on an equidistant path. Electronic music, digital effects, and hallucinations make up a film that is as strange as it is entertaining.

cast_names: Jeremy Sumpter, Laura Ashley Samuels, John Pirruccello, Rae Gray, Patrick Zielinski, Patrick Agada, Israel Idonije, Jim Johnson, Antoine McKay, Nina Ganet
review_content: Worst ending ever for an otherwise crappy movie

cast_names: Hannah Mae Beatty, Abbas Wahab
review_content: The acting's not so great but the story's wild. Definitely better than I expected going in.

cast_names: Bryan Larkin, Armin Karima, David Hayman, Douglas Russell
review_content: Was just at the world premiere with many of the cast, the director, writer etc. Hearing about how the film was made gives you an appreciation of the difficulties and makes it hard to be critical.  It's not my type of film though, some corny lines very early on got me worried but I needn't have been; it soon got better and the performances made up for a story with a couple of weak spots. What I did like was that I was always trying to guess the ending, would there be a twist? what will it be? Despite the whole film being shot in one prison cell, it was engaging enough.

movie_title: I Fell in Love with a Z-Grade Director in Brooklyn
cast_names: Estevan Muñoz, Ui Mihara, Lissa Carandang-Sweeney, Daniel Martin Berkey, Larry Fessenden, Lloyd Kaufman, Johnny Zito, Christopher Kahler
review_content: [Fantasia '25] The meta quality is the funniest in a film that could have had a much more elaborate script, because it ends up being an overly simple story that uses the lack of communication as a humorous resource. The director uses the tropes of the horror genre that the protagonists film, mixing them with those of the romantic comedy in an intelligent way. And it charmingly reflects the atmosphere of young filmmakers who dream of recognition while shooting films that will end up in underground circuits, in a city like New York that has space for the big neon lights of Times Square and for the dreams that are forged in a Brooklyn neighborhood.

[Berlinale '26] The documentary becomes a personal journey in which the director attempts to understand her alcoholic brother, not as a childhood hero and popular teenager, but as his current self—more chaotic yet equally fascinating—through a process of understanding addiction itself, its roots, and its essence. But along the nearly two-hour journey that unfolds in a film of considerable creative freedom, there are some detours that seem unnecessary and certain conclusions that are debatable.

cast_names: Sumeet Thakur, Indira Tiwari, Chitrangada Satarupa, Murari Kumar, Soumendra Bhattacharya
review_content: [Raindance '25] Through a disturbing atmosphere and a persistent soundtrack, the director manages to create an esoteric environment while simultaneously crafting social commentary on the pornography of poverty, but without managing to make the two elements find a way to coexist within a balanced narrative. The film aims to offer an atmospheric experience, but this pretense sometimes clouds a perspective that seeks to be more resonant and relevant. There is a creative and original visual concept, and an interesting elaboration of disturbing environments that are underscored by a constant music, but it fails to fully unite the supernatural background with the denunciation of social realities.

A society of clay figures. They worship a hand shaped divinity, their creator we can assume. One day one of them finds a cut finger, bringing chaos into the society. Until one who seem to be the high priest, [spoiler]kills the one who found it and uses the finger as new divinity[/spoiler].    No words, no explanation. Beyond the story I take it as a criticism of religions that always find a way to manipulate and control the masses, even if their core has been shaken. But that may be a biased opinion.    The design is ok, a bit crude, but that fits the story.

cast_names: Rose Dehgan, Chloë MacLeod, Talisa Mae Stewart, Cassie Collis, Matthew Graham, Atrin Saghafi, Asal Torabi, Madi Drescher, Mackenzie Smart, Caylee Watrin
review_content: [Fantasia '25] Like any immigrant, Yasamin faces a duality between the customs of her family and birthplace at home and the need to adapt to a different cultural environment when she attends high school. Although the balance between the first part of the story and a more chaotic third act is sometimes not entirely achieved, it skillfully uses the genre as a narrative tool to reflect on more relevant themes. However, by introducing a demonic figure instead of delving deeper into the protagonist's obsession with fitting in, it somewhat reduces the effectiveness of the message it seeks to convey.

cast_names: David Millbern, Calix Quan, Mel England, Barbera Ann Howard, Derek Ringold
review_content: The film fails to leave any lasting impression, offering little beyond the visual appeal of its attractive cast. While some of the actors are undeniably easy on the eye, the story itself is dull and uninspired.   The plot points managed to stir not much excitement or emotional investment. Ultimately, my experience felt like a waste of time, with no redeeming qualities beyond its cast’s appearance.

A narrative and visual mess. pretty much in line with his previous work. You can recognize the style with japanese narration over a parchment illustrating the story (but just in the beginning).    It's very fast paced with original designs and very bright colors, ok watch as it is short enough that it doesn't become unbearable.    Now is there really a story to understand underneath... maybe ? Is it worth trying... maybe not ?

cast_names: Patrick Voss Davis, Johnny Zito
review_content: Weird stuff, a driver is looking for a parking spot. Apparently he's been at it for hours. And it seems natural, so this is the way this world works since he picks up a hitch-hiker that doesn't find it weird and will just help find a spot.  As it goes the guy becomes more of a nuisance, but instead of kicking him he keeps picking others that take all the space in the car and are becoming more and more invasive.  Until [spoiler]he ends up in the trunk while the six other guys go on, one driving, the others telling why he can't park here or there.[/spoiler]    This is absolutely weird and short enough that it doesn't get annoying. Nothing special in the realization beside the idea though.

cast_names: Ross Noble
review_content: This one wasn’t too bad, the second half was much stronger than the first half.   The first half leant more into the comedy side of comedy horror, with lots of dogging jokes. The second half is where it picks up with more action and gore. It looks like they saved the budget for the second half of the movie.   I’m genuinely surprised by the acting, I watch a lot of low budget movies and this is up there with the better acting. Some of these faces looked so familiar but I couldn’t figure out where I’ve seen them before haha.   I would’ve loved to see more of Colin Baker and Ross Noble though, Johnny Vegas got a decent amount of on screen time which was good. But Ross Noble specifically got a bit of a raw deal with his part haha.

cast_names: Dilan Gwyn, Feyyaz Duman, Nicola Perot
review_content: [IFFR '26] With a certain tendency towards family drama featuring common characteristics and latent conflicts, the film relies on an X-ray of the expatriates' inner lives and the family ties that connect the past and the present. Returning to some themes addressed by the director in previous short and feature films, he finds in the whispers of that song idea that evokes the title and the reference to the poet Langston Hughes, some new paths, open to exploring the effects of generational trauma at the margins of the diaspora.

cast_names: Renate Reinsve, Helene Bjørneby, Numan Acar, Lillian Müller
review_content: [IFFR '26] tells a transformative journey whose main reference is in its title, but the constant interplay of contrasts can be frustrating, juxtaposing the nuances of mysticism enveloped in dance and song with the incredulous gaze of two sisters reunited on the island of Gran Canaria after the death of their ex-alcoholic mother who ultimately embraced spirituality. Different forms of colonization coexist in this story of self-discovery, from the enduring presence of the caves where the ancestral inhabitants of the Canary Islands lived to the invasion of all-inclusive tourist resorts, but the protagonists' emotional journey never finds a harmonious balance.

[VdR '25] The director returns to the search for the invisible Mokélé-Mbembé creature that she told about in the film "L'hypothèse du Mokélé-M'Bembé" (2011), now offering three perspectives: that of the Western cryptozoologist Michel Ballot, that of the locals, and that of the women, who are fed up with men appropriating their discoveries. They thus suffer a double abuse: from colonialism and from the men who relegate them to the second place. But there is no real connection between the narratives, remaining as inserts that demand a greater female role without achieving it in the film itself.

cast_names: Chloe Cherry, Felipe Di Poi
review_content: One of the best "so bad it's good" horror movies I have ever... Experienced is the correct word. I was rollin in my seat from laughter. Very Evil Dead vibes, and the movie totally knows it. Anybody who watches this should have a fantastic time.

At the Cinekid film festival in Amsterdam, I accompanied a group of primary school kids (11-12 years old) to this documentary. The filmmaker did a great job of connecting superheroes, such as Batman, to real-life people who, in the filmmaker's eyes, are heroes. We see the story of WW II survivors, a refugee from Gaza, and a social worker who helps troubled youth, and we hear their experiences and motivations. We see how they managed to do what they did and how certain events shaped their lives and personalities.    All in all, it was great to see a documentary that helps youngsters (and even adults, to be honest) find inspiration to do good.

cast_names: Isis Valverde, Marianna Santos, André Ramiro, Marco Pigossi, Caco Ciocler, Leopoldo Pacheco, Dudu de Oliveira, Clarissa Kiste
review_content: Some houses are built to protect the people inside them, but end up revealing exactly what those people are trying to hide from themselves. The safe space turns into a trap, the refuge becomes exposure, and the architecture starts working like a silent mirror of human fragility. “Quarto do Pânico,” directed by Gabriela Amaral Almeida, builds its whole dramatic setup around that kind of spatial idea. By adapting a story audiences already know into a Brazilian context, the film leans on the strength of a contained suspense structure with few characters and a single enclosed environment, trying to turn the house into something almost alive within the narrative. The result is a movie that shows solid control of atmosphere and pacing in several moments, but struggles to fully justify itself as a reinterpretation, especially because it stays too closely tied to the structure that came before it.  Narratively, the film follows Mari (Isis Valverde) and her daughter Bel (Marianna Santos) after moving into a sophisticated house equipped with a high-level security system, including the panic room itself. The home invasion carried out by a trio of criminals quickly turns that space into the dramatic center of the story, pushing the plot forward in a nearly real-time progression built on the rising tension between intruders and victims. Gabriela Amaral Almeida handles this kind of domestic suspense with confidence, organizing the flow of events well and using confinement as the story’s main dramatic engine. The house, often filmed with low lighting and framing that emphasizes corridors, doors, and transitional spaces, takes on an almost active role in the narrative. There’s a clear effort to turn architecture into a source of threat, creating a sense of vulnerability that works especially well during the early invasion sequences. Still, that spatial construction rarely unfolds into deeper psychological layers, staying mostly in the realm of functional suspense.  That limitation becomes even more noticeable in the dramatic construction of the protagonist. Isis Valverde carries the film with a convincing presence, balancing fragility and determination throughout. Her performance provides the emotional weight needed to keep the audience engaged, especially since the script doesn’t offer enough material to fully explore the character’s inner dimension. The attempt to connect Mari to the trauma of her husband’s death feels like a promising element, but it’s only briefly suggested and never really affects the story’s dramatic progression. That lack of psychological depth weakens the emotional core of the narrative, reducing conflicts that could have enriched the suspense into simple background information. As a result, the film ends up depending heavily on Valverde’s performance to maintain its dramatic intensity.  Among the antagonists, Marco Pigossi delivers an unstable and unpredictable presence that initially feels promising but ends up weakened by questionable script choices and by a physical characterization that never fully convinces. The makeup for his injuries, for example, shows clear technical limitations and directly affects the credibility of moments that should heighten tension. André Ramiro, on the other hand, gives a more consistent performance as Benito, especially in how the film explores his moral ambiguity. His presence introduces an interesting layer of empathy within the group of intruders, creating an effective contrast with the violence of the situation. Still, that dramatic layer doesn’t get enough space to develop more fully. Marianna Santos, meanwhile, struggles to make Bel feel like a truly expressive character within the story, which weakens the central mother-daughter relationship and reduces the emotional impact of several sequences that depend on that dynamic.  Fábio Mendes’ screenplay reveals what’s probably the film’s biggest weakness. The narrative progression feels too rushed, and the dialogue rarely has time to deepen conflicts or build more complex relationships between the characters. Instead of exploring the psychological possibilities of confinement and threat, the script chooses a more direct and surface-level approach, prioritizing plot movement over dramatic development. That sense of urgency keeps the film from creating truly memorable suspense moments, turning potentially dense situations into functional episodes. Even when the film tries to introduce elements specific to the Brazilian context, like urban violence and the paranoia surrounding home security, those ideas remain underdeveloped and work more like references than real dramatic drivers.  Visually, the direction shows competence in building atmosphere, but it struggles to create a strong aesthetic identity of its own. The Brazilian house has a cleaner, more contemporary design, but it’s less expressive as a dramatic space. The technological potential of that modern environment, including security systems and digital devices, is rarely explored in creative ways within the narrative, which feels like a missed opportunity to update the material for a current context. Instead of reinventing the suspense dynamics with new storytelling tools, the film mostly follows already established paths, reinforcing the sense that it stays structurally close to its earlier reference.  In the end, “Quarto do Pânico” works as an effective suspense exercise in several moments, supported by confident direction and a strong central performance from Isis Valverde. Still, its strong attachment to the original narrative model keeps it from developing a more distinct identity of its own. The few structural changes it introduces don’t significantly reshape the experience, and the lack of deeper psychological development limits the dramatic reach of the project. Even so, it remains an interesting entry within recent Brazilian cinema, capable of engaging viewers through the tension of its premise, even if it leaves the lingering feeling that there was room for a bolder reinterpretation more deeply rooted in its own context.

cast_names: Joan Collins, Nadia Bjorlin, Trent Garrett, Toby-Alexander Smith, Jacob Young, Hana Vagnerová, Simon Cotton, Jim Borstelmann
review_content: Badly written, badly acted, cringeworthy plot twists. Special mention to Joan Collins for managing to stand up or lean against things.

cast_names: You Chea-myung, Moon Chae-won, Seo Young-hee, Solar, Cha Sun-woo, Sumin, Seo Ji-soo, Son Ju-yeon
review_content: To summarize, it's a film that tells different stories experienced by different people, all starting from the same point. But it's so complicated, so disjointed, that I couldn't understand what it was trying to say. The ending is even more absurd, in my opinion. Each story is watchable on its own, but the film as a whole is very, very confusing. Also, East Asian culture is very different from ours. Maybe that's why it seemed so complicated to me. It's as if the person who edited the film didn't put any effort into it, as if something was deliberately left out. I gave it a 5 out of 10. In short, it's like a Korean version of an American horror movie, but half-baked. Still, thanks for your efforts.

cast_names: You Chea-myung, Moon Chae-won, Seo Young-hee, Solar, Cha Sun-woo, Sumin, Seo Ji-soo, Son Ju-yeon
review_content: Several stories. With a small link in between them all.    1) A guardian tree that, once it has tasted blood, spread pollen that infects people, making its flower bloom out of them.  Great start. A good dark ambiance, some mystery, some gore. Would have been great if everything was like that.    2) A woman steals beauty treatment packages from her neighbour, that happen to be full organ replacements.  Weird beauty products that come with a price. Not a very original theme, specially in Asiatic cinema, but it's done well, has a totally different vibe from the first one, more energetic, more colourful, and with extra comedic stuff.    3) A woman buying a ghost to help her daughter's studies.  Yet another very Asiatic theme. A bit less interesting in the realization, except for the ghost market part. But yeah, school ghost, couldn't do without I imagine. Barely average, specially comparing with the other parts.    4) Cops chasing a guy.   This was such a downgrade from the beginning that I'm not even sure I saw it completely, I micro slept several times and don't remember much.    5) Girls exploring an haunted school building.  Very typical, completely copied on Ring (it's even said). Better than the cops part, but not that much.    It then comes together, linking the pieces between the different parts, but either it was a bit messy, or I micro slept a bit more and missed some stuff.    Great potential, the first two parts are great, the way all is subtly interconnected is good, but in fine it looks more like a cheap Netflix short series than actual cinema.

cast_names: Will Poulter, Noah Centineo, Elise Kibler, Emily Meade, Danny Wolohan, Sylvie Mix, Nicole Stubbs
review_content: I like what this is going for and the pacing of the movie seems to be deliberate to put you in the character’s place, but overall it’s kind of a miss. I like Will Poulter but his lack of presence holds the movie back

[Locarno '25] Shown unedited, the images taken from 2001 recordings in Gaza in search of a man with whom the director shared an Israeli cell in 1989, reflect glimpses of daily life in a territory that, despite everything, remained a place crushed by Israeli attacks. This found-footage documentary shows images of a past that may already be irretrievable, but it serves to counteract forgetting and the existence of a people whose identity is meant to be erased. Reflecting the similarities and differences between the past and the present is a way of fighting against the fabricated narratives that seek to erase memory.

[ArteKino '25] It portrays youth facing war as a constant threat that, when it truly manifests, always ends up deeply shaking their roots and identity, especially when exile and refuge in other countries occur. It could be the portrait of a lost generation, shrouded in rubble and scars, robbed of a normality now punctuated by the sound of bombs or news from social media. It is a story of resistance, but one that is enveloped in irrelevant reflections and never quite grasps the true meaning of its message.
Tense and scary story but the film never delves deeper into some of the questions posed. The best part is the pettiness between the Sky Pirate Sahib and the other rescuer Ali. Give them a sitcom

cast_names: Teele Kaljuvee-O'Brock, Johanna-Aurelia Rosin, Hannah Gross, Ciaron Davies, Denise Moreno, Steve Vanoni, Katariina Unt, Ivo Uukkivi, Anne Paluver, Sissi Nylia Benita
review_content: [Fantasia '24] Miguel Llansó reflects on the future of a humanity that is increasingly connected to new technologies and devoid of its own nature, referring to transhumanization, that aspiration to improve brain capacities through biotechnology and nanotechnology. But the most reflective concepts about transhumanization are connected to the experience of change that a teenager experiences. It is the director's most ambitious film, not only because of its production levels, but because of the complexity of its approaches.

cast_names: Sara Minami, Aoi Yamada, Oshirō Maeda, Akari Takaishi, Takumi Saitoh, Joe Kujo, Fuku Suzuki, Mizuki Maehara, Marina Mizushima, Junsei Motojima
review_content: It starts as a generic Japanese urban legend horror. However, it is cut in segments focusing on the different victims. It never lingers too long on the same characters and quickly switches context. As story goes there actually are a lot of twists that completely change the story. This makes it really shine compared to what I expected.    In the beginning, Mag Mag is a female ghosts that targets male students she's in love with and kills them, pretty basic.    First switch is when Sanae, a girl with an unrequited love for one of the victims starts investigating. after witnessing his death. It's funny that, despite being quite recent, everybody seems to know about Mag Mag. Newspaper articles, youtube testimonies, even people knowing who the ghost is.    Then a big twist when we see [spoiler]Sanae was a crazy stalker[/spoiler], and we're talking rarely seen level of crazy weirdo.    After that the next victim is an older married man, breaking the student pattern. Except from Hiroshi (Sanae's love), they all seem to be assholes and may have deserved some revenge.    But twist again, the next target is just [spoiler]a child[/spoiler].    Now we've seen several time who the ghost is supposed to be. Boom: [spoiler]she's actually alive[/spoiler]!    Getting closer to the truth, she might have started it using [spoiler]a weird tree in a shrine, then other people did the same[/spoiler].    Final twist, in the end it was all cause by a [spoiler]jealous guy that didn't like seeing lovey couples in restaurant[/spoiler].    This is a wild ride, quite a lot of unexpected twists, in a genre where it's not really the norm. Can't say it is really scary, but the funny parts are quite good. The whole Sanae part is probably the best one, not only on the [spoiler]crazy stalker part[/spoiler], but the traps she sets for the ghost are excellent too.    Overall it still speaks about the omnipresent bullying culture in Japan. I've been reading mangas  and seeing movies denouncing it for 30 years now, and it is still a major anchor of japanese media culture. Crazy that nothing has changed in all this time.

cast_names: Sara Minami, Aoi Yamada, Oshirō Maeda, Akari Takaishi, Takumi Saitoh, Joe Kujo, Fuku Suzuki, Mizuki Maehara, Marina Mizushima, Junsei Motojima
review_content: I was surprised that the Japanese horror filmmakers don't seem to have advanced since the time Ringu and Ju-on introduced the Western audiences to the, then scary, long-haired scruffy girl creeping around in a freaky way. I also felt that this was targeted at the YA market and it may go down well with anyone who doesn't mind very ham acting. There was some interesting content but it felt jammed in for the sake of it.

cast_names: Clarisa Navas
review_content: [VdR '25] The protagonist of this documentary grows in his own environment through a human perspective that establishes a permanent identification with the creator and her work. Thus, we witness not only Ángel's changes, but also the transformation of a cinematic project that increasingly focuses on itself, crossing the boundaries of documentary to embrace autofiction. There is something of Helena Trestíková's "time-lapse documentaries," only the Czech director doesn't need three and a half hours to create portraits filmed over several decades.

cast_names: Jane Merrow, Laurence R. Harvey, Lawrie Brewster, Jonathan Hansler, Michael Daviot, Stephen Kerr, Nick Ford
review_content: Amicus is back!    Amicus is back for the first time in 46 years with In the Grip of Terror. And just like classic Amicus, we have another anthology with 3 different segments.     The movie was well written and the acting was great for the most part, especially the acting from Megan Tremethick and Jonathan Hansler who did an outstanding job. The combination of excellent music, beautiful costumes, set designs, camera angles and lighting brought everything together to make this feel like it was a late 60s horror movie.     The movie did come with some downsides, albeit minor ones. I felt like some of the actors were too young to play some of their characters, it felt odd having such young looking men in certain professions. Also, as much as I loved the music and it played a big part in giving that classic horror vibe, it was overused. There were several scenes in the movie that could've done with no music at all, letting ambience and the dialogue take over for a change.     The biggest issue I had was near the end of the final segment, [spoiler] the sudden change to a musical number felt very out of place, it certainly didn't need to be as long as it was either. [/spoiler]    Overall, Lawrie Brewster and the team did a great job at bringing Amicus Productions back. I can't wait to see what more is to come from them

genres: mystery, thriller, romance, comedy, crime, horror
cast_names: Jack Dunphy, Frank V. Ross, Nina Hartley, Ginger Lynn Allen, Trevor Dawkins, Paul Gordon
review_content: [Fantasia '25] Like a parody of 70s porn films, this story about a sex worker caught up in serial murder has less explicit sex scenes than they seem and some sordid moments that reflect on the wave of conservatism sweeping the United States, with authority figures portrayed as corrupted elements. There's a sexual insouciance that conveys the feeling of an almost mystical experience, but it's shattered when confronted with a hypocritical society. The director expresses the political nature of open intimacy, even if it ultimately comes off as too corny.

cast_names: Andrew Lam, Will Or Wai-Lam, Christine Ng Wing-Mei, Chloe So Ho-Yee, Lo Meng, Alice Fung So-Bor, Anson Chan, Ng Siu-Hin, Ken Law Ho-Ming, Philip Keung Ho-Man
review_content: [Fantasia '25] The mixing of players from different generations on an eSports team makes for some amusing relationships, but the film ultimately takes the classic sports-themed route without managing to be particularly exciting. Showing the in-game avatars as real actors allows for some nice action sequences that alternate between first-person and third-person viewpoints, creating a spectacular effect. But the construction of the game's environment seems too lazy, and the character development outside of it ends up being too superficial.

[VdR '25] Photographer Monica Strømdahl's debut documentary avoids poverty pornography by taking an intimate look at a family that, although plagued by the consequences of alcoholism, distances itself from normal family dynamics. Through parents who care for their son but are unable to escape the cycle of addiction, it is the story of Mikal, a child for whom this way of life is already a psychological trauma, that raises a reflection on the failure of a society that is unable to guarantee access to housing to prevent a fractured childhood from developing in environments like this.

cast_names: Belle O'Hara, Eric Roberts, Kevin Rooney, Sheldon Larry, Jake Berry
review_content: Tyson is the Temu version of John Wick.

The only reason I watched this movie was because it was Joe’s choice for our weekly movie swapping. This was a very poorly made movie. These super low budget, faith-based Christian movies aren’t normally that good but this was especially bad. The heart was in the right place and it meant well but it failed. It’s a sports movie about an alcoholic head coach. The potential was there but the script was so incompetent. I couldn’t get over the obvious flaws in the script. It seriously felt like the writer of this movie has never watched a football game or understand how the game works. The addiction aspect also felt so tacky and poorly told. The acting was pretty horrible. The lead actor was easily the worst part of this movie. This was definitely on the worse end of faith-based movies I’ve seen.  * Movie swapping with Joe

cast_names: Dheekshith Shetty, Sadhu Kokila, Sruthi Hariharan
review_content: Boring movie, give it a skip

cast_names: Jackie Kelly, Jeffrey Decker
review_content: Human tries to be edgy but ends up indulgently dull—25 minutes of phone texting proves the thesis.    The structure is three films stapled together. First, half an hour of pure screenlife: Dani texts her best mate and an ex while the night gets twitchy. Then the door bangs and we’re off into a practical-effects splatter storm, all bile, limbs and shrieks on a locked-down studio set. Finally, the brakes slam for late-night philosophising about life and meaning. Some will love the tonal whiplash; for me it plays self-indulgent and patience-testing, a punk vibe hiding a thin core.    If you liked… The Evil Dead’s manic energy, but you’re tolerant of long wind-ups.

cast_names: Chrissie Wunna, Dimitrios Pantos
review_content: Well, with a movie called “Bikini Nuns” it is certainly hard to have any expectations. Without an affinity for trash you won’t even remotely consider watching something like that. I was in a trash-y mood. This must be some dumb sexy action like Bitch Slap, right?   No, not at all. 2/3 of the movie are women getting dressed. The rest is filled with hilariously bad acting and a script that is so ridonculously bad, it elevates porn dialogue on a Citizen Kane level of grandeur.   What threw me off the most is the shotcaller priest character who looks like Judith Butler.   If you like to come together with ppl to watch the most stupid bs movies that you can find, that would probably be the scenario in which Bikini Nuns makes sense.

cast_names: John Lennon, Paul McCartney, George Harrison, Ringo Starr
review_content: [Venice '24] The Beatles are essential but not main characters in this fictional story built from reality. Because the film reveals itself as a time capsule that focuses on the year 1965 to offer a representation of the social transformations that were experienced in the United States. It expands to other events that occurred in that year, contrasting the modernity and casualness of the New York World's Fair with the Watts Riots in Los Angeles. The musical revolution among young people, the projection into the future of a global representation of technological advances, and racial movements make up a very heterogeneous representation of the upheaval that was taking place in the United States. An extraordinary reflection of the past and its influence on the present.

cast_names: Shernaz Patel
review_content: [IFFR '25] Directly embracing folk horror, the story connects with the tradition of nature worship and mysticism of the Sikkim region, which is part of the Himalayas. The first part of the film establishes some concepts about social classes that are liberated upon entering the forest, where most of the film takes place. The natural setting eliminates social barriers to establish a greater balance. The director manages to create an atmosphere of unease on this journey, which is too long, in which more and more dangers seem to lurk, and uses all the traditional elements, from hallucinogenic mushrooms to disturbing signs, nightmares or demonic possessions.

cast_names: Luna Fujimoto, Hunter Nance, Toni Weiss, Allegra Sweeney, Simone Lockhart, Liam Woodrum, Elizabeth O’Brien, Ernest Keith Walker, Dean Cameron, Colin Egglesfield
review_content: A slick whodunnit about anthropology students stalked by a masked killer. It plays like a time capsule: brisk, tidy kills, red herrings, a sheriff on the trail.  The problem is freshness; it recreates the ’90s groove without evolving it, so you’re left wishing it pushed the formula rather than just wearing it.    If you liked…Urban Legend, I Know What You Did Last Summer, Scream 2

cast_names: Agnes Born, Micki Stoltt, Claes Bang, Ali Abdul Amir Najel, Tommy Prang Vonsyld, Mads Koudal, Peter Pilegaard, Marcel Zyskind
review_content: Kedelig langtrukken film. Der sker først noget de sidste 20 min af filmen.

[VdR '25] While the camera remains distant yet seems close, the film raises questions about parenthood, but also about the shortcomings of social protection. This portrait of a recognizable but fragile family structure reflects the complex situation faced by children, although the director's approach tries not to blame anyone, focusing on those brief moments in which a subtle connection between children and their biological parents seems to return. By highlighting the work of social workers, it confronts the alarming lack of political commitment to the social protection of minors.

cast_names: Martin Kemp, Peter Woodward, David Yip, Wendy Glenn, Daisy Beaumont, Johnny Palmiero, Gary Webster, Andy Gatenby, Tara Sumner, Atul Sharma
review_content: Doctor Plague deserves a generous 1/10 — for trying, not for succeeding. The film squanders an intriguing premise (a modern detective chasing a cult of Plague Doctors tied to Jack the Ripper) on a sloppy script, flat direction, and performances that never bring the mystery or menace to life. At just 82 minutes it feels rushed where it should linger and padded where it should build suspense. Scenes jump from one thinly sketched set piece to another with little logic or emotional payoff, and the conspiracy thread is more cliché than clever.    Production values are uneven: a few effective images can’t hide cheap-looking effects and inconsistent tone that flips between horror, thriller, and police procedural without committing to any. Martin Kemp and the rest of the cast try, but the material gives them nothing to work with. In short: promising idea, poor execution. Skip it — there are far better modern cult-horror thrillers to spend your time on.

cast_names: Sid Lucero, Bea Binene, Rafa Siguion-Reyna, Carlos Siguion-Reyna, Ryza Cenon, Andrea Del Rosario, John Christopher Ventura, Bobby Bonifacio, Rob Rownd
review_content: I felt this was one of those films created for filmmakers. Us outside that bubble may not get as much out of this. I found it predictable because the scriptwriters dropped too many clues. I was with someone who loved it, though.

John Dickson is quite a character and an excellent and entertaining guide through this well paced documenary. It was more interesting and moving than I thought it would be, and especially powerful to see a song written during such an intense period of Christian persecution be brought forward millennia as a gift to the modern global church.

cast_names: Nick Ryan, Krystal Shay
review_content: If Devil’s Revenge left you thinking, “Surely cinema has hit rock bottom,” then allow Bears on a Ship to grab a shovel and dig enthusiastically downward.  This is not just a bad film. This is a commitment to bad decisions—creative, narrative, and apparently linguistic.  Let’s start with the acting. Or, more accurately, the concept of acting. Every performance feels like it was delivered by someone who had just been handed a script five minutes earlier and told, “Don’t worry about understanding it—just pick an accent and go wild.” The result? A dazzling carousel of accents that seem to travel more than the characters do. One minute you’re in vaguely American territory, the next you’ve drifted into something that sounds like a confused Australian doing a London impression after two pints.  And yet, somehow, the bears are the most believable things on screen.  Now, the plot. If we can call it that.  A flight gets cancelled due to what may be the shortest strike in recorded human history. Airports are shut. Chaos reigns. Panic sets in. And then—blink and you’ll miss it—the strike is basically over before you’ve even processed what’s happening. It’s less “industrial action” and more “mild inconvenience with a tea break.” You half expect someone to pop up and say, “Right, that’s enough striking for today, back to work everyone.”  So naturally—naturally—instead of simply waiting the extra five minutes for civilisation to resume, or doing what literally every human being would do and rebooking the next available flight, our cast of logistical masterminds decides to… charter a boat.  A boat.  Because when your primary concern is getting somewhere quickly, nothing screams efficiency quite like abandoning aviation entirely in favour of a scenic cruise into disaster.  And what a boat it is.  This thing looks less like a functioning vessel and more like it was minutes away from being scuttled for scrap before the production crew stumbled across it and said, “Perfect, we’ll take it.” You can practically hear the hull creaking in protest, like it knows it was meant for a dignified end rather than starring in whatever this is. It genuinely feels like the location team got a freebie—possibly with the condition that they didn’t ask too many questions and signed something that said “no refunds.”  Naturally, this bold strategic pivot leads them to the film’s central selling point: bears. On a ship. Because clearly the next logical escalation from “minor airport disruption” is “maritime bear encounter.”  By this point, you’re no longer questioning the logic—you’ve accepted that logic was never invited to this production in the first place.  But here’s the real masterstroke: why does absolutely nobody just get off the boat? We’re meant to believe that a group of adults, faced with literal bears roaming around a floating rust bucket, collectively decide that their best survival strategy is… to stay put?  No one thinks, “Shall we use the lifeboat?” No one suggests, “Maybe grab a life preserver and get literally anywhere else?” No emergency drill, no evacuation, no common sense—just a unanimous agreement to remain on board the world’s least appealing cruise while apex predators wander about like they’ve booked an all-inclusive.  And unless this vessel is somehow the only ship in maritime history to ignore basic safety regulations, there must be life preservers, lifeboats, something that doesn’t involve waiting your turn to be picked off. But no—apparently the script demanded everyone stay exactly where the danger is, because leaving would risk ending the film early. And we can’t have that.  The film then proceeds exactly as you’d expect if it were written during a chaotic brainstorming session where every idea was accepted and none were challenged. Dialogue feels like it was assembled from rejected GCSE drama improvisations, and character decisions are guided by a strict “what’s the worst possible choice?” philosophy.  And let’s not forget the emotional journey of the viewer, which transitions rapidly from confusion, to disbelief, to actively rooting for the bears. By the halfway mark, the bears are no longer the villains—they’re the only characters demonstrating purpose, efficiency, and a clear understanding of what needs to be done.  The humans? Still arguing in dodgy accents about finding the bears and taking them out, but f### knows why.   Now, in the film’s only redeeming twist—credit where it’s due—the bears actually look damn cool. Genuinely. Whether it’s the effects, the movement, or just the fact they’re the only things behaving in a way that makes any sense, they end up being the unexpected highlight. You almost feel bad that they’ve been dropped into this mess.  By the end, you’re not shocked or entertained—you’re simply relieved. Relieved that it’s over. Relieved the bears finally got involved. Relieved that no one decided to escalate things further with wolves in a submarine. (Note to self, write “Wolves on a Submarine” starring Samuel L Jackson!)  In summary, Bears on a Ship (2025) is a cinematic achievement in all the wrong ways—a film where a blink-and-you-miss-it strike leads to a series of decisions so baffling they make you question whether anyone involved has ever travelled, spoken to another human, or understood the concept of getting off a boat.  One star. Generously awarded to the bears—for looking brilliant, having clear goals, and for ending things far quicker than that strike ever did.  Update: I found out that the MV Aurora took on water during refurbishment in 2024 and partially sank, then was scrapped in 2025 😂 It was also used in From Russia With Love.

cast_names: Riteish Deshmukh, Vivek Oberoi, Aftab Shivdasani, Ruhi Singh, Shreya Sharma, Elnaaz Norouzi, Shaad Randhawa, Arshad Warsi, Nargis Fakhri, Tusshar Kapoor
review_content: Wtfh was that? Do they even care or see what they are making? Women objectification, misogyny, and leave aside all that, the movie doesn't have basic value to make the audience sit throughout. Absolute disaster, CBFC needs to intervene in such cases.

review_content: One of the worst movies ever made.

review_content: I’m not sure how or why producers agreed to spend money on this movie! The story itself doesn’t even have that many double-meaning jokes. The writing is so weak, and most of the jokes are so lame that they don’t even feel funny. Overall, it’s a total waste of time.

review_content: A complete Shit movie. 😡

cast_names: Anushka Shetty, Vikram Prabhu, Ramya Krishnan, Jagapati Babu, Ravindra Vijay, VTV Ganesh, Larissa Bonesi, John Vijay, Sudhasri Madhusmita
review_content: A revenge drama elevated by Anushka’s magnetic screen presence, blending raw emotion with commanding charisma

There’s a good idea hiding in the bones of this film — the “lost phones and cameras found in the wilderness” conceit can be legitimately eerie — but everything about this 52-minute episode squanders it. Writer-director Brandon Walker and Ghost Machine Pictures aim for atmospheric dread and end up delivering a tedious, derivative slog that feels padded and incomplete.    What goes wrong    Execution over originality: The anthology/found-footage setup had real potential, but the episode leans on tired tropes (shaky camerawork, frantic cuts, and jump-scare timing) without offering fresh perspective or clever subversion. It feels like a checklist of things we’ve seen done far better elsewhere.    Thin characters: “Prospector Pete” and the small cast (Thomas Burke, Nathan Church, Eliot Host) are sketched so shallowly you never care what happens to them. Without emotional stakes, the scares have no weight.  Pacing and payoff: At 52 minutes it should be tight; instead it drags. Scenes meander and the buildup never delivers a satisfying payoff — the “supernatural madness” promised in the logline arrives as a vague, underdeveloped climax rather than a memorable reveal.    Production limitations show: Low-budget choices aren’t inherently bad, but here they compound the problems. Audio is inconsistent, the editing feels chaotic rather than artful, and the cinematography relies on discomfort rather than composition. The result is more irritating than immersive.    Bottom line This installment tries to ride the coattails of the found-footage revival but lacks the craft, characters, and imagination to make it worthwhile. Only a very specific appetite for amateurish scare cinema or an interest in seeing how not to build an anthology entry would make this watchable. For most viewers: skip it.

cast_names: Grace Glowicki, Ben Petrie, Leah Doz
review_content: Comedy with a bit of an original setup. It starts well, but gets a bit boring in the last third.    I guess it's missing a little dynamism as it looks more like a theatre play than a movie, probably de to a lack of budget I assume. So there is very little decor, a lot of close ups, everything happens at night and there are a lot of scenes with just a black background.  It gives a certain style in the beginning, but becomes a bit monotonous after a while.    I basically lost interest after the stealing of the sister's body. At this point it turned from an original story to a kind of vaudeville, sure with corpses, but, meh.

Love how this uses the vibes and aesthetics of FMV and PSOne horror to express the unique horror of modern technology. Only part of the horror here is that the horror of modern internet is not wholly unique. There was a sort of fun and larger than life weirdness to the early Internet that made even its dark side feel special. This uses surreality to expose reality- the brazen and wasteful laziness of AI, the entitlement to your success and to other people, the non blasé disregard for others as you drive and mess with your phone, the world exposed before your eyes as a series of scams and lies and nobody actually believing anything and websites barely hiding anything anymore because what are you going to do, say no? Use a different website? You don’t. It’s apocalyptic, and you’re resigned to it. The hole’s all around you. Why not get in?

cast_names: Yeon Ye-ji, Kim Pureum, Moon Hye-in
review_content: [Locarno '25] Three characters meet in a dystopian world where the two Koreas have been unified to establish a different kind of division, one that separates humans from mutants born with amphibious limbs. Intelligently managing the modest budget, the director achieves an appropriate balance between melancholic drama, disillusioned dystopia, and a political gaze toward the promises of post-capitalist society. The film creates a disturbing future after an ecological disaster that is imbued with an intelligent political charge, dwelling on the danger of the deterioration of human relations that ultimately provokes collective fear.

cast_names: Jamie Marshall
review_content: If you are going to "steal" money, in the millions, you don't stay where you are and buy a bunch of stuff. You move to a new location where people don't know you and then feel free to go crazy.

cast_names: Maria Bakalova, Margita Gosheva, Julian Vergov, Julian Kostov, Stanislav Ganchev, Ivan Savov, Ivan Barnev, Nikolay Todorov, Milko Lazarov
review_content: I had low expectations but it was even worse than that.    It's a typical artsy fartsy Bulgarian movie filled with tons of nonsense, illogical scenes, weak plot, profanity and unlikeable characters.    I understand that the point is to show how bad and dumb the communist party was and that the government lies to us and spends tons of money on stupid and useless things. But imo it should have been done with more humour. There was nothing funny at all (at least to me).    The true story that the movie was inspired by wasn't explained very well. If you have no idea what the real event was and you just watch this movie, you would be totally lost. I have a basic knowledge about it and I was still confused.    Maria Bakalova basically plays the role of Tutar form Borat again. She has around 20 lines.  Julian Kostov had like 3 lines.  Julian Vergov is a really good actor, but I simply can't stand him, ever since the allegations against him were made public.

cast_names: Chun Sin-hwan, Park Jong-hwan, Jin Ye-sol
review_content: [Fantasia '24] Self-defined as "an oriental western action film", it has an attractive premise that ends up developing chaotically and with few positive results in its intended absurd humor. During the first half hour, the viewer adjusts to the introduction of cartoonish characters, fake beards, bright colors in which the greenish yellows of the desert predominate, as if it were Wes Anderson, and a narrative that alternates different plots that come together in one alone. But it ends up being a disappointing experience because it ends up absorbed by the irrelevance of its proposal and the limited seductive capacity of its development.
From a narrative POV it was well executed, the Story was confidently told (Even lost me a Bit at some Point). What threw me Off was the mostly stiff acting and the histrionic enactment.

cast_names: Rose-Marie Perreault, Simon Landry-Désy
review_content: [Fantasia '25] In much the same way that the protagonist feels the frustration of motherhood that envelops her in postpartum depression, the film ends up being a frustrating experience that has an interesting premise only to waste it on a narrative of easy resources and superficial characters surrounding Pénélope. At times it can work as a psychological drama, but its introduction within the horror genre is questionable, because not even the description of the mother's point of view ends up adopting that approach. The progressive isolation of the main character and the bubble she shares with her son are well described, but the introduction of disturbing elements to build a suspense structure is unnecessary.

cast_names: Bani Khoshnoudi
review_content: [VdR '25] Through the disappearance of her mother's cousin during Khomeini's regime, the director creates a dual narrative about her family and the transformation of a nearly immutable country. She speaks of the value of different types of resistance, even silent resistance, which nevertheless erupted in the Green Movement of 2009, a topic she has previously addressed in other documentaries. In this case, Bani Khoshnoudi fails to truly engage with the images, establishing a perspective that seems too detached to imbue a story told so often with a certain distinctiveness.

cast_names: Shiva, Priya Anand, Yoshinori Tashiro, VTV Ganesh, Yogi Babu, Sathish, Chetan, Nizhalgal Ravi, Srinath
review_content: As a film, it is terrible I would watch it again just for Agila Ulaga Superstar.

cast_names: Steve Little, Tipper Newton, Robert Longstreet, George Sample III, Bryan Connolly, John Gholson, Vincent James Prendergast, John Merriman, Ron Ford, Elizabeth Maxwell
review_content: Meh. The pitch is funny, could be some good kidnapping flick there, but as soon as you see the monstrous son, we'll, that's it, there is basically almost nothing after that.    It's some disgusting stuff, but it's cheap, not really well done, not really gore, not really funny, nothing subtle or original or even a bit unexpected.    The material is really spread too thin. It should have been a short. They knew it so much that there is a fake cinema rules intro in the beginning (funny enough), and two extra endings (not too bad for the first one, but not essential either, useless for the second one). And despite that, the main story part was too long.    The different "fuck my son" song scenes were annoying. They really didn't bring anything and were just too long and boring.    The meaty whatever imaginary friends were kinda OK. Let's say it brings a small interest in the daughter character, otherwise she is just useless, not seen, not heard. But it's just filler material too.    There is actually just two good things. The mother explaining the step by step, though it worked better as the trailer.  And when they get back to the house and the cop tell her she's gonna [spoiler]fuck her son[/spoiler].  The movie should have ended here, it was a great ending.    It's all down hill and without interest after that, and the longer it kept going on, the more boring it was.    It does not even have half the shock value it's going for.

cast_names: Melissanthi Mahut, Aurora Marion, Christos Loulis, Kostas Nikouli, Stavros Svigos, Ksenia Dania, Erifili Kitzoglou, Myrto Kontoni, Tonia Sotiropoulou
review_content: This is mostly following a young woman taking power. But the whole movie is weird, not in a good or an interesting way, more of a general "hmmm, ok but why ?" vibe, like it never knows where it wants to go.    It's supposed to be a post apocalyptic world.  But apart from tales once or twice on tv - of whole cities with people killing their children then themselves - there's nothing. They live pretty comfortably, have electricity, alcohol, parties, cars, jetskis... Ah yeah, they say the food is not great.    The gang is supposed to be what maintain order in the city, and keep others from coming in.  But we never see any conflict, or someone else coming, or any of them doing any policing work, or any other kind by the way.     According to the intro, women are supposed to be currency, like gas.  But if there's certainly a culture that the men should lead, and a lot are probably prostitutes, this is a looong way from treating them that badly.    The main subject is supposed to be the selection of the next leader.  But there's not really anything showing that except them shooting at targets once. And there seem to be little elimination more like there are new candidates instead. No selection process, no progress, nothing.    There's this whole thing about the leader wanting her to take part in the successor selection, even against the others, and even wanting her to win, he even kills one of the guys over that.  But then their relation looks just as artificial as their falling out.    She somehow discovers that she has [spoiler]supernatural powers, inherited from her mother[/spoiler].  But it mostly just happens, is not really developed properly.    So once all that removed, it could be about her story with the new girl.  But then the ending scene is just [spoiler]the girl going somewhere else[/spoiler].    It was presented as supposedly feminist.  But, it's even mentioned, she's behaving just like the boys. And half of the scenes involve hot women doing sexy poses.    Overall there is still an interesting enough progression in her story. With the [spoiler]power[/spoiler], the relationship with the new girl, her discovering her mother's story (that somehow she was the only one not aware as her sisters knew everything, and the new girl learned about it in a few days). But the whole thing feels a bit random.

cast_names: Melissanthi Mahut, Aurora Marion, Christos Loulis, Kostas Nikouli, Stavros Svigos, Ksenia Dania, Erifili Kitzoglou, Myrto Kontoni, Tonia Sotiropoulou
review_content: [Venice '25] One of the problems with this Greek dystopia is that it's difficult to establish whether the exposure of male and female stereotypes is presented with an ironic eye, or simply a limited representation of a cinematic genre that the director has failed to adequately subvert. This places the film on unbalanced terrain that is never clearly defined. But at the same time, it's a narratively simple and aesthetically empty story, without providing many elements that establish a distinctive dystopian world.

cast_names: Ashton James, Henri Picard, Blair Underwood, Shawn Doyle, Emidio Lopes, Oluniké Adeliyi, Tamara Podemski, Keris Hope Hill, Donald MacLean Jr., Joris Jarsky
review_content: I was kind of skeptical when I had seen they remade this and sure enough there was good reason for skepticism. I absolutely loved the original since I was a kid. I don’t normally have any issues with movies being remade. I always say do your own thing and make it even better. That didn’t happen here at all.  Every single character was a a huge step down from the original. The supporting characters in particular were very underdeveloped. The romance felt so bland and forced. The story lacked focus and felt uninspired. This wasn’t all that entertaining either and had a lot of plot holes. The hockey scenes were easily the best part of this movie. They tried to convey a clear message about anger and family but it doesn’t deliver it all that well. There’s one excellent speech near the end of the movie that packs a punch but outside of that it felt pretty average. At the end of the day this just didn’t need to be remade at all and will most likely be very unsuccessful.  * Double Feature at AMC Crestwood 18

movie_title: The Woman in the White Car
cast_names: Jung Ryeo-won, Lee Jung-eun, Jang Jin-hee, Kang Jeong-woo
review_content: A fat cop in a remote mountain village, tyrannized by her father, investigates a strange case. The girl brought her sister to the hospital after a knife attack, but something about her story is off. And it's not her sister at all. She has her own version. Unreliable narrators are multiplying. A tightly-guarded thriller for the observant.

cast_names: Elsa Wolliaston, Olivier Rabourdin, Alain Figlarz, Paul Hamy, Idir Chender
review_content: [ArteKino '25] A playful story about a relationship that explores multicultural coexistence and complex understandings, from the perspective of a refugee in survival mode to the more comfortable stance of a woman finding a way to avoid loneliness. It's based on a true experience, but the narrative never quite defines the characters clearly, except through superficial and broad strokes, portraying immigrants almost as stereotypes stemming from a Western perspective. The story's development is logical because, in reality, the film itself doesn't quite understand its characters.

cast_names: Matthew Daddario, Abigail Breslin, Lainie Kazan, Perrey Reeves, David DeLuise, Rob Estes, Olivia Luccardi, Michelle Danner
review_content: The story acrualky very good. The only 2 problems I have with this film are: - Breslin looked like she had just gave birth - some parts of the scripts was overly extended that it became boring  Other than those 2 problems, it's an okay film.

cast_names: Kimberley Crossman, James Blake, Sam Berkley, Daniel Cresswell, Kieran Charnock, Maggie Robertson, Adam Scherr, Logan Vanghel
review_content: Having lost the originality of the first one, nothing much is left.    A bit funny seeing what Brody has become. The Jesse character is fun. That's about it.    Otherwise it's basically the same thing.    The fact that the guys they brought back to life are eating people and they're just casually ok with it is really strange and kinda disturbing.  The [spoiler]evil Dion[/spoiler] story was not really interesting but the way Giles treated him before was disturbing too.  The 10 minutes dick cutting scenes was far from being as funny as they probably thought it would be (and nobody seems to care about previous corpses ??).    Not enough Medina, it looks like she would have been more interesting. And she's treated kinda weirdly too. Every interaction with Ana, she's seems to (rightfully) think "oh my god, is there any limit to how dumb that bitch can be ?", and yet somehow it turns out to be [spoiler]love[/spoiler] ???    By the time the climax arrives, I had already mostly lost interest.    Ah yeah, kudos for Matt Heafy though.

cast_names: Kimberley Crossman, James Blake, Sam Berkley, Daniel Cresswell, Kieran Charnock, Maggie Robertson, Adam Scherr, Logan Vanghel
review_content: This was the Frightfest film I was looking forward to the most as I'd seen the original with friends at a movie night and it went down a storm. Such films are meant to be watched in groups. I enjoyed it. The comedy is typical of New Zealand and as tasteless as you would expect from heavy metal fans. Despite the terrible music, it's a fun film and very well made on a limited budget. I had the fortune of meeting Daniel Cresswell after the film for an opportunity to give him my appreciation of both films; very nice humble feller.

cast_names: Chris Colfer, Alice Krige, Daniel Adegboyega, Declan Reynolds, Gabriela Garcia Vargas, Matthew McMahon
review_content: Camp comedy body horror with very few likeable characters. I'd say it is the poor cousin of The Royal Tenenbaums, Saltburn and Tales of the Unexpected.  Not my thing really and not what I hope for at a horror film festival (I saw it at FrightFest)

[DOK.fest '25] While the film focuses on the routine journeys and work performed by the young crew members of a container ship, it also focuses on moments of pause in the kitchen or sitting during breaks, talking about their families or partners. The camera achieves a remarkable level of closeness, occasionally capturing situations that are out of the ordinary. Ultimately, it's a coming-of-age story in which we witness the growth of the main character, but instead of taking place in an urban setting, it unfolds within the space of a cargo ship, enveloped in an ethereal atmosphere provided by Mirjam Skal's music.
