cast_names: Blanche Barton, Peter H. Gilmore
review_content: Strong opening scene. Overall, it’s a a cheap horror with a twist of avant-garde “force the audience to stare at nothing for 5 minutes straight” kinda energy. There were some scenes that seemed very lazy and the actresses at times seemed confused in a way that the director may not have intended, but what do I know? I’m just a guy who paid money to watch a goat giving birth, though I can’t say that I saw that coming.

cast_names: Dee Wallace
review_content: Ted Bunny (2026) is the kind of misfire that makes you wonder how many red flags were ignored on the way to release. What should have been a sharp, darkly comic riff on a bizarre premise instead collapses into a confused, tonally incoherent slog that feels stitched together from half-finished ideas.    Story & Tone  The film can’t decide whether it wants to be a parody, a thriller, or a quirky character study. Instead of blending genres, it ricochets between them with no rhythm. Scenes meant to be funny land with a thud, dramatic beats evaporate instantly, and the plot meanders so aimlessly that even the movie seems bored with itself.    Characters  Ted himself is written with all the depth of a mascot costume. The supporting cast fares no better—everyone feels like they’re acting in a different movie, and none of those hypothetical movies would be good. Attempts at emotional resonance are so forced they border on unintentional comedy.    Direction & Pacing  The pacing is a disaster. Long stretches drag without purpose, only to be interrupted by abrupt tonal shifts that feel like reshoots stapled onto the original cut. The direction offers no vision, no energy, and no sense of what the film wants to be.    Technical Craft  Even the technical side can’t save it. Flat lighting, awkward editing, and a score that seems to be guessing what emotion the audience should feel all contribute to the sense of a production that never found its footing.    Final Verdict  1/10.    A bewildering, shapeless mess that fails as comedy, fails as drama, and fails as entertainment. The only real mystery is how it made it past the pitch stage.

cast_names: Catherine Brunet, Charlotte Aubin, Jade Hassouné, Rosalie Bonenfant, Caroline Néron, Benoît McGinnis, Anne-Marie Cadieux, Manuel Tadros, Karl Graboshas, Fayolle Jean
review_content: [Fantasia '25] A story of redemption inspired by the cinema of Jean-Claude Lauzon that proposes an almost imaginary Montreal to confront two worlds: the criminal world and the artistic and elitist circles. There is an interesting aesthetic confrontation between the two environments, which, however, reveal themselves to be less opposed than they initially appear. But the film is more interesting in its visual conception, endowed with a certain sophisticated look even if it portrays a certain sordidness, than in a script that develops along traditional and overly predictable paths.

cast_names: Vicenç Altaió, Elaine Collins, Paul J. Dove
review_content: Wow, this required a lot of people, probably not all real actors. It's mostly a parliament like assembly for a trial. It's almost all absurd, and they're shouting, chanting,talking above each other in a continuous uproar. Which becomes annoying really fast.    There's also bits of normal nowadays street scenes in the middle. No idea how they're supposed to fit. I missed the beginning of the seance, so probably only saw the second half, but somehow it doesn't feel like I missed anything. The full 25 minutes would probably have been to much.

cast_names: Jane de Wet, Adore Delano, Darron Meyer, Frances Sholto-Douglas, Anja Taljaard, Alice Viskat, Stephanie Kerbis
review_content: This plods along like a premise that got stuck in development hell. Sam Wineman’s idea — a tense, intimate showdown between Rowan and a self-styled “hag” — sounds promising on paper, but the execution is painfully thin. The 1h40m runtime feels twice as long: scenes drag with tiresome repetition, the stakes never escalate, and the character motivations are murky rather than mysterious. Ryan de Villiers and Jane de Wet try to carry it, but their performances can’t rescue a script that offers few surprises and worse, little emotional truth. The South African setting is underused, the tone wobblily split between thriller and overwrought drama, and what should be claustrophobic tension collapses into predictable beats. If you want real dread or smart psychological horror, look elsewhere — Hag wastes its concept and its cast.

movie_title: Why Do I See You in Everything?
review_content: [IFFR '26] It finds a powerful metaphor in the idea of ​​olive trees as a reflection of the forced displacement of Arab peoples, but it strives for such density in its poetic vision that it ultimately becomes tedious, a problem exacerbated by the ethereal tone of the personal reflections. This hybrid documentary, whatever its intentions may be, suffers from the flaws of supposedly transcendent cinema that sacrifices understanding in favor of a succession of disparate mixtures of archival footage, videos captured by the protagonists themselves, glimpses into their personal lives, and depictions of the effects of citizen protests.

cast_names: Arturo Valls, Andrea Duro, Vito Sanz, Marta Belenguer, Berta Vázquez, Lorena López, Pablo Molinero, Enrique Arce, Javier Coronas
review_content: [Raindance '25] An anthology of stories around absurd situations, that never finds the balance to have a cohesion, although they are united by a common segment starring a watchmaker named Carlo, who through the gears of a clock leads the stories told by listeners who call into a radio program to extreme situations. There is care in the production design, but the attempt to reflect the eccentricity of many everyday situations taken to the extreme, from a vacation turned into a nightmare to the banality of meetings between friends, never finds the right way to be as funny as it pretends to be.

cast_names: Kofi Baffour, Brittney Ayona Clemons, Damon Preston
review_content: Don't watch. Do something else with your time.

cast_names: Stephen Samson, Simon Ellis, Julia Quayle
review_content: 🎬 Why It Fails        Concept execution: The idea of turning Oz into a horror slasher playground could have been campy fun, but instead it’s a muddled mess. Dorothy unleashing Tinman and Scarecrow as bloodthirsty killers sounds intriguing, yet the film never balances horror with coherent storytelling.    Low production values: The film is a British indie horror, part of the “public domain horror” trend (like Winnie the Pooh: Blood and Honey), but the budget constraints are painfully obvious. Sets look cheap, effects are laughable, and the atmosphere is nonexistent.    Acting & characters: Performances are wooden, with Dorothy (Alina Desmond) reduced to a shrill scream machine. The villains—Tinman and Scarecrow—come off more silly than scary, undermining the intended menace.    Plot incoherence: The rehab setting, psychiatric undertones, and “nightmares made real” angle are confusingly stitched together. Instead of psychological horror, it feels like random scenes strung together without payoff.    Audience reaction: Even horror fans on forums have dismissed it as “trash” and “blow it out yurass,” showing how poorly it landed with its target audience.    ⚠ Risks & Trade-Offs        Derivative trend: This film is another entry in the “Poohniverse” wave of low-budget horror adaptations. If you disliked those, you’ll hate this one.        Time sink: At best, it’s unintentionally funny; at worst, it’s a waste of 90 minutes.        No cult charm: Unlike some bad horror films that become “so bad it’s good,” this one lacks charm or memorable camp.    📉 Final Verdict    1/10 — A cynical cash-grab that drains all magic from Oz. Instead of reimagining a beloved classic with creativity, Dead Walk stumbles into incoherence, cheap scares, and uninspired gore. Unless you’re collecting bad horror curiosities, skip this one.

cast_names: Lucy Fry, Sarah Rich, Madeline Brewer, Lara Clear, Matt Rife, Sheryl Lee, Cara Seymour, Alex Gaumond
review_content: On a visual and vibe level, it's pretty good.    At the story level it begins ok, it's good when it's start getting weird. Sadly it's quickly apparent that there's no story any more and the whole thing is the [spoiler]it was all a dream/subconscious thing[/spoiler] trope.    There are good parts in the beginning, the job interview, the talk with the mother (even if I've already seen pretty much the exact same scene in other movies), the arrival at the motel.    I'm a bit uncomfortable with the underlying message though. As usual it's presented as a feminist work from a woman, but is it ? In the end she goes through with it and ends up with a crown, making her a queen, highlighting her mastery of her own body, sure. But in between ?    The whole motel experience is just a metaphor for abortion. (In the beginning I was wondering whether the whole clinic and the motel they sent her to was fake, like it exists in the US, going into a kind of sequestration story where they would force her to finish the pregnancy). And there are lots of ambiguous messaging.    - She refers to herself as "broken", because she thought she couldn't get pregnant, not a good start.  - The end scene presents the abortion (at a very early stage) to be as painful as actual childbirth.  - The whole absolute weird experience is traumatic and alienating    And mostly, the main symbolism of the climax scene. There is Lilian (amazing performance by the way), who is the archetypal free girl: no boyfriend, drinks, smokes, enjoys her time at the spa, ambiguous lesbian. And not only is she presented as evil (at some point you wonder if she's a ghost or a devil), what is the whole motel metaphor's goal ? To literally [spoiler]kill her, and make her eat broken glass to, quote: "make her feel it"[/spoiler]. That's a bit much.    Anyway, artistically it's a good first shot. Though the [spoiler]"it was all a dream"[/spoiler] is always disappointing since you usually spend the whole movie looking for different theories that would all have been better.

cast_names: Alma Pöysti, Pirkko Saisio, Hannu-Pekka Björkman, Mikko Kauppila, Tambet Tuisk, Juhan Ulfsak, Lauri Maijala, Ilo-Ann Saarepera, Olivier De Sagazan
review_content: [IFFR '25] The island of Jurmo in Finland becomes a relevant landscape to define the relationship between the two women protagonists of this film that visually recreates the impact of an isolated but beautiful environment, of single-family houses separated by rocks. Evoking fragments of Baudelaire, inspired by the cinema of Ingmar Bergman, tracing the tradition of Handel's opera, it is a reflective film, almost a cinematic essay, that raises philosophical questions about religion, memory and the consequences of decisions in the past.

review_content: This could've been a good film if only the director hadn't felt the need to channel his inner avantgardist...

review_content: A Canadian steam-punk sin-city-esque gangster film. The director called it a love letter to his favourite films, including irreversible, surprisingly. Does that excuse the cliches and unoriginal script? not for me. There were some inventive kills for those that enjoy such things. Most of the cast did well with what they were given.

cast_names: Thom Yorke
review_content: Thom Yorke rallies against the notion that everything he touches turns to gold. This movie is a bit of a turd that is only in theatres because it has Thom’s name attached to it.   You could ponder what the deeper meaning of this movie is, or you could see it as an average art school year end exhibition piece where each of his blender projects are presented as vignettes in a delightful game interface.   Skip.

cast_names: Stan Steinbichler, Graham McTavish, Susie Porter, Alice Lucy, Olivia Chenery
review_content: [Raindance '25] Divided into segments that reference different biblical chapters like Genesis and Exodus, the film benefits from the setting in the mountains of Austria, which hides a cult that uses young immigrants to commit satanic acts. The film is set in a time that on one hand seems to take place in the early 20th century, but on the other hand uses devices that communicate with social media, thereby establishing a kind of conservative dystopia. Despite interesting ideas about mental health and religious oppression, it feels like the story never knows exactly where to go, and ends up leading to a climax that feels like an overly disturbing twist.

Outside of that there is a bit more to be desired because they are wading into very familiar waters. So while it never elevates passed the standard road movie, the actors keep it honest.

cast_names: Alexandra McVicker, Scott Major, Jordan Dulieu, Drew Droege, Joe Lynch, Betsey Brown
review_content: [Fantasia '25] One of Alice Maio Mackay's recurring characteristics is her proposal for a revision of nineties fantasy cinema, providing a reality based on diversity, but also exposing current discriminations. Her new film adopts a fantasy story tone to construct a development that is as fluid in its exposition of cinematic genres as it is in its representation of identity-based genders. At times, the story's execution may not be entirely effective, but the direction does not feel indecisive, instead confidently adopting a reflective look at contemporary society that is inspired by the tropes of nineties society.

cast_names: Anwen O'Driscoll, Jessica Paré, Skylar Radzion, Ava Anton, Keenan Tracey, Linden Porco, Toby Hargrave, Austin Trapp, Harrison Browne, Kendall Gender
review_content: [Fantasia '25] The story of a young nonconformist who is really just content with leading a normal life without hiding the fact that she comes from a long tradition of bearded women. The director proposes a story that has to do with rebellion, but wraps it in the atmosphere of a circus community that serves as a bubble from the rest of society, with some references to the queer community and a rural environment that has something of a fairy tale in the way it is represented (the colors, the production design...). It is well told and, in its own way, addresses an ambiguity wrapped in fantasy that nevertheless deals with contemporary themes.

cast_names: Ebony Aboagye, Nina Singh, Phillip Avramov, Jacob Utzon Krefeld
review_content: [Raindance '25] Raises existential and philosophical questions about the meaning of life, but without being overly pretentious. It also raises questions about identity, especially for a young man of Bulgarian origin who was educated in the United States. Through creative staging, it offers a thoughtful look at how we construct our lives through the way we relate to each other and asks questions about whether forgiveness is possible, defining a protagonist who wraps himself in a kind of armor of arrogance to avoid facing his emotions.

cast_names: Fiona Shaw, Katherine Waterston, Chaske Spencer, Allison McKay, Nelson Ascencio, Gabriella Baldacchino, Gino Cafarelli, Didi Conn, Skye P. Marshall, Robert Lenzi
review_content: A performance so powerful by Fiona Shaw that the rest of the film suffers when she isn't on screen. The character of Kit is complex and layered and a real joy to watch.

cast_names: Nicolas de Lavergne, Arcadi Radeff
review_content: [ArteKino '25] Victor Segalen, doctor and poet, traces the footsteps of painter Paul Gauguin in his Tahitian retreat at the beginning of the 20th century, where ghosts of the past and the painter's controversial relationship with the island's inhabitants emerge. Like a tragic mirror, the figures of Paul Gauguin, who died at 55, and the doctor Victor Segalen, who committed suicide at 41, are reflected in a film with a poetic tone that resonates with the echoes of the colonial era. More successful in its visual representation than in its somewhat languid narrative, the film functions as a historical reflection that doesn't seem to develop a particularly clear message.

cast_names: Greg Dorchak
review_content: Thought this movie was going to be about gooners at a lagoon. On a serious note, Cinematography is pretty good. Can’t say the same for the story. It’s full of cliche horror movie scripting, predictable thriller scenes, and a story that is kind of hard to understand what is happening. Actors are monotone or hard to understand. One of the scenes where the group is at the camp fire and mr.leonard comes up is probably the funniest thing i’ve ever seen, how in the hell did so much fog come out that it was obstructing the scene. The fight scene between the “20 year old - old lady” and the generic “popular social media girl” felt like it came out of a youtube short story. Also feels like some of the actors over exaggerated their injuries, like why did Mia start crawling as if she broke her ankle when she got a scratch on her lower abdomen (mind you the “goon” barely even grabs on to her to cause such injury). Ending makes no sense, after the goon gets stabbed by the magical dagger, she doesn’t die???? But then Mr.Leonard shoots her (in her water form) with the cross bow bolt and dies? Doesn’t add up. If you’re looking for a laugh, watch this lol. Side note, Triple A never pulls up.

review_content: Well produced and some decent cinematography, but overall the story felt flat and the actors were very hammy. Entertaining in itself, but not really scary.

cast_names: Helena Howard, Inanna Sarkis, Jake Abel, Kelli Garner, James A. Janisse, Chelsea Rebecca
review_content: A screenwriter, hired to revive the creaky Red Mask franchise, holes up at an Airbnb with her fiancée to break the script. Uninvited guests crash, the 'game' turns lethal, and fiction bleeds into reality. The setup's neat and the stabbings land, but the film chases meta commentary so hard it forgets suspense. You can see the signposts to Scream, New Nightmare and Behind the Mask from miles out, and the movie keeps pointing at them. Clever in places, pleased with itself often, scary only in flashes.    If you liked… Scream, Wes Craven’s New Nightmare, Behind the Mask: The Rise of Leslie Vernon.

[CPH:DOX '24] The director won a BAFTA TV Award for the short film Missed call (2018), in which she showed her son Jim's attempt to reconnect with his father. But in the film there are also fragments that are part of other shorts such as The waiting room (2019), in which she showed her experience following the diagnosis of breast cancer. It can be somewhat strange to witness this kind of obsession with continually recording your son, and it is precisely the moments in which Jim feels uncomfortable, and that kind of escape that he carries out when he is a teenager, that best delve into the vulnerability of this shared path. It is built from a choppy montage, in which images of the past and present are mixed, a fragmented edition that perhaps wants to convey the disordered condition of memory, but that ends up being detrimental to constructing a narrative that truly acquires a logical path. and structured. The film has some emotional exposition that may be understandable in the mother/director, but is much more debatable in the son/protagonist. And that blurs the film's effectiveness as an honest story.

cast_names: Rachel Alejandro, Mica Javier, Jay-R
review_content: This movie is well intentioned but pretty predictable. As soon as I saw the "girl with glasses" playing piano and the lead singer with the dolled up outfit, I knew. All of the characters are straight out of a telenovela. I did like Rachel's acting and it was nice to see Jay R on the screen again.

cast_names: Mugen Rao, Bhavya Trikha, Balasaravanan, Imman Annachi, Radha Ravi, Nizhalgal Ravi, Vinodhini Vaidyanathan, George Vijay Nelson
review_content: Didnt know this movie was a "Kids Animated Movie". Not sure if it was meant to be scary (didnt get scared) or was meant to be funny (didnt even laugh once). This movie had one of the dumbest climax scene you would ever see.    Bad VFX, plot & execution. Maybe kids would enjoy it? :person_shrugging:♂    One positive, the songs/soundtrack were good.

cast_names: Erkan Acar, Ronald Nitschke, Tito Uysal, Franziska Machens, Philippe Reinhardt, Eskindir Tesfay, Lotta Herzog
review_content: einfach schlechte deutsche inception 🙃

movie_title: I Know What You Did Last Christmas
cast_names: Dan Robins, Leona Clarke, Julia Quayle
review_content: A waste of a spooky premise. I Know What You Did Last Christmas squanders a potentially tense setup — estranged friends, a secluded holiday reunion, and a dark prank in the past — on paper-thin characters, flat dialogue, and clumsy pacing. At just 1h10m the film rushes through plot beats without building suspense or emotional stakes, so the mystery never feels threatening and the scares land with a thud. Gregory Randolph Jr.’s direction and C.M. Wright’s script lean on genre tropes instead of fresh ideas, and while the cast (Helen Baird, Jules Dan Robins and others) try to sell the material, they’re given little to work with. An amateurish effort that’ll leave horror fans disappointed.

cast_names: Rafiel Soto
review_content: I wasn't expecting much.  But I definitely wasn't expecting anything this abysmal.  I don't know who it was made for, but it wasn't me, and I would be very surprised if it was you.

[Sheffield '25] This film features splendid photography by the director and Fabio Nascimento, which shows spectacular images of the Amazon while also capturing, in intense close-ups, the emotions reflected on the face of activist and government official Juma Xipaia. In this sense, it is often overwhelming, such as during the birth of her daughter, and establishes an almost poetic tone that nevertheless maintains its political charge. However, it is disappointing in its treatment of indigenous issues, without delving into the most important aspects or offering much information about the real conflicts, being much less relevant and less revealing than it aims to be.

review_content: A Serbian Documentary rewrites expectation for infamy—it’s smart, sly, and sharp. More context than controversy.    Built from 162 hours of behind-the-scenes footage and fresh interviews, this reframes a notorious lightning rod as a deliberate, constructed howl. You see the crew’s method, the effects work, the black-comic tone between takes, and the arguments about meaning, not just shock. It also revisits the 2010 FrightFest censorship saga, which adds bite to its premiere here. Whether you rate the original or not, this is engrossing cinema history.    If you liked… Cursed Films, Room 237.

review_content: So first off, the subtitles for the movie just straight up tell you who the killer is, because every noise he makes is subtitled with his name, which was... bizarre! [spoiler][Big Ed grunts][/spoiler] - but truth be told I didn't really get if it was even supposed to be a secret the way they constantly made it so obvious...    The two sex-focused "teens" were so odd (were we supposed to think they were teens or just old people with "youthful" style???). Are they real actors or porn actors? :rofl: I cringed at almost every scene they had...    Half the scenes felt almost randomly added, and by that I mean they had nothing to do with character or plot development. Kind of like they were just padding the runtime?    Now, with all that said (and I could complain a lot more) I actually think the movie itself is _kind of successful_ in a way, because it really feels like the same level of talent from the first movie. I don't know, it was kind of endearing in a way. It was not a good movie, sure, but I think I liked it anyway?    _(The effects I have to assume were made to look bad, because they were better in cheap 80s movies)_

review_content: [Veoasecan] Despite any good intentions, everything in this film is swallowed up by a simplistic, pamphleteer approach: from the shallow script to the basic animation and the bland, voice-overs that sound more like reading than acting. There's as much depth in the characters as there is in the flat art direction surrounding them, resulting in a complete lack of emotion. Although based on a true story, its approach to gender violence is artificial and superficial. The final addition reinforces the tone of an institutional video masquerading as a feature film.

review_content: [Raindance '25] Little happens outside of Johnny Calvi's walks through the streets of London, which at least pass by recognizable places like the century-old Watkins Books, a bookstore specializing in esoteric and religious books and artifacts, which emerged during the boom in interest in esoteric subjects that occurred in England in the late 19th century. Johnny's journey makes less sense than the film itself, but this kind of guerrilla filmmaking, sustained by the bare minimum, also finds the creation of a kind of abstract science fiction. But the film's script conveys more a lack of interest than a lack of budget.

review_content: Serial-killer marriage therapy. Great hook, strong moments, then it gets soap-y and tangled. The better film is in there somewhere.    Charlie wants her husband to quit killing. He tries. Their love curdles as she covers for him and slides into violence herself. The core idea rips, and the performances pop, but the plot sprawls, detouring into melodrama and losing the cold focus that a story like this needs. You can see why THN singled it out pre-fest, and the premise could have sung like Strange Darling if it stayed leaner and meaner. As is, it fascinates in pieces rather than as a whole. cast_names: Marcus Woods, Lara Pictet
review_content: A child lost in endless corn with masked men on her heels. Bold idea, thin payoff. Overlong, indulgent, and too many scenes go nowhere.    Seven-year-old Lucy wakes in a cornfield with a head wound, a gun nearby, and hunters closing in. The set-up rules but the execution drifts. Long dialogue-light stretches promise myth and then stall; subplots appear and vanish; indulgent detours kill the pulse. Festival notes promised a potboiler in the vein of No Country for Old Men and The Strangers with a golden creature stalking the rows. On screen it needs trimming and clarity; atmosphere alone can't carry 98 minutes.    If you liked… The Strangers, The Town That Dreaded Sundown, No Country for Old Men, A Quiet Place

cast_names: Svenja Liesau, Dagmar Manzel, Milan Peschel, Florian Lukas, Steffi Kühnert
review_content: End of the war, the Russians arrive. A couple that wanted to flee to America enter an hotel with their daughter, Sophie. Inside a Scharf, a nazi officer and Beckman, a Hitler youth guy. Wassili, a russian soldier falls through the roof. The parents are killed, everybody is hurt, and looks like several lost their memory (at least completely for Sophie).    The main story takes place later in the hotel. Sophie works in the kitchen, Wassili manages it, and it serves to feed russian troops. Not sure what Scharf is doing, Beckman in hiding underneath inside the walls, stealing some of the food that Sophie sends up from the kitchen. He is clearly mentally retarded, but not clear whether that comes from the incident.    For a while the whole focus is on these missing pieces of meal. At some point the Russian general wants Wassili to marry Sophie. And then she discovers Beckman and slowly regains her memory.    It's stop motion, the character design is underwhelming, as is the animation, at the most basic level.    But mainly, oh my god, it was so boring. Literally painful to watch. The combination of the ugly graphics and the uninteresting story made it difficult to keep my eyes open, and at no moment was there anything that would have encouraged me to do so.

cast_names: Eddie McClintock, Dean Cain, Jason Campbell
review_content: The Marley of “A Christmas Carol” adaptations…  This is one of the worst attempts at adapting Dickens’ A Christmas Carol that I have ever seen… it only being… 1 hour and 3 minutes long and I kept checking to see how much longer I had to go. Honestly it could’ve been done in 20 minutes or less….  And honestly the first ~37 minutes of the film was setting up had big of a bum and out of touch Eddie McClintock’s character was… and focusing on the round table of “purgatory” second chance helpers make blasé comments over and over again as the try to push the story along.  This attempted adaptation is the Marley of A Christmas Carol adaptations… one that may save others from following the same fate.

Just "people dancing"  - an enormous number of minutes when you don't know what you are watching and why    so. the children stopped laughing after 30 minutes — they all fell asleep. review_content: I stumbled across an add for this on Instagram I believe. From the brief glimpse I had seen it looked like something I’d be into. It was really nice to see Sean William Scott back on the big screen. I totally dug how they filmed this in 16mm. It really gave it that old school, grimy, slasher feel to it. The movie had some promising ideas but it never fully executed everything. The practical effects and make up design were excellent. This had a few incredible looking gory moments. The doll face look was really creepy and intimidating. It’s a serviceable low budget horror movie. It just moves a bit slow and slightly drags but it was decent enough.  * Dolly Early Access at AMC Crestwood 18

cast_names: Max Lindsey, Fabianne Therese, Seann William Scott, Ethan Suplee, Michalina Scorzelli, Kate Cobb
review_content: A classic horror slasher monster. Even a bit forced as it reuses everything it can.      A gimmick: she's playing with dolls, has dolls all around, uses her victims as dolls  A creepy mask: big doll head.   Family trauma: clearly an abusive father, may have killed her own mother.  Near invulnerable: whatever happens she always gets up  Inhuman strength: she seems to be able to lift all the other characters.  Dolls: dolls everywhere    Pretty classic slasher stuff otherwise, with a bit of prisoner movie.    The first reveal is good though. We only see a woman, that may have killed her daughter, and when she turns around the first time: this huge doll face. Really effective.  At the beginning we could think she is a mother who killed her daughter. When it is revealed she is in fact the daughter, I thought then she might have been burying her mother, but then the ranger at the end says Macy is not the first one.  This is a really weird statement, as it means that:  1) she might have been burying just yet another victim  2) there were several others before  3) that escaped (assuming lot more that didn't)  4) the cops did nothing? Didn't investigate, didn't go to the house?    As for the slasher part, the shovel in the leg, then in the face at the beginning, combined with ripping out the jaw was afterwards is probably the only original, or at least at out of the basics, slasher move.  Oh yeah, and the punching through the father was good too.  All the rest stays pretty basic, nothing fancy, nothing gory.    Dolly's attitudes are fun, and she doesn't seem to be evil. Just probably mentally undeveloped. She doesn't really want to hut her prisoner, and she seems genuinely distressed when they don't want to play. She's also trying to help against her father.    The director is apparently looking to make both a sequel and a prequel. Would it be worth it ? Maybe. It ticks all the boxes, but it's not really novel, so it may be missing a little something to have its own appeal.

review_content: [Fantasia '25] In much the same way that the protagonist feels the frustration of motherhood that envelops her in postpartum depression, the film ends up being a frustrating experience that has an interesting premise only to waste it on a narrative of easy resources and superficial characters surrounding Pénélope. At times it can work as a psychological drama, but its introduction within the horror genre is questionable, because not even the description of the mother's point of view ends up adopting that approach. The progressive isolation of the main character and the bubble she shares with her son are well described, but the introduction of disturbing elements to build a suspense structure is unnecessary.

review_content: [Fantasia '25] A post-apocalyptic story that focuses more on the relationships between people than on describing the consequences of a virus affecting the water supply. Throughout the film, an interesting question is raised about the need to rebalance societies when extreme situations arise, faced with the maintenance of certain privileges. Forgetting the past to restart the present, referring to the river of oblivion that the souls of the dead drank from to forget their earthly lives. But the idea is not fully developed enough to drive a narrative that ends up drowning in dialogue and performances that are too languid.

review_content: To summarize, it's a film that tells different stories experienced by different people, all starting from the same point. But it's so complicated, so disjointed, that I couldn't understand what it was trying to say. The ending is even more absurd, in my opinion. Each story is watchable on its own, but the film as a whole is very, very confusing. Also, East Asian culture is very different from ours. Maybe that's why it seemed so complicated to me. It's as if the person who edited the film didn't put any effort into it, as if something was deliberately left out. I gave it a 5 out of 10. In short, it's like a Korean version of an American horror movie, but half-baked. Still, thanks for your efforts.

cast_names: You Chea-myung, Moon Chae-won, Seo Young-hee, Solar, Cha Sun-woo, Sumin, Seo Ji-soo, Son Ju-yeon, Hứa Vĩ Văn
review_content: Several stories. With a small link in between them all.    1) A guardian tree that, once it has tasted blood, spread pollen that infects people, making its flower bloom out of them.  Great start. A good dark ambiance, some mystery, some gore. Would have been great if everything was like that.    2) A woman steals beauty treatment packages from her neighbour, that happen to be full organ replacements.  Weird beauty products that come with a price. Not a very original theme, specially in Asiatic cinema, but it's done well, has a totally different vibe from the first one, more energetic, more colourful, and with extra comedic stuff.    3) A woman buying a ghost to help her daughter's studies.  Yet another very Asiatic theme. A bit less interesting in the realization, except for the ghost market part. But yeah, school ghost, couldn't do without I imagine. Barely average, specially comparing with the other parts.    4) Cops chasing a guy.   This was such a downgrade from the beginning that I'm not even sure I saw it completely, I micro slept several times and don't remember much.    5) Girls exploring an haunted school building.  Very typical, completely copied on Ring (it's even said). Better than the cops part, but not that much.    It then comes together, linking the pieces between the different parts, but either it was a bit messy, or I micro slept a bit more and missed some stuff.    Great potential, the first two parts are great, the way all is subtly interconnected is good, but in fine it looks more like a cheap Netflix short series than actual cinema.

review_content: So first off, the subtitles for the movie just straight up tell you who the killer is, because every noise he makes is subtitled with his name, which was... bizarre! [spoiler][Big Ed grunts][/spoiler] - but truth be told I didn't really get if it was even supposed to be a secret the way they constantly made it so obvious...    The two sex-focused "teens" were so odd (were we supposed to think they were teens or just old people with "youthful" style???). Are they real actors or porn actors? :rofl: I cringed at almost every scene they had...    Half the scenes felt almost randomly added, and by that I mean they had nothing to do with character or plot development. Kind of like they were just padding the runtime?    Now, with all that said (and I could complain a lot more) I actually think the movie itself is _kind of successful_ in a way, because it really feels like the same level of talent from the first movie. I don't know, it was kind of endearing in a way. It was not a good movie, sure, but I think I liked it anyway?    _(The effects I have to assume were made to look bad, because they were better in cheap 80s movies)_
